topics.title: Introduction: The Impact of Hardware and Software Technology Trends; Self Review - Instruction Set Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction: The Impact of Hardware and Software Technology Trends; Self Review - Instruction Set Architecture # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Defining the Domain — Advanced Computer Architecture **Advanced Computer Architecture (ACA)** is the scientific and engineering discipline concerned with the *holistic design, organisation, optimisation, and evaluation* of computing systems — spanning the instruction set, microarchitecture, memory hierarchy, I/O, interconnection, and parallel execution fabric. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, ACA is treated as a *post-graduate cognitive extension* of the digital systems curriculum: it expects students to reason not just about *how* a processor executes instructions, but *why* the industry pivoted from frequency scaling, to multi-core, to heterogeneous and domain-specific architectures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECST528 / Module 1):** *"Classes of computers, parallel and scalable architectures, technology trends and their impact on architectures, cost-performance issues, quantitative approach to computer design."* ## 1.2 The "Impact" — A Multi-Decadal Hardware & Software Reality The architecture of any CPU is the *equilibrium point* of five relentless forces: **transistor density, clock frequency, power dissipation, memory latency, and parallelism**. When one force breaks, the entire design philosophy pivots. This is the central thesis of Module 1. ### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — The City Traffic Analogy Imagine a single-lane road (single-core CPU). Adding more cars (instructions) does not help once the road is full. **Moore's Law** keeps building wider highways, but **Dennard Scaling** (the constant-power-per-mm² rule) broke down around 2005. So instead of making one *very fast* supercar, we now build *many moderate cars running in parallel lanes* (multi-core). The "traffic cop" (compiler, OS scheduler) must now decide *which car goes in which lane* — that is the new architectural problem. | Real-World Element | Architectural Counterpart | |---|---| | Highway lanes | CPU cores | | Cars | Threads / Instructions | | Traffic signals | Pipeline stages, branch predictors | | Speed limit sign | Power wall, TDP budget | | City map | Memory hierarchy & address space | | GPS routing | Compiler optimisations, prefetchers | ## 1.3 Self-Review Primer — What is an Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)? The **ISA** is the *contractual interface* — a clean abstraction layer — between the *hardware* (microarchitecture) and the *software* (compiler, OS, applications). It is the *only* level at which a program's binary is portable across vastly different physical implementations of the same architecture. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (Patterson & Hennessy, 1990, canonical, no edition invented):** *"The ISA of a processor is the set of programmer-visible resources and behaviours the processor exposes — including the instruction set, registers, memory addressing modes, data types, and the effect of each operation on machine state."* ### 1.3.1 ISA — The "Language" of the Machine Just as a human language has an *alphabet* (instructions), *grammar* (instruction formats), and *vocabulary* (addressing modes, registers), an ISA defines the machine's language. Two dominant philosophical schools exist: - **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** — Rich, variable-length instructions (e.g., x86). Each instruction does "a lot." - **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** — Lean, fixed-length, load/store architecture (e.g., ARM, MIPS, **RISC-V**). Compiler does the heavy lifting. ### 1.3.2 Key ISA Components (Quick Recap) | Component | Role | Example (RISC-V `rv64imac`) | |---|---|---| | Instruction formats | R, I, S, B, U, J | 32-bit fixed width | | Registers | Fast on-chip storage | `x0`–`x31` (64-bit) | | Addressing modes | How operands are located | Immediate, register, base+offset | | Data types | Bit-widths of operands | 8/16/32/64-bit int, IEEE-754 FP | | Memory model | Ordering, consistency | RVWMO (RISC-V Weak Memory Ordering) | | Privilege levels | User/supervisor/machine | U-mode, S-mode, M-mode | > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU Viva:** *"ISA = What the programmer writes. Microarchitecture = How the hardware runs it."* Same ISA, different microarchitecture (e.g., Intel Skylake vs. AMD Zen) ⇒ same binary, different performance. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Walls and Five Pivots — A Structured Chronology Advanced Computer Architecture is best understood as a *sequence of constraint-violations* forcing architectural pivots. The five classical "walls" form the spine of every KTU Module 1 answer on technology trends. ### 2.1.1 The Memory Wall (Wulf & McKee, 1995) - **Observation:** Processor speed was improving at **~55%/year**, DRAM latency was improving at only **~7%/year**. - **Consequence:** Memory access became the dominant cost in a typical instruction. - **Architectural Pivot:** Deeper, smarter *cache hierarchies*; prefetching; out-of-order execution hiding latency. ### 2.1.2 The Power Wall (Dennard Scaling Breakdown, ~2005) - **Original Dennard Rule (1974):** As transistors shrink, *power density remains constant* — voltage and current scale proportionally. - **Breakdown:** Sub-threshold leakage current stops scaling below ~90 nm. Voltage could not be reduced further. - **Consequence:** Power $\propto C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ — frequency $f$ could no longer rise without melting the chip. - **Architectural Pivot:** End of single-core frequency scaling → **multi-core revolution** (Intel Core 2 Duo, 2006). ### 2.1.3 The ILP Wall (Instruction-Level Parallelism Wall) - **Observation:** Studies (Wall, 1991; Ranganathan & Jouppi) showed *most programs* have very limited inherent ILP — typically **4-wide to 8-wide** issue was the practical ceiling. - **Architectural Pivot:** From *extracting more ILP in one core* to *exploiting Thread-Level Parallelism (TLP)* across cores. ### 2.1.4 The Bandwidth/Communication Wall - **Observation:** On-chip wires do not scale as gracefully as transistors; pin bandwidth and off-chip signalling limit throughput. - **Architectural Pivot:** Network-on-Chip (NoC), 3D-stacked DRAM (HBM), chiplet designs (AMD Infinity Fabric, Apple M-series Ultra). ### 2.1.5 The Memory / Storage Wall (Amdahl's Memory System) - **Observation:** Amdahl's rule for memory hierarchy: *system speed-up is limited by the fraction of accesses that miss*. - **Architectural Pivot:** Big caches, HBM, near-data processing, processing-in-memory (PIM). ## 2.2 Quantitative Approach — The KTU Exam Hot Zone KTU examiners *love* quantitative reasoning. Three formulas are non-negotiable for Module 1. ### 2.2.1 KTU Formula Sheet | # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | F1 | $\text{CPU Time} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | Basic execution-time equation | $IC$ = instruction count, $CPI$ = cycles per instruction, $T_{clk}$ = clock period (seconds) | | F2 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Clock rate from period | Hz (or GHz) | | F3 | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{\,t/2}$ | Moore's Law (transistor count doubles every 24 months) | $t$ in years, $N_0$ at $t=0$ | | F4 | $S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | Amdahl's Law — speed-up from parallelising fraction $f$ over $n$ processors | Dimensionless; $f \in [0,1]$, $n \in \mathbb{N}$ | | F5 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Dynamic power per transistor (switching) | Watts; $\alpha$ = activity factor | | F6 | $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \cdot CPI_i \big/ IC$ | Weighted average CPI across instruction classes $i$ | Used in CPI-stacked problems | | F7 | $MIPS = \dfrac{IC}{\text{CPU Time} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | Higher is better, but *ISA-dependent* | | F8 | $MFLOPS = \dfrac{\#\text{FP ops}}{\text{Execution time} \times 10^{6}}$ | Floating-point throughput metric | Used in HPC benchmarking | | F9 | $AMAT = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \cdot (T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \cdot (T_{mem}))$ | Average Memory Access Time | $T_{Lx}$ hit time, $MR_{Lx}$ miss rate | | F10 | $R_{\text{bus}} = f_{\text{bus}} \cdot W$ | Maximum bus / interconnect bandwidth | Bytes/sec | > [!CAUTION] > **KTU Pitfall:** MIPS is **not** a fair cross-ISA metric. A RISC-V program may have a higher MIPS rating than an x86 program running the *same task* simply because RISC-V instructions do less per instruction. Always prefer **execution time** for fair comparison. ## 2.3 Self-Review of ISA — Classifying the Design Space ### 2.3.1 Internal Organisation of an ISA A complete ISA specification must define: 1. **Instruction formats** — bit-level layout of opcode, operands, immediates. 2. **Data types & sizes** — integer, floating-point, packed SIMD, vector. 3. **Registers** — number, width, special-purpose vs general-purpose. 4. **Addressing modes** — register, immediate, direct, indirect, indexed, base+offset, PC-relative. 5. **Memory model** — byte-addressability, endianness, consistency model. 6. **Operand interpretation** — big vs little endian, signed vs unsigned. 7. **Control flow** — conditional branches, jumps, calls/returns, branches delay slots (legacy). 8. **Exception/interrupt model** — traps, privilege escalation, vectoring. 9. **I/O model** — memory-mapped vs port-mapped. ### 2.3.2 CISC vs RISC vs VLIW vs EPIC — A Comparative Lens | Feature | CISC (e.g., x86-64) | RISC (e.g., ARMv8, RISC-V) | VLIW / EPIC (Itanium) | |---|---|---|---| | Instruction width | Variable (1–15 bytes) | Fixed (32-bit) | Fixed (bundle of 3×41 bits in Itanium) | | Memory operands | Many | None (load/store) | Few | | Decoder complexity | High | Low | Lowest | | Compiler role | Limited | High | Critical (parallelism baked at compile time) | | Parallelism source | Hardware OoO | Hardware OoO / multi-issue | Compiler-scheduled | | Market trajectory | Dominant in servers/PCs | Dominant in mobile/edge | Discontinued (Itanium 2021) | > [!NOTE] > **Why Itanium failed (great KTU 14-mark answer):** EPIC required the *compiler* to expose parallelism the hardware simply executed. Compilers of the 1990s–2000s were not mature enough; meanwhile, x86 hardware added OoO execution and re-claimed the high-performance niche. *Lesson:* Architectural decisions are *co-evolutionary* with software capability. ### 2.3.3 Stack vs Register vs Accumulator Machines A more fundamental taxonomy classifies ISAs by where operands *live*: - **Accumulator** — 1 implicit accumulator (e.g., early Intel 8086, many DSPs). - **Stack** — operands on a stack (e.g., JVM bytecode, x87 FPU historically). - **Register-Memory** — operands can be in memory or registers (e.g., x86). - **Register-Register (Load/Store)** — only registers hold operands, memory only via load/store (e.g., RISC-V, ARM, MIPS). **Code density of `C = A + B`:** | ISA Class | Code Sequence | |---|---| | Stack | `push A; push B; add; pop C` | | Accumulator | `load A; add B; store C` | | Register-Memory | `add C, A, B` (one instruction!) | | Load/Store (RISC-V) | `lw t0, 0(A); lw t1, 0(B); add t2, t0, t1; sw t2, 0(C)` | > [!TIP] > **Observation:** Register-Memory (CISC) has the highest *code density*; Load/Store (RISC) has the highest *regularity*. Trade-off: density reduces I-cache pressure; regularity simplifies pipelining and OoO hardware. ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering - **Mobile SoC designers** (Apple M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon) explicitly *co-design* the ISA extension (e.g., Apple AMX, ARM SVE) with the compiler and the target workload (image processing, ML). - **Datacenter architects** (AWS Graviton, Google Axion ARM-based) choose RISC for energy-efficiency, since power and cooling are the dominant operational costs. - **Embedded IoT designers** choose RISC-V for *cost and openness* — no licensing royalty, custom extensions, deterministic real-time behaviour. - **HPC & ML accelerators** (NVIDIA Hopper, Google TPU) implement *bespoke ISAs* (tensor instructions, matrix engines) — the *ultimate* expression of the technology-trend response. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The CPU Performance Equation The fundamental performance equation underlies every KTU quantitative problem. We derive it from first principles. ### 3.1.1 Starting Definitions Let: - $IC$ = total number of instructions executed in a program - $CPI_i$ = average cycles per instruction of class $i$ (e.g., ALU, load, branch) - $IC_i$ = count of instructions of class $i$ - $f$ = clock frequency in Hz - $T_{clk} = 1/f$ = clock period in seconds ### 3.1.2 Total Execution Cycles The total number of clock cycles is the sum of cycles contributed by each class: $$ C_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \cdot CPI_i $$ where $n$ is the number of instruction classes (ALU, load, store, branch, FP, etc.). ### 3.1.3 Average CPI By definition, the *average* CPI is the total cycles divided by the instruction count: $$ CPI \;=\; \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{IC} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \cdot CPI_i}{IC} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{IC_i}{IC} \cdot CPI_i $$ The factor $\dfrac{IC_i}{IC}$ is the *fraction* of instructions belonging to class $i$ — call it $f_i$. Hence: $$ CPI \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot CPI_i $$ This is a *weighted average* with weights summing to 1: $\sum f_i = 1$. ### 3.1.4 Execution Time Each cycle takes $T_{clk}$ seconds, so total execution time is: $$ \text{CPU Time} \;=\; C_{\text{total}} \cdot T_{clk} \;=\; \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \cdot CPI_i\right) \cdot T_{clk} $$ Substituting the average-CPI form: $$ \text{CPU Time} \;=\; IC \cdot CPI \cdot T_{clk} \;=\; \frac{IC \cdot CPI}{f} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **canonical KTU formula F1**. Every performance question is a variation on this. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law ### 3.2.1 Setup Suppose a fraction $f$ of a program's execution time can be *parallelised* (perfectly scalable), and the remaining fraction $(1-f)$ is *strictly sequential*. We have $n$ identical processors working in parallel. ### 3.2.2 Speed-up on the Parallel Fraction If the parallel fraction takes time $T_p$ on one processor, on $n$ processors it takes $\dfrac{T_p}{n}$ (ideal linear scaling). ### 3.2.3 Total Speed-up Derivation Original execution time: $T_1 = T_s + T_p = 1$ (normalised to 1 unit). New execution time: $T_n = T_s + \dfrac{T_p}{n} = (1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}$. Speed-up is the ratio: $$ S_n \;=\; \frac{T_1}{T_n} \;=\; \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} $$ ### 3.2.4 Limiting Behaviour As $n \to \infty$: $$ S_{\infty} \;=\; \frac{1}{1-f} $$ This is the *asymptotic ceiling* — no matter how many processors you add, the sequential part dominates. > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** If only 10% of a program is sequential ($f=0.9$), the *absolute maximum* speed-up is $\frac{1}{0.1} = 10\times$, even with infinite processors. This is why real-world multi-core speed-ups plateau at 4–8× for typical workloads. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Moore's Law as a Continuous Exponential Empirically (Gordon Moore, 1965), transistor counts doubled roughly every 24 months. Let the doubling period be $\tau = 2$ years. $$ \frac{N(t+\tau)}{N(t)} \;=\; 2 $$ The continuous exponential satisfying this is: $$ N(t) \;=\; N_0 \cdot 2^{t/\tau} \;=\; N_0 \cdot 2^{t/2} $$ The *time constant* of the exponential is $\tau/\ln 2 \approx 2.885$ years. ## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023 adapted]:** A processor runs at **2.5 GHz**. A benchmark has $IC = 100 \times 10^6$ instructions, broken down as: 40% ALU (CPI = 1), 30% load (CPI = 4), 20% store (CPI = 3), 10% branch (CPI = 2). Compute (a) effective CPI, (b) CPU time, (c) MIPS rating. ### 3.4.1 Solution **(a) Effective CPI** (weighted average using $f_i$ and $CPI_i$): $$ CPI = (0.40)(1) + (0.30)(4) + (0.20)(3) + (0.10)(2) $$ $$ CPI = 0.40 + 1.20 + 0.60 + 0.20 = 2.40 $$ **(b) CPU Time** using F1: $$ \text{CPU Time} = IC \cdot CPI \cdot T_{clk} = IC \cdot CPI / f $$ $$ \text{CPU Time} = \frac{100 \times 10^{6} \times 2.40}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{240 \times 10^{6}}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} $$ $$ \text{CPU Time} = 96 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{s} = 96 \; \text{ms} $$ **(c) MIPS Rating** using F7: $$ MIPS = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{2.40 \times 10^{6}} = 1041.67 \; \text{MIPS} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification check:** $MIPS = IC / (\text{Time} \times 10^6) = 100 \times 10^6 / (96 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6) = 100 / 96 = 1041.67$ ✓. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — ISA Class × Application Fit | ISA Class | Code Density | Decode HW | Compiler Burden | Power | Best Fit Application | KTU Discuss? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | CISC (x86) | High | Heavy | Low | High | Desktop, server | Yes | | RISC (ARM/RISC-V) | Medium | Light | Medium | Low | Mobile, edge, IoT | Yes | | VLIW / EPIC | Medium | Minimal | **Very High** | Low | DSP, embedded | Yes (historical) | | Accumulator | Low | Minimal | Low | Very Low | Microcontrollers, DSPs | Optional | | Stack (JVM) | N/A (bytecode) | JVM JIT | N/A | N/A | Cross-platform | Optional | | Vector / SIMD | N/A (extension) | Sub-word ALUs | High | Medium | HPC, ML inference | Yes (Module 2) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Technology-Trends-to-Architecture Causal Map A Mermaid block-level schematic showing the causal chain from transistor physics to current architectural state. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Transistor Scaling
Moore 1965"] --> B["Denser Chips
More Transistors"] B --> C["Dennard Scaling
1974 2005"] C --> D["Power Wall
Voltage Stops Scaling"] D --> E["Frequency Plateau
~3 to 5 GHz Ceiling"] E --> F["Multi Core Pivot
2006 Onwards"] B --> G["Memory Wall
DRAM Latency Gap"] G --> H["Cache Hierarchy
Prefetch OoO"] B --> I["ILP Wall
Inherent Parallelism Limit"] I --> F F --> J["TLP Era
Threads and Cores"] J --> K["Heterogeneous SoCs
Big Little Cores"] K --> L["Domain Specific
GPUs TPUs NPUs"] L --> M["Chiplet and 3D Stacking
HBM Advanced Packaging"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow is a *cause→consequence→architectural response* chain. Tracing any leaf node back to root "Transistor Scaling" demonstrates the central KTU Module 1 thesis. ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — ISA Layered Abstraction Stack ```mermaid flowchart TD L1["Application Layer
Browsers Compilers ML Models"] --> L2["High Level Language
C C plus plus Java Python"] L2 --> L3["Compiler Optimisation Passes
Instruction Selection Register Allocation"] L3 --> L4["ISA Instruction Set Architecture
Registers Opcodes Addressing Modes Memory Model"] L4 --> L5["Microarchitecture
Pipeline OoO Cache Branch Predictor"] L5 --> L6["Logic and Circuit Design
ALUs FPU Register File"] L6 --> L7["Transistors and Physical Layout
CMOS FinFET GAA"] L4 -.ISA Boundary.-> L5 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The horizontal line between L4 and L5 is the ISA boundary.** Everything *above* the line is portable across microarchitectures; everything *below* is a private implementation detail. The ISA is therefore the most *politically* important layer in computer architecture. ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — RISC-V RV32I Instruction Format Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart LR F["32 bit Instruction Word"] --> OF["Opcode 7 bits"] F --> RD["rd 5 bits Destination Register"] F --> F3["funct3 3 bits"] F --> RS1["rs1 5 bits Source Register 1"] F --> RS2["rs2 5 bits Source Register 2"] F --> F7["funct7 7 bits"] OF --> OPM["R type Arithmetic Logical"] RD --> OPM F3 --> OPM RS1 --> OPM RS2 --> OPM F7 --> OPM ``` **Total bit check:** $7 + 5 + 3 + 5 + 5 + 7 = 32$ ✓. ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Memory Hierarchy Latency Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TD R["Registers 1 cycle 0.3 ns"] --> L1["L1 Cache 4 cycles 1 ns 32 KB"] L1 --> L2["L2 Cache 12 cycles 3 ns 256 KB"] L2 --> L3["L3 Cache 40 cycles 10 ns 8 MB shared"] L3 --> RAM["Main DRAM 200 cycles 50 ns 32 GB"] RAM --> SSD["NVMe SSD 10000 cycles 25 us 1 TB"] SSD --> NET["Network Storage 10 to the 6 cycles 1 ms Remote"] ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading:** Each level down is **~10× larger** but **~10× slower** — the geometric ratio that defines the *cache hierarchy*. The programmer-visible memory is the union of all levels; the *effective* access time is the AMAT (F9). ## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Generic CPU) ```mermaid flowchart LR PC["Program Counter"] --> IF["Instruction Fetch Unit"] IF --> ID["Instruction Decode Register Fetch"] ID --> ISSUE["Issue Queue and Dispatch"] ISSUE --> EXE1["ALU Integer Pipeline"] ISSUE --> EXE2["FPU FP Pipeline"] ISSUE --> EXE3["Load Store Unit LSU"] ISSUE --> EXE4["Branch Unit"] EXE1 --> WB["Writeback and Retire"] EXE2 --> WB EXE3 --> WB EXE4 --> WB WB --> RF["Architectural Register File x0 to x31"] RF --> ID EXE3 --> CACHE["L1 D Cache and L2"] CACHE --> MEM["DRAM Controller and Main Memory"] IF --> ICACHE["L1 I Cache"] ICACHE --> MEM ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mapping to ISA:** The *architectural register file* is *the* most visible ISA-defined structure. The *commit/retire logic* implements the architectural memory consistency model. The *issue queue* is purely microarchitectural — invisible at the ISA level. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]** *"List any three major technology trends that have shaped the evolution of modern computer architecture in the last two decades, briefly explaining each."* **Model Answer (≈120 words, 3 marks):** 1. **End of Dennard Scaling (Power Wall, ~2005):** Voltage could no longer be reduced with transistor size, so $P \propto V^2 \cdot f$ made further frequency scaling thermally impossible. This forced the industry to switch from faster single cores to **multi-core** CPUs. 2. **Slowing of Moore's Law / Diminishing returns:** Transistor density growth has slowed; cost per transistor is no longer falling. The architectural response is **specialisation** — domain-specific accelerators (GPUs, TPUs, NPUs) that pack more useful work per watt than general-purpose cores. 3. **Memory wall:** DRAM latency has improved only ~7%/year versus 55%/year for CPUs. Modern designs invest heavily in **deeper cache hierarchies, HBM, prefetching, and processing-in-memory (PIM)** to bridge the gap. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Do not list *"Moore's Law"* and *"Dennard Scaling"* as the same item — they are *related but distinct*. Examiners will deduct ½–1 mark for this conflation. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Expected Pattern]** *"Differentiate between RISC and CISC ISAs. Give one example architecture for each."* **Model Answer (≈120 words, 3 marks):** | Aspect | RISC | CISC | |---|---|---| | Instruction width | Fixed (typically 32-bit) | Variable (1–15 bytes) | | Memory operands | Load/Store only | Many | | Decoding | Hardwired, simple | Microcoded, complex | | Registers | Many (32–64) | Few (8–16 legacy) | | Cycles per instruction | Mostly 1 | Variable, often >1 | | Compiler reliance | High | Moderate | | Example | **ARMv8 / RISC-V** | **x86-64 (AMD64/Intel 64)** | RISC optimises for *hardware simplicity and pipelining throughput*; CISC optimises for *code density and backward compatibility*. Modern x86 chips internally translate CISC instructions into RISC-like *µops* before execution — a pragmatic convergence. ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Choice A — 14 Marks: Quantitative Performance Analysis **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern, Adapted]** > *"A processor runs at 3.0 GHz. A program has $IC = 200 \times 10^6$ instructions, distributed as: 50% ALU instructions (CPI = 1), 25% load instructions (CPI = 5), 15% store instructions (CPI = 3), 10% branch instructions (CPI = 2).* > > *(a) Compute the effective CPI, the CPU execution time, and the MIPS rating of the processor on this program. (7 marks)* > > *(b) An architectural optimisation replaces the load unit with one having CPI = 2 (instead of 5), at a clock-frequency cost reducing the clock to 2.4 GHz. Compute the new effective CPI, the new execution time, and the speed-up. Comment on whether the optimisation is worthwhile. (7 marks)"* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks** *Step 1: Effective CPI (weighted average).* $$ CPI_{\text{old}} = (0.50)(1) + (0.25)(5) + (0.15)(3) + (0.10)(2) $$ $$ CPI_{\text{old}} = 0.50 + 1.25 + 0.45 + 0.20 = 2.40 $$ *Step 2: CPU Time.* $$ \text{CPU Time}_{\text{old}} = \frac{IC \cdot CPI_{\text{old}}}{f} = \frac{200 \times 10^6 \times 2.40}{3.0 \times 10^9} $$ $$ \text{CPU Time}_{\text{old}} = \frac{480 \times 10^6}{3.0 \times 10^9} = 160 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{s} = 160 \; \text{ms} $$ *Step 3: MIPS.* $$ MIPS_{\text{old}} = \frac{f}{CPI_{\text{old}} \times 10^6} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^9}{2.40 \times 10^6} = 1250 \; \text{MIPS} $$ **Part (b) — 7 marks** *Step 1: New CPI (load CPI is now 2 instead of 5).* $$ CPI_{\text{new}} = (0.50)(1) + (0.25)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.10)(2) $$ $$ CPI_{\text{new}} = 0.50 + 0.50 + 0.45 + 0.20 = 1.65 $$ *Step 2: New CPU Time at $f_{\text{new}} = 2.4$ GHz.* $$ \text{CPU Time}_{\text{new}} = \frac{200 \times 10^6 \times 1.65}{2.4 \times 10^9} = \frac{330 \times 10^6}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 137.5 \; \text{ms} $$ *Step 3: Speed-up.* $$ S = \frac{\text{Time}_{\text{old}}}{\text{Time}_{\text{new}}} = \frac{160}{137.5} = 1.1636 \approx 1.16\times $$ *Comment:* Yes, the optimisation is worthwhile — a **16% execution-time reduction** is significant, and in real designs CPI reductions of this magnitude are common wins. The trade-off (lower frequency) is acceptable because the *aggregate work per second* ($IC$/time) still improves. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall — KTU Examiner Alert:** A frequent error is computing speed-up as the CPI ratio directly. The correct definition is *time ratio*, not CPI ratio. Marks will be docked for this. > **Second pitfall:** Forgetting to update $f$ to 2.4 GHz in the new CPU time. This is a 2-mark deduction. > **Third pitfall:** Reporting the *frequency reduction* (3.0 → 2.4 GHz) without computing the *net* effect. Always quote the speed-up number, not the frequency change. --- ### Choice B — 14 Marks: Conceptual/Design Question **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023 Pattern, Adapted]** > *"(a) Describe Amdahl's Law. With the help of a numerical example, show that even with 1000 processors, a program with 5% sequential code cannot exceed 20× speed-up. (7 marks)* > > *(b) Explain the role of the ISA as an abstraction layer between hardware and software. Discuss why a 'clean' ISA matters for both compiler design and for hardware innovation, using the RISC-V open ISA as an illustrative case. (7 marks)"* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks** *Definition:* Amdahl's Law quantifies the maximum achievable speed-up $S$ when a fraction $f$ of a computation is parallelisable and the remaining $(1-f)$ is strictly sequential, executed on $n$ identical processors: $$ S(n) = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} $$ *Numerical demonstration:* - Sequential fraction: $1 - f = 0.05 \Rightarrow f = 0.95$ - Number of processors: $n = 1000$ $$ S(1000) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{1000}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.00095} = \frac{1}{0.05095} \approx 19.63 $$ *Asymptotic limit* ($n \to \infty$): $$ S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1-f} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 $$ Therefore, the speed-up is *bounded above by 20×*, and 1000 processors give only **19.63×** — within 1.8% of the absolute ceiling. Adding more cores beyond ~100 yields *negligible* return. **Part (b) — 7 marks** The **ISA** is a *contractual specification* of the programmer-visible state and operations of a processor. It sits at the *only* boundary where software is portable and hardware is hidden. *Why a clean ISA matters:* - **Compiler portability:** A clean, orthogonal ISA (regular instruction formats, symmetric register file, few special-purpose registers) lets compiler backends generate efficient code for *any* conforming implementation. x86 accumulated 40+ years of legacy special cases; RISC-V was designed *from scratch* for orthogonality. - **Hardware innovation:** Once the ISA is fixed, hardware teams can innovate freely on microarchitecture (pipelines, OoO, speculation, cache policies) without breaking software. The same ARMv8 ISA runs on 1-W IoT cores (Cortex-M) and 200-W server cores (Neoverse). - **RISC-V case study:** RISC-V (UC Berkeley, 2010 onwards) is an *open, modular, extensible* ISA — institutions and companies (SiFive, Western Digital, NVIDIA) add custom extensions (vector, bitmanip, crypto) without paying royalties. This has *democratised* processor design and accelerated both academic research and industrial innovation. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** In Part (a), students often forget to state the *asymptotic limit* explicitly. Examiners specifically test the *understanding* that speed-up is *bounded* — a single numerical answer without the limit statement loses 1 mark. > **In Part (b),** do not list 7 different ISAs as examples. Pick **one** (RISC-V is canonical) and develop the argument in *depth* — examiners reward *depth* over *breadth* in 14-mark questions. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > Print this section before your KTU Module 1 exam. It is the densest distillation in this notes file. - ✅ **CPU Time** $= IC \times CPI \times T_{clk} = IC \times CPI / f$. *Memorise this verbatim.* - ✅ **Amdahl's Law** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}$. *Always quote the asymptotic limit* $S_\infty = 1/(1-f)$. - ✅ **Moore's Law** = doubling every 24 months, expressed as $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/2}$. - ✅ **Power Wall** = Dennard Scaling broke at ~90 nm. Multi-core pivot followed. - ✅ **Memory Wall** = DRAM-CPU latency gap; resolved by caches, prefetch, OoO. - ✅ **ILP Wall** = inherent ~4–8 wide issue ceiling; multi-thread / multi-core pivot. - ✅ **RISC = fixed, load/store, simple decode, compiler-heavy.** CISC = variable, memory operands, microcoded, hardware-heavy. - ✅ **ISA ≠ Microarchitecture.** Same ISA, different microarchitectures coexist (e.g., x86 across Intel, AMD, Zhaoxin). - ✅ **Five ISA components:** formats, registers, addressing modes, data types, memory model. - ✅ **Endianness, addressing modes, data types** are *all* ISA-defined; *cache size, pipeline depth, branch predictor algorithm* are *all* microarchitectural. - ✅ **MIPS is ISA-dependent**; do **not** use it for cross-architecture comparison. - ✅ **Speed-up ≠ CPI ratio.** Speed-up is *time ratio*. Common KTU trap. - ✅ **Vector / SIMD ISAs** are extensions; they are discussed in Module 2 but the *concept* of packed data types is an ISA-level decision. --- *End of Module 1 Notes — PECST528 Advanced Computer Architecture, KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020).*

topics.title: Memory Addressing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Addressing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Memory Addressing** is the mechanism by which a processor specifies, locates, and accesses data or instructions stored in the memory hierarchy. It defines the rules, formats, and computational methods used to translate a *logical (virtual) address* issued by a program into a *physical address* that corresponds to a real storage location in main memory (RAM) or cache.

In the context of **Advanced Computer Architecture**, memory addressing encompasses:
- **Address Space**: The range of addresses a processor can generate (determined by address bus width, $n$ bits $\Rightarrow$ $2^n$ locations).
- **Address Modes**: The methods available in the instruction set to specify operand locations.
- **Address Translation**: The mapping from virtual to physical addresses via mechanisms such as segmentation and paging.
- **Byte Ordering (Endianness)**: The convention for storing multi-byte words in memory.
- **Alignment**: The placement of data at addresses divisible by the data size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: A processor with an address bus of width $W$ bits can directly access $2^W$ unique memory locations. For example, a 32-bit address bus addresses **4 GB** ($2^{32}$ bytes) of physical memory, while a 64-bit bus addresses **16 EB** ($2^{64}$ bytes).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **Indian Postal System** as a memory system:
- **Logical Address** = Your house name and locality (what you write on a letter). It is *virtual* and human-friendly.
- **Address Translation** = The postal sorting office that converts your house name into a precise **PIN code** with GPS coordinates.
- **Physical Address** = The actual GPS coordinates of your house (a real, unique location in the world).
- **Addressing Mode** = The way you describe where to deliver: *"to my father's office"* (indirect), *"to Flat 3B"* (direct), *"two houses next to the temple"* (displacement/indexed).
- **Memory Hierarchy** = The chain of delivery: local postman (registers) $\rightarrow$ city post office (L1 cache) $\rightarrow$ state sorting hub (L2/L3 cache) $\rightarrow$ national central depot (RAM) $\rightarrow$ archive warehouse (disk).

> [!NOTE]
> Just as multiple houses in different states can share the name *"Shanti Niwas"* but have unique PIN codes, multiple programs can use the same logical address **0x0040**, but the **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** translates each into a unique physical frame.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Logical Address** | Address generated by the CPU (program-visible) |
| **Physical Address** | Real address seen by the memory hardware |
| **Effective Address (EA)** | Final computed address after applying the addressing mode |
| **MMU** | Memory Management Unit - hardware for translation |
| **Word** | Natural data unit of the processor (e.g., 32-bit or 64-bit) |
| **Byte** | Smallest addressable unit (**8 bits**) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Memory Addressing Modes

The **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** defines *how* the operand of an instruction is located. Each addressing mode is essentially a formula for computing the **Effective Address (EA)**.

### 2.1.1 Immediate Addressing
- The operand value is *embedded directly* in the instruction.
- **No memory access** is required to fetch the operand.
- **EA**: not applicable; Operand = constant in instruction.
- **Use**: Loading constants, small arithmetic values.
- **Example (x86)**: `MOV AL, 25` $\Rightarrow$ AL $\leftarrow$ 25.

### 2.1.2 Direct (Absolute) Addressing
- The instruction contains the **full memory address** of the operand.
- **EA = Address field of instruction**.
- **Example**: `MOV AL, [2000H]` $\Rightarrow$ AL $\leftarrow$ M[2000H].

### 2.1.3 Register Addressing
- The operand is located in a **CPU register**.
- **EA = Register number encoded in instruction** (no memory access).
- **Fastest** addressing mode - no address calculation, no bus cycle.

### 2.1.4 Register Indirect Addressing
- The instruction specifies a **register that holds the memory address** of the operand.
- **EA = [R]** (contents of register R).
- **Example**: `MOV AL, [BX]` (x86) - BX holds the address.

### 2.1.5 Displacement (Base-Register) Addressing
- Combines a constant offset with a register value.
- **EA = [R] + Offset**, where offset is part of the instruction.
- **Example**: `MOV AL, [BX + 8]` $\Rightarrow$ AL $\leftarrow$ M[[BX] + 8].

### 2.1.6 Indexed Addressing
- Uses a dedicated **index register** plus a constant base.
- **EA = Base + [Index $\times$ Scale]**.
- Common for array traversal: `EA = Address_of_array + index $\times$ size_of_element`.

### 2.1.7 Relative Addressing
- EA computed relative to the **Program Counter (PC)**.
- **EA = [PC] + Offset**. Used heavily in branch instructions.
- Example: `BEQ R1, R2, +8` (branch to PC+8 if R1==R2).

### 2.1.8 Auto-increment / Auto-decrement
- The register used in indirect mode is **automatically updated** before or after the access.
- Pre-decrement: `EA = [R]; R = R - 1`.
- Post-increment: `EA = [R]; R = R + 1`.
- Used for stack operations and sequential buffer access.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table — KTU exam questions frequently ask to compute EA for a given mode or determine the address bus size for a memory capacity.

| Mode | Effective Address (EA) Formula | Memory Access Required | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Immediate | Operand is constant in IR | **No** | Constants |
| Direct (Absolute) | EA = Address field | Yes | Global variables |
| Register | EA = R (register) | **No** | ALU operations |
| Register Indirect | EA = [R] | Yes | Pointer dereference |
| Displacement | EA = [R] + Disp | Yes | Local variables, structures |
| Indexed | EA = Base + [Idx] $\times$ Scale | Yes | Array access |
| Relative | EA = [PC] + Offset | Yes (for branch) | Loops, branches |
| Auto-increment | EA = [R]; R $\leftarrow$ R + $k$ | Yes | Stack pop, sequential read |
| Auto-decrement | R $\leftarrow$ R - $k$; EA = [R] | Yes | Stack push |

### 2.2.1 Address Bus & Memory Capacity

$$
\text{Memory Capacity (bytes)} = 2^{W}
$$

where $W$ is the address bus width in bits.

### 2.2.2 Word-to-Byte Address Translation

For a machine with word size $w$ bytes, the byte address of word $i$ is:

$$
\text{Byte Address of Word } i = i \times w
$$

### 2.2.3 Effective Address for Indexed Mode (Array Element)

For an array `A[0], A[1], ..., A[n-1]` with element size $s$ bytes, the address of element `A[i]` is:

$$
\text{EA}(A[i]) = \text{Base Address of A} + (i \times s)
$$

### 2.2.4 Physical Address from Page Number and Offset

$$
\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Frame Number} \times \text{Page Size}) + \text{Offset}
$$

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

Memory addressing underpins **every** software-driven engineering field:
- **Embedded Systems (KTU ECE/EEE)**: Microcontrollers like ARM Cortex-M use **bit-banding** addressing to atomically access individual bits in memory-mapped peripheral registers.
- **Operating Systems**: Virtual memory systems in Linux/Windows rely on **paged address translation** via the MMU and TLB.
- **High-Performance Computing (HPC)**: NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) architectures assign addresses such that memory access time depends on the *processor-memory proximity*.
- **Compilers**: Code generators emit addressing modes that match the data structure layout (e.g., indexed mode for arrays, displacement for struct fields).
- **Network Processors**: Use **content-addressable memory (CAM)** where data is located by content, not address.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Effective Address Computation in Indexed Mode

**Problem**: Consider an `int` array `ARR` declared at base address **0x1000**. Each `int` occupies 4 bytes. Compute the effective address of `ARR[5]` using indexed addressing with index register `IX = 5` and scale factor $s = 4$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Identify the addressing mode formula.

For indexed mode, the general effective address formula is:

$$
\text{EA} = \text{Base} + [\text{IX}] \times s
$$

where `Base` is the starting address of the array, `IX` is the index register, and $s$ is the scale factor (size of each element).

**Step 2**: Substitute the known values.

We are given:
- $\text{Base} = 0\text{x}1000$
- $[\text{IX}] = 5$ (the index value stored in register IX)
- $s = 4$ (because each `int` is 4 bytes)

Substituting:

$$
\text{EA} = 0\text{x}1000 + 5 \times 4
$$

**Step 3**: Perform the multiplication.

$$
5 \times 4 = 20
$$

In hexadecimal, $20_{10} = 0\text{x}14$.

**Step 4**: Perform the addition.

$$
\text{EA} = 0\text{x}1000 + 0\text{x}0014
$$

$$
\text{EA} = 0\text{x}1014
$$

**Step 5**: Verify by logical reasoning.

Element 0 is at $0\text{x}1000$, element 1 is at $0\text{x}1004$, element 2 at $0\text{x}1008$, element 3 at $0\text{x}100C$, element 4 at $0\text{x}1010$, and element 5 at $0\text{x}1014$. ✓

**Final Answer**: The effective address of `ARR[5]` is $\mathbf{0\text{x}1014}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Physical Address from Logical (Virtual) Address

**Problem**: A paging system uses pages of size **4 KB** ($2^{12}$ bytes). The logical address is **32 bits**. The page table entry for the accessed page indicates that the **Frame Number is 0x0000A3** (in hex). Given a logical address of **0x00005A20**, compute the physical address.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Recognize the structure of the 32-bit logical address.

For a 4 KB page, the offset occupies the lower **12 bits** and the page number occupies the upper **20 bits**.

$$
\text{Logical Address} = \underbrace{\text{Page Number (20 bits)}}_{\text{upper bits}} \; \vert \; \underbrace{\text{Offset (12 bits)}}_{\text{lower bits}}
$$

**Step 2**: Extract the page number from the logical address.

Logical address in binary (hex $\rightarrow$ binary):

$$
0\text{x}00005A20 = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0101\,1010\,0010\,0000
$$

Taking the upper 20 bits:

$$
\text{Page Number} = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0101 = 0\text{x}00005
$$

**Step 3**: Extract the offset (lower 12 bits).

$$
\text{Offset} = 1010\,0010\,0000 = 0\text{x}A20
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The offset is **preserved unchanged** during translation — this is a fundamental property of paging.

**Step 4**: Replace the page number with the frame number from the page table.

The page table says: Page $0\text{x}00005$ maps to Frame $0\text{x}0000A3$.

**Step 5**: Construct the physical address.

The physical address is formed by concatenating the frame number (20 bits) with the offset (12 bits):

$$
\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Frame Number} \ll 12) \; \vert \; \text{Offset}
$$

Computing:

$$
\text{Frame Number shifted} = 0\text{x}0000A3 \, \ll 12 = 0\text{x}0000A3000
$$

Adding the offset:

$$
\text{Physical Address} = 0\text{x}0000A3000 + 0\text{x}A20
$$

$$
\text{Physical Address} = 0\text{x}0000A3A20
$$

**Step 6**: Verify with the standard formula.

$$
\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Frame Number} \times \text{Page Size}) + \text{Offset}
$$

$$
\text{Physical Address} = 0\text{x}0000A3 \times 0\text{x}1000 + 0\text{x}A20
$$

$$
= 0\text{x}000A3000 + 0\text{x}A20 = 0\text{x}000A3A20
$$

**Final Answer**: The physical address is $\mathbf{0\text{x}000A3A20}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Memory Capacity from Address Bus Width

**Problem**: A processor has a 36-bit address bus. Compute the maximum directly addressable memory capacity in (a) bytes, (b) kilobytes, (c) megabytes, (d) gigabytes, and (e) terabytes.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Apply the capacity formula.

$$
\text{Capacity} = 2^{W}
$$

With $W = 36$:

$$
\text{Capacity} = 2^{36} \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2**: Convert step by step using powers of 2.

$$
2^{36} = 2^{6} \times 2^{30} = 64 \times 2^{30} \text{ bytes}
$$

Since $2^{10} = 1\,\text{KiB}$, $2^{20} = 1\,\text{MiB}$, $2^{30} = 1\,\text{GiB}$:

$$
2^{36} \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ GiB}
$$

**Step 3**: Final tabulated answers.

| Unit | Value |
|---|---|
| Bytes | $2^{36} = 68{,}719{,}476{,}736$ bytes |
| Kilobytes (KiB) | $2^{26} = 67{,}108{,}864$ KiB |
| Megabytes (MiB) | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ MiB |
| Gigabytes (GiB) | $2^{6} = 64$ GiB |
| Terabytes (TiB) | $2^{-4} = 1/16$ TiB |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Shortcut**: To convert $2^{W}$ to GiB, simply compute $2^{W-30}$. For $W=36$, that is $2^{6} = 64$ GiB.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Byte Ordering (Endianness) for a 32-bit Word

**Problem**: Store the 32-bit hexadecimal value **0x12345678** at byte address 1000 in both **little-endian** and **big-endian** formats. The machine has a 32-bit data bus and uses byte addressing.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Decompose the 32-bit value into four 8-bit bytes.

The most significant byte (MSB) of `0x12345678` is `0x12`, and the least significant byte (LSB) is `0x78`:

$$
0\text{x}12345678 = \underbrace{0\text{x}12}_{\text{MSB byte}} \;\; \underbrace{0\text{x}34} \;\; \underbrace{0\text{x}56} \;\; \underbrace{0\text{x}78}_{\text{LSB byte}}
$$

**Step 2**: Little-endian storage (LSB at the lowest address).

In little-endian, the LSB occupies the smallest address:

| Byte Address | 1000 | 1001 | 1002 | 1003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stored Value | 0x78 | 0x56 | 0x34 | 0x12 |
| Significance | LSB | | | MSB |

**Step 3**: Big-endian storage (MSB at the lowest address).

In big-endian, the MSB occupies the smallest address:

| Byte Address | 1000 | 1001 | 1002 | 1003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stored Value | 0x12 | 0x34 | 0x56 | 0x78 |
| Significance | MSB | | | LSB |

**Step 4**: Concise representation.

$$
\text{Little-Endian: } 1000:\,78, \; 1001:\,56, \; 1002:\,34, \; 1003:\,12
$$

$$
\text{Big-Endian: } 1000:\,12, \; 1001:\,34, \; 1002:\,56, \; 1003:\,78
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **x86 / x64** architectures (Intel, AMD) are **little-endian**. **ARM** supports *bi-endian* configuration. **Older PowerPC / SPARC / Internet network byte order** use **big-endian**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Memory Address Translation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Core] -->|Logical Address| MMU
    MMU[Memory Management Unit] -->|Physical Address| L1
    L1[L1 Cache] -->|Hit| CPU
    L1 -->|Miss| L2
    L2[L2 Cache] -->|Hit| CPU
    L2 -->|Miss| L3
    L3[L3 Cache] -->|Hit| CPU
    L3 -->|Miss| RAM
    RAM[Main Memory RAM] -->|Data| CPU
    TLB[Translation Lookaside Buffer] -.->|Cached Translation| MMU
    PT[Page Table in Memory] -.->|Page Fault Fill| TLB
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Addressing Mode Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Addressing Modes] --> B[Immediate]
    A --> C[Direct]
    A --> D[Indirect]
    A --> E[Register]
    A --> F[Register Indirect]
    A --> G[Displacement]
    A --> H[Indexed]
    A --> I[Relative]
    A --> J[Auto Inc Dec]

    G --> G1[Base plus Offset]
    H --> H1[Array Element Access]
    I --> I1[PC Relative Branch]
    J --> J1[Stack Push Pop]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Effective Address Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IR[Instruction Fetch IR] --> DEC[Decode Opcode]
    DEC --> MOD[Identify Addressing Mode]
    MOD --> EA1[Compute EA]
    EA1 --> MEM[Memory Access at EA]
    MEM --> EX[Execute ALU Op]
    EX --> WB[Write Back Result]
    REG[Register File] -.->|Read Write| EA1
    REG -.->|Read Write| WB
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map: Address Space Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VA[Virtual Address Space]
        V1[User Program A]
        V2[User Program B]
        V3[Kernel Space]
    end
    subgraph PA[Physical Address Space]
        P1[DRAM Bank 0]
        P2[DRAM Bank 1]
        P3[Memory Mapped IO]
    end
    V1 --> MMU1
    V2 --> MMU1
    V3 --> MMU1
    MMU1[MMU and TLB] --> P1
    MMU1 --> P2
    MMU1 --> P3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Effective Address. Explain Immediate and Direct addressing modes with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The **Effective Address (EA)** is the final, computed memory location of the operand after the addressing-mode formula has been applied. It is the address that is actually placed on the address bus to fetch/store the operand.

**Immediate Addressing**: The operand value is embedded directly inside the instruction itself, so no memory fetch is required.

**EA formula**: Not applicable — operand is the constant field of the instruction.

**Example (x86)**: `MOV AX, 1234H` $\Rightarrow$ AX $\leftarrow$ 1234H immediately. Used for loading constants.

**Direct (Absolute) Addressing**: The instruction contains the full memory address of the operand.

**EA formula**: $\text{EA} = \text{Address field of instruction}$.

**Example (x86)**: `MOV AX, [2000H]` $\Rightarrow$ AX $\leftarrow$ M[2000H]. Used for accessing global variables.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Distinguish between Logical Address and Physical Address. What is the role of the MMU?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | Logical Address | Physical Address |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Address generated by the CPU program | Real address of memory hardware |
| Visibility | Visible to the program/application | Invisible to the program |
| Uniqueness | May be same in different processes | Always unique per location |
| Generation | Issued by instruction execution | Resolved by MMU |

The **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** is a dedicated hardware unit that translates logical (virtual) addresses into physical addresses using **page tables** and a **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)**. It also enforces **memory protection** by checking access rights, and it implements **virtual memory** by allowing programs to use more address space than physically available RAM (backed by disk).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 - Modified]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the different types of memory addressing modes with neat diagrams and effective address calculations. State one application of each mode.

**Model Solution**:

Memory addressing modes determine how the operand of an instruction is located. The major modes are:

**1. Immediate Addressing**
- The operand is part of the instruction.
- **EA**: not applicable.
- **Application**: Loading small constants.
- **Example**: `MVI A, 05H` (8085) $\Rightarrow$ A $\leftarrow$ 05H.

**2. Direct (Absolute) Addressing**
- Instruction contains the memory address of the operand.
- $\text{EA} = \text{Address field}$.
- **Application**: Accessing global/static variables.
- **Example**: `LDA 2000H` (8085) $\Rightarrow$ A $\leftarrow$ M[2000H].

**3. Register Addressing**
- Operand is in a CPU register.
- $\text{EA} = \text{Register identifier}$.
- **Application**: Fast ALU operations.
- **Example**: `ADD B` (8085) $\Rightarrow$ A $\leftarrow$ A + B.

**4. Register Indirect Addressing**
- A register holds the address of the operand.
- $\text{EA} = [R]$.
- **Application**: Pointer dereference, array traversal.
- **Example**: `MOV A, M` where HL pair = address.

**5. Displacement (Indexed) Addressing**
- $\text{EA} = \text{Base} + [\text{Index}] \times \text{Scale}$.
- **Application**: Accessing array elements, struct fields.
- **Example**: `MOV AX, [SI + 4]` (x86).

**6. Relative Addressing**
- $\text{EA} = [\text{PC}] + \text{Offset}$.
- **Application**: Branch instructions, position-independent code.
- **Example**: `JNZ +8` (8085).

**7. Auto-increment / Auto-decrement**
- The pointer register is updated automatically.
- **Application**: Sequential buffer access, stack operations.

**Diagram**: *(See Section 4.2 of these notes for the classification flowchart.)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] A processor has a 32-bit address bus and a 16-bit data bus. The memory is byte-addressable.
- **(i)** Find the maximum directly addressable memory capacity.
- **(ii)** How many memory locations of 1 byte each can be accessed?
- **(iii)** If the memory is organized as $4 \text{ M} \times 16$ chips, how many such chips are required to fill the address space?

**Model Solution**:

**(i) Maximum addressable memory capacity**:

$$
\text{Capacity} = 2^{32} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GiB} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(ii) Number of byte locations**:

$$
N = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ locations}
$$

**(iii) Number of $4\text{M} \times 16$ chips required**:

Each $4\text{M} \times 16$ chip stores $4\text{M}$ words of 16 bits each, i.e., $4 \times 2^{20} \times 2 \text{ bytes} = 8 \text{ MiB}$.

To cover $4 \text{ GiB} = 2^{32} \text{ bytes}$:

$$
\text{Chips needed} = \frac{2^{32} \text{ bytes}}{8 \text{ MiB}} = \frac{2^{32}}{2^{23}} = 2^{9} = 512 \text{ chips}
$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Modified]

#### (a) [7 Marks] What is paging? Explain how logical address is translated to physical address using a page table. A system uses a page size of 4 KB and a 32-bit logical address. If a page table entry indicates that page number 5 maps to frame number 20 (decimal), find the physical address corresponding to logical address 0x00005000.

**Model Solution**:

**Paging** is a memory management scheme that eliminates the need for contiguous physical memory allocation. Both logical and physical memory are divided into fixed-size blocks called **pages** (logical) and **frames** (physical). A **page table** maintained by the OS stores the mapping from page number to frame number.

**Translation Process**:
1. The logical address is split into a **page number** (high-order bits) and an **offset** (low-order bits).
2. The MMU uses the page number as an index into the page table.
3. The corresponding entry gives the **frame number**.
4. The frame number is concatenated with the original offset to form the **physical address**.

**Numerical Part**:

- Page size = $4 \text{ KB} = 2^{12}$ bytes $\Rightarrow$ offset is 12 bits.
- Logical address = 32 bits $\Rightarrow$ page number is 20 bits.

**Extract page number and offset**:

$$
0\text{x}00005000 = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0101\,0000\,0000\,0000
$$

- Page number = upper 20 bits = $0\text{x}00005 = 5$ (decimal).
- Offset = lower 12 bits = $0\text{x}000$.

**Apply mapping**: Page 5 $\rightarrow$ Frame 20 (decimal) = $0\text{x}14$.

**Form the physical address**:

$$
\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Frame Number} \ll 12) \;\vert\; \text{Offset}
$$

$$
\text{Physical Address} = (20 \times 4096) + 0 = 81{,}920 = 0\text{x}00014000
$$

**Final Answer**: The physical address is $\mathbf{0\text{x}00014000}$.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain Big-Endian and Little-Endian byte ordering with an example. A 32-bit word `0xABCD1234` is stored at byte address `2000`. Show the memory layout for both schemes. Mention two processors that use each scheme.

**Model Solution**:

**Byte ordering (endianness)** defines the order in which the bytes of a multi-byte word are stored in memory.

**Big-Endian**: The **Most Significant Byte (MSB)** is stored at the **lowest memory address**. This is the natural "left-to-right" reading order used by humans.

**Little-Endian**: The **Least Significant Byte (LSB)** is stored at the **lowest memory address**. This is favored by x86 processors for its arithmetic simplicity.

**Example**: Store `0xABCD1234` at address 2000 (byte-addressable memory).

**Big-Endian Layout** (MSB at 2000):

| Address | 2000 | 2001 | 2002 | 2003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | AB | CD | 12 | 34 |

**Little-Endian Layout** (LSB at 2000):

| Address | 2000 | 2001 | 2002 | 2003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 34 | 12 | CD | AB |

**Processors using each scheme**:

| Scheme | Processors |
|---|---|
| **Big-Endian** | Motorola 68k, Sun SPARC, PowerPC (legacy), Internet (network byte order) |
| **Little-Endian** | Intel x86, AMD x86-64, RISC-V (configurable), most ARM (default) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students frequently lose 1–2 marks by:
> 1. Confusing the direction of byte storage (writing `AB` at 2003 in big-endian).
> 2. Forgetting to mention the **size of the page offset** (12 bits for 4 KB pages) when extracting page number vs. offset.
> 3. Writing EA formulas without showing the *substitution step*.
> 4. Stating "the address bus is 32 bits" but computing capacity as $32$ bytes instead of $2^{32}$ bytes.
> 5. Forgetting to mention the **MMU/TLB** in any address-translation question — KTU examiners expect this terminology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memory Addressing**

- **Address bus width** $W$ $\Rightarrow$ capacity $= 2^W$ bytes.
- **Effective Address (EA)** is the *final computed* operand location.
- **Logical vs. Physical** address translation is performed by the **MMU** using **page tables** and a **TLB cache**.
- **Page offset is preserved** during virtual-to-physical translation.
- **Physical Address** = (Frame Number $\times$ Page Size) + Offset.
- **Indexed mode EA** = Base + (Index $\times$ Scale).
- **Relative mode EA** = [PC] + Offset (used in branches).
- **Auto-increment/decrement** modes are used for stacks and sequential buffers.
- **Big-endian** stores MSB at the lowest address; **Little-endian** stores LSB at the lowest address.
- **x86/x64 = little-endian**; **older SPARC/PowerPC = big-endian**; **ARM = bi-endian** (configurable).
- **Aligned access** is faster and is required on many RISC ISAs (e.g., ARM, SPARC); **misaligned** access can cause faults.
- **Byte addressing** (most modern CPUs) allows accessing any single byte; **word addressing** is faster but cannot access sub-word data.
- For a machine with word size $w$ bytes, **word $i$** resides at byte address $i \times w$.
- The **MMU** also enforces **memory protection** via access bits in page table entries (Read/Write/Execute permissions).
- **TLB hit** $\Rightarrow$ fast translation (1 cycle); **TLB miss** $\Rightarrow$ page table walk (tens of cycles).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressing Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Addressing Modes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Addressing Modes** are the algorithmic procedures or hardware-supported techniques used by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) to compute and locate the **Effective Address (EA)** of an instruction's **operand** (data) residing in memory, a CPU register, or as an immediate constant within the instruction stream itself. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context for *Advanced Computer Architecture (PECST528)*, addressing modes are studied as a critical interface between the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** and the underlying **micro-architecture**. The addressing mode field, decoded by the **Control Unit (CU)**, dictates the operand-fetching strategy for every machine-level instruction. The choice of addressing mode directly influences the **program size**, **execution speed**, **memory bandwidth utilization**, and the **complexity of the pipelined datapath**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECST528 – Module 1):** Addressing modes form the foundational basis for understanding instruction formats, micro-operations, addressing structure, and the design of program counter (PC) and instruction registers in advanced processor design. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Treasure Map" Intuition Imagine you are telling a robot exactly **where** to find a specific book in a massive library: | Robot Instruction | Equivalent Addressing Mode | Intuitive Meaning | |---|---|---| | *"Pick up the book that is **literally** in your hand."* | **Immediate** | The data is part of the instruction itself. | | *"Go to **Aisle 7, Shelf 3**."* | **Direct** | The instruction directly names the memory address. | | *"Look at the **chalkboard** – the chalkboard has the address written on it."* | **Indirect** | The instruction points to a pointer that points to data. | | *"The book is in your **left pocket**."* | **Register** | Data is inside a fast CPU register. | | *"Check the note inside your **left pocket** – that note tells you the shelf."* | **Register Indirect** | A register holds the address of the data. | | *"Start from Aisle 7 and move **5 shelves forward**."* | **Indexed / Displacement** | A base address plus an offset. | In essence, **the addressing mode is the strategy** the CPU uses to *find* or *interpret* an operand. ## 1.3 Why Addressing Modes Matter (Engineering Perspective) - **Flexibility**: Different programs need different operand access patterns (array indexing, pointer dereferencing, looping). - **Code Density**: Powerful addressing modes (like *Indexed* or *Base+Offset*) reduce the number of instructions, saving memory. - **Performance**: *Register-based* modes are **10x to 100x faster** than memory-based modes. - **Compiler Optimization**: Compilers (like GCC targeting RISC-V) select addressing modes to minimize load-store overhead. - **Hardware Design**: Each mode requires specific hardware in the **Address Generation Unit (AGU)** of the CPU. > [!TIP] > **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:** Memory access typically takes **\sim 100 ns** while register access takes **\sim 1 ns**. Addressing modes heavily influence this performance gap. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Addressing Modes Addressing modes are broadly classified based on the **location of the operand**: ### A. Immediate Addressing Mode - The operand is a **constant** embedded within the instruction itself. - **No memory reference** is required to fetch the data. - **Use case:** Initializing registers, setting constants. - **Disadvantage:** Limited operand size; cannot be modified at runtime. ### B. Direct (Absolute) Addressing Mode - The instruction's address field contains the **full effective address** of the operand in memory. - **Effective Address (EA) = Address field of the instruction**. - **Use case:** Accessing static/global variables. - **Disadvantage:** Large address field → bigger instruction size. ### C. Indirect Addressing Mode - The address field of the instruction points to a **memory location (or register) that contains the effective address**. - **EA = M[Address field]** (where M[x] denotes contents of memory location x). - **Use case:** Implementing pointers, dynamic data structures. - **Disadvantage:** Requires **multiple memory accesses**. ### D. Register Addressing Mode - The operand resides **inside a CPU register**, specified by the instruction. - **EA = R[i]** (the register itself is the operand). - **Fastest mode** – no memory access required. - **Use case:** Arithmetic operations between registers. ### E. Register Indirect Addressing Mode - A register holds the **memory address** of the operand. - **EA = R[i]** (the register contains the address, not the data). - **Use case:** Pointer-based memory access, array traversal. ### F. Displacement Addressing Mode - Combines a **base/relative address** with an **offset** specified in the instruction. - **Three sub-variants:** 1. **Relative Addressing:** EA = PC + Offset (used for branch instructions). 2. **Base Register Addressing:** EA = BaseReg + Offset. 3. **Indexed Addressing:** EA = IndexReg + Base Address (where IndexReg is auto-incremented/decremented). ### G. Auto-increment / Auto-decrement Addressing Mode - A variant of register indirect mode where the register is **automatically updated** before or after the access. - **Pre-increment:** EA = ++R[i] (R[i] incremented first). - **Post-increment:** EA = R[i]++ (R[i] incremented after use). - **Use case:** Stack operations, sequential array processing. ### H. Stack (Implied) Addressing Mode - The operand is implicitly on the **Top of Stack (TOS)**, pointed to by the **Stack Pointer (SP)**. - **EA = SP** (or TOS register). - **Use case:** Zero-address machines (e.g., Java Virtual Machine stack model), function calls. ## 2.2 The Three Steps of Operand Resolution For every instruction, the **Control Unit** performs these steps to fetch the operand: 1. **Decode Mode Bits:** Extract the addressing mode from the instruction opcode. 2. **Compute Effective Address (EA):** Apply the addressing mode formula. 3. **Fetch Operand:** Read the operand from `M[EA]` (memory) or `R[i]` (register). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet – Effective Address Calculations > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **highest-yield** content for KTU board exams. Memorize every formula. | # | Addressing Mode | Effective Address (EA) Formula | Operand Location | Memory References | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Immediate | EA = Not Applicable (data is in instruction) | Instruction itself | **0** | | 2 | Direct (Absolute) | $EA = A$ | $M[A]$ | **1** | | 3 | Indirect (Memory) | $EA = M[A]$ | $M[M[A]]$ | **2** | | 4 | Register | $EA = R[i]$ | $R[i]$ | **0** | | 5 | Register Indirect | $EA = R[i]$ | $M[R[i]]$ | **1** | | 6 | Displacement (Base+Offset) | $EA = R[Base] + Offset$ | $M[R[Base] + Offset]$ | **1** | | 7 | Relative (PC-Relative) | $EA = PC + Offset$ | $M[PC + Offset]$ | **1** | | 8 | Indexed | $EA = R[Index] + Base$ | $M[R[Index] + Base]$ | **1** | | 9 | Auto-increment | $EA = R[i]; R[i] \leftarrow R[i] + d$ | $M[R[i]]$ | **1** | | 10 | Auto-decrement | $R[i] \leftarrow R[i] - d; EA = R[i]$ | $M[R[i]]$ | **1** | | 11 | Stack (Implied) | $EA = SP$ | $M[SP]$ | **1** | > [!NOTE] > **Notation used:** $A$ = Address field in instruction, $R[i]$ = Register $i$, $M[x]$ = Memory contents at address $x$, $PC$ = Program Counter, $SP$ = Stack Pointer, $d$ = step size (usually 1 or operand size). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **CISC Architectures** (x86): Use **many complex addressing modes** (e.g., `SIB` byte in x86 supporting 24 indexed combinations). - **RISC Architectures** (ARM, RISC-V, MIPS): Use **simple, regular addressing modes** – typically only Register, Immediate, Base+Offset (for loads/stores), and PC-relative (for branches). - **DSPs** (Digital Signal Processors): Use **specialized addressing modes** like *circular buffering* and *bit-reversed* addressing for fast FFT computation. - **GPU Architectures** (CUDA cores): Use *predicated* and *uniform* addressing for SIMT execution. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example – Tracing the Effective Address > [!NOTE] > **Given:** A hypothetical processor has registers $R_0 = 400$, $R_1 = 1000$, $PC = 200$. Memory contents (decimal) are shown below. An instruction at address `200` uses the **Indexed Addressing Mode** with `Base = 1000` (from $R_1$) and `Index = R_0`. Determine the **Effective Address (EA)** and the **final operand value**. | Memory Address | Contents | |---|---| | 400 | 700 | | 700 | 800 | | 800 | 200 | | 1000 | 400 | | 1400 | 1000 | ### Step-by-Step Solution (Exhaustive Derivation) **Step 1 – Identify the Addressing Mode and Extract Inputs** - Mode: Indexed Addressing - Index Register: $R_0 = 400$ - Base Address: $R_1 = 1000$ (provided as part of the instruction operand) **Step 2 – Apply the Effective Address Formula for Indexed Mode** The general formula is: $$EA = \text{Base} + R[\text{Index}]$$ **Step 3 – Substitute the Numeric Values** Substitute the given values of the base and index register: $$EA = 1000 + R_0$$ $$EA = 1000 + 400$$ $$EA = 1400$$ **Step 4 – Interpret the Result** The CPU computes the EA as $1400$. This means the operand is located at memory address $1400$. **Step 5 – Fetch the Operand** Read the contents of memory location $1400$ from the memory table: $$\text{Operand} = M[1400] = 1000$$ **Step 6 – Final Answer** - **Effective Address (EA)** = $1400$ - **Final Operand Value** = $1000$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Solving for EA Across All Modes Using the same machine state ($R_0 = 400$, $R_1 = 1000$, $PC = 200$) and the memory table above, we now solve for the **Effective Address and Final Operand** for each addressing mode. This matrix is the **single most important analytical table** for KTU problems. | # | Instruction Operand | Mode | EA Formula | EA Calculation | Operand Value | Reasoning | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `ADD R2, #50` | Immediate | Data is `#50` | N/A | $50$ | Operand is part of instruction. | | 2 | `LDR R3, 1000` | Direct | $EA = 1000$ | $EA = 1000$ | $M[1000] = 400$ | Instruction gives full address. | | 3 | `LDR R3, (1000)` | Indirect | $EA = M[1000]$ | $EA = 400$ | $M[400] = 700$ | Goes through a pointer at `1000`. | | 4 | `ADD R4, R0` | Register | $EA = R_0$ | $EA = 400$ | $400$ | Data is in $R_0$. | | 5 | `LDR R5, (R0)` | Reg. Indirect | $EA = R_0$ | $EA = 400$ | $M[400] = 700$ | $R_0$ holds the address. | | 6 | `LDR R6, 50(R0)` | Displacement | $EA = R_0 + 50$ | $EA = 400 + 50 = 450$ | $M[450]$ (assumed 0) | Offset added to base. | | 7 | `BR 50 (PC-Rel)` | Relative | $EA = PC + 50$ | $EA = 200 + 50 = 250$ | $M[250]$ (assumed 0) | PC-relative branch. | | 8 | `LDR R7, 1000(R0)` | Indexed | $EA = 1000 + R_0$ | $EA = 1000 + 400 = 1400$ | $M[1400] = 1000$ | Worked example above. | | 9 | `LDR R8, (R1)+` | Auto-increment | $EA = R_1; R_1 \leftarrow R_1 + 4$ | $EA = 1000$ (then $R_1 = 1004$) | $M[1000] = 400$ | $R_1$ auto-updated. | | 10 | `LDR R9, -(R1)` | Auto-decrement | $R_1 \leftarrow R_1 - 4; EA = R_1$ | $R_1 = 996$; $EA = 996$ | $M[996]$ (assumed 0) | $R_1$ decremented first. | | 11 | `POP R10` | Stack Implied | $EA = SP$ (assumed $R_1$) | $EA = 1000$ | $M[1000] = 400$ | Uses Stack Pointer. | > [!TIP] > **Board Exam Strategy:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Identify the addressing mode for the instruction `X`"*, always look for **three clues**: (1) Is the operand in **parentheses** (→ Indirect/Reg-Indirect)? (2) Is there a **`#` or constant without parentheses** (→ Immediate)? (3) Is there a **register name without parentheses** (→ Register)? ## 3.3 Comparative Performance Analysis For the same operation (`Operand = A + B`), the **number of memory accesses** differs drastically: | Mode Used for `A` | Mode Used for `B` | Total Memory Accesses | Relative Speed | |---|---|---|---| | Register | Register | **0** | **Fastest** | | Register Indirect | Register | 1 | Fast | | Direct | Direct | 2 | Medium | | Indirect | Indirect | **4** | **Slowest** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked *"Which addressing mode is the fastest?"*, the answer is always **Register Addressing Mode** (operand is in CPU, zero memory access). Do **NOT** confuse this with *Register Indirect*, which still requires memory access. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification Flowchart – All Addressing Modes The following Mermaid diagram provides a hierarchical classification of all addressing modes, structured exactly as they appear in the KTU syllabus. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Addressing Modes] --> nodeB[Immediate] nodeA --> nodeC[Direct Absolute] nodeA --> nodeD[Indirect] nodeA --> nodeE[Register Based] nodeD --> nodeD1[Memory Indirect] nodeD --> nodeD2[Register Indirect] nodeE --> nodeE1[Register Direct] nodeE --> nodeE2[Register Indirect] nodeA --> nodeF[Displacement Family] nodeF --> nodeF1[Relative PC] nodeF --> nodeF2[Base Register] nodeF --> nodeF3[Indexed] nodeA --> nodeG[Auto Modify] nodeG --> nodeG1[Auto Increment] nodeG --> nodeG2[Auto Decrement] nodeA --> nodeH[Implied Stack] nodeB --> nodeBLabel[Operand IS in instruction] nodeC --> nodeCLabel[Address field IS the EA] nodeD1 --> nodeD1Label[EA = M of Address] nodeE1 --> nodeE1Label[Operand IS in register] nodeF1 --> nodeF1Label[EA = PC plus Offset] nodeF2 --> nodeF2Label[EA = Base Reg plus Offset] nodeF3 --> nodeF3Label[EA = Index plus Base] nodeH --> nodeHLabel[EA = Top of Stack SP] classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef branch fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 classDef labelBox fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#000 class nodeA root class nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG,nodeH branch class nodeD1,nodeD2,nodeE1,nodeE2,nodeF1,nodeF2,nodeF3,nodeG1,nodeG2 leaf class nodeBLabel,nodeCLabel,nodeD1Label,nodeE1Label,nodeF1Label,nodeF2Label,nodeF3Label,nodeHLabel labelBox ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Operand Resolution Pipeline The following diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of how the CPU, the **Control Unit (CU)**, and the **Address Generation Unit (AGU)** collaborate to compute the Effective Address (EA) and fetch the operand. ```mermaid graph LR stepA[Instruction Fetch
from Memory] --> stepB[Decode in
Control Unit] stepB --> stepC{Identify
Addressing Mode} stepC -->|Immediate| stepD1[Read operand
from IR field] stepC -->|Direct or Indirect| stepD2[Extract address
field A] stepC -->|Register| stepD3[Read operand
from Register Bank] stepC -->|Displacement or Indexed| stepD4[Add base plus offset
in ALU] stepD2 --> stepE[Memory Address
Register MAR] stepD4 --> stepE stepD3 --> stepF[Register Bank
Output Bus] stepD1 --> stepG[Operand Ready] stepE --> stepH[Memory Read
M of MAR] stepH --> stepI[Memory Data
Register MDR] stepF --> stepG stepI --> stepG stepG --> stepJ[Execute Operation
in ALU] stepJ --> stepK[Write Back
to Register or Memory] classDef fetchStyle fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000 classDef decodeStyle fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 classDef executeStyle fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 classDef ioStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 class stepA,stepH,stepK ioStyle class stepB,stepC decodeStyle class stepD1,stepD2,stepD3,stepD4,stepE,stepI fetchStyle class stepG,stepJ executeStyle class stepF fetchStyle ``` ## 4.3 Operand Location Visualization Table The following table visualizes **where the operand actually lives** for each addressing mode. In KTU exams, drawing a diagram like this (with arrows from instruction to operand) fetches full marks. | Mode | Instruction Points To | Operand Location | Visual Arrow Chain | |---|---|---|---| | Immediate | The data itself | Inside instruction | `Instr → Data` | | Direct | The memory cell | Memory at address A | `Instr → A → Data` | | Indirect | A pointer cell | Memory at $M[A]$ | `Instr → A → Ptr → Data` | | Register | The register | Inside CPU register | `Instr → R[i]` | | Reg. Indirect | A register holding an address | $M[R[i]]$ | `Instr → R[i] → Data` | | Indexed | $Base + R[Idx]$ sum | $M[Base + R[Idx]]$ | `Instr → Base + R[Idx] → Data` | | Stack | Stack Pointer SP | $M[SP]$ | `Instr → SP → Data` | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Addressing Mode. List any four common addressing modes with one example each. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Model Answer:** > An **addressing mode** specifies the rule or method by which the instruction's operand field is interpreted to locate the operand for execution. **Four common addressing modes:** 1. **Immediate Addressing:** `MOV R1, #5` → Operand `5` is embedded in the instruction. 2. **Direct Addressing:** `LOAD R1, 2000` → Operand is at memory address `2000`. 3. **Register Addressing:** `ADD R1, R2` → Operand is the content of register `R2`. 4. **Indirect Addressing:** `LOAD R1, (2000)` → Operand is at the address stored in memory location `2000`. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Register Addressing Mode and Register Indirect Addressing Mode. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Register Addressing | Register Indirect | |---|---|---| | **What register holds** | The actual operand (data) | The address of the operand | | **Effective Address** | $EA = R[i]$ (the register is the operand) | $EA = R[i]$ (register points to memory) | | **Memory Access Required** | No | Yes (1 memory access) | | **Speed** | Fastest | Slower (memory bottleneck) | | **Example** | `ADD R1, R2` | `LOAD R1, (R2)` | | **Use Case** | ALU operations | Pointer dereferencing | --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each choice has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]** **(a)** Explain the different types of addressing modes used in computer systems with neat diagrams. Discuss their relative merits and demerits. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The main addressing modes are: 1. **Immediate Mode:** Operand is part of instruction. *Merits:* No memory access, fast. *Demerits:* Limited operand size, inflexible. 2. **Direct Mode:** Address field gives memory address. *Merits:* Simple. *Demerits:* Large address field increases instruction length. 3. **Indirect Mode:** Address field points to a pointer. *Merits:* Supports pointers. *Demerits:* Two memory accesses required (slow). 4. **Register Mode:** Operand is in a register. *Merits:* Fastest (no memory access). *Demerits:* Limited number of registers. 5. **Register Indirect:** Register holds address. *Merits:* Compact. *Demerits:* Still needs memory access. 6. **Displacement Mode:** Combines base + offset. *Merits:* Excellent for arrays, structures, and local variables. *Demerits:* Complex hardware. 7. **Relative Mode:** PC + offset. *Merits:* Enables position-independent code. *Demerits:* Limited range. 8. **Indexed Mode:** Base + index register. *Merits:* Perfect for array traversal. *Demerits:* Needs index update logic. 9. **Auto-increment/Decrement:** Register updates automatically. *Merits:* Ideal for sequential access. *Demerits:* Side effects can cause bugs. 10. **Stack (Implied):** Uses SP implicitly. *Merits:* Compact, good for nested calls. *Demerits:* Stack overflow risk. *Conclusion:* The choice depends on program structure; modern RISC ISAs prefer **Register + Base+Offset + PC-Relative** for simplicity and pipelining. --- **(b)** A processor uses the following memory and register state. For each instruction, determine the **Effective Address (EA)** and the **final operand value**. **[7 Marks]** | Memory Address | 200 | 400 | 500 | 600 | 800 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Contents | 400 | 700 | 200 | 900 | 600 | **Registers:** $R_0 = 400$, $R_1 = 100$, $R_2 = 500$, $PC = 200$ **Instructions:** 1. `LOAD R3, 800` (Direct) 2. `LOAD R4, (R0)` (Register Indirect) 3. `LOAD R5, 100(R1)` (Displacement) 4. `LOAD R6, (R2)+` (Auto-increment) 5. `LOAD R7, -(R0)` (Auto-decrement) 6. `BRANCH 50` (PC-Relative) 7. `LOAD R8, (500)` (Memory Indirect) **Model Solution (Step-by-Step):** 1. **Direct (800):** $EA = 800$. Operand $= M[800] = 600$. 2. **Reg Indirect (R0):** $EA = R_0 = 400$. Operand $= M[400] = 700$. 3. **Displacement:** $EA = 100 + R_1 = 100 + 100 = 200$. Operand $= M[200] = 400$. 4. **Auto-increment (R2):** $EA = R_2 = 500$, then $R_2 \leftarrow 504$. Operand $= M[500] = 200$. 5. **Auto-decrement (R0):** $R_0 \leftarrow 396$, $EA = 396$. Operand $= M[396]$ (assumed 0). 6. **PC-Relative:** $EA = PC + 50 = 200 + 50 = 250$. Operand $= M[250]$ (assumed 0). 7. **Memory Indirect (500):** $EA = M[500] = 200$. Operand $= M[200] = 400$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of the **Displacement Addressing Mode**. How does it differ from **Relative** and **Indexed** modes?
