Give two real-world use cases for each. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Displacement Addressing Mode** adds a signed constant offset (specified in the instruction) to the contents of a register (or PC) to compute the EA. $$EA = R[Base] + \text{Offset}$$ **Diagram:** ``` Instruction: +----------------+ | Op | Mode | Off| +----+------+----+ | | v v +-------------------+ | ALU (Addition) | | R[Base] + Offset| +-------------------+ | v +---------+ | EA | --> M[EA] = Operand +---------+ ``` **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Relative | Base Register | Indexed | |---|---|---|---| | **Formula** | $EA = PC + Offset$ | $EA = R[Base] + Offset$ | $EA = R[Idx] + Base$ | | **What changes** | Offset (during compilation) | Offset (known at compile time) | Index register (at runtime) | | **Use Case 1** | Branch instructions | Accessing local variables | Array traversal | | **Use Case 2** | Position-independent code | Structure member access | Loop counters | | **Source of EA** | PC | Base register | Index register | | **Hardware Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium | **Real-World Use Cases:** - **Relative:** `JMP +4` in x86, branch-to-skip in `if-else`. - **Base Register:** Accessing local variables in C using a frame pointer (`EBP` in x86). - **Indexed:** `for (i=0; i [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Relative with Indexed:** Many students write $EA = PC + \text{Offset}$ when the instruction says $R[\text{Index}] + \text{Base}$. Always re-read the instruction operand format. > 2. **Forgetting the Auto-update:** In Auto-increment mode, students often forget that **$R[i]$ changes after the access**. State both the *old* and *new* values of the register. > 3. **Mixing Up Indirect Variants:** *Memory Indirect* uses $M[A]$; *Register Indirect* uses $R[i]$. The former takes **2 memory accesses**, the latter takes **1**. > 4. **Stack Mode Address:** Many forget that the **Stack Pointer (SP) is not explicitly mentioned** in the instruction — the addressing is **implied**. > 5. **Direct vs Displacement Trap:** If the instruction is `LOAD R1, 500`, it is **Direct** (EA = 500). If it is `LOAD R1, 500(R2)`, it is **Displacement** (EA = 500 + R2). The parentheses/offset makes the difference. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this before the exam):** - ☐ **Definition:** Addressing mode = *how* the operand is located. - ☐ **Fastest Mode:** Register Addressing (0 memory access). - ☐ **Slowest Mode:** Memory Indirect (2 memory accesses). - ☐ **Immediate:** Data is *inside* the instruction (no `#` confusion with direct). - ☐ **Direct:** $EA = A$ (single memory access). - ☐ **Indirect:** $EA = M[A]$ (double memory access via pointer). - ☐ **Register:** Operand *is* the register content. - ☐ **Register Indirect:** Register holds the *address*, not the data. - ☐ **Relative:** $EA = PC + Offset$ (used for branches). - ☐ **Base-Register:** $EA = R[Base] + Offset$ (used for structure fields, local vars). - ☐ **Indexed:** $EA = R[Index] + Base$ (used for arrays). - ☐ **Auto-increment:** $EA = R[i]$, then $R[i] \mathrel{+}= d$. - ☐ **Auto-decrement:** $R[i] \mathrel{-}= d$, then $EA = R[i]$. - ☐ **Stack (Implied):** $EA = SP$ (no explicit address in instruction). - ☐ **Formula to memorize:** $EA$ always = whatever holds the **address** of the operand. - ☐ **CISC vs RISC:** CISC = many modes; RISC = Register + Base+Offset + PC-Relative. - ☐ **DSP special modes:** Circular and bit-reversed addressing. - ☐ **Performance rule:** More memory references → slower execution.

topics.title: Classes of Computers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classes of Computers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Advanced Computer Architecture (PECST528)**, *Classes of Computers* refers to the formal taxonomy of computing systems categorised by their **physical scale, economic price point, expected performance envelope, application domain, and operating environment**. The standard classification (originating from Hennessy and Patterson's *Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach*) identifies three principal classes, each governed by a different set of design constraints and optimisation goals:

1. **Desktop / Personal Computers (PCs)** – General-purpose, single-user machines.
2. **Server Computers** – High-availability, network-centric, multi-user machines.
3. **Embedded Computers** – Hidden, resource-constrained, single-function machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 establishes the *quantitative* design philosophy. The phrase *"Classes of Computers"* is not a marketing term — it is a rigorous engineering classification based on the **Price / Performance / Power / Reliability** design envelope.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine three different types of vehicles on the road:

- 🚗 A **personal sedan** (Desktop) — flexible, used daily, balances fuel economy and comfort. You choose where to go.
- 🚌 A **city transit bus** (Server) — built for many passengers at once, runs 24×7, optimised for *throughput* (how many people per hour), not raw top speed.
- 🔌 A **washing-machine motor** (Embedded) — does *one thing only*, ultra-cheap, ultra-reliable, hidden inside a larger device.

In the same way, a *Desktop* is built to run any software the user installs, a *Server* is built to handle thousands of simultaneous client requests, and an *Embedded* system is built to do a single job (e.g., brake-by-wire, microwave timing) with minimum cost and power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Philosophy:** The dominant optimisation target *differs* per class.
> - Desktop → **Response time (latency)** for one user.
> - Server → **Throughput + Availability** for many users.
> - Embedded → **Cost + Power + Real-time deadline** for one task.

## 1.3 Why This Classification Matters

Different classes place radically different weights on the four axes that drive all computer architecture decisions:

| Design Axis | Desktop | Server | Embedded |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Price (USD)** | $500 – $2,000 | $5,000 – $Millions | $0.10 – $500 |
| **Performance Goal** | Response Time | Throughput + Availability | Hard / Soft Real-Time |
| **Power Budget** | 50 – 300 W | 200 W – 10 kW | 0.001 – 5 W |
| **Reliability** | 5×8 (office hours) | 24×7 (always-on) | Lifetime of product (10 yrs) |
| **Memory** | ECC *optional* | ECC *mandatory* | Often no ECC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Blurring:** Modern *smartphones* and *IoT edge devices* blur the line — a 2024 flagship phone has more compute than a 2005 server. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Are smartphones servers or embedded systems?"* The textbook answer is **Embedded-class with desktop-class performance aspirations**, but the architectural *design methodology* (energy-first, cost-first) remains that of embedded design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Class 1 — Desktop / Personal Computers

**Defining Constraints:**
- **General purpose**: The user installs arbitrary software (MS Office, games, IDEs, browsers).
- **Price-sensitive** but with a higher ceiling than embedded: $500 – $2,000.
- **Optimisation metric**: *Response time* for a single interactive user.
- **Software ecosystem**: 3rd-party binary compatibility (x86/ARM ecosystem, OS, drivers).
- **Rich I/O**: Display, keyboard, mouse, audio, USB, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, NVMe SSD, GPU.

**Architectural Implications:**
- Aggressive use of **caches**, **pipelining**, **superscalar / out-of-order execution**, **SIMD/GPU acceleration** — because latency hiding directly improves the user experience.
- DRAM sized in **gigabytes (4 GB – 128 GB)**.
- Power not a primary constraint (always plugged into mains), but thermal design power (TDP) of 65–250 W is enforced.

## 2.2 Class 2 — Server Computers

**Defining Constraints:**
- **Always-on**, network-attached, serving many users simultaneously.
- **Optimisation metric**: *Throughput* (transactions per minute, requests per second) and *Availability* (uptime, measured in *nines*: 99.999% = "five nines").
- **Reliability features**: ECC memory, redundant power supplies, redundant disks (RAID), hot-swappable components.
- **Scale**: From a 1U rack server ($5,000) to a *warehouse-scale computer* (WSC, hundreds of millions of $) like AWS data centres.

**Architectural Implications:**
- Large **last-level caches (LLC)**, **high-bandwidth memory subsystems**, **many-core CPUs** (16 – 128+ cores).
- Support for **virtualisation** (hardware-assisted VT-x, AMD-V).
- **Throughput-oriented** features: larger line/block transfers, prefetching, larger TLBs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Warehouse-Scale Computer (WSC):** A modern extreme of the server class — thousands of commodity servers connected by a fast fabric, treated as a *single* computer. Google's search infrastructure is a WSC. KTU 2024 may test this as a "modern evolution of the server class."

## 2.3 Class 3 — Embedded Computers

**Defining Constraints:**
- **Hidden** inside a larger device (microwave, ECU, pacemaker, smartphone SoC).
- **Single function** (or small fixed set of functions).
- **Hard or soft real-time deadlines** — e.g., an airbag must deploy within 5 ms.
- **Tight cost/power ceiling** (pennies per chip, milliwatts).
- Often no user interface, no disk, sometimes no OS (bare-metal firmware or RTOS like FreeRTOS, VxWorks).

**Architectural Implications:**
- Use of **ASICs**, **FPGAs**, **microcontrollers** (ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V).
- **Aggressive clock gating**, **power gating**, **DVFS** (Dynamic Voltage & Frequency Scaling).
- **Tailored ISA** (custom instruction set extensions for DSP, crypto, ML).
- Examples: ESP32 in IoT, Snapdragon SoC in phones, Aurix TC3xx in automotive.

## 2.4 The Quantitative Foundation (What All Classes Share)

Across all three classes, designers use the same three *quantitative principles*:

1. **Take Advantage of Parallelism** — increase throughput by doing many things in parallel.
2. **Principle of Locality** — programs spend most of their time in small regions of code/data; exploit via caches.
3. **Focus on the Common Case** — make the frequent case fast, the rare case merely correct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amdahl's Law (the master equation of this module):** Improving one component has diminishing returns bounded by the fraction of time that component is used. The full derivation appears in Section 3.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table. Every formula here is examinable in Part A or Part B of ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{new} = T_{old} \cdot \left[(1-F) + \dfrac{F}{S}\right]$ | Amdahl's Law — new execution time | seconds |
| 2 | $\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + \dfrac{F}{S}}$ | Overall speedup from an enhancement | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{Speedup}_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-F}$ (as $S \to \infty$) | Maximum possible speedup | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}}$ | Basic CPU performance equation | seconds |
| 5 | $\text{IPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI}$ | Instructions Per Second (rate) | instr/s |
| 6 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | $10^6$ instr/s |
| 7 | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic power dissipation | Watts |
| 8 | $\text{Energy} = P_{\text{dyn}} \cdot T = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^2$ | Energy per switching event | Joules |
| 9 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure (for constant failure rate $\lambda$) | hours |
| 10 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | System availability fraction | 0 – 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table-Syntax Safeguard:** The vertical bar `\|` for absolute value is **forbidden** in KTU-rendered tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$).

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of This Classification

The *class* of a system **dictates the architecture team's first three decisions**:

1. **What microarchitecture?**  (e.g., in-order scalar for embedded, OoO superscalar for desktop, many-core + coherence for server).
2. **What memory hierarchy?**  (e.g., no cache in a 50-cent MCU; 3-level cache + ECC in a server).
3. **What is the SLA / KPI?**  (e.g., 100 µs interrupt latency for embedded ECU; 99.999% uptime for telecom server).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (Module 1 Anchor Derivation)

This derivation is **routinely asked for 7 marks** in KTU ESE. We derive it exhaustively from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Definitions

Let:

- $T_{old}$ = execution time of the *un-enhanced* (original) program on the original machine.
- $F$ = fraction of $T_{old}$ that can be improved by the enhancement. Note that $0 \le F \le 1$.
- $(1 - F)$ = fraction of $T_{old}$ that is *unaffected* by the enhancement.
- $S$ = speedup factor achieved on the improved portion. So $S \ge 1$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Decompose the old execution time into two parts.**

The original execution time is split between the *enhanceable* portion and the *unenhanceable* portion:

$$
T_{old} = T_{\text{enhanceable}} + T_{\text{unenhanceable}}
$$

By definition of $F$:

$$
T_{\text{enhanceable}} = F \cdot T_{old}
$$

$$
T_{\text{unenhanceable}} = (1 - F) \cdot T_{old}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the speedup to the enhanceable part only.**

The enhancement speeds up only the enhanceable portion by a factor of $S$. So the new time for that portion becomes:

$$
T_{\text{enhanceable,new}} = \frac{T_{\text{enhanceable}}}{S} = \frac{F \cdot T_{old}}{S}
$$

**Step 3 — Leave the unenhanceable part unchanged.**

The unenhanceable portion runs at the *same* speed, so:

$$
T_{\text{unenhanceable,new}} = (1 - F) \cdot T_{old}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the two new portions to get the total new execution time.**

$$
T_{new} = T_{\text{enhanceable,new}} + T_{\text{unenhanceable,new}}
$$

$$
T_{new} = \frac{F \cdot T_{old}}{S} + (1 - F) \cdot T_{old}
$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $T_{old}$ (this is the standard Amdahl form).**

$$
T_{new} = T_{old} \cdot \left[ (1 - F) + \frac{F}{S} \right]
$$

This is the *Amdahl's Law equation for execution time*. ✔

**Step 6 — Derive the overall speedup formula.**

By definition:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}
$$

Substituting Step 5:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{old}}{T_{old} \cdot \left[ (1 - F) + \dfrac{F}{S} \right]}
$$

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - F) + \dfrac{F}{S}}
$$

**Step 7 — Take the limit as $S \to \infty$ to obtain the maximum possible speedup.**

If we make the enhanced part *infinitely* fast ($S \to \infty$), then $\dfrac{F}{S} \to 0$:

$$
\lim_{S \to \infty} \text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - F) + 0} = \frac{1}{1 - F}
$$

This is the **theoretical maximum speedup** — a hard ceiling that *cannot* be exceeded regardless of how fast the enhancement is. ✔

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Even if you make 90% of a program infinitely fast, the remaining 10% caps your speedup at $1 / 0.10 = 10\times$. This is why architects obsess about the *unenhanceable* (serial) portion.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (typical KTU 7-mark sub-question)

**Problem:** A program takes 100 s. 40% of it can be sped up by 3×, the rest is unchanged. Find (a) new execution time, (b) overall speedup, (c) maximum possible speedup of the enhanced portion.

**Given:** $T_{old} = 100$ s, $F = 0.40$, $S = 3$.

**Solution:**

(a) New execution time:
$$
T_{new} = 100 \cdot \left[(1 - 0.40) + \frac{0.40}{3}\right]
$$
$$
T_{new} = 100 \cdot \left[0.60 + 0.1333\right] = 100 \cdot 0.7333 = 73.33 \text{ s}
$$

(b) Overall speedup:
$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{100}{73.33} = 1.364\times
$$

(c) Maximum possible speedup (as $S \to \infty$):
$$
\text{Speedup}_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.40} = \frac{1}{0.60} = 1.667\times
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the CPU Performance Equation

The CPU performance equation is the **second most-tested formula** in Module 1.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the three basic hardware/software parameters.**

- $\text{IC}$ = *Instruction Count* — total dynamic instructions executed by the program. Determined by the **ISA + Compiler + Program**.
- $\text{CPI}$ = *Cycles Per Instruction* — average clock cycles per instruction. Determined by the **microarchitecture** (pipelining, caches, etc.).
- $T_{\text{clock}}$ = *Clock cycle time* — seconds per cycle, the inverse of frequency $f$.

**Step 2 — Multiplicative composition.**

CPU Time is the product of (number of instructions) × (cycles per instruction) × (seconds per cycle):

$$
\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}}
$$

Equivalently, substituting $T_{\text{clock}} = 1 / f$:

$$
\text{CPU Time} = \frac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{f}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive IPS and MIPS.**

Instructions per second:

$$
\text{IPS} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI}} \quad \text{[instructions/s]}
$$

Million instructions per second (a legacy but still-taught metric):

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Dynamic Power (Brief — for Part A)

The dynamic power consumed by CMOS gates is given by:

$$
P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^2 \cdot f
$$

Where $\alpha$ = activity factor, $C$ = switched capacitance, $V$ = supply voltage, $f$ = clock frequency. Since $f \propto V$ (for constant threshold scaling), power scales **cubically** with voltage, which is the basis of DVFS in embedded and mobile classes.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Class-vs-Class)

| Property | Desktop | Server | Embedded |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optimisation Target** | Response Time (1 user) | Throughput + Availability (N users) | Cost + Power + Real-Time |
| **CPU Microarchitecture** | OoO, superscalar, 4–16 cores | OoO, many-core, 16–128+ cores | In-order, scalar, 1–4 cores or MCU |
| **Cache Hierarchy** | L1/L2/L3, 4–64 MB LLC | L1/L2/L3 + huge LLC, snoop/coherent | Small L1 (8–32 KB), sometimes no L2 |
| **Memory** | 8–128 GB DDR4/DDR5, ECC optional | 64 GB – 6 TB, ECC **mandatory** | KBs – MBs of SRAM, no ECC |
| **Storage** | NVMe SSD, 256 GB – 8 TB | NVMe + HDD tiers, RAID | NOR flash, eMMC, or none |
| **Network** | 1 GbE, Wi-Fi, BT | 10–100 GbE, fabric | CAN, I2C, SPI, sometimes Wi-Fi |
| **OS** | Windows / macOS / Linux | Linux / UNIX server | Bare-metal / RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) |
| **Power Budget** | 65–300 W (TDP) | 150 W – 10 kW (per rack) | 1 mW – 5 W |
| **Cost** | $500 – $2,000 | $5 K – $Millions | $0.10 – $500 |
| **Failure Cost** | Annoying | Catastrophic ($$$) | Life-threatening (automotive/medical) |
| **Lifetime** | 3–7 yrs | 5–10 yrs | 10–20 yrs (no upgrades) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Taxonomy of Computer Classes (Hierarchical View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Computing Systems] --> B[Personal Desktop Class]
    A --> C[Server Class]
    A --> D[Embedded Class]
    A --> E[Warehouse Scale WSC Modern Extreme]
    B --> B1[PC Laptop Workstation]
    C --> C1[Rack Server]
    C --> C2[Blade Server]
    C --> C3[Mainframe Legacy]
    C --> C4[Cloud Instance VM]
    D --> D1[Microcontroller MCU]
    D --> D2[ASIC]
    D --> D3[FPGA]
    D --> D4[SoC Smartphone]
    E --> E1[Hyperscale Data Center]
    E1 --> E2[AWS Azure GCP Google]
    B1 --> F1[Optimised for Response Time One User]
    C1 --> F2[Optimised for Throughput N Users]
    D1 --> F3[Optimised for Cost and Power One Task]
    E1 --> F4[Optimised for Aggregate Throughput at Scale]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The root *A* is the universal set of computing systems. The four children (B, C, D, E) are the canonical KTU classes. Leaves list concrete subclasses. The bottom row **F1–F4** is the optimisation target — note how the *target metric* is the most fundamental differentiator.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Design Trade-off Triangle (Cost vs Performance vs Power)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Cost] -- Reduce --> B[Performance]
    B -- Increase --> P[Power]
    P -- Reduce --> A
    A -- Embed --> E[Embedded Class Low Cost Low Power]
    B -- Push --> D[Desktop Class High Performance High Cost]
    P -- Push --> S[Server Class High Throughput High Power]
    E -.Hybrid.-> S
    D -.Hybrid.-> S
    style A fill:#ffd9b3
    style B fill:#b3d9ff
    style P fill:#d9b3ff
    style E fill:#ccffcc
    style D fill:#ffcccc
    style S fill:#ccccff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the triangle:** Each vertex represents an optimisation extreme. In reality, no class lives at a single vertex — but the *direction* of pull is what defines the class. Embedded is pulled toward *Cost*, Desktop toward *Performance*, Server toward *Power* (allowed, since mains-fed).

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Multi-Class SoC

```mermaid
graph TB
    CPU[Application CPU Cluster OoO Superscalar] --> FABRIC[NoC Fabric]
    GPU[GPU Cluster SIMT] --> FABRIC
    DSP[DSP Audio Video Acceleration] --> FABRIC
    NPU[NPU Neural Engine ML] --> FABRIC
    SRAM[On-Chip SRAM KB to MB] --> FABRIC
    DRAM[External DRAM LPDDR] --> SRAM
    FLASH[External Flash eMMC UFS] --> DRAM
    PMU[PMU DVFS Clock Gating] --> CPU
    PMU --> GPU
    PMU --> DSP
    PMU --> NPU
    IO[IO Peripherals USB PCIe I2C SPI] --> FABRIC
    FABRIC --> CPU
    style CPU fill:#ffcccc
    style GPU fill:#ccccff
    style DSP fill:#ccffcc
    style NPU fill:#ffffcc
    style PMU fill:#ffccff
    style FABRIC fill:#cccccc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is the **modern smartphone SoC** (e.g., Apple M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, MediaTek Dimensity). Notice how *all three* KTU classes coexist on a single die: the **Application CPU** is desktop-class, the **DSP / NPU** are embedded-class accelerators, and the whole chip behaves as a **tiny server-class system-on-chip** for the device OS. This diagram directly answers the *"smartphone = embedded or desktop?"* viva question.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Workflow of a Quantitative Designer (How Class Drives Design)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Step 1 Identify Target Class] --> S2[Step 2 Define KPI Response Time Throughput Cost Power]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Profile Workload Benchmark]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Identify Bottleneck Amdahl Analysis]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Apply Locality and Parallelism]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Iterate Validate Against KPI]
    S6 -.Feedback.-> S1
    style S1 fill:#ffd9b3
    style S2 fill:#b3d9ff
    style S3 fill:#d9b3ff
    style S4 fill:#ccffcc
    style S5 fill:#ffcccc
    style S6 fill:#ccccff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** If a KTU 14-mark question gives you a scenario (e.g., *"Design a computer for a satellite payload"*), the answer MUST begin with *"First, I classify this as an Embedded Class system because… → KPI → Workload → Bottleneck → Solution"*. This 6-step loop is the expected answer structure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Alert (KTU 2024 ESE):** Module 1 carries **~20–25%** of the 100-mark ESE paper. Within that, *Classes of Computers + Quantitative Principles + Amdahl's Law* is a high-weightage topic — expect at least **one 14-mark question** (with internal choice) and **two 3-mark Part A questions**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 marks)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Q1a]**
*List and briefly explain the three classes of computers as defined in advanced computer architecture. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per class):**

The three classes of computers are:

1. **Desktop / Personal Computers** (1 mark) — General-purpose, single-user machines optimised for **response time**; the user installs arbitrary 3rd-party software. *Example:* A laptop running MS Windows with an x86 CPU and discrete GPU.

2. **Server Computers** (1 mark) — Network-attached, multi-user machines optimised for **throughput and availability**; they run 24×7 with ECC memory, redundancy, and support for virtualisation. *Example:* A 2U rack server in a data centre running a database engine.

3. **Embedded Computers** (1 mark) — Hidden, single-function machines optimised for **cost, power, and real-time deadlines**; often run a fixed firmware on an MCU/ASIC. *Example:* The airbag controller ECU in a car.

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Q1b]**
*State Amdahl's Law. A program runs in 200 s on a machine. 30% of the execution time is spent in a function that can be sped up by 10×. Compute the new execution time and overall speedup.*

**Model Answer:**

**Amdahl's Law Statement (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - F) + \dfrac{F}{S}}
$$
where $F$ is the enhanceable fraction and $S$ is the speedup of that fraction.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $T_{old} = 200$ s, $F = 0.30$, $S = 10$.

New execution time:
$$
T_{new} = T_{old} \cdot \left[(1 - F) + \frac{F}{S}\right] = 200 \cdot \left[0.70 + \frac{0.30}{10}\right]
$$
$$
T_{new} = 200 \cdot \left[0.70 + 0.03\right] = 200 \cdot 0.73 = 146 \text{ s}
$$

Overall speedup:
$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{200}{146} = 1.370\times
$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 marks) — *Full Module 1 Treatment*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024, Q8a, 14 marks]**

*(a)* Classify the major classes of computers and explain their **defining characteristics, design constraints, and architectural implications**. Use at least two examples per class and highlight the differing *optimisation metrics*. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* With the help of a clear derivation, state and apply **Amdahl's Law** to the following scenario: A web server spends 65% of its time in the **database query handler**, 20% in **HTTP parsing**, and the rest in **logging**. The database handler is to be optimised to run 8× faster. Calculate: **(i)** New execution time relative to a 100 s baseline, **(ii)** overall speedup, **(iii)** maximum possible speedup if the database handler were infinitely fast, **(iv)** what additional speedup would further parallelising HTTP parsing by 4× provide? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Computer systems are not designed identically. The **class** of a system dictates the trade-offs among **price, performance, power, and reliability**. The KTU 2024 syllabus recognises three canonical classes.

**Class 1 — Desktop / Personal Computers (2 marks):**
- **Defining characteristics:** General-purpose, single-user, runs 3rd-party software, rich I/O, mains-powered.
- **Design constraints:** Balanced — moderate cost ($500–$2000), moderate power (65–300 W), high performance focus on *response time* of one user.
- **Architectural implications:** OoO superscalar CPU, multi-level cache, dedicated GPU, large DRAM, NVMe SSD.
- **Examples:** Dell Inspiron laptop, Apple iMac, Lenovo ThinkStation workstation.

**Class 2 — Server Computers (2 marks):**
- **Defining characteristics:** Always-on, network-attached, multi-user, runs network services (web, DB, mail).
- **Design constraints:** High throughput, high availability (24×7), ECC, redundancy. Power allowed to be high (150 W – 10 kW per rack).
- **Architectural implications:** Many-core CPUs, large LLC, snoop/directory coherence, hardware virtualisation, hot-swappable PSUs and disks, RAID.
- **Examples:** Dell PowerEdge R750 server, AWS EC2 instance, HPE ProLiant DL380.

**Class 3 — Embedded Computers (2 marks):**
- **Defining characteristics:** Hidden, single-function, real-time deadlines, ultra-low cost and power.
- **Design constraints:** Hard constraints on cost (cents), power (mW), size (mm²), real-time latency (µs–ms).
- **Architectural implications:** In-order scalar or MCU, custom ISA extensions, DVFS, clock gating, ASIC/FPGA implementation, no OS or RTOS.
- **Examples:** ESP32 in a smart bulb, Aurix TC3xx in an automotive ECU, STM32 in a washing machine, Snapdragon SoC in a smartphone (hybrid).

### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Amdahl's Law Derivation (3 marks):**
Decompose execution time:
$$
T_{old} = F \cdot T_{old} + (1 - F) \cdot T_{old}
$$
Apply speedup $S$ only to the enhanceable part:
$$
T_{new} = \frac{F \cdot T_{old}}{S} + (1 - F) \cdot T_{old} = T_{old} \cdot \left[(1 - F) + \frac{F}{S}\right]
$$
Speedup:
$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - F) + \dfrac{F}{S}}
$$

**Application (4 marks — 1 each):**

Given baseline: $T_{old} = 100$ s, $F_{\text{DB}} = 0.65$, $S_{\text{DB}} = 8$.

**(i) New execution time:**
$$
T_{new} = 100 \cdot \left[(1 - 0.65) + \frac{0.65}{8}\right] = 100 \cdot [0.35 + 0.08125] = 43.125 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Overall speedup:**
$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{100}{43.125} = 2.318\times
$$

**(iii) Maximum speedup (S → ∞):**
$$
\text{Speedup}_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.65} = \frac{1}{0.35} = 2.857\times
$$

**(iv) Additional speedup if HTTP parsing is also parallelised 4×:**
Now we apply Amdahl twice (compositional form). Let $F_1 = 0.65, S_1 = 8$ and $F_2 = 0.20, S_2 = 4$. The unenhanceable fraction is $1 - 0.65 - 0.20 = 0.15$.

$$
T_{new} = 100 \cdot \left[0.15 + \frac{0.65}{8} + \frac{0.20}{4}\right]
$$
$$
T_{new} = 100 \cdot [0.15 + 0.08125 + 0.05] = 100 \cdot 0.28125 = 28.125 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{combined}} = \frac{100}{28.125} = 3.556\times
$$

So the **additional** gain from the HTTP-parsing optimisation is $3.556 - 2.318 = 1.238\times$.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q8b, 14 marks]**

*(a)* With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a modern system-on-chip (SoC)** and explain how it integrates features from all three classes of computers. Discuss why the smartphone is best classified as an *embedded* system even though it has *desktop-class* CPU performance. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A scientific workload runs in 50 s. 25% of it is **floating-point (FP)** code that can be vectorised to run 16× faster using SIMD. The remaining 75% is **integer/scalar** code that is unaffected. After vectorisation, the integer portion is profiled and 40% of *it* is found to be memory-bound; a cache optimisation speeds up that memory-bound portion by 5×. Calculate the **final** execution time and overall speedup. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks) — refer to Diagram 3 in Section 4.3:**

```
Application CPU Cluster (OoO, desktop-class) ◄──► NoC Fabric ◄──► GPU (SIMT, server-class) ◄──► NPU (embedded DSP-class) ◄──► PMU (DVFS, embedded-class) ◄──► DRAM (server-class ECC) ◄──► Peripherals
```

**Explanation of integration (2 marks):**
The **Application CPU** cluster (e.g., Cortex-A78 cores) is *desktop-class* in microarchitecture (OoO, 3-issue, deep pipeline). The **GPU** is *server-class* in throughput design (hundreds of shader cores). The **NPU/DSP** is *embedded-class* (ASIC, ultra-low power, fixed function). The **PMU** with DVFS is *embedded* design philosophy. The **DRAM controller** with ECC is *server-class* discipline.

**Why smartphone = embedded, not desktop (2 marks):**
Despite having OoO cores, the smartphone is classified as **embedded** because:
1. **Form factor constraint** — must fit in pocket, battery-fed, power ceiling of ~5 W SoC.
2. **Fixed-function role** — primary task is the same (calls, apps, media) — not user-replaceable computing.
3. **Thermal constraint** — no active cooling; the device throttles aggressively.
4. **Embedded design methodology** (DVFS, power gating, dark silicon) dominates the SoC design.
5. The CPU cores are *desktop-class in design* but are used in an *embedded-class energy envelope*.

### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Setup (1 mark):**
Baseline: $T_{old} = 50$ s.
Phase 1: Vectorise FP code: $F_1 = 0.25, S_1 = 16$.
After Phase 1, we then apply a cache fix to *part* of the remaining integer portion: $F_2 = 0.40 \times 0.75 = 0.30$ of the total program, $S_2 = 5$.

**Step 1 — Apply FP vectorisation (1 mark):**
$$
T_1 = 50 \cdot \left[(1 - 0.25) + \frac{0.25}{16}\right] = 50 \cdot [0.75 + 0.015625] = 38.281 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the new enhanceable fraction (1 mark):**
After Phase 1, the integer portion is 75% of *original*. Within that integer portion, 40% is memory-bound. So:
$$
F_2 = 0.40 \times 0.75 = 0.30 \text{ (of original baseline)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply cache optimisation to that fraction (1 mark):**
Using compositional Amdahl across both phases:
$$
T_{new} = 50 \cdot \left[(1 - 0.25 - 0.30) + \frac{0.25}{16} + \frac{0.30}{5}\right]
$$
$$
T_{new} = 50 \cdot \left[0.45 + 0.015625 + 0.06\right] = 50 \cdot 0.525625
$$
$$
T_{new} = 26.281 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Overall speedup (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{50}{26.281} = 1.902\times
$$

**Step 5 — Discussion (2 marks):**
- Despite the FP portion getting a *massive* 16× boost, the overall speedup is only ~1.9× because the FP portion was only 25% of the workload (Amdahl bound).
- The cache fix on a *larger* 30% fraction gives a *bigger* absolute speedup than the SIMD boost, illustrating the principle **"target the largest hot fraction first."**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "class" with "OS category"** — A Raspberry Pi running Linux is still an *embedded-class* system, not a server. Class is defined by **design constraints**, not by software.
> 2. **Forgetting the unenhanceable $(1-F)$ term** in Amdahl's Law — many students write $\text{Speedup} = S / F$ and lose 3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Mixing up IC, CPI, and clock period** in the CPU equation — remember: $\text{IC}$ is a *software* property, $\text{CPI}$ is a *microarchitecture* property, $T_{\text{clock}}$ is a *circuit* property.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation** in 14-mark Amdahl questions — examiners award 3 of the 7 marks *only* for the step-by-step derivation. Memorising the formula without showing $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{enh}} + T_{\text{unenh}}$ is fatal.
> 5. **Calling the smartphone a "desktop-class" system** — the *correct* KTU phrasing is "**embedded-class system with desktop-class CPU islands on a server-class fabric**". This level of nuance earns the top box.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Three classes of computers** = Desktop, Server, Embedded. They differ in **optimisation metric**: *response time* / *throughput + availability* / *cost + power + real-time*.
- ✅ **Desktop** = general purpose, single user, 3rd-party software, response-time optimised, OoO superscalar.
- ✅ **Server** = 24×7, network-attached, multi-user, ECC memory mandatory, throughput + availability optimised, many-core + coherence.
- ✅ **Embedded** = hidden, single-function, real-time deadlines, cost + power constrained, in-order MCU / ASIC / SoC.
- ✅ **Warehouse-Scale Computer (WSC)** = modern extreme of server class; thousands of commodity servers as one system.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law formula** = $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + F/S}$. **Maximum** speedup (S→∞) = $\dfrac{1}{1-F}$.
- ✅ **CPU Performance Equation** = $\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}}$.
- ✅ **Three quantitative principles** = Parallelism, Locality, Common-Case-Fast.
- ✅ **Dynamic power** = $\alpha \cdot C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ — explains why DVFS is the single most effective power-saving technique in embedded/mobile.
- ✅ **Compositional Amdahl** — when multiple optimisations are applied, the unenhanceable fraction is $(1 - F_1 - F_2 - \dots)$.
- ✅ **Modern SoCs blur the class lines** — the smartphone SoC is the canonical example: desktop-class CPUs, server-class memory fabric, embedded-class PMU.
- ✅ **Common viva questions** — *"Are smartphones servers?"*, *"Is a Raspberry Pi a desktop?"*, *"What is dark silicon?"* — answer all with reference to **class-defining constraints**, not raw specs.
- ✅ **Always show derivation steps** for Amdahl + CPU equation in 14-mark questions — they carry dedicated marks.
- ✅ **Use `\vert` not `\|`** in any LaTeX tables; KTU scripts may auto-render pipes as column separators and break your table.
- ✅ **Mention real-world examples** (Aurix ECU, Snapdragon SoC, AWS Graviton, ESP32) — examiners reward industrial context in 14-mark answers.

---

*End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Classes of Computers (PECST528 — KTU 2024 Scheme).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Computer Hardware and Organization (Patterson and Hennessy, 5th Edition) - Measuring Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Computer Hardware and Organization (Patterson and Hennessy, 5th Edition) - Measuring Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Measuring Performance** in computer architecture refers to the systematic quantitative evaluation of a computer system's capability to execute programs, expressed through metrics such as **execution time**, **throughput**, **clock rate**, **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)**, and **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)**. As per Patterson and Hennessy's framework, the fundamental performance metric is the **time to complete a task** (i.e., response time or latency), and every other measurement is a derivative of this core concept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Patterson & Hennessy, 5th Edition):**
> *"Performance is defined as the reciprocal of execution time. A computer X is n times faster than computer Y if the execution time of Y on a given task is n times longer than that of X on the same task."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine two delivery trucks completing a daily delivery run across a city:

- **Truck A (Old Design):** Takes **3 hours** to deliver 100 packages.
- **Truck B (New Design):** Takes **1.5 hours** to deliver the same 100 packages.

**Truck B is 2× faster** because its time is half that of Truck A. The *number of packages delivered* is the same, but **time** is what we measure.

In computing, two CPUs running the **same program** can be compared simply by **measuring how long each takes**. The one that finishes first is faster — regardless of how many instructions were issued, how high the clock speed is, or what the MIPS rating claims. The KTU syllabus emphasizes this single foundational idea: **performance means execution time, and nothing else.**

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Computer System Design

Patterson and Hennessy identify three competing goals that designers must balance:

| Pillar | Definition | Example |
|--------|------------|---------|
| **Performance** | Time to complete a task | Lower execution time = better |
| **Cost** | Money to design/build the system | Chip fabrication expense |
| **Power** | Energy consumed during operation | Watts dissipated by CPU |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Iron Triangle):** Improving one pillar (e.g., performance) often increases another (e.g., cost or power). For example, raising clock frequency increases performance but also raises power dissipation quadratically.

## 1.4 Two Distinct Views of Performance

- **Response Time (Latency):** Total time between task submission and completion.
- **Throughput (Bandwidth):** Number of tasks completed per unit time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** When a system is upgraded, both metrics often improve together, but they are **not** identical. A pipelined processor increases throughput without necessarily reducing the response time of a single instruction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental CPU Performance Equation

The **CPU Execution Time** is the foundation of all performance analysis. Patterson and Hennessy derive it from three independent factors:

### Logical Breakdown (Why & How)

1. **Instruction Count (IC):** Determined by the **algorithm**, **programming language**, **compiler**, and **ISA**. It is the dynamic number of instructions executed to complete a program.
2. **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** The average number of clock cycles each instruction consumes. It is determined by the **ISA** and the **microarchitecture** (pipelining, cache, etc.).
3. **Clock Cycle Time (T):** The duration of one clock pulse, determined by the **hardware technology** and **CPU organization**.

By combining these, the **CPU time** for running a program is:

$$
\text{CPU Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI} \times \text{Clock Cycle Time}
$$

Equivalently, in terms of clock rate $f = 1/T$:

$$
\text{CPU Time} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}}{f}
$$

### Why This Equation Matters

Each factor belongs to a **different layer** of the system:

| Factor | Determined By | Designer Has Control? |
|--------|---------------|----------------------|
| Instruction Count | Algorithm, Language, Compiler, ISA | ✅ Yes (multiple layers) |
| CPI | ISA + Microarchitecture | ✅ Yes (CPU design) |
| Clock Cycle Time | Hardware technology, organization | ✅ Yes (process node) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Point:** To optimize performance, you must attack *all three* factors. Optimizing only one (e.g., raising clock rate) hits physical and economic limits — hence the modern shift toward **multi-core** and **architectural parallelism**.

## 2.2 Clock Rate and Cycle Time

The **clock** synchronizes all CPU operations. The clock period is the reciprocal of clock rate:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} \quad \text{and} \quad f = \frac{1}{T}
$$

- $T$ is measured in **seconds (s)**, **nanoseconds (ns)**, or **picoseconds (ps)**.
- $f$ is measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, commonly **GHz** ($10^9$ Hz).
- A 4 GHz processor has $T = 0.25$ ns.

## 2.3 Performance Comparison and Speedup

**Speedup** is the ratio of execution times, comparing two systems running the same workload:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{\text{Performance}_X}{\text{Performance}_Y} = \frac{\text{Execution Time}_Y}{\text{Execution Time}_X}
$$

> If Speedup = 3, then X is 3 times faster than Y.

## 2.4 Amdahl's Law — The Law of Diminishing Returns

When a portion of a system is improved, the **overall speedup** is limited by the **unimproved portion**:

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{n}}
$$

Where:
- $f$ = fraction of execution time affected by the enhancement ($0 \le f \le 1$).
- $n$ = speedup of the enhanced portion.
- $(1 - f)$ = fraction that remains unchanged.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Amdahl's Law is used in:
- **Multicore CPU design** (parallel speedup limits).
- **GPU computing** (kernel optimization).
- **Database query optimization** (index speedup).
- **Network protocol design** (compression speedup).

## 2.5 MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)

A simple but **flawed** performance metric:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^6} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** MIPS depends on the instruction set. A program on a RISC machine may have a high MIPS but still be slower than a CISC machine. Always prefer **execution time** over MIPS for comparisons.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|--------------|
| 1 | $\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T$ | IC=Instruction Count, T=Cycle Time | Seconds |
| 2 | $\text{CPU Time} = \dfrac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{f}$ | $f$ = Clock Rate | Seconds |
| 3 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Cycle time, frequency | s, Hz |
| 4 | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{new}}}{P_{\text{old}}}$ | Performance ratio | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Speedup}_{\text{Amdahl}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}$ | Amdahl's Law | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{\text{IC}}{T_{\text{exec}} \times 10^{6}}$ | Instruction throughput | Millions/sec |
| 7 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$ | Alternative form | Millions/sec |
| 8 | $\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{IC}_i \times \text{CPI}_i) \, / \, \text{IC}_{\text{total}}$ | Weighted average CPI | Cycles/instruction |
| 9 | $\text{MFLOPS} = \dfrac{\text{FP operations}}{T_{\text{exec}} \times 10^{6}}$ | Floating-point rate | Millions/sec |
| 10 | $\text{SPEC Score} = \sqrt[n]{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{Ratio}_i}$ | Geometric mean of ratios | Reference $n$ benchmarks |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CPU Performance Equation

The derivation proceeds from first principles of CPU operation. Each CPU instruction requires a certain number of clock cycles, and each clock cycle has a fixed time duration.

### Step 1: Total Clock Cycles Required

For each instruction $i$, the number of clock cycles needed is $\text{CPI}_i$. The total cycles for the entire program is the sum over all instructions:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IC}_i \times \text{CPI}_i
$$

**Textual Logic:** Each instruction class $i$ is executed $\text{IC}_i$ times, with each instance consuming $\text{CPI}_i$ cycles. Summing across all classes gives the total cycles.

### Step 2: Average CPI

Dividing the total cycles by the total instruction count yields the **weighted average CPI**:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IC}_i \times \text{CPI}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IC}_i} = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{\text{IC}_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The average is weighted by how often each instruction class appears in the program.

### Step 3: Total Execution Time

Since every cycle has the same duration $T$, the total execution time is the product of total cycles and cycle duration:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = C_{\text{total}} \times T_{\text{cycle}}
$$

**Textual Logic:** If $C$ cycles are executed and each lasts $T$ seconds, the wall-clock time is $C \times T$.

### Step 4: Substitution

Substituting $C_{\text{total}} = \text{IC}_{\text{total}} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}$ into Step 3:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \text{IC}_{\text{total}} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \times T_{\text{cycle}}
$$

This is the **master performance equation** used in all KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

**Problem:** A program has $1.0 \times 10^9$ instructions, split as follows:

- 40% ALU instructions with CPI = 1
- 30% Load/Store with CPI = 2
- 20% Branches with CPI = 3
- 10% Floating-point with CPI = 4

The clock rate is **2 GHz**. Find the CPU execution time.

### Step 1: Compute Instruction Counts

$$
\text{IC}_{\text{ALU}} = 0.40 \times 10^9 = 4.0 \times 10^8
$$

$$
\text{IC}_{\text{LS}} = 0.30 \times 10^9 = 3.0 \times 10^8
$$

$$
\text{IC}_{\text{BR}} = 0.20 \times 10^9 = 2.0 \times 10^8
$$

$$
\text{IC}_{\text{FP}} = 0.10 \times 10^9 = 1.0 \times 10^8
$$

**Textual Logic:** The program consists of $10^9$ total instructions, distributed across four categories according to the given percentages.

### Step 2: Compute Total Cycles

$$
C_{\text{total}} = (4.0 \times 10^8)(1) + (3.0 \times 10^8)(2) + (2.0 \times 10^8)(3) + (1.0 \times 10^8)(4)
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 4.0 \times 10^8 + 6.0 \times 10^8 + 6.0 \times 10^8 + 4.0 \times 10^8
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 2.0 \times 10^9 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Multiply each instruction class count by its CPI, then sum all contributions to obtain the total number of clock cycles the program requires.

### Step 3: Compute Average CPI

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^9}{1.0 \times 10^9} = 2.0 \text{ cycles/instruction}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Total cycles divided by total instructions yields the weighted average cycles per instruction.

### Step 4: Compute Cycle Time

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.5 \text{ ns} = 5.0 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The clock period is the reciprocal of the clock frequency. A 2 GHz signal has a half-nanosecond period.

### Step 5: Compute CPU Time

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \times T_{\text{cycle}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = (1.0 \times 10^9)(2.0)(5.0 \times 10^{-10})
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 1.0 \text{ second}
$$

**Final Answer:** The program takes **1.0 second** to execute on this processor.

## 3.3 Amdahl's Law Worked Example

**Problem:** A program spends 30% of its time in a function that can be optimized to run 5× faster. What is the overall speedup?

### Step 1: Identify Parameters

- $f = 0.30$ (fraction that is optimized)
- $n = 5$ (local speedup of that fraction)
- $(1 - f) = 0.70$ (unimproved fraction)

### Step 2: Apply Amdahl's Law

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.30) + \frac{0.30}{5}}
$$

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.06} = \frac{1}{0.76}
$$

$$
\text{Speedup} \approx 1.316
$$

**Textual Logic:** Even though the function itself becomes 5× faster, the other 70% of the program is unchanged, so the overall speedup is only about 31.6%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** As $n \to \infty$, speedup is bounded by $1/(1-f)$. To get large overall speedups, you must optimize **most** of the program.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Amdahl's Law Limits

The table below shows overall speedup for various $f$ and $n$ values (a frequent KTU exam topic):

| Improved Fraction $f$ | Local Speedup $n$ | Overall Speedup |
|----------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 0.50 | 2 | 1.33 |
| 0.50 | 10 | 1.82 |
| 0.50 | $\infty$ | 2.00 |
| 0.90 | 2 | 1.82 |
| 0.90 | 10 | 5.26 |
| 0.90 | $\infty$ | 10.00 |
| 0.95 | 2 | 1.90 |
| 0.95 | $\infty$ | 20.00 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Diminishing returns are evident. Doubling $n$ does not double speedup unless $f$ is already close to 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPU Performance Equation — Functional Flow

The diagram below decomposes the master performance equation into its three independent factors and traces their origin to different layers of the system stack.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program to Execute] --> B[Compiler and ISA]
    B --> C[Instruction Count IC]
    D[Microarchitecture and Pipeline Design] --> E[CPI Cycles Per Instruction]
    F[Hardware Technology and Organization] --> G[Clock Cycle Time T]
    C --> H[CPU Execution Time]
    E --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Performance is the reciprocal of CPU Time]
    C --> J[Algorithmic and Software Layer]
    E --> K[CPU Design Layer]
    G --> L[Process and Fabrication Layer]
    H --> M[Trade off Triangle: Performance vs Cost vs Power]
```

## 4.2 Amdahl's Law — Bottleneck Visualization

This block diagram shows how the improved and unimproved portions of a workload combine, with the unimproved portion acting as a hard ceiling on the overall speedup.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Execution Time 100 percent] --> B[Unimproved Fraction 1 minus f]
    A --> C[Improved Fraction f]
    C --> D[Local Speedup factor n]
    B --> E[Final Speedup Output]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Speedup bounded by 1 over 1 minus f}
    F --> G[As n approaches infinity]
    G --> H[Upper Limit equals 1 over 1 minus f]
```

## 4.3 Benchmarking Hierarchy — Block Architecture

Modern performance evaluation uses a layered benchmark suite. Each layer aggregates results from the layer below using a statistical mean (typically geometric mean for normalized ratios).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Individual Benchmark Program] --> B[SPEC Reference Time]
    B --> C[Runtime Ratio per Benchmark]
    C --> D[SPECint or SPECfp Suite]
    D --> E[Geometric Mean Aggregation]
    E --> F[Final SPEC Score]
    G[Compiler Optimization Flags] --> A
    H[System Configuration] --> A
    I[Workload Characteristics] --> D
```

## 4.4 Multi-Layer Performance Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Performance Bottleneck Identified] --> B{Bottleneck Location}
    B --> C1[High Instruction Count]
    B --> C2[High CPI]
    B --> C3[High Clock Cycle Time]
    C1 --> D1[Improve Algorithm]
    C1 --> D2[Optimize Compiler]
    C1 --> D3[Use RISC ISA]
    C2 --> E1[Pipeline the CPU]
    C2 --> E2[Add Cache Memory]
    C2 --> E3[Branch Prediction]
    C3 --> F1[Reduce Process Node Size]
    C3 --> F2[Lower Voltage and Frequency]
    C3 --> F3[Improve Circuit Design]
    D1 --> G[Improved Performance]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define CPU execution time. Why is it considered the most reliable measure of performance compared to MIPS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
CPU execution time is the total time a processor takes to complete a given program, measured in seconds. It is the most reliable performance measure because it reflects the actual user-perceived speed of the system. Unlike MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second), CPU time is independent of the instruction set architecture — a RISC processor may show a high MIPS but still run slower than a CISC processor if its execution time is larger. Hence, Patterson and Hennessy state that execution time is the **only consistent and unambiguous metric** for performance comparison. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**State Amdahl's Law. What is the maximum possible speedup if 75% of a program can be optimized?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Amdahl's Law states that the overall speedup obtained by improving one portion of a system is limited by the fraction that remains unimproved:

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{n}}
$$

Given $f = 0.75$, the maximum speedup occurs as $n \to \infty$:

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.75} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4
$$

Hence, the overall performance can be improved by **at most 4 times**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the CPU performance equation. Explain each of the three factors that determine CPU execution time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A processor runs at 3 GHz. A program consists of 8 classes of instructions with the following CPI distribution:

| Class | IC | CPI |
|-------|-----|-----|
| 1 | $1.0 \times 10^6$ | 1 |
| 2 | $3.0 \times 10^6$ | 2 |
| 3 | $5.0 \times 10^6$ | 3 |
| 4 | $2.0 \times 10^6$ | 4 |
| 5 | $4.0 \times 10^6$ | 5 |
| 6 | $6.0 \times 10^6$ | 2 |
| 7 | $2.0 \times 10^6$ | 1 |
| 8 | $1.0 \times 10^6$ | 6 |

Compute the average CPI, MIPS rating, and total CPU execution time. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

The CPU performance equation is derived from three observable quantities:

**Step 1:** Total cycles $C = \sum_i \text{IC}_i \times \text{CPI}_i$.

**Step 2:** Cycle time $T = 1/f$.

**Step 3:** Therefore,

$$
\text{CPU Time} = C \times T = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \times T
$$

**The Three Factors:**
1. **Instruction Count (IC):** Depends on algorithm, language, compiler, ISA.
2. **CPI:** Depends on ISA design and microarchitecture (pipelining, caches).
3. **Clock Cycle Time:** Depends on hardware technology and organization. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Total Instruction Count**

$$
\text{IC} = (1 + 3 + 5 + 2 + 4 + 6 + 2 + 1) \times 10^6 = 24 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 2: Total Cycles**

$$
C = (1)(1) + (3)(2) + (5)(3) + (2)(4) + (4)(5) + (6)(2) + (2)(1) + (1)(6)
$$

$$
C = 1 + 6 + 15 + 8 + 20 + 12 + 2 + 6 = 70 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3: Average CPI**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{70 \times 10^6}{24 \times 10^6} = 2.917
$$

**Step 4: CPU Execution Time**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^9} = 3.33 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 24 \times 10^6 \times 2.917 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-10} = 0.0233 \text{ s} \approx 23.3 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5: MIPS Rating**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{IC}}{T_{\text{exec}} \times 10^6} = \frac{24 \times 10^6}{0.0233 \times 10^6} \approx 1029.6 \text{ MIPS}
$$

Alternatively,

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{2.917 \times 10^6} \approx 1028.6 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Alternative Set]

**(a)** Explain Amdahl's Law with a suitable formula. A system spends 60% of its time in I/O operations that can be made 10 times faster. What is the overall speedup achieved? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between response time and throughput. A server completes 500 transactions in 10 seconds. If the response time per transaction is 25 ms, what is the average number of concurrent transactions being processed? **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Amdahl's Law Formula:**

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{n}}
$$

**Given:** $f = 0.60$ (I/O fraction), $n = 10$ (I/O speedup).

**Step 1: Substitute into formula**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.60) + \frac{0.60}{10}}
$$

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{0.40 + 0.06} = \frac{1}{0.46} \approx 2.174
$$

**Step 2: Interpretation**

The overall system speedup is approximately **2.17×**, even though the I/O subsystem is 10× faster. The remaining 40% (CPU computation) limits the gain.

> **Note:** If the I/O were infinitely fast ($n \to \infty$):

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\max} = \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.5
$$

**(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Definitions:**
- **Response Time (Latency):** Time taken to complete **one** task from start to finish.
- **Throughput:** Number of tasks completed **per unit time**.

**Step 1: Compute Server Throughput**

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{500 \text{ transactions}}{10 \text{ s}} = 50 \text{ transactions/second}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Average Response Time**

$$
T_{\text{response}} = 25 \text{ ms} = 0.025 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Little's Law (Concurrency Formula)**

$$
\text{Concurrency} = \text{Throughput} \times \text{Response Time}
$$

$$
\text{Concurrency} = 50 \times 0.025 = 1.25
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

The server is processing an average of **1.25 transactions concurrently** at any instant. This is consistent with a lightly loaded system where most transactions complete before the next arrives. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Measuring Performance Questions:**
> 1. **Using MIPS instead of execution time** for comparison — this loses 2–3 marks instantly. KTU expects **CPU time** as the gold standard.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert GHz to Hz** in cycle-time calculations. Always write $3 \text{ GHz} = 3 \times 10^9$ Hz.
> 3. **Confusing weighted average CPI with simple average.** CPI must be weighted by instruction count, not by CPI alone.
> 4. **Misapplying Amdahl's Law** — students often forget the $(1-f)$ denominator term. Always show full substitution.
> 5. **Mixing up throughput and response time** — they are NOT the same and cannot be used interchangeably.
> 6. **Ignoring the SPEC benchmark geometric mean** — KTU sometimes asks why geometric mean is used (it correctly handles ratios).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Performance = 1 / Execution Time.** This is the only true definition.
- ✅ **CPU Time = IC × CPI × Cycle Time.** Master this equation.
- ✅ **Three layers affect performance:** Algorithm/Compiler, ISA/Microarchitecture, Hardware/Process.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law limits speedup** by the unimproved portion; maximum speedup = $1/(1-f)$.
- ✅ **MIPS is flawed** because it depends on the instruction set.
- ✅ **MFLOPS is used for floating-point** intensive scientific workloads.
- ✅ **SPEC benchmarks** use geometric mean to aggregate normalized ratios.
- ✅ **Speedup = Old Time / New Time** (note the direction).
- ✅ **Cycle Time = 1 / Frequency**; convert GHz to Hz carefully.
- ✅ **Throughput vs Response Time** — both are valid metrics but measure different things.
- ✅ **CPI avg must be weighted** by instruction count, not just averaged.
- ✅ **Improving the algorithm** is often the cheapest, fastest performance win.
- ✅ **Diminishing returns** — Amdahl's Law shows that 90% of a system optimized 100× still gives only ~10× overall gain.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reporting and Summarizing Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reporting and Summarizing Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Performance in Computer Architecture** is formally defined as the inverse of the time taken to execute a given workload. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, "Reporting and Summarizing Performance" is the structured framework used by architects to *measure*, *quantify*, *compare*, and *report* the execution efficiency of computer systems using standardized metrics and mathematical models.

The two fundamental pillars of performance measurement are:

- **Response Time (Execution Time / Latency):** The total time elapsed between the start and completion of a task. *Lower is better.*
- **Throughput (Bandwidth):** The number of tasks (or work units) completed per unit of time. *Higher is better.*

### 1.2 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **mandatory board-exam vocabulary** under KTU 2024 Scheme:
>
> 1. **CPU Execution Time** — measured in seconds (real wall-clock time or simulated).
> 2. **Clock Cycle Time (T<sub>clock</sub>)** — duration of one clock tick, measured in nanoseconds (ns) or picoseconds (ps).
> 3. **Clock Rate (f)** — frequency, in Hertz (Hz), where **f = 1 / T<sub>clock</sub>**.
> 4. **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** — average clock cycles consumed per instruction.
> 5. **IC (Instruction Count)** — total number of instructions executed for a given program.
> 6. **MIPS** — Million Instructions Per Second.
> 7. **MFLOPS** — Million Floating-Point Operations Per Second.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine two chefs preparing the same 10 dishes in a restaurant.

- **Response Time** = How long **Chef A** takes to deliver your single plate. If Chef A is slower, your meal arrives late.
- **Throughput** = How many total plates leave the kitchen per hour. A faster microwave (higher throughput) might still leave a single customer waiting if prep takes time.
- **CPU** = The chef. **Memory** = The pantry. **I/O** = The waiter.

To *report* performance fairly, you cannot just say "Chef A cooked 50 dishes/hour" — because Chef B might cook faster *per dish*. This is exactly why computer architects use **CPU Execution Time** as the canonical metric: *time is the only fully reliable measure of performance*.

### 1.4 The Reporting Problem

When comparing machines **M1** and **M2** running benchmark programs **P1, P2, …, Pn**, there is no single number that fully captures performance. The KTU syllabus expects students to understand:
- **Why arithmetic means lie.**
- **Why geometric means are statistically correct** for normalized benchmarks.
- **Why Amdahl's Law** governs the realistic maximum speedup of any enhancement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CPU Performance Equation (The "Iron Triangle")

The most important equation in computer architecture:

$$\text{CPU Execution Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI} \times \text{Clock Cycle Time}$$

$$T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock} = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f}$$

Where:
- **IC** = Instruction Count (architectural/program dependent).
- **CPI** = Cycles Per Instruction (organization/microarchitecture dependent).
- **T<sub>clock</sub>** = Clock cycle time (technology/HW dependent).

> [!NOTE]
> This single equation is the **foundation of Module 1** — it ties together all three levers an architect can pull: **ISA design (IC)**, **microarchitecture (CPI)**, and **VLSI technology (T<sub>clock</sub>)**.

### 2.2 Amdahl's Law (The Architect's Reality Check)

When you enhance a fraction **f** of a system by a factor of **s**, the overall speedup **S** is:

$$S_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}}$$

**The "Why":** No matter how fast you make the improved portion, the unimproved portion **1 − f** becomes a hard ceiling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Cases (Board-Exam Favorite):**
> - If **s → ∞** (infinite enhancement): $S_{max} = \frac{1}{1-f}$ — *this is the asymptotic ceiling.*
> - If **f = 1** (whole system improved): $S = s$ — *perfect speedup.*
> - If **f = 0** (no improvement possible): $S = 1$ — *no gain.*

### 2.3 MIPS, MFLOPS & Derived Metrics

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{IC}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\text{MFLOPS} = \frac{\text{Number of FP Operations}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\text{Speedup of M}_2 \text{ over M}_1 = \frac{T_{M1}}{T_{M2}} = \frac{\text{Performance}_{M2}}{\text{Performance}_{M1}}$$

### 2.4 Summarizing Performance Across Multiple Benchmarks

Given **n** benchmarks with execution times **T1, T2, …, Tn** on a reference machine (with times **T1<sub>ref</sub>, …, Tn<sub>ref</sub>**), we compute per-program speedup ratios:

$$r_i = \frac{T_{i,ref}}{T_i}$$

The two accepted summary methods (KTU board-exam relevant):

| **Method** | **Formula** | **When Used / Properties** |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic Mean of Speedups | $\bar{r}_{arith} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i$ | Easy but **misleading** — biased toward fast machines. |
| Geometric Mean of Speedups | $\bar{r}_{geom} = \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} r_i\right)^{1/n}$ | **Statistically correct** — invariant under reference choice. |
| Total Execution Time Method | $T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | Most accurate workload-weighted summary. |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Style)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Execution Time | $T = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | seconds |
| 2 | Clock Rate | $f = \frac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Cycles per Instruction | $CPI = \frac{T \times f}{IC}$ | cycles/instr |
| 4 | MIPS | $MIPS = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | million instr/s |
| 5 | MFLOPS | $MFLOPS = \frac{FP_{ops}}{T \times 10^{6}}$ | million FP ops/s |
| 6 | Amdahl's Speedup | $S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{s}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Max Speedup (s→∞) | $S_{max} = \frac{1}{1-f}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Geometric Mean Speedup | $G = \left(\prod r_i\right)^{1/n}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Speedup of M2 over M1 | $S_{M2/M1} = \frac{T_{M1}}{T_{M2}}$ | dimensionless |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Processor Vendors (Intel, AMD, ARM)** use these formulas to advertise performance — and courts have ruled that **MIPS alone is misleading** (it ignores instruction complexity and memory effects).
- **Cloud Providers (AWS, Azure)** bill customers using execution-time-based pricing, making the CPU time equation a literal billing formula.
- **HPC (High Performance Computing)** clusters report performance via **MFLOPS** for scientific workloads (LINPACK, SPECfp).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the CPU Time Equation from First Principles

We start from the definition of **CPU Execution Time** as the time spent by the processor actively working on a program.

**Step 1 — Express time as a product of cycles and cycle duration.**

A CPU executes one instruction per clock cycle (in the simplest case). Total clock cycles for a program = Instruction Count × CPI. Each clock cycle lasts T<sub>clk</sub> seconds.

$$T_{CPU} = (\text{Total Clock Cycles}) \times T_{clk}$$

**Step 2 — Replace Total Clock Cycles with IC × CPI.**

$$\text{Total Clock Cycles} = IC \times CPI$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back.**

$$T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$$

**Step 4 — Convert T<sub>clk</sub> to frequency f.**

Since $f = \frac{1}{T_{clk}}$, we can equivalently write:

$$T_{CPU} = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f}$$

This is the **final form** — boxed and memorized by every KTU student.

> **Conversion Logic Recap:** Cycles = (Instructions) × (Cycles/Instruction); Time = Cycles × (Seconds/Cycle). Multiply units: instr × (cycle/instr) × (s/cycle) = seconds. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Amdahl's Law

**Scenario:** A system has a fraction **f** of its execution time spent on an *enhanceable* component. The remaining fraction **(1 − f)** is *unenhanceable*.

**Step 1 — Original execution time (assume T = 1 for normalized analysis).**

$$T_{original} = (1 - f) \cdot T + f \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Apply enhancement of factor s to the enhanceable portion.**

The enhanceable portion's time reduces from $f \cdot T$ to $\frac{f \cdot T}{s}$.

$$T_{new} = (1 - f) \cdot T + \frac{f \cdot T}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute speedup S = T<sub>original</sub> / T<sub>new</sub>.**

$$S = \frac{T}{(1 - f) \cdot T + \frac{f \cdot T}{s}}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel T (the assumption T = 1 yields the canonical form).**

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}}$$

**Step 5 — Limiting case as s → ∞.**

As s becomes very large, $\frac{f}{s} \to 0$, so:

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - f}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Even with an infinitely fast enhancement, the *unenhanced* 1 − f portion of the work still has to run. If only 10% (f = 0.1) is enhanced, max speedup is only $1/0.9 = 1.11\times$ — *never more than 11% improvement*, regardless of hardware.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Multi-Class CPI Calculation

**Problem (KTU style):** A processor runs three instruction classes:

| **Class** | **CPI** | **Instruction Mix (IC%)** |
|---|---|---|
| A (Arithmetic) | 1 | 40% |
| B (Memory Load) | 2 | 30% |
| C (Branch) | 3 | 30% |

Clock rate = **2 GHz**. Compute **overall CPI**, **MIPS**, and **CPU time** for a program of **1 billion (10⁹) instructions**.

**Step 1 — Compute overall CPI using weighted average.**

$$CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i} (CPI_i \times IC_{fraction_i})$$

$$CPI_{avg} = (1 \times 0.40) + (2 \times 0.30) + (3 \times 0.30)$$

$$CPI_{avg} = 0.40 + 0.60 + 0.90 = 1.90$$

**Step 2 — Compute MIPS.**

$$MIPS = \frac{f}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{9}}{1.90 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$MIPS = \frac{2 \times 10^{3}}{1.90} = 1052.63 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 3 — Compute CPU time.**

$$T = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f} = \frac{10^{9} \times 1.90}{2 \times 10^{9}} = 0.95 \text{ seconds}$$

> **Validation:** T = IC / (MIPS × 10⁶) = 10⁹ / (1052.63 × 10⁶) ≈ 0.95 s ✓ (matches).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Amdahl's Law in Practice

**Problem:** A CPU spends **30%** of its time in floating-point (FP) operations. We redesign the FP unit to run **5× faster**. What is the overall speedup?

**Step 1 — Identify f and s.**
- f = 0.30 (fraction enhanced)
- s = 5 (enhancement factor)

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law.**

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.30) + \frac{0.30}{5}} = \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.06} = \frac{1}{0.76}$$

$$S \approx 1.316$$

> **Insight:** A 5× FP speedup yields only **31.6%** total speedup. To double overall performance, the FP unit alone cannot do it — the architect must attack the remaining 70%.

**Step 3 — Maximum possible speedup if FP were infinitely fast.**

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.30} = \frac{1}{0.70} \approx 1.428$$

So no matter what we do to the FP unit, *we can never exceed 1.428×*.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Geometric Mean Summarization

**Problem:** Machine M2 vs reference M1 on 4 benchmarks:

| **Benchmark** | T<sub>ref</sub> (s) | T<sub>M2</sub> (s) | r<sub>i</sub> = T<sub>ref</sub>/T<sub>M2</sub> |
|---|---|---|---|
| gcc | 100 | 80 | 1.25 |
| parser | 200 | 150 | 1.33 |
| vortex | 300 | 250 | 1.20 |
| bzip2 | 400 | 350 | 1.143 |

**Step 1 — Compute Arithmetic Mean (the wrong way).**

$$\bar{r}_{arith} = \frac{1.25 + 1.33 + 1.20 + 1.143}{4} = \frac{4.923}{4} = 1.231$$

**Step 2 — Compute Geometric Mean (the right way).**

$$\bar{r}_{geom} = (1.25 \times 1.33 \times 1.20 \times 1.143)^{1/4}$$

Compute the product:

$$1.25 \times 1.33 = 1.6625$$
$$1.6625 \times 1.20 = 1.995$$
$$1.995 \times 1.143 = 2.280$$

Take the fourth root:

$$\bar{r}_{geom} = (2.280)^{0.25} \approx 1.229$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**

$$\bar{r}_{geom} \approx 1.229 < \bar{r}_{arith} = 1.231$$

> **Why the difference?** Geometric mean **penalizes any benchmark with a slowdown** (r<sub>i</sub> < 1) more heavily, providing a more honest summary. It is also **invariant under the choice of reference machine** — a property the arithmetic mean does not satisfy.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Summary Method

| **Situation** | **Recommended Summary** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Single program comparison | Direct execution time | Simplest, most accurate |
| Multi-program with equal workload | Arithmetic mean of times (with caution) | Equal weights |
| SPEC-style benchmark reporting | **Geometric mean of speedup ratios** | Industry standard, statistically robust |
| Weighted workload (e.g., 70% web, 30% DB) | Weighted sum of execution times | Reflects real usage mix |
| High-throughput server | Total execution time for task set | Captures throughput |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Performance Reporting Pipeline

The diagram below maps the logical flow an architect follows when **reporting and summarizing** system performance — from raw execution data to final published metric.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Benchmark Suite"] --> B["Run Program Pi on Machine Mj"]
    B --> C["Record Raw Execution Time Tij"]
    C --> D{"Single Program?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Report Direct Time or MIPS or MFLOPS"]
    D -- No --> F["Compute Speedup Ratio ri = Tref divided by Tij"]
    F --> G["Apply Summarization Method"]
    G --> H{"Method Selection"}
    H -- I1 --> I["Arithmetic Mean of Times"]
    H -- I2 --> J["Geometric Mean of Speedup Ratios"]
    H -- I3 --> K["Weighted Total Execution Time"]
    I --> L["Publish Reported Performance"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    E --> L
    L --> M["End Report"]
```

### 4.2 Amdahl's Law — Architecture Flow View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Execution Time T"] --> B["Fraction f - Enhanceable"]
    A --> C["Fraction 1 minus f - Unenhanceable"]
    B --> D["Apply Enhancement Factor s"]
    D --> E["New Enhanceable Time fT divided by s"]
    C --> F["New Unenhanceable Time 1 minus f times T"]
    E --> G["Compute Overall Speedup S"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"s approaches infinity?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Asymptotic Speedup 1 divided by 1 minus f"]
    H -- No --> J["General Speedup 1 divided by 1 minus f plus f divided by s"]
```

### 4.3 CPU Time Decomposition (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU Execution Time T"] --> B["Instruction Count IC"]
    A --> C["CPI Cycles Per Instruction"]
    A --> D["Clock Cycle Time Tclk"]
    B --> E["Driven by ISA and Compiler"]
    C --> F["Driven by Microarchitecture and Memory"]
    D --> G["Driven by VLSI Technology"]
    E --> H["Optimization Lever 1 - Reduce IC"]
    F --> I["Optimization Lever 2 - Reduce CPI"]
    G --> J["Optimization Lever 3 - Reduce Tclk"]
    H --> K["Faster Program Execution"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.4 Performance Metric Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Choose Reporting Metric"] --> B{"Program Type?"}
    B -- C1 --> C["CPU-bound Scientific"]
    C --> D["Use MFLOPS or Execution Time"]
    B -- C2 --> E["Integer General Purpose"]
    E --> F["Use Execution Time or MIPS with Caveat"]
    B -- C3 --> G["Embedded Real Time"]
    G --> H["Use Worst Case Execution Time"]
    B -- C4 --> I["Multiprogrammed Throughput"]
    I --> J["Use Jobs per Minute or Throughput"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the terms MIPS and MFLOPS. State one limitation of MIPS as a performance metric.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> - **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second):** A measure of processor speed defined as $\text{MIPS} = \frac{IC}{T \times 10^{6}} = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$, indicating how many millions of instructions the CPU executes per second.
> - **MFLOPS (Million Floating-Point Operations Per Second):** A measure specialized for floating-point intensive workloads, defined as $\text{MFLOPS} = \frac{\text{Number of FP operations}}{T \times 10^{6}}$.
> - **Limitation of MIPS:** MIPS does **not account for instruction complexity** — different ISAs require different numbers of instructions for the same task. A RISC machine may have higher MIPS but lower actual performance than a CISC machine running the same program. MIPS also ignores memory hierarchy effects.
>
> **[Mark Allocation Hint:** Definition 1 + 1, Limitation 1 = **3 marks**]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State Amdahl's Law. What is the maximum achievable speedup if 20% of a program's execution time can be enhanced?**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> Amdahl's Law states that the overall speedup $S$ obtained by enhancing a fraction $f$ of a system by a factor $s$ is:
>
> $$S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{s}}$$
>
> Given $f = 0.20$, the maximum speedup as $s \to \infty$ is:
>
> $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.20} = \frac{1}{0.80} = 1.25$$
>
> Hence, even with infinite enhancement of the 20% portion, the overall speedup cannot exceed **1.25×**.
>
> **[Mark Allocation Hint:** Amdahl's statement 1, Formula 1, Computation 1 = **3 marks**]**

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** A processor has a clock rate of **3 GHz**. The instruction mix and CPI values are:

| **Instruction Type** | **CPI** | **Frequency** |
|---|---|---|
| ALU | 1 | 40% |
| Load | 4 | 30% |
| Store | 3 | 15% |
| Branch | 2 | 15% |

For a program with **2 × 10⁹** instructions, calculate:
1. The average CPI of the processor.
2. The MIPS rating.
3. The total CPU execution time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Amdahl's Law** with its mathematical formulation. A system spends **40%** of its time in I/O operations. If we replace the I/O subsystem with one that is **10× faster**, what is the overall system speedup? What is the maximum possible speedup if I/O could be made infinitely fast? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute average CPI** *(3 marks)*

$$CPI_{avg} = \sum (CPI_i \times f_i)$$

$$CPI_{avg} = (1 \times 0.40) + (4 \times 0.30) + (3 \times 0.15) + (2 \times 0.15)$$

$$CPI_{avg} = 0.40 + 1.20 + 0.45 + 0.30 = 2.35$$

**Step 2 — Compute MIPS** *(2 marks)*

$$MIPS = \frac{f}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{9}}{2.35 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$MIPS = \frac{3000}{2.35} = 1276.6 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 3 — Compute CPU Time** *(2 marks)*
