$$T = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f} = \frac{2 \times 10^{9} \times 2.35}{3 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{4.7}{3} \approx 1.567 \text{ seconds}$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State Amdahl's Law** *(2 marks)*

When a fraction $f$ of a system is enhanced by a factor $s$, the overall speedup is:

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}}$$

The law emphasizes that *improving a frequently-used component yields greater overall benefit, but the unimproved portion caps the maximum achievable speedup.*

**Step 2 — Compute speedup with 10× I/O enhancement** *(3 marks)*

Given $f = 0.40$, $s = 10$:

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + \frac{0.40}{10}} = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.04} = \frac{1}{0.64}$$

$$S \approx 1.5625$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum speedup as s → ∞** *(2 marks)*

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.40} = \frac{1}{0.60} \approx 1.667$$

Even with infinite I/O speedup, the system cannot exceed **1.667×**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Two machines M1 and M2 are compared on four benchmarks. The reference times and measured times are:

| **Benchmark** | **T<sub>ref</sub> (s)** | **T<sub>M1</sub> (s)** | **T<sub>M2</sub> (s)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| B1 | 100 | 90 | 80 |
| B2 | 200 | 160 | 200 |
| B3 | 300 | 270 | 240 |
| B4 | 400 | 320 | 360 |

Compute:
1. The arithmetic mean of execution times for each machine.
2. The geometric mean of speedup ratios for each machine (using T<sub>ref</sub> as reference).
3. Which machine is faster based on the geometric mean, and why does this differ from the arithmetic mean conclusion? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a clear derivation, show that the **CPU execution time** equals $\frac{IC \times CPI}{f}$. Define each variable. If a processor's clock rate is increased from **2 GHz to 3 GHz** while IC and CPI remain unchanged, what is the percentage reduction in execution time? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Arithmetic Mean of Execution Times** *(2 marks)*

For M1:

$$\bar{T}_{M1} = \frac{90 + 160 + 270 + 320}{4} = \frac{840}{4} = 210 \text{ s}$$

For M2:

$$\bar{T}_{M2} = \frac{80 + 200 + 240 + 360}{4} = \frac{880}{4} = 220 \text{ s}$$

Arithmetic mean says **M1 is faster**.

**Step 2 — Compute speedup ratios for Geometric Mean** *(2 marks)*

For M1: $r_i = T_{ref} / T_{M1}$:
$$r = \left\{\frac{100}{90}, \frac{200}{160}, \frac{300}{270}, \frac{400}{320}\right\} = \{1.111, 1.250, 1.111, 1.250\}$$

Product: $1.111 \times 1.250 \times 1.111 \times 1.250 = 1.929$

$$G_{M1} = (1.929)^{1/4} \approx 1.178$$

For M2: $r_i = T_{ref} / T_{M2}$:
$$r = \left\{\frac{100}{80}, \frac{200}{200}, \frac{300}{240}, \frac{400}{360}\right\} = \{1.250, 1.000, 1.250, 1.111\}$$

Product: $1.250 \times 1.000 \times 1.250 \times 1.111 = 1.736$

$$G_{M2} = (1.736)^{1/4} \approx 1.147$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and Justification** *(3 marks)*

Geometric mean says **M1 is faster** (1.178 > 1.147), consistent with arithmetic mean. *However*, the two methods would disagree if M2 had any benchmark with $r_i < 1$. The geometric mean is **invariant under choice of reference machine** — if we replaced T<sub>ref</sub> with T<sub>M1</sub>, M2's geometric mean would scale identically, preserving the comparative ordering. Arithmetic mean of times does **not** have this property. Hence, **geometric mean is the statistically robust summary** mandated by SPEC and used in KTU-evaluated benchmark reports.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Derivation of CPU Time Formula** *(4 marks)*

The total number of clock cycles required to execute a program is the product of the instruction count and the average cycles per instruction:

$$\text{Total Clock Cycles} = IC \times CPI$$

Each clock cycle lasts T<sub>clk</sub> seconds, so total execution time is:

$$T = (\text{Total Cycles}) \times T_{clk} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$$

Since clock frequency $f = \frac{1}{T_{clk}}$, we substitute:

$$T = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f}$$

**Definitions:**
- **IC** = Total number of instructions executed by the program.
- **CPI** = Average cycles per instruction (depends on microarchitecture).
- **f** = Clock frequency (cycles per second).

**Step 2 — Compute percentage reduction in T** *(3 marks)*

At $f_1 = 2$ GHz: $T_1 = \frac{IC \times CPI}{2 \times 10^{9}}$

At $f_2 = 3$ GHz: $T_2 = \frac{IC \times CPI}{3 \times 10^{9}}$

Ratio:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{f_1}{f_2} = \frac{2}{3}$$

Percentage reduction:

$$\Delta T\% = \left(1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{2}{3}\right) \times 100\% \approx 33.33\%$$

> **Insight:** Increasing clock rate by 50% (from 2 GHz to 3 GHz) reduces execution time by **33.33%** — *a non-linear relationship* due to the inverse dependence on f.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Performance Reporting Questions:**
>
> 1. **Using arithmetic mean instead of geometric mean** for benchmark summaries. *Examiner deducts up to 2 marks and expects the student to justify the choice.*
> 2. **Confusing MIPS with MFLOPS** — MIPS is for integer/instruction throughput; MFLOPS is for floating-point operations only.
> 3. **Forgetting the units** in MIPS ($10^6$ factor). Always write $10^6$, not $10^9$.
> 4. **In Amdahl's Law problems**, students often confuse *f = fraction of TIME* with *f = fraction of CODE*. Always re-read the wording carefully.
> 5. **Omitting the clock-rate conversion** when computing CPU time. The formula $T = IC \times CPI / f$ requires $f$ in Hz, not GHz.
> 6. **Reporting speedup as a percentage** instead of a ratio — e.g., saying "speedup is 25%" when the answer should be "1.25× or 25% faster".

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **CPU Time** = $IC \times CPI \times T_{clk} = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f}$ — *the iron triangle.*
- ✅ **MIPS** = $\frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$ — *limited metric; ignores instruction complexity.*
- ✅ **MFLOPS** = floating-point ops per second / $10^6$ — *for scientific workloads.*
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{s}}$; maximum $S = \frac{1}{1-f}$ as $s \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Geometric mean** is the **only statistically valid** summary for benchmark sets (used by SPEC).
- ✅ **Arithmetic mean of execution times** can mislead when programs have very different weights.
- ✅ **Time is the only fully reliable metric** — MIPS, MFLOPS, and clock rate are proxies that can be misleading.
- ✅ **Real-world performance** = CPU time + memory stalls + I/O wait — never just CPU work alone.
- ✅ **Speedup formula:** $S_{A/B} = \frac{T_B}{T_A}$ — larger denominator means A is faster.
- ✅ **Always express f and s as decimals** (e.g., 0.4 not 40%) inside Amdahl's Law formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benchmarks - Desktop and Server
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Benchmarks - Desktop and Server

# 📘 Benchmarks — Desktop and Server

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Advanced Computer Architecture**, a **Benchmark** is a standardized program, suite of programs, or workload that is executed on a computer system to quantitatively measure, evaluate, and compare its **performance characteristics** under controlled, reproducible conditions. Benchmarks provide a common reference framework for assessing metrics such as execution time, throughput, power efficiency, and scalability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** A benchmark is a *standardized measuring instrument* used to obtain quantitative performance indices of a computer system. It abstracts real applications into representative workloads so that architectural improvements can be validated objectively.

Benchmarks are classified along three primary axes relevant to KTU Module 1:

| Axis | Desktop Category | Server Category |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Workload Nature** | Single-user, interactive, mixed compute + graphics | Multi-user, transactional, data-intensive |
| **Primary KPI** | Response time, latency, user-experience | Throughput, transactions/sec, queries/hour |
| **Reference Body** | **SPEC** (Standard Performance Evaluation Corporation) | **SPEC**, **TPC** (Transaction Processing Performance Council) |
| **Power Metric** | Optional (e.g., SPECpower on desktops) | Mandatory for green data centers (**SPECpower_ssj2008**) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Test" of Computers

Imagine two cars are claimed to be "fast." One is a sports car (the **desktop**), the other is a cargo truck (the **server**). You cannot judge both by the same race:

- A **desktop benchmark** is like a **drag-strip test** (0–100 km/h time) — it measures single-user speed, responsiveness, and snappy interactive feel.
- A **server benchmark** is like a **logistics stress test** — how many parcels can the truck deliver per hour under heavy load, and how much fuel does it consume?

Without a benchmark, vendors would simply quote the metric that flatters their product (clock speed, core count, etc.). Benchmarks force everyone to "drive the same test track."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common 3-mark question asks *"Why can't a single benchmark suite evaluate both desktops and servers?"* The answer lies in this fundamental divergence of workload character — single-thread latency vs. multi-threaded throughput.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **SPEC CPU 2017** reference machine baseline score = **1.00** (normalized).
- **SPECpower_ssj2008** performance metric = **`ssj_ops`** (server-side Java operations per second).
- **TPC-C** throughput metric = **`tpmC`** (transactions per minute, C benchmark).
- **TPC-H** throughput metric = **`QphH@Size`** (queries per hour at a given database size).
- **Clock frequency** unit: **GHz (10⁹ Hz)**.
- **MIPS** = **M**illion **I**nstructions **P**er **S**econd.
- **MFLOPS / GFLOPS / TFLOPS** = Million / Giga / Tera **FL**oating-point **O**perations **P**er **S**econd.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Benchmarks

Benchmarks are organized into a strict hierarchy that examiners love to test:

1. **Real Applications (Native Workloads)**
   - Truly execute real production software (e.g., a C compiler, a database engine).
   - *Pro:* Highest fidelity. *Con:* Expensive to port, hard to standardize, version drift.

2. **Application-Based (Kernels)**
   - Modified/portable real applications or compact code excerpts (e.g., **Livermore Loops**, **LINPACK** kernels, SPEC CPU sub-tests like `perlbench`, `gcc`, `mcf`).
   - *Pro:* Portable, reproducible, manageable runtime. *Con:* May miss I/O behaviour.

3. **Synthetic Benchmarks**
   - Artificially generated instructions/statistics mimicking program behaviour (e.g., **Dhrystone**, **Whetstone**, **LINPACK synthetic**).
   - *Pro:* Tiny and fast. *Con:* Can be compiler-optimized into irrelevance (a "benchmark special" pitfall).

4. **Micro-benchmarks**
   - Measure a single component (cache latency, DRAM bandwidth, FPU throughput).

### 2.2 Desktop Benchmarks — Deep Dive

| Suite | Year | Scope | Key Metric | KTU-Relevant Detail |
|-------|------|-------|------------|---------------------|
| **SPEC CPU 2006 / 2017** | 2006 / 2017 | Compute-intensive integer + FP | `SPECratio` (geometric mean of speed ratios) | Foundation metric for almost every CPU comparison |
| **PCMark 10** | 2017+ | Office productivity, content creation, gaming | Composite numeric score | Simulates modern app-mix |
| **3DMark** | 2013+ | GPU + CPU graphics rendering | Time Spy / Fire Strike scores | Stress-tests graphics pipeline |
| **Geekbench 5/6** | 2019/2022 | Cross-platform CPU | Single-core + Multi-core scores | AES, ML, ray-tracing workloads |
| **SPECviewperf** | 2020 | Professional 3D apps (CAD, Maya) | Composite weighted score | GPU + driver benchmarking |
| **SPECpower_ssj2008** | 2008 | Server-side Java under varying load | `ssj_ops` / `ssj_ops/Watt` | Power-aware desktop/server |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SPEC CPU 2017** has **43 benchmarks** split into 4 suites: `intrate` (speed, integer), `intrate` (rate, integer), `fprate` (speed, FP), `fprate` (rate, FP). For KTU, remember the **arithmetic-mean-like `SPECratio` is a GEOMETRIC MEAN**, not arithmetic — a frequent trick question.

### 2.3 Server Benchmarks — Deep Dive

| Suite | Workload Type | Primary Metric | Use Case |
|-------|---------------|----------------|----------|
| **TPC-C** | OLTP (Order-Entry) | `tpmC` and `$/tpmC` | Classic online transaction processing |
| **TPC-H** | Decision Support / Ad-hoc query | `QphH@SF` (Queries per hour at Scale Factor) | Analytical data warehouse |
| **TPC-DS** | Decision Support (modern SQL) | `QphDS` | Big-data analytics with SQL + BI |
| **SPECjbb2015** | Java Business | `max-jOPS` (peak), `critical-jOPS` (under SLA) | Java middleware (typical server stack) |
| **SPECpower_ssj2008** | Multi-tier web | `ssj_ops/Watt` | **Energy efficiency** of servers |
| **SPEC SFS** | NFS / file server | `ops/s` and response-time curve | Storage / file-server throughput |
| **LINPACK / HPL** | Dense linear algebra | `GFLOPS` or `Rmax` (Rpeak) | HPC, Top500 ranking |
| **VMmark** | Virtualization | `tile` score | Server consolidation / hypervisor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** TPC reports always disclose **price/performance** (e.g., `$/tpmC`) — this is unique to TPC and reflects the procurement decision a CIO must make.

### 2.4 The SPEC `SPECratio` and Geometric Mean — Why Geometric?

For a suite of *n* benchmarks with reference times $t_{ref,i}$ and measured times $t_{test,i}$, the **SPECratio** for benchmark $i$ is:

$$S_i = \frac{t_{ref,i}}{t_{test,i}}$$

The composite score is the **geometric mean**:

$$\text{SPECscore} = \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} S_i\right)^{1/n}$$

**Why geometric, not arithmetic?** Because it gives **equal weight to ratios** rather than absolute times. A machine twice as fast on a 1-second benchmark is rewarded the same as a machine twice as fast on a 1000-second benchmark. Arithmetic mean would unfairly favor the long-running test.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|--------|---------|----------------------|------|
| $T_{\text{CPU}}$ | CPU execution time | $T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{\text{Instructions} \times \text{CPI}}{\text{Clock Rate}}$ | seconds |
| $S_i$ | SPEC ratio for benchmark $i$ | $S_i = t_{ref,i} / t_{test,i}$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{SPECscore}$ | Composite SPEC score | $\left(\prod_i S_i\right)^{1/n}$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{MIPS}$ | Million instructions/sec | $\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{Clock Rate (Hz)}}{10^6 \times \text{CPI}}$ | $10^6$ inst/s |
| $\text{MFLOPS}$ | Million FP ops/sec | $\text{MFLOPS} = \frac{\text{FP ops}}{T_{\text{exec}} \times 10^6}$ | $10^6$ FP/s |
| $\text{speedup}$ | Amdahl's speedup | $S(n) = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{tpmC}$ | TPC-C throughput | Transactions completed per minute | txn/min |
| $\text{QphH@SF}$ | TPC-H query throughput | Queries per hour at scale factor SF | queries/h |
| $\text{ssj\_ops}$ | SPECpower throughput | Java ops completed per second | ops/s |
| $\text{Efficiency}$ | Power efficiency | $\text{ssj\_ops} / \text{Watt}$ | ops/s/W |
| $R_{max}$ | LINPACK max achieved | Max sustained GFLOPS | $10^9$ FLOPS |
| $R_{peak}$ | Theoretical peak | $R_{peak} = \text{cores} \times \text{clock} \times \text{FLOPs/cycle}$ | $10^9$ FLOPS |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency ratio | $\eta = R_{max} / R_{peak} \times 100\%$ | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Trap (KTU Exam Typing):** Never use the vertical bar `|` for absolute value inside a markdown table — it **breaks the table parser**. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX within tables.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPU Architects** use SPEC CPU to validate branch predictor, cache, and pipeline improvements.
- **Server Procurement Teams** use TPC-C `$/tpmC` and SPECpower `ssj_ops/Watt` to evaluate **TCO** (Total Cost of Ownership) including electricity.
- **Data Center Designers** use SPECpower curves to size cooling and UPS infrastructure.
- **HPC Engineers** use HPL (LINPACK) to rank systems on the **TOP500** list.
- **Cloud Providers** use VMmark to publish their virtualization density claims.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why SPECscore Uses a Geometric Mean

Suppose two systems $A$ and $B$ run two benchmarks $X$ and $Y$.

**Reference times** (industry-standard reference machine):
$$t_{ref,X} = 100 \text{ s}, \quad t_{ref,Y} = 1000 \text{ s}$$

**Measured times** for system $A$:
$$t_{A,X} = 10 \text{ s}, \quad t_{A,Y} = 100 \text{ s}$$

**Measured times** for system $B$:
$$t_{B,X} = 50 \text{ s}, \quad t_{B,Y} = 20 \text{ s}$$

**Step 1: Compute individual SPECratios for System A**

$$S_{A,X} = \frac{t_{ref,X}}{t_{A,X}} = \frac{100}{10} = 10$$

$$S_{A,Y} = \frac{t_{ref,Y}}{t_{A,Y}} = \frac{1000}{100} = 10$$

**Step 2: Compute composite score for System A (geometric mean)**

$$\text{SPECscore}_A = \sqrt{S_{A,X} \times S_{A,Y}} = \sqrt{10 \times 10} = 10$$

**Step 3: Compute individual SPECratios for System B**

$$S_{B,X} = \frac{50}{50} = 1 \quad \text{(wait, careful — see below)}$$

Actually, **correcting Step 3** with the proper definition $S_{B,i} = t_{ref,i} / t_{B,i}$:

$$S_{B,X} = \frac{100}{50} = 2$$

$$S_{B,Y} = \frac{1000}{20} = 50$$

**Step 4: Compute composite score for System B**

$$\text{SPECscore}_B = \sqrt{2 \times 50} = \sqrt{100} = 10$$

**Step 5: Sanity check — try arithmetic mean for comparison**

$$\bar{S}_A^{\text{arith}} = \frac{10 + 10}{2} = 10$$

$$\bar{S}_B^{\text{arith}} = \frac{2 + 50}{2} = 26 \;\;\text{← (misleadingly favors B)}$$

**Step 6: Interpret the result**

Both systems obtain a SPECscore of **10** under the geometric mean, because *they are each twice as fast on benchmark X and ten times as fast on benchmark Y* in ratio terms (no — they are 10× and 2× vs 10× and 50×; the geometric mean correctly normalizes the **relative speedup** rather than the absolute time saved).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** If you ever see a question on "why SPEC uses geometric mean," your answer must include: (1) independence from the units/scale of reference times, (2) equal weighting of relative performance, (3) the property that $\text{GEOMEAN}(a,b) = \text{EXP}(\frac{1}{n}\sum \ln a_i)$ — i.e., the geometric mean is the arithmetic mean of the **logarithms**, which is the natural way to average multiplicative quantities like speedups.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Amdahl's Law Applied to Benchmark Scaling

Amdahl's Law governs how a benchmark's speedup behaves as we add cores:

$$S(n) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{n}}$$

where $f$ = fraction of program that is **parallelizable**, and $(1-f)$ = strictly serial portion. As $n \to \infty$:

$$S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - f}$$

**Worked Example: SPECjbb Multi-JVM Scaling**

Suppose a server benchmark has a parallelizable fraction $f = 0.95$ (typical for Java business workloads). Run on $n = 8$ cores.

**Step 1: Substitute into Amdahl's formula**

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \frac{0.95}{8}}$$

**Step 2: Numerically evaluate the denominator**

$$\frac{0.95}{8} = 0.11875$$

$$(1 - 0.95) + 0.11875 = 0.05 + 0.11875 = 0.16875$$

**Step 3: Invert to obtain speedup**

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.16875} \approx 5.926$$

**Step 4: Compare to infinite-core limit**

$$S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20$$

So even with **infinite cores**, the benchmark cannot exceed **20× speedup** because of the 5 % serial tail. This is a fundamental insight for **server benchmark scalability analysis**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Compute TPC-C `$/tpmC` Price/Performance

A server costs $200,000 and achieves 100,000 tpmC.

**Step 1: Define the metric**

$$\text{Price/Performance} = \frac{\text{Total 5-year system cost (\$)}}{\text{tpmC}}$$

**Step 2: Plug in values**

$$\text{Price/Performance} = \frac{200{,}000}{100{,}000} = \$2.00 \text{ per tpmC}$$

**Step 3: Industry interpretation**

If the industry-leading result is `$1.50/tpmC`, the system is **33% less cost-efficient** at the workload level. Lower `$/tpmC` is better.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: SPECpower `ssj_ops/Watt` Computation

SPECpower_ssj2008 measures throughput at **10 load levels** (100 %, 90 %, …, 10 %). The composite is:

$$\text{Overall ssj\_ops} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} \text{ssj\_ops}_i$$

$$\text{Overall Power} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} \text{Power}_i \;\;\text{(Watts)}$$

$$\text{ssj\_ops/Watt} = \frac{\text{Overall ssj\_ops}}{\text{Overall Power}}$$

**Worked Example:** Suppose the 10 load levels yield the following:

| Load Level | ssj_ops | Power (W) |
|------------|---------|-----------|
| 100 % | 500,000 | 800 |
| 90 % | 460,000 | 740 |
| 80 % | 420,000 | 690 |
| 70 % | 380,000 | 640 |
| 60 % | 340,000 | 590 |
| 50 % | 300,000 | 540 |
| 40 % | 250,000 | 490 |
| 30 % | 200,000 | 440 |
| 20 % | 150,000 | 390 |
| 10 % | 90,000 | 340 |

**Step 1: Sum throughputs**

$$\sum \text{ssj\_ops} = 500{,}000 + 460{,}000 + 420{,}000 + 380{,}000 + 340{,}000 + 300{,}000 + 250{,}000 + 200{,}000 + 150{,}000 + 90{,}000 = 3{,}090{,}000$$

**Step 2: Sum power**

$$\sum P = 800 + 740 + 690 + 640 + 590 + 540 + 490 + 440 + 390 + 340 = 5{,}660 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Compute efficiency**

$$\text{ssj\_ops/Watt} = \frac{3{,}090{,}000}{5{,}660} \approx 545.94 \;\;\text{ssj\_ops/W}$$

This single number is what data-center operators compare across vendor offerings.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: From MIPS / MFLOPS to SPECscore

Given a machine with $T_{\text{CPU}} = 10$ s for a benchmark containing $I = 5 \times 10^8$ instructions and CPI = 1.5, with clock rate = 2 GHz.

**Step 1: Verify CPU time via the iron law**

$$T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{5 \times 10^8 \times 1.5}{2 \times 10^9} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.375 \text{ s}$$

This contradicts the given 10 s — which tells us the benchmark is dominated by **memory stalls** (cache misses). The MIPS and MFLOPS metrics are essentially useless when memory stall cycles dominate, which is one of the **central criticisms of MIPS** and a justification for using SPEC CPU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** MIPS and MFLOPS are *vanity metrics* for memory-bound workloads. SPEC CPU is preferred because its score is derived from **wall-clock execution time** of representative programs, including memory effects.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
