## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]** 
**Differentiate between desktop benchmarks and server benchmarks with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Desktop Benchmark | Server Benchmark |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| **User Model** | Single user, interactive | Multiple concurrent users |
| **Primary Metric** | Response time / latency | Throughput (tpmC, QphH, jOPS) |
| **Workload Type** | Mixed compute + graphics + I/O | Transactional, data-intensive |
| **Examples** | SPEC CPU 2017, PCMark 10, 3DMark | TPC-C, SPECjbb2015, SPECpower |
| **Power Metric** | Often optional | Frequently mandatory (`ssj_ops/Watt`) |

*(Award 1 mark for the differentiating criterion, 1 mark for the metric, 1 mark for the examples.)*

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Why does SPEC use the geometric mean rather than the arithmetic mean to combine individual benchmark scores into a single composite score?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The geometric mean $\text{GM} = \left(\prod S_i\right)^{1/n}$ treats every benchmark as an **equal contributor in ratio terms**, independent of its absolute runtime on the reference machine. *(1 mark)*
2. It is equivalent to the arithmetic mean of the **logarithms** of the speedups, which is the natural way to average *multiplicative* quantities. *(1 mark)*
3. It avoids the pathology where a single very long-running benchmark dominates the composite, which would happen with the arithmetic mean. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **taxonomy of benchmarks** used in computer architecture evaluation. Differentiate between real, application-based, and synthetic benchmarks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A benchmark is a standardized workload used to evaluate computer system performance. The standard taxonomy (per KTU Module 1) is:

1. **Real Applications** — Actual production code (e.g., gcc, a commercial DBMS). Highest fidelity, but expensive and version-sensitive. *(1 mark)*
2. **Application-Based (Kernels)** — Portions of real code or modified real programs (e.g., Livermore Loops, SPEC CPU sub-tests like `mcf`, `perlbench`). Best balance of fidelity and portability. *(1 mark)*
3. **Synthetic Benchmarks** — Artificially generated code (e.g., Dhrystone, Whetstone). Fast and tiny, but compilers can "game" them (a phenomenon known as *benchmark specials*). *(1 mark)*

The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes) places these as siblings under "Benchmarks," with desktop, server, and embedded sub-trees. *(1 mark)*

**Comparison Table** *(3 marks)*:

| Property | Real | Kernel | Synthetic |
|----------|------|--------|-----------|
| Fidelity | High | Medium | Low |
| Portability | Low | High | Very High |
| Compile-game risk | Low | Medium | High |
| Runtime | Long | Medium | Short |
| Example | gcc | SPEC `mcf` | Dhrystone |

---

**(b)** A server executes the SPECjbb2015 benchmark on 4 cores. 92 % of the workload is parallelizable. Using **Amdahl's Law**, compute the achieved speedup and the **theoretical maximum speedup** if the cores were increased without bound. Comment on the implications for benchmark scalability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: State Amdahl's Law**

$$S(n) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{n}}$$

Given: $f = 0.92$, $n = 4$.

**Step 2: Compute the speedup at n = 4**

$$S(4) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.92) + \frac{0.92}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.23} = \frac{1}{0.31} \approx 3.226$$

*(1 mark for setup, 1 mark for arithmetic)*

**Step 3: Compute the theoretical maximum as n → ∞**

$$S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.92} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Calculate efficiency at n = 4**

$$\eta = \frac{S(4)}{n} = \frac{3.226}{4} \approx 0.8065 = 80.65\%$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5: Implications** *(2 marks)*

- Even with **infinite cores**, the system cannot exceed **12.5× speedup** because of the 8 % serial overhead (locks, JVM GC, OS services).
- Going from 4 cores to 8 cores would yield only:
$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.92/8} = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.115} = \frac{1}{0.195} \approx 5.13$$
which is **diminishing returns** — adding cores beyond ~16 yields negligible benefit.
- **Benchmark-design implication:** To fairly evaluate high-core-count servers, the benchmark's serial fraction must be minimized (e.g., by sharding the database in TPC-C).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of the **SPEC CPU 2017 benchmark suite** in detail. What are the four sub-suites, what workloads do they contain, and how is the composite score computed? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: SPEC organization** *(1 mark)*

SPEC CPU 2017 is maintained by the **Standard Performance Evaluation Corporation**. It consists of **43 benchmarks** derived from real applications, grouped into 4 sub-suites.

**Step 2: Sub-suite descriptions** *(3 marks)*

| Sub-suite | Focus | Sample Benchmarks |
|-----------|-------|-------------------|
| `intrate` (speed) | Integer, single-task latency | `perlbench`, `gcc`, `mcf`, `omnetpp`, `xalancbmk`, `deepsjeng`, `leela`, `exchange2`, `xz` |
| `intrate` (rate) | Integer, multi-copy throughput | same 10 integer benchmarks, run with multiple copies |
| `fprate` (speed) | FP, single-task latency | `bwaves`, `cactuBSSN`, `lbm`, `wrf`, `cam4`, `imagick`, `nab`, `fotonik3d`, `roms`, `parest` |
| `fprate` (rate) | FP, multi-copy throughput | same 10 FP benchmarks, run with multiple copies |

**Step 3: SPECratio computation** *(1 mark)*

For benchmark $i$, the ratio is the reference time divided by the test time:

$$S_i = \frac{t_{ref,i}}{t_{test,i}}$$

**Step 4: Composite SPECscore** *(1 mark)*

The composite is the **geometric mean** of the individual $S_i$:

$$\text{SPECscore} = \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} S_i\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 5: Mandatory rules** *(1 mark)*

- Compiler flags must be **disclosed** in the report.
- **Three runs** are required, and the **median** time is taken.
- The reference machine is fixed (a specific Sun Fire X2270 / AMD Opteron 8354 baseline).

---

**(b)** A server was tested using **SPECpower_ssj2008**. The 10 load levels yielded total throughput of 2,500,000 ssj_ops and total power of 5,000 W. The same workload class on a competing server used 4,000 W for the same 2,500,000 ssj_ops. Compute the `ssj_ops/Watt` for each and explain why SPECpower matters in green data-center design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute the efficiency metric for Server 1** *(1 mark)*

$$E_1 = \frac{2{,}500{,}000 \text{ ssj\_ops}}{5{,}000 \text{ W}} = 500 \text{ ssj\_ops/W}$$

**Step 2: Compute the efficiency metric for Server 2** *(1 mark)*

$$E_2 = \frac{2{,}500{,}000 \text{ ssj\_ops}}{4{,}000 \text{ W}} = 625 \text{ ssj\_ops/W}$$

**Step 3: Compare** *(1 mark)*

Server 2 is $\frac{625 - 500}{500} \times 100\% = 25\%$ more power-efficient than Server 1 at the same workload.

**Step 4: Annual energy and cost savings** *(2 marks)*

For Server 1, annual energy = $5{,}000 \text{ W} \times 8{,}760 \text{ h/year} = 43.8 \text{ MWh}$.

For Server 2: $4{,}000 \times 8{,}760 = 35.04 \text{ MWh}$.

Saving = $8.76$ MWh per server per year. At industrial electricity tariffs of $0.10/kWh, that is **$876 per server per year** — a tangible data-center TCO reduction.

**Step 5: Why this matters in green data-center design** *(2 marks)*

- Data centers account for ~1–2 % of global electricity; SPECpower provides a **standardized, vendor-neutral** efficiency number.
- High `ssj_ops/Watt` directly reduces **PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness)** and cooling load.
- It also correlates with **carbon footprint** for ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) reporting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Arithmetic vs. Geometric Mean Confusion** — Many students write "SPECscore = arithmetic mean of ratios." This is **wrong** and costs the 1-mark differentiation point. Always write the explicit geometric-mean formula.
> 2. **MIPS / MFLOPS vs. Real Time** — Do not equate MIPS with performance. MIPS is **inversely proportional to CPI** and ignores memory hierarchy; SPEC CPU uses wall-clock time.
> 3. **Server vs. Desktop Mix-up** — When asked to "give two examples of server benchmarks," do **not** list SPEC CPU (which is **workload-agnostic and used for both**). Better server-specific examples: **TPC-C, SPECjbb2015, SPECpower, VMmark**.
> 4. **Amdahl's Law Units** — Speedup $S$ is **dimensionless**. The number of cores $n$ is **unitless**. Do not write "$S$ in seconds."
> 5. **SPECpower Metric Inversion** — Higher `ssj_ops/Watt` = better. Examiners often invert the ratio to test your understanding.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- **Benchmark = standardized performance yardstick** — its purpose is *objective comparison*, not raw marketing.
- **Desktop benchmarks** (SPEC CPU 2017, PCMark, 3DMark, Geekbench) target **single-user latency** and **graphics/responsiveness**.
- **Server benchmarks** (TPC-C, TPC-H, SPECjbb, SPECpower, LINPACK, VMmark) target **throughput**, **scalability**, and **power efficiency**.
- **SPECscore = geometric mean of SPECratios**, not arithmetic — this is the most-tested single fact on this topic.
- **SPECratio** = reference time / test time; a SPECscore of **100** means the system is 100× faster than the reference.
- **TPC-C** measures OLTP throughput in **`tpmC`** plus **`$/tpmC`** (price/performance).
- **TPC-H** measures decision-support workload in **`QphH@Size`**.
- **SPECpower_ssj2008** = 10 load levels × (throughput / power) → composite **`ssj_ops/Watt`**. Critical for **green data centers**.
- **Amdahl's Law** $S(n) = 1 / [(1-f) + f/n]$ limits scalability of *every* server benchmark by the **serial fraction** $(1-f)$.
- **LINPACK / HPL** measures **`Rmax`** and **`Rpeak`** (GFLOPS) and feeds the **TOP500** supercomputer ranking; efficiency $\eta = R_{max}/R_{peak}$ typically 60–90 %.
- **MIPS / MFLOPS are flawed** for modern systems because they ignore memory stall cycles; SPEC's wall-clock approach is preferred.
- **Compiler disclosure** is mandatory in all SPEC submissions to prevent benchmark specialisation.
- **Reference machine baseline** = 1.00 (a fixed SPEC reference); all scores are relative to it.
- **TPC** is the only major body that mandates **price disclosure** in its reports, making it a procurement tool, not just a performance tool.
- **Workload divergence** between desktop and server is the *primary reason* no single benchmark can fairly evaluate both.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amdahl's Law
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Amdahl's Law

# Amdahl's Law — Foundational Principle of Performance Bottleneck Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amdahl's Law** is a fundamental analytical model formulated by **Gene M. Amdahl in 1967** that quantifies the **theoretical maximum speedup** achievable in a computing system when only a *fraction* of the system's overall execution time is subjected to an architectural or technological enhancement.

The law establishes a mathematical relationship between the **fraction of a task that can be improved (f)**, the **speedup factor of that improved fraction (s)**, and the resulting **overall system speedup (S)**. It is the cornerstone of performance evaluation in advanced computer architecture because it exposes the harsh reality that **diminishing returns dominate performance engineering** — no matter how aggressively one component is accelerated, the un-enhanced portion of the workload eventually becomes the dominant bottleneck.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Commute" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a daily office commute that takes exactly 10 hours round-trip.**

| Segment | Original Time | Fraction of Total |
|---|---|---|
| Driving on a rural highway | **9 hours** | **90%** (f = 0.9) |
| Crawling through city traffic | **1 hour** | **10%** (1 − f = 0.1) |

You invest in a **Ferrari** that makes the highway portion **10× faster**.

- **New highway time**: 9 ÷ 10 = 0.9 hours
- **City time remains**: 1 hour (unchanged — no engine can fix traffic)
- **New total commute**: 0.9 + 1 = **1.9 hours**
- **Overall speedup**: 10 ÷ 1.9 ≈ **5.26×** (not 10×!)

**The lesson:** The un-improved 10% (city traffic) consumed **52.6% of the new commute time**, despite being only a tiny fraction of the original workload. This is the *exact* phenomenon Amdahl's Law predicts — **the bottleneck dictates the ceiling**.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Vocabulary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three metrics MUST be memorized for the board examination:
> - **f** — the *enhanceable fraction* of execution time (a value between **0 and 1**, or **0% to 100%**).
> - **s** — the *speedup factor* of the enhanced fraction (a positive integer or real number ≥ **1**).
> - **S** — the *overall system speedup* (always ≤ 1 / (1 − f), even as **s → ∞**).

### 1.4 Why This Law Matters in Advanced Computer Architecture

Amdahl's Law governs every architectural decision: choosing between **pipelining depth, cache size, multicore count, vector unit width, or hardware accelerator integration**. Before any optimization is committed to silicon, architects invoke Amdahl's Law to predict whether the optimization is *worth the die-area, power, and design complexity cost*. It is the **mathematical conscience** of computer architects.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Decomposition of Amdahl's Reasoning (Step-by-Step)

The law is derived from a single premise: **execution time is additive across independent sub-tasks**. The conceptual chain is:

- **Step 1 — Partition the workload.** Every program is decomposed into two logical parts: the *enhanceable part* and the *un-enhanced (serial/bottleneck) part*.
- **Step 2 — Express original execution time.** The baseline time $T_{original}$ is split as $(1-f) \cdot T_{original}$ for the serial portion and $f \cdot T_{original}$ for the enhanceable portion.
- **Step 3 — Apply enhancement.** The enhanceable portion runs $s$ times faster, so its new time becomes $(f \cdot T_{original}) / s$.
- **Step 4 — Sum new execution times.** Because the parts run *sequentially* on a single logical timeline, total new time is the sum.
- **Step 5 — Compute speedup ratio.** Overall speedup $S$ is the ratio of original time to enhanced time.
- **Step 6 — Take the limit.** As $s \to \infty$, the law reduces to a brutal constant ceiling: $S_{max} = 1/(1-f)$.

### 2.2 The Famous "Diminishing Returns" Curve

When $S$ is plotted against $s$ for a fixed $f$, the curve exhibits:
- **Linear growth initially** (small $s$ values).
- **Asymptotic saturation** as $s$ grows large.
- **Horizontal asymptote at $S = 1/(1-f)$** — the unbreakable ceiling.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — every KTU question on Amdahl's Law pivots on these relations.**

| **Symbol** | **Name** | **Mathematical Form** | **Domain / Boundary Condition** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f$ | Enhanceable fraction | $0 \le f \le 1$ | Typically expressed as percentage | Dimensionless |
| $1-f$ | Un-enhanced (bottleneck) fraction | $0 \le 1-f \le 1$ | Serial portion of program | Dimensionless |
| $s$ | Speedup of enhanced part | $s \ge 1$ | Ratio of old time to new time of enhanced part | Dimensionless |
| $S$ | Overall system speedup | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}$ | $S \ge 1$ | Dimensionless |
| $S_{max}$ | Maximum achievable speedup | $S_{max} = \lim_{s \to \infty} S = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ | Reached as $s \to \infty$ | Dimensionless |
| $T_{old}$ | Original execution time | $T_{old} = T$ | Baseline reference | Seconds |
| $T_{new}$ | Enhanced execution time | $T_{new} = (1-f)\cdot T + \dfrac{f\cdot T}{s}$ | Sum of residual + enhanced parts | Seconds |
| $S$ (alt) | Alternative form | $S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | Direct ratio form | Dimensionless |
| $f_{new}$ | Fraction required for target speedup | $f = \dfrac{1 - \dfrac{1}{S}}{\dfrac{1}{s} - \dfrac{1}{S}}$ | Rearranged for required enhancement | Dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Amdahl's Law is invoked across every layer of modern computing:
> - **Multicore CPU design** — predicting returns from increasing core count when serial code limits parallelism.
> - **GPU/CPU heterogeneous computing** — quantifying speedup from offloading kernels to a GPU.
> - **Cloud computing & distributed databases** — analyzing latency improvements from caching (e.g., Memcached/Redis).
> - **Hardware accelerator design (TPU, NPU, FPGA)** — justifying silicon investment for AI inference pipelines.
> - **Compiler optimization passes** — evaluating the impact of loop unrolling, vectorization, and inlining.
> - **Storage hierarchy design** — predicting hit-rate requirements of caches for target speedup.

### 2.5 Critical Logical Insight — The Gustafson Counterpoint

> [!NOTE]
> **Gustafson's Law (1988)** rebuts Amdahl's pessimistic ceiling by observing that **problem sizes scale with available resources**. In practice, when more processors are added, users run *larger* problems — so the serial fraction does not remain a fixed wall-clock bottleneck. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *contrast* the two laws.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Canonical Formula

We begin with the most rigorous step-by-step algebraic derivation of Amdahl's Law from first principles.

**Given:**
- Total original execution time of program = $T$ (seconds)
- Enhanceable fraction of execution time = $f$
- Therefore, un-enhanced (serial) fraction = $1 - f$
- Speedup applied to enhanceable portion = $s$

**Derivation:**

The original execution time can be decomposed additively:

$$
T_{original} = (1 - f) \cdot T + f \cdot T
$$

*Explanation:* The first term $(1-f) \cdot T$ represents the time spent in the part of the program that **cannot** be improved. The second term $f \cdot T$ represents the time spent in the part that **will** be improved by a factor of $s$.

After applying the enhancement of factor $s$ to only the enhanceable portion:

$$
T_{new} = (1 - f) \cdot T + \frac{f \cdot T}{s}
$$

*Explanation:* The serial portion $(1-f) \cdot T$ remains unchanged. The enhanceable portion is divided by $s$ because the new time equals the old time divided by the speedup factor.

By the fundamental definition of speedup as the ratio of old time to new time:

$$
S = \frac{T_{original}}{T_{new}}
$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$
S = \frac{(1 - f) \cdot T + f \cdot T}{(1 - f) \cdot T + \dfrac{f \cdot T}{s}}
$$

*Explanation:* We substitute $T_{original}$ and $T_{new}$ into the ratio.

Factor out $T$ from numerator and denominator (since $T > 0$, it cancels):

$$
S = \frac{T \cdot \left[(1 - f) + f\right]}{T \cdot \left[(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s}\right]}
$$

*Explanation:* The numerator contains $(1-f) + f = 1$, and $T$ cancels identically from both numerator and denominator.

This simplifies to the **canonical Amdahl's Law equation**:

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the closed-form expression that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce verbatim in the answer sheet.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Speedup Ceiling

We take the limit as the per-component speedup $s$ approaches infinity:

$$
S_{max} = \lim_{s \to \infty} S = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}
$$

As $s \to \infty$, the term $\dfrac{f}{s} \to 0$:

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + 0} = \frac{1}{1 - f}
$$

*Explanation:* No matter how aggressively you accelerate the enhanceable fraction, you can never overcome the serial $(1-f)$ portion. This is the **brutal mathematical truth** Amdahl exposed.

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Classic Board Problem (Full Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A program spends **40%** of its time in a function that can be made **5 times faster**. Calculate the overall speedup.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- $f = 0.40$ (enhanceable fraction)
- $s = 5$ (speedup of the enhanced portion)
- $1 - f = 0.60$ (un-enhanced fraction)

**Step 2 — Apply the canonical formula.**

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.60 + \dfrac{0.40}{5}}
$$

*Explanation:* We plug the values directly into the denominator.

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator numerically.**

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.08}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.68}
$$

*Explanation:* $\dfrac{0.40}{5} = 0.08$. Adding gives $0.68$.

**Step 4 — Final answer.**

$$
S \approx 1.47
$$

*Explanation:* The overall system is only **1.47× faster**, NOT 5× faster, despite a 5× speedup on 40% of the code.

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Maximum Speedup Question

> **Problem:** What is the maximum possible speedup if **25%** of the program is inherently serial?

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- $1 - f = 0.25$ (serial fraction)
- $f = 0.75$ (enhanceable fraction)
- $s \to \infty$

**Step 2 — Apply the ceiling formula.**

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4
$$

*Explanation:* The absolute maximum speedup is **4×**, no matter how many cores or how fast the hardware becomes.

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Reverse Calculation (Required Fraction)

> **Problem:** To achieve an overall speedup of **10×**, with a per-component speedup of **100×**, what fraction of the program must be enhanced?

**Step 1 — Rearrange Amdahl's formula to solve for $f$.**

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}
$$

Taking the reciprocal:

$$
\frac{1}{S} = (1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}
$$

Grouping the $f$ terms:

$$
\frac{1}{S} = 1 - f + \frac{f}{s} = 1 - f \left(1 - \frac{1}{s}\right)
$$

Rearranging to isolate $f$:

$$
f \left(1 - \frac{1}{s}\right) = 1 - \frac{1}{S}
$$

$$
f = \frac{1 - \dfrac{1}{S}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{s}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $S = 10$, $s = 100$.**

$$
f = \frac{1 - \dfrac{1}{10}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{100}} = \frac{0.9}{0.99}
$$

$$
f \approx 0.9091 = 90.91\%
$$

*Explanation:* To achieve a 10× overall speedup with 100× per-component speedup, nearly **91% of the program** must be parallelizable.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Speedup Behavior Across Scenarios

| **Enhanceable Fraction $f$** | **Per-component speedup $s$** | **Overall Speedup $S$** | **$S_{max}$ as $s \to \infty$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.50 (50%) | 2 | 1.333 | **2.000** |
| 0.50 (50%) | 10 | 1.818 | **2.000** |
| 0.50 (50%) | 100 | 1.980 | **2.000** |
| 0.90 (90%) | 10 | 5.263 | **10.000** |
| 0.90 (90%) | 100 | 9.174 | **10.000** |
| 0.99 (99%) | 100 | 50.251 | **100.000** |
| 0.95 (95%) | 20 | 10.256 | **20.000** |

*Interpretation:* Notice how the ceiling $S_{max}$ is **strictly bound by $f$**, never by $s$. Doubling $s$ beyond a point yields negligible benefit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Amdahl's Law Conceptual Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Original Program Execution"] --> nodeB["Decompose Time into Two Fractions"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Serial Fraction 1 minus f Times T"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Enhanceable Fraction f Times T"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Apply Speedup Factor s"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["New Time equals f Times T divided by s"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Sum Both Times"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Compute Speedup S equals Told over Tnew"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Output Overall Speedup S"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{"Is s very large?"}
    nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeK["S approaches 1 over 1 minus f"]
    nodeJ -- No --> nodeL["S equals 1 over 1 minus f plus f over s"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Performance Bottleneck Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Program Workload 100 percent"] --> splitA["Logical Partitioner"]
    splitA --> serialA["Serial Fraction 1 minus f Unenhanceable"]
    splitA --> parA["Parallel Fraction f Enhanceable"]
    parA --> accelA["Hardware Enhancer Speedup s"]
    serialA --> mergeA["Execution Time Combiner"]
    accelA --> mergeA
    mergeA --> outputA["Overall Speedup S equals Told over Tnew"]
    serialA -. Bottleneck Constraint .-> outputA
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Graph — Speedup Saturation Curve (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    origin["Origin s equals 1, S equals 1"] --> rise["Linear Region Small s"]
    rise --> knee["Inflection Point s approximately 10"]
    knee --> bend["Bending Region Increasing s"]
    bend --> flat["Saturation Region Large s"]
    flat --> ceiling["Asymptote S equals 1 over 1 minus f"]
    ceiling --> infinity["Limit s approaches infinity, S approaches Smax"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Amdahl's Evaluation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["Input Layer f and s parameters"] --> p2["Validation Layer ensure 0 less than or equal to f less than or equal to 1"]
    p2 --> p3["Compute Denominator D equals 1 minus f plus f over s"]
    p3 --> p4["Compute Speedup S equals 1 over D"]
    p4 --> p5{"Compute Max Speedup?"}
    p5 -- Yes --> p6["Limit Layer Smax equals 1 over 1 minus f"]
    p5 -- No --> p7["Report S to Output Layer"]
    p6 --> p7
    p7 --> p8["Output Layer Board Ready Result with Units"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
**State and explain Amdahl's Law. Why is it considered the fundamental limit of parallel computing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amdahl's Law** (formulated by Gene Amdahl, 1967) states that the overall speedup $S$ achievable by enhancing a fraction $f$ of a computation by a factor $s$ is given by:
>
> $$S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}$$
>
> The law is considered the fundamental limit of parallel computing because, as $s \to \infty$, the maximum achievable speedup is bounded by:
>
> $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - f}$$
>
> This means **the serial (un-enhanced) portion of a program imposes a hard ceiling** on performance, regardless of how many processors, cores, or hardware accelerators are deployed. **It justifies the architectural dictum**: *"Make the common case fast, but first identify and minimize the common case."*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
**A program spends 30% of its time in a loop that is optimized to run 4 times faster. What is the overall speedup achieved?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $f = 0.30$, $s = 4$, $1 - f = 0.70$.

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.70 + \dfrac{0.30}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.075} = \frac{1}{0.775}
$$

$$
S \approx 1.29
$$

> **Overall speedup ≈ 1.29×** (not 4× as students often incorrectly assume).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Choice 1]**

**(a)** Derive Amdahl's Law from first principles. State all assumptions and explain each algebraic step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multiprocessor system has a program where **80%** of the code is parallelizable. Calculate:  
    (i) The maximum theoretical speedup achievable.  
    (ii) The actual speedup if the parallel portion runs on **8 processors** with equal workload distribution.  
    (iii) Comment on why adding more processors beyond a point yields negligible improvement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumption:** Execution time $T$ can be additively partitioned into an enhanceable part and a serial part.

Let $f$ be the enhanceable fraction and $1-f$ be the serial fraction:

$$
T_{original} = (1-f) \cdot T + f \cdot T
$$

The enhanceable part is accelerated by factor $s$:

$$
T_{new} = (1-f) \cdot T + \frac{f \cdot T}{s}
$$

Speedup is the ratio:

$$
S = \frac{T_{original}}{T_{new}} = \frac{(1-f)T + fT}{(1-f)T + \frac{fT}{s}}
$$

Factoring $T$ (cancels):

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{s}}
$$

Taking limit as $s \to \infty$:

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{1-f}
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $f = 0.80$, therefore $1 - f = 0.20$.

**(i) Maximum theoretical speedup:**

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{1}{0.20} = \mathbf{5.0}
$$

**(ii) Actual speedup with 8 processors** (where $s = 8$ for the parallel portion):

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.10} = \frac{1}{0.30}
$$

$$
S \approx \mathbf{3.33}
$$

**(iii) Comment (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> Even though we have 8 processors, the speedup (3.33×) is far below 8× and even below $S_{max} = 5×$. Adding more processors (e.g., 16, 32, 64) will only marginally increase speedup because the **20% serial portion dominates the execution timeline**. For 16 processors: $S = 1 / (0.20 + 0.05) = 4.0×$. For 64 processors: $S = 1 / (0.20 + 0.0125) = 4.71×$. The asymptotic ceiling of 5× is never exceeded.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain Amdahl's Law with a suitable real-world analogy. Derive the formula and discuss the concept of *maximum achievable speedup*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a database server where **95%** of query time is spent on disk I/O. The IT team deploys an SSD that is **20× faster** than the existing HDD for I/O operations.  
    (i) Compute the overall system speedup.  
    (ii) If the goal is to achieve a **10× overall speedup**, what fraction of the system must be further accelerated, and by what factor? Discuss the practical implications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Explanation + Derivation (7 marks):**

**Analogy:** A 10-hour trip consists of 9 hours highway driving and 1 hour city driving. Buying a faster car speeds up the highway portion but the city portion remains the bottleneck. This is precisely what Amdahl's Law models in computer systems.

**Formula derivation** (as shown in Q.A. above):

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{s}}
$$

**Maximum speedup concept:**

$$
S_{max} = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{s}} = \frac{1}{1-f}
$$

This ceiling means the un-enhanced fraction $1-f$ fundamentally limits performance.

**(b) Database Server Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $f = 0.95$ (disk I/O fraction), $s = 20$ (SSD speedup).

**(i) Overall system speedup:**

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \dfrac{0.95}{20}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.0475} = \frac{1}{0.0975}
$$

$$
S \approx \mathbf{10.26}
$$

**(ii) Achieving exactly 10× speedup — finding required fraction and factor:**

Assuming we keep $s = 20$ and solve for required $f$:

$$
f = \frac{1 - \frac{1}{S}}{1 - \frac{1}{s}} = \frac{1 - \frac{1}{10}}{1 - \frac{1}{20}} = \frac{0.90}{0.95} \approx 0.9474 = 94.74\%
$$

*Alternatively, if we already have $f = 0.95$ giving $S = 10.26$, and want exactly $S = 10$ with the same $f$, we solve for $s$:* $0.10 = 0.05 + 0.95/s \Rightarrow s = 19$. So reducing the SSD speedup marginally to 19× yields exactly 10×.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implications:** Even a 20× faster SSD yields only a 10.26× overall speedup because the **5% non-I/O time (CPU, network, lock contention) acts as a hard bottleneck**. To achieve dramatic further gains, the team must attack the serial 5% (e.g., optimize queries, add caching, reduce lock contention). This demonstrates Amdahl's central lesson — *identify and minimize the serial fraction before investing in accelerator hardware*.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percentage to decimal.** Students write $f = 80$ instead of $f = 0.80$, producing nonsense answers. *Always convert.*
> 2. **Confusing $f$ with $s$.** The enhanceable fraction $f$ and the per-component speedup $s$ are *different parameters*. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if confused.
> 3. **Forgetting the serial term $(1-f)$.** Some students write $S = s/f$ (an incorrect formula). Always include $(1-f)$ in the denominator.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions.** A derivation without the assumption of *additive time decomposition* loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing units in the final answer.** Although $S$ is dimensionless, explicitly stating *"speedup is dimensionless"* impresses the examiner.
> 6. **Not commenting on the ceiling.** Board answers that compute $S$ without noting $S_{max} = 1/(1-f)$ lose marks for *incomplete analysis*.
> 7. **Ignoring Gustafson's Law context.** When asked to *"critically discuss"*, failing to mention Gustafson's rebuttal (problem size scaling) costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Amdahl's Law** quantifies the maximum speedup from enhancing *only a fraction* of a system.
- **Canonical formula:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}$ — **never forget the serial term $(1-f)$.**
- **Maximum speedup ceiling:** $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ — reached as $s \to \infty$.
- **$f$** = enhanceable fraction (decimal between 0 and 1). **$s$** = per-component speedup ($\ge 1$). **$S$** = overall speedup ($\ge 1$).
- **Three forms to memorize:** Direct ratio $S = T_{old}/T_{new}$, canonical formula, and rearranged form $f = \dfrac{1 - 1/S}{1 - 1/s}$.
- **The bottleneck rule:** A small serial fraction (e.g., 5%) caps the speedup at $1/0.05 = 20\times$, no matter how many cores.
- **Amdahl vs. Gustafson:** Amdahl assumes *fixed problem size*; Gustafson assumes *scaled problem size*. KTU often asks for a *contrast*.
- **Practical takeaway for architects:** Optimize the *most frequently executed* (common case) path first; minimize serial portions before scaling parallelism.
- **Real-world applications:** multicore scaling, GPU offloading, cache hit-rate analysis, hardware accelerator (TPU/NPU) ROI.
- **Numerical trap:** Students often think "10× speedup of 50% gives 10× overall" — **wrong!** It gives $1/(0.5 + 0.5/10) = 1.818\times$.
- **Board answer format:** Always state given values, write the formula, substitute, compute, and conclude with a *commentary sentence*.
- **Validating extreme cases:** If $f = 0$, then $S = 1$ (no enhancement possible). If $f = 1$, then $S = s$ (fully enhanceable). Use these to verify your derivation.
- **Curve shape:** $S$ vs $s$ is *concave, monotonically increasing*, asymptotically bounded by $1/(1-f)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processor Performance Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Processor Performance Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Processor Performance Equation** (also called the **CPU Performance Equation** or **Iron Law of Processor Performance**) is the fundamental first-principles model that expresses the **execution time of a program** on a given processor as a product of three independent, measurable factors derived from the Instruction Set Architecture (ISA), the micro-architecture, and the underlying CMOS technology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> The Processor Performance Equation is a multiplicative model of the form:
> $$\text{CPU Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI} \times \text{Clock Cycle Time}$$
> It is the canonical starting point for every quantitative analysis performed in Computer Architecture (Hennessy & Patterson tradition, also adopted in the KTU 2024 PECST528 syllabus).

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of CPU Time

1. **Instruction Count (IC):** Total number of machine instructions executed to complete the program. Determined by the **Algorithm**, the **Programming Language**, the **Compiler**, and the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**.
2. **Cycles Per Instruction (CPI):** Average number of clock cycles the processor requires to retire each instruction. Determined by the **CPU Organization (datapath & control)**, the **Micro-architecture**, and the **Memory Hierarchy**.
3. **Clock Cycle Time (T_clk) or Clock Rate (f_clk):** The duration (or reciprocal frequency) of one clock pulse. Determined by the **CMOS Technology node** and the **Critical Path** of the combinational logic.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check (Read this first time students):**
> Imagine a car factory building **100 cars (IC)**. Each car requires on average **3 hours of work (CPI)**, and the factory clock ticks once every **30 minutes (T_clk = 0.5 hr)**.
> Total factory time = 100 cars × 3 hours/car = 300 hours of labor.
> But the clock "sees" this in ticks: 300 hours ÷ 0.5 hours/tick = **600 ticks**.
> If you speed up the clock (smaller T_clk) → fewer ticks per car → faster completion.
> If you redesign the assembly line (lower CPI) → less work per car → faster completion.
> If you order fewer cars (lower IC, e.g. by a smarter algorithm) → fewer total cars → faster completion.
> This is **exactly** the Processor Performance Equation in disguise.

### 1.4 The Performance Consequence

Because the equation is **multiplicative**, a 10% improvement in **any one** of the three factors yields a 10% improvement in total CPU time — assuming the others are held constant. This is the bedrock principle behind every architectural optimization studied in PECST528: pipelining reduces CPI, faster transistors reduce T_clk, and compiler/ISA improvements reduce IC.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Decomposition of the Performance Equation

The three factors in the equation are **not independent in practice** (a phenomenon called the *performance paradox*), but for analysis they are decoupled as follows:

- **Step 1 — Instruction Count (IC):** Fixed by the source code and the compiler. ISA-level decisions (e.g., RISC vs CISC, presence of a MUL instruction vs. software multiply loop) drastically change IC. A CISC `REPMOVSB` may be 1 instruction that does what 50 RISC load/store instructions do, lowering IC.
- **Step 2 — Cycles Per Instruction (CPI):** Measured as a *weighted average* over an instruction mix. If instruction class $i$ has $CPI_i$ and occurs $IC_i$ times, then:
  $$CPI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \cdot CPI_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i}$$
- **Step 3 — Clock Cycle Time (T_clk):** Set by the slowest logic path between two flip-flops (the **critical path**). Pipelining and deeper submicron technologies reduce T_clk.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table (Print This for Exam Day)**

| # | Formula Name | LaTeX Form | Boundary / Units | Key Insight |
|---|--------------|------------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | CPU Time (basic) | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | seconds | The Iron Law |
| 2 | CPU Time (frequency form) | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$ | seconds | $f_{clk} = 1/T_{clk}$ in Hz |
| 3 | MIPS Rating | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{IC}{T_{CPU} \times 10^6} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | millions/sec | Throughput-style metric |
| 4 | Weighted CPI | $CPI = \dfrac{\sum_i IC_i \cdot CPI_i}{IC_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Use instruction mix |
| 5 | Amdahl's Law (speedup) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}$ | dimensionless | $f$ = enhanced fraction, $s$ = speedup of that fraction |
| 6 | Amdahl's Law (overall) | $\text{Speedup}_{overall} = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | dimensionless | Always $< \frac{1}{1-f}$ |
| 7 | Execution Time After Enhancement | $T_{new} = T_{old} \cdot \left[(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{s}\right]$ | seconds | Used in CPU design trade-offs |
| 8 | Clock Rate vs Cycle Time | $f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Hz | Reciprocal relation |
| 9 | Performance Ratio between two machines A and B | $n = \dfrac{T_{CPU,B}}{T_{CPU,A}} = \dfrac{IC_B \cdot CPI_B \cdot T_{B}}{IC_A \cdot CPI_A \cdot T_{A}}$ | dimensionless | $n>1 \Rightarrow$ A is faster |
| 10 | CPI with stalls (pipelined) | $CPI_{actual} = CPI_{base} + \text{Stall cycles per instruction}$ | cycles | Includes memory/data hazards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Break Rule (Markdown Tables):** All absolute value / divisibility signs are written as `\vert` or `\mid`, never the bare pipe character, to keep KTU PDF converters from splitting the table.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Processor Performance Equation is the **primary tool** used in:

- **CPU Design Decisions** — Should Intel/AMD/ARM spend die area on more cache (lowers CPI via fewer stalls) or on a deeper pipeline (lowers T_clk but increases CPI from hazards)?
- **Compiler Engineering** — Should `gcc -O3` prioritize reducing IC (loop unrolling) or reducing CPI (instruction scheduling)?
- **Datacenter Procurement** — Cloud providers (AWS, Azure) use weighted CPI to compare ARM vs x86 performance per watt for specific instruction mixes (e.g. SPECint, SPECfp).
- **Mobile SoC Design** — A 1% T_clk reduction is worth much more in a thermally-throttled phone than a 1% CPI reduction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Basic Performance Equation

**Starting Principle:** Every program is a sequence of machine instructions, and the CPU retires them one clock cycle (or several) at a time.

Let:

- $IC$ = total number of **dynamic** instructions executed (not static instructions in the source file).
- $CPI$ = average number of clock cycles per instruction for the **whole program**.
- $T_{clk}$ = duration of one clock period in seconds.

The total number of clock cycles required is the product of (number of instructions) × (cycles per instruction):

$$\text{Total Clock Cycles} = IC \times CPI$$

Since each clock cycle takes $T_{clk}$ seconds, the wall-clock execution time is:

$$T_{CPU} = (\text{Total Clock Cycles}) \times T_{clk} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$$

Because $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ where $f_{clk}$ is the clock frequency in Hz, we can equivalently write:

$$T_{CPU} = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$$

This is **Derivation 1 (Iron Law of Processor Performance).** Q.E.D.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Weighted CPI

Suppose a program contains $n$ different classes of instructions, with:

- Class $i$ having $IC_i$ instructions in the dynamic mix.
- Class $i$ requiring $CPI_i$ clock cycles per instruction.

The total clock cycles for class $i$ is $IC_i \cdot CPI_i$. The total clock cycles for the whole program is the sum across all classes:

$$\text{Total Clock Cycles} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \cdot CPI_i$$

The total number of instructions is:

$$IC_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i$$

The average CPI is the ratio of total clock cycles to total instructions:

$$\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= \frac{\text{Total Clock Cycles}}{IC_{total}} \\
          &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \cdot CPI_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i}
\end{aligned}$$

This is **Derivation 2 (Weighted Average CPI).** Q.E.D.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Amdahl's Law (for KTU 14-Mark Problems)

**Setup:** An enhancement $E$ improves only a *fraction* $f$ of the original execution time by a factor of $s$. The remaining fraction $(1-f)$ is unaffected.

Let $T_{old}$ be the original execution time. The portion of time that is enhanced is $f \cdot T_{old}$. The portion that is unchanged is $(1-f) \cdot T_{old}$.

After enhancement, the enhanced portion runs $s$ times faster, so its new time is:

$$T_{enhanced,\,new} = \frac{f \cdot T_{old}}{s}$$

The unchanged portion remains $(1-f) \cdot T_{old}$. The new total execution time is:

$$T_{new} = (1-f) \cdot T_{old} + \frac{f \cdot T_{old}}{s}$$

Factor out $T_{old}$:

$$T_{new} = T_{old} \left[ (1-f) + \frac{f}{s} \right]$$

The overall speedup is defined as $S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{old}}{T_{old}\left[(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{s}\right]} \\
  &= \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Asymptotic Behaviour (very important for KTU):** As $s \to \infty$, the term $\dfrac{f}{s} \to 0$, giving the **maximum achievable speedup**:

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1-f}$$

This is **Derivation 3 (Amdahl's Law).** Q.E.D.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Hallmark KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A processor runs at **2 GHz** clock frequency. A program consists of the following dynamic instruction mix:

| Instruction Class | IC (millions) | Base CPI |
|-------------------|---------------|----------|
| ALU / Integer | 200 | 1 |
| Load | 120 | 2 |
| Store | 80 | 2 |
| Branch | 50 | 3 |
| Floating Point | 50 | 4 |

**Compute (i) the weighted CPI, (ii) total CPU time, (iii) MIPS rating, and (iv) the new CPU time if the FP unit is redesigned to be 4× faster and the compiler is rewritten to eliminate 20% of the FP instructions.**

#### (i) Weighted CPI

$$\begin{aligned}
IC_{total} &= 200 + 120 + 80 + 50 + 50 = 500 \text{ million instructions} \\
\sum IC_i \cdot CPI_i &= (200 \times 1) + (120 \times 2) + (80 \times 2) + (50 \times 3) + (50 \times 4) \\
&= 200 + 240 + 160 + 150 + 200 \\
&= 950 \text{ million cycles} \\
CPI_{avg} &= \frac{950}{500} = 1.9 \text{ cycles/instruction}
\end{aligned}$$

#### (ii) Total CPU Time

$$T_{CPU} = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clk}} = \frac{500 \times 10^6 \times 1.9}{2 \times 10^9} = \frac{950 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.475 \text{ seconds}$$

#### (iii) MIPS Rating

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^6} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{1.9 \times 10^6} = 1052.63 \text{ MIPS}$$

#### (iv) Combined Enhancement (FP 4× faster + 20% fewer FP instructions)

First, compute the original FP time fraction $f$. Original FP cycles = 50M × 4 = 200M out of 950M total:

$$f = \frac{200}{950} \approx 0.2105$$

The compiler removes 20% of FP instructions, so effective FP cycles now = 40M × 4 = 160M. The new FP portion's effective speedup over the *remaining* FP portion is bounded by both effects. For board exams we treat them sequentially.

After compiler rewrite: FP IC drops from 50M to 40M, FP cycles drop from 200M to 160M. New total cycles = 950M − 200M + 160M = 910M. CPU time becomes 910M / 2GHz = 0.455 s.

Now apply FP unit 4× speedup on the *new* FP portion (160M cycles):

$$\text{FP cycles after 4× speedup} = \frac{160}{4} = 40 \text{ million cycles}$$

New total cycles = 910M − 160M + 40M = 790M. Final CPU time:

$$T_{new} = \frac{790 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.395 \text{ seconds}$$

Overall speedup:

$$S = \frac{0.475}{0.395} \approx 1.203 \;\text{(about 20.3% faster)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — The Three-Factor Decomposition of CPU Time

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Source Code] --> B[Compiler Frontend]
    B --> C[ISA Selection]
    C --> D[Instruction Count IC]
    E[CPU Datapath Design] --> F[Micro-architecture]
    F --> G[Cycles Per Instruction CPI]
    H[CMOS Technology Node] --> I[Critical Path Analysis]
    I --> J[Clock Cycle Time Tclk]
    D --> K[CPU Time = IC x CPI x Tclk]
    G --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Performance Bottleneck?}
    L -- IC dominated --> M[Optimize Algorithm and Compiler]
    L -- CPI dominated --> N[Pipelining and Cache]
    L -- Tclk dominated --> O[Deeper Submicron and Better Logic]
    M --> P[Faster Execution]
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Amdahl's Law Geometric Intuition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Execution Time T_old] --> B[Fraction f Enhanced]
    A --> C[Fraction 1-f Unchanged]
    B --> D[Speedup Factor s Applied]
    D --> E[New Time f x T_old / s]
    C --> F[Unchanged Time 1-f x T_old]
    E --> G[New Total T_new]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Overall Speedup S = T_old / T_new]
    H --> I{S approaches infinity?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Maximum Speedup = 1 divided by 1-f]
    I -- No --> K[Compute S = 1 divided by 1-f + f over s]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Instruction Mix → Weighted CPI Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[ALU Instruction] --> M1[IC1 count x CPI1 cycles]
    A2[Load Instruction] --> M2[IC2 count x CPI2 cycles]
    A3[Store Instruction] --> M3[IC3 count x CPI3 cycles]
    A4[Branch Instruction] --> M4[IC4 count x CPI4 cycles]
    A5[FP Instruction] --> M5[IC5 count x CPI5 cycles]
    M1 --> S[Sum of ICi x CPIi]
    M2 --> S
    M3 --> S
    M4 --> S
    M5 --> S
    S --> D[Divide by total IC]
    D --> R[Weighted Average CPI]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Safety Note:** All Mermaid node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g. `A1`, `M1`, `S`, `D`, `R`) and contain no reserved keywords or markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels, complying with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
**State and explain the Processor Performance Equation. Mention the three factors on which the CPU execution time depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — full marks distribution):**

The Processor Performance Equation expresses CPU execution time as:

$$T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$$

The three factors are:
1. **Instruction Count (IC)** — Total dynamic instructions executed; determined by algorithm, compiler, and ISA.
2. **Cycles Per Instruction (CPI)** — Average clock cycles per instruction; determined by CPU micro-architecture.
3. **Clock Cycle Time (T_clk)** — Duration of one clock tick; determined by CMOS technology and critical path.

Since the three factors are **multiplicative**, an improvement in any one factor (with the others held constant) directly improves CPU performance.

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]
**Define Amdahl's Law. What is the maximum speedup achievable when 30% of the execution time can be enhanced?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Amdahl's Law gives the overall speedup $S$ when a fraction $f$ of execution time is improved by a factor $s$:

$$S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}$$

For $f = 0.30$, the asymptotic maximum speedup (as $s \to \infty$) is:

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1-0.30} = \frac{1}{0.70} \approx 1.4286$$

Therefore, **no matter how aggressively the 30% portion is enhanced, the overall program can be at most 1.4286× (≈ 42.86%) faster**. This illustrates the diminishing-returns principle.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often write $T_{CPU} = IC / (CPI \times f_{clk})$ — **wrong.** It is $IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ or equivalently $IC \times CPI / f_{clk}$.
> - In Amdahl's Law problems, many students forget to convert percentage $f$ into a **decimal** (e.g. use 30 instead of 0.30). This single slip loses 2–3 marks.
> - When computing MIPS, the unit is *millions* of instructions per second. Missing the $10^6$ factor is a common 1-mark deduction.
> - Board examiners expect **unit annotations** in every numerical answer. Always state seconds, Hz, or cycles explicitly.

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]

**a)** A processor runs at **3 GHz**. For a given program, **40% of the instructions are loads/stores**, **20% are branches**, and the **remaining 40% are ALU/Integer**. The CPI of each class is: load/store = 3, branch = 4, ALU = 1. Compute the **weighted average CPI**, the **total CPU time** for a program of **200 million instructions**, and the **MIPS rating** of the processor. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Using **Amdahl's Law**, analyze what happens if a designer adds a **cache** that makes memory accesses **5 times faster**, and the original program spent **25% of its time in memory accesses**. What is the **overall speedup**? What if the cache made memory **infinitely fast**? **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Weighted CPI, CPU Time, MIPS:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= \frac{IC_1 \cdot CPI_1 + IC_2 \cdot CPI_2 + IC_3 \cdot CPI_3}{IC_{total}} \\
          &= \frac{(0.40 \times 200 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 200 \times 4) + (0.40 \times 200 \times 1)}{200} \\
          &= \frac{240 + 160 + 80}{200} = \frac{480}{200} = 2.4 \text{ cycles/instruction}
\end{aligned}$$

Total CPU time:

$$T_{CPU} = \frac{IC \times CPI_{avg}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{200 \times 10^6 \times 2.4}{3 \times 10^9} = 0.16 \text{ seconds}$$

MIPS rating:

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{2.4 \times 10^6} = 1250 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Part (b) — Amdahl's Law with 5× faster memory and 25% fraction:**

$$S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{s}} = \frac{1}{(1-0.25) + \dfrac{0.25}{5}} = \frac{1}{0.75 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.80} = 1.25$$

So the program is **1.25× faster** (25% time reduction).

For **infinitely fast** memory ($s \to \infty$):

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1-0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} \approx 1.3333$$

So even with infinite memory speedup, the program can be at most **1.333× faster** — only an additional 6.67% improvement beyond the 5× case. This demonstrates Amdahl's diminishing-returns principle: the un-enhanced 75% of the code becomes the bottleneck.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]

**a)** A program has a dynamic instruction count of **1 billion**, runs on a processor with a **clock rate of 2.5 GHz** and an **average CPI of 2.0**. Compute the CPU execution time. Now, a design team proposes two alternatives:
- **Alternative 1:** Reduce CPI by 20% (better pipelining) but increase clock cycle time by 10%.
- **Alternative 2:** Reduce instruction count by 30% (better compiler) but increase CPI by 15%.

Which alternative gives the **lowest CPU time**? Show full calculation. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A system spends **60% of its time** in a parallelizable portion and **40% in sequential** code. By **Amdahl's Law**, what is the maximum speedup achievable if you add an **infinite number of processors**? If instead you use only **4 processors** for the parallel portion, what is the achievable speedup? **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Baseline and Two Alternatives:**

Baseline:

$$T_{base} = \frac{10^9 \times 2.0}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.8 \text{ seconds}$$

**Alternative 1** (CPI × 0.8, T_clk × 1.10):

$$T_{alt1} = \frac{10^9 \times (2.0 \times 0.8)}{2.5 \times 10^9 / 1.10} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^9}{2.2727 \times 10^9} \approx 0.704 \text{ seconds}$$

**Alternative 2** (IC × 0.7, CPI × 1.15):

$$T_{alt2} = \frac{(10^9 \times 0.7) \times (2.0 \times 1.15)}{2.5 \times 10^9} = \frac{0.7 \times 10^9 \times 2.3}{2.5 \times 10^9} = \frac{1.61}{2.5} = 0.644 \text{ seconds}$$

**Comparison:** $T_{alt2}$ (0.644 s) < $T_{alt1}$ (0.704 s) < $T_{base}$ (0.8 s).

**Verdict:** **Alternative 2** is best, with a 19.5% speedup over baseline. Even though its CPI worsened, the 30% IC reduction dominated the calculation because IC is multiplied by both CPI and T_clk.

**Part (b) — Amdahl's Law for Multiprocessing:**

In Amdahl's Law, the parallelizable fraction $f = 0.60$ gets speeded up by the number of processors $p$. The maximum speedup with infinite processors:

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1-0.60} = \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.5$$

So even with infinite processors, the program is at most 2.5× faster because the 40% sequential portion is a hard ceiling.

With $p = 4$ processors:

$$S = \frac{1}{(1-0.60) + \dfrac{0.60}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.40 + 0.15} = \frac{1}{0.55} \approx 1.818$$

So 4 processors give a **1.818× speedup**, and the gap to the 2.5× ceiling is 0.682× — an incentive to reduce the sequential fraction rather than add more cores.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam)**

- ✅ **Iron Law of Performance:** $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ — must be memorized in both forms (with $T_{clk}$ and with $f_{clk}$).
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** IC (algorithm + compiler + ISA) | CPI (micro-architecture) | $T_{clk}$ (technology).
- ✅ **Multiplicative nature** → 10% improvement in any one factor → 10% faster program.
- ✅ **Weighted CPI formula:** $CPI = \dfrac{\sum IC_i \cdot CPI_i}{IC_{total}}$ — must include the *total* IC in the denominator, not the number of instruction classes.
- ✅ **MIPS = $f_{clk} / (CPI \times 10^6)$** — remember the $10^6$ unit conversion; forgetting it is a 1-mark loss.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/s}$ — convert percentages to decimals; the bottleneck is the un-enhanced $(1-f)$ portion.
- ✅ **Maximum Amdahl speedup** occurs at $s \to \infty$: $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ — this is the ceiling that no engineering effort can break.
- ✅ **Multiprocessor corollary:** $f$ = parallelizable fraction, $s$ = number of processors $p$. The sequential portion is the hard limit.
- ✅ **Unit hygiene:** always annotate seconds, Hz, MIPS, or cycles explicitly in numerical answers.
- ✅ **Cross-check direction:** if $T_{new} < T_{old}$, then speedup $S > 1$. If $S < 1$, you have *slowed down* the system — re-check your arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Activity: Beyond the Books - Visit www.spec.org, explore High Performance Computing benchmarks, compare vendor results for the same benchmark, appreciate the need for benchmarks, learn about retired benchmarks, and write/publish a paper based on a retired benchmark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌐 Activity: Beyond the Books — A Field Trip to SPEC.org

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note (PECST528 / Module 1):** This is an *Activity-Based Learning (ABL)* task mapped to **CO1 / CO2** of the *Advanced Computer Architecture* syllabus. The goal is to move beyond textbook theory and engage with the *real, living standard* by which modern computer architectures (CPUs, GPUs, HPC clusters) are measured: the **Standard Performance Evaluation Corporation (SPEC)** benchmarks.

---

## 1. What Exactly is SPEC.org?

The **Standard Performance Evaluation Corporation (SPEC)** is a non-profit consortium founded in **1988** that develops and maintains standardised **benchmarks** for evaluating computer system performance, power efficiency, and cloud/virtualisation throughput. It is the de-facto industry standard for fair, reproducible, vendor-neutral performance comparison.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board Definition):**
> *SPEC is a vendor-neutral, consensus-based organisation that produces benchmark suites designed to measure and compare the performance of computer systems across CPU, HPC, Java, virtualisation, storage, and power-efficiency workloads using real-world application programs.*

The official website is **<https://www.spec.org>**, where every published result is accompanied by:
- The full **hardware disclosure** (CPU model, cores, frequency, cache, memory, OS, compiler flags).
- The full **software disclosure** (compiler version, optimisation flags, libraries, kernel tunables).
- A **raw, audited disclosure** so that any third party can reproduce the run.

---

## 2. Conceptual Analogy — The "Standardised Cooking Competition"

Imagine two chefs claim their ovens bake the best cake. If one uses a chocolate cake and the other uses a soufflé, you can never fairly judge. **SPEC is the rulebook** that forces both ovens to bake the **same 40+ identical recipes (benchmarks)** under **identical conditions**, with **timings, temperatures, and ingredients fully disclosed**.

| Real-world analogy | SPEC equivalent |
|---|---|
| The "Best Bakery" contest | The **SPEC benchmark suite** |
| Identical recipe given to every baker | A **workload** (e.g., `505.mcf_r`, `549.fotonik3d_r`) |
| Fully disclosed oven settings | **Disclosure** of HW/SW configuration |
| A scoreboard with normalised scores | **SPECratio / SPECspeed / SPECpower** metrics |
| Old, discontinued contests | **Retired benchmarks** (e.g., CPU95, CPU2000) |

---

## 3. The Big Question This Activity Answers

> *Why can't we just compare two systems by running a single program on them?*

Because:
1. **Workload bias** — A benchmark that favours vectorised FP will mislead on a database workload.
2. **Hardware bias** — Without disclosure, vendor "optimisations" can game the test.
3. **Time bias** — A benchmark from 1995 cannot reflect modern pipelines, prefetchers, or chiplets.
4. **Power/Energy bias** — A fast but 1000 W system is not necessarily better in a data centre.

This activity walks you through **applying**, **comparing**, **critiquing**, and **publishing** in the SPEC ecosystem.

---

## 4. Taxonomy of SPEC Benchmarks You Will Encounter

When you open www.spec.org, the homepage organises benchmarks into families. You must explore each family.

### 4.1 CPU Sub-systems
- **SPEC CPU 2017** — The current flagship integer + floating-point suite (43 workloads, derived from real C/C++/Fortran programs).
- **SPEC CPU 2006** — The previous generation, still widely cited.
- **SPECrate 2017** — Throughput-oriented (many concurrent copies).
- **SPECspeed 2017** — Single-task turnaround time.

### 4.2 HPC / Parallel Benchmarks (the heart of this activity)
- **SPEC MPI 2007 / SPEC MPI 2021** — Distributed-memory HPC clusters using MPI.
- **SPEC OMP 2012 / SPEC OMP L** — Shared-memory multi-threaded workloads.
- **SPEC HPC 2021** — Full-scale scientific HPC suites (MPI + OpenMP + accelerators).
- **SPEC ACCEL** — OpenACC / OpenCL / SYCL accelerator (GPU) benchmarks.

### 4.3 Power, Java, Virtualisation, Storage
- **SPECpower_ssj2008** — Server power/performance.
- **SPECjbb, SPECjEnterprise** — Java middleware.
- **SPECvirt_sc2013** — Server virtualisation.
- **SPEC SFS 2014** — Network file server throughput.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield for Module 1:** KTU examiners often ask *"List any 4 SPEC benchmark suites"* or *"Differentiate SPECspeed and SPECrate"*. Memorise the families above.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis — How SPEC Works Under the Hood

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is an "Activity" topic, the *theory of benchmark metrics* is fully examinable. The mathematics of SPEC scoring is a common 3-mark and 7-mark question.

---

## 1. The Core Metric: SPECratio

Every SPEC workload is run on a **Reference Machine** (a fixed, well-characterised baseline system) and on the **System Under Test (SUT)**. The workload is run with multiple **copies** for throughput metrics, or a single copy for speed metrics.

The fundamental building block is the **SPECratio**:

$$\text{SPECratio}_i = \frac{T_{\text{ref},i}}{T_{\text{sut},i}}$$

where:
- $T_{\text{ref},i}$ = elapsed time of workload $i$ on the reference machine (seconds).
- $T_{\text{sut},i}$ = elapsed time of workload $i$ on the SUT (seconds).
- A SPECratio of **10.0** means the SUT is **10× faster** than the reference on that workload.

The SUT's overall score is **not** the arithmetic mean. SPEC uses the **Geometric Mean (GM)** to prevent a single outlier from dominating:

$$GM = \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{SPECratio}_i\right)^{1/n}$$

Equivalently, in log form (which is how SPEC actually computes it for numerical stability):

$$\log(GM) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log(\text{SPECratio}_i)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Using the *arithmetic* mean of SPECratios. KTU valuation strictly requires the *geometric* mean. Many students lose 2 marks here.

---

## 2. Speed vs Rate — A Subtle but Critical Distinction

| Metric | Workload Copies | What it measures | KTU one-liner |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SPECspeed** | One copy per run, single-threaded | **Turnaround time** of a single task | "How fast is one job?" |
| **SPECrate** | $N$ concurrent copies (one per core) | **Throughput** — jobs completed per unit time | "How many jobs per hour?" |

For SPECrate 2017 Integer, the metric is:

$$\text{SPECrate2017\_int} = GM\left(\frac{N \times T_{\text{ref},i}}{T_{\text{sut},i}}\right)$$

The factor $N$ accounts for the fact that $N$ copies ran in parallel, so the per-job time is $T_{\text{sut},i} / N$.

---

## 3. The SPEC Power Formula

For **SPECpower_ssj2008**, performance is normalised by power. The composite metric is:

$$\text{ssj\_ops}/\text{Watt} = \frac{\sum_{j} \text{ssj\_ops}@\,\text{load}_j \times \text{load}_j}{\sum_{j} \text{Power}_j \times \text{load}_j}$$

where $j$ indexes the ten load levels (10%, 20%, … 100%). This single number captures *energy proportionality*.

---

## 4. The Vendor Comparison Logic

When you compare two vendor results, you normalise both against the same reference and examine:

1. **Per-workload SPECratios** — which workloads favour which vendor.
2. **Composite GM** — overall winner.
3. **Performance-per-Watt** — if applicable.
4. **Performance-per-Dollar** — if the vendor disclosed price.
5. **Compiler/OS stack** — sometimes a "win" is a compiler flag, not a silicon win.

---

## 5. The KTU Formula Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Where it appears in the syllabus |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SPECratio}_i = T_{\text{ref}} / T_{\text{sut}}$ | Per-workload normalised speed | CPU benchmarks |
| 2 | $GM = \left(\prod \text{SPECratio}_i\right)^{1/n}$ | Composite score | All SPEC suites |
| 3 | $\log GM = \frac{1}{n}\sum \log(\text{SPECratio}_i)$ | Computationally stable form | All SPEC suites |
| 4 | $\text{SPECrate} = GM(N \cdot T_{\text{ref}} / T_{\text{sut}})$ | Throughput metric | SPECrate 2017 |
| 5 | $\text{ssj\_ops/Watt} = \frac{\sum \text{ssj\_ops} \cdot L}{\sum P \cdot L}$ | Energy efficiency | SPECpower |
| 6 | $\text{Speedup}_i = T_{\text{base}} / T_{\text{optimised}}$ | Architectural improvement | Amdahl + SPEC together |

> [!TIP]
> Real-world utility: Every cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) and every HPC procurement (Top500, NASA, ISRO) ultimately uses these normalised SPEC-style ratios to make million-dollar procurement decisions. A 5 % SPECratio improvement on a benchmark that mirrors your workload can translate into 5 % more revenue per rack.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Worked Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through three KTU-style numerical problems based on SPEC scoring. Every algebraic step is shown.

---

## 📘 Problem 1 — Computing the Geometric Mean of SPECratios

**Question.** A system runs four SPEC CPU 2017 integer workloads. The SPECratios obtained are **8.20, 9.50, 7.30, 10.40**. Compute the composite SPECscore2017_int using the geometric mean.

### Step 1 — Write down the GM definition

$$GM = \left(\prod_{i=1}^{4} r_i\right)^{1/4}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the four ratios

$$GM = (8.20 \times 9.50 \times 7.30 \times 10.40)^{1/4}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the product

$$8.20 \times 9.50 = 77.90$$

$$77.90 \times 7.30 = 568.67$$

$$568.67 \times 10.40 = 5914.17$$

### Step 4 — Take the fourth root

$$GM = (5914.17)^{1/4}$$

$$GM = \sqrt{\sqrt{5914.17}} = \sqrt{76.90} = 8.77$$

### ✅ Final Answer
$$\boxed{\text{SPECspeed2017\_int\_base} \approx 8.77}$$

### Step 5 — Verification using logarithms (this is how SPEC actually computes it)

$$\log_{10} 8.20 = 0.9138$$
$$\log_{10} 9.50 = 0.9777$$
$$\log_{10} 7.30 = 0.8633$$
$$\log_{10} 10.40 = 1.0170$$

Sum = $3.7718$. Mean = $3.7718 / 4 = 0.9430$.

$$GM = 10^{0.9430} = 8.77 \;\; ✅$$

The two methods agree to two decimal places.

---

## 📘 Problem 2 — SPECrate Throughput Computation

**Question.** A dual-socket server with 64 physical cores runs SPECrate 2017 Integer. The reference time for workload `505.mcf_r` is $T_{\text{ref}} = 1700$ s. The elapsed wall-clock time of all 64 parallel copies on the SUT is $T_{\text{sut}} = 180$ s. Find the SPECratio for `505.mcf_r`.

### Step 1 — Identify the formula

$$\text{SPECratio}_{505.\text{mcf\_r}} = \frac{N \times T_{\text{ref}}}{T_{\text{sut}}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$= \frac{64 \times 1700}{180}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the numerator

$$64 \times 1700 = 108{,}800$$

### Step 4 — Divide

$$= 108{,}800 / 180 = 604.44$$

### ✅ Final Answer
$$\boxed{\text{SPECratio}_{505.\text{mcf\_r}} = 604.4}$$

**Interpretation:** Running 64 copies in parallel, the SUT completes each copy 604× faster than the single-threaded reference would have completed one copy.

---

## 📘 Problem 3 — SPECpower_ssj2008 Composite (3 Load Levels Shown)

**Question.** A server reports the following data. Compute its `ssj_ops/Watt` (using the three shown load levels as illustration; in a real submission there are 10 levels).

| Load level $L$ | ssj_ops | Power (W) |
|---|---|---|
| 30 % | 350 000 | 220 |
| 70 % | 780 000 | 410 |
| 100 % | 1 050 000 | 580 |

### Step 1 — Write the SPECpower formula

$$\text{ssj\_ops}/\text{W} = \frac{\sum_j (\text{ssj\_ops}_j \cdot L_j)}{\sum_j (P_j \cdot L_j)}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the numerator

$$N = (350{,}000 \times 0.30) + (780{,}000 \times 0.70) + (1{,}050{,}000 \times 1.00)$$

$$N = 105{,}000 + 546{,}000 + 1{,}050{,}000 = 1{,}701{,}000$$

### Step 3 — Compute the denominator

$$D = (220 \times 0.30) + (410 \times 0.70) + (580 \times 1.00)$$

$$D = 66 + 287 + 580 = 933$$

### Step 4 — Compute the ratio

$$\text{ssj\_ops}/\text{W} = 1{,}701{,}000 / 933 = 1823$$

### ✅ Final Answer
$$\boxed{\text{ssj\_ops/Watt} \approx 1823}$$

### Step 5 — Real-world interpretation

A higher `ssj_ops/W` is always better. Modern top-tier servers exceed **15 000 ssj_ops/W** (e.g., HPE ProLiant, Fujitsu RX). A 2010-era server would be in the 1 000–3 000 range. The 3 orders of magnitude growth reflects architectural advances — clock gating, DVFS, big.LITTLE-style cores, and deep idle states.

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Mapping SPEC Families to Architectural Lessons

| SPEC Suite | Architectural Concept Tested | Module 1 / Module 2 Link |
|---|---|---|
| SPEC CPU 2017 (Integer) | IPC, branch prediction, cache | M1: Pipelining, ILP |
| SPEC CPU 2017 (FP) | Vector units, FMA, AVX-512 | M1: SIMD, DLP |
| SPEC MPI 2007 / 2021 | Interconnect, MPI overhead | M2: Memory hierarchy, NUMA |
| SPEC OMP 2012 | Threading, synchronisation | M2: Multi-core, cache coherence |
| SPEC HPC 2021 | Hybrid MPI+OpenMP+GPU | M3: GPU, heterogeneous |
| SPECpower_ssj2008 | Energy proportionality, DVFS | M2: Power-aware design |
| SPEC ACCEL | OpenACC/OpenCL compilers | M3: Accelerator programming |

This matrix is exactly how to answer the KTU question *"Which SPEC benchmark best evaluates the cache performance of a processor? Justify."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> These Mermaid diagrams render the **methodology**, the **vendor-comparison workflow**, and the **retired-benchmark timeline** you must internalise for the activity paper.

## 📊 Diagram 1 — The SPEC Benchmark Methodology (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference Machine Configuration] --> B[Define 43 Workloads C Cpp Fortran]
    B --> C[Port to Target SUT]
    C --> D[Apply Compiler Flags - Disclosure]
    D --> E[Run n Copies on SUT]
    E --> F[Measure Elapsed Time T_sut]
    A --> G[Measure T_ref on Reference]
    F --> H[Compute SPECratio = T_ref divided by T_sut]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Take Geometric Mean over all workloads]
    I --> J[Publish Final SPECscore with full HW SW Disclosure on spec.org]
    J --> K[Auditable Peer Review by SPEC]
    K --> L[Result Live on spec.org Results Page]
```

```mermaid
    B --> D[Filter by Vendor B e.g. AMD]

    D --> F[Extract SPECspeed and SPECratio per workload]

    F --> G
    G --> H[Tabulate 20 Plus Workloads Side by Side]
    H --> I[Identify Workloads Vendor A wins]
    H --> J[Identify Workloads Vendor B wins]
    I --> K[Correlate Wins to Architecture e.g. AVX 512 vs wider issue]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute Composite GM for Both]
    L --> M[Write Comparative Section of Paper]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Activity hint for the paper:** SPEC CPU 2000 and SPEC CPU 95 are *retired*. They are perfect substrates for a short paper because (a) all results are frozen, (b) the workloads are small enough to fully analyse, and (c) you can study *why* SPEC retired them (e.g., the CINT2000 `176.gcc` workload fits in 2 MB cache of a 2000-era CPU but 2 KB of a modern one — completely unrepresentative).
