# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks, direct), Part B (14 marks, internal choice with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks). Each carries a simulated past-year tag.

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**List any four SPEC benchmark suites and state the metric each one measures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **SPEC CPU 2017** — Measures single-threaded **integer and floating-point** performance using 43 workloads. Composite metric: **SPECspeed 2017** and **SPECrate 2017**.
2. **SPEC MPI 2007 / 2021** — Measures **distributed-memory HPC** performance using MPI. Metric: **SPEC MPI2007/2021 score**.
3. **SPEC OMP 2012** — Measures **shared-memory multi-threaded** performance using OpenMP. Metric: **SPEC OMP2012 score**.
4. **SPECpower_ssj2008** — Measures **server-side energy efficiency** (Java business workload at 10 load levels). Metric: **ssj_ops/Watt**.
5. *(Optional 5th)* **SPECjEnterprise / SPECjbb** — Java application-server performance.

*(Full 3 marks require naming suite + metric clearly. 1 mark per correct pair.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample QP]`
**Why does SPEC use the *geometric mean* rather than the *arithmetic mean* to compute composite scores?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The **arithmetic mean** of ratios is dominated by a single outlier workload, masking the behaviour of the rest. (1 mark)
2. The **geometric mean** treats high and low ratios **symmetrically in log space** — a 10× improvement on one workload and a 10× degradation on another both shift the GM by the same amount in opposite directions. (1 mark)
3. The GM equals the **exponential of the mean of the logs**, which corresponds to a **fair multiplicative aggregation** — i.e., if every workload gets the same proportional speedup $k$, the GM also reflects exactly $k$. The arithmetic mean fails this property. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> This question appears almost every year. Memorise the **three properties**: (i) outlier resistance, (ii) multiplicative symmetry, (iii) consistency under uniform speedup.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full (14 marks). Each is split into part (a) for 7 marks and part (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Sample QP]`

#### (a) `[7 marks]`
**Explain the architecture of a SPEC benchmark submission in detail. Include the role of the reference machine, the disclosure requirement, and the SPECratio computation. Why is full disclosure considered the cornerstone of SPEC's credibility?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Reference machine (2 marks):** A fixed, peer-reviewed system whose timings $T_{\text{ref},i}$ form the denominator of every SPECratio. The reference machine has not changed in years for a given suite, so all results are comparable across years.

2. **System Under Test (SUT) and disclosure (3 marks):** The vendor (or any submitter) runs the same workload binaries on their hardware and reports the wall-clock time $T_{\text{sut},i}$. Crucially, the **entire software stack** — compiler version, optimisation flags, OS, kernel tunables, memory configuration, frequency settings — is **publicly disclosed**. This is what stops a vendor from "cheating" by tuning for the benchmark.

3. **SPECratio and GM (1 mark):** $\text{SPECratio}_i = T_{\text{ref},i} / T_{\text{sut},i}$, and the composite is the geometric mean over all workloads.

4. **Why disclosure matters (1 mark):** Without disclosure, results would be unfalsifiable marketing claims. The full disclosure means any independent researcher can challenge or reproduce a result — this is what gives SPEC its scientific credibility.

#### (b) `[7 marks]`
**A server reports the following SPECrate 2017 Integer results. Compute the composite SPECrate2017_int score.**

| Workload | $T_{\text{ref}}$ (s) | $T_{\text{sut}}$ (s) with $N=32$ copies |
|---|---|---|
| 500.perlbench_r | 1800 | 240 |
| 502.gcc_r | 1900 | 280 |
| 505.mcf_r | 1700 | 220 |
| 520.omnetpp_r | 2000 | 300 |
| 523.xalancbmk_r | 1750 | 250 |
| 525.x264_r | 1850 | 270 |
| 531.deepsjeng_r | 1950 | 290 |
| 541.leela_r | 1880 | 260 |
| 548.exchange2_r | 1920 | 275 |
| 557.xz_r | 2000 | 310 |

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the per-workload SPECratio using $N \cdot T_{\text{ref}} / T_{\text{sut}}$.** (3 marks)

| Workload | $N \times T_{\text{ref}}$ | $\div T_{\text{sut}}$ | SPECratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| 500.perlbench_r | 32 × 1800 = 57 600 | 57 600 / 240 | 240.00 |
| 502.gcc_r | 32 × 1900 = 60 800 | 60 800 / 280 | 217.14 |
| 505.mcf_r | 32 × 1700 = 54 400 | 54 400 / 220 | 247.27 |
| 520.omnetpp_r | 32 × 2000 = 64 000 | 64 000 / 300 | 213.33 |
| 523.xalancbmk_r | 32 × 1750 = 56 000 | 56 000 / 250 | 224.00 |
| 525.x264_r | 32 × 1850 = 59 200 | 59 200 / 270 | 219.26 |
| 531.deepsjeng_r | 32 × 1950 = 62 400 | 62 400 / 290 | 215.17 |
| 541.leela_r | 32 × 1880 = 60 160 | 60 160 / 260 | 231.38 |
| 548.exchange2_r | 32 × 1920 = 61 440 | 61 440 / 275 | 223.42 |
| 557.xz_r | 32 × 2000 = 64 000 | 64 000 / 310 | 206.45 |

**Step 2 — Take the geometric mean of the 10 SPECratios.** (2 marks)

$$\prod r_i = 240 \times 217.14 \times 247.27 \times 213.33 \times 224.00 \times 219.26 \times 215.17 \times 231.38 \times 223.42 \times 206.45$$

Computing in log form:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{10} \log_{10} r_i = 7.5602$$

$$\log_{10} GM = 0.75602$$

$$GM = 10^{0.75602} \approx 5.704$$

**Step 3 — Final answer with units.** (1 mark)

$$\boxed{\text{SPECrate2017\_int} \approx 5.70}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** (1 mark)
A composite rate of 5.70 means that running 32 copies in parallel, the SUT completes each workload on average ~5.7× faster than the reference per core-time unit. Modern top-of-the-line dual-socket EPYC/Xeon systems achieve **SPECrate2017_int > 100**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Sample QP]`

#### (a) `[7 marks]`
**What are *retired benchmarks* in the context of SPEC? Give two examples. Why does SPEC retire benchmarks, and what unique research opportunities do retired benchmarks offer to a student writing a paper?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** A retired benchmark is one that SPEC has formally **withdrawn from active submission** — no new results are accepted. The full historical result set, however, is preserved on spec.org in the *Results Archive*.

2. **Two examples (1 mark):**
   - **SPEC CPU 2000** — retired in 2018. Includes `164.gzip`, `176.gcc`, `197.parser`, `253.perlbmk`, `300.twolf`, `403.gcc`, etc.
   - **SPEC CPU 95** — retired in 2000. Includes `099.go`, `124.m88ksim`, `129.compress`, `130.li`, `132.ijpeg`, `134.perl`, `147.vortex`, etc.
   - Other valid examples: SPEC HPC 2002, SPEC SFS 97, SPECjbb 2005.

3. **Reasons for retirement (2 marks):**
   - **Workload size stagnation** — `176.gcc`'s working set is ~2 MB; modern LLCs are 100+ MB, so the benchmark no longer stresses the memory hierarchy.
   - **Compiler saturation** — modern compilers can vectorise and constant-fold large portions, so the original "honest" C semantics are lost.
   - **Lack of modern APIs** — older benchmarks lack threading, OpenMP, SIMD intrinsics.
   - **Industry irrelevance** — workloads no longer representative of cloud/HPC/AI.

4. **Research opportunities (3 marks):**
   - **Reproducibility:** A retired benchmark is *frozen* — no new tuning is possible, so a student paper can be definitive.
   - **Architectural archaeology:** You can study how *one* benchmark evolved in its measured score across hardware generations (e.g., plot `176.gcc` SPECratio on every Intel/AMD chip from 2000 to 2024).
   - **Methodology critique:** A student paper can argue *why* SPEC made the right (or wrong) retirement decision — this is a publishable, peer-reviewed insight.
   - **Easy reproduction:** The workloads are small, fast, and run on any laptop, making a 6-month M.Tech/B.Tech project feasible.

#### (b) `[7 marks]`
**You are writing a short paper titled *"A Retrospective on SPEC CPU 2000: An Eight-Generation Architectural Case Study"*. Outline a complete paper structure (six sections with sub-points) and explain which three SPEC results you would pull from the spec.org archive to make the paper compelling.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Section structure (4 marks):**

1. **Abstract (0.5):** 150-word summary — what SPEC CPU 2000 is, what generations you covered, your headline finding.
2. **Introduction (0.5):** Why a 24-year-old benchmark still matters — pedagogy, reproducibility, and the "memory-wall lesson".
3. **Background on SPEC CPU 2000 (0.5):** The 12 integer + 14 floating-point workloads, the reference machine (UltraSPARC-II 296 MHz), the original SPECratio definition.
4. **Methodology (1):** Pick three workloads, run them on a modern GCC + modern Intel/AMD machine (with disclosure!), then compare to the original spec.org result. The modern score is your *delta* against the historical one.
5. **Results and Per-Workload Analysis (1):** Table of SPECratio across generations (e.g., Pentium 4 vs Core 2 vs Nehalem vs Skylake vs Zen 4).
6. **Discussion: Why SPEC Retired It in 2018 (0.5):** Argue that the working sets no longer stress the cache, that compilers now auto-vectorise `181.mcf`'s pointer chasing, etc.

**Three SPEC results to pull (3 marks):**

1. **The 2000-era original `176.gcc` result on an UltraSPARC-II 296 MHz** — the *reference* result. Pull it from https://www.spec.org/cpu2000/results/. This is your baseline.
2. **The peak historical result for `176.gcc` on a high-end 2010-era Westmere-EX** — establishes the "ceiling" of what a tuned-out-of-production chip could do.
3. **A modern 2023 result for `176.gcc` on an AMD EPYC 9654 (Zen 4) submitted by AMD or an OEM** — establishes the present-day score. The 1000×–5000× improvement over 23 years is a publishable observation.

**Bonus (in any of the 3):** Quote SPEC's official 2018 retirement announcement (https://www.spec.org/cpu2000/) and reference it.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this activity-based module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SPECspeed with SPECrate (–2 marks).** SPECspeed is *one copy, single-threaded turnaround*; SPECrate is *N copies, throughput*. Mixing them up on the same SUT is a fatal error.
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean instead of geometric mean (–2 marks).** If a workload has SPECratio = 1000 and another has 0.1, the arithmetic mean is 500.05, but the geometric mean is 10. The GM correctly reflects the *geometric symmetry*.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by $N$ in SPECrate (–1 mark).** Students often write $\text{SPECratio} = T_{\text{ref}}/T_{\text{sut}}$ even for SPECrate submissions, missing the $N$ factor.
> 4. **Citing "www.spec.org" without specifying the suite (–1 mark).** Always say *"SPEC CPU 2017 Integer, found at https://www.spec.org/cpu2017/results/"* — the URL alone is not specific.
> 5. **Skipping the disclosure discussion in vendor comparisons (–2 marks).** If you compare Intel vs AMD on a benchmark, you *must* mention the compiler, OS, and frequency — otherwise the comparison is invalid.
> 6. **Listing retired benchmarks as "currently active" (–1 mark).** SPEC CPU 2000 is **retired**. SPEC CPU 95 is **retired**. SPEC CPU 2017 is **active**. State the status.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> Use this checklist as your **last-night revision** for any question from Module 1 that touches benchmarking.

- [ ] **SPEC** = Standard Performance Evaluation Corporation, founded 1988, vendor-neutral, <https://www.spec.org>.
- [ ] **SPECratio** = $T_{\text{ref}} / T_{\text{sut}}$ — the atomic metric of every SPEC suite.
- [ ] **Composite score** = **Geometric Mean** of all SPECratios — never arithmetic.
- [ ] **SPECspeed** = single-copy turnaround (latency). **SPECrate** = $N$-copy throughput.
- [ ] **SPECrate formula** = $GM(N \cdot T_{\text{ref}} / T_{\text{sut}})$.
- [ ] **SPECpower** = `ssj_ops / Watt` averaged over 10 load levels.
- [ ] **Active suites** (2024): SPEC CPU 2017, SPEC MPI 2021, SPEC OMP 2012, SPEC ACCEL, SPECpower_ssj2008, SPECjbb 2015, SPECjEnterprise 2018, SPECvirt_sc2013, SPEC SFS 2014.
- [ ] **Retired suites**: SPEC CPU 2000 (retired 2018), SPEC CPU 95 (retired 2000), SPEC HPC 2002, SPEC SFS 97, SPECjbb 2005, SPECjAppServer 2004.
- [ ] **Why retire?** Workload size stagnation, compiler over-optimisation, loss of representativeness.
- [ ] **Why full disclosure matters:** It is the *only* mechanism that prevents vendor gaming and makes results auditable.
- [ ] **Vendor comparison workflow:** Same suite → same workloads → same reference → tabulate per-workload SPECratios → correlate wins to architectural features (cache size, AVX-512, frequency, compiler).
- [ ] **Paper structure (retired benchmark):** Abstract → Intro → Background → Methodology → Results → Discussion → Conclusion → References. Use the *frozen* result set on spec.org.
- [ ] **Top three candidate retired benchmarks for a paper:** `176.gcc` (CPU 2000), `179.art` (CPU 2000 FP), `099.go` (CPU 95).
- [ ] **The one URL to bookmark:** https://www.spec.org/benchmarks.html — the master index of every active and retired benchmark.
- [ ] **Examiner's favourite question pattern:** *"Differentiate SPECspeed and SPECrate with a numerical example."* — always carry a 3-mark prepared answer.

> 🎓 **Closing Note for Students:** This activity is the *only* Module 1 topic that lets you cite a *living* industry resource in your answer. Always mention the **specific suite, the specific result page URL, and the SPECratio** in any 14-mark answer — this signals to the examiner that you have actually visited the site, not just memorised the textbook.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of Basic Concepts of Parallel Processing and Pipelining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Review of Basic Concepts of Parallel Processing and Pipelining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Parallel Processing** is a computational paradigm in which multiple instructions and/or data elements are processed simultaneously across multiple processing elements (PEs) connected in a coordinated architecture. The primary objective is to reduce program execution time by exploiting **instruction-level parallelism (ILP)**, **data-level parallelism (DLP)**, and **task-level parallelism (TLP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Pipelining**
> **Instruction Pipelining** is an implementation technique in which a sequential instruction execution cycle is decomposed into a series of discrete sub-operations (called **stages**), each performed by a dedicated hardware unit. Instructions flow continuously through these stages in an overlapping fashion, analogous to an industrial assembly line, so that multiple instructions can be in different stages of completion at any given clock cycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Parallel Processing — The Multi-Lane Highway
Imagine a toll booth on a national highway. A **single-lane** toll booth processes cars **one at a time** — this is sequential processing. Now, suppose the authorities open **four toll booths** working in parallel. Four cars pass through simultaneously every few seconds. The aggregate throughput multiplies, and the average waiting time per car drops drastically. This is **parallel processing** in its purest form — multiple independent units executing work simultaneously.

### Pipelining — The Restaurant Kitchen Assembly Line
Picture a fast-food kitchen where making a burger involves five stages:
1. Toasting the bun
2. Grilling the patty
3. Adding toppings (lettuce, sauce)
4. Wrapping the burger
5. Billing and handover

If **one chef** does all five steps for each customer sequentially, serving 5 customers takes 25 time units. But if **five specialized stations** (one per stage) work in an assembly line fashion, the first customer is served after 5 units, but a new customer finishes every 1 unit thereafter. The **latency** of one burger is 5 units, but the **throughput** is 1 burger per unit — a massive improvement. This is **pipelining**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Pipelining does **not** reduce the latency of a single instruction; it increases the **throughput** of the entire instruction stream.

## 1.3 Fundamental Metrics and Terminology

The following standardized performance metrics are repeatedly used throughout this module:

- **Clock Cycle Time ($T_{clk}$)** — the duration of one clock period, measured in nanoseconds (ns) or picoseconds (ps).
- **Pipeline Depth ($k$)** — the number of stages in the pipeline.
- **Speedup ($S$)** — the ratio of sequential execution time to parallel/pipelined execution time.
- **Efficiency ($\eta$)** — the fraction of useful work performed by the processing resources; ranges from **0 to 1**.
- **Throughput ($W$)** — the number of instructions (or tasks) completed per unit time.
- **Latency ($L$)** — the time required to complete a single instruction from start to finish.

## 1.4 Why Parallel Processing and Pipelining Matter

Modern microprocessors derive their raw computational power from a layered combination of both techniques:

1. **Pipelining** provides the foundational throughput enhancement within each processor core.
2. **Parallel processing** (multi-core, many-core, multi-threaded SIMD/MIMD units) multiplies the throughput further by executing independent work-streams simultaneously.

Both techniques are direct engineering responses to the **memory wall**, the **ILP wall**, and the **power wall** that have stalled single-core clock-frequency scaling since the mid-2000s.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Parallelism — Flynn's Taxonomy

Michael Flynn (1966) proposed the most enduring classification of computer architectures based on the multiplicity of **instruction streams** and **data streams**:

| Class | Instruction Stream | Data Stream | Example | Intuition |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **SISD** | Single | Single | Classical von Neumann uniprocessor | One chef, one recipe, one stove |
| **SIMD** | Single | Multiple | GPU vector units, SSE, AVX | One conductor leading many musicians playing the same tune on different instruments |
| **MISD** | Multiple | Single | Fault-tolerant flight-control computers (rare) | Multiple judges scoring one athlete |
| **MIMD** | Multiple | Multiple | Multi-core CPUs, clusters, HPC systems | Multiple independent chefs, each with their own recipe and ingredients |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point**: Most modern desktop processors are **hybrid SISD + SIMD + MIMD** — pipelined superscalar cores (SISD/SIMD) replicated on multiple cores (MIMD).

## 2.2 Levels of Parallelism (Hierarchy)

From the finest grain to the coarsest:

1. **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** — overlap the execution of independent instructions within a single thread (pipelining, superscalar, out-of-order execution, VLIW).
2. **Data-Level Parallelism (DLP)** — apply the same operation to many data elements simultaneously (vector processors, SIMD, GPU).
3. **Thread-Level Parallelism (TLP)** — multiple software threads executing concurrently on multiple cores.
4. **Process / Task-Level Parallelism** — independent programs running on different cores, nodes, or machines.
5. **Request-Level Parallelism** — independent user requests served by different servers (cloud, web farms).

## 2.3 Pipelined Instruction Execution — The Classic 5-Stage RISC Pipeline

A canonical RISC pipeline decomposes instruction execution into five stages:

| Stage | Mnemonic | Hardware Unit | Operation |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **IF** | Instruction Memory + PC | Instruction Fetch from memory |
| 2 | **ID** | Register File + Decoder | Instruction Decode and operand register read |
| 3 | **EX** | ALU | Execute the operation (arithmetic, address calculation) |
| 4 | **MEM** | Data Memory | Memory access (load/store) |
| 5 | **WB** | Register File | Write Back the result to the destination register |

> [!NOTE]
> The **structural decomposition** of these stages is the design choice that enables pipelining — each stage is implemented by combinational logic followed by a pipeline register clocked at the end of the stage.

## 2.4 Pipeline Performance — Theoretical Maximum

For a pipeline with $k$ stages processing $N$ instructions:

- **Unpipelined execution time** (no overlap): $T_{seq} = N \cdot k \cdot T_{clk}$.
- **Ideal pipelined execution time** (no hazards, no fill/drain overhead): $T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$.
- **Ideal speedup**: $S_{ideal} = k$ (i.e., a $k$-stage pipeline is at most $k$ times faster).

In the limit $N \to \infty$, the per-instruction completion rate approaches **1 instruction per clock cycle** (IPC = 1).

## 2.5 Pipeline Hazards

Hazards are conditions that prevent the next instruction from executing in its designated clock cycle, thereby breaking the ideal throughput.

| Hazard Type | Cause | Typical Solution |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Structural** | Hardware resource conflict (two stages need the same unit in the same cycle) | Resource duplication (e.g., separate instruction and data memories — Harvard architecture) |
| **Data** | An instruction depends on the result of a previous in-flight instruction | **Forwarding (bypassing)**, pipeline **interlock** insertion, instruction reordering by the compiler |
| **Control** | Branch and jump instructions alter the PC, leaving fetched instructions in the pipeline invalid | **Branch prediction**, **delayed branch**, **branch target buffer (BTB)**, speculative execution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus Area**: Data hazards are sub-classified into **RAW (Read After Write — true dependence)**, **WAR (Write After Read — anti-dependence)**, and **WAW (Write After Write — output dependence)**. Only RAW is a *true* data hazard in an in-order pipeline; WAR and WAW are *name-dependence* hazards relevant to out-of-order execution.

## 2.6 Amdahl's Law — The Fundamental Limit of Parallelism

> [!NOTE]
> **Amdahl's Law (1967)**: The theoretical maximum speedup achievable by parallelizing a fraction $f$ of a program on $p$ processors is given by the equation below. The remaining fraction $(1 - f)$ must execute serially and forms an inescapable bottleneck.

$$S(p) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$$

Special cases:
- $f = 1$ (fully parallelizable): $S_{max} = p$.
- $f = 0$ (entirely sequential): $S_{max} = 1$.
- $p \to \infty$: $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f}$.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **complete set of equations** required for solving KTU 2024 university exam questions on this topic. Master each entry.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{par}}$ | Speedup (general) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $S_{pipe,\,ideal} = \dfrac{N \cdot k \cdot T_{clk}}{(k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}}$ | Speedup of $k$-stage pipeline on $N$ tasks | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $S_{Amdahl} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$ | Amdahl's law | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{p} = \dfrac{1}{p \cdot \left[(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}\right]}$ | Parallel efficiency | $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 5 | $W = \dfrac{N}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{N}{(k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}}$ | Throughput (instructions / second) | Instructions per second |
| 6 | $IPC = \dfrac{W \cdot T_{clk}}{1} = \dfrac{N}{k + N - 1}$ | Instructions per cycle (ideal pipeline) | $\le 1$ for scalar pipeline |
| 7 | $CPI_{avg} = 1 + \sum_{i} H_{i} \cdot P_{i}$ | Average CPI with hazards | Where $H_i$ = stall cycles of hazard $i$, $P_i$ = probability |
| 8 | $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f}$ | Max possible speedup as $p \to \infty$ | Upper bound |

> [!WARNING]

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles studied in this module underpin every layer of contemporary computing:

- **Smartphone SoCs** (Apple A17, Snapdragon 8 Gen 3) combine an out-of-order superscalar pipelined CPU with a wide SIMD GPU.
- **Datacenter accelerators** (NVIDIA H100, Google TPU v5) push SIMD/MIMD parallelism to thousands of lanes.
- **Embedded real-time controllers** in automotive ECUs rely on deterministic pipeline timing to meet hard deadlines.
- **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** systems scale Amdahl's law limits by minimizing the serial fraction $1 - f$ through algorithm redesign and hardware specialization.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pipeline Speedup from First Principles

### Statement to Prove
For a $k$-stage pipeline processing $N$ instructions, with each stage taking $T_{clk}$ time, the ideal speedup is:

$$S_{pipe} = \frac{N \cdot k \cdot T_{clk}}{(k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}}$$

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Compute the sequential (unpipelined) execution time.**
Without pipelining, each instruction must complete all $k$ stages before the next begins. The total time is the product of the number of instructions, the number of stages, and the clock period.

$$T_{seq} = N \cdot k \cdot T_{clk}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the pipelined execution time.**
In a pipelined processor, the first instruction still requires $k$ clock cycles to traverse all stages (the *pipeline fill* phase). After that, a new instruction completes every additional cycle. Thus the total number of clock cycles is:

$$\text{Total cycles} = k + (N - 1)$$

Multiplying by the clock period yields the total time:

$$T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio to obtain speedup.**
By definition, speedup is the ratio of the unoptimized to the optimized execution time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{pipe} &= \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} \\
&= \frac{N \cdot k \cdot T_{clk}}{(k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk}} \\
&= \frac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$ (steady-state).**
As the number of instructions grows, the pipeline fill overhead becomes negligible:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S_{pipe} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1} = k$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum speedup of a $k$-stage pipeline equals $k$, achieved asymptotically as $N$ becomes very large.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law from First Principles

### Statement to Prove
The maximum speedup of a program with parallel fraction $f$ running on $p$ processors is:

$$S(p) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$$

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Decompose the program runtime.**
Let the original sequential execution time on a single processor be $T_{seq} = 1$ (normalized). A fraction $f$ of this work is parallelizable, and the remaining $(1 - f)$ is inherently sequential.

$$T_{seq} = T_{par} + T_{ser} = f + (1 - f) = 1$$

**Step 2 — Compute the parallel execution time on $p$ processors.**
The parallel portion of the work, originally taking time $f$, is now divided across $p$ processors. Assuming perfect load balancing and no communication overhead, it now takes time $\dfrac{f}{p}$. The serial portion remains unchanged.

$$T_{par} = (1 - f) + \frac{f}{p}$$

**Step 3 — Form the speedup ratio.**
Since $T_{seq} = 1$, the speedup simplifies to the reciprocal of the parallel execution time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(p) &= \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{par}} \\
&= \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $p \to \infty$.**
As the number of processors grows without bound, the parallel fraction $\dfrac{f}{p} \to 0$. The bottleneck becomes the serial portion alone:

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S(p) = \frac{1}{1 - f}$$

**Conclusion:** Even with an infinite number of processors, the speedup is bounded above by $\dfrac{1}{1 - f}$. This is the **fundamental physical limit of parallel speedup** and the central insight of Amdahl's law.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

### Problem
A 4-stage pipeline processes 100 instructions. The clock period is 2 ns. A branch instruction causes a 2-cycle flush penalty on 20% of the instructions.

**Compute:**
1. The ideal (no-hazard) execution time.
2. The actual execution time including the branch penalty.
3. The actual speedup versus a non-pipelined implementation.
4. The throughput in MIPS.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part 1 — Ideal execution time (no hazards).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ideal} &= (k + N - 1) \cdot T_{clk} \\
&= (4 + 100 - 1) \cdot 2\,\text{ns} \\
&= 103 \cdot 2\,\text{ns} \\
&= 206\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 2 — Add the branch penalty.**

Out of 100 instructions, 20 cause a 2-cycle penalty. Additional cycles = $0.20 \times 100 \times 2 = 40$ cycles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{actual} &= (103 + 40) \cdot 2\,\text{ns} \\
&= 143 \cdot 2\,\text{ns} \\
&= 286\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 3 — Speedup versus non-pipelined implementation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{nonpipe} &= N \cdot k \cdot T_{clk} = 100 \cdot 4 \cdot 2\,\text{ns} = 800\,\text{ns} \\
S_{actual} &= \frac{T_{nonpipe}}{T_{actual}} = \frac{800}{286} \approx 2.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 4 — Throughput in MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{N}{T_{actual}} = \frac{100\,\text{instructions}}{286 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}} \approx 3.497 \times 10^{8}\,\text{IPS} \\
&= 349.7\,\text{MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Analytical Matrix — Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Pipeline depth | $k$ | 4 |
| Number of instructions | $N$ | 100 |
| Clock period | $T_{clk}$ | 2 ns |
| Ideal cycles | $k + N - 1$ | 103 |
| Ideal time | $T_{ideal}$ | 206 ns |
| Hazard stall cycles | $0.20 \times 100 \times 2$ | 40 |
| Actual cycles | $103 + 40$ | 143 |
| Actual time | $T_{actual}$ | 286 ns |
| Non-pipelined time | $T_{nonpipe}$ | 800 ns |
| Speedup | $S$ | 2.80 |
| Throughput | $W$ | 349.7 MIPS |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between instruction-level parallelism (ILP) and data-level parallelism (DLP). Give one example of an architecture that exploits each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | ILP | DLP |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Overlaps the execution of *independent instructions* from the *same* thread | Applies the *same operation* to *multiple data elements* simultaneously |
| Grain | Fine (within one instruction stream) | Medium-to-fine (across array elements) |
| Typical Mechanism | Pipelining, superscalar, out-of-order, VLIW | Vector units, SIMD, GPU lanes |
| Example Architecture | Intel Core i7 superscalar out-of-order pipeline | NVIDIA A100 GPU streaming multiprocessor |

*(1 mark for each correct definition, 1 mark for the examples.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain Amdahl's law. Why does the speedup saturate as the number of processors increases?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Amdahl's law states that the maximum speedup of a program with parallel fraction $f$ running on $p$ processors is:

$$S(p) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$$

The term $(1 - f)$ represents the *serial portion* of the program, which cannot be parallelized and must be executed on a single processor regardless of $p$. As $p$ increases, $\dfrac{f}{p} \to 0$, and the speedup asymptotically approaches $\dfrac{1}{1 - f}$, a finite upper bound. Therefore, the speedup saturates — adding more processors yields progressively smaller gains. *(1 mark statement, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark saturation logic.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks. The student must answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically 7 marks each.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **5-stage instruction pipeline** in a RISC processor. Identify the **pipeline registers** and explain their role. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-stage pipeline processes 200 instructions. Each stage takes 1 ns. Due to a data hazard, **20% of the instructions incur a 2-cycle stall**. Compute:
1. The total execution time including the stall.
2. The actual speedup compared to a non-pipelined implementation.
3. The pipeline efficiency.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution A(a) — 5-Stage Pipeline Diagram and Explanation

The 5 stages are:

| Stage | Operation | Hardware Unit |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **IF** | Instruction Fetch | Instruction Memory, PC, PC+4 adder |
| **ID** | Instruction Decode + Operand Fetch | Decoder, Register File (read ports) |
| **EX** | Execute / Address Calculation | ALU |
| **MEM** | Memory Read / Write | Data Memory |
| **WB** | Write Back to Register File | Register File (write port) |

**Pipeline Registers** (4 sets, all clocked simultaneously at the end of each stage):

1. **IF/ID register** — latches the fetched instruction and PC+4.
2. **ID/EX register** — latches decoded control signals, read register values, sign-extended immediate.
3. **EX/MEM register** — latches the ALU result, the destination register number, and the store data.
4. **MEM/WB register** — latches the value to be written back (either memory data or ALU result).

**Role of pipeline registers**: They *decouple* the combinational logic of consecutive stages, allowing each stage to operate on a *different* instruction in the *same* clock cycle. They define the **clock-cycle boundary** of the pipeline. Their setup-and-hold time forms the lower bound on the achievable clock period. *(Full 7 marks awarded for a clean diagram with all five stages, four pipeline registers clearly labelled, and a 2–3 line description of each stage's function.)*

---

#### Solution A(b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $k = 5$ stages, $N = 200$ instructions, $T_{clk} = 1$ ns, stall fraction = 20%, stall penalty = 2 cycles.

**Step 1 — Total execution time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ideal cycles} &= k + N - 1 = 5 + 200 - 1 = 204 \\
\text{Stall cycles} &= 0.20 \times 200 \times 2 = 80 \\
\text{Total cycles} &= 204 + 80 = 284 \\
T_{actual} &= 284 \times 1\,\text{ns} = 284\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Speedup vs. non-pipelined.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{nonpipe} &= N \cdot k \cdot T_{clk} = 200 \times 5 \times 1 = 1000\,\text{ns} \\
S &= \frac{1000}{284} \approx 3.52
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pipeline efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{3.52}{5} = 0.704 = 70.4\%
$$

*(2 marks for the time, 2 marks for the speedup, 3 marks for the efficiency with clear logic.)*

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**(a)** Explain **Flynn's classification** of computer architectures. Provide one example architecture for each class and discuss why modern CPUs are described as **hybrid SISD/SIMD/MIMD**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply **Amdahl's law** to compute the maximum speedup for the following scenarios:
1. A program where **90%** of the execution time is parallelizable, running on **16 processors**.
2. The same program running on **1024 processors**.
3. Comment on the result and identify the bottleneck.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution B(a) — Flynn's Taxonomy

Flynn's taxonomy classifies architectures along two orthogonal axes: the *number of instruction streams* and the *number of data streams* active at any instant.

| Class | I-Streams | D-Streams | Description | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| SISD | 1 | 1 | Single sequential processor | Intel 8086 |
| SIMD | 1 | N | One instruction operates on many data elements | Intel SSE / AVX units, GPU |
| MISD | N | 1 | Multiple instructions on same data | Flight-control triple-redundant systems |
| MIMD | N | N | Fully independent processors | Multi-core CPUs, compute clusters |

**Why modern CPUs are hybrid**: A modern desktop CPU (e.g., Intel Core i9) is a **single core** that is *pipelined and superscalar* — so it appears as SISD at the user-visible level. Internally, it issues multiple instructions per cycle (ILP), and its AVX-512 unit applies the same instruction to 512-bit vectors (SIMD). At the *chip* level, it contains 8–24 such cores operating independently on different instruction streams with different data (MIMD). Hence the architecture is best described as a **hybrid SISD + SIMD + MIMD** system. *(2 marks classification, 2 marks examples, 3 marks hybrid explanation.)*

---

#### Solution B(b) — Amdahl's Law Calculations

**Given:** $f = 0.90$ (parallel fraction), $(1 - f) = 0.10$ (serial fraction).

**Scenario 1 — $p = 16$:**

$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625} = \frac{1}{0.15625} = 6.40
$$

**Scenario 2 — $p = 1024$:**

$$
S(1024) = \frac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{1024}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.000879} \approx \frac{1}{0.100879} \approx 9.91
$$

**Scenario 3 — Comment on the bottleneck:**

Increasing the processor count from 16 to 1024 (a 64× increase) only improves the speedup from 6.40 to 9.91 — a **mere 1.55× gain**. The theoretical upper bound as $p \to \infty$ is:

$$S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10$$

The **bottleneck** is the **10% serial portion** of the program. Even with a million processors, the speedup can never exceed 10. This is the **fundamental insight of Amdahl's law** — improving hardware parallelism is futile if the algorithmic serial fraction is not reduced. *(2 marks each scenario + 1 mark bottleneck identification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students frequently compute $S_{Amdahl} = p \cdot f$ or $S = p$ when the serial fraction is small but non-zero. This is **wrong** — Amdahl's law is *not* a linear relation.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: When asked for "speedup" in pipeline problems, students often forget to subtract 1 from $(k + N - 1)$ or omit the clock-period units in the final answer. Always show the **dimensional units** explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Confusion between *latency* and *throughput*. A pipeline does **not** reduce the latency of a single instruction; it improves the steady-state throughput. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Forgetting to include the **pipeline fill and drain** cycles ($k - 1$ additional cycles beyond the $N$ ideal ones) in pipeline timing problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following checklist consolidates every high-priority concept from this topic. Use it as a 5-minute pre-exam revision sheet.

- **Parallel Processing** = simultaneous execution of multiple instructions or data items. Goal: reduce wall-clock execution time.
- **Pipelining** = a specific parallel technique that overlaps the *stages* of instruction execution. Goal: increase instruction throughput.
- **Flynn's Taxonomy**: SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD — based on instruction-stream and data-stream count.
- **5-Stage RISC Pipeline** = IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB. Four pipeline registers: IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB.
- **Ideal Speedup** of a $k$-stage pipeline is asymptotically $k$, achieved as $N \to \infty$.
- **Pipeline Hazards**: Structural (hardware), Data (RAW/WAR/WAW), Control (branches/jumps).
- **Data Hazard Solutions**: Forwarding (bypassing), pipeline interlocks, compiler scheduling.
- **Control Hazard Solutions**: Branch prediction, delayed branch, BTB, speculative execution.
- **Amdahl's Law** = $S(p) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + f/p}$. Serial fraction $1 - f$ sets the upper bound $S_{\infty} = 1/(1-f)$.
- **Efficiency** $\eta = S/p$. Always $\le 1$.
- **Throughput** = $N / T_{pipe}$. Measured in **MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second) or **IPC** (Instructions Per Cycle).
- **Latency** of one instruction in a $k$-stage pipeline is $k \cdot T_{clk}$. Pipelining does **not** reduce this.
- **Modern CPUs are hybrid** SISD + SIMD + MIMD, combining pipelined superscalar cores, vector units, and multi-core replication.
- **Grain hierarchy** of parallelism: ILP $<$ DLP $<$ TLP $<$ process-level $<$ request-level.
- **Gustafson's Law** (counterpart to Amdahl's) addresses *scaled-speedup* for problems that grow with the machine size — relevant for KTU higher-level questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip**: When a KTU question asks for "speedup," always state *with respect to what* (sequential reference, non-pipelined reference, single-core reference). The examiner expects this distinction explicitly written in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Level Parallelism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Instruction Level Parallelism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** is a family of processor and compiler design techniques that allows **overlapping execution of multiple instructions drawn from a single sequential program stream**, by identifying and exploiting the independent operations within that stream.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with Hennessy & Patterson's *Computer Architecture* (the de-facto reference for this course), ILP is defined as the **measure of how many instructions in a program can be executed simultaneously without changing the program's externally visible behavior**. It is the architectural answer to the **memory wall** and the **ILP wall** — the idea that there exists a theoretical ceiling on how much parallelism can be extracted from a sequential program.

The two primary quantitative metrics used to express ILP are:

- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** — the average number of clock cycles the processor consumes per instruction. Lower is better.
- **IPC (Instructions Per Cycle)** — the mathematical reciprocal, $IPC = \dfrac{1}{CPI}$. Higher is better.

A scalar pipelined processor has a theoretical $CPI = 1$ (one instruction completed per cycle). A **superscalar / multiple-issue** processor attempts to push $CPI < 1$ (equivalently $IPC > 1$) by issuing $n$ instructions per cycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a restaurant kitchen preparing a multi-course meal.**

A single chef who must complete *wash → chop → cook → plate → serve* for dish A before starting dish B is a **non-pipelined scalar processor** ($CPI \gg 1$). When the chef is washing, his chopper, stove, and server are all **idle** — a clear waste of resources.

A **pipelined kitchen** assigns one cook per stage (washer, chopper, cook, server). Now, while dish B is being chopped, dish A is being cooked, and dish C is being washed. The kitchen completes one dish per *stage time* rather than per *total time*. This is **instruction pipelining**.

A **superscalar kitchen** would have *two stoves, two prep counters, two servers* working in parallel, completing two dishes per stage time. This is **multiple-issue ILP** — the kitchen exploits the fact that dish A (perhaps a soup) and dish B (perhaps a salad) use **disjoint resources** and can be prepared simultaneously.

A **Tomasulo-style kitchen** would dynamically re-route partially prepared orders to whichever station is free — a **dynamically scheduled, out-of-order** execution engine.

A **speculative kitchen** might start preparing dessert (the most likely next course) *before* the customer confirms the order, discarding the dessert if the customer chose a cheese plate instead. This is **branch speculation** with rollback.

The unifying insight: **ILP exists whenever a program contains operations whose data dependencies do not constrain their execution order**. The architect's job is to expose and exploit that independence.

## 1.3 The KTU 2024 Module-2 Learning Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scope of Module 2 (per KTU 2024 PECST528 syllabus):**
> - Pipelining review & its performance limits
> - **Data, name, and control dependencies** (True / Anti / Output / Name / Structural / Control)
> - **Static techniques** — pipeline scheduling, loop unrolling, compiler dependence analysis
> - **Dynamic techniques** — scoreboarding, Tomasulo's algorithm, register renaming
> - **Multiple issue** — static (VLIW) and dynamic (superscalar)
> - **Speculation** — hardware and compiler supported
> - **Branch prediction** — 1-bit, 2-bit, correlating, tournament
> - **Limits of ILP** — the Wallach study and the empirical ceilings
> - Case studies — Intel Core i7, ARM Cortex, AMD Zen pipelines

The unifying engineering goal: **drive CPI below 1** while preserving **program correctness**, **power efficiency**, and **design complexity tractability**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of ILP

ILP techniques can be classified along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Dimension 1 | Dimension 2 |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Who decides what runs in parallel?** | **Hardware** (dynamic / out-of-order) | **Compiler** (static / VLIW / EPIC) |
| **When is the decision made?** | **At runtime** in the reservation stations | **At compile time** in the scheduler |
| **Are results committed in order?** | **In-order issue, out-of-order execution, in-order commit** (ROB) | All in-order (VLIW) or in-order commit only |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern x86 (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen) and high-end ARM (Cortex-A7xx, Neoverse) are **dynamic, out-of-order, speculatively issued, in-order committed** processors — the most aggressive ILP design style.

## 2.2 The Five Hazards of ILP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **hazard** is anything that prevents an instruction from executing in its designated pipeline cycle. The five canonical hazards (Hennessy & Patterson taxonomy) are:

| # | Hazard | Cause | Symptom | Typical Fix |
|---|--------|-------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Structural** | Hardware resource conflict | Two instructions need the same unit | Duplicate units / stall |
| 2 | **Data — RAW (Read After Write)** | True dependence: $I_j$ needs $I_i$'s result | Pipeline stall or forwarding | Forwarding / scheduling |
| 3 | **Data — WAR (Write After Read)** | Anti-dependence: $I_j$ writes a register $I_i$ reads | Out-of-order writeback wrecks $I_i$ | Register renaming |
| 4 | **Data — WAW (Write After Write)** | Output dependence: both write the same register | Out-of-order write wrecks order | Register renaming |
| 5 | **Control (branch)** | Outcome of branch not known | Fetch must wait / mis-speculation | Branch prediction + speculation |

> [!WARNING]
> WAR and WAW are *not* true data dependencies — they are **name dependencies** that exist only because the programmer/compiler reused register names. **Register renaming** eliminates them entirely.

## 2.3 Loop Unrolling — The Compiler's Primary ILP Lever

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loop unrolling** is the process of replicating the body of a loop $k$ times, adjusting the step and trip count accordingly, to expose more independent instructions that the scheduler can pipeline.

**Example — original FP loop accumulating a sum-of-products:**

```text
for (i = 1000; i != 0; i = i - 1)
    x[i] = x[i] + s;
```

Unrolled 4× version exposes **4 independent loads, 4 independent adds, 4 independent stores** — a **4× increase in available ILP** between dependent operations.

## 2.4 Scoreboarding vs. Tomasulo — The Two Classical Dynamic Schedulers

| Feature | **Scoreboard (CDC 6600, 1964)** | **Tomasulo (IBM 360/91, 1967)** |
|---------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Issue stage | **In-order**, structural check only | **In-order**, but **register renaming** via RS |
| Read operands | Wait until operand producers finish | **CDB broadcast** — wait-free |
| Execution | Out-of-order | Out-of-order |
| Write-back | Wait if WAR exists | CDB broadcasts result to all waiting RS |
| Handles WAR/WAW? | **No** (stalls on them) | **Yes** (renames registers) |
| Hardware cost | Centralized scoreboard | Distributed RS + CDB |
| Modern descendants | Original concept, mostly obsolete | Intel P6, Core i7 frontend; AMD Zen |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects every quantitative formula you will need to score full marks on Module 2 numerical/derivation questions. Master every row.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / KTU usage |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ | Foundation of all performance questions |
| 2 | Pipeline Ideal Speedup | $S_{ideal} = k$ for $k$-stage balanced pipeline | $k$ = number of stages |
| 3 | Pipeline CPI (with stalls) | $CPI_{pipe} = CPI_{ideal} + \sum_{i} \text{stall}_i \times freq_i$ | $stall_i$ = stall cycles for hazard $i$ |
| 4 | Pipeline Throughput | $TP = \dfrac{IC}{IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}} = \dfrac{1}{CPI \cdot T_{clock}}$ | Instructions per second |
| 5 | Amdahl's Law | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | $f$ = fraction enhanced, $n$ = speedup of that fraction |
| 6 | Multiple-Issue CPI | $CPI_{MI} = \dfrac{CPI_{unpipelined}}{n} + \text{hazard stalls per issue slot}$ | $n$ = issue width |
| 7 | ILP Bound | $ILP_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \text{stall cycles per instruction}}$ | Operational definition |
| 8 | Branch Mispredict Penalty | $T_{mispred} = (d_{fetch} + d_{decode} + d_{exec}) \times T_{clock}$ | $d$ = pipeline depth of each stage |
| 9 | Loop Unrolling Speedup | $S_{LU} = \dfrac{N \times n_{ops}}{n_{ops} + (n_{ops}-1)\cdot b_{stall}}$ | $N$ = unroll factor, $b_{stall}$ = branch overhead fraction |
| 10 | 2-bit Saturating Counter Predict | $\hat{P} \in \{00, 01, 10, 11\}$ with hysteresis thresholds | Branch prediction accuracy metric |
| 11 | Speculation Rollback Cost | $T_{rollback} = k \cdot T_{clock}$ on mispredict | $k$ = pipeline depth at predict site |
| 12 | Tomasulo RS Occupancy | $n_{in-flight} = \sum_{i} RS_i + \text{ROB depth}$ | Limits ILP exploitation |

> [!NOTE]
> In all formulas, $\vert x \vert$ denotes absolute value. Never write the vertical bar $\mid$ inside markdown tables — use $\vert$ or $\mathrm{abs}(\cdot)$ to avoid breaking table parsing.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why ILP Matters in the Real World

- **Datacenter CPUs** (Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC) — exploit 4–6 issue width to feed many cores
- **Mobile SoCs** (Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon) — wide issue keeps the memory pipeline full
- **GPU shaders** (NVIDIA, AMD) — *thread-level* parallelism overlaid on top of ILP inside each core
- **Embedded DSPs** (TI C6x, Qualcomm Hexagon) — **VLIW / static ILP** for power-constrained inference
- **RISC-V boom-core, OoO processors** — open-source dynamic ILP implementations
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is the **calculus-and-arithmetic backbone** that KTU board examiners reward. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no jumps, no skipped rows.

## 3.1 Derivation: Pipelined Speedup vs. an Unpipelined Processor

Let a processor contain $k$ functional stages, each of latency $\tau$ seconds, processing $N$ instructions.

**Unpipelined (single-cycle multi-cycle) processor:** every instruction traverses all $k$ stages sequentially before the next begins.

$$T_{unpipe} = N \cdot k \cdot \tau$$

**Pipelined processor:** once the pipeline is full, one instruction completes every $\tau$ seconds.

$$T_{pipe} = (k + N - 1) \cdot \tau$$

The pipeline **speedup** is therefore:

$$S = \frac{T_{unpipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{N \cdot k \cdot \tau}{(k + N - 1) \cdot \tau}$$

Cancelling $\tau$:

$$S = \frac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1}$$

Taking the limit as $N \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1} = k$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A $k$-stage balanced pipeline yields a *theoretical* maximum speedup of $k$ over an equivalent unpipelined implementation. The $(k-1)$ term in the denominator is the *fill-drain overhead* — for short programs with small $N$, the pipeline underperforms.

**Numerical sanity check.** Let $k = 5$ (classic RISC IF/ID/EX/MEM/WB), $N = 100$:

$$S = \frac{100 \times 5}{5 + 100 - 1} = \frac{500}{104} = 4.808$$

The 5-stage pipeline achieves **96.15% of its ideal speedup** for $N=100$ instructions.

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to ILP

Suppose a fraction $f$ of a program's execution time can be sped up by a factor of $n$ (e.g., via wider issue or speculation), while the remaining $(1-f)$ cannot.

**Original execution time:**

$$T_{old} = (1-f) \cdot T_{old} + f \cdot T_{old}$$

**New execution time** — the enhanced portion becomes $\frac{f \cdot T_{old}}{n}$:

$$T_{new} = (1-f) \cdot T_{old} + \frac{f \cdot T_{old}}{n}$$

**Speedup:**

$$S = \frac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \frac{T_{old}}{(1-f) \cdot T_{old} + \dfrac{f \cdot T_{old}}{n}}$$

Cancelling $T_{old}$:

$$S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU hot take-away:** Even with $n \to \infty$, the speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$. For example, if only 50% of the program is parallelizable ($f = 0.5$), the **maximum possible speedup is 2** — no matter how wide or deep your ILP engine.

## 3.3 Derivation: Loop Unrolling Speedup (Worked Numerical Example)

Consider the following unoptimized MIPS loop accumulating a sum:

```text
Loop:  L.D   F0, 0(R1)        ; F0 = x[i]
       ADD.D F4, F0, F2       ; F4 = x[i] + s
       S.D   F4, 0(R1)        ; x[i] = F4
       DADDUI R1, R1, #-8     ; i = i - 1
       BNE   R1, R2, Loop     ; branch if i != 0
```

Assume: 1 load-use stall per L.D, no forwarding on the load, and the branch is resolved in the EX stage (1-cycle branch penalty if predicted-not-taken mispredicts).

**Original loop cycle count per iteration:** $5 \text{ inst} + 1 \text{ load stall} + 1 \text{ branch stall} = 7 \text{ cycles}$

**Unroll 4 times** — body becomes 20 straight-line instructions with 4 independent L.Ds, 4 adds, 4 stores, 1 decrement, 1 branch.

**Unrolled cycle count per 4 iterations:** $20 + 1 \text{ (final load-use)} + 1 \text{ (branch)} = 22 \text{ cycles}$

**Per-iteration cycle count after unrolling:**

$$C_{unroll} = \frac{22}{4} = 5.5 \text{ cycles/iter}$$

**Speedup factor:**

$$S_{LU} = \frac{7}{5.5} = 1.273 \times$$

A 27.3% throughput improvement from unrolling alone, before any dynamic scheduling.

## 3.4 Derivation: Multiple-Issue CPI in a Superscalar Pipeline

Let a processor issue up to $n$ instructions per cycle, with a base unpipelined $CPI_{base}$ and a per-issue-slot stall penalty $h$ cycles per hazard encountered.

**Ideal CPI** (every cycle issues $n$):

$$CPI_{ideal} = \frac{CPI_{base}}{n}$$

**Actual CPI:**

$$CPI_{MI} = \frac{CPI_{base}}{n} + h \cdot \text{stalls per issue slot}$$

For a 4-issue processor with $CPI_{base} = 4$ and $h = 0.25$:

$$CPI_{MI} = \frac{4}{4} + 0.25 = 1.25$$

$$IPC_{MI} = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.8$$

Even an aggressively wide issue with significant stalls delivers **less than 1 IPC** — the **ILP wall** in action.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Tomasulo's Algorithm — Instruction Trace

Consider the following MIPS FP instruction sequence:

```text
I1:  L.D    F6, 32(R2)
I2:  L.D    F2, 44(R3)
I3:  MUL.D  F0, F2, F4
I4:  SUB.D  F8, F2, F6
I5:  DIV.D  F10, F0, F6
I6:  ADD.D  F6, F8, F2
```

**Key observations for Tomasulo execution:**

1. `I1` and `I2` are **independent loads** — both can be issued in cycle 1.
2. `I3` and `I4` both need `F2` — must wait for `I2` to complete.
3. `I5` needs `F0` (from `I3`) and `F6` (from `I1`) — longest-latency chain.
4. `I6` needs `F8` (from `I4`) and `F2` (from `I2`) — independent of `I3, I5`.
5. **WAR hazard** — `I6` writes `F6` which `I5` reads. Tomasulo **renames** `I6`'s `F6` to a new physical register to avoid the stall.

**State at end of execution (CDB broadcasts per cycle):**

| Cycle | CDB Broadcast | Comments |
|-------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | — | I1, I2 issued to Load1, Load2 |
| 2 | — | I3, I4, I6 issued (renamed) to RS Mul1, Add1, Add2 |
| 3 | I1 result → F6, I2 result → F2 | I4 and I6 now have both operands; I3 has F2 |
| 4–6 | — | MUL.D in MUL unit (3-cycle latency) |
| 7 | I3 result → F0 | I5's F0 operand now ready |
| 8 | I4 result → F8, I6 result → F6' | Last CDB uses |
| 9–14 | — | DIV.D in DIV unit (4-cycle latency) |
| 15 | I5 result → F10 | Done |

Total: 15 cycles for 6 instructions = **$CPI = 2.5$**, $IPC = 0.4$. Without Tomasulo, the strict in-order execution would require stall cycles for every RAW on F2, F6, F0, F8 — pushing the count well above 20.

## 3.6 Limits of ILP — The Wallach Empirical Study (1995)

The canonical 1995 Wallach study (later refined by Rau, Fisher, and the IPC-1 study) measured upper bounds on ILP assuming perfect branch prediction, perfect renaming, infinite registers, and arbitrary memory disambiguation:

| Assumption Set | Avg. ILP | Max ILP |
|----------------|----------|---------|
| Perfect branch, in-order issue | 7.3 | 22 |
| Perfect branch, out-of-order issue | 11.5 | 45 |
| + Perfect memory disambiguation | 14.7 | 73 |
| + Infinite registers, no aliasing | 25.8 | 372 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Even under maximally optimistic hardware assumptions, the empirical average ILP is bounded near 25–50 for general-purpose code.** This is the *ILP wall*. Beyond it, architects must turn to **thread-level parallelism (TLP)** — i.e., multicore, SMT, or GPUs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are designed to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 anti-collision rules: all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, all labels are raw text, no reserved keywords appear as IDs.

## 4.1 ILP Techniques Taxonomy (Hierarchical Mind-Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[ILP Techniques]
    rootA --> nodeB[Static Compiler]
    rootA --> nodeC[Dynamic Hardware]
    rootA --> nodeD[Hybrid Speculative]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Loop Unrolling]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Software Pipelining]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Trace Scheduling]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[VLIW Bundling]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Scoreboard]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Tomasulo Algorithm]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Register Renaming]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Branch Prediction]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Speculative Execution]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Reorder Buffer ROB]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[Memory Disambiguation]

    nodeD1 --> nodeD1a[1 Bit Counter]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1b[2 Bit Saturating]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1c[Correlating Predictor]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1d[Tournament Predictor]
```

## 4.2 The Five-Stage Pipeline with Hazard Injection Points

```mermaid
graph LR
    stageA[IF Instruction Fetch]
    stageB[ID Instruction Decode and Read Reg]
    stageC[EX Execute or Address Calc]
    stageD[MEM Data Memory Access]
    stageE[WB Write Back]

    stageA -- bubble on control hazard --> stageB
    stageB -- structural hazard on regfile --> stageC
    stageC -- forwarding paths active --> stageD
    stageD -- load use stall back to stageC --> stageC
    stageE -- write back to regfile --> stageB

    hazX[RAW Stall]:::redBox
    hazY[WAR Rename]:::yellowBox
    hazZ[Control Mispredict]:::orangeBox

    classDef redBox fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef yellowBox fill:#FFFFCC,stroke:#999900,color:#000
    classDef orangeBox fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#CC6600,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Tomasulo Architecture — Block-Level Data Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    qA[Instruction Queue]
    rsM[MUL Reservation Stations x2]
    rsA[ADD Reservation Stations x3]
    rsL[Load Buffers x3]
    fpuM[MUL Functional Unit]
    fpuA[ADD Functional Unit]
    fpuL[Load Functional Unit]
    cdb[Common Data Bus CDB]
    rf[FP Register File with Mapping Table]

    qA -- dispatch --> rsM
    qA -- dispatch --> rsA
    qA -- dispatch --> rsL

    rf -- current tag and value --> rsM
    rf -- current tag and value --> rsA
    rf -- current tag and value --> rsL

    rsM -- operands ready --> fpuM
    rsA -- operands ready --> fpuA
    rsL -- operands ready --> fpuL

    fpuM -- result broadcast --> cdb
    fpuA -- result broadcast --> cdb
    fpuL -- result broadcast --> cdb

    cdb -- tag match and write --> rf
    cdb -- tag match and capture --> rsM
    cdb -- tag match and capture --> rsA
    cdb -- tag match and capture --> rsL
```

## 4.4 Scoreboard State Machine — Instruction Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    sA[Issue]
    sB[Read Operands]
    sC[Execution Complete]
    sD[Write Result]

    sA -- structural check OK --> sB
    sB -- no RAW conflict and no WAR on WB --> sC
    sC -- functional unit done --> sD
    sD -- no WAW pending --> sA

    sA -- stall if structural busy --> sA
    sB -- stall if RAW --> sB
    sD -- stall if WAR --> sD
```

## 4.5 Branch Predictor Evolution (Comparative Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    bpRoot[Branch Prediction Evolution]
    bpRoot --> bp1[Static Fixed Not Taken]
    bpRoot --> bp2[Static Backward Taken]
    bpRoot --> bp3[1 Bit Dynamic]
    bpRoot --> bp4[2 Bit Saturating Counter]
    bpRoot --> bp5[2 Level Local History]
    bpRoot --> bp6[Correlating Global History]
    bpRoot --> bp7[Tournament Gshare]
    bpRoot --> bp8[TAgged GEometric History Length TAGE]

    bp3 -- hysteresis fix --> bp4
    bp4 -- context added --> bp5
    bp5 -- path history --> bp6
    bp6 -- choice between local and global --> bp7
    bp7 -- multi table tagged --> bp8
```

## 4.6 Superscalar Pipeline with Reorder Buffer (ROB)

```mermaid
graph LR
    fetA[Fetch Unit with BTB and RAS]
    decA[Decode and Rename]
    robA[Reorder Buffer ROB]
    schA[Reservation Station Scheduler]
    exuA[Execution Units ALU x3 FP x3 Load Store x2]
    retA[Retire or Commit]

    fetA -- up to 4 inst per cycle --> decA
    decA -- allocate ROB entry --> robA
    decA -- dispatch with renamed operands --> schA
    schA -- wakeup and select --> exuA
    exuA -- result written back to ROB and RS --> robA
    robA -- in order commit when head ready --> retA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions mirror the **typical KTU University End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern** for PECST528 Module 2 under the 2024 Scheme. Marks are split into **Part A (3 marks × 10 = 30 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks × 2 choice = 28 marks, internal choice per slot)**. Solutions are written in the same step-by-step model-answer style KTU examiners award full marks for.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Instruction Level Parallelism. List any four techniques used to increase ILP in modern processors.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Instruction Level Parallelism is the property of a program that allows its instructions to be executed in parallel (overlapping in time) without affecting the program's final result. It is quantitatively measured as the average number of instructions that can be issued per cycle ($IPC$) or, equivalently, the reciprocal of CPI.

**Techniques to increase ILP (1.5 marks — list any four, 0.375 each):**

1. **Instruction pipelining** — overlap stages of different instructions.
2. **Multiple instruction issue** — issue 2, 4, or more instructions per cycle (superscalar / VLIW).
3. **Dynamic scheduling with out-of-order execution** — scoreboard, Tomasulo.
4. **Speculative execution** — execute past predicted branches, commit in order.
5. **Branch prediction** — reduce control-hazard stalls.
6. **Register renaming** — eliminate WAR and WAW hazards.
7. **Loop unrolling / software pipelining** — compiler-side exposure of independent ops.
8. **Hardware prefetching / memory disambiguation** — hide load-use latency.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between the three data dependencies — RAW, WAR, and WAW — with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Data dependencies** are constraints on instruction ordering. The three classical types are:

| Type | Full Name | Nature | Example | Solution |
|------|-----------|--------|---------|----------|
| **RAW** | Read After Write (True / Flow dependence) | $I_1$ writes a register that $I_2$ reads — *real data flow* | `MUL F0,F2,F4` followed by `ADD F6,F0,F8` | Forwarding, scheduling, stall |
| **WAR** | Write After Read (Anti-dependence) | $I_2$ writes a register that $I_1$ reads — *name only* | `ADD F2,F4,F6` followed by `MUL F2,F0,F8` | Register renaming |
| **WAW** | Write After Write (Output dependence) | Both write the same register — *name only* | `MUL F0,F2,F4` followed by `ADD F0,F8,F10` | Register renaming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Only **RAW** is a *true* data dependency. **WAR and WAW** are *name* dependencies that vanish if registers are renamed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do not** confuse *hazards* with *dependencies*. A *dependency* is a program property; a *hazard* is a *dynamic* stall that a specific pipeline suffers.
> - When asked to "explain Tomasulo," students often draw only the **Register File** and forget the **Reservation Stations, Common Data Bus, and Load/Store Buffers** — losing 2–3 marks.
> - For Amdahl's Law, the *unit* of $f$ matters. If $f$ is given as a percentage, convert to fraction (e.g., 60% → 0.6) before substituting.
> - For loop unrolling, **show both** the original and the unrolled code; many students lose marks for skipping the original.
> - For branch prediction, **name at least three** predictors (1-bit, 2-bit, correlating) — listing only one is insufficient for full marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 marks)

#### (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]
**Explain Tomasulo's algorithm with a neat diagram. Using the instruction sequence below, show the status of the reservation stations and register result tags at each cycle until all instructions complete.**

```text
I1:  L.D    F6, 32(R2)
I2:  L.D    F2, 44(R3)
I3:  MUL.D  F0, F2, F4
I4:  SUB.D  F8, F2, F6
I5:  DIV.D  F10, F0, F6
I6:  ADD.D  F6, F8, F2
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Architecture (3 marks):**

Tomasulo's algorithm integrates three structures:

1. **Instruction Queue** — holds fetched instructions awaiting issue.
2. **Reservation Stations (RS)** — one set per functional unit class (FP Adders, FP Multipliers, Load Buffers, Store Buffers). Each RS entry has fields: `Op`, `Vj`, `Vk`, `Qj`, `Qk`, `Busy`.
3. **Common Data Bus (CDB)** — broadcasts completed results to *all* RS and to the **Register File with tag fields** (the *result tags* / *mapping table*).
4. **Register Renaming** is implicit — when an RS issues, the *destination register tag* in the RF is updated to the RS's tag. Subsequent consumers read the tag, not the value, eliminating WAR/WAW.

**Step 2 — Issue phase (1 mark):** Each instruction reads its source operand tags from the RF and is dispatched to a free RS. *WAR is avoided* because $I_6$ writes a new tag for F6 (since $I_1$ already reserved the original F6 tag for its load).

**Step 3 — Execute phase (1 mark):** An RS monitors the CDB. When both `Qj` and `Qk` are cleared (operands captured), the RS starts the FU. The load RS computes the effective address immediately.

**Step 4 — Write-back phase (2 marks):** When the FU finishes, the result + tag is broadcast on the CDB. All RS with matching `Qj` or `Qk` capture the value and clear the tag. The RF updates the destination register.

**Per-cycle status table (illustrative for a 2-issue Tomasulo machine):**

| Cycle | Issue | Execute Start | Write-back via CDB | Notes |
|-------|-------|---------------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | I1 → Load1, I2 → Load2 | — | — | Both loads issued |
| 2 | I3 → Mul1, I4 → Add1 | I1, I2 (mem access) | — | I3 waits for F2 tag, I4 waits for both |
| 3 | I5 → Mul2 (wait F0), I6 → Add2 (wait F8) | — | I1 → F6, I2 → F2 (CDB x2) | I3, I4, I6 capture values |
| 4–6 | — | I3 (MUL, 3 cyc) | — | |
| 5 | — | I4 (ADD, 1 cyc), I6 (ADD, 1 cyc) | — | |
| 6 | — | I5 (DIV, 4 cyc) | I3 → F0 (CDB) | I5 captures F0 |
| 7 | — | — | I4 → F8, I6 → F6-renamed (CDB) | |
| 8–10 | — | I5 (DIV continues) | — | |
| 11 | — | — | I5 → F10 (CDB) | All done |

Total: 11 cycles for 6 instructions → $CPI = 1.83$, $IPC = 0.545$.

#### (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the 2-bit saturating counter branch predictor. Why is it preferred over a 1-bit predictor? Describe the global 2-level correlating predictor and the tournament predictor with diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

**2-bit saturating counter (3 marks):**

Each branch's Branch History Table (BHT) entry is a 2-bit saturating up/down counter with four states:

- `ST` (Strongly Taken, `11`)
- `WT` (Weakly Taken, `10`)
- `WN` (Weakly Not-Taken, `01`)
- `SN` (Strongly Not-Taken, `00`)

Prediction rule: predict Taken if MSB = 1, Not-Taken if MSB = 0.

**Update rule:** On a Taken outcome, increment (cap at `11`); on Not-Taken, decrement (cap at `00`).

**Why preferred over 1-bit (1 mark):** A 1-bit predictor mispredicts **twice** on every loop exit (once on the misprediction, once on the re-entry). A 2-bit predictor only mispredicts **once** because it requires *two* consecutive wrong outcomes to flip the prediction — adding hysteresis that absorbs single noise events.

**Global 2-level correlating predictor (2 marks):** Maintains a **Global History Register (GHR)** of the last $k$ branch outcomes. The GHR indexes a **Pattern History Table (PHT)** of 2-bit counters. The predictor therefore captures *correlations* between branches — e.g., a branch's outcome may depend on the immediately preceding branch.

**Tournament predictor (1 mark):** Combines a local 2-level predictor (per-branch history) and a global 2-level predictor (GHR-based), with a third **choice** predictor that selects, on a per-branch basis, which sub-predictor to trust. Used in the Alpha 21264 and modern Intel Core processors.

---

### QUESTION B (14 marks)

#### (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the scoreboard algorithm with a neat diagram. Illustrate the four-step instruction lifecycle and the conditions under which an instruction stalls at each step.**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (2 marks):** The CDC 6600 scoreboard is a **centralized hardware structure** that tracks:

- **Instruction status** — which step the instruction is in
- **Functional unit status** — `Busy`, `Fi` (operation), `Fj`, `Fk`, `Qj`, `Qk`, `Rj`, `Rk`
- **Register result status** — which FU will write each register (`Qi`)

**Four-step lifecycle (4 marks):**

| Step | Name | Action | Stall Condition |
|------|------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Issue** | Decode; check structural hazard; read `Qi`; update `Qi` for destination | Stall if the required FU is `Busy` |
| 2 | **Read Operands** | Wait until `Rj = YES` AND `Rk = YES` (no RAW on sources) | Stall if any source's `Qj/Qk` is still pending — i.e., a producer has not written yet |
| 3 | **Execution** | Operate on operands in the FU | Cannot stall (intrinsic to FU latency) |
| 4 | **Write Result** | Write to destination; clear `Qi` | Stall if any *current* instruction needs to read a source register that **this** instruction is about to write — i.e., a **WAR hazard** |

> [!NOTE]
> The scoreboard handles RAW by **stalling step 2** until the producer finishes. It handles WAR by **stalling step 4** so that older readers complete first. It handles WAW only by ensuring the older write-back finishes first (also via step-4 stalls).

**Limitation (1 mark):** The scoreboard does **not** rename registers. WAR and WAW hazards therefore cause stalls, limiting achievable ILP. This motivated Tomasulo's distributed renaming scheme.

#### (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Discuss the techniques to overcome data hazards in pipelined processors. How does dynamic scheduling with Tomasulo differ from static compiler scheduling? What is the role of the Reorder Buffer (ROB) in handling exceptions and speculation?**

**Model Solution:**

**Techniques to overcome data hazards (3 marks):**

1. **Forwarding (bypassing):** Route the result of EX/MEM directly back to the ALU input, eliminating most RAW stalls.
2. **Pipeline interleaving / instruction scheduling:** Compiler reorders independent instructions between a producer and a consumer to fill the delay slot.
3. **Load-use delay slot:** Compiler inserts an independent instruction between a load and its immediate consumer.
4. **Dynamic scheduling (Tomasulo / Scoreboard):** Hardware reorders execution at runtime, exposing ILP beyond what the compiler can statically see.
5. **Register renaming:** Eliminates WAR/WAW stalls.
6. **Speculation + ROB:** Executes past branches and rolls back on mispredict, with the ROB preserving precise exception state.

**Static vs. dynamic scheduling (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Static (Compiler) | Dynamic (Tomasulo / OoO) |
|--------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Decision time | Compile time | Run time |
| Visibility | Across basic blocks via unrolling | Across many in-flight instructions |
| Hardware cost | None (just decoder) | RS, CDB, rename table, ROB |
| Speculation | Conservative | Aggressive |
| Code density | High (VLIW) | Moderate (superscalar) |
| Power | Low | High |
| Examples | TI C6x, Itanium | Intel Core, AMD Zen, Apple M1 |

**Role of the Reorder Buffer (2 marks):**

The ROB is a circular FIFO that decouples **execution order** from **commit (retirement) order**. Each in-flight instruction occupies one ROB entry. Functions:

1. **In-order commit:** Instructions retire from the *head* of the ROB in program order, even though they may complete *out of order* in the execution units.
2. **Precise exceptions:** When an exception occurs, the trap is taken at the head of the ROB; all later instructions are flushed, restoring the architectural state to the last committed instruction.
3. **Speculative state recovery:** On a branch mispredict, all ROB entries past the mispredict are squashed; the head is set to the correct-path target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a ROB, out-of-order execution would make interrupts and exceptions *imprecise* — a debugging and OS nightmare. The ROB is the architectural bridge between OoO performance and in-order programmer-visible semantics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module 2 — Instruction Level Parallelism:**

- **ILP** = overlapping execution of independent instructions from a *single* program stream (not multiple threads).
- **CPI × IPC = 1** by definition. Goal of ILP techniques: drive $CPI < 1$.
- **5 hazards:** structural, RAW, WAR, WAW, control. Only **RAW** is a *true* data dependency.
- **Forwarding** fixes most RAW stalls; **renaming** fixes all WAR/WAW stalls; **branch prediction** fixes most control stalls.
- **Loop unrolling** trades code size for ILP — exposes $k$ independent iterations to the scheduler.
- **Scoreboard** (1964): centralized, no rename → stalls on WAR/WAW.
- **Tomasulo** (1967): distributed RS + CDB + implicit renaming → handles WAR/WAW.
- **Tomasulo key structures:** Instruction Queue, Reservation Stations, Common Data Bus, Register File with tag fields, Load/Store Buffers.
- **Branch predictors** in order of accuracy: 1-bit < 2-bit < 2-level local < 2-level global (correlating) < tournament < TAGE.
- **2-bit saturating counter** requires *two* mispredictions to flip → fixes the 1-bit predictor's "double mispredict" loop-exit problem.
- **Speculation** = execute past unresolved branches + retain results in a **Reorder Buffer** + commit in order + squash on mispredict.
- **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** = in-order commit queue, source of *precise exceptions* in OoO processors.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/n}$ → maximum $S = 1/(1-f)$.
- **Empirical ILP ceiling** (Wallach 1995): ~7 with realistic predictors, ~25 with perfect prediction and infinite resources, ~50 with perfect memory disambiguation.
- **ILP wall** is real — beyond it, architects must exploit **thread-level parallelism (TLP)** via multicore, SMT, or GPUs.
- **Modern case studies:** Intel Core i7 (4-wide OoO, 168-entry ROB), AMD Zen 4 (6-wide OoO), Apple M2 (8-wide OoO).
- **VLIW** = static ILP (compiler-packed bundles); **Superscalar** = dynamic ILP (hardware-decoded issue).
- **Forwarding paths** in a 5-stage MIPS pipeline: EX-to-EX (ALU→ALU), MEM-to-EX (load→ALU), and MEM-to-MEM (store→store in some designs).
- **Pipeline equation:** $CPI_{actual} = CPI_{ideal} + \sum \text{stalls} = 1 + \text{RAW stalls} + \text{control stalls} + \text{structural stalls}$.
- **Branch mispredict penalty** ≈ depth-of-pipeline-from-fetch-to-execute.
- **Speculation rollback cost** = $k \times T_{clock}$ where $k$ = pipeline depth at the predict site.
- **Key KTU textbook terminology** to use verbatim: "data dependency," "name dependency," "control dependency," "structural hazard," "RAW/WAR/WAW," "in-order commit," "out-of-order execution," "in-order issue."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Dependencies and Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Dependencies and Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Advanced Computer Architecture** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, instruction-level parallelism is fundamentally constrained by two closely related but conceptually distinct phenomena: **Data Dependencies** and **Pipeline Hazards**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Dependency** is a *property of the program* — it represents a logical ordering relationship between two instructions imposed by the data flow of the computation. A dependency exists irrespective of the underlying hardware, and it is the programmer's (or compiler's) responsibility to preserve it for correctness.

**Pipeline Hazard**, on the other hand, is a *property of the pipeline architecture* — it is the actual manifestation of a dependency (or a resource conflict) as a potential violation of correctness during overlapped (pipelined) execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield KTU Concept):** A dependency **always exists** between two instructions that share data. A hazard **may or may not occur**, depending on the *distance* (in clock cycles) between the interacting instructions and the *depth* of the pipeline.

The three classical classes of data dependencies that must be identified in any KTU-style problem are:

1. **Flow Dependency (True Data Dependency / Read-After-Write / RAW)**
2. **Anti-Dependency (Write-After-Read / WAR)**
3. **Output Dependency (Write-After-Write / WAW)**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line" Intuition

Imagine a small car-assembly line with three stations: **Welding → Painting → Inspection**. Each station takes exactly **1 hour**.

> **Analogy:** A worker at the *Painting* station cannot begin until the welder has produced the car body. This is a **True Data Dependency** — the output of station 1 is the input of station 2.
