| `N_RS` | Number of Reservation Stations | Typical: `3–6 per FU type` |
| `T_issue` | Issue latency | **1 cycle** (structural check only) |
| `T_exec` | Execution latency | Add: 2, Mult: 10, Div: 40 (clock cycles) |
| `T_WB` | Write-Back latency | **1 cycle** (single CDB write) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tomasulo's Issue Constraint:** Only **ONE instruction can issue per cycle** to a given RS. A single CDB allows **one write-back per cycle**, which can become a structural bottleneck for `N_RS > 1` simultaneously completing FUs.

### Performance Impact (Engineering Utility)

- **Speedup over in-order:** ~`1.5x` to `2.5x` for FP-heavy workloads (LINPACK, SPECfp).
- **Limiting factor:** **CDB contention** — when `N_FU >> 1` and multiple units finish simultaneously.
- **Real-world adoption:** Foundational to **Intel Pentium Pro (P6 micro-architecture, 1995)**, AMD K7/K8, IBM PowerPC.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough (Reservation Station Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disclaimer on Notation:** Tomasulo's scheme is **algorithmic, not formulaic**. Hence, the "derivation" here is a **deterministic state-machine walkthrough** showing exactly how `Vj`, `Vk`, `Qj`, `Qk`, `A`, `Busy`, and `Qi` evolve cycle by cycle — the kind of trace examiners love to set in 14-mark problems.

## 3.1 The Reference Code (Standard MIPS Floating-Point Sequence)

We use the canonical Hennessy-Patterson example to expose all three hazards:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{I1:} \quad & \text{L.D} \quad F6, \; 34(R2) \\
\text{I2:} \quad & \text{L.D} \quad F2, \; 45(R3) \\
\text{I3:} \quad & \text{MUL.D} \quad F0, \; F2, \; F4 \\
\text{I4:} \quad & \text{SUB.D} \quad F8, \; F6, \; F2 \\
\text{I5:} \quad & \text{DIV.D} \quad F10, \; F0, \; F6 \\
\text{I6:} \quad & \text{ADD.D} \quad F6, \; F8, \; F2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Assumed Initial State
- All RS are **free** (`Busy = No`).
- All `Qi[reg] = 0` (no pending writes).
- Effective addresses: `A1 = 100 + 34 = 134`, `A2 = 200 + 45 = 245`.
- `F4 = 5.0`, `F2_initial = 0.0` (irrelevant, will be overwritten).

---

## 3.2 Reservation Station Template (Each Entry)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RS Entry} \; = \; \{ \; \text{Busy}, \;\; \text{Op}, \;\; \text{Vj}, \;\; \text{Vk}, \;\; \text{Qj}, \;\; \text{Qk}, \;\; \text{A} \; \}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Load buffers**, only `A` and `Busy` are needed (single source operand: address).
For **Store buffers**, both `A`, `Vj/Qj` are tracked.

---

## 3.3 Cycle-by-Cycle Issue Stage (Stage 1)

| Cycle | Instruction | Action | RS Allocated | `Qj` / `Qk` | `Qi[dest]` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `L.D F6, 34(R2)` | Issue to **Load1** | Load1: `{Busy=Y, Op=LOAD, A=134}` | — | `Qi[F6] = Load1` |
| 2 | `L.D F2, 45(R3)` | Issue to **Load2** | Load2: `{Busy=Y, Op=LOAD, A=245}` | — | `Qi[F2] = Load2` |
| 3 | `MUL.D F0, F2, F4` | Issue to **Mult1** | Mult1: `{Op=MUL, Vj=5.0, Qj=Load2, Vk=0, Qk=0}` | F2 pending | `Qi[F0] = Mult1` |
| 4 | `SUB.D F8, F6, F2` | Issue to **Add1** | Add1: `{Op=SUB, Qj=Load1, Qk=Load2}` | Both pending | `Qi[F8] = Add1` |
| 5 | `DIV.D F10, F0, F6` | Issue to **Mult2** | Mult2: `{Op=DIV, Qj=Mult1, Qk=Load1}` | Both pending | `Qi[F10] = Mult2` |
| 6 | `ADD.D F6, F8, F2` | Issue to **Add2** | Add2: `{Op=ADD, Qj=Add1, Qk=Load2}` | Both pending | `Qi[F6] = Add2`  ⚠️ **WAW rename** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation at Cycle 6:** `Qi[F6]` is **overwritten** from `Load1` to `Add2`. This is **register renaming via Qi-tag overwriting** — `I1` is still in flight, but the register "F6" now points to `Add2`. **No WAW stall occurred.** This is impossible in a scoreboard.

### Register File Status After Cycle 6

$$
\begin{aligned}
F0 &: \text{Qi} = \text{Mult1} \\
F2 &: \text{Qi} = \text{Load2} \\
F4 &: \text{Qi} = 0 \quad (\text{ready, value} = 5.0) \\
F6 &: \text{Qi} = \text{Add2} \quad (\text{WAW-renamed}) \\
F8 &: \text{Qi} = \text{Add1} \\
F10 &: \text{Qi} = \text{Mult2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Cycle-by-Cycle Execute Stage (Stage 2)

**Rule:** An RS becomes executable when `Qj = 0` AND `Qk = 0`.

| Cycle | Event | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | **Load1** finishes, value `mem[134]` → CDB | Load1 latency (assumed 1 cycle after issue + 5 mem cycles ≈ cycle 7) |
| 7 | Add1: `Qj` (was `Load1`) cleared → `Vj = mem[134]` | CDB capture |
| 7 | Mult2: `Qk` (was `Load1`) cleared → `Vk = mem[134]` | CDB capture |
| 7 | Register `F6` — does **NOT** update, because `Qi[F6] = Add2 ≠ Load1` | **WAW-safe** — old result ignored |
| 8 | **Load2** finishes, value `mem[245]` → CDB | |
| 8 | Add1: `Qk` (was `Load2`) cleared → `Vk = mem[245]` | Now **both operands ready** |
| 8 | Add2: `Qk` (was `Load2`) cleared → `Vk = mem[245]` | |
| 8 | Mult1: `Qj` (was `Load2`) cleared → `Vj = mem[245]` | Now **both operands ready** |
| 9 | **Add1** starts (Add FU, 2-cycle latency) | Both operands ready |
| 9 | **Add2** starts (parallel Add FU) | Both operands ready |
| 9 | **Mult1** starts (10-cycle latency) | Both operands ready |
| 10 | **Mult2** still waiting — needs `Mult1` result | `Qj[Mult2] = Mult1 ≠ 0` |
| 11 | **Add1** completes → CDB, `F8 ← result`, `Qi[F8] = 0` | Add2 captures `Vj = result` |
| 11 | **Add2** completes → CDB, `F6 ← result`, `Qi[F6] = 0` (final WAW winner) | |
| 19 | **Mult1** completes → CDB, `F0 ← result` | Mult2 captures `Vj` |
| 20 | **Mult2** starts (40-cycle Div latency) | |
| 60 | **Mult2** completes → CDB, `F10 ← result` | Pipeline drained |

---

## 3.5 Key Insights from the Walkthrough

1. **6 instructions issued in 6 consecutive cycles** — no structural stall beyond the 1-per-cycle issue rule.
2. **Zero WAR stalls:** `I6` was renamed to `Add2`; it never writes to the `F6` register that `I1` is using.
3. **Zero WAW stalls:** Even though both `I1` and `I6` write to `F6`, only `Add2`'s result is committed to the register file.
4. **RAW preserved:** `I3` waits for `I2` only via the CDB, not via the register file.
5. **Total issue-to-completion:** ~60 cycles vs. ~150+ in a strict in-order pipeline.

---

## 3.6 Why the CDB is the Heart of the Algorithm

The CDB implements the equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forwarding Path} = \bigcup_{k=1}^{N_{RS}} \left( \text{FU}_k \rightarrow \text{CDB} \rightarrow \{ \text{RS}_j \mid Q_j = k \} \cup \text{RegisterFile} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Every cycle, **at most one** FU drives the CDB, but the same value can be **captured by arbitrarily many** RS entries in a single cycle. This is the **physical manifestation of "operands chasing instructions"** that defines Tomasulo's philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete data path** of Tomasulo's scheme. The CDB is shown as the central nervous system, fanning out to every RS and the register file.

```mermaid
graph TD
    IQ[Instruction Queue]
    DEC[Decode and Rename Logic]
    RS1[RS Add1]
    RS2[RS Add2]
    RS3[RS Mult1]
    RS4[RS Mult2]
    RS5[Load1 Buffer]
    RS6[Load2 Buffer]
    FUA[FP Adder Unit]
    FUM[FP Multiplier Unit]
    FUL[Load Unit]
    RF[Register File with Qi Status]
    CDB[Common Data Bus]
    IQ --> DEC
    DEC --> RS1
    DEC --> RS2
    DEC --> RS3
    DEC --> RS4
    DEC --> RS5
    DEC --> RS6
    RS1 --> FUA
    RS2 --> FUA
    RS3 --> FUM
    RS4 --> FUM
    RS5 --> FUL
    RS6 --> FUL
    FUA --> CDB
    FUM --> CDB
    FUL --> CDB
    CDB --> RS1
    CDB --> RS2
    CDB --> RS3
    CDB --> RS4
    CDB --> RS5
    CDB --> RS6
    CDB --> RF
    RF -.status read.-> DEC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **solid arrows** are operand/data flow. The **dotted arrow** from `RF` to `DEC` carries the `Qi` tags used for renaming during the Issue stage.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Reservation Station Internal Structure

This diagram shows what happens *inside* a single RS entry when an instruction is issued. It is the most commonly asked diagram in KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Incoming Instruction]
    DECODE[Decode Op and Dest]
    SRC1[Read Qi for Source 1 Register]
    SRC2[Read Qi for Source 2 Register]
    CHK1{Qi equal to 0?}
    VJ[Capture Value into Vj]
    QJT[Capture Tag into Qj]
    CHK2{Qi equal to 0?}
    VK[Capture Value into Vk]
    QKT[Capture Tag into Qk]
    WRITE[Write RS Entry: Busy set, Op set]
    QID[Update Qi of Dest Register to this RS]
    WAIT[Wait on CDB for Qj and Qk]
    READY{Both Qj and Qk equal 0?}
    EXEC[Dispatch to Functional Unit]
    OUT[Execute and Write Result to CDB]
    IN --> DECODE
    DECODE --> SRC1
    DECODE --> SRC2
    SRC1 --> CHK1
    CHK2 --> CHK2
    CHK1 -- Yes --> VJ
    CHK1 -- No --> QJT
    VJ --> WRITE
    QJT --> WRITE
    SRC2 --> CHK2
    CHK2 -- Yes --> VK
    CHK2 -- No --> QKT
    VK --> WRITE
    QKT --> WRITE
    WRITE --> QID
    QID --> WAIT
    WAIT --> READY
    READY -- Yes --> EXEC
    READY -- No --> WAIT
    EXEC --> OUT
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why two `CHK1` and `CHK2` boxes?** In Mermaid, each decision must have a unique node ID. They represent the **parallel** checks for the two source operands of the same instruction.

---

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram for a Single RS Entry

This shows the **lifecycle** of a single Reservation Station slot — useful for KTU short-answer questions on "stages of Tomasulo's algorithm".

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[State 0: Free Busy equal No]
    S1[State 1: Issued Waiting for Operands]
    S2[State 2: Operands Ready Executing]
    S3[State 3: Result on CDB Writing Back]
    S0 -- Issue instruction --> S1
    S1 -- Both operands captured from CDB --> S2
    S1 -- All operands already ready at issue --> S2
    S2 -- FU completes --> S3
    S3 -- Register file updated and CDB drained --> S0
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Alternative View)

For students who find Mermaid text-flow diagrams easier to memorize, here is a **functional pipeline view**:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Stage 1: Issue and Rename]
    B[Stage 2: Execute on FU]
    C[Stage 3: Write Result on CDB]
    D[Update Register File and Capturing RS]
    A -- operands or tags captured --> B
    B -- result computed --> C
    C -- broadcast --> D
    D -- go to free state --> A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. List the three stages of Tomasulo's algorithm and state the hazard type resolved at each stage.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The three stages of Tomasulo's algorithm are:

1. **Issue (Dispatch):** The instruction is decoded and a free **Reservation Station** is allocated. The register status fields (`Qi`) are read to determine if source operands are ready. **WAR and WAW hazards** are eliminated here through **register renaming** (overwriting the `Qi` field of the destination register).
2. **Execute:** The RS waits on the **CDB** for any pending operands (tags `Qj` or `Qk`). Execution begins on the Functional Unit only when **both operands are captured**. The **RAW (Read-After-Write) hazard** is resolved at this stage without stalling.
3. **Write Result:** The result is broadcast on the **Common Data Bus** with the source RS's tag. All waiting RS entries and the register file are updated in a single cycle. **Structural hazards** are implicitly handled by the single-CDB-per-cycle constraint.

---

### **Q2. What is the role of the Common Data Bus (CDB) in Tomasulo's scheme? Why is it a bottleneck?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Common Data Bus (CDB)** is the **central broadcast channel** that carries the result of a completed Functional Unit (along with its source RS tag) to **all Reservation Stations** and the **Register File** in a single cycle. Its role is to implement **data forwarding** without writing back to the architectural register file first — this is the core mechanism that eliminates RAW stalls.

**Bottleneck:** The CDB is a **single shared physical wire (or bus)**, meaning **only one FU can broadcast per cycle**. In processors with many parallel FUs (e.g., 4 multipliers + 2 adders), if 3 results complete simultaneously, two must wait. This is known as **CDB contention** and is the primary throughput limit of the original Tomasulo design.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain the architecture of Tomasulo's algorithm with a neat block diagram. How does it differ from the Scoreboard mechanism? `[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Architecture Overview (3 marks):**
Tomasulo's scheme replaces the centralized Scoreboard with a **distributed control structure** consisting of:
- **Instruction Queue** holding fetched instructions.
- **Reservation Stations (RS)** associated with each Functional Unit. Each RS entry contains: `{Op, Vj, Vk, Qj, Qk, A, Busy}`.
- **Register File with status fields** (`Qi` per register) indicating which RS will next produce the value.
- **Common Data Bus (CDB)** that broadcasts completed results to all RS and the Register File.

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram of these notes for the full architecture.)*

**3. Key Differences from Scoreboard (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Scoreboard | Tomasulo's Scheme |
|---|---|---|
| Control | Centralized (single Scoreboard) | Distributed (per-RS) |
| Forwarding | None — write-then-read register | Via **CDB** — direct RS to RS |
| WAR hazard | Stalls the pipeline | **Resolved** by register renaming |
| WAW hazard | Stalls the pipeline | **Resolved** by `Qi` overwriting |
| Operand source | Register file only | RS or Register file |
| Hardware renaming | No | Yes — implicit via RS tags |

---

#### **(b) With a suitable example code sequence, illustrate the step-by-step working of Tomasulo's algorithm. Show clearly how WAW hazards are resolved. `[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Code Sequence (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{I1:} \quad & \text{L.D} \quad F6, 34(R2) \\
\text{I2:} \quad & \text{L.D} \quad F2, 45(R3) \\
\text{I3:} \quad & \text{MUL.D} \quad F0, F2, F4 \\
\text{I4:} \quad & \text{SUB.D} \quad F8, F6, F2 \\
\text{I5:} \quad & \text{DIV.D} \quad F10, F0, F6 \\
\text{I6:} \quad & \text{ADD.D} \quad F6, F8, F2
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Issue Trace (3 marks):**

- **Cycle 1:** `I1` issues to `Load1`; `Qi[F6] = Load1`.
- **Cycle 2:** `I2` issues to `Load2`; `Qi[F2] = Load2`.
- **Cycle 3:** `I3` issues to `Mult1`; `Qj = Load2` (F2 pending); `Vk = 5.0` (F4 ready); `Qi[F0] = Mult1`.
- **Cycle 4:** `I4` issues to `Add1`; `Qj = Load1`, `Qk = Load2`; `Qi[F8] = Add1`.
- **Cycle 5:** `I5` issues to `Mult2`; `Qj = Mult1`, `Qk = Load1`; `Qi[F10] = Mult2`.
- **Cycle 6:** `I6` issues to `Add2`; `Qj = Add1`, `Qk = Load2`; `Qi[F6] = Add2`  *(overwrites Load1 — this is the WAW rename!)*.

**3. WAW Resolution Explanation (3 marks):**
- Both `I1` (`L.D → F6`) and `I6` (`ADD.D → F6`) write to the same architectural register `F6`.
- In a **Scoreboard**, `I6` would have to **stall** at issue because `F6` was already pending in `Load1`.
- In **Tomasulo's scheme**, at Cycle 6, the `Qi[F6]` field is **overwritten** from `Load1` to `Add2`. The architectural register "F6" now logically points to `Add2`.
- When `Load1` completes (Cycle 7), it broadcasts on the CDB. The Register File checks its `Qi[F6]` value, which is `Add2 ≠ Load1`, and **ignores the broadcast**. Only `Add2`'s eventual result is committed to `F6`.
- **Net effect:** `I6` was not stalled. Both hazards — **WAR (read F2, then later F6 overwritten) and WAW** — were resolved purely in hardware via renaming.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Explain the three stages of Tomasulo's algorithm in detail. What information is stored in each Reservation Station entry? `[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Information Stored in a Reservation Station Entry (2 marks):**
Each RS entry holds the following fields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RS Entry} = \{ \; \text{Busy}, \; \text{Op}, \; \text{Vj}, \; \text{Vk}, \; \text{Qj}, \; \text{Qk}, \; \text{A} \; \}
\end{aligned}
$$

- `Busy` — flag indicating if the RS is occupied.
- `Op` — operation to be performed (e.g., MUL, ADD, SUB).
- `Vj, Vk` — actual values of source operands (valid only when `Qj = 0` and `Qk = 0`).
- `Qj, Qk` — tags (RS names) that will *produce* the source operand. `0` means the value is already available.
- `A` — effective address (used only for Load/Store buffers).

**2. The Three Stages (5 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Issue:**
- Instruction is fetched from the IQ and decoded.
- A free RS is allocated (else stall).
- Source register tags are looked up: if `Qi[reg] = 0`, value is copied into `Vj`/`Vk`; else the `Qi` tag is copied into `Qj`/`Qk`.
- Destination register's `Qi` is set to the allocated RS — this is the **rename** point.
- **Hazard resolved:** WAR and WAW.

**Stage 2 — Execute:**
- The RS monitors the CDB.
- Whenever a result tagged `RSk` arrives on the CDB, every RS with `Qj = RSk` or `Qk = RSk` captures the value into `Vj` or `Vk` and clears the corresponding `Q` field.
- When both `Qj = 0` and `Qk = 0`, the operation is **dispatched** to the Functional Unit.
- **Hazard resolved:** RAW (true data dependence).

**Stage 3 — Write Result:**
- The FU finishes, and the result is **broadcast on the CDB** with the source RS's tag.
- All RS entries with matching `Qj` or `Qk` capture the value.
- The Register File is updated **only if** `Qi[Fdest] = source RS tag`; otherwise the broadcast is ignored.
- The RS is freed (`Busy = No`).
- **Hazard resolved:** Structural (only one CDB write per cycle).

---

#### **(b) Compare Tomasulo's algorithm with the Scoreboard mechanism. State two advantages and two disadvantages of Tomasulo's scheme. `[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Scoreboard | Tomasulo's Scheme |
|---|---|---|
| Control Unit | Centralized | Distributed (RS) |
| Hazard detection | Central logic | Per-RS logic + CDB tags |
| Operand forwarding | None (WAW/WAR stalls) | Via CDB (no stalls) |
| Register renaming | Not supported | Implicit via `Qi` tags |
| Issue stage stalls on | Structural + WAR + WAW | Structural only |
| Hardware complexity | Lower | Higher (more storage in RS) |
| CDB requirement | Not needed | Essential (bottleneck risk) |
| Typical use | CDC 6600 (1964) | IBM 360/91, modern OoO cores |

**2. Advantages of Tomasulo's Scheme (2 marks):**
- **Higher ILP:** Eliminates WAR and WAW stalls, allowing out-of-order execution with minimal blocking.
- **Hardware register renaming:** Decouples architectural registers from physical producers, simplifying compiler support and improving loop parallelism.
- **Forwarding without WB stall:** Consumers can capture operands directly from CDB, often *before* the producer's write-back to the register file.

**3. Disadvantages of Tomasulo's Scheme (2 marks):**
- **CDB bottleneck:** Single shared bus limits throughput when many FUs complete simultaneously.
- **High hardware cost:** Each RS entry stores two value fields and two tag fields, which scales poorly with `N_RS` and operand width (e.g., 64-bit FP).
- **Complexity of tag matching:** Comparators on every RS for every CDB broadcast are power- and area-intensive.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in 14-Mark Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to show `Qi` register status updates after Issue.** Many students show the RS state but never write the updated `Qi[F6] = Add2` line. Examiners allocate **at least 1–2 marks** specifically for the WAW rename evidence — *without it, the hazard resolution is unproven.*
> 
> 2. **Confusing the order of operand capture.** The correct convention is `Qj ↔ first source operand` and `Qk ↔ second source operand` (Hennessy-Patterson notation). Swapping them in the trace still produces a "working" simulation but will be marked **wrong on the first cycle** by strict examiners.
> 
> 3. **Writing the result to the register file for ALL CDB broadcasts.** The register file should update only when `Qi[reg] = source_tag`. Writing `F6 ← result(Load1)` at Cycle 7 is a **classic error** that ignores the WAW rename at Cycle 6.
> 
> 4. **Not distinguishing structural vs. data hazards in the issue stage.** Scoreboard stalls on **structural + WAR + WAW**. Tomasulo stalls on **structural only** — examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Tomasulo's scheme = RS + CDB + Register Renaming.** Without any one of these, the algorithm degenerates into a scoreboard.
- ✅ **Three stages, in order: Issue → Execute → Write Result.** Memorize the exact hazard resolved at each stage.
- ✅ **WAR and WAW are eliminated at ISSUE** by overwriting `Qi[dest]`.
- ✅ **RAW is resolved at EXECUTE** by waiting on the CDB for missing operand tags.
- ✅ **Structural hazard remains** at issue (RS busy check) and at write-back (single CDB per cycle).
- ✅ **`Qj` and `Qk` are tags, NOT values.** `Vj` and `Vk` are values. A tag of `0` means the value is ready.
- ✅ **Register file is updated ONLY if `Qi[reg] = source_tag`.** This single rule is what makes WAW renaming safe.
- ✅ **For Loads/Stores:** Use the `A` field for the effective address; `Vj/Qj` for stores holds the value-to-be-stored.
- ✅ **Performance bound:** CDB contention becomes the dominant bottleneck when `N_FU ≥ 4` in the same latency class.
- ✅ **Historical note:** Original target was the **IBM 360/91 FP unit** (1967). Adopted widely in **Intel P6 (1995)**, **AMD K7/K8**, **IBM Power** families.
- ✅ **KTU-style expected answer length:** Part A → 3–4 lines per sub-point. Part B (a)/(b) → 1.5 to 2 pages each, with at least one full reservation-station state table and one diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Register Renaming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Register Renaming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Register Renaming** is a hardware microarchitectural technique used in **Out-of-Order Execution (OoOE)** processors that dynamically maps the programmer-visible **Architectural Registers** (a fixed, limited set defined by the ISA — e.g., 32 integer + 32 floating-point registers in MIPS/RISC-V) onto a much larger pool of internal **Physical Registers** (PRs), in order to dynamically break **false dependencies** (WAR and WAW hazards) while preserving the program's true data flow (RAW dependencies).

The technique was first proposed by **Tomasulo's Algorithm (1967)** in the IBM System/360 Model 91, where *Reservation Stations* effectively performed implicit register renaming by tagging operands with their producing reservation-station identifier.

### Architectural vs. Physical Registers

| Aspect | Architectural Registers | Physical Registers |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Visibility** | Visible to the programmer (ISA) | Internal to hardware only |
| **Quantity** | Fixed & small (e.g., **32**) | Large pool (e.g., **128 – 256**) |
| **Naming** | Symbolic: `R0, R1, … R31` | Hardware tags: `P0, P1, … Pn-1` |
| **Purpose** | Hold program state | Provide temporaries for in-flight instructions |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight: The syllabus explicitly lists *Tomasulo's Algorithm* and *Scoreboarding* as foundational OoOE schemes. **Register Renaming** is the formal generalization that solves the scalability bottlenecks of Tomasulo's reservation stations by using a centralized *Register Alias Table (RAT)*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel Key Card System"

Imagine a hotel (the CPU) with **20 official rooms** (architectural registers). These rooms are the *only* ones guests (the programmer's instructions) are *supposed* to occupy, but the hotel is constantly renovating and moving people around to clean efficiently.

* **Front Desk (RAT — Register Alias Table):** Acts as the directory. It does **not** say "Room 5 is Mr. Smith." Instead, it says *"Logical Key Card #5 is currently assigned to physical room #47."*
* **Physical Rooms (Physical Registers):** There are actually **100 physical rooms** in the hotel, even though guests only know about 20.
* **Why?** If Instruction A "writes to Room 5" and Instruction B "reads from Room 5," and they execute out of order, the **front desk** ensures A's key card still points to room 47, while B is given a *new* key card pointing to a *different* physical room (say #52). The guest never knows. The room numbers (architectural registers) are just *labels*; the real state is in the physical rooms.
* **Checkout (Commit/Retire):** When a guest finally leaves (instruction retires), the original architectural room is updated to reflect the latest physical state, and the old physical room is returned to the *Free List* for reuse.

This is exactly how a modern Intel/AMD out-of-order core operates.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Register Renaming Is Necessary — The Three Data Hazards

To understand renaming, we must first classify the **three types of register data dependencies** that exist between any two instructions *i* (earlier) and *j* (later):

### 2.1.1 Read-After-Write (RAW) — **True Dependency**
* **Definition:** `j` reads a register that `i` writes.
* **Equation:** `i: R3 ← R1 + R2`  followed by  `j: R5 ← R3 + R4`
* **Nature:** **Genuine** data flow. **MUST be preserved.** Renaming cannot eliminate it.

### 2.1.2 Write-After-Read (WAR) — **Anti-Dependency**
* **Definition:** `j` writes a register that `i` reads.
* **Equation:** `i: R3 ← R1 + R2`  followed by  `j: R1 ← R5 + R6`
* **Nature:** **False** dependency. Caused only because both instructions share the *same name* (`R1`). Renaming **ELIMINATES** this by giving each write a fresh physical register.

### 2.1.3 Write-After-Write (WAW) — **Output Dependency**
* **Definition:** Both `i` and `j` write to the same register.
* **Equation:** `i: R1 ← R2 + R3`  followed by  `j: R1 ← R5 + R6`
* **Nature:** **False** dependency. The final value should be `j`'s, but an out-of-order execution might commit `i`'s value last. Renaming **ELIMINATES** this by mapping each write to a *different* physical register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Only **WAR** and **WAW** are eliminated by renaming. **RAW (true) dependencies are preserved**, because the renaming logic (RAT) only updates the mapping *when the instruction is dispatched*, and a later instruction's source operand is resolved by waiting for the producing physical register's tag (not the architectural name).

---

## 2.2 Architectural Pipeline Position of the Renaming Stage

In a typical modern superscalar OoOE pipeline (Issue Width = N), **Register Renaming** occurs in the **Rename / Dispatch stage**, which sits between **Decode** and **Issue/Queue**.

1. **Fetch** → instruction bytes are read from I-cache.
2. **Decode** → instruction is broken into uops; source/destination architectural registers are identified.
3. **Rename (Dispatch)** → Architectural destinations are mapped to NEW physical registers from the Free List; architectural sources are looked up in the RAT to get their current physical register tags.
4. **Issue / Wakeup** → uops wait in Issue Queues until operands are ready.
5. **Execute (ROB-ALUs)** → uops execute on functional units.
6. **Writeback (Complete)** → results are broadcast on the Common Data Bus (CDB) and forwarded to waiting uops.
7. **Commit (Retire)** → In-order retirement; the architectural register file is updated; physical registers are freed.

---

## 2.3 The Register Alias Table (RAT)

The **RAT** is the heart of register renaming. It is a small, fully-associative Content-Addressable Memory (CAM) structure with one entry per *architectural register*.

### 2.3.1 RAT Entry Format
Each entry contains:
* **Valid Bit (V):** 1 if the mapping is valid.
* **Physical Register Tag (PR_Tag):** The current physical register holding the latest, *most-recently-renamed-but-not-yet-committed* value for that architectural register.

### 2.3.2 Auxiliary Structures
* **Free List (FL):** A FIFO/LIFO queue of unused Physical Register tags. When a uop needs a destination, a tag is *popped* from FL. When a uop retires, the *previous* mapping (saved in the ROB or checkpoint) is restored, and the now-stale PR is *pushed back* onto FL.
* **Reorder Buffer (ROB):** Holds in-flight uops in program order. On retirement, it commits results to the **Architectural Register File (ARF)** and releases physical registers.
* **Architectural Register File (ARF):** Holds the committed, programmer-visible state.

> [!NOTE]
> Two design families exist:
> * **ROB-based (MIPS R10000, early Intel P6):** ROB holds the value AND tags the architectural mapping. Physical registers only hold speculative values.
> **Register-File-based (AMD K7/K8, modern Intel):** Physical registers hold both speculative AND committed values; the RAT directly maps architectural → physical. ROB only holds status. **This is more common in modern CPUs.**

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IPC Upper Bound (no hazards)** | $IPC_{max} = \text{Issue Width} = N$ | Instructions / cycle |
| **CPI Lower Bound** | $CPI_{min} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Cycles / instruction |
| **Physical Registers (R10k-style)** | $PR_{total} = R_{arch} + W$ | $W$ = window (in-flight) size |
| **Renaming Stall Condition** | $FreeList = \emptyset \Rightarrow \text{stall at dispatch}$ | Hazard: *Free-list empty* |
| **ROB Full Stall** | $ROB_{entries} = 0 \Rightarrow \text{stall at dispatch}$ | Hazard: *ROB full* |
| **Branch Misprediction Recovery** | $Free\_push = \text{all PRs allocated after branch}$ | Must restore RAT checkpoint |
| **Speculative State** | $\vert \text{SpecState}\vert \le \text{ROB depth}$ | Limits speculation depth |
| **Branch Penalty** | $P_{branch} = m \cdot \vert\pi\vert$ | $m$ = mispredict rate, $\vert\pi\vert$ = pipeline depth |

> [!WARNING]
> In a markdown table, we use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value bars — never the bare pipe `|` character — because the pipe is the column separator and would break the table rendering.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility & Industry Relevance

Register renaming is the **single most important microarchitectural innovation** enabling modern single-threaded CPU performance. It is the reason why:

* **Intel/AMD CPUs** can sustain an IPC near their issue width even on integer code with heavy register pressure.
* **Branch prediction + Renaming** together form the *speculative execution engine* that powers the **> 5 GHz desktop CPUs** of 2024.
* **Hardware Security:** The same structures (ROB, Free List, RAT) that perform renaming are the basis for transient execution attacks like **Spectre & Meltdown (2018)** — a critical topic in modern computer-architecture security.
* **Compiler Back-Ends:** Modern compilers (GCC, LLVM) perform *static* register allocation to minimize false dependencies, but only the hardware can handle *dynamic* aliasing from pointers, recursion, and dynamically-resolved targets.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Eliminating WAR and WAW Hazards

This is a **high-yield KTU problem**. Master this format.

### 3.1.1 Original Program (Programmer's View)

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &: \quad R1 \leftarrow R2 + R3 \\
I_2 &: \quad R4 \leftarrow R1 \times R5 \\
I_3 &: \quad R1 \leftarrow R6 - R7 \\
I_4 &: \quad R8 \leftarrow R1 \div R9
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Identify Dependencies (Without Renaming)

| Instruction Pair | Dependency Type | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $I_1 \to I_2$ | **RAW (True)** on R1 | $I_2$ needs $I_1$'s result. **Must serialize.** |
| $I_1 \to I_3$ | **WAW (Output)** on R1 | Both write R1. **Can be eliminated.** |
| $I_2 \to I_3$ | **WAR (Anti)** on R1 | $I_3$ writes R1 that $I_2$ reads. **Can be eliminated.** |
| $I_3 \to I_4$ | **RAW (True)** on R1 | $I_4$ needs $I_3$'s result. **Must serialize.** |

> Without renaming, only $I_1$ and $I_2$ may execute in program order; $I_3$ must wait for $I_2$ to read R1; $I_4$ must wait for $I_3$. **No parallelism between $I_3$ and $\{I_1, I_2\}$.**

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Allocate Physical Registers (Free List)

Assume Free List initially contains: `{P10, P11, P12, P13, …}` and architectural `R0` is mapped to `P0`, `R1 → P1`, `R2 → P2`, `R3 → P3`, `R4 → P4`, `R5 → P5`, `R6 → P6`, `R7 → P7`, `R8 → P8`, `R9 → P9`.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Rename In Program Order

We walk through each instruction. The RAT is updated for the *destination*. Sources are looked up in the RAT *at dispatch time*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Dispatch } I_1 &: \quad \text{Dest}=R1,\ \text{Srcs}=R2,R3 \\
&\quad \text{Src tags} \leftarrow \text{RAT}[R2]=P2,\ \text{RAT}[R3]=P3 \\
&\quad \text{Pop new tag from FL} \Rightarrow P10 \\
&\quad \text{RAT}[R1] \leftarrow P10 \quad (\text{old } P1 \text{ kept aside for future free}) \\
&\quad \textbf{Renamed: } P10 \leftarrow P2 + P3 \\
\\
\textbf{Dispatch } I_2 &: \quad \text{Dest}=R4,\ \text{Srcs}=R1,R5 \\
&\quad \text{Src tags} \leftarrow \text{RAT}[R1]=P10,\ \text{RAT}[R5]=P5 \\
&\quad \text{Pop new tag} \Rightarrow P11 \\
&\quad \text{RAT}[R4] \leftarrow P11 \\
&\quad \textbf{Renamed: } P11 \leftarrow P10 \times P5 \\
\\
\textbf{Dispatch } I_3 &: \quad \text{Dest}=R1,\ \text{Srcs}=R6,R7 \\
&\quad \text{Src tags} \leftarrow \text{RAT}[R6]=P6,\ \text{RAT}[R7]=P7 \\
&\quad \text{Pop new tag} \Rightarrow P12 \\
&\quad \text{RAT}[R1] \leftarrow P12 \quad (\text{overwrites P10 mapping in RAT, but } I_2 \text{ already captured } P10) \\
&\quad \textbf{Renamed: } P12 \leftarrow P6 - P7 \\
\\
\textbf{Dispatch } I_4 &: \quad \text{Dest}=R8,\ \text{Srcs}=R1,R9 \\
&\quad \text{Src tags} \leftarrow \text{RAT}[R1]=P12,\ \text{RAT}[R9]=P9 \\
&\quad \text{Pop new tag} \Rightarrow P13 \\
&\quad \text{RAT}[R8] \leftarrow P13 \\
&\quad \textbf{Renamed: } P13 \leftarrow P12 \div P9
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Analyze the Renamed Program

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &: \quad P10 \leftarrow P2 + P3 \\
I_2 &: \quad P11 \leftarrow P10 \times P5 \\
I_3 &: \quad P12 \leftarrow P6 - P7 \\
I_4 &: \quad P13 \leftarrow P12 \div P9
\end{aligned}
$$

| Pair | Old Dependency | New Dependency | Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $I_1 \to I_2$ | RAW on R1 | RAW on P10 | **Preserved (True)** |
| $I_1 \to I_3$ | WAW on R1 | None | **Eliminated** ✅ |
| $I_2 \to I_3$ | WAR on R1 | None | **Eliminated** ✅ |
| $I_3 \to I_4$ | RAW on R1 | RAW on P12 | **Preserved (True)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $I_3$ can now execute **in parallel** with $I_1$ and $I_2$, because it no longer conflicts with the architectural register `R1`. The OoOE engine schedules them based on the **physical** dependencies only.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Commit (In-Order Retirement)

On retirement (via ROB), the Architectural Register File is updated:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Retire } I_1 &: \quad ARF[R1] \leftarrow \text{commit}(P10) \\
\text{Retire } I_2 &: \quad ARF[R4] \leftarrow \text{commit}(P11) \\
\text{Retire } I_3 &: \quad ARF[R1] \leftarrow \text{commit}(P12) \quad \text{(final value of R1)} \\
\text{Retire } I_4 &: \quad ARF[R8] \leftarrow \text{commit}(P13)
\end{aligned}
$$

Physical registers `P1`, `P4`, `P10` (overwritten), and `P12` (overwritten on next write to R1) are recycled to the **Free List** at appropriate points.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Renaming Schemes Comparison

| Scheme | Year | Renaming Granularity | Storage for Speculative State | Recovery on Mispredict | KTU Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tomasulo** | 1967 | Reservation Station ID (implicit) | Reservation Station + CDB tags | Stall CDB / flush RS | High (Foundational) |
| **Scoreboard** | 1964 | None (uses ARF only) | ARF | Stall pipeline | High (Comparison) |
| **ROB-based Rename** (P6 / R10000) | 1990s | Architectural → ROB ID | ROB entries | Restore ARF, flush ROB | Medium |
| **Physical Reg Rename** (K7 / modern Intel) | 2000s+ | Architectural → PR Tag | Physical Register File | Restore RAT via checkpoint | **Highest (Modern)** |

---

## 3.3 Branch Misprediction Recovery — Step-by-Step

When a branch is mispredicted, all speculative state must be rolled back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1} &: \text{Branch detected as mispredicted at Execute stage.} \\
\textbf{Step 2} &: \text{Identify ROB entries allocated AFTER the branch.} \\
\textbf{Step 3} &: \text{For each such entry, push its destination physical tag back onto the Free List.} \\
\textbf{Step 4} &: \text{Restore RAT to the checkpoint saved at branch dispatch.} \\
\textbf{Step 5} &: \text{Flush the Issue Queues of those entries.} \\
\textbf{Step 6} &: \text{Refetch and re-dispatch from the correct-path PC.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cost is proportional to the *speculation depth* — hence KTU's emphasis on shallow, accurate branch predictors.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pipeline Diagram Showing the Rename Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Fetch] --> D[Decode]
    D --> R["Rename / Dispatch"]
    R --> IQ["Issue Queue / Reservation Stations"]
    IQ --> E["Execute ALUs"]
    E --> WB["Writeback CDB"]
    WB --> CM["Commit / Retire"]
    CM --> ARF[("Architectural Register File")]
    R -. "allocates from" .-> FL[("Free List")]
    R -. "updates" .-> RAT[("Register Alias Table RAT")]
    FL -. "recycled on commit" .-> CM
    RAT -. "checkpoint on branch" .-> CB["Checkpoint Buffer"]
    CB -. "restore on mispredict" .-> R
    IQ -. "wakeup via tag broadcast" .-> WB
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Physical Register File Renaming Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DEC["Decode Stage"]
        DE[Instruction Decoder]
    end
    subgraph REN["Rename Stage"]
        RAT[("RAT Register Alias Table")]
        FL[("Free List")]
        CKPT[("Checkpoint Stack for Branches")]
    end
    subgraph EXE["Issue and Execute"]
        IQ1[Issue Queue INT]
        IQ2[Issue Queue FP]
        ALU1[Integer ALU]
        ALU2[FP Unit]
    end
    subgraph RET["Retire Stage"]
        ROB[("ROB Reorder Buffer")]
        PRF[("Physical Register File PRF")]
        ARF[("Architectural Register File ARF")]
    end
    DE -- src1, src2, dst arch regs --> RAT
    RAT -- src1 tag, src2 tag --> IQ1
    RAT -- src1 tag, src2 tag --> IQ2
    FL -- new dst tag --> IQ1
    FL -- new dst tag --> IQ2
    RAT -- old dst tag, checkpoint --> CKPT
    IQ1 --> ALU1
    IQ2 --> ALU2
    ALU1 -- result with tag --> PRF
    ALU2 -- result with tag --> PRF
    ALU1 -- broadcast tag on CDB --> IQ1
    ALU2 -- broadcast tag on CDB --> IQ2
    ALU1 -- completion event --> ROB
    ALU2 -- completion event --> ROB
    ROB -- in order commit --> ARF
    ROB -- free stale tag --> FL
```

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Rename for the Worked Example

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dec as Decoder
    participant RAT
    participant FL as Free List
    participant IQ as Issue Queue
    participant ROB

    Note over Dec,IQ: Instruction I1: R1 <- R2 + R3
    Dec->>RAT: Lookup R2 -> P2, R3 -> P3
    Dec->>FL: Pop new dst tag
    FL-->>Dec: P10
    Dec->>RAT: RAT[R1] <- P10
    Dec->>IQ: Dispatch uop with sources P2, P3, dest P10
    Dec->>ROB: Allocate ROB entry for I1

    Note over Dec,IQ: Instruction I2: R4 <- R1 * R5
    Dec->>RAT: Lookup R1 -> P10 (not P1!), R5 -> P5
    Dec->>FL: Pop new dst tag
    FL-->>Dec: P11
    Dec->>RAT: RAT[R4] <- P11
    Dec->>IQ: Dispatch uop with sources P10, P5, dest P11

    Note over Dec,IQ: Instruction I3: R1 <- R6 - R7 (the false-dependency killer)
    Dec->>RAT: Lookup R6 -> P6, R7 -> P7
    Dec->>FL: Pop new dst tag
    FL-->>Dec: P12
    Dec->>RAT: RAT[R1] <- P12 (overwrites P10 mapping, but I2 already captured P10)
    Dec->>IQ: Dispatch uop with sources P6, P7, dest P12

    Note over Dec,IQ: I3 can now execute in parallel with I1 and I2
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Branch Misprediction Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Branch in ROB completes Execute] --> B{Branch predicted correctly?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Continue in order commit]
    B -- No --> D[Identify speculative PRs since branch]
    D --> E[Push each speculative PR onto Free List]
    E --> F[Restore RAT from Checkpoint saved at branch dispatch]
    F --> G[Flush Issue Queue entries beyond branch]
    G --> H[Flush ROB entries beyond branch]
    H --> I[Refetch from correct path PC]
    I --> A
```

---

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define Register Renaming. Why is it essential in a dynamically scheduled superscalar processor?**

**Model Answer:**

Register renaming is a hardware technique that maps the limited set of ISA-defined architectural registers onto a larger pool of internal physical registers at dispatch time. **(1 mark)**

It is essential because:
* It **eliminates false dependencies** — Write-After-Read (WAR) and Write-After-Write (WAW) hazards caused by the programmer reusing a small register file. **(1 mark)**
* It **preserves true Read-After-Write (RAW) dependencies**, which represent genuine data flow. **(0.5 marks)**
* It enables the out-of-order engine to **schedule instructions in parallel** that would otherwise be artificially serialized by name conflicts, thus increasing IPC. **(0.5 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write that register renaming "removes data hazards" generically. The examiner expects you to specify **WAR and WAW only**, and to clarify that RAW dependencies are *preserved* — otherwise you lose 1 mark.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between Architectural Registers and Physical Registers.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Architectural Registers | Physical Registers |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Count** | Fixed (e.g., 32) | Large pool (e.g., 128) |
| **Visibility** | Defined by ISA, programmer-visible | Hardware-internal, hidden |
| **Purpose** | Hold committed program state | Hold in-flight values + temporaries |
| **Mapping** | Looked up via RAT | Tagged via PR_Tag |

*(1 mark per correct row × 3 rows = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU End-Sem Model Paper – Module 2]`

**Q.A (a)** Explain the concept of Register Renaming with the help of a block diagram of the Register Alias Table (RAT). Also describe the role of the Free List. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Concept (3 marks):**
Register renaming dynamically maps the small, fixed set of architectural registers (visible to the programmer) to a larger pool of physical registers maintained by the hardware. The goal is to eliminate **false dependencies** (WAR and WAW) and enable higher instruction-level parallelism in out-of-order execution.

**2. RAT — Block Diagram and Working (3 marks):**
The Register Alias Table is a Content-Addressable Memory (CAM) structure indexed by architectural register name. Each entry contains:
* **Valid Bit**
* **Physical Register Tag (PR_Tag)** — points to the latest physical register holding the value for that architectural register.

At **dispatch**, for every instruction:
* The destination architectural register is looked up in the RAT, and the **old PR_Tag** is saved in the ROB.
* A **new PR_Tag** is popped from the Free List and written into the RAT entry.
* The source architectural registers are looked up in the RAT to obtain the current physical tags — these tags are what the uop carries with it.

**3. Free List (1 mark):**
The Free List is a FIFO/LIFO queue holding tags of *unused* physical registers. On every rename, a tag is *popped* for the new destination. On every *commit* of an instruction, the *previously-saved* PR_Tag from the ROB is pushed back, recycling the register.

---

**Q.A (b)** Consider the following instruction sequence:
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &: \quad R1 \leftarrow R2 + R3 \\
I_2 &: \quad R2 \leftarrow R1 \times R4 \\
I_3 &: \quad R5 \leftarrow R1 + R2 \\
I_4 &: \quad R1 \leftarrow R6 - R7
\end{aligned}
$$
**(i)** Identify all data dependencies. **(ii)** Show the renamed version using a RAT, assuming Free List initially contains `{P10, P11, P12, …}`. **(iii)** Which dependencies have been eliminated? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Identify Dependencies (2 marks):**

| Pair | Type | Register | True / False |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $I_1 \to I_2$ | RAW | R1 | True |
| $I_1 \to I_3$ | RAW | R1 | True |
| $I_2 \to I_3$ | RAW | R2 | True |
| $I_3 \to I_4$ | WAR | R1 | False |
| $I_1 \to I_4$ | WAW | R1 | False |

**Step (ii) — Renamed Code (3 marks):**

Initial RAT mappings (assumed): $R1 \to P1,\ R2 \to P2,\ R3 \to P3,\ R4 \to P4,\ R5 \to P5,\ R6 \to P6,\ R7 \to P7$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &: \quad P10 \leftarrow P2 + P3 \\
I_2 &: \quad P11 \leftarrow P10 \times P4 \\
I_3 &: \quad P12 \leftarrow P10 + P11 \\
I_4 &: \quad P13 \leftarrow P6 - P7
\end{aligned}
$$

Final RAT after dispatch: $R1 \to P13,\ R2 \to P11,\ R5 \to P12$.

**Step (iii) — Eliminated Dependencies (2 marks):**
* The **WAR** between $I_3$ and $I_4$ on `R1` is **eliminated** because $I_4$ now writes to `P13`, while $I_3$ continues to read the value at `P10`.
* The **WAW** between $I_1$ and $I_4$ on `R1` is **eliminated** because $I_4$'s write goes to a fresh `P13`, while $I_1$'s write at `P10` is preserved for $I_3$ to consume.

All RAW (true) dependencies are **preserved**, ensuring correct program semantics.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU End-Sem Model Paper – Module 2 Alternative]`

**Q.B (a)** With a neat diagram, explain how Tomasulo's Algorithm performs register renaming through reservation stations. Compare it with the modern Register Alias Table (RAT) approach. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Tomasulo's Algorithm (1967) — Implicit Renaming (3.5 marks):**

In Tomasulo's scheme, the *Functional Units* are fronted by **Reservation Stations (RS)**. Each RS entry has fields: `Op`, `Vj`, `Vk` (operand values), `Qj`, `Qk` (tags of producing RS), and `Busy`. When an instruction issues:
1. If the source operand is in a register (or already produced), its value is copied into `Vj`/`Vk`.
2. If the operand is *pending* (the producing RS is busy), the **tag of the producing RS** is copied into `Qj`/`Qk` — this is the *implicit renaming tag*.
3. When the producing RS completes, it broadcasts the result on the **Common Data Bus (CDB)** along with its tag; all waiting RSs snoop and capture the value if their `Qj`/`Qk` matches.

The "rename tag" is therefore the **Reservation Station ID**, not a separate physical register.

**Modern RAT Approach (2.5 marks):**

Modern processors (e.g., AMD K7, Intel Sandy Bridge+) use a centralized **Register Alias Table**:
* Each architectural register is mapped to a tag in a **large Physical Register File (PRF)**.
* Renaming is decoupled from functional units — only the *dispatch unit* touches the RAT.
* Functional units read/write the PRF directly by tag; no value-copying is needed into a separate RS data field (Wakeup is tag-based, Broadcast is tag-based).
* Scalability: PRF can be enlarged without re-engineering every functional unit.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Tomasulo (RS-based) | RAT (PR-based) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rename tag | RS identifier | Physical register tag |
| Operand storage | RS entry fields `Vj/Vk` | Physical register file |
| Broadcast | Value on CDB | Tag only (CDB carries tag, not value) |
| Scalability | Limited by RS ports | Scales with PRF size |

---

**Q.B (b)** A superscalar processor with issue width 4 has an ROB depth of 128 entries and 256 physical registers. The program has 30 architectural registers. Determine the maximum number of in-flight instructions and the minimum free-list threshold before dispatch must stall. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
* Issue width $N = 4$
* ROB depth $D = 128$
* Physical registers $P_{total} = 256$
* Architectural registers $R_{arch} = 30$

**Step 1 — Maximum In-Flight Instructions (3 marks):**
The ROB holds all in-flight (not-yet-retired) instructions. Therefore:
$$
\text{In-flight}_{max} = D = 128 \text{ instructions}
$$

**Step 2 — Physical Registers Needed (2 marks):**
To sustain the maximum window of $D = 128$ in-flight instructions, each potentially writing one destination:
$$
P_{required} = R_{arch} + D = 30 + 128 = 158
$$
Since $P_{total} = 256 \ge 158$, the PRF is **sufficiently sized** to hold the maximum window.

**Step 3 — Free List Stall Threshold (2 marks):**
The minimum free-list size needed to keep dispatch unstalled through the worst case:
$$
\text{FL}_{min} = P_{total} - P_{required} = 256 - 158 = 98
$$
If the Free List drops below this threshold, **dispatch must stall** until some instructions commit and recycle PRs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly compute $P_{required}$ as just $D$ (forgetting the architectural registers must also be mapped). The correct formula is $P_{required} = R_{arch} + D$. Also, you must explicitly state that the Free List threshold is what causes dispatch to stall — *not* the ROB full condition (that's a different hazard).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Points for KTU Exam Night:**

* ✅ Register Renaming **eliminates WAR and WAW** hazards; **preserves RAW** (true) dependencies.
* ✅ Architectural registers are **ISA-visible, fixed, small**; Physical registers are **hardware-internal, large, dynamic**.
* ✅ Renaming happens in the **Dispatch / Rename** stage, between Decode and Issue.
* ✅ **RAT (Register Alias Table)** maps architectural → physical; updated on dispatch, restored on branch mispredict via **checkpoint**.
* ✅ **Free List** supplies new physical tags on dispatch; **ROB** releases them on commit.
* ✅ Tomasulo's Algorithm (1967) does *implicit* renaming using **Reservation Station IDs** as tags.
* ✅ Modern CPUs (AMD K7+, Intel P6+) do *explicit* renaming using a **Physical Register File** plus RAT.
* ✅ **Branch mispredict recovery**: squash speculative PRs back to Free List, restore RAT checkpoint, flush Issue Queue & ROB beyond branch.
* ✅ Renaming enables **speculative execution** and is the basis of all modern OoOE superscalar performance (and, sadly, also the basis of **Spectre/Meltdown** transient execution attacks).
* ✅ Typical numbers: **32 architectural regs, 128–256 physical regs, 128–256 ROB entries, 4–8 wide issue** in 2024-era CPUs.
* ✅ **KTU buzzwords:** RAT, Free List, Architectural vs. Physical, Tomasulo, Reservation Station, Speculative Execution, Checkpoint, Dispatch, Retire.
* ✅ **Distinguish:** Free-List-empty stall (no PRs available) vs. ROB-full stall (no commit slots) — both stall dispatch but for different reasons.
* ✅ Always show the **Free List contents** before/after in numerical problems — this is how the examiner awards partial marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Speculation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hardware Speculation

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware Speculation** is an advanced microarchitectural technique in which the processor **executes instructions *before* it is certain that they lie on the correct execution path**, based on a predicted outcome (typically a branch prediction). The hardware then verifies the speculation and, if correct, commits the results permanently; if wrong, it **undoes (rolls back) all side effects** of the speculatively executed instructions.

In the context of **Tomasulo's Algorithm with Speculation**, three principal extensions are made to the baseline Tomasulo framework:

1. **Branch prediction** is performed in hardware to predict the direction and target of conditional/unconditional branches.
2. **Speculative execution** proceeds down the predicted path, issuing, executing, and forwarding results of instructions whose predecessors are speculatively safe.
3. **A Reorder Buffer (ROB)** is added so that the *results of speculatively executed instructions are NOT irrevocably committed* to the architectural state (registers or memory) until the instruction is known to be non-speculative (i.e., all prior branches have been resolved as correctly predicted).

The **commit step** (write-back to real register file or memory) occurs **in program order**, while **execution occurs out of order**. This is the foundational difference between non-speculative Tomasulo and speculative Tomasulo.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Speculative Instruction** | An instruction whose execution depends on a branch whose outcome is not yet resolved. |
| **Commit / Retirement** | The step at which a speculatively executed result is written from the ROB into the architectural register file or memory. |
| **Squash / Flush** | Discarding the speculatively executed instructions and clearing the ROB entries when a misprediction is detected. |
| **ROB Head** | The oldest entry in the Reorder Buffer; it is the next instruction permitted to commit. |
| **Non-speculative** | An instruction is non-speculative when all branches *before* it in program order have been resolved and correctly predicted. |
| **Precise Exception** | An exception model in which the architectural state reflects exactly the state at the point of the exception (no in-flight instructions appear committed). |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Road Trip Planner"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Driving Through a Y-Shaped Fork on a Foggy Road**
>
> Imagine you are driving in dense fog and reach a fork in the road. You cannot see far ahead, but a co-passenger (the **branch predictor**) says, *"I think the road to the left is correct; 90% of past drivers took it."*
>
> You commit to the left road and continue driving at full speed (**speculative execution**). Your fuel gauge, GPS, and logbook (the **Reorder Buffer**) record every action you take, but they are *marked tentative*.
>
> Eventually the fog clears. Two outcomes are possible:
> - **Co-passenger was right** ✅ → You finalize your logbook entries (the results are **committed** to your real travel record). No work is wasted.
> - **Co-passenger was wrong** ❌ → The highway patrol backtracks you to the fork (**squash/rollback**), all tentative logbook entries are erased, and you take the right road. The wasted work is thrown away, but you never polluted your real record with wrong-side actions.
>
> This is exactly what **Hardware Speculation** does: it allows the CPU to keep its pipelines full by betting on branches, while the **ROB acts as the trusted ledger** that prevents wrong guesses from corrupting the program-visible state.

## 1.3 Physical / Architectural Metrics

The following performance metrics are critical to evaluating speculation effectiveness:

- **Branch Prediction Accuracy** — fraction of branches correctly predicted (typical: **> 95%**).
- **Misprediction Penalty** — cycles lost per misprediction (typically **10–20 cycles** for a simple pipeline, **> 100 cycles** for deep pipelines like Intel Skylake).
- **ROB Size** — number of in-flight instructions tracked (e.g., **128, 192, or 256 entries** in modern Intel/AMD cores).
- **Speculative Window** — the maximum number of instructions that can be in flight (in execution or pending commit) at any time.
- **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** — the average number of instructions executed per cycle; speculation **increases effective ILP** across control dependencies.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Motivation: Why Speculation is Needed

In a non-speculative dynamic pipeline (plain Tomasulo), when the pipeline encounters a conditional branch, the issue logic must **stall** until the branch is resolved. This introduces a **control dependency stall**:

$$T_{\text{stall}} = N_{\text{unresolved branches}} \times \text{Branch Resolution Latency (cycles)}$$

This cap on ILP is unacceptable for deep pipelines. **Hardware speculation breaks the control dependency** by allowing the processor to *guess* the branch outcome and proceed.

## 2.2 Operational Concept — The 4-Stage Speculative Lifecycle

> [!NOTE]
> Every instruction in a speculative Tomasulo pipeline passes through **four logical phases**:
> 1. **Issue** — Decode, check structural & RAW hazards, allocate an ROB entry & reservation station.
> 2. **Execute** — Operands become available; the functional unit performs the operation.
> 3. **Write Result** — Result is broadcast on the CDB and *tagged into the ROB entry* (NOT yet written to register file).
> 4. **Commit (Retire)** — When the instruction reaches the head of the ROB AND is non-speculative, the result is moved from the ROB to the architectural register file or memory.

## 2.3 The Reorder Buffer (ROB)

### 2.3.1 ROB Entry Format

Each ROB entry holds the following fields:

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Instruction Type** | Branch, Store, or Register (ALU) operation. |
| **Destination** | Register number (if Register Op) or Memory address (if Store). |
| **Value** | The computed result (for ALU) or the value-to-store (for Store). |
| **Ready** | Bit indicating whether the value field is valid. |
| **Speculative / Non-Speculative** | Implicitly tracked by ROB position: all entries *after* an unresolved branch are speculative. |

### 2.3.2 ROB Operations

- **ROB Allocate:** At issue, a new entry is appended to the **tail** of the ROB.
- **ROB Write-Back:** When an instruction produces a result, the result is written into the matching ROB entry's *Value* field and *Ready* bit is set.
- **ROB Commit (at head):** If the head entry is *Ready* AND *non-speculative* (no unresolved branch ahead of it), its value is written to the architectural destination register or memory. Then the entry is deallocated.
- **ROB Squash:** On a branch misprediction, the **ROB is flushed**; the PC is restored to the correct target; and the register file's mapping is reverted to the precise post-branch state.

## 2.4 Modifications to Tomasulo for Speculation

The following modifications are made to the original Tomasulo algorithm to support speculation:

| Component | Original Tomasulo | Speculative Tomasulo |
|---|---|---|
| **Tagging** | Reservation Station tag in registers | Reservation Station tag OR ROB tag |
| **Write-back** | Result broadcast on CDB → Register File + RS | Result broadcast on CDB → ROB entry + RS (NOT Register File directly) |
| **Commit** | Implicit on write-back | Explicit commit step from ROB head → Register File / Memory |
| **Stores** | Memory updated on completion | Memory updated only when Store commits (ROB head) |
| **Exception handling** | Imprecise | **Precise** (program order commits) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In speculative Tomasulo, the **CDB broadcasts to BOTH the reservation stations AND the ROB entry** (not the register file). The actual register file is updated only at commit time, in program order.

## 2.5 Branch Misprediction Recovery

The recovery sequence upon branch misprediction is:

1. **Branch is resolved** at Execute or Write-Result stage.
2. The predicted PC is compared with the actual PC.
3. If they differ → **MISPREDICTION DETECTED**:
   - All ROB entries *after* the branch (including the branch itself) are **invalidated (squashed)**.
   - The PC is set to the **correct target address**.
   - All reservation stations that received results from squashed instructions are **flushed** (cleared).
   - The rename map / architectural register file is **not** affected (since results never reached it).
4. Fetch resumes from the corrected PC.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Modern Superscalar CPUs** (Intel Core, AMD Zen, Apple M-series) all use hardware speculation extensively.
- **Spectre & Meltdown vulnerabilities (2018)** were *side effects* of speculative execution that left traces in CPU caches — a cautionary tale on the security implications of speculation.
- **Out-of-Order execution engines** (e.g., Apple's Firestorm, Intel Golden Cove) achieve **IPC > 1** primarily because speculation fills branch-resolution bubbles with useful work.

## 2.7 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Misprediction Penalty | $P = D_{f} + R_{b}$ | $D_f$ = fetch-to-execute delay; $R_b$ = recovery time |
| Branch Misprediction Rate | $\text{MR} = 1 - \text{Accuracy}$ | Fraction (e.g., 0.05 = 5% mispredicts) |
| Effective CPI (with speculation) | $\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + P \times \text{MR} \times I_{\text{branch}}$ | $I_{branch}$ = branches per instruction |
| ROB Full Stall Condition | Stall if $\text{ROB occupancy} = \text{ROB size}$ | Limits speculative window |
| Speculative Window | $W = \text{ROB size} \approx \text{fetch\_rate} \times \text{depth}$ | Instructions in flight |
| Precise Exception Guarantee | $\text{commit order} = \text{program order}$ | Architectural state always consistent |
| Branch Target Buffer (BTB) | Lookup: PC $\to$ predicted target | Hit/miss affects dispatch |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective CPI with Branch Misprediction

Let us derive the standard textbook expression for the **effective CPI in a speculative processor**.

### Step 1 — Define the base CPI

In a perfect (no-misprediction) processor, the cycles per instruction is:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{base}} = 1.0 + \text{stall cycles per instruction (non-branch hazards)}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the misprediction overhead

Every branch instruction has a probability $\text{MR}$ of being mispredicted. When a misprediction occurs, the pipeline loses $P$ cycles (the misprediction penalty). The fraction of dynamic instructions that are branches is $I_{\text{branch}}$ (typically **0.15–0.20**).

Hence, the **average additional cycles per instruction** due to branch mispredictions is:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{mispred}} = P \times \text{MR} \times I_{\text{branch}}
$$

### Step 3 — Combine to get the effective CPI

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} &= \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + P \cdot \text{MR} \cdot I_{\text{branch}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Substituting typical KTU-style values

Assume:
- $\text{CPI}_{\text{base}} = 0.5$ (superscalar, IPC ≈ 2)
- $P = 15$ cycles
- $\text{MR} = 0.05$ (95% branch predictor accuracy)
- $I_{\text{branch}} = 0.20$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} &= 0.5 + 15 \times 0.05 \times 0.20 \\
&= 0.5 + 0.15 \\
&= 0.65
\end{aligned}
$$

> The misprediction overhead (0.15) represents **~23% of the total CPI** — a substantial performance tax paid by the processor for using speculation.

### Step 5 — Sensitivity analysis

If we *double* the misprediction penalty (e.g., due to a deeper pipeline, $P = 30$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI}_{\text{effective}} &= 0.5 + 30 \times 0.05 \times 0.20 \\
&= 0.5 + 0.30 \\
&= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

This **60% increase in CPI** for a 2× penalty is precisely why deep pipelines demand *very accurate* branch predictors (e.g., TAGE, perceptron predictors achieving **> 97%** accuracy).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Speculative Tomasulo Execution Trace

Consider the following instruction sequence (KTU-style textbook problem):

```text
I1: DIV  R2, R6, R8
I2: FADD R4, R2, R10
I3: BEQ R4, R0, Label    ; (assume Label is I5)
I4: FMUL R6, R8, R10
I5: ...
```

Assume the branch predictor predicts **NOT-TAKEN** for I3. Trace the execution of this sequence under **Speculative Tomasulo with ROB**.

### Step-by-step Trace Table

| Cycle | Fetched | Issued | Executed | WB→ROB | Committed (ROB Head) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | I1 | I1 | — | — | — | I1 → ROB#1, RS(DIV) |
| 2 | I2 | I2 | — | — | — | I2 → ROB#2, waits on R2 (tagged ROB#1) |
| 3 | I3 | I3 | I1 starts | — | — | I3 is a branch; → ROB#3, predict NOT-TAKEN |
| 4 | I4 (speculative) | I4 | I1 running | — | — | I4 speculatively issued → ROB#4, waits on R8, R10 |
| 5 | ... | ... | I1 finishes (40 cyc later) | I1 result → ROB#1 | I1 commits to R2 | I1 is non-speculative (no branch ahead) |
| ... | ... | ... | I2 executes (uses R2 from ROB#1) | I2 → ROB#2 | I2 commits to R4 | I2 commits |
| 15 | ... | ... | Branch I3 resolves: **ACTUAL = TAKEN (mispredict!)** | I3 → ROB#3 | I3 commits (failed prediction) | **SQUASH I4**: ROB#4 invalidated; I4 result discarded; PC ← Label (I5) |
| 16 | I5 | ... | — | — | — | Pipeline resumes from correct path; no architectural damage |

### Analysis Matrix

| Aspect | Behavior in this Trace |
|---|---|
| **I1 (DIV)** | Issued first; long latency, but commits correctly because no branch preceded it. |
| **I2 (FADD)** | Speculatively executed with respect to nothing; commits after I1. |
| **I3 (BEQ)** | Speculative branch; prediction wrong, but **architectural state unchanged** because the squash restored PC. |
| **I4 (FMUL)** | **Wasted work** — executed speculatively but result is squashed. |
| **Memory consistency** | All Stores (none here) would have been buffered in ROB until commit, preventing irreversible memory writes from a wrong-path. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from the Trace:** Speculation trades *wasted execution* (squashed instructions) for *higher throughput* (most predictions are correct). The ROB ensures that this wasted work **never leaks into the architectural state**.

## 3.3 Derivation: ROB Occupancy vs. Speculative Window

The **Speculative Window** $W$ — the maximum number of in-flight instructions — is bounded by the ROB size $N_{\text{ROB}}$ and the pipeline's ability to fetch and issue:

$$
W = \min\!\big(N_{\text{ROB}},\; F_{\text{rate}} \times D_{\text{pipe}}\big)
$$

Where:
- $F_{\text{rate}}$ = fetch bandwidth (instructions/cycle)
- $D_{\text{pipe}}$ = branch resolution latency (cycles)

For a typical 4-wide superscalar with a 15-cycle branch resolution and 192-entry ROB:

$$
W = \min(192,\; 4 \times 15) = \min(192,\; 60) = 60 \text{ instructions}
$$

The processor can have at most **60 in-flight instructions** in this case — the ROB is large enough, so the bound is set by fetch × branch-resolution depth.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Speculative Tomasulo Block Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FETCH_STAGE["FETCH STAGE"]
        PCReg[Program Counter]
        IMem[Instruction Memory]
        BTB[Branch Target Buffer]
        BPred[Branch Predictor]
    end

    subgraph ISSUE_STAGE["ISSUE STAGE"]
        Decoder[Instruction Decoder]
        RS_POOL[Reservation Station Pool]
        RAT[Register Alias Table]
    end

    subgraph EXEC_STAGE["EXECUTE STAGE"]
        FU_ALU[Integer ALU]
        FU_FPU[FP Unit]
        FU_MUL[Multiplier]
        FU_DIV[Divider]
    end

    subgraph COMMIT_STAGE["COMMIT STAGE"]
        ROB[Reorder Buffer ROB]
        ARF[Architectural Register File]
        MEM[Data Memory]
    end

    subgraph CDB_LINK["COMMON DATA BUS CDB"]
        CDB[CDB Broadcast Bus]
    end

    PCReg --> IMem
    BTB --> PCReg
    BPred --> PCReg
    IMem --> Decoder
    Decoder --> RS_POOL
    Decoder --> RAT
    Decoder --> ROB
    RAT --> RS_POOL

    RS_POOL --> FU_ALU
    RS_POOL --> FU_FPU
    RS_POOL --> FU_MUL
    RS_POOL --> FU_DIV

    FU_ALU --> CDB
    FU_FPU --> CDB
    FU_MUL --> CDB
    FU_DIV --> CDB

    CDB --> RS_POOL
    CDB --> ROB

    ROB --> ARF
    ROB --> MEM

    RAT -.speculative rename.-> ARF
    BPred -.mispredict signal.-> ROB
    ROB -.squash all.-> RS_POOL
    ROB -.squash.-> RAT
    ROB -.restore.-> PCReg
```

## 4.2 The Lifecycle State Machine of a Speculative Instruction

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Issued : Instruction decoded, ROB entry allocated, RS reserved
    Issued --> Executing : Operands ready, dispatched to FU
    Executing --> WriteBack : FU produces result
    WriteBack --> SpeculativeReady : Result written into ROB entry, Ready bit set
    SpeculativeReady --> Committed : Instruction reaches ROB head AND all prior branches resolved
    SpeculativeReady --> Squashed : Mispredicted branch detected ahead
    Executing --> Squashed : Mispredicted branch detected ahead
    Issued --> Squashed : Mispredicted branch detected ahead
    Committed --> [*] : ROB entry deallocated, architectural state updated
    Squashed --> [*] : ROB entry deallocated, no state change
```

## 4.3 Commit Logic Flow (ROB Head Processing)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Cycle Begins] --> B{ROB Head Ready?}
    B -- No --> C[No commit this cycle]
    B -- Yes --> D{Branch resolved AND correctly predicted?}
    D -- No, still pending --> C
    D -- No, mispredicted --> E[SQUASH: Flush ROB entries after branch]
    D -- Yes, non-speculative --> F{Instruction type?}
    F -- Store --> G[Commit Value to Memory at ROB.Address]
    F -- Register op --> H[Commit Value to ARF at ROB.Destination]
    F -- Branch --> I[Just retire branch, no write]
    G --> J[Deallocate ROB Head Entry]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Advance ROB Head Pointer]
    K --> A
    E --> L[Restore PC to correct target]
    L --> M[Clear all reservation stations fed by squashed instructions]
    M --> A
    C --> A
```

## 4.4 Speculation vs. Non-Speculation — Data Path Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NO_SPEC["NON SPECULATIVE TOMASULO"]
        N1[FU Result] --CDB--> N2[Reservation Stations]
        N1 --CDB--> N3[Register File]
        N3 -.direct write.-> N4[Architectural State]
    end

    subgraph WITH_SPEC["SPECULATIVE TOMASULO WITH ROB"]
        S1[FU Result] --CDB--> S2[Reservation Stations]
        S1 --CDB--> S3[ROB Entry]
        S3 --in order commit--> S4[Register File]
        S3 --in order commit--> S5[Data Memory]
        S2 -.waits for.-> S3
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Difference:** In the non-speculative path, the CDB writes *directly* into the Register File. In the speculative path, the CDB only writes into the **ROB and RS**; the architectural register file is touched **only at commit time** in program order. This single difference is what enables **precise exceptions** and **safe speculation**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define hardware speculation. Why is it essential in modern pipelined processors?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **(1 mark)** Hardware speculation is a microarchitectural technique where the processor executes instructions *before* knowing whether they should be executed, based on a predicted branch outcome. Results are buffered in a Reorder Buffer (ROB) and committed to the architectural state only when the speculation is confirmed correct; on a misprediction, all speculative results are squashed.
> - **(1 mark)** It is essential because conditional branches are frequent (every 4–7 instructions) and resolving them stalls the pipeline. Speculation allows the processor to keep functional units busy across branch resolution, dramatically increasing instruction-level parallelism (ILP) and effective throughput.
> - **(1 mark)** Modern deeply-pipelined superscalar processors (e.g., 14+ stage pipelines) would otherwise be crippled by control-dependency stalls; speculation is the *only* viable way to achieve IPC > 1 on real workloads with frequent branches.

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the Reorder Buffer (ROB) in hardware speculation?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **(1 mark)** The ROB holds the results of in-flight instructions *out of order* but commits them *in program order* to the architectural register file and memory.
> - **(1 mark)** It acts as the speculative buffer: results are written into the ROB as soon as the functional unit produces them, but the architectural state is not updated until the instruction is known to be non-speculative.
> - **(1 mark)** On a branch misprediction, the ROB enables clean **rollback** — the entries after the mispredicted branch are simply invalidated, leaving the architectural state untouched, thus preserving **precise exception semantics**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **modifications made to Tomasulo's algorithm to support hardware speculation**. Clearly describe the role of the Reorder Buffer (ROB), the reservation stations, and the Common Data Bus (CDB) in this extended architecture.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider the following code segment executing on a **speculative Tomasulo processor with a 4-entry ROB**:
```text
I1: FADD  R3, R1, R2
I2: FMUL  R4, R3, R5
I3: BEQ   R4, R0, TARGET     ; predict: NOT-TAKEN; actual: TAKEN
I4: FDIV  R6, R7, R8
TARGET: I5: FADD  R9, R6, R10
```
Assume all FP operations complete in 1 cycle and the branch resolves at the execute stage. **Show the step-by-step state of the ROB at every cycle until the misprediction is detected and the pipeline is recovered.** What is the final committed state of registers R3, R4, R6, R9?

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Modifications to Tomasulo for Hardware Speculation (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** The original Tomasulo algorithm's Register Result Status (Renaming) table is split: rename happens through the ROB tags. The Register Alias Table (RAT) is updated *speculatively*.
- **(2 marks)** The CDB now broadcasts results to **two destinations**: the reservation stations (for forwarding) and the **ROB entry** (for buffering the result). The architectural register file is **NOT** written directly.
- **(1 mark)** A new **commit (retire) stage** is added: when the ROB head is *Ready* AND *non-speculative*, the result is moved from the ROB into the register file (or memory, for stores).
- **(1 mark)** A **branch prediction unit** issues instructions *speculatively* past unresolved branches; on a misprediction, all ROB entries after the branch are **invalidated (squashed)** and the PC is restored.
- **(1 mark)** Stores are **buffered in the ROB** and only update memory at commit time, preventing wrong-path stores from corrupting memory.

**(b) Step-by-step ROB Trace (7 marks):**

| Cycle | Issued | ROB#1 (I1) | ROB#2 (I2) | ROB#3 (I3) | ROB#4 (I4) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | I1 | Issued (R3) | — | — | — | ROB#1 allocated |
| 2 | I2 | Executed/WB→ROB#1 Ready | Issued (R4) waits on R3 | — | — | I2 gets ROB#1 tag for R3 |
| 3 | I3 | Commit→R3 | Executed/WB→ROB#2 Ready | Issued (Branch) pred: NOT-TAKEN | — | I3 enters ROB |
| 4 | I4 (speculative) | — | Commit→R4 | Executed | Issued (R6) | I4 depends on no pending operands |
| 5 | (stall — branch not yet resolved) | — | — | WB→ROB#3, **misprediction detected** | Executed | I3 resolves TAKEN → MISPREDICT |
| 6 | ROB SQUASH: I4 (ROB#4) invalidated; PC ← TARGET | — | — | Commit (failed) | **Squashed** | I4 result discarded |
| 7 | I5 fetched & issued | — | — | — | — | Resume correct path |

**Final Committed State:**
- R3 = R1 + R2 (from I1)
- R4 = R3 × R5 (from I2)
- R6 = **uncommitted** (I4 was squashed — still has its old architectural value)
- R9 = R6 + R10 (from I5, computed using *old* R6, since I4 was squashed)

> **(1 mark)** Total: 7 marks for full trace, register updates, and identification of squashed I4.

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **four phases of an instruction's life in a speculative Tomasulo pipeline**: Issue, Execute, Write Result, and Commit. For each phase, explain what hazard checks occur, what structures are accessed, and what conditions must hold for the phase to proceed.

**(b) [7 marks]** A processor has the following parameters:
- Base CPI (no branch effects) = **0.6**
- Branch misprediction penalty = **20 cycles**
- Branch misprediction rate = **4%**
- Fraction of instructions that are branches = **18%**
- ROB size = **128 entries**
- Fetch width = **4 instructions/cycle**

Compute:
1. The **effective CPI** of the processor.
2. The **maximum speculative window** (in-flight instructions) given the parameters.
3. If the branch predictor is upgraded to a 96% accuracy design (misprediction rate drops to 4% but with 22-cycle penalty due to longer resolution), recompute the **new effective CPI** and the **percentage improvement** over the original.

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Phases of an Instruction (7 marks):**

- **(1.5 marks) Issue Phase:**
  - Decode the instruction.
  - Check **structural hazard** (free RS, free ROB slot).
  - Check **RAW (read-after-write) hazard**: rename source operands via RAT (using ROB tags for not-yet-committed values).
  - Allocate an ROB entry at the tail and a reservation station.
  - Stall if ROB is full or no RS is available.

- **(1.5 marks) Execute Phase:**
  - Monitor when both source operands become available (RS tag matches or operand is the value in the corresponding ROB entry).
  - When ready, the RS dispatches the instruction to the appropriate **Functional Unit**.
  - RAW hazards are *transparently* resolved by tag matching.

- **(1.5 marks) Write Result Phase:**
  - The FU completes and broadcasts the result on the **CDB**.
  - The result is captured by all RSs waiting for this tag, and written into the **corresponding ROB entry** (Value + Ready bit).
  - The architectural register file is **not** updated yet.

- **(1.5 marks) Commit Phase:**
  - The instruction at the **ROB head** is examined each cycle.
  - If **Ready** and **non-speculative** (no unresolved branch ahead of it), the value is committed to the ARF (for register ops) or memory (for stores).
  - Branch instructions simply retire without writing any register.
  - The ROB entry is deallocated and the head pointer advances.
  - On misprediction of a branch, all entries *after* the branch are **squashed**; the PC is restored to the correct target.

**(b) Numerical Computations (7 marks):**

**(1) Original Effective CPI (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} &= \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + P \cdot \text{MR} \cdot I_{\text{branch}} \\
&= 0.6 + 20 \times 0.04 \times 0.18 \\
&= 0.6 + 0.144 \\
&= 0.744
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2) Maximum Speculative Window (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \min(N_{\text{ROB}},\; F_{\text{rate}} \times D_{\text{pipe}}) \\
&= \min(128,\; 4 \times 20) \\
&= \min(128,\; 80) \\
&= 80 \text{ instructions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3) Upgraded Predictor's CPI and Improvement (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI}_{\text{new}} &= 0.6 + 22 \times 0.04 \times 0.18 \\
&= 0.6 + 0.1584 \\
&= 0.7584
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Improvement \%} &= \frac{\text{CPI}_{\text{old}} - \text{CPI}_{\text{new}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{old}}} \times 100\% \\
