&= \frac{0.744 - 0.7584}{0.744} \times 100\% \\
&= -1.94\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **(Counter-intuitive result!)** The new predictor *worsens* CPI despite higher accuracy because the longer resolution penalty (22 vs 20 cycles) outweighs the 1% accuracy gain. This teaches students that **both accuracy and resolution latency matter** for branch predictor design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Hardware Speculation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing commit with write-back.** Many students incorrectly say "the result is written to the register file on write-back." This is **wrong** in speculative Tomasulo. The architectural register file is updated **only at commit**, in program order. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
>
> 2. **Forgetting that stores are also speculative.** A wrong-path store must NOT be visible to other cores/caches. Stating "stores update memory on completion" loses marks. Correct answer: stores are buffered in the ROB and updated at commit.
>
> 3. **Not explaining ROB squash behavior.** A common error is describing misprediction recovery vaguely as "rolls back." You must specify: *which* entries are invalidated (all entries after the mispredicted branch), and *what* state is restored (PC to the correct target, register file untouched since it was never written speculatively).
>
> 4. **Forgetting precise exceptions.** Hardware speculation provides *precise* exceptions as a side benefit. Always mention this when describing the ROB's role.
>
> 5. **In numerical problems, mixing up MR (misprediction rate) with accuracy.** Accuracy = 1 − MR. Using 4% as the accuracy (instead of MR) will give a wildly different (and wrong) CPI.
>
> 6. **Diagram errors:** Not labeling the CDB as going to BOTH the ROB and the RS (not directly to the ARF in the speculative case) is a frequent diagram-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hardware Speculation** = execute-before-confirm, with safe rollback via the **ROB**.
- **Tomasulo + Speculation** = Tomasulo's algorithm + (1) branch prediction, (2) ROB for in-order commit, (3) CDB broadcasts to RS and ROB (not ARF).
- **ROB operations:** Allocate (at tail), write-back (set Ready), commit (at head, non-speculative), squash (on mispredict).
- **Misprediction recovery:** Squash all ROB entries after the branch; restore PC; clear RS entries dependent on squashed results. Architectural state is *never* polluted.
- **Precise exceptions** are guaranteed because architectural state is updated strictly in program order.
- **Stores** are speculative and buffered in the ROB — they hit memory only at commit.
- **Effective CPI formula:** $\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + P \cdot \text{MR} \cdot I_{\text{branch}}$
- **Speculative window** is bounded by $\min(N_{\text{ROB}}, F_{\text{rate}} \times D_{\text{pipe}})$.
- **Branch predictor metrics** — both **accuracy** and **resolution latency** matter. A slower but more accurate predictor can be *worse* overall.
- **Real-world implication:** Speculation exposes ILP across control dependencies; Spectre/Meltdown showed it can have security side effects (out-of-scope for KTU but worth knowing).
- **Memory trick for exams:** *"Speculative Tomasulo: **CDB → ROB → ARF**"* (the register file is the LAST stop, not the first).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reorder Buffers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reorder Buffers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** is a hardware structure used in dynamically scheduled, out-of-order execution processors that ensures *in-order commit* (also called *in-order retirement*) of instructions. It temporarily holds the results of executed instructions between the time they complete execution and the time they are committed (permanently written back to the architectural register file or memory), thereby preserving the precise exception and interrupt behavior required by the sequential architectural model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Reorder Buffer is the central data structure of the *commit* (or *graduation*) stage in modern superscalar processors. It bridges the gap between the *Execution* stage (where instructions finish out of order) and the *Commit* stage (where results are made architecturally visible in order).

In formal KTU terminology, the ROB is defined as:

> An **FIFO-ordered circular buffer** that records, in program order, the destination register (or memory address), the computed value, and the readiness status of every in-flight instruction, guaranteeing that the processor can *undo* (roll back) the effects of speculative or faulty instructions and can *commit* only those whose preceding instructions have all successfully completed.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are the **manager of a busy kitchen** in a fine-dining restaurant. Chefs (execution units) cook dishes (instructions) in whatever order is fastest — maybe the dessert finishes before the soup. However, the **waiter must serve dishes to the customer in the exact order they were ordered** (appetizer, then soup, then main course, then dessert).

You solve this by stacking finished dishes in a **heated holding area (the ROB)**, in the order they were placed on the stove (program order). The waiter only picks up the **front-most dish** that is ready. If a dish is burnt (exception) or the customer cancels (branch misprediction), you simply throw away that dish and everything placed after it — none of those were ever "served" to the customer.

In this analogy:
- **Reservation Stations** = the stoves/ovens where dishes are cooked in parallel
- **Functional Units** = the chefs
- **Reorder Buffer** = the heated holding/cart area
- **Architectural Register File** = the customer's table (the final destination)
- **In-order commit** = the waiter's strict delivery order

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Properties

- **Width:** Typically **64, 128, or 192 entries** in modern high-performance cores.
- **Associativity / Fields per entry:** Each entry contains multiple fields (detailed in §2).
- **Critical Path:** The ROB is on the *commit* critical path; its head pointer comparison logic limits clock frequency.
- **Operating Principle:** Strictly **First-In-First-Out (FIFO)** for commit, but allows out-of-order completion internally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Concept:** The ROB was introduced in the **Tomasulo-with-ROB** scheme (a refinement of the original 1967 Tomasulo Algorithm) and is the defining feature of dynamically scheduled processors such as the MIPS R10000, Intel Pentium Pro/Pentium 4, AMD K5/K7, and modern out-of-order cores.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need a Reorder Buffer?

Without a ROB, the original Tomasulo scheme writes results directly to the register file (or to a Common Data Bus, CDB) where waiting reservation stations pick them up. This causes three serious problems:

1. **Exceptions are imprecise:** When an instruction faults, later instructions that have already updated the register file cannot be undone — the architectural state is corrupted.
2. **Branch misprediction recovery is impossible:** Speculative instructions may have polluted the architectural state.
3. **Interrupts cannot be precisely placed:** The processor cannot resume at the exact faulting instruction.

The ROB solves all three by **decoupling execution from architectural visibility**.

## 2.2 Architecture of a Reorder Buffer Entry

Each ROB entry typically contains the following fields:

| Field | Width (typical) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Status (Busy/Ready)** | 1 bit | Indicates whether the result has been computed and written into the value field. |
| **Destination Register (Dest)** | 5–8 bits | Architectural register number where the result must be committed. |
| **Destination Type (DestType)** | 1–2 bits | Distinguishes *register* vs. *memory store* vs. *branch*. |
| **Value (Result)** | 32 / 64 bits | The actual computed result waiting to be committed. |
| **Memory Address** | 32 / 64 bits | For store instructions, the effective address to be written. |
| **Store Data** | 32 / 64 bits | For store instructions, the value to be written. |
| **PC (Program Counter)** | 32 / 64 bits | For precise exception reporting — the address of this instruction. |
| **Branch Mask / Speculative Tag** | bits | Marks whether the instruction is speculative and under which branch. |
| **Exception Flag** | bits | Set if the instruction raised an exception. |

## 2.3 Operational Workflow (The Five-Stage Flow)

The ROB participates in a five-stage pipeline interaction:

1. **Dispatch / Issue (ROB Allocation):** When an instruction is *issued*, a free ROB entry is allocated at the *tail* of the buffer. The instruction's destination register tag is *renamed* to point to this ROB entry instead of the architectural register.

2. **Execute (Out of Order):** The instruction executes in a reservation station. Sources are read from the register file, the bypass network, or forwarded from other ROB entries.

3. **Write Result (Complete):** When execution finishes, the result is broadcast on the **Common Data Bus (CDB)** and **simultaneously written into the value field of the corresponding ROB entry**. The Ready bit is set.

4. **Commit / Retire (In Order):** The ROB *head pointer* checks the entry at the front. If it is *Ready* and not speculative, the value is written to the **architectural register file** (or to memory for stores), and the head pointer advances.

5. **Recovery (Exception / Misprediction):** If the head entry has an *exception flag* set, or if a branch was mispredicted, the ROB is **flushed from the mispredicted entry onward**. The tail pointer is rewound, and fetching restarts from the corrected PC.

## 2.4 Branch Speculation Interaction

The ROB integrates tightly with the branch predictor. When a branch is dispatched:
- It is allocated a ROB entry marked *speculative*.
- When the branch resolves correctly, its ROB entry is marked *non-speculative* — this implicitly validates all younger entries under it.
- When the branch resolves incorrectly, the ROB is squashed *up to and including* the branch entry, and the tail pointer is reset.

## 2.5 Memory Store Handling

For a `store` instruction, the ROB entry holds the **effective memory address** and the **store data** until commit. The store does **not** update the data cache until it reaches the head of the ROB and is committed. This allows easy rollback on misprediction (just discard the uncommitted store).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU-Favorite):** A **Store Buffer** (write buffer) is *separate* from the ROB and sits between the ROB and the L1 data cache. The ROB retires the store by writing into the store buffer; the store buffer then drains into the cache.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Metric | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ROB capacity utilization | $U_{rob} = \dfrac{N_{active}}{N_{entries}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le U_{rob} \le 1$ |
| Mean ROB occupancy | $\overline{O}_{rob} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \cdot i$ | entries | $p_i$ = probability of $i$ active entries |
| IPC limit by ROB | $IPC \le \dfrac{N_{entries}}{D_{avg}}$ | instr/cycle | $D_{avg}$ = average dispatch-to-commit latency in cycles |
| Wakeup bandwidth | $B_{wake} = W_{issue} \cdot N_{ROB\_ports}$ | operands/cycle | $W_{issue}$ = issue width |
| Commit throughput | $T_{commit} = \min(W_{commit}, \, f_{clk})$ | instr/cycle | $W_{commit}$ = commit width |
| Misprediction penalty | $P_{mis} = D_{branch\_to\_commit} \cdot N_{mispred}$ | cycles | includes full ROB drain |
| Required ROB size for tolerance | $N_{req} = \lceil D_{mis} \cdot IPC \rceil$ | entries | $D_{mis}$ = misprediction detection latency |
| Speculative entries | $N_{spec} = \sum_{b \in branches} D_{resolve}(b)$ | entries | sum across unresolved branches |
| Branch confidence threshold | $C_{th}$ | probability | only speculate if $P_{correct} > C_{th}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When a question says *"the ROB is full"*, it means **no entry can be allocated**. The *front-end* (dispatch) must *stall*, even if reservation stations and functional units are idle. This is the **head-of-line blocking** characteristic of the ROB.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler / Hardware Co-design:** ROB-aware scheduling must avoid generating instruction sequences that exceed ROB capacity.
- **Verification & Debugging:** Modern post-silicon debug tools use the ROB PC field to give cycle-accurate instruction traces.
- **Performance Modeling:** ROB size is one of the most heavily tuned parameters in tools like gem5, McPAT, and ChampSim.
- **Power Optimization:** Large ROBs are power-hungry; commercial designs often use **clustered ROBs** or **issue queues** to reduce wakeup energy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum ROB Size to Hide Misprediction Latency

The processor can only retire an instruction after it is the *oldest* completed instruction in the ROB. Therefore, while a mispredicted branch is still in flight, the ROB must hold *all instructions fetched between the misprediction and its resolution*.

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**

Let:
- $D_{mis}$ = the number of cycles between *fetching* a branch and *resolving* (executing) it (misprediction detection latency).
- $f$ = clock frequency in Hz, $T_{clk} = 1/f$ = clock period.
- $IPC$ = sustained instructions per cycle.
- $N_{req}$ = minimum number of ROB entries required to *hide* the misprediction latency (so that the front-end is never stalled by a full ROB on a misprediction).

**Step 2 — Number of in-flight instructions during the misprediction window.**

The number of instructions fetched (and dispatched, in steady state) in the $D_{mis}$-cycle window is:

$$
N_{inflight} = IPC \cdot D_{mis}
$$

**Step 3 — Add safety margin and head-of-line headroom.**

To avoid the *exact* boundary condition where the ROB becomes full precisely at resolution, we take the *ceiling* and add 1 entry (the mispredicted branch itself):

$$
N_{req} = \lceil IPC \cdot D_{mis} \rceil + 1
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute example values (KTU-style numerical).**

Suppose a KTU textbook example uses:
- $IPC = 4$ instructions/cycle (4-wide issue)
- $D_{mis} = 20$ cycles (typical for a 4-issue out-of-order core with a 20-cycle branch resolution pipeline)

**Step 5 — Compute the in-flight count.**

$$
N_{inflight} = 4 \cdot 20 = 80 \text{ instructions}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the ceiling operator.**

$$
\lceil 80 \rceil = 80
$$

**Step 7 — Add 1 for the branch instruction itself.**

$$
N_{req} = 80 + 1 = \boxed{81 \text{ entries}}
$$

**Step 8 — Practical rounding.**

In real designs, ROB sizes are powers of two or multiples of 16 for alignment. Hence designers would provision $N = 96$ or $N = 128$ entries to leave headroom for cache misses and other long-latency events.

## 3.2 Derivation: Commit Throughput Bound

Let $W_{commit}$ be the number of ROB entries that can be retired per cycle, $f_{clk}$ the clock, and $N_{entries}$ the total ROB size.

**Step 1 — Maximum commit rate.**

The maximum commit rate (instructions retired per second) is:

$$
R_{commit} = W_{commit} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

**Step 2 — Time to drain a full ROB.**

$$
t_{drain} = \frac{N_{entries}}{W_{commit}} \cdot T_{clk} = \frac{N_{entries}}{R_{commit} / f_{clk}} \cdot T_{clk} = \frac{N_{entries} \cdot T_{clk}}{W_{commit}}
$$

**Step 3 — Specialize for $W_{commit} = 1$ (single commit port).**

$$
t_{drain} = N_{entries} \cdot T_{clk}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

For $N_{entries} = 128$, $f_{clk} = 3 \text{ GHz}$:

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{9}} \approx 0.333 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
t_{drain} = 128 \cdot 0.333 \text{ ns} = 42.67 \text{ ns}
$$

This means after a branch misprediction flush, the worst-case time to drain the speculative instructions is ~43 ns — a hard lower bound on the front-end stall.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ROB Behavior Under Events

| Event | Effect on ROB | Recovery Action |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction issued | Allocate entry at tail; mark *not ready*, *not speculative* (initially) | None |
| Branch issued | Allocate entry at tail; mark *speculative* | None |
| Branch resolved correctly | Mark entry & all younger entries *non-speculative* | None |
| Branch mispredicted | Mark exception-like flag at branch entry | Flush from branch entry to tail; redirect fetch |
| Instruction completes | Broadcast result; set *Ready* bit; write Value | None |
| Instruction at head is *Ready* + non-speculative | Write Value to architectural register (or store buffer) | Advance head pointer |
| Instruction at head raises exception | Set exception flag | Flush from this entry onward; deliver exception to OS with correct PC |
| Store at head | Write (addr, data) to store buffer | Advance head pointer |
| Load executes (out of order) | Check store buffer / ROB for forwarding | None |
| ROB is full | Cannot dispatch new instructions | Stall front-end until head entry retires |

## 3.4 Quantitative Worked Example (KTU Past-Year Style)

> **Question setup:** A 2-wide superscalar processor uses a ROB with $N=64$ entries. A long-latency instruction (L2 miss) takes 100 cycles to complete. The processor dispatches 2 instructions per cycle. After the long-latency instruction, no more instructions can commit (head-of-line block). How many cycles does the processor take to recover its full commit bandwidth?

**Step 1 — Total instructions in flight when long-latency L2 miss occurs.**

Assume 2 IPC sustained:

$$
N_{inflight} = 2 \cdot 100 = 200 \text{ instructions}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare to ROB capacity.**

$$
N_{entries} = 64 < 200
$$

The ROB will fill up long before the miss resolves.

**Step 3 — Time to fill the ROB.**

$$
t_{fill} = \frac{64}{2} = 32 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Stall duration.**

After cycle 32, dispatch stalls. The miss resolves at cycle 100. So the stall lasts:

$$
t_{stall} = 100 - 32 = 68 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 5 — Throughput loss.**

Lost commits in stall window = 0 (nothing commits during stall — head is blocked by the in-flight miss).

**Step 6 — Insight.**

This is exactly why **ROB size must scale with both issue width and memory latency**; otherwise the ROB becomes a *throughput bottleneck*, not a feature.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ROB Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Dispatch Stage] -- Allocate entry --> B[Reorder Buffer]
    B -- Read result from CDB --> C[Write-Back Stage]
    C -- Broadcast result --> D[Reservation Stations]
    C -- Write into entry --> B
    B -- Check head Ready --> E[Commit Stage]
    E -- Write to Register File --> F[Architectural Register File]
    E -- Forward store to --> G[Store Buffer]
    G -- Drain into --> H[L1 Data Cache]
    B -- Exception / Misprediction --> I[Recovery / Flush Logic]
    I -- Rewind tail, redirect --> A
```

## 4.2 Detailed ROB Entry Layout (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Entry[ROB Entry i] --> F1[Busy / Ready bit]
    Entry --> F2[Instruction Type]
    Entry --> F3[Destination Register Tag]
    Entry --> F4[Value Result Field]
    Entry --> F5[Program Counter PC]
    Entry --> F6[Speculative / Branch Mask]
    Entry --> F7[Exception Flag]
    Entry --> F8[For Store: Memory Address]
    Entry --> F9[For Store: Store Data]
```

## 4.3 ROB State Machine for a Single Entry (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Issued
    Issued --> Executing : Dispatched to Reservation Station
    Executing --> Ready : Result arrives on CDB
    Ready --> Committed : Head pointer reaches this entry, not speculative, no exception
    Ready --> Flushed : Branch misprediction squashes this entry
    Ready --> Exception : Exception flag set at this entry
    Committed --> [*]
    Flushed --> [*]
    Exception --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Interaction with Front-End During Flush (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant FE as Front End (Fetch/Decode)
    participant RS as Reservation Station
    participant FU as Functional Unit
    participant ROB as Reorder Buffer
    participant ARF as Architectural Register File

    FE->>RS: Dispatch I1 (with dest RegX)
    FE->>ROB: Allocate ROB tail entry for I1
    RS->>FU: Execute I1
    FU->>ROB: Write result, set Ready=1
    ROB->>ARF: Commit when at head
    Note over ROB,FE: If branch mispredicted:
    ROB-->>RS: Invalidate matching RS entries
    ROB-->>FE: Signal redirect PC
    FE->>FE: Flush pipeline, restart fetch
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Conceptual Layout)

```
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  FRONT END (Fetch/Decode/Dispatch)         |
|   [I-Cache] -> [Pre-decoder] -> [Rename] -> [Dispatch]     |
+----------------------------+-------------------------------+
                             |
                             v
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|              REORDER BUFFER (Circular FIFO)                |
|   [Head] [Entry0] [Entry1] ... [EntryN-1] [Tail]           |
|        ^                              ^                    |
|        | Allocate                     | Commit/Retire     |
+----------------------------+-------------------------------+
                             |
        +--------------------+--------------------+
        |                                         |
        v                                         v
+-------------------+                   +-----------------------+
| RESERVATION       |                   | FUNCTIONAL UNITS      |
| STATIONS          |  --- Execute ---> | (ALU, FPU, LSU, BRU) |
| (issue queues)    |                   +-----------+-----------+
+---------+---------+                               |
          ^                                         |
          |              CDB (bypass)               |
          +-----------------------------------------+
                             |
                             v
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|              COMMIT / WRITE-BACK (Retire)                 |
|   [Architectural Reg File]   [Store Buffer]   [Exceptions]|
+------------------------------------------------------------+
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q:** What is a Reorder Buffer (ROB)? State its primary function in a dynamically scheduled superscalar processor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Reorder Buffer (ROB) is a FIFO hardware structure that holds the results of executed instructions in program order, between the *execute* and *commit* stages of a superscalar pipeline (1 mark). Its primary function is to **decouple out-of-order execution from in-order retirement**, thereby providing *precise exceptions*, *speculative execution recovery*, and *in-order architectural state updates* (2 marks).

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q:** Differentiate between the *in-order issue with in-order completion* approach and the *in-order issue with out-of-order completion* approach that uses a ROB. How does the ROB enable the latter?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In the *in-order issue, in-order completion* approach, an instruction cannot be issued until the previous one has completed, leading to idle functional units (1 mark). In the *in-order issue, out-of-order completion* approach, instructions are issued in order but execute in parallel; however, their results must be retired in program order to maintain precise exceptions (1 mark). The ROB enables this by buffering completed results and committing them in program (FIFO) order at the head, while execution proceeds out of order at the tail (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**a)** Explain the architecture of a Reorder Buffer entry in detail. List all fields and justify why each is required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Reorder Buffer is organized as a circular array of fixed-size entries, each representing one in-flight instruction. Each entry contains the following fields (1 mark per field, 7 marks total):

1. **Instruction Status / Busy bit (1 bit):** Indicates whether the entry is currently *occupied* (allocated) or *free*. Prevents the dispatcher from overwriting an in-use entry.

2. **Destination Register Tag (5–8 bits):** Identifies the architectural destination register. Necessary for *register renaming* — the tag is used by younger instructions to read this instruction's result via the CDB.

3. **Destination Type (1–2 bits):** Distinguishes between *integer*, *floating-point*, *store*, and *branch* destinations. This determines where the result is committed (register file, store buffer, or branch recovery unit).

4. **Value / Result Field (32 or 64 bits):** Stores the actual computed result broadcast on the CDB. Required so that, even if the architectural register is later renamed away, the value is preserved for commit.

5. **Memory Address (32 or 64 bits):** For store instructions, holds the effective address. Required for two reasons: (i) the address is needed for forwarding to later loads, and (ii) the store cannot be committed to memory until the head pointer reaches it.

6. **Store Data (32 or 64 bits):** For store instructions, the value to be written to memory. Held in the ROB until commit because stores must be retired in order.

7. **Program Counter (PC) (32 or 64 bits):** Required for *precise exception reporting* — the OS exception handler must be told the exact address of the faulting instruction.

8. **Speculative / Branch Mask bits:** Identifies under which unresolved branch the instruction was issued. If the branch mispredicts, all entries with that bit set are squashed.

9. **Exception Flag:** Set by the functional unit if the instruction raised a fault (e.g., page fault, divide-by-zero). When such an entry reaches the head, the processor delivers a precise exception and flushes the pipeline from that point.

**Justification summary:** Every field is essential for either *correctness* (precise exceptions, in-order commit) or *performance* (speculation, out-of-order execution). Together they form a complete "instruction record" that survives from issue to retirement.

---

**b)** A 4-wide superscalar out-of-order processor has a 128-entry ROB, a 20-cycle branch misprediction penalty, and sustains 3 IPC on average. Misprediction rate is 5% of all branches, and 20% of all instructions are branches. Calculate:
   (i) the steady-state ROB occupancy, and
   (ii) whether the ROB is a bottleneck. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Instructions between mispredictions.**

Misprediction rate = 5% of branches.
Branch frequency = 20% of instructions.

$$
P_{mis} = 0.20 \times 0.05 = 0.01
$$

So 1% of all instructions are mispredictions.

**Step 2 — Instructions per misprediction (geometric mean in steady state).**

$$
N_{per\_mis} = \frac{1}{P_{mis}} = 100 \text{ instructions}
$$

**Step 3 — In-flight instructions at the moment of misprediction.**

With $D_{mis} = 20$ cycles and $IPC = 3$:

$$
N_{inflight} = 3 \times 20 = 60 \text{ instructions}
$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state ROB occupancy.**

Assume the average *useful* in-flight count (between mispredictions) is half the maximum:

$$
\overline{N}_{rob} = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ entries}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to ROB capacity.**

$$
\overline{N}_{rob} = 30 \ll N_{rob} = 128
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**

The ROB is **not a bottleneck**. Only 30 of 128 entries are used on average, so the front-end will rarely stall. Designers could reduce ROB to 64 entries and save power without performance loss. **(Full 7 marks awarded for the calculation chain and the justified conclusion.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how the Reorder Buffer supports *speculative execution* and enables recovery from branch misprediction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block diagram (textual representation, equivalent to Figure 4.5 above):**

```
Front End --> Dispatcher --> [ROB Tail Alloc] --> Reservation Stations
                                  |                       |
                                  v                       v
                            (in-order allocation)   (out-of-order execute)
                                                          |
                                                          v
                                              CDB <-- Write Result
                                               |
                                               v
                                          [ROB Entry Ready]
                                               |
                                               v
                                         [Commit Logic]
                                               |
                              +----------------+----------------+
                              |                                 |
                              v                                 v
                    Architectural Register File         Store Buffer
```

**Explanation (7 marks broken down):**

1. **Marking (1 mark):** When a branch is dispatched, the dispatcher allocates a ROB entry and sets its *speculative bit*. All subsequent instructions also have their *branch mask* set to indicate they are under this branch.

2. **Restricted commit (1 mark):** The commit logic refuses to retire any entry whose *speculative bit* is set, even if the entry is *Ready*. This prevents architectural state from being polluted by speculative results.

3. **Branch resolution (1 mark):** When the branch executes, the Branch Functional Unit (BRU) writes the outcome into the branch's ROB entry and broadcasts it.

4. **Correct prediction path (1 mark):** If the branch is correctly predicted, the branch's ROB entry has its *speculative bit* cleared. This implicitly clears the speculation mask of all younger entries, and they become eligible for commit.

5. **Misprediction path (1 mark):** If the branch is *incorrectly* predicted, the recovery unit (1) marks the branch's ROB entry with an exception-like flag, (2) flushes the ROB from the branch entry to the tail, invalidating all matching RS entries, and (3) redirects the fetch unit to the corrected PC.

6. **Why this works (1 mark):** Because the ROB preserves *all* in-flight results in program order, the architectural state (register file, memory) is never modified by a speculative instruction. Thus, recovery requires no rollback of architectural state — only discarding uncommitted ROB entries.

7. **Precise restart (1 mark):** After flush, the tail pointer is rewound, the reservation stations are invalidated, and the front-end restarts fetching from the correct path with the original architectural state intact.

---

**b)** Compare the original Tomasulo algorithm (without ROB) with the Tomasulo-with-ROB scheme. Under what circumstances does the original scheme produce *imprecise exceptions*, and how does adding a ROB fix this? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Original Tomasulo (1 mark):**
- Uses reservation stations with implicit register renaming via tags.
- Completed results are written *directly* to the **register file** (or to a Common Data Bus that updates the RF).
- The architectural register file is updated as soon as an instruction completes, regardless of program order.

**Tomasulo-with-ROB (1 mark):**
- Adds the ROB between the CDB and the architectural register file.
- Completed results are written to the ROB entry, *not* to the RF.
- The RF is updated only at commit, in program order.

**Why original Tomasulo produces imprecise exceptions (3 marks):**

Consider this instruction sequence:

```
I1: DIV r1, r2, r3   (will trap — divide by zero)
I2: ADD r4, r5, r6
I3: MUL r7, r1, r8   (depends on I1)
```

In original Tomasulo:
- I2 and I3 may issue before I1 completes.
- I2 may complete and write r4 to the RF.
- I1 traps.
- The architectural state is now: r4 has been updated, but r1 (used by I3) has not. The program counter is at the trap handler, but the state is "in between" two instructions — **imprecise**.
- Resuming execution after the trap handler finishes is impossible without saving and restoring the entire register file.

**How the ROB fixes this (2 marks):**

- I1's ROB entry is allocated first (at the tail), then I2's, then I3's.
- I1 executes, traps, and its ROB entry is marked with an *exception flag*.
- I2 completes and writes its result into its *ROB entry* (not the RF).
- I3 completes and writes into its ROB entry.
- I1's entry is at the head. The commit logic sees the exception flag, *flushes all ROB entries*, and delivers the exception to the OS with I1's PC.
- The RF is in the exact state it was *before* I1 — the OS can handle the trap and resume precisely at I1.
- This is a **precise exception**.

**Summary (closing 1 mark for trade-off):**
The ROB adds hardware cost (entries, comparators, head/tail pointers) and a cycle of latency on the commit path, but it provides precise exceptions, speculative execution support, and in-order architectural updates — features essential for modern ISA compatibility.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU ROB Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "completion" with "commit":** Students frequently write "the result is written to the register file when the instruction completes." This is WRONG in a ROB scheme — the result is written to the **ROB entry** on completion and to the **register file** only on commit. **(–2 marks per occurrence)**
> 2. **Forgetting the *precise exception* requirement:** A definition of ROB that omits "precise exception" or "in-order commit" is incomplete. **(–1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing ROB with Reservation Station:** The RS holds *in-flight operands waiting to execute*; the ROB holds *executed results waiting to retire*. They are separate structures.
> 4. **Mis-stating branch recovery:** The recovery is *flush from the branch ROB entry to the tail*, not "flush everything." Earlier (older) instructions are still valid.
> 5. **Ignoring store handling:** Many students forget that *stores* do not update the data cache until the ROB head retires them — they go through a *store buffer* first.
> 6. **Units in numerical problems:** Always state the units of $D_{mis}$ (cycles), $IPC$ (instr/cycle), and $N_{rob}$ (entries). KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ROB (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- **Definition:** A circular FIFO buffer that holds executed-but-uncommitted instructions in program order.
- **Three roles:** (1) In-order commit, (2) Precise exceptions, (3) Speculative execution recovery.
- **Entry fields:** Busy, Dest, DestType, Value, PC, Speculative mask, Exception flag, (for stores) Address and Data.
- **Lifecycle:** Issue → Execute → Write Result to ROB → Commit to RF/Store Buffer → Free.
- **Head pointer:** Advances only when the front entry is *Ready* and *non-speculative* and *exception-free*.
- **Tail pointer:** Advances on each dispatch (whenever a free entry is available).
- **Full ROB:** Stalls the *dispatch* stage; functional units may be idle.
- **Branch recovery:** Flush from branch entry to tail; rewind tail pointer; redirect fetch.
- **Store commit:** Stores retire to a *store buffer* (not directly to L1 cache); store buffer then drains.
- **Load forwarding:** Loads may scan the ROB and store buffer for matching addresses (memory dependence prediction).
- **Capacity design rule:** $N_{rob} \ge IPC \cdot D_{mis} + 1$ (misprediction-driven lower bound).
- **Trade-off:** Larger ROB → better IPC, but more power, longer wakeup latency, harder clocking.
- **Industry use:** MIPS R10000, Intel Pentium Pro/II/III, AMD K7/K8/K10, ARM Cortex-A77/A78, Apple M1/M2.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to *compare* schemes, always structure the answer as a **3-row table** — (1) Feature, (2) Without ROB, (3) With ROB. Examiners reward systematic presentation with full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Issue and Static Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple Issue and Static Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Multiple Issue** is a microarchitectural technique in advanced pipelined processors where the CPU dispatches (issues) **more than one instruction per clock cycle** to the available execution units. The theoretical lower bound of the base CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) is reduced to $1/k$, where $k$ is the maximum number of instructions issued in a single cycle.

**Static Scheduling** is the process of reordering, bundling, and organizing instructions **at compile time** (i.e., by the compiler) to expose and exploit Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP), without any runtime hardware reordering logic. The hardware is kept simple, and all scheduling intelligence is pushed into the compiler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Static Multiple Issue processors rely on the compiler to find independent instructions and pack them into a single wide "issue packet" (also called a *bundle* or *VLIW instruction*). The hardware blindly executes whatever the compiler packages together, fetching one bundle per cycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multi-Lane Toll Booth"

Imagine a single-lane toll booth on a highway. Even if cars arrive at the exact same time, only one can pay and pass per second. This is a single-issue processor.

Now, suppose the highway authority builds **4 toll booths side-by-side** that all open at the exact same moment when the traffic light turns green. A traffic controller (the **compiler**) must pre-sort the cars into groups of 4 so that:
- No two cars in the same group need the same booth.
- The 4 cars in a group have no dependencies on each other (a sports car does not need to wait for a truck ahead of it).

The controller can even **pre-print combo passes** (representing NOPs or pre-computed independent work) to fill empty slots. Once sorted, the cars are released in 4-wide bundles every clock cycle. This is **Static Multiple Issue**.

| Concept | Toll Booth Analogy |
|---|---|
| Pipeline stage | One toll booth / cashier window |
| Issue width ($k$) | Number of parallel booths |
| Static scheduling | Traffic controller sorting cars in advance |
| VLIW bundle | A group of $k$ cars released together |
| NOP / Padding | Pre-printed "no-car" passes to fill empty slots |
| Compiler speculation | Controller pre-estimating future car arrivals |

## 1.3 The Two Dominant Static Multiple Issue Paradigms

KTU examiners frequently test the distinction between two industrial implementations:

1. **VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word):**
   - Fixed-width instruction packet (e.g., always 128 bits containing 4 operations).
   - Each operation slot is hard-wired to a specific functional unit.
   - Examples: Multiflow TRACE, Transmeta Crusoe, TI TMS320C6x DSP family.

2. **EPIC (Explicitly Parallel Instruction Computing):**
   - Variable-size bundles, but template bits in the bundle tell the hardware which operations can execute in parallel.
   - The compiler is explicitly responsible for marking parallelism.
   - Example: **Intel Itanium (IA-64) architecture.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Issue Slot:** A position inside a wide instruction packet (the "bundle") that may contain one operation, a NOP, or a marker indicating parallelism boundaries. The number of issue slots per packet defines the **issue width** of the machine.

## 1.4 Why Static Scheduling Matters

- **Hardware Simplicity:** No out-of-order logic, no reorder buffer, no reservation stations. This lowers power and silicon cost.
- **Determinism:** Behavior is fully predictable, which is excellent for real-time and embedded DSP systems.
- **Compiler Intelligence:** The burden of finding parallelism shifts from transistors to sophisticated compiler algorithms (list scheduling, trace scheduling, software pipelining).
- **The Trade-off:** Since the compiler cannot know the runtime data values perfectly, **speculative execution** must also be done at compile time, requiring special hardware support (like the Itanium's *advanced load address table*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Static Issue Pipeline — Step-by-Step Operational Flow

A static multiple-issue pipeline typically augments the standard 5-stage MIPS pipeline into a **wider, multi-lane structure**. The stages are identical conceptually, but the *Issue* and *Execute* stages operate on $k$ instructions in parallel.

### Step 1 — Instruction Fetch (IF)
A single wide instruction fetch reads an entire **bundle** (or *VLIW word*) of $k$ instructions per cycle. The PC advances by $k$ instruction slots, not by 1.

### Step 2 — Decode / Issue (ID/ISS)
All $k$ instructions are decoded simultaneously. The hardware verifies the **template** (in EPIC) or the **slot-to-function-unit mapping** (in VLIW). The compiler is responsible for ensuring there are no data hazards *within* the same bundle.

### Step 3 — Read Operands / Register Fetch (RF)
Operands are read from the register file in parallel for all $k$ instructions. This means the register file must have **$k$ read ports** to avoid structural hazards.

### Step 4 — Execution (EX)
Instructions are dispatched to their corresponding **functional units**. Latencies vary (e.g., ALU = 1 cycle, Load = 2 cycles, FP Divide = 20+ cycles). The pipeline naturally has multiple parallel ALU, Memory, and Branch units.

### Step 5 — Write Back (WB)
Results are written back. To avoid Write-After-Write (WAW) structural hazards in the register file, **$k$ write ports** are required.

## 2.2 The "Issue Packet" and Bundling Logic

The compiler groups instructions into issue packets subject to three strict rules:

1. **No structural conflicts:** The packet cannot demand more than one operation of the same type if only one such unit exists.
2. **No data hazards inside the packet:** No result produced in packet $i$ is consumed within packet $i$.
3. **At most $k$ instructions** are placed in a packet.

If the compiler cannot find enough independent instructions, it inserts explicit **NOPs (no-operation padding)** into the remaining slots.

## 2.3 Loop Unrolling — The Compiler's Primary Tool

To fill wide issue packets, the compiler **unrolls loops** — duplicating the loop body multiple times and adjusting the induction variable. This:
- Increases the basic block size.
- Exposes more independent instructions across iterations.
- Reduces branch overhead.

For a loop unrolled $n$ times on a $k$-issue machine, the average cycles per original iteration ideally approaches $1/k$.

## 2.4 Software Pipelining vs. Loop Unrolling

| Technique | Mechanism | Pro | Con |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Loop Unrolling** | Replicates loop body, schedules flat | Simple, intuitive | Increases code size, may exceed I-cache |
| **Software Pipelining** | Initiates next iteration before current finishes; uses a *prologue*, *kernel*, *epilogue* | Constant, compact code; high throughput | Complex compiler analysis, harder to debug |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULA BOX — Memorize for ESE**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CPI}_{\text{base}} = \dfrac{1}{k}$ | Lower bound of CPI for an ideal $k$-issue machine (no stalls) | cycles/instruction |
| 2 | $\text{IPC}_{\text{peak}} = k$ | Peak instructions per cycle | instructions/cycle |
| 3 | $\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \text{Stall Cycles per Bundle}$ | Measured CPI after accounting for hazards | cycles/instruction |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{\text{Time}_{\text{single}}}{\text{Time}_{\text{multiple}}}$ | Speedup of multiple issue over single issue | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Time} = \dfrac{N \cdot \text{CPI} \cdot \text{Clock Period}}{1}$ | Execution time for $N$ instructions | seconds |
| 6 | $\text{Loop Unroll Factor} = n$ | Number of times loop body is replicated | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Effective CPI after unroll} = \dfrac{\text{CPI}_{\text{original}}}{n} + \text{overhead}$ | Approximation for unrolled loops | cycles/instruction |
| 8 | $\text{Bundle size} = k \times \text{op-size} + \text{template bits}$ | EPIC/VLIW fetch width | bits |
| 9 | $\text{Reg. file ports} = k_{\text{read}} + k_{\text{write}}$ | To support $k$-issue without structural stalls | ports |
| 10 | $\text{Speedup}_{\text{limit}} \le k$ | Theoretical upper bound of multiple issue | dimensionless |

> **Absolute value safety:** Use $\vert x \vert$ written as `\vert x\vert` inside any table cell to avoid breaking markdown pipes. Example: penalty factor $= \vert r_{i} - r_{j}\vert$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DSP Processors (TI C6000):** VLIW-style static issue is the standard. The compiler schedules exactly which of the 8 functional units to use in each cycle, achieving deterministic performance in audio/video codecs and 5G baseband processing.
- **Embedded Real-Time Systems:** Determinism is critical; static scheduling avoids runtime jitter from out-of-order logic.
- **Compiler Research:** Ideas like **trace scheduling**, **superblock scheduling**, and **hyperblock scheduling** all target the static multiple issue model.
- **Code Size Pressure:** To address code bloat from unrolling/NOPs, architectures like Itanium added **predicated execution** (using `cmov`/predicates) to eliminate branches and pack more useful work into each bundle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Base CPI of an Ideal $k$-Issue Processor

**Given:**
- A pipeline that issues a *bundle* of $k$ instructions per clock cycle.
- Zero structural, data, and control hazards (ideal scenario).
- $N$ total instructions to execute.

**To find:** The minimum achievable CPI.

**Derivation:**

In an ideal $k$-issue machine, the pipeline completes one bundle (containing $k$ instructions) every clock cycle. Therefore, the number of clock cycles required to execute $N$ instructions is:

$$C_{\text{cycles}} = \frac{N}{k}$$

By definition, $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cycles}}{\text{Total Instructions}}$:

$$\text{CPI} = \frac{C_{\text{cycles}}}{N} = \frac{N / k}{N}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{k}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Substituting $C_{\text{cycles}} = N/k$ and cancelling $N$ in the numerator and denominator yields the base CPI bound. This shows that doubling the issue width halves the CPI, which is the fundamental driver of VLIW/EPIC design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Speedup of $k$-Issue Over Single-Issue

**Given:**
- A single-issue machine with base CPI = 1 (ideal) and clock period $T$.
- A $k$-issue machine with CPI = $1/k$ (ideal) and identical clock period $T$.
- $N$ instructions to execute on both.

**To find:** The speedup $S$.

**Derivation:**

Time on single-issue machine:

$$T_{\text{single}} = N \cdot 1 \cdot T = N \cdot T$$

Time on ideal $k$-issue machine:

$$T_{\text{multi}} = N \cdot \frac{1}{k} \cdot T = \frac{N \cdot T}{k}$$

Speedup is the ratio of the two:

$$S = \frac{T_{\text{single}}}{T_{\text{multi}}} = \frac{N \cdot T}{N \cdot T \,/\, k} = \frac{N \cdot T \cdot k}{N \cdot T}$$

$$\boxed{S = k}$$

**Conversion logic:** A perfectly scheduled $k$-issue machine with no stalls provides exactly a $k$-fold speedup. This is the *theoretical upper bound* of multiple issue.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective CPI of a $k$-Issue Machine With Stalls

**Given:**
- Ideal base CPI = $1/k$.
- An average of $S_b$ stall cycles per bundle (caused by hazards that the compiler could not hide).

**To find:** Actual CPI.

**Derivation:**

In an $N$-instruction program, the number of bundles is $N/k$. Total cycles = (cycles for bundles) + (stall cycles):

$$C_{\text{total}} = \frac{N}{k} + S_b \cdot \frac{N}{k} = \frac{N}{k} \left( 1 + S_b \right)$$

Dividing by $N$ instructions:

$$\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{1}{k} \left( 1 + S_b \right)$$

$$\boxed{\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{1 + S_b}{k}}$$

**Conversion logic:** A 4-issue machine ($k=4$) with 1 stall cycle per bundle has CPI $= 2/4 = 0.5$, equivalent to 2.0 IPC. This formula is heavily used in KTU problems where the compiler is unable to perfectly schedule.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Loop Unrolling Speedup

**Given:**
- Original loop: $N$ iterations.
- Per-iteration body executes $m$ instructions.
- Loop unrolled by factor $n$ on a $k$-issue machine.
- Total instructions in unrolled loop body = $m \cdot n$.

**To find:** Cycles saved per original iteration.

**Derivation (naive approach):**

Without unrolling, the compiler may issue only 1 useful instruction per cycle due to dependencies, leaving $k-1$ NOPs per cycle. CPI without unroll $\approx 1/m$ (one useful instruction per cycle if $m$ sequential operations).

After unrolling, the unrolled body of $m \cdot n$ instructions is executed in roughly:

$$C_{\text{unrolled}} = \frac{m \cdot n}{k} \text{ cycles (ideal)}$$

Cycles per original iteration:

$$\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C_{\text{unrolled}}}{n} = \frac{m}{k}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Speedup}_{\text{unroll}} = \frac{m}{m/k} = k}$$

**Conversion logic:** In the ideal case, unrolling on a $k$-issue machine achieves the same $k$-fold speedup as the multiple-issue hardware, but only if the compiler successfully exposes cross-iteration parallelism.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU ESE Style)

**Problem:** Consider a 4-issue statically scheduled VLIW processor. The compiler reports an average of 0.5 stall cycles per bundle due to unavoidable load-use dependencies. Calculate (a) the actual CPI, (b) the actual IPC, and (c) the speedup over a single-issue pipeline running the same program with 1.2 CPI.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Actual CPI:**

Using $\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{1 + S_b}{k}$:

$$\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{1 + 0.5}{4} = \frac{1.5}{4} = 0.375 \text{ cycles/instruction}$$

**Part (b) — Actual IPC:**

$$\text{IPC} = \frac{1}{\text{CPI}} = \frac{1}{0.375} = 2.667 \text{ instructions/cycle}$$

**Part (c) — Speedup over single-issue:**

$$S = \frac{\text{CPI}_{\text{single}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{multi}}} = \frac{1.2}{0.375} = 3.2$$

$$\boxed{S = 3.2 \times}$$

**Conversion logic:** Even with stall overhead, the 4-issue VLIW machine delivers a $3.2\times$ real-world speedup, demonstrating why static multiple issue remains attractive for embedded DSP and high-performance workloads.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Static Multiple Issue Pipeline Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates the $k$-wide datapath of a statically scheduled multiple-issue processor.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ICache[Instruction Cache\nWide Fetch, k instructions] --> FETCH[IF Stage\nPC advanced by k]
    FETCH --> DECODE[ID Stage\nDecode k instructions]
    DECODE --> RFRF[RF Stage\nk Read Ports]
    RFRF --> EX1[EX Stage\nALU 1]
    RFRF --> EX2[EX Stage\nALU 2]
    RFRF --> EX3[EX Stage\nMEM Unit]
    RFRF --> EX4[EX Stage\nBranch Unit]
    EX1 --> WB[WB Stage\nk Write Ports]
    EX2 --> WB
    EX3 --> WB
    EX4 --> WB
    WB --> RFReg[Register File]
```

## 4.2 Compiler Scheduling Flow — From Source Code to VLIW Bundles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code HLL] --> B[Front End\nLex + Parse]
    B --> C[Intermediate\nRepresentation]
    C --> D[Dependence Analysis\nDAG Construction]
    D --> E[List Scheduling\nAlgorithm]
    E --> F{Bundle Full?\nslot = k?}
    F -- No --> G[Add NOP to\nRemaining Slots]
    F -- Yes --> H[Emit VLIW\nBundle]
    G --> H
    H --> I{More Instructions?}
    I -- Yes --> F
    I -- No --> J[Final VLIW\nBinary]
```

## 4.3 Static Scheduling Algorithm — List Scheduling Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: DAG of Basic Block]) --> Ready[Identify Ready Nodes\nno unscheduled predecessors]
    Ready --> Pick[Pick Highest Priority\nlongest latency path]
    Pick --> Check{Bundle has\nfree slot?}
    Check -- Yes --> Place[Place in Current\nVLIW Bundle]
    Check -- No --> New[Start New\nBundle]
    New --> Place
    Place --> Update[Update DAG\nremove scheduled node]
    Update --> More{Unsorted\nNodes Left?}
    More -- Yes --> Ready
    More -- No --> Done([Emit Scheduled Code])
```

## 4.4 Hazard Resolution Mapping in Static Issue

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HAZ[TYPES OF HAZARDS]
        H1[Structural]
        H2[Data RAW]
        H3[Data WAR]
        H4[Data WAW]
        H5[Control]
    end
    H1 --> R1[Compiler: Assign\nDistinct Functional Units]
    H2 --> R2[Compiler: Schedule Dependent\nInstruction k cycles apart]
    H3 --> R3[Compiler: Rename via\nRegister Allocation]
    H4 --> R4[Compiler: Reorder Writes\nto Avoid WAW]
    H5 --> R5[Compiler: Branch Prediction\nor Predicated Execution]
```

## 4.5 Comparison — Static vs. Dynamic Multiple Issue (Schematic View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STATIC[STATIC MULTIPLE ISSUE]
        S1[Compiler] --> S2[Schedule at Build Time]
        S2 --> S3[Hardware: Simple,\nIn-Order Pipeline]
        S3 --> S4[Lower Power,\nDeterministic]
    end
    subgraph DYNAMIC[DYNAMIC MULTIPLE ISSUE]
        D1[Hardware] --> D2[Schedule at Run Time]
        D2 --> D3[Reorder Buffer,\nReservation Stations]
        D3 --> D4[Higher Performance,\nHigher Power]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Multiple Issue Processors. Differentiate between static and dynamic multiple issue with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Multiple Issue Processors** are pipelined CPUs that fetch, decode, and dispatch more than one instruction per clock cycle to exploit Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP). The base CPI approaches $1/k$ for an issue width of $k$.

| Aspect | Static Multiple Issue | Dynamic Multiple Issue |
|---|---|---|
| Scheduling done by | Compiler (at compile time) | Hardware (at run time) |
| Hardware complexity | Low (in-order) | High (ROB, RS, renaming) |
| Example architecture | VLIW (TI C6000), EPIC (Itanium) | Superscalar (Intel Core i9) |
| Power consumption | Lower | Higher |
| Determinism | High (predictable timing) | Lower (runtime variability) |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the comparison table.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a VLIW architecture? Explain the role of the compiler in achieving instruction-level parallelism in a VLIW machine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word)** is a static multiple-issue architecture in which the compiler packs multiple operations into one wide instruction word. Each field of the word corresponds to a specific functional unit, and the hardware has no intelligence to re-order instructions.

**Role of the compiler:**
- Performs **dependence analysis** to detect independent operations.
- Uses **list scheduling** to pack operations into bundles.
- Performs **loop unrolling** and **software pipelining** to expose cross-iteration parallelism.
- Inserts **NOPs** to fill empty slots and satisfy latency constraints.
- Conducts **register allocation** to avoid WAW/WAR hazards.

*(Award 1 mark for VLIW definition, 2 marks for compiler roles.)*

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget to compute the effective CPI using stalls**, instead quoting only the ideal $1/k$. This costs up to 3 marks.
> - In loop-unrolling problems, **failure to show NOP insertion** loses at least 2 marks.
> - Mixing up **VLIW and EPIC** (e.g., saying VLIW has template bits) is a 1-mark penalty.
> - Forgetting to **count branch instructions** in the issue packet is a recurring mistake.
> - Always **justify why** a particular hazard cannot be hidden by the compiler.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the static multiple-issue pipeline used in VLIW processors. With a neat diagram, describe all five stages of the pipeline and highlight the hardware requirements (register file ports, functional units) needed to support a 4-issue machine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following MIPS code segment:

```asm
loop:
    lw   $t0, 0($s1)      # Load element
    add  $t0, $t0, $s2    # Add constant
    sw   $t0, 0($s1)      # Store back
    addi $s1, $s1, -4     # Decrement pointer
    bne  $s1, $zero, loop # Branch if not zero
```

Apply **loop unrolling** to this loop by a factor of **3**, and then **schedule the unrolled code** for a **2-issue statically scheduled VLIW** processor. Assume the following latencies: `lw` = 2 cycles, `add`/`addi` = 1 cycle, `sw` = 1 cycle, `bne` = 1 cycle. Show all inserted NOPs explicitly. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

A static multiple-issue (VLIW) pipeline fetches a wide instruction word containing multiple operations per cycle. The five stages are:

1. **Instruction Fetch (IF):** Fetches a *bundle* of $k$ instructions. PC increments by $k$.
2. **Instruction Decode/Issue (ID):** All $k$ operations decoded in parallel. Hardware checks the slot-to-unit mapping.
3. **Read Operands (RF):** Reads up to $2k$ source registers (2 per instruction) from a multi-ported register file.
4. **Execution (EX):** Operations dispatched to parallel functional units — typically 2 ALUs, 1 memory port, 1 branch unit.
5. **Write Back (WB):** Up to $k$ results written back to the register file.

**Hardware requirements for $k=4$ issue:**
- 4-way **instruction cache** port.
- Register file with **8 read ports** and **4 write ports** (assuming 2 sources per inst).
- 2–4 **ALUs**, 1–2 **Memory ports**, 1–2 **Branch units**.
- Wide internal data buses.

*(Award 1 mark per stage, 2 marks for the hardware requirement discussion.)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Unroll 3 times (and adjust induction):**

```asm
loop:
    lw   $t0, 0($s1)       # I1
    lw   $t1, -4($s1)      # I2
    lw   $t2, -8($s1)      # I3
    add  $t0, $t0, $s2     # I4
    add  $t1, $t1, $s2     # I5
    add  $t2, $t2, $s2     # I6
    sw   $t0, 0($s1)       # I7
    sw   $t1, -4($s1)      # I8
    sw   $t2, -8($s1)      # I9
    addi $s1, $s1, -12     # I10
    bne  $s1, $zero, loop  # I11
```

**Step 2 — Schedule for 2-issue (ALU + MEM slot):**

Latency rules: `lw` result available after 2 cycles; `add` needs the value the same cycle it is issued → a `lw` followed by dependent `add` in the *next* cycle is safe.

| Cycle | ALU Slot | MEM Slot | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NOP | `lw $t0, 0($s1)` | First load |
| 2 | NOP | `lw $t1, -4($s1)` | Second load |
| 3 | `add $t0,$t0,$s2` | `lw $t2, -8($s1)` | Add uses $t0 (2-cycle latency OK) |
| 4 | `add $t1,$t1,$s2` | NOP | — |
| 5 | `add $t2,$t2,$s2` | `sw $t0, 0($s1)` | — |
| 6 | `addi $s1,$s1,-12` | `sw $t1, -4($s1)` | — |
| 7 | NOP | `sw $t2, -8($s1)` | — |
| 8 | NOP | `bne $s1,$zero,loop` | Branch |

**Analysis:**
- **Original loop:** 5 instructions × 5 cycles = 25 cycles (1 IPC due to dependencies).
- **Unrolled + scheduled:** 11 useful instructions in 8 cycles → ~1.375 IPC, with **5 NOPs** required.
- Effective cycles per original iteration = 8/3 ≈ 2.67.

*(Award 2 marks for unrolling, 3 marks for scheduling table, 2 marks for analysis.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare the VLIW and EPIC architectural styles. Explain with an example how **predicated execution** and **speculative loads** in EPIC help the compiler overcome branches and memory latency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-issue statically scheduled processor has a clock period of 0.5 ns. A program with $10^9$ dynamic instructions runs on it. The compiler achieves an average of 1.2 stall cycles per bundle. Compute:
   1. The actual CPI.
   2. The total execution time.
   3. The speedup over a single-issue pipeline with a 1.5 CPI on the same program and clock. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Comparison VLIW vs. EPIC:**

| Feature | VLIW | EPIC |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction grouping | Fixed wide word | Variable bundles with template bits |
| Parallelism marker | Implicit (slot position) | Explicit (template field) |
| Predication support | Limited / none | First-class (e.g., Itanium) |
| Speculative loads | Limited | Hardware-supported (ALAT) |
| Code density | Lower (more NOPs) | Higher (compaction via templates) |
| Example | TI C6000, Multiflow TRACE | Intel Itanium 2 |

**Predication example (IA-64 style):**
```asm
(p1) add r1 = r2, r3
(!p1) add r1 = r4, r5
```
Both add operations are issued in the same bundle, but only one executes depending on predicate `p1`. This eliminates a branch and its delay slot, allowing the compiler to fill the issue slot with useful work.

**Speculative load example:**
```asm
ld.s r1 = [r2]      ;; Speculative load, may not be needed
add   r3 = r1, r4   ;; Use the value
chk.s r1, recover   ;; If mispredicted, jump to recover
```
The `ld.s` is hoisted above a branch. If the branch is taken, the load is squashed via the `chk.s`. This hides memory latency, which is a major bottleneck.

*(Award 2 marks for comparison, 2 marks for predication example, 3 marks for speculative load example.)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $k = 4$, clock period $T = 0.5$ ns, $N = 10^9$ instructions, $S_b = 1.2$ stall cycles per bundle.

**(1) Actual CPI:**

$$\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{1 + S_b}{k} = \frac{1 + 1.2}{4} = \frac{2.2}{4} = 0.55 \text{ cycles/instruction}$$

**(2) Total execution time:**

First compute total bundles:
$$N_b = \frac{N}{k} = \frac{10^9}{4} = 2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ bundles}$$

Total cycles = $N_b \times (1 + S_b) = 2.5 \times 10^8 \times 2.2 = 5.5 \times 10^8$ cycles.

$$T_{\text{exec}} = 5.5 \times 10^8 \times 0.5 \text{ ns} = 2.75 \times 10^8 \text{ ns} = 0.275 \text{ s}$$

**(3) Speedup over single-issue:**

Single-issue cycles: $N \cdot 1.5 = 1.5 \times 10^9$ cycles.
Single-issue time: $1.5 \times 10^9 \times 0.5 \text{ ns} = 0.75 \text{ s}$.

$$S = \frac{0.75}{0.275} \approx 2.727$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 2.73 \times}$$

**Conversion logic:** Notice how the stalls (1.2 per bundle) drag the ideal 4× speedup down to 2.73×, a clear illustration of why static scheduling quality is critical.

*(Award 2 marks for (1), 2 marks for (2), 3 marks for (3).)*

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Multiple Issue** dispatches $k$ instructions/cycle → ideal base CPI = $1/k$.
- ⭐ **Static scheduling** moves parallelism discovery from hardware to **compiler**.
- ⭐ **VLIW** = fixed wide word; **EPIC** = variable bundle with template bits.
- ⭐ **Three hazards** must be solved by the compiler: structural, data (RAW/WAR/WAW), and control.
- ⭐ **Loop unrolling** + **list scheduling** are the two primary compiler techniques.
- ⭐ **Software pipelining** uses prologue-kernel-epilogue pattern.
- ⭐ **Predication** (EPIC) eliminates branches; **speculative loads** hide memory latency.
- ⭐ **Hardware cost:** multi-ported register file (2k read + k write ports) and parallel functional units.
- ⭐ **Key formula:** $\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{1 + S_b}{k}$.
- ⭐ **Key formula:** Speedup upper bound = $k$ (ideal) but actual is always lower due to stalls.
- ⭐ **Code size blow-up** is the main disadvantage of static issue; addressed via predication & compaction.
- ⭐ **Determinism** makes VLIW ideal for DSP and real-time embedded systems.
- ⭐ **Itanium (IA-64)** is the canonical EPIC example; **TI C6000** is the canonical VLIW DSP example.
- ⭐ **Stalls per bundle** $S_b$ in ESE problems typically come from: load-use (2-cycle), cache miss, or branch misprediction not hidden by the compiler.
- ⭐ **NOP padding** is required in VLIW; in EPIC, template bits allow a single bundle to span two cycles — fewer NOPs needed.
- ⭐ Remember to **count branch instructions** in the issue packet; a 4-issue packet can hold at most one branch.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VLIW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** VLIW # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Very Long Instruction Word (VLIW)** is an ILP (Instruction-Level Parallelism) exploitation architecture in which the compiler statically bundles multiple independent operations into a single, very long instruction word. Each "long word" contains several operation slots, and each slot is dispatched in parallel to a dedicated functional unit during the same clock cycle. The hardware contains **no dynamic scheduling, no out-of-order issue logic, and no inter-instruction dependency checks** — the responsibility of detecting parallelism and packing it into long words is shifted entirely to the **optimizing compiler (static scheduling)**. $$ \text{VLIW Instruction Word} = [\text{Op}_1 \,|\, \text{Op}_2 \,|\, \text{Op}_3 \,|\, \ldots \,|\, \text{Op}_n] $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – VLIW):** > The architectural pillars to remember are: > 1. Multiple operations packed in **one** instruction word. > 2. Operations within a word execute **simultaneously** on **parallel functional units**. > 3. The **compiler** is the "smart" component; the **hardware is deliberately simple**. > [!NOTE] > **Origin & Industry Adoption:** > The VLIW concept was pioneered by **Fisher (1983)** in the context of "VLIW microprocessors." Commercial implementations include the **Intel Itanium (IA-64/EPIC)**, **TI TMS320C6x DSPs**, **Transmeta Crusoe (with code-morphing software)**, and **Multiflow TRACE**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Master Chef's Recipe Card Imagine a restaurant kitchen: - **Scalar processor (traditional RISC/CISC)** = A single chef executing one recipe step at a time. Even if four stoves are available, only one is used per minute. - **Superscalar processor** = Four chefs working independently, but they must constantly **shout at each other** to avoid collisions (dependency checks, reservation stations, reorder buffers) — a lot of overhead. - **VLIW processor** = A **master chef (the compiler)** writes a single huge recipe card (the long instruction word). On each card, four independent tasks are written side-by-side: *"boil pasta" | "chop onions" | "fry garlic" | "set the table"*. The four assistant chefs (functional units) simply pick up the card and execute **all four tasks at once**, no questions asked. The brilliance: the *thinking* (scheduling) is done **once at compile time** by the master chef, not repeated on every order at runtime. --- ## 1.3 Defining Properties of VLIW (Examination Bullets) - **Long instruction word** — typically **128 to 512 bits** wide, holding 4–8 operations. - **Multiple functional units** — e.g., 2 integer ALUs, 2 FP units, 2 memory ports, 1 branch unit. - **Static scheduling** — all parallelism decisions made at **compile time**. - **No hazard detection hardware** — data/structural hazards are guaranteed absent by the compiler. - **Predicated execution** — supports conditional execution via predicate registers to avoid branches. - **In-order issue, in-order completion** — deterministic execution. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Answers:** > In a **superscalar**, the *hardware* discovers parallelism dynamically. > In a **VLIW**, the *compiler* discovers parallelism statically and the hardware is just a "dumb" executor. > This trade-off is the central theme of the VLIW philosophy. --- ## 1.4 Comparison Snapshot — VLIW vs Superscalar | Feature | Superscalar | VLIW | |---|---|---| | Scheduler | Hardware | Compiler | | Issue Logic | Complex (out-of-order) | None (in-order, static) | | Instruction Size | Fixed (32/64 bit) | Very long (128–512 bit) | | Power Consumption | High (dynamic checks) | Low (no runtime checks) | | Compiler Role | Minor | **Central** | | Binary Compatibility | Strong | Weak (code-morphing needed) | | Example | Intel Core i7 | Intel Itanium, TI C6x | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 VLIW Architecture — Operational Logic Decomposed ### Step 1: Program Analysis at Compile Time The compiler constructs a **Data Dependence Graph (DDG)** of the program. Each node is an operation; each edge is a **true data dependency (RAW)**, an **anti-dependency (WAR)**, or an **output dependency (WAW)**. ### Step 2: Instruction Scheduling Using techniques such as **list scheduling**, **trace scheduling**, or **software pipelining**, the compiler packs as many *independent* operations as possible into each long instruction word (often called a "**bundle**"). ### Step 3: Bundle Encoding Each bundle has: - **Operation slots** (one per functional unit, e.g., `.L1`, `.L2`, `.S1`, `.S2`, `.M1`, `.M2` in TI C6x). - A **template field** that tells the hardware which operations may legally be placed in which slots, and which slots execute in parallel. ### Step 4: Hardware Execution At runtime, the CPU simply **fetches one bundle per cycle**, splits it into its sub-operations, and routes each sub-operation to its pre-assigned functional unit. There is **no dependency check**, **no stall** (other than cache misses), and **no reordering**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Why" and "How" of VLIW:** > - **Why shift scheduling to the compiler?** Because hardware dependency checks are expensive in transistors and power; the same analysis done once at compile time amortizes across billions of executions. > - **How is performance gained?** By issuing **multiple operations per cycle** (high IPC, up to the number of functional units) **without runtime overhead**. --- ## 2.2 Core Architectural Components 1. **Wide Instruction Memory / Cache** — must fetch a 256-bit (or wider) word each cycle. 2. **Wide fetch/decode unit** — splits the long word into its constituent operations. 3. **Multiple parallel functional units**: - Integer ALU - Floating-point ALU - Multiply unit - Load/Store unit - Branch unit 4. **Large register file** — must provide enough read/write ports to feed all units simultaneously (a major VLIW design bottleneck). 5. **Predicate register file** — for conditional execution. --- ## 2.3 The Template Field — How Bundles Are Decoded In architectures like the **TI C6x**, the instruction word is divided into: - An **execute packet** = the set of operations that execute in the same cycle. - A **template** (e.g., 4 bits) that maps each of up to 8 operation slots to a specific functional unit class. Example template meanings (simplified): | Template | Meaning | |---|---| | 0000 | All 8 slots in same cycle | | 0010 | Slots 1–4 in cycle *k*, slots 5–8 in cycle *k+1* | | ... | ... | > [!NOTE] > The template allows a single long word to encode **multiple cycles** of operations, saving I-cache bandwidth. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for VLIW Performance Problems** | # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Theoretical Peak IPC** | $\text{IPC}_{\max} = n$ | $n$ = number of parallel functional units | | 2 | **Achievable Speedup** | $S = \dfrac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}}$ | Ratio of execution times | | 3 | **Ideal VLIW Speedup (no hazards)** | $S_{\text{VLIW}}^{\text{ideal}} = n$ | Upper bound, $n$ = issue width | | 4 | **Amdahl's Law (parallel fraction $p$)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$ | Limits VLIW gain by serial portions | | 5 | **Execution Time** | $T = \dfrac{N \times \text{cpi}}{f_{\text{clock}}}$ | $N$ = instructions, cpi = cycles/instruction | | 6 | **CPI of VLIW bundle** | $\text{CPI}_{\text{VLIW}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{ops per bundle}}$ | Avg. cycles per *long instruction* | | 7 | **Bundle Width** | $W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{op}_i$ | Bits per long word | | 8 | **Code Size Blowup** | $\text{CSB} = \dfrac{\text{VLIW code size}}{\text{scalar code size}}$ | Typically 1.5×–3× larger | | 9 | **Multipath Latency Bound** | $L = \max(L_{\text{ALU}}, L_{\text{MUL}}, L_{\text{MEM}})$ | Dictates minimum II in software pipelining | | 10 | **Throughput (MFLOPS)** | $\text{MFLOPS} = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{T \times 10^{6}}$ | Used in DSP / VLIW benchmarks | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical caveat on $S_{\text{VLIW}}^{\text{ideal}} = n$:** > This is **never achieved in practice** because of: > - Remaining serial (non-parallelizable) portions → **Amdahl's Law** penalty. > - **Memory latency** not hidden by parallel ALUs. > - **Branch divergence** in control flow. > - **Register pressure** forcing spills. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** TI TMS320C67x uses VLIW for real-time audio/雷达/communication kernels where loops are predictable. - **Embedded Multimedia:** Used in video codecs (H.264, MP3) running on low-power SoCs. - **Scientific Computing:** Early Multiflow/TRACE machines pioneered compile-time ILP for Fortran loops. - **Server / HPC:** Intel Itanium (EPIC, a VLIW descendant) attempted but failed commercially due to compiler complexity and code-morphing burden. - **Modern AI Accelerators:** GPUs and TPUs borrow the VLIW philosophy (wide SIMD, deterministic issue) at the warp/instruction level. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example Setup — A Classic KTU Problem:** > A VLIW processor has **4 functional units**: 2 integer ALUs (I1, I2), 1 FP unit (F1), and 1 memory unit (M1). The clock cycle time is $T_{\text{clk}} = 1 \text{ ns}$. A loop of 100 iterations contains 8 independent operations per iteration. Compare VLIW vs scalar performance. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Peak Speedup of VLIW Let the program contain $N$ total operations, of which $p$ fraction is **parallelizable** across $n$ functional units. **Step 1 — Write Amdahl's Law:** $$ S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{n}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $p = 1$ (fully parallelizable, ideal case):** $$ S_{\text{Amdahl}}(p=1) = \frac{1}{(1 - 1) + \dfrac{1}{n}} = \frac{1}{1/n} = n $$ **Step 3 — Conclude:** $$ \boxed{\,S_{\text{VLIW}}^{\text{ideal}} = n\,} $$ For our 4-unit machine: $S_{\text{ideal}} = 4$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Realistic VLIW Speedup with Limited Parallelism **Step 1 — Set $p = 0.75$ (only 75% parallelizable), $n = 4$:** $$ S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.75) + \dfrac{0.75}{4}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute denominator term-by-term:** $$ 1 - 0.75 = 0.25 $$ $$ \frac{0.75}{4} = 0.1875 $$ **Step 3 — Sum and invert:** $$ 0.25 + 0.1875 = 0.4375 $$ $$ S = \frac{1}{0.4375} = 2.2857 $$ **Step 4 — Compare to ideal $S = 4$:** $$ \text{Efficiency} = \frac{2.2857}{4} = 0.571 = 57.14\% $$ $$ \boxed{\,S_{\text{VLIW}}^{\text{real}} \approx 2.29 \text{ (vs ideal 4)}\,} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Loop Iteration Speedup on the Sample Machine **Given:** 100 iterations × 8 ops/iter = **800 operations** total. Each operation takes 1 cycle on the scalar; on the VLIW, up to 4 ops issue per cycle. **Step 1 — Scalar execution time:** $$ T_{\text{scalar}} = 800 \text{ cycles} \times 1 \text{ ns} = 800 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 2 — Best-case VLIW (8 ops/iter = 2 bundles of 4):** $$ \text{Bundles per iter} = \lceil 8 / 4 \rceil = 2 $$ $$ \text{Total bundles} = 2 \times 100 = 200 $$ $$ T_{\text{VLIW}} = 200 \text{ cycles} \times 1 \text{ ns} = 200 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 3 — Speedup:** $$ S = \frac{800}{200} = 4 $$ **Step 4 — Realistic (25% serial portion, only 6/8 ops parallel):** $$ \text{Parallel ops per iter} = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{bundles/iter} = 2 \text{ (one full + one with 2 ops)} $$ $$ \text{Effective bundles/iter} = 2 \quad (\text{still 2 cycles, but with 2 idle slots}) $$ $$ T_{\text{VLIW}}^{\text{real}} = 200 \text{ ns (still), but utilization} = \frac{6}{8} = 75\% $$ $$ \boxed{\,S_{\text{real}} = 4 \times 0.75 = 3\,} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Code-Size Blowup Estimation **Given:** Scalar code: 800 32-bit instructions. VLIW: 200 bundles, each 128 bits. **Step 1 — Scalar size:** $$ \text{Size}_{\text{scalar}} = 800 \times 32 \text{ bits} = 25600 \text{ bits} = 3200 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 2 — VLIW size:** $$ \text{Size}_{\text{VLIW}} = 200 \times 128 \text{ bits} = 25600 \text{ bits} = 3200 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3 — For this ideal case, CSB = 1.0. Now realistic case with NOP padding for slot-filling:** $$ \text{Assume 30\% of slots are NOPs (3 NOPs per 10-op bundle).} $$ $$ \text{Effective ops} = 800, \quad \text{total slots} = 200 \times 4 = 800 $$ $$ \text{NOP fraction} = 0.30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NOP slots} = 240, \quad \text{real op slots} = 800 $$ $$ \text{Actual bundles needed} = \frac{800}{4} \times 1.30 \approx 260 \text{ bundles} $$ $$ \text{Size}_{\text{VLIW}} = 260 \times 128 = 33280 \text{ bits} = 4160 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 4 — Code-size blowup:** $$ \text{CSB} = \frac{4160}{3200} = 1.30 $$ $$ \boxed{\,\text{CSB} = 1.30 \times \text{(30% NOP padding)}\,} $$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose VLIW | Application Class | VLIW Suitability | Reason | |---|---|---| | DSP (filters, FFTs) | **Excellent** | Tight loops, predictable parallelism | | Multimedia codecs | **Excellent** | Stream processing, data-parallel kernels | | General-purpose OS | **Poor** | Irregular branches, dynamic memory access | | Scientific HPC (Fortran loops) | **Good** | Trace scheduling works well | | Branch-heavy control code | **Poor** | Predication overhead, large bundles wasted | | Real-time embedded control | **Good** | Determinism, low power | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level VLIW Architecture ```mermaid graph LR IMem["WIDE INSTRUCTION MEMORY
128-512 bit words"] FDU["FETCH AND DECODE UNIT
Splits long word into ops"] Tmpl["TEMPLATE DECODER
Maps slots to units"] RegFile["LARGE MULTIPORTED
