REGISTER FILE"] Pred["PREDICATE REGISTER FILE"] ALU1["INTEGER ALU 1"] ALU2["INTEGER ALU 2"] FPU1["FLOATING POINT UNIT 1"] MUL["MULTIPLY UNIT"] LSU["LOAD STORE UNIT"] BRU["BRANCH UNIT"] DCache["DATA CACHE / MEMORY"] IMem --> FDU FDU --> Tmpl Tmpl --> ALU1 Tmpl --> ALU2 Tmpl --> FPU1 Tmpl --> MUL Tmpl --> LSU Tmpl --> BRU RegFile --> ALU1 RegFile --> ALU2 RegFile --> FPU1 RegFile --> MUL RegFile --> LSU RegFile --> BRU ALU1 --> RegFile ALU2 --> RegFile FPU1 --> RegFile MUL --> RegFile LSU --> DCache Pred --> ALU1 Pred --> ALU2 Pred --> FPU1 Pred --> MUL Pred --> BRU ``` --- ## 4.2 VLIW Instruction Bundle Format ```mermaid graph TB Bundle["VLIW INSTRUCTION BUNDLE
Total Width = 128 to 512 bits"] T["TEMPLATE FIELD
4 to 8 bits
Specifies slot mapping"] S1["SLOT 1
Integer Op
~32 bits"] S2["SLOT 2
Integer Op
~32 bits"] S3["SLOT 3
FP / MUL Op
~32 bits"] S4["SLOT 4
Load Store Op
~32 bits"] S5["SLOT 5
Branch Op
~32 bits"] Bundle --> T Bundle --> S1 Bundle --> S2 Bundle --> S3 Bundle --> S4 Bundle --> S5 ``` --- ## 4.3 VLIW vs Superscalar — Compiler vs Hardware Scheduling ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SuperscalarFlow["SUPERSCALAR FLOW"] S1["Fetch
32-64 bit instr"] S2["Decode"] S3["Rename and Dispatch
HARDWARE checks RAW, WAR, WAW"] S4["Issue from RS
Out of order allowed"] S5["Execute on multiple units"] S6["Commit In Order"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 end subgraph VLIWFlow["VLIW FLOW"] V1["Compiler builds DDG
Detects ALL dependencies"] V2["List or Trace Scheduling
Packs ops into bundles"] V3["Emit 128-512 bit bundles"] V4["Fetch wide bundle"] V5["Split into ops
NO dependency check"] V6["Route to units
ALL in parallel"] V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4 --> V5 --> V6 end ``` --- ## 4.4 VLIW Pipeline Timing — Bundle Execution ```mermaid graph LR C1["CYCLE 1
Bundle A: op1, op2, op3, op4"] C2["CYCLE 2
Bundle B: op5, op6, op7, op8"] C3["CYCLE 3
Bundle C: op9, op10, op11, NOP"] C4["CYCLE 4
Bundle D: op12, op13, op14, op15"] C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Each cycle, **up to 4 operations** issue in lockstep. The **NOP** in cycle 3 represents an unfilled slot due to dependency constraints — illustrating the *NOP-padding / code-size blowup* issue. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define VLIW architecture. List any two advantages of VLIW over superscalar.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word) is a processor architecture in which the compiler packs multiple independent operations into a single long instruction word, and the hardware executes them in parallel on multiple functional units without any dynamic scheduling. **Advantages (any two):** 1. **Simpler hardware** — No out-of-order issue logic, reservation stations, or reorder buffer; hence lower power and chip area. 2. **Higher clock rate potential** — Removal of complex dispatch hardware reduces critical path delay. 3. **Deterministic execution** — Easier to bound worst-case timing (useful for real-time systems). 4. **Compiler can use global program view** — enabling aggressive trace/superblock scheduling. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023] **What is a "bundle" in VLIW? Explain the role of the template field.** **Model Answer:** A **bundle** is the fixed-size long instruction word in a VLIW processor, containing multiple operation slots (typically 4–8) and a small **template field**. **Role of template:** - The template is a small bit-field (e.g., 4 bits in TI C6x) that specifies **which slots execute in the same cycle** and **to which functional unit class** each slot is bound. - It allows a single bundle to span **multiple cycles**, with some slots executing in cycle $k$ and the rest in cycle $k+1$, conserving I-cache bandwidth. - For example, template `0000` may mean all slots are in one cycle, while `0010` may split them across two cycles. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > Common mistakes students make on VLIW questions: > 1. **Confusing VLIW with superscalar** — Always emphasize that scheduling is **static (compiler)** vs **dynamic (hardware)**. > 2. **Forgetting the template field** in bundle diagrams — KTU examiners specifically look for this in 7+ mark questions. > 3. **Assuming $S = n$ without qualification** — Always cite Amdahl's Law and state the conditions. > 4. **Ignoring code-size blowup** — A trade-off unique to VLIW worth 2–3 marks in descriptive answers. > 5. **Not labeling functional units** in block diagrams — clearly mark ALU, FPU, LSU, BRU. --- ### **Q3 (A).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **(14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the architectural design of a typical VLIW processor with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the compiler in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the process of **list scheduling** as performed by the VLIW compiler to pack operations into bundles. Show how data dependencies constrain packing. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) VLIW Architectural Design — 7 Marks** A typical VLIW processor (refer to Section 4.1 diagram) consists of: 1. **Wide Instruction Memory / I-Cache** — Stores bundles of 128–512 bits. 2. **Fetch & Decode Unit** — Reads one bundle per cycle, splits it into its sub-operations. 3. **Template Decoder** — Interprets the template field to determine cycle mapping and slot-to-unit assignment. 4. **Multiple Functional Units** (parallel): - 2 × Integer ALU - 1 × Floating-Point Unit - 1 × Multiply Unit - 1 × Load/Store Unit - 1 × Branch Unit 5. **Large Multiported Register File** — Provides simultaneous read/write access for all units. 6. **Predicate Register File** — Supports conditional execution (predicated instructions). **Role of the Compiler (Detailed):** - **Step 1 — Analysis:** Build Data Dependence Graph (DDG); identify RAW, WAR, WAW dependencies. - **Step 2 — Scheduling:** Apply list scheduling / trace scheduling / software pipelining to pack independent ops into bundles. - **Step 3 — Register Allocation:** Use graph coloring to minimize register pressure; spill to memory if needed. - **Step 4 — Predication:** Convert short branches to predicate-defining instructions to keep the bundle width full. - **Step 5 — Code Emission:** Output wide VLIW bundles, padding with NOPs where parallelism is unavailable. The compiler is the **"brain"** of the VLIW system; the hardware is deliberately a "dumb" executor. --- **(b) List Scheduling Example — 7 Marks** Consider the following sequence of operations with their data dependencies: | Op | Latency (cycles) | Depends On | |---|---|---| | I1: `a = load M[0]` | 3 | — | | I2: `b = load M[1]` | 3 | — | | I3: `c = a + b` | 1 | I1, I2 | | I4: `d = a * 2` | 2 | I1 | | I5: `e = c + d` | 1 | I3, I4 | | I6: `store M[2] = e` | 3 | I5 | | I7: `store M[3] = d` | 3 | I4 | **Step 1 — Initialize:** Ready set = {I1, I2} (no dependencies). **Step 2 — Cycle 1 (Bundle 1):** Schedule I1 → ALU/LSU, I2 → LSU. - (Two slots filled; remaining slots can hold NOPs or be filled with independent operations from outer scope.) **Step 3 — Cycle 2 (Bundle 2):** I1 and I2 still in memory pipeline (latency 3). I3 not yet ready (waits for I1 and I2 to complete). Schedule **NOP** or unrelated ops. **Step 4 — Cycle 3 (Bundle 3):** I1 completes at end of cycle 3, I2 completes at end of cycle 3. I3, I4 now ready. - Schedule I3 → ALU, I4 → MUL unit (2 slots filled). **Step 5 — Cycle 4 (Bundle 4):** I3 and I4 completing. I5 now ready. - Schedule I5 → ALU. **Step 6 — Cycle 5 (Bundle 5):** I5 completes. I6, I7 ready. - Schedule I6 → LSU, I7 → LSU (2 slots filled). **Resulting Bundle Trace (4 functional units available, of which I1/I2 and I6/I7 use LSU):** | Cycle | Slot 1 (ALU) | Slot 2 (ALU) | Slot 3 (MUL) | Slot 4 (LSU) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | NOP | NOP | NOP | I1, I2 | | 2 | NOP | NOP | NOP | NOP | | 3 | I3 | NOP | I4 | NOP | | 4 | I5 | NOP | NOP | NOP | | 5 | NOP | NOP | NOP | I6, I7 | **Observation:** The bundle cannot be fully filled due to the **serial dependency chain** through I1 → I3 → I5 → I6. This illustrates the **fundamental limitation of ILP** and explains why real speedup $\ll n$. --- ### **Q3 (B) — Internal Choice Alternative.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(14 Marks)** **(a)** Compare VLIW and superscalar architectures in terms of hardware complexity, power consumption, and compiler responsibility. Use a comparative table. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Apply **Amdahl's Law** to a VLIW processor with **8 functional units**. The program has: - 80% of operations parallelizable, - 20% strictly serial. Compute the speedup. What speedup would be achieved if 95% were parallelizable? Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Comparison Table — 7 Marks** | Parameter | VLIW | Superscalar | |---|---|---| | **Scheduling** | Static (compiler) | Dynamic (hardware) | | **Hardware Complexity** | Low (no issue logic) | Very High (ROB, RS, rename) | | **Power Consumption** | Low | High (dynamic checks) | | **Clock Frequency** | Potentially high | Limited by critical path of scheduler | | **Compiler Responsibility** | Central — does all parallelism work | Minor — only basic optimization | | **Code Size** | Larger (NOP padding) | Normal | | **Binary Compatibility** | Poor across implementations | Good (ISA stable) | | **Branch Handling** | Predication | Dynamic prediction | | **Real-Time Suitability** | High (deterministic) | Low (variable latency) | | **Worst-Case Execution** | Bounded by compiler analysis | Unbounded (cache, branch) | | **Examples** | TI C6x, Intel Itanium | Intel Core, AMD Ryzen | **Concluding Remark:** VLIW trades hardware complexity for compiler complexity, gaining power and clock-rate advantages at the cost of code size and compatibility. --- **(b) Amdahl's Law Computation — 7 Marks** **Case 1: $p = 0.80$, $n = 8$** **Step 1 — Write formula:** $$ S = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{n}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$ S_1 = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{8}} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate denominator:** $$ 1 - 0.80 = 0.20 $$ $$ \frac{0.80}{8} = 0.10 $$ $$ 0.20 + 0.10 = 0.30 $$ **Step 4 — Invert:** $$ \boxed{\,S_1 = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.33\,} $$ --- **Case 2: $p = 0.95$, $n = 8$** **Step 1 — Substitute:** $$ S_2 = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \dfrac{0.95}{8}} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate:** $$ 1 - 0.95 = 0.05 $$ $$ \frac{0.95}{8} = 0.11875 $$ $$ 0.05 + 0.11875 = 0.16875 $$ **Step 3 — Invert:** $$ \boxed{\,S_2 = \frac{1}{0.16875} = 5.93\,} $$ --- **Commentary:** | Metric | $p = 0.80$ | $p = 0.95$ | Ideal $p = 1$ | |---|---|---|---| | Speedup $S$ | 3.33 | 5.93 | 8.00 | | Efficiency $\eta = S/n$ | 41.6% | 74.1% | 100% | **Inference:** 1. The **serial 20%** in case 1 caps speedup at 3.33, far below the ideal 8. 2. Even with $p = 0.95$, we achieve only $S = 5.93$ — a 5% serial portion costs us ~26% of potential speedup. 3. **This is the core lesson of Amdahl's Law:** the **serial portion dominates** the achievable performance, regardless of how many parallel units we add. To use VLIW effectively, **the program must be highly parallelizable**, and the compiler must minimize the unavoidable serial regions (e.g., loop-carried dependencies, memory latency). --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — VLIW** - ⭐ **Definition:** VLIW = Very Long Instruction Word architecture; compiler packs multiple ops into one wide word. - ⭐ **Compiler does the work:** Static scheduling, list scheduling, trace scheduling, software pipelining. - ⭐ **Hardware is simple:** No dependency checks, no out-of-order logic, no reservation stations. - ⭐ **Bundle structure:** `[Template | Slot1 | Slot2 | ... | SlotN]`, width typically 128–512 bits. - ⭐ **Template field:** Maps slots to functional units and specifies cycle boundary. - ⭐ **Predication:** Used instead of branches to fill bundles with conditional ops. - ⭐ **Key formulas to memorize:** - $S_{\text{ideal}} = n$ - $S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + p/n}$ - $\text{CPI}_{\text{VLIW}} = 1 / \text{ops per bundle}$ - ⭐ **Trade-offs:** - ✅ Lower power, higher clock, deterministic timing - ❌ Larger code (NOPs), weaker binary compatibility, requires aggressive compiler - ⭐ **Real-world examples:** Intel Itanium (IA-64/EPIC), TI TMS320C6x DSP, Transmeta Crusoe, Multiflow TRACE. - ⭐ **Comparison mantra for exams:** *VLIW = smart compiler, dumb hardware; Superscalar = smart hardware, simple compiler.* - ⭐ **Pitfall to avoid:** Never state $S = n$ without Amdahl's Law qualification. - ⭐ **Block diagram must include:** Wide I-cache, fetch/decode, template decoder, parallel functional units (ALU/FPU/MUL/LSU/BRU), multiported register file, predicate register file. - ⭐ **Modern relevance:** VLIW philosophy underlies modern **SIMD, Vectors, EPIC, and AI accelerators** (GPU warps, TPU instructions). ---

topics.title: Data Level Parallelism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Level Parallelism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Level Parallelism (DLP)** is a form of parallel computing in which a single instruction (or a single thread of control) is applied concurrently to **multiple distinct data elements**, exploiting the property that many computational problems exhibit the same operation being performed on a large set of data.

In the **Flynn's Taxonomy** framework adopted by the KTU 2024 syllabus, Data Level Parallelism is the operational foundation of two architectural classes:

| Flynn Class | Full Form | Parallelism Type |
|-------------|-----------|------------------|
| **SIMD** | Single Instruction, Multiple Data | Pure DLP |
| **MISD** | Multiple Instruction, Single Data | Rare (fault-tolerant) |

A processor is said to exploit **Data Level Parallelism** when it issues **one instruction** that simultaneously operates on **a vector of data items** stored in wide registers, rather than looping scalar-style through individual operands. The two principal hardware realizations studied in Module 3 are:

1. **Vector Processors** (deep pipelines, vector functional units, memory-memory / register-register variants).
2. **SIMD Extensions** in modern general-purpose CPUs (SSE, AVX, NEON, SVE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Washing Machine Factory"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a laundry company receiving **1,000 identical white shirts** that all need the same three operations: *wash → rinse → iron*.

- **Scalar Worker (SISD):** One worker picks up shirt #1, washes it, rinses it, irons it, sets it aside, then moves to shirt #2. Slow because the worker spends time on **overhead** (fetching each shirt).
- **Data Level Parallel (SIMD/Vector):** The worker grabs **64 shirts at once** (a "vector load"), places them all into **64 parallel wash basins** simultaneously, rinses them in one cycle, and irons them in one cycle. The instruction (wash) is the **same** — the **data** is **multiple**.

**Key insight from the analogy:** DLP saves time by **amortizing instruction-issue overhead across many data items per cycle**, instead of re-issuing the same instruction N times for N data items.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Bounds

The following are the **canonical DLP performance metrics** defined in the KTU 2024 PECST528 syllabus:

- **$R_\infty$** — Maximum theoretical performance (upper bound) measured in **FLOPs/cycle** or as a **speedup limit** assuming pipeline overhead vanishes. Expressed in *steady-state* vector performance.
- **$N_v$** — **Vector Length** of a single vector instruction (number of elements processed per vector instruction).
- **$N_{1/2}$** — The **half-performance vector length** — the vector length at which the vector processor achieves **half of $R_\infty$**. It quantifies the **start-up overhead** of the pipeline. Units: **elements**.
- **$T_v$** — Total time for one vector instruction, including **start-up latency** $T_{\text{start}}$ and **per-element cost** $T_{\text{elem}}$.
- **$\text{Chime}$** — The unit of time equal to one iteration of a vectorizable loop on a single vector functional unit (≈ one vector instruction issue cycle under ideal conditions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU board examinations, the relationship $T_v = T_{\text{start}} + (N_v - 1) \times T_{\text{elem}}$ is **mandatory** memorization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vector Instruction Model

A vector processor issues an instruction of the form:

$$
\text{VOP}\ \ \vec{V}_1 \leftarrow \vec{V}_2 \odot \vec{V}_3
$$

where $\vec{V}_i$ are **vector registers** holding $N_v$ elements each, and $\odot$ is a scalar arithmetic operator applied **element-wise**. The architectural steps are:

1. **Vector Load** — Fetch $N_v$ consecutive (or strided) elements from memory into a vector register.
2. **Vector Operate** — Issue arithmetic on vector functional units.
3. **Vector Store** — Write results back to memory.

> [!NOTE]
> This three-stage decomposition is the reason DLP achieves high throughput: **memory bandwidth, compute throughput, and register bandwidth** all scale with $N_v$.

## 2.2 Pipelined Vector Functional Units

A vector functional unit is **pipelined** with a fixed **initiation interval** (II) — typically **1 cycle** for fully pipelined units. The unit produces **one result per cycle** after an initial **start-up latency** $T_{\text{start}}$ (e.g., 6 cycles for a floating-point multiply pipeline).

- **Throughput per unit** = $1/\text{II}$ results per cycle in steady state.
- **Total elements per cycle (theoretical peak)** = $\text{lanes} \times 1/\text{II}$ where **lanes** is the number of parallel functional-unit pipes (called **lanes** in vector terminology).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value in tables to avoid breaking markdown.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $T_v = T_{\text{start}} + (N_v - 1) \cdot T_{\text{elem}}$ | Time for one vector instruction | cycles |
| 2 | $R_\infty = \lim_{N_v \to \infty} \dfrac{N_v}{T_v} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{elem}}}$ | Asymptotic performance (no overhead) | elements/cycle |
| 3 | $N_{1/2} = T_{\text{start}} \cdot \dfrac{R_\infty}{1} + 1$ | Vector length for $0.5 \cdot R_\infty$ | elements |
| 4 | $R(N_v) = \dfrac{N_v}{T_{\text{start}} + (N_v-1) T_{\text{elem}}}$ | Performance for finite $N_v$ | elements/cycle |
| 5 | $S = \dfrac{T_{\text{scalar}}}{T_{\text{vector}}} = \dfrac{N_v \cdot T_{\text{elem}}}{T_{\text{start}} + (N_v-1)T_{\text{elem}}}$ | Speedup of vector over scalar | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Efficiency} = \dfrac{R(N_v)}{R_\infty} = \dfrac{N_v}{N_v + (N_{1/2} - 1)}$ | Fraction of peak achieved | $[0,1]$ |
| 7 | $\text{Chime} = T_{\text{start}} + (N_v - 1) T_{\text{elem}}$ | One iteration of a vector loop | cycles |
| 8 | $N_{v,\text{conv}} = \left\lvert \dfrac{n + VL - 1}{VL} \right\rvert$ | Strip-mined vector length, $n$ = total elements, $VL$ = max | elements |
| 9 | $f_{\text{conv}} = 1 - \dfrac{(N_v \bmod VL)}{n}$ | Fraction of vector length used in strip-mine | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Speedup}_{\text{convoy}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{convoy}} \cdot T_{\text{chime}}}{\max_{k} T_{\text{start},k} + (N_v-1) \cdot T_{\text{elem}}}$ | Speedup under convoy limitation | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DLP underpins:

- **Scientific computing:** Weather simulation, molecular dynamics, CFD — operations on regular grids.
- **Graphics & ML:** GPU SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) extends classical SIMD with scalar control per lane.
- **DSP:** Audio/video codecs use **packed arithmetic** (16-bit lanes inside a 64-bit register).
- **Database acceleration:** Vectorized scan operators exploit **predication** to skip elements meeting a condition.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU valuations, students are expected to **identify convoy stalls, structural hazards, and memory bandwidth bottlenecks** as the three primary reasons actual performance falls below $R_\infty$.

## 2.5 Architectural Variants of DLP

| Variant | Description | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Memory-Memory Vector** | Operands read directly from memory | Original Cray-1 style |
| **Register-Register Vector** | Operands must be loaded into vector registers first | Cray-1, NEC SX-Aurora |
| **SIMD ISA Extension** | Wide 128/256/512-bit registers partitioned into lanes | x86 AVX-512, ARM SVE |
| **SIMT (GPU)** | Scalar thread ID + vector-style execution | NVIDIA CUDA, AMD CDNA |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From $T_v$ to $R_\infty$ and $N_{1/2}$

We start from the canonical time equation for a single vector instruction:

$$
T_v = T_{\text{start}} + (N_v - 1) \cdot T_{\text{elem}}
$$

Here $T_{\text{start}}$ is the **pipeline start-up latency** (in cycles) and $T_{\text{elem}}$ is the **per-element time** after steady state is reached.

**Step 1 — Define the achieved throughput (performance in elements/cycle):**

$$
R(N_v) = \dfrac{N_v}{T_v} = \dfrac{N_v}{T_{\text{start}} + (N_v - 1) \cdot T_{\text{elem}}}
$$

The reasoning: we processed $N_v$ elements in $T_v$ cycles, so dividing elements by cycles gives throughput.

**Step 2 — Take the limit as $N_v \to \infty$ (asymptotic case, all overheads vanish):**

$$
R_\infty = \lim_{N_v \to \infty} \dfrac{N_v}{T_{\text{start}} + (N_v - 1) \cdot T_{\text{elem}}}
$$

For very large $N_v$, the term $(N_v - 1) \cdot T_{\text{elem}}$ dominates the constant $T_{\text{start}}$. Substituting $N_v - 1 \approx N_v$:

$$
R_\infty = \lim_{N_v \to \infty} \dfrac{N_v}{N_v \cdot T_{\text{elem}}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{elem}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive the half-performance vector length $N_{1/2}$.**

Set $R(N_v) = 0.5 \cdot R_\infty$ and solve for $N_v$:

$$
\dfrac{N_v}{T_{\text{start}} + (N_v - 1) \cdot T_{\text{elem}}} = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{elem}}}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
2 \cdot N_v \cdot T_{\text{elem}} = T_{\text{start}} + (N_v - 1) \cdot T_{\text{elem}}
$$

Expand the right-hand side:

$$
2 N_v T_{\text{elem}} = T_{\text{start}} + N_v T_{\text{elem}} - T_{\text{elem}}
$$

Bring $N_v T_{\text{elem}}$ to the left:

$$
N_v T_{\text{elem}} = T_{\text{start}} - T_{\text{elem}}
$$

Solve for $N_v$:

$$
N_v = \dfrac{T_{\text{start}} - T_{\text{elem}}}{T_{\text{elem}}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{start}}}{T_{\text{elem}}} - 1
$$

**Conclusion (the KTU board-exam canonical form):**

$$
N_{1/2} = \dfrac{T_{\text{start}}}{T_{\text{elem}}} - 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU valuations, some textbooks add the $``+1"$ back via the $(N_v-1)$ approximation. The cleaner practical formula written on the exam answer is $N_{1/2} \approx \dfrac{T_{\text{start}}}{T_{\text{elem}}}$. **Always state your assumption** about whether you used $N_v$ or $N_v - 1$ in the limit.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Strip-Mining and Vector Length Reduction

Suppose a problem has $n = 1000$ elements but the hardware maximum vector length is $VL = 64$ (called $MVL$ in the original Hennessy-Patterson notation). We must execute the loop in **chunks of 64**.

**Step 1 — Determine the number of full vector iterations:**

$$
N_{\text{full}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{n}{VL} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1000}{64} \right\rfloor = 15
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the leftover (remainder) elements:**

$$
n_{\text{leftover}} = n - N_{\text{full}} \cdot VL = 1000 - 15 \cdot 64 = 1000 - 960 = 40
$$

**Step 3 — Total chime count for the whole loop:**

$$
\text{Total chimes} = N_{\text{full}} + 1 = 16 \text{ (15 full + 1 partial)}
$$

The final partial chime processes only 40 elements. The **efficiency loss** is:

$$
f_{\text{loss}} = \dfrac{VL - n_{\text{leftover}}}{n} = \dfrac{64 - 40}{1000} = 0.024 = 2.4\%
$$

**Step 4 — Convey the result in speedup form:**

$$
S_{\text{vector}} = \dfrac{n \cdot T_{\text{scalar,elem}}}{N_{\text{full}} \cdot (T_{\text{start}} + (VL-1) T_{\text{elem}}) + (T_{\text{start}} + (n_{\text{leftover}}-1) T_{\text{elem}})}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Memory Interleaving and Bank Conflicts

A vector memory system with $M$ banks serves $N_v$ sequential elements. With **low-order interleaving**, element $i$ is placed in bank $(i \bmod M)$.

**Step 1 — Time to read $N_v$ elements with $M$ banks and one element per bank per cycle:**

For $N_v \le M$: $T_{\text{mem}} = T_{\text{start,m}} + (N_v - 1) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ (one element per cycle after start-up).

**Step 2 — Stride conflicts.** If the access stride $s$ and number of banks $M$ share a common factor $\gcd(s, M) > 1$, multiple consecutive elements fall in the same bank, **serializing** the access.

The **bank busy time** for a stride-$s$ access is:

$$
T_{\text{bank}} = \dfrac{N_v}{M / \gcd(s, M)}
$$

For $M = 8$ banks, $s = 2$, $\gcd(2, 8) = 2$, only $8/2 = 4$ banks are active each cycle — throughput is halved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 3, Section "Memory Interleaving", requires you to compute the number of memory banks needed to avoid stalls. The rule is $M \ge s$ where $s$ is the maximum stride. Always choose **prime or large-power-of-2** bank counts in design problems.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of DLP Realizations

| Parameter | Vector Processor (Cray-1) | SIMD (AVX-512) | SIMT (GPU) |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------|------------|
| Vector length | 64 (configurable, 64×64-bit) | 64 (512 bits ÷ 8 bytes) | 32 threads / warp |
| Issue width | 1 vector instruction | 1 packed instruction | 1 scalar inst → 32 lanes |
| Register file | $8 \times 64$-element vectors | $32 \times 512$-bit regs | 65,536 × 32-bit regs |
| Predication | Vector mask register | k-mask registers (k1–k7) | Per-lane predicate |
| Memory model | Strided / scatter-gather | Aligned contiguous | Coalesced loads |
| Typical $N_{1/2}$ | 10–30 | 4–8 | 1 (lanes already in flight) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pipelined Vector Functional Unit with Multiple Lanes

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram models the data flow inside a typical **vector functional unit (VFU)** with **4 parallel lanes** and a 5-stage pipeline. Each lane independently executes the operation on a 64-bit chunk of the wide vector register.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["VECTOR INSTR ISSUE STAGE 1: FETCH AND DECODE"] --> B["STAGE 2 OPERAND FETCH FROM VRF"]
    B --> C["STAGE 3 DISPATCH TO LANES"]
    C --> D1["LANE 0 PIPELINE STAGE 4 EXECUTE"]
    C --> D2["LANE 1 PIPELINE STAGE 4 EXECUTE"]
    C --> D3["LANE 2 PIPELINE STAGE 4 EXECUTE"]
    C --> D4["LANE 3 PIPELINE STAGE 4 EXECUTE"]
    D1 --> E1["STAGE 5 LANE 0 WRITE BACK"]
    D2 --> E2["STAGE 5 LANE 1 WRITE BACK"]
    D3 --> E3["STAGE 5 LANE 2 WRITE BACK"]
    D4 --> E4["STAGE 5 LANE 3 WRITE BACK"]
    E1 --> F["RESULT BYPASS NETWORK TO NEXT VECTOR OP"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G["VECTOR REGISTER FILE WRITE PORT 8 ELEMENTS PER CYCLE"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Vector Load-Store Unit with Interleaved Memory Banks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VRF["VECTOR REGISTER FILE 8 BANKS 64 ELEMENTS"]
        V0["V0 BANK 0 ELEMENTS 0 8 16 24 32 40 48 56"]
        V1["V1 BANK 1 ELEMENTS 1 9 17 25 33 41 49 57"]
        V2["V2 BANK 2 ELEMENTS 2 10 18 26 34 42 50 58"]
        V3["V3 BANK 3 ELEMENTS 3 11 19 27 35 43 51 59"]
        V4["V4 BANK 4 ELEMENTS 4 12 20 28 36 44 52 60"]
        V5["V5 BANK 5 ELEMENTS 5 13 21 29 37 45 53 61"]
        V6["V6 BANK 6 ELEMENTS 6 14 22 30 38 46 54 62"]
        V7["V7 BANK 7 ELEMENTS 7 15 23 31 39 47 55 63"]
    end
    VRF --> LSU["VLSU VECTOR LOAD STORE UNIT CROSSBAR SWITCH"]
    LSU --> MBUS["MEMORY BUS 64 BYTES PER CYCLE"]
    MBUS --> DRAM["MAIN MEMORY 8 WAY INTERLEAVED BANKS"]
    DRAM --> MBUS
    MBUS --> LSU
    LSU --> VRF
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Memory Interleaving (Bank Conflict Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CYCLE1["CYCLE 1 REQUESTS IN FLIGHT"]
        R1["ADDR 0 BANK 0"]
        R2["ADDR 2 BANK 2"]
        R3["ADDR 4 BANK 4"]
        R4["ADDR 6 BANK 6"]
    end
    subgraph CYCLE2["CYCLE 2 REQUESTS IN FLIGHT"]
        S1["ADDR 8 BANK 0"]
        S2["ADDR 10 BANK 2"]
        S3["ADDR 12 BANK 4"]
        S4["ADDR 14 BANK 6"]
    end
    CYCLE1 -->|"STRIDE 2 ACCESS"| CYCLE2
    note1["ONLY 4 OUT OF 8 BANKS ACTIVE EACH CYCLE BECAUSE STRIDE 2 AND BANK COUNT 8 SHARE GCD 2"] -.-> CYCLE1
    note2["RESOLUTION ADD ONE EXTRA BANK OR USE PRIME BANK COUNT TO AVOID CONFLICT"] -.-> CYCLE2
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Strip-Mining Loop Transformation (Vector Length Reduction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ORIGINAL SCALAR LOOP FOR I 0 TO N MINUS 1"] --> B["CHECK N LESS THAN OR EQUAL MVL"]
    B -->|"YES VECTORIZE DIRECTLY"| C["SET VL EQUAL TO N"]
    B -->|"NO STRIP MINE"| D["SET VL EQUAL TO MVL 64"]
    D --> E["OUTER LOOP ITERATIONS N DIVIDED BY VL"]
    E --> F["INNER VECTOR LOOP PROCESSES VL ELEMENTS"]
    F --> G["DECREASE REMAINING COUNT"]
    G -->|"REMAIND GT VL"| F
    G -->|"REMAIND LT VL"| H["FINAL CHIME PROCESSES LEFTOVER ELEMENTS WITH VECTOR MASK"]
    H --> I["LOOP TERMINATION"]
    C --> F
```

## 4.5 Diagram — Predicated SIMD Operation (Vector Masking)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["VECTOR REGISTER VR2 8 16 BIT LANES"] --> B["COMPARISON UNIT CMP VR2 ZERO"]
    C["VECTOR MASK REGISTER VM"] --> B
    B --> D["VM BITMAP 1 WHERE VR2 GT 0 ELSE 0"]
    D --> E["PREDICATED ADD VR1 EQUALS VR1 PLUS VR3 UNDER VM"]
    A --> E
    F["VR3 CONSTANT 1 IN EVERY LANE"] --> E
    E --> G["VR1 UPDATED ONLY IN LANES WHERE VM IS 1"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Data Level Parallelism (DLP). With a suitable example, distinguish between SIMD and MIMD classes of parallelism.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **DLP Definition (1 mark):** Data Level Parallelism is a parallelism paradigm in which a **single instruction stream** operates concurrently on **multiple data elements** (vectors, arrays, matrices), exploiting the regularity of operations across data.

> **SIMD vs MIMD (2 marks):**
> - **SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data):** One instruction is broadcast to many processing elements, each operating on its own data item simultaneously. Example: AVX-512 fused multiply-add on 16 single-precision floats.
> - **MIMD (Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data):** Each processor independently fetches and executes its own instruction stream on its own data. Example: multi-core CPU running independent threads.
> - **Key distinction:** SIMD exploits **regularity of operations**, MIMD exploits **regularity of task structure**.

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is meant by vector length register? Why is strip-mining necessary in vector processors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Vector Length Register (VLR) (1.5 marks):** The VLR is a special control register that specifies the **number of elements** to be processed by the current vector instruction. It allows the programmer (or compiler) to execute a vector loop on fewer than the maximum hardware-supported elements (e.g., when handling a leftover partial vector at the tail of a loop).

> **Need for Strip-Mining (1.5 marks):** When the **problem size $n$ exceeds the maximum vector length (MVL)** of the hardware, the loop must be decomposed into an outer loop that processes $MVL$ elements at a time and an inner vector loop. This transformation is called **strip-mining** (or **vector length reduction**). It ensures all vector instructions operate on hardware-supported vector lengths and prevents length-register overflow.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **omit units** in $R_\infty$ and $N_{1/2}$ problems — always write "elements/cycle" or "cycles" explicitly.
> - When asked for **speedup**, never forget the **scalar baseline cost** — many students compute $R(N_v)$ alone and call it speedup.
> - For **memory interleaving** sub-parts, you must show the **bank number** $b = i \bmod M$ computation for at least one row — valuation key explicitly demands it.
> - Strip-mining problems: **state MVL value, compute both full and partial chimes, then compute efficiency loss** — partial credit is given per step.

---

### Question B (Choice 1) — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a)** A vector processor has a **start-up latency of 12 cycles** for floating-point multiplication and a **steady-state per-element time of 1 cycle**. Compute the **maximum performance $R_\infty$**, the **half-performance vector length $N_{1/2}$**, and the **speedup** obtained when processing a vector of length **64** compared to a scalar processor that requires **3 cycles per element**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Write the time equation (1 mark):**
> Given: $T_{\text{start}} = 12$ cycles, $T_{\text{elem}} = 1$ cycle.
> 
> $$T_v = T_{\text{start}} + (N_v - 1) T_{\text{elem}}$$

> **Step 2 — Compute $R_\infty$ (2 marks):**
> 
> $$R_\infty = \lim_{N_v \to \infty} \dfrac{N_v}{T_{\text{start}} + (N_v-1) T_{\text{elem}}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{elem}}} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1\ \text{element/cycle}$$

> **Step 3 — Compute $N_{1/2}$ (2 marks):**
> 
> $$N_{1/2} = \dfrac{T_{\text{start}}}{T_{\text{elem}}} - 1 = \dfrac{12}{1} - 1 = 11\ \text{elements}$$
> 
> Interpretation: when the vector length is 11, the vector processor achieves **half of its peak throughput**.

> **Step 4 — Compute speedup for $N_v = 64$ (2 marks):**
> Scalar time: $T_{\text{scalar}} = 64 \times 3 = 192$ cycles.
> Vector time:
> 
> $$T_v = 12 + (64 - 1) \times 1 = 12 + 63 = 75\ \text{cycles}$$
> 
> $$S = \dfrac{T_{\text{scalar}}}{T_v} = \dfrac{192}{75} = 2.56$$

> **Final Answer:** $R_\infty = 1$ element/cycle, $N_{1/2} = 11$ elements, **Speedup $S = 2.56\times$**.

---

**Q (b)** Explain the concept of **memory interleaving** in a vector processor. A vector processor with **8 memory banks** needs to load a vector of **64 elements** with a **stride of 3**. Determine the number of **bank conflicts** per pass through the vector and the **effective bandwidth utilization** if the ideal (conflict-free) throughput is 8 elements/cycle. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Memory interleaving definition (2 marks):**
> Memory interleaving distributes consecutive (or strided) memory addresses across multiple banks so that **successive accesses hit different banks**, allowing parallel service. With low-order interleaving, address $a$ maps to bank $b = a \bmod M$.

> **Step 2 — Compute bank sequence for stride 3, 8 banks (2 marks):**
> Starting address: 0. Bank for element $i$: $b_i = (i \times 3) \bmod 8$.
> Sequence: $i=0 \to 0$, $i=1 \to 3$, $i=2 \to 6$, $i=3 \to 1$, $i=4 \to 4$, $i=5 \to 7$, $i=6 \to 2$, $i=7 \to 5$, then **repeats** at $i=8 \to 0$.

> **Step 3 — Identify conflict pattern (2 marks):**
> $\gcd(3, 8) = 1$, so the **stride is coprime to the bank count** and **all 8 banks are visited** in a single pass. Therefore there are **0 bank conflicts per pass**. The vector of 64 elements takes $\lceil 64/8 \rceil = 8$ cycles.

> **Step 4 — Compute effective bandwidth (1 mark):**
> 
> $$\text{Bandwidth} = \dfrac{64\ \text{elements}}{8\ \text{cycles}} = 8\ \text{elements/cycle}$$
> 
> $$\text{Utilization} = \dfrac{8}{8} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

> **Final Answer:** **Zero bank conflicts, 100% bandwidth utilization** because $\gcd(3,8) = 1$ (the stride is coprime to the number of banks).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a vector processor** and describe the role of the **vector length register (VLR)** and **vector mask register (VMR)**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Block diagram description (3 marks):**
> A vector processor consists of:
> - **Scalar unit** — handles integer/floating-point scalar instructions and branch control.
> - **Vector unit** — contains the **vector register file (VRF)** with $N$ vector registers (each holding $MVL$ elements), a set of **vector functional units** (integer, FP add, FP multiply, divide, logical), and a **vector load-store unit (VLSU)** connected to interleaved memory banks.
> - **Control registers** — VLR, VMR, vector interrupt register, etc.
> - **Cross-bar / bypass network** — routes results between functional units and the VRF.

> **Step 2 — Role of VLR (2 marks):**
> The **Vector Length Register** specifies the number of elements to be processed in the current vector instruction. It allows the processor to handle **leftover elements** at the end of a strip-mined loop without requiring special mask logic. The VLR is decremented by software after each partial-vector iteration.

> **Step 3 — Role of VMR (2 marks):**
> The **Vector Mask Register** enables **predicated execution**. Each bit of the VMR corresponds to one element of a vector register. A "1" enables the operation for that lane; a "0" disables it. This is used to handle:
> - **Conditional statements** (`if (A[i] > 0) B[i] = ...`)
> - **Sparse / irregular operations** (gather-scatter with predicate)
> - **Loop tails** without separate scalar code.

> **Final Answer:** VLR controls vector length, VMR controls per-lane enabling — both together provide **flexibility** for non-trivial vector programs beyond simple unit-stride dense loops.

---

**Q (b)** A vector processor executes a loop that processes a vector of **1000 single-precision elements** with a hardware **maximum vector length MVL = 64**. The **start-up latency is 6 cycles** and **per-element time is 1 cycle**. Compute:
   (i) the number of full vector instructions, (ii) the leftover elements, (iii) the total time, and (iv) the speedup over a scalar processor that takes 4 cycles per element. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Number of full vector instructions (1 mark):**
> 
> $$N_{\text{full}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{n}{MVL} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1000}{64} \right\rfloor = 15$$

> **Step 2 — Leftover elements (1 mark):**
> 
> $$n_{\text{leftover}} = 1000 - 15 \times 64 = 1000 - 960 = 40\ \text{elements}$$

> **Step 3 — Time for one full vector instruction (1 mark):**
> 
> $$T_{\text{full}} = T_{\text{start}} + (MVL - 1) T_{\text{elem}} = 6 + 63 \times 1 = 69\ \text{cycles}$$

> **Step 4 — Time for the partial (leftover) instruction (1 mark):**
> 
> $$T_{\text{partial}} = T_{\text{start}} + (n_{\text{leftover}} - 1) T_{\text{elem}} = 6 + 39 \times 1 = 45\ \text{cycles}$$

> **Step 5 — Total vector time (1 mark):**
> 
> $$T_{\text{vector}} = 15 \times 69 + 45 = 1035 + 45 = 1080\ \text{cycles}$$

> **Step 6 — Scalar baseline and speedup (2 marks):**
> 
> $$T_{\text{scalar}} = 1000 \times 4 = 4000\ \text{cycles}$$
> 
> $$S = \dfrac{T_{\text{scalar}}}{T_{\text{vector}}} = \dfrac{4000}{1080} \approx 3.70$$

> **Final Answer:** 15 full instructions, 40 leftover elements, total vector time = 1080 cycles, **Speedup $\approx 3.70\times$**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 3, Data Level Parallelism:**

- **DLP definition:** One instruction → many data items (Flynn's SIMD). Exploits **regularity** in data, not in control.
- **Two main realizations:** (1) **Vector processors** (Cray-1, NEC SX), (2) **SIMD extensions** (x86 SSE/AVX, ARM NEON/SVE), (3) **GPUs via SIMT**.
- **Key performance metrics:** $R_\infty$ (peak), $N_{1/2}$ (startup sensitivity), $N_v$ (vector length), **chime** (loop iteration time).
- **Canonical equation:** $T_v = T_{\text{start}} + (N_v - 1) T_{\text{elem}}$ — must be remembered verbatim.
- **$N_{1/2}$ formula:** $N_{1/2} \approx T_{\text{start}} / T_{\text{elem}}$ — small $N_{1/2}$ = good (low start-up penalty).
- **Memory interleaving rule:** bank count $M \ge$ maximum stride, prefer $M$ as a prime or power-of-2; bank conflict occurs when $\gcd(\text{stride}, M) > 1$.
- **Strip-mining:** outer loop iterates $\lceil n / MVL \rceil$ times; the last iteration uses **VLR** to process leftover.
- **Vector mask register (VMR):** enables predicated/conditional vector execution — no scalar fall-back needed.
- **Gather-scatter:** allows indexed vector loads/stores — needed for sparse / linked-data parallelism.
- **Speedup formula:** $S = T_{\text{scalar}} / T_{\text{vector}}$; efficiency $\eta = R(N_v) / R_\infty \le 1$.
- **Three reasons $R(N_v) < R_\infty$:** convoy stalls (instruction dependence), structural hazards (single VFU), memory bandwidth saturation.
- **Bank conflict example for KTU:** stride 2, $M = 8$ → $\gcd(2,8) = 2$ → throughput halved. Stride 3, $M = 8$ → $\gcd = 1$ → no conflict.
- **Modern DLP context:** GPU SIMT extends SIMD with per-lane **predicate**, **PC**, and **register file** — it is "SIMD executed as MIMD" for irregular code.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector Processors - How Do They Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vector Processors - How Do They Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vector Processor (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A **Vector Processor** is a class of SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) parallel computer architecture designed to perform mathematical operations on multiple data elements (vectors) simultaneously using a single instruction, exploiting **data-level parallelism (DLP)**. Unlike scalar processors that operate on one pair of operands per instruction, a vector processor applies a single instruction stream to an ordered set of data items stored in dedicated **vector registers**, achieving high throughput on array-oriented scientific and engineering workloads.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, vector processors are studied as an evolution of the SISD pipeline architecture, introducing **vector registers**, **vector functional units**, and a specialized **vector control unit** containing a **Vector Length Register (VL)** and a **Vector Mask Register (VM)**. The dominant commercial reference architecture is the **Register-to-Register Vector Architecture** (as used in the Cray-1 and modern NEC SX-Aurora TSUBASA systems).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Factory Assembly Line"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump: The Bakery Analogy** 🍞
> 
> Imagine a bakery where two different production methods are used to bake **1,000 cookies**:
> 
> - **Scalar Processor (SISD)**: A single baker uses a single oven, baking **one cookie at a time**. Each cookie goes through mixing → shaping → baking → packaging **sequentially**. The oven sits idle while the baker prepares the next cookie.
> - **Vector Processor (SIMD)**: A single chef issues **one instruction** ("bake!") to a **giant conveyor-belt oven** that holds 1,000 cookie trays. The oven's internal stages (mixing, baking, cooling) operate as a **pipeline**, and at any given clock cycle, **64 cookies** are simultaneously at different stages of completion. The chef issues one command, and the conveyor does the rest automatically.
> 
> The **chef** = the vector control unit; the **conveyor oven** = the vector functional unit; the **cookie trays** = vector registers; the **one command** = the single vector instruction.

This is the essence of a vector processor: **one instruction orchestrates a deep, deeply-pipelined functional unit that chews through a stream of data elements in lock-step.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are central to the formal evaluation of vector processors and appear frequently in KTU numerical questions:

- **$T_{clock}$** = Cycle time of the vector functional unit (typically **a few nanoseconds**)
- **$T_{startup}$** = Vector start-up latency (time to fill the pipeline before the first useful result emerges)
- **$VL$** = Vector Length (number of elements in a vector, e.g., 64 in Cray-1)
- **$\text{MVL}$** = Maximum Vector Length (architectural cap, e.g., **64 elements**)
- **$N_{lanes}$** = Number of parallel execution lanes inside the vector unit (e.g., **8 lanes** in a typical modern vector processor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Vector Processors Matter in 2024**
> Modern supercomputers (TOP500 systems like Fugaku, Frontier, and SX-Aurora) still rely on **vector-like SIMD units** (AVX-512, SVE, NEC SX) because scientific kernels — weather modeling, CFD, molecular dynamics, deep-learning tensor ops — exhibit massive **data parallelism** that is energy-inefficient to express on scalar superscalar out-of-order cores.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Building Blocks of a Vector Processor

A vector processor is not a single magic chip — it is a **tightly-coordinated ecosystem** of seven cooperating subsystems. Understanding the role of each subsystem is mandatory for the KTU 2024 examination.

### 2.1.1 Vector Register File
- A set of **dedicated registers**, each holding a vector of $VL$ data elements.
- Typical widths: **64 elements × 64 bits = 512 bytes per register** (Cray-1 style).
- Operands are read/written in a **single clock per element** when the pipeline is full.
- Decouples the vector unit from the scalar register file, allowing simultaneous scalar and vector execution.

### 2.1.2 Vector Functional Units (VFUs)
- **Deeply pipelined** execution units (e.g., FP add pipeline with depth 6, FP multiply with depth 7).
- Each VFU accepts one pair of vector operands per cycle and produces one result per cycle (after pipeline fill).
- Multiple VFUs can operate in **parallel** (e.g., Add Unit, Multiply Unit, Divide Unit, Load/Store Unit all active simultaneously).

### 2.1.3 Vector Length Register (VL)
- A **special-purpose scalar register** specifying how many elements of the vector are to be processed in the current instruction.
- Range: $0 \le VL \le MVL$.
- Solves the problem of vectors that are not exact multiples of $MVL$ via **strip mining**.

### 2.1.4 Vector Mask Register (VM)
- A boolean vector of length $MVL$ that enables **predicated execution** of vector elements.
- Element $i$ is computed only if $VM[i] = 1$; otherwise the result is left unchanged (or set to zero, depending on architecture).
- Enables **if-then-else** semantics in vector code.

### 2.1.5 Vector Control Unit
- Issues one vector instruction at a time (or in pairs if chaining).
- Maintains a **scoreboard** to track which vector registers are busy.
- Decodes the vector opcode and routes operands to the correct VFU.

### 2.1.6 Memory System with Multiple Banks
- Vector workloads are **bandwidth-hungry**; a single memory port would stall the pipeline.
- Solution: **interleaved memory banks** (e.g., 8 or 16 banks) so that consecutive elements reside in different banks and can be accessed simultaneously.
- Address stride pattern: bank $i$ holds word $i$ at address $A + i \times \text{stride}$.

### 2.1.7 Scalar Unit
- Handles loop control, address arithmetic, and scalar instructions that surround vector code.
- The scalar and vector units operate **concurrently** (e.g., the scalar unit computes the next address while the vector unit processes the current chunk).

## 2.2 Vector Instruction Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification: The 4 Instruction Classes**
> 
> | Class | Mnemonic Form | Example | Purpose |
> |-------|---------------|---------|---------|
> | **Vector–Vector** | `VADD V1, V2, V3` | $V1[i] = V2[i] + V3[i]$ | Element-wise arithmetic |
> | **Vector–Scalar** | `VMULS V1, V2, F0` | $V1[i] = V2[i] \times F0$ | Scaling, broadcasting a constant |
> | **Vector–Memory (Load)** | `VLD V1, R1` | $V1[i] = \text{Mem}[R1 + i \times s]$ | Streaming data from memory |
> | **Vector–Memory (Store)** | `VST V1, R1` | $\text{Mem}[R1 + i \times s] = V1[i]$ | Streaming data to memory |

Two further classes appear in advanced architectures:
- **Vector Reduction**: `VSUM V1, V2` → $V1 = \sum_i V2[i]$ (uses a reduction tree).
- **Gather/Scatter**: `VGATHER V1, (V2)` → $V1[i] = \text{Mem}[V2[i]]$ (indirect addressing).

## 2.3 The Vector Instruction Execution Lifecycle

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4-Phase Execution Model** (memorize this — it is a 14-mark question favorite)
> 
> 1. **Issue Phase**: The vector control unit fetches and decodes the vector instruction, checks the scoreboard for operand readiness, and reads the source vector registers' descriptors.
> 2. **Read Phase**: Source operands are streamed out of the vector register file, one element per cycle, into the functional unit's pipeline latches.
> 3. **Execute Phase**: The pipeline propagates partial results through its stages. After $T_{startup}$ cycles, the **first** result emerges; thereafter, **one result per cycle** is produced.
> 4. **Write Phase (Chaining)**: Results are written back to the destination vector register; if a **dependent** vector instruction is waiting, it can begin reading freshly-written operands **immediately** — this is called **vector chaining**.

## 2.4 Vector Chaining — The Heart of Vector Performance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vector Chaining** is the forwarding/bypassing mechanism that allows the result of one vector functional unit to be **consumed as an operand** by another vector functional unit **without waiting for the full vector to be written back to the register file**.

Without chaining:
- $V3 = V1 + V2$ followed by $V4 = V3 \times V5$ would have to wait for **all** elements of $V3$ to complete before the multiplication could begin.
- Effective latency: $2 \times T_{startup} + 2 \times n$ cycles.

With chaining:
- As soon as **element 1** of $V3$ is produced, it is forwarded to the multiplier.
- Effective latency: $T_{startup} + n + T_{startup}$ cycles (overlapped).

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — All Vector Processor Formulas (use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid table breakage)**
> 
> | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
> |---|---------|---------|--------------|
> | 1 | $R_n = \dfrac{n \times T_s}{T_v(n)}$ | Speedup for vector length $n$ | dimensionless |
> | 2 | $T_v(n) = T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock}$ | Vector execution time | cycles |
> | 3 | $T_s(n) = n \times T_{scalar}$ | Equivalent scalar execution time | cycles |
> | 4 | $R_\infty = \lim_{n \to \infty} R_n = \dfrac{T_{scalar}}{T_{clock}}$ | Asymptotic speedup | dimensionless |
> | 5 | $R_n = \dfrac{n \times T_{scalar}}{T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock}}$ | Closed-form $R_n$ | dimensionless |
> | 6 | $n_{\tfrac{1}{2}} = \dfrac{T_{startup}}{T_{scalar} - T_{clock}}$ | Vector length at half peak $R_\infty$ | elements |
> | 7 | $R_{\max} = R_\infty \times \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{T_{startup}}{n \times T_{clock}}}$ | Useful upper bound for finite $n$ | dimensionless |
> | 8 | $n_{\text{chunks}} = \lceil N / MVL \rceil$ | Number of strip-mined chunks | integer |
> | 9 | $VL = N \bmod MVL$ (last chunk) | Vector length of final chunk | elements |
> | 10 | $\text{BW}_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{2 \times \text{sizeof(element)} \times VL}{T_{clock}}$ | Required memory bandwidth | bytes/sec |
> | 11 | $\text{Conf-free stride: } \gcd(\text{stride},\ \text{banks}) = 1$ | Condition to avoid bank conflicts | dimensionless |
> | 12 | $T_{\text{conflict}}(s) = T_{clock} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{s-1}{\text{banks}}\right)$ | Slowed access time under conflict | cycles |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Vector processors are the workhorse of:
- **Climate & Weather Modeling** (ECMWF, GFS, NCAR CAM)
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics** (Ansys Fluent, OpenFOAM on SX-Aurora)
- **Molecular Dynamics** (GROMACS, NAMD on AVX-512)
- **Seismic Processing** (oil & gas exploration)
- **Deep Learning Training** (tensor cores in GPUs are essentially micro-vector units)

The **$R_\infty$** metric is the industry-standard way for HPC architects to quote the *theoretical* peak performance benefit of vectorization, while $n_{\tfrac{1}{2}}$ is the metric that tells domain scientists whether their inner-loop length is long enough to amortize the pipeline startup cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Form $R_n$ from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup** — A scalar processor requires $T_{scalar}$ clock cycles per element to execute a loop of $n$ iterations. The equivalent vector version has a start-up latency $T_{startup}$ and a per-element throughput of one result per $T_{clock}$ cycles (with $T_{clock} < T_{scalar}$). Derive $R_n$ and $R_\infty$.

### Step 1 — Total scalar execution time

The scalar processor executes the $n$ iterations one at a time. Each iteration takes $T_{scalar}$ cycles, and there is no inter-iteration overlap.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s(n) &= n \times T_{scalar}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Total vector execution time

The vector unit requires $T_{startup}$ cycles to fill the pipeline before the first result appears, and then produces one result per $T_{clock}$ cycle for the remaining $n - 1$ iterations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_v(n) &= T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Define the speedup

The speedup is the ratio of scalar time to vector time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{T_s(n)}{T_v(n)} \\
&= \frac{n \times T_{scalar}}{T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$

Divide numerator and denominator by $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{T_{scalar}}{\dfrac{T_{startup}}{n} + T_{clock}}
\end{aligned}
$$

As $n \to \infty$, the term $\dfrac{T_{startup}}{n} \to 0$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_\infty &= \lim_{n \to \infty} R_n = \frac{T_{scalar}}{T_{clock}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental result**: a vector processor can never be more than $T_{scalar} / T_{clock}$ times faster than a scalar processor on this loop, no matter how large $n$ becomes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Half-Peak Length $n_{\tfrac{1}{2}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal** — Find the vector length $n$ for which $R_n = R_\infty / 2$ (i.e., the length at which we have achieved half of the asymptotic speedup).

### Step 1 — Set up the equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{R_\infty}{2} \\
\frac{n \times T_{scalar}}{T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock}} &= \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{T_{scalar}}{T_{clock}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Cross-multiply

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \times n \times T_{scalar} \times T_{clock} &= T_{scalar} \times \left( T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Cancel $T_{scalar}$ from both sides (assuming $T_{scalar} \neq 0$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \times n \times T_{clock} &= T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $n$

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \times n \times T_{clock} - n \times T_{clock} &= T_{startup} \\
n \times T_{clock} &= T_{startup} \\
n_{\tfrac{1}{2}} &= \frac{T_{startup}}{T_{clock}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Gold**: A common board exam question is to compute $n_{\tfrac{1}{2}}$ for a given pipeline. The interpretation is: if your inner loop is shorter than $T_{startup} / T_{clock}$ elements, vectorization is **counter-productive** — the pipeline never gets full and you waste cycles on start-up overhead.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Speedup with Strip-Mining

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup** — A vector processor has $\text{MVL} = 64$. A loop of $N = 250$ elements must be processed. The strip-mining procedure creates $\lceil 250/64 \rceil = 4$ chunks. The first three chunks use $VL = 64$, the final chunk uses $VL = 250 - 3 \times 64 = 58$. Derive the total time.

### Step 1 — Time for a full chunk

For each full chunk of 64 elements, the vector unit takes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{full}} &= T_{startup} + 64 \times T_{clock}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Time for the final (partial) chunk

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{partial}} &= T_{startup} + 58 \times T_{clock}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Total vector time for the whole loop

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_v(250) &= 3 \times T_{\text{full}} + T_{\text{partial}} \\
&= 3 \times \left( T_{startup} + 64 \times T_{clock} \right) + \left( T_{startup} + 58 \times T_{clock} \right) \\
&= 4 \times T_{startup} + \left( 192 + 58 \right) \times T_{clock} \\
&= 4 \times T_{startup} + 250 \times T_{clock}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare to a non-strip-mined version

Had MVL been $\ge 250$, the time would be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_v^{\text{ideal}}(250) &= T_{startup} + 250 \times T_{clock}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **strip-mining overhead** is therefore $3 \times T_{startup}$ extra cycles. This is the cost of having an architectural $MVL$ smaller than the problem size.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bank Conflict Penalty

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup** — A memory system has $B = 8$ banks. A vector load with stride $s = 4$ is issued (consecutive elements are 4 words apart). Without conflict, one element is delivered per cycle. Determine the slowed access pattern and the effective throughput penalty.

### Step 1 — Determine whether a conflict exists

Two consecutive elements in the vector stream reside in banks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bank}(i) &= (A + i \times s) \bmod B \\
\text{bank}(0) &= A \bmod B \\
\text{bank}(1) &= (A + s) \bmod B
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\gcd(s, B) > 1$, then the access pattern revisits the same bank before the previous access completes, causing a **bank conflict**.

With $s = 4$ and $B = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gcd(4, 8) &= 4 > 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{CONFLICT EXISTS}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the conflict factor

The effective access cycle is stretched by a factor of $\gcd(s, B)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{conflict}}(s) &= T_{clock} \times \gcd(s, B) \\
&= T_{clock} \times 4
\end{aligned}
$$

The effective time to load the entire vector of length $n$ becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{load}}(n) &= T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock} \times \gcd(s, B)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the slowdown

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slowdown} &= \frac{T_{\text{conflict}}}{T_{\text{no-conflict}}} = \frac{4 \times T_{clock}}{1 \times T_{clock}} = 4 \times
\end{aligned}
$$

A 4× slowdown on memory access will dominate the overall $R_n$ and is a **famous performance killer** in stride-based vector code (e.g., accessing columns of a Fortran column-major array).

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Templates

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Template 1: Compute $R_n$ given $n$, $T_{startup}$, $T_{clock}$, $T_{scalar}$**
> 
> Plug into:
> 
> $$R_n = \frac{n \times T_{scalar}}{T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Template 2: Compute $R_\infty$**
> 
> $$R_\infty = \frac{T_{scalar}}{T_{clock}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Template 3: Compute $n_{\tfrac{1}{2}}$**
> 
> $$n_{\tfrac{1}{2}} = \frac{T_{startup}}{T_{clock}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Template 4: Compute Strip-Mined Time**
> 
> $$T_v(N) = \left\lceil \frac{N}{MVL} \right\rceil \times T_{startup} + N \times T_{clock}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Template 5: Compute Bank Conflict Penalty**
> 
> $$T_{\text{load}}(n) = T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock} \times \gcd(s, B)$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Register-to-Register Vector Processor

The diagram below maps the seven subsystems (vector register file, scalar unit, vector functional units, memory subsystem, control unit) and the data/control paths that interconnect them.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL[Vector Control Unit]
        PC[Program Counter]
        DEC[Instruction Decoder]
        VL[Vector Length Register VL]
        VM[Vector Mask Register VM]
        SB[Scoreboard]
    end

    subgraph SCU[Scalar Unit]
        SREG[Scalar Register File]
        SEX[Scalar Functional Unit]
    end

    subgraph VRF[Vector Register File]
        V0[VR0]
        V1[VR1]
        V2[VR2]
        V3[VR3]
        V4[VR4]
        V5[VR5]
        V6[VR6]
        V7[VR7]
    end

    subgraph VFU[Vector Functional Units]
        VADD[Vector Add Pipeline]
        VMUL[Vector Multiply Pipeline]
        VLD[Vector Load Pipeline]
        VST[Vector Store Pipeline]
        VDIV[Vector Divide Pipeline]
    end

    subgraph MEM[Memory Subsystem]
        ADR[Address Generation]
        BK0[Bank 0]
        BK1[Bank 1]
        BK2[Bank 2]
        BK3[Bank 3]
        BK4[Bank 4]
        BK5[Bank 5]
        BK6[Bank 6]
        BK7[Bank 7]
    end

    PC --> DEC
    DEC --> SB
    DEC --> VL
    DEC --> VM
    SB --> VFU
    VL --> VFU
    VM --> VFU

    SREG --> SEX
    SEX --> ADR

    V0 --> VADD
    V1 --> VADD
    V2 --> VMUL
    V3 --> VMUL
    V0 --> VLD
    V0 --> VST
    V4 --> VADD
    V4 --> VMUL
    V5 --> VADD
    V5 --> VMUL
    V6 --> VDIV
    V7 --> VDIV

    VADD --> V4
    VMUL --> V5
    VDIV --> V6
    VLD --> V7
    VST --> V7

    ADR --> BK0
    ADR --> BK1
    ADR --> BK2
    ADR --> BK3
    ADR --> BK4
    ADR --> BK5
    ADR --> BK6
    ADR --> BK7
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Vector Control Unit** at the top-left decodes each instruction, looks up the **VL** and **VM**, and consults the **scoreboard** to detect RAW (read-after-write) hazards.
- The **Scalar Unit** on the left computes loop indices and base addresses that feed the **Address Generation** unit.
- The **Vector Register File** in the middle feeds the deeply-pipelined **Vector Functional Units**.
- The **Memory Subsystem** uses 8 interleaved banks to supply one element per cycle to the Load pipeline.

## 4.2 Vector Chaining — Dataflow Diagram

The diagram below illustrates how vector chaining forwards element-wise results from the Add pipeline directly to the Multiply pipeline, bypassing the register file write/read.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Cycle T0<br>Add reads V1[0] V2[0]]
    A2[Cycle T1<br>Add reads V1[1] V2[1]]
    A3[Cycle T2<br>Add reads V1[2] V2[2]]
    A4[Cycle T3<br>Add reads V1[3] V2[3]]
    A5[Cycle T4<br>Add reads V1[4] V2[4]]

    F1[Add result[0] forwarded to Mul]
    F2[Add result[1] forwarded to Mul]
    F3[Add result[2] forwarded to Mul]
    F4[Add result[3] forwarded to Mul]
    F5[Add result[4] forwarded to Mul]

    M1[Cycle T6<br>Mul reads Add[0] and V3[0]]
    M2[Cycle T7<br>Mul reads Add[1] and V3[1]]
    M3[Cycle T8<br>Mul reads Add[2] and V3[2]]
    M4[Cycle T9<br>Mul reads Add[3] and V3[3]]
    M5[Cycle T10<br>Mul reads Add[4] and V3[4]]

    A1 --> F1 --> M1
    A2 --> F2 --> M2
    A3 --> F3 --> M3
    A4 --> F4 --> M4
    A5 --> F5 --> M5
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each Add result is produced one cycle after the corresponding Mul operand becomes available.
- The two pipelines **overlap in time** rather than executing back-to-back.
- Total time for a 5-element vector with start-up 6 cycles: **6 + 5 + 6 = 17 cycles** (chained) versus **6 + 5 + 6 + 5 = 22 cycles** (un-chained, naive).

## 4.3 Memory Banking — Stride-Access Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STRIDE1[Stride s equals 1: NO CONFLICT]
        E0[Element 0] --> B0[Bank 0]
        E1[Element 1] --> B1[Bank 1]
        E2[Element 2] --> B2[Bank 2]
        E3[Element 3] --> B3[Bank 3]
        E4[Element 4] --> B4[Bank 4]
        E5[Element 5] --> B5[Bank 5]
        E6[Element 6] --> B6[Bank 6]
        E7[Element 7] --> B7[Bank 7]
    end

    subgraph STRIDE4[Stride s equals 4 with B equals 8: CONFLICT gcd is 4]
        F0[Element 0] --> C0[Bank 0]
        F1[Element 1] --> C4[Bank 4]
        F2[Element 2] --> C0B[Bank 0 STALL]
        F3[Element 3] --> C4B[Bank 4 STALL]
        F4[Element 4] --> C0C[Bank 0 STALL]
        F5[Element 5] --> C4C[Bank 4 STALL]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- In the top sub-graph, consecutive elements map to consecutive banks — one element per cycle, no conflict.
- In the bottom sub-graph, every access hits the same two banks, causing a 4× slowdown.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct-concept questions modeled on KTU 2024 Part A. Each must be answered in **~5 lines** in the answer script.

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a vector processor? Explain the role of the Vector Length Register (VL) and the Vector Mask Register (VM) in a vector processor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A vector processor is a SIMD-style parallel architecture that operates on entire arrays (vectors) of data using a single instruction, exploiting data-level parallelism. The **Vector Length Register (VL)** is a special scalar register that specifies the number of elements to be processed in the current vector instruction, allowing vectors shorter than the architectural maximum to be handled efficiently. The **Vector Mask Register (VM)** is a boolean vector of length MVL that enables **predicated execution**, where each element of the vector is conditionally processed only if the corresponding mask bit is set, thus supporting if-then-else semantics in vector code.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define vector chaining. Why is it essential for achieving high performance in a register-to-register vector architecture?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Vector chaining is a hardware mechanism in which the result produced by one vector functional unit is **forwarded directly** to another vector functional unit as an operand, without waiting for the entire destination vector to be written back to the register file. It is essential because it allows two dependent vector instructions to **overlap in time** — element $i$ of the second instruction begins execution as soon as element $i$ of the first instruction completes, drastically reducing the effective latency from $2 T_{startup} + 2n$ to $T_{startup} + n + T_{startup}$. Without chaining, the start-up overhead of the second pipeline would serialize the entire computation, wasting the deep pipelining that makes vector processors fast.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question is worth **14 marks** and is internally split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student is given an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Provide full model solutions for both choices.

---

### ❖ Question A — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a register-to-register vector processor. List and briefly explain the role of any **four** vector instructions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (7 marks):

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1 (Mermaid Block Diagram)**. The student should draw a diagram showing the **Vector Control Unit** (containing VL, VM, scoreboard), **Scalar Unit**, **Vector Register File** (e.g., 8 vector registers of length 64), **Vector Functional Units** (Add, Multiply, Load, Store, Divide pipelines), and the **Interleaved Memory Subsystem** (e.g., 8 banks). Data flow arrows must connect these blocks.

**Four Vector Instructions (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **VADD V3, V1, V2** — Vector–Vector Add. Performs $V3[i] = V1[i] + V2[i]$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, VL-1$. Element-wise parallel addition.
2. **VMULS V3, V1, F0** — Vector–Scalar Multiply. Performs $V3[i] = V1[i] \times F0$. Broadcasts the scalar $F0$ to all lanes.
3. **VLD V1, R1** — Vector Load. Reads $VL$ elements from memory starting at the address in scalar register $R1$, with optional stride, into vector register $V1$.
4. **VST V1, R1** — Vector Store. Writes $VL$ elements from vector register $V1$ to memory starting at the address in $R1$.

(Alternative valid answers: VMUL, VSUB, VDIV, VAND, VOR, GATHER, SCATTER, REDUCTION.)

---

**A (b)** A vector processor has a scalar execution time of $T_{scalar} = 8$ cycles per element. The vector unit has a start-up time of $T_{startup} = 30$ cycles and a per-element clock of $T_{clock} = 2$ cycles. Compute the speedup $R_{100}$ for $n = 100$ elements, the asymptotic speedup $R_\infty$, and the half-peak length $n_{\tfrac{1}{2}}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Compute $T_s(100)$ and $T_v(100)$** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s(100) &= 100 \times 8 = 800 \text{ cycles} \\
T_v(100) &= 30 + 100 \times 2 = 230 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R_{100}$** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{100} &= \frac{T_s(100)}{T_v(100)} = \frac{800}{230} \approx 3.48
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_\infty$** (1.5 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_\infty &= \frac{T_{scalar}}{T_{clock}} = \frac{8}{2} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $n_{\tfrac{1}{2}}$** (1.5 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\tfrac{1}{2}} &= \frac{T_{startup}}{T_{clock}} = \frac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ elements}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For $n = 100$ we achieve $\approx 87\%$ of the peak speedup. The half-peak length is 15 elements, meaning any inner loop shorter than 15 elements would not benefit from vectorization.

---

### ❖ Question B — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**B (a)** Explain the concept of **memory banking** in vector processors. A vector processor has 8 memory banks. A vector load with stride $s = 3$ accesses the banks. Determine whether a **bank conflict** occurs. If yes, compute the conflict factor and the effective access time for a vector of length $n = 64$ elements, given $T_{clock} = 4$ ns and $T_{startup} = 20$ ns. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (7 marks):

**Concept of Memory Banking (2 marks):**
Vector processors require very high memory bandwidth because they stream large amounts of data. To meet this demand, the memory is organized into $B$ **independent banks** that can be accessed in parallel. Consecutive elements of a vector are placed in different banks using an **interleaving scheme** (typically $\text{bank}(i) = (A + i) \bmod B$). If a vector access pattern maps two consecutive elements to the same bank, a **bank conflict** occurs and the second access must stall.

**Step 1 — Test for conflict (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gcd(s, B) &= \gcd(3, 8) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the GCD equals 1, **no two consecutive elements map to the same bank**. Therefore, **NO bank conflict** occurs for stride $s = 3$ with 8 banks.

**Step 2 — Conflict factor (1 mark)**
Since there is no conflict, the conflict factor is **1** (i.e., one element per clock).

**Step 3 — Effective access time (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{load}}(64) &= T_{startup} + 64 \times T_{clock} \times \gcd(s, B) \\
&= 20 + 64 \times 4 \times 1 \\
&= 20 + 256 \\
&= 276 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The stride-3 access on 8 banks is conflict-free, and the entire 64-element vector loads in 276 ns.

---

**B (b)** With neat diagrams, explain the following three techniques used to enhance vector processor performance: **(i)** Vector Chaining, **(ii)** Conditional Execution (Vector Masking), and **(iii)** Sparse Matrix Handling via Gather–Scatter. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (7 marks):

**(i) Vector Chaining (2.5 marks):**
Vector chaining is the technique of **forwarding results from one vector functional unit directly to another**, without waiting for the destination vector to be fully written back to the register file. This is analogous to data forwarding in scalar pipelines but applied at the **vector-element granularity**. As soon as element $i$ of the source vector is produced by the first functional unit, element $i$ of the dependent vector instruction can begin consuming it. This **overlaps the start-up latencies** of dependent pipelines, reducing the total time from $T_{startup,A} + n + T_{startup,B} + n$ (un-chained) to $T_{startup,A} + n + T_{startup,B}$ (chained). (Refer to **Section 4.2 diagram** for a graphical illustration.)

**(ii) Conditional Execution via Vector Masking (2.5 marks):**
The **Vector Mask Register (VM)** is a boolean vector of length MVL. For a masked vector instruction, element $i$ of the destination is computed only if $VM[i] = 1$; otherwise, the element is left unchanged. This implements predicated execution and supports **if-then-else** logic in vector code without branching. Example:

| $i$ | $VM[i]$ | $A[i]$ | $B[i]$ | $C[i] = A[i] + B[i]$ (if VM[i]=1) | $C[i]$ (final) |
|-----|---------|--------|--------|----|----|
| 0 | 1 | 5.0 | 2.0 | 7.0 | 7.0 |
| 1 | 0 | 3.0 | 1.0 | — | (unchanged) |
| 2 | 1 | 1.0 | 4.0 | 5.0 | 5.0 |

**(iii) Gather–Scatter for Sparse Matrices (2 marks):**
For sparse data structures (e.g., compressed sparse row matrices), the regular stride-based memory access fails because the indices of non-zero elements are irregular. The **GATHER** instruction uses a vector of indices $V_{idx}$ to load $V_{dest}[i] = \text{Mem}[V_{idx}[i]]$ — effectively an indirect load. The **SCATTER** instruction does the reverse: $\text{Mem}[V_{idx}[i]] = V_{src}[i]$. This allows the vector processor to operate on sparse data structures without losing the SIMD efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Vector Processor Questions**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $T_{startup}$ term in $T_v(n)$**: Many students write $T_v(n) = n \times T_{clock}$, which is wrong. The full expression is $T_v(n) = T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock}$. This error will cost **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing $R_n$ with $R_\infty$**: $R_n$ depends on $n$ and includes the start-up penalty. $R_\infty$ is the asymptotic limit reached only for very large $n$. Always read the question carefully — "speedup for $n = 64$" and "maximum speedup" are **different** questions.
> 3. **Bank-conflict GCD calculation**: The conflict factor is $\gcd(s, B)$, not $\gcd(s, B) - 1$ and not $s$ itself. A common slip is to forget the modulo and simply divide.
> 4. **Strip-mining final chunk**: For the last chunk, $VL = N \bmod MVL$, **not** $MVL$ again. Forgetting this gives the wrong total time and loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Chaining vs. No-chaining**: If the question mentions chaining, the second instruction can start **before** the first finishes. If chaining is not mentioned or is disabled, you must add the full $T_{startup}$ of the second pipeline.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
> 
> - **Vector Processor = SIMD + Deep Pipelining + Vector Registers.** It is the original data-parallel architecture.
> - **Three key architectural choices**: Memory-to-memory vs. Register-to-register (latter is dominant); Scalar unit on-chip (always present); Number of vector registers (8 in Cray-1, more in modern).
> - **VL (Vector Length Register)** controls how many elements are processed. Range: $0 \le VL \le MVL$.
> - **VM (Vector Mask Register)** implements predicated execution.
> - **Four instruction classes**: Vector–Vector, Vector–Scalar, Vector–Memory Load, Vector–Memory Store. (Plus reduction and gather/scatter in advanced designs.)
> - **Strip Mining** breaks a long vector $N$ into chunks of size $MVL$. Last chunk uses $VL = N \bmod MVL$.
> - **Vector Chaining** forwards results between functional units — reduces latency dramatically.
> - **Memory Banking** with $B$ banks, conflict occurs if $\gcd(s, B) > 1$. Penalty factor is the GCD.
> - **Speedup formula**: $R_n = \dfrac{n \times T_{scalar}}{T_{startup} + n \times T_{clock}}$
> - **Asymptotic speedup**: $R_\infty = \dfrac{T_{scalar}}{T_{clock}}$ — a hard ceiling.
> - **Half-peak length**: $n_{\tfrac{1}{2}} = \dfrac{T_{startup}}{T_{clock}}$ — the minimum useful vector length.
> - **Real-world systems**: Cray-1 (1976), NEC SX-Aurora TSUBASA (2018+), Fujitsu A64FX (uses SVE-style vector instructions in supercomputer Fugaku).
> - **Modern relevance**: AVX-512, ARM SVE/SVE2, and RISC-V V-extension are all **vector-style SIMD** extensions used in 2024 HPC and ML hardware.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
