2. **Vector Element Processing**: The vector functional unit processes elements in a **deep pipeline** — one element per cycle after pipeline fill.
3. **Strip Mining**: If the loop's iteration count exceeds the **Maximum Vector Length (MVL)**, the compiler/hardware partitions the loop into *chunks of MVL* plus a *remainder chunk*.
4. **Predicate (Mask) Handling**: Each element can be conditionally written using the VM register.
5. **Chaining**: Result of vector unit U1 can be forwarded to U2 in the same cycle → overlapped execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Vector Processors Excel**
> Vector units hide memory latency through **deep pipelining** and through *streaming* of elements directly from memory (bypassing cache for huge arrays). They also reduce *instruction bandwidth pressure* — one vector instruction replaces `N` scalar instructions, freeing the pipeline for other work.

## 2.3 Operational Logic of GPU (SIMT)

1. **Thread Block (CTA) Launch**: The host (CPU) launches a kernel consisting of a *grid* of *thread blocks*.
2. **Warp Formation**: Threads within a block are grouped into **warps of 32** (NVIDIA) or **wavefronts of 64** (AMD). Threads in a warp share a single PC.
3. **Lockstep Execution**: All 32 threads in a warp execute the same instruction each cycle.
4. **Predication / Branch Divergence**: When threads in a warp take different paths, the hardware executes both paths serially (reconvergence stack), causing a **SIMD lane under-utilization** penalty.
5. **Latency Hiding via Warp Scheduling**: The SM **switches to another ready warp** on every memory stall, hiding hundreds of cycles of latency through TLP (Thread-Level Parallelism).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why GPUs are Throughput Engines**
> GPUs sacrifice **single-thread latency** (no deep out-of-order, no branch prediction of CPU caliber) to maximize **throughput per mm² and per watt**. The architectural bet: *launch 10,000 threads, hide all latencies by context switching* — a fundamentally different philosophy from CPU design.

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Use / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SIMD Speedup** (ideal, no overhead) | $S_{SIMD} = \dfrac{T_{scalar}}{T_{SIMD}} = N$ (for `N` lanes) | Theoretical max speedup when loop is fully vectorizable |
| 2 | **Amdahl's Law** (with vector fraction $f$) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | Computes real speedup accounting for serial portion |
| 3 | **Memory Bandwidth Bound** | $BW = \dfrac{\text{Bytes transferred}}{\text{Time (s)}}$ | Roofline analysis: is workload memory-bound or compute-bound? |
| 4 | **Operational Intensity (OI)** | $OI = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{Bytes}}$ | Used in Roofline model; GPU/SIMD prefer OI > ridge point |
| 5 | **Ridge Point** | $R = \dfrac{\pi_{peak}}{BW}$ | Boundary between memory-bound and compute-bound regions |
| 6 | **Roofline Performance** | $P = \min\bigl(\pi_{peak},\, BW \cdot OI\bigr)$ | Max achievable performance in GFLOPs/s |
| 7 | **Vector Length on Element Count** | $VL = \min(N_{loop},\, MVL)$ | Determines actual vector length for a given loop |
| 8 | **Strip Mining Chunks** | $k = \lceil N_{loop}/MVL \rceil$ | Number of full + remainder vector iterations |
| 9 | **GPU Occupancy** | $Occ = \dfrac{\text{Active Warps per SM}}{\text{Max Warps per SM}}$ | Higher occupancy → better latency hiding |
| 10 | **SIMD Lane Utilization** | $U_{lane} = \dfrac{\text{Active Lanes}}{\text{Total Lanes}}$ | Reduced by predicated/divergent operations |
| 11 | **Speedup with Overhead** | $S_{real} = \dfrac{N}{1 + N \cdot t_{overhead}/t_{scalar}}$ | Models loop-setup, mask-generation cost |
| 12 | **CPI for SIMD Pipeline** | $CPI_{SIMD} = 1 + \text{stall cycles per vector inst.}$ | Used in performance evaluation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical bars in formulas use `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.** E.g., $\vert x \vert$ is rendered as the absolute value without confusing the parser with table column separators.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SIMD** powers real-time **video codecs (H.264/HEVC)**, **image processing pipelines (Photoshop filters)**, and **cryptography (AES-NI)** in every modern CPU.
- **Vector processors** dominate **weather forecasting (Cray XC40)**, **CFD simulations**, and **supercomputing TOP500 entries** for memory-bound HPC.
- **GPUs** drive **deep learning training (CUDA + cuDNN)**, **LLM inference (Tensor Cores)**, and **scientific simulation (AMBER, GROMACS)** for compute-bound throughput kernels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — SIMD Speedup Using Amdahl's Law

**Problem Statement**: A 256-bit SIMD unit processes 8 lanes of 32-bit floats. A loop has total iteration count $N_{loop} = 1024$ elements. The vectorizable fraction is $f = 0.85$, and the serial control overhead per vector instruction is $t_{ovh} = 20$ cycles vs. $t_{scalar} = 100$ cycles per element. Find the actual speedup.

**Step 1 — Identify the lane count (N).**

The AVX-256 register holds $256 \text{ bits} / 32 \text{ bits per float} = 8$ lanes. So $N = 8$.

**Step 2 — Write Amdahl's Law without overhead.**

$$
S_{ideal} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{N}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
S_{ideal} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.85) + \frac{0.85}{8}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator.**

$$
(1 - 0.85) + \frac{0.85}{8} = 0.15 + 0.10625 = 0.25625
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the speedup.**

$$
S_{ideal} = \frac{1}{0.25625} \approx 3.902
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the overhead correction model.**

Real SIMD speedup with overhead ratio $\rho = t_{ovh}/t_{scalar}$:

$$
S_{real} = \frac{N}{1 + N \cdot \rho}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the overhead values.**

$$
\rho = \frac{20}{100} = 0.2
$$

$$
S_{real} = \frac{8}{1 + 8 \times 0.2} = \frac{8}{1 + 1.6} = \frac{8}{2.6} \approx 3.077
$$

**Step 8 — Sanity-check against the Amdahl bound.**

We expected $S_{real} \le S_{ideal}$. Indeed $3.077 \le 3.902$. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The serial 15% portion caps our speedup at $1/0.15 \approx 6.67\times$ no matter how wide SIMD gets. This is why **wide SIMD is NOT a silver bullet** — the algorithm's *Amdahl fraction* dominates.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Roofline Model Bound for a GPU Kernel

**Problem Statement**: An NVIDIA A100 GPU has peak FP32 throughput $\pi_{peak} = 19.5$ TFLOPs/s and peak HBM2 memory bandwidth $BW = 1.555$ TB/s. A given kernel performs 2 FP32 FLOPs per element and reads 16 bytes per element. Find the Operational Intensity, the ridge point, and the achievable performance.

**Step 1 — Compute Operational Intensity (OI).**

$$
OI = \frac{\text{FLOPs per element}}{\text{Bytes per element}} = \frac{2}{16} = 0.125 \text{ FLOPs/Byte}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Ridge Point (R).**

$$
R = \frac{\pi_{peak}}{BW} = \frac{19.5 \times 10^{12} \text{ FLOPs/s}}{1.555 \times 10^{12} \text{ Bytes/s}}
$$

$$
R = 12.54 \text{ FLOPs/Byte}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the region.**

Since $OI = 0.125 < R = 12.54$, the kernel is **memory-bound**.

**Step 4 — Compute achievable performance.**

$$
P = \min(\pi_{peak},\, BW \cdot OI)
$$

$$
P = \min(19.5 \times 10^{12},\, 1.555 \times 10^{12} \times 0.125)
$$

$$
P = \min(19.5 \times 10^{12},\, 0.1944 \times 10^{12})
$$

$$
P = 0.1944 \text{ TFLOPs/s} = 194.4 \text{ GFLOPs/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the achieved fraction of peak.**

$$
\text{Achieved \%} = \frac{194.4}{19500} \times 100 \approx 0.997\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A "fast" GPU achieves <1% of peak on memory-bound kernels. The remedy is **data reuse** (tiling, shared memory) to push OI above the ridge point.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vector Strip Mining Computation

**Problem Statement**: A vector processor has MVL = 64. A loop iterates over $N_{loop} = 250$ elements. Determine the number of full vector iterations, the remainder iteration length, and the total vector instructions issued.

**Step 1 — Number of full vector iterations.**

$$
k_{full} = \left\lfloor \frac{N_{loop}}{MVL} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{250}{64} \right\rfloor = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Remainder iteration length.**

$$
VL_{rem} = N_{loop} \bmod MVL = 250 \bmod 64 = 250 - 192 = 58
$$

**Step 3 — Total vector instructions issued.**

We have 3 instructions covering 64 elements each (handling 192 elements) + 1 instruction covering 58 elements.

$$
N_{instr} = k_{full} + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ vector instructions}
$$

**Step 4 — Total scalar operations replaced.**

Each vector instruction replaces 64 scalar instructions (in the full case) or 58 scalar (in the remainder).

$$
\text{Scalar instructions replaced} = 3 \times 64 + 58 = 250
$$

**Step 5 — Compute instruction count reduction ratio.**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{250}{4} = 62.5 \text{ scalar ops per vector instruction (average)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Vector instructions reduce **dynamic instruction bandwidth pressure** on the front-end by ~62×. This is the reason vector machines achieve high performance on memory-bound streaming workloads — the pipeline stays full even when the cache misses.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Comparison Axis | SIMD (CPU Extension) | Vector Processor (e.g., NEC SX-Aurora) | GPU (e.g., NVIDIA A100) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parallelism Exploited** | DLP | DLP | DLP + TLP (within warps) |
| **Lane Width** | 128/256/512 bits | 256+ bits (vector registers) | 32 threads/warp × many warps |
| **Register File** | 16–32 vector registers | 32–64 vector registers | Thousands of scalar registers (per thread) |
| **Memory Model** | Cache-resident | Streaming / cache-hybrid | Massive HBM, cache hierarchy |
| **Latency Hiding** | Out-of-order, prefetching | Deep pipelining, chaining | Massive warp context switching |
| **Branches/Divergence** | Predication, masks | VM register masks | Branch divergence penalty |
| **Programming Model** | Intrinsics, autovectorization | Fortran vector directives, OpenMP simd | CUDA, OpenCL, SYCL, HIP |
| **Single-Thread Perf** | High (OOO, branch pred.) | High (vector + scalar units) | Low (latency-optimized) |
| **Best Workload** | Tight inner loops, kernels | HPC streaming, CFD, weather | Throughput, ML, irregular parallelism |
| **Power Efficiency (perf/W)** | Moderate | High (purpose-built) | Very High (for DLP) |
| **Cost** | Low (in CPU) | Very High | Moderate (datacenter GPUs) |
| **Lane Utilization Issue** | Predication wastes lanes | Masking wastes lanes | Divergence wastes warp slots |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SIMD Execution Unit Block Diagram (AVX-style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Control Unit] --> B[Wide Instruction Register]
    B --> C1[Lane 0 ALU]
    B --> C2[Lane 1 ALU]
    B --> C3[Lane 2 ALU]
    B --> C4[Lane 3 ALU]
    B --> C5[Lane 4 ALU]
    B --> C6[Lane 5 ALU]
    B --> C7[Lane 6 ALU]
    B --> C8[Lane 7 ALU]
    C1 --> D[Destination Register]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    C8 --> D
    E[Source Register A] --> C1
    E --> C2
    E --> C3
    E --> C4
    E --> C5
    E --> C6
    E --> C7
    E --> C8
    F[Source Register B] --> C1
    F --> C2
    F --> C3
    F --> C4
    F --> C5
    F --> C6
    F --> C7
    F --> C8
```

**Explanation:** The control unit broadcasts one instruction. Two source registers (each 256-bit / 8 lanes) feed the **8 SIMD lanes**, each computing the same operation on its slice. Results are written back to the 256-bit destination register.

## 4.2 Vector Processor Architectural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VLI[Vector Length Register VL] --> VU[Vector Functional Unit]
    VMI[Vector Mask Register VM] --> VU
    VR1[Vector Register File] --> VU
    VR2[Vector Register File] --> VU
    VU --> VRD[Destination Vector Register]
    VU --> CH[Chaining Forwarding Network]
    CH --> VU
    SCU[Scalar Control Unit] --> VLI
    SCU --> VMI
    MVS[Memory Vector Stream Unit] --> VR1
    MVS --> VR2
    MVS --> VRD
```

**Explanation:** The Vector Length Register (VL) controls how many elements are active. The Mask Register (VM) enables predicated writes. The vector unit can chain outputs to inputs for back-to-back pipelined operations. A dedicated memory-streaming unit loads/stores vectors with optional stride/gather/scatter.

## 4.3 GPU SIMT Execution Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    K[Kernel Launch] --> G[Grid of CTAs]
    G --> B1[CTA 0]
    G --> B2[CTA 1]
    G --> B3[CTA N]
    B1 --> W1[Warp 0 32 threads]
    B1 --> W2[Warp 1 32 threads]
    B1 --> W3[Warp 2 32 threads]
    W1 --> SM[Streaming Multiprocessor SM]
    W2 --> SM
    W3 --> SM
    SM --> SS[SIMD Lane Stack 32 wide]
    SS --> REG[Per-thread Register File]
    SS --> SHM[Shared Memory]
    SS --> L1[L1 Cache]
    L1 --> L2[L2 Cache]
    L2 --> HBM[HBM Memory]
    WS[Warp Scheduler] --> SM
```

**Explanation:** A kernel launches a grid of Cooperative Thread Arrays (CTAs). Each CTA is partitioned into warps of 32 threads, all of which are resident on a Streaming Multiprocessor (SM). The SM's **warp scheduler** issues one warp at a time to the **32-wide SIMD unit**, which executes the instruction across all 32 thread contexts simultaneously.

## 4.4 Side-by-Side Architecture Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU_SIMD [CPU SIMD]
        direction TB
        A1[Fetch Decode] --> A2[256-bit SIMD Unit]
        A2 --> A3[Out-of-Order Engine]
        A3 --> A4[L1 L2 L3 Cache]
    end
    subgraph VEC [Vector Processor]
        direction TB
        B1[Scalar Front End] --> B2[Vector Length VL]
        B2 --> B3[Deep Pipeline Vector Unit]
        B3 --> B4[Vector Cache Hybrid Memory]
    end
    subgraph GPU_SIMT [GPU SIMT]
        direction TB
        C1[Host CPU Dispatch] --> C2[Many SMs Each 128 Cores]
        C2 --> C3[Warp Scheduler]
        C3 --> C4[Massive Warp Pool]
        C4 --> C5[HBM2 1.5 TBs Bandwidth]
    end
```

**Explanation:** The three architectures differ fundamentally in **control philosophy** — CPU SIMD is *latency-optimized* with one execution per cycle, Vector processors are *streaming* with deep pipelines, and GPUs are *throughput-optimized* with massive warp-level concurrency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between SIMD and SIMT execution models with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SIMD | SIMT |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A single instruction operates on multiple data lanes in lockstep. | Threads are grouped into warps; the hardware executes one instruction across all threads in a warp. |
| **Programming** | Explicit vector intrinsics (e.g., `_mm256_add_ps`). | Scalar thread code; the hardware manages the SIMD execution (e.g., CUDA `a[i] + b[i]`). |
| **Register State** | One wide register per instruction operand. | Each thread has its own scalar register; warp has 32 scalar registers per operand. |
| **Example** | Intel AVX `VADDPS YMM0, YMM1, YMM2` | NVIDIA CUDA kernel with 256 threads / 8 warps |

> **Marking Note (1 mark per row × 3 rows = 3 marks).**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the Vector Mask Register (VM) in vector processors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** The Vector Mask Register (VM) is a special register in a vector processor whose individual bits correspond to each **vector lane**. When a vector instruction executes, only those lanes whose mask bit is `1` are allowed to **write their result** to the destination vector register.

2. **(1 mark)** The VM is set by a **compare / test vector instruction** (e.g., `VMASK = (A > B)`), and is then used by a subsequent **predicated vector operation** (e.g., `C = (VM ? A + B : C)`). This implements **conditional vector execution** without branch divergence.

3. **(1 mark)** Engineering utility: VM eliminates **predicated scalar branching overhead** in vectorized loops, e.g., `if (x[i] > 0) y[i] = sqrt(x[i])` becomes a single predicated vector `SQRT` instruction. It is the **vector equivalent of predication** in scalar CPUs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **SIMD execution model**. Discuss its **advantages** and **limitations** over scalar execution.

**(b) [7 marks]** A loop performs 512 iterations of a vectorizable operation. The SIMD unit has a width of $N = 8$ lanes, the vectorizable fraction is $f = 0.9$, and the serial startup overhead per vector iteration is $t_{ovh} = 30$ cycles with scalar cost $t_{scalar} = 200$ cycles/iter. Compute the **ideal Amdahl speedup**, the **overhead-corrected speedup**, and comment on the result.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### (a) [7 marks] SIMD Execution Model

**(1 mark) Definition:** SIMD = Single Instruction, Multiple Data. A single control unit fetches one instruction; the same operation is applied to $N$ data elements simultaneously using $N$ parallel functional units (lanes).

**(1 mark) Block diagram reference:** (As shown in SECTION 4.1 — wide instruction broadcast to 8 lanes, each processing a slice of the source registers).

**(1 mark) Operational Steps:**
1. Fetch & decode a *packed* instruction (e.g., 256-bit register = 8 floats).
2. Read two source operands from the wide register file.
3. Each of the 8 lanes performs the same arithmetic on its slice.
4. Write all 8 results back to a 256-bit destination register in one cycle.

**(1 mark) Advantages:**
- Energy-efficient: one instruction fetch, one decode → amortized across N data items.
- High throughput per watt for tight loops.
- Easy to use: compiler auto-vectorization + intrinsics.

**(1 mark) Advantages (cont.):**
- No synchronization overhead between lanes.
- Predictable performance for regular data.

**(1 mark) Limitations:**
- Wasted lanes when operations are predicated (e.g., masking).
- Requires contiguous, aligned data; gather/scatter is expensive.
- Not suited to irregular data-dependent control flow.

**(1 mark) Limitation (cont.) and summary:**
- Speedup is fundamentally capped by Amdahl's serial fraction.
- Conclusion: SIMD is best for **regular DLP** in inner loops with contiguous data.

---

#### (b) [7 marks] Numerical Computation

**Given:** $N_{loop} = 512$, $N = 8$, $f = 0.9$, $t_{ovh} = 30$, $t_{scalar} = 200$.

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Ideal Amdahl Speedup:**

$$
S_{ideal} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{N}} = \frac{1}{0.1 + \frac{0.9}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.1125} = \frac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.706
$$

**Step 2 [1 mark] — Total scalar time:**

$$
T_{scalar} = N_{loop} \times t_{scalar} = 512 \times 200 = 102{,}400 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3 [1 mark] — SIMD time including vectorizable + serial:**

$$
T_{serial} = (1-f) \times T_{scalar} = 0.1 \times 102{,}400 = 10{,}240 \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
N_{vec\_instr} = \frac{f \times N_{loop}}{N} = \frac{0.9 \times 512}{8} = \frac{460.8}{8} = 57.6 \approx 58 \text{ vector instructions}
$$

**Step 4 [1 mark] — Time for vector portion (each vector instruction processes N elements, plus overhead):**

Per vector instruction cost: $t_{scalar} \times N + t_{ovh} = 200 \times 8 + 30 = 1630$ cycles.

$$
T_{vector} = 58 \times 1630 = 94{,}540 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 5 [1 mark] — Total SIMD time:**

$$
T_{SIMD} = T_{serial} + T_{vector} = 10{,}240 + 94{,}540 = 104{,}780 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 6 [1 mark] — Real Speedup:**

$$
S_{real} = \frac{T_{scalar}}{T_{SIMD}} = \frac{102{,}400}{104{,}780} \approx 0.977
$$

**Step 7 [1 mark] — Comment:**

The real speedup is **less than 1.0** (a slowdown!) because the per-vector-instruction overhead of 30 cycles is too high relative to the 200 cycles saved by processing 8 elements at once. The break-even requires $t_{ovh} < t_{scalar} \times (N-1)/1 = 200 \times 7 = 1400$ cycles, so 30 is well within budget — but the per-instruction overhead compounds over 58 instructions. The lesson: **small loops with significant per-instruction overhead are unsuitable for SIMD; loops should be large and overhead amortized.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> Students often compute only $S_{ideal}$ and present it as the final answer. You will be **awarded only 1 mark** for that. To get full 7 marks, you **must compute the overhead-corrected speedup** AND **comment on the result** with engineering insight. Always distinguish *ideal* vs *real* speedup in KTU answers.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **architectural features of Vector Processors and GPUs**. Use a comparison table covering programming model, parallelism type, memory hierarchy, branch handling, and typical use cases.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **SIMT execution model** of modern GPUs. Discuss how GPUs hide memory latency and the penalty imposed by **branch divergence**.

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### (a) [7 marks] Vector Processor vs GPU — Comparison Table

**(7 marks awarded for the table; 1 mark per filled row of analytical depth):**

| Comparison Axis | Vector Processor | GPU (SIMT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallelism Type** | DLP (deep vector pipelines, chaining). | DLP (within warps) + TLP (across many warps). |
| **Programming Model** | Fortran `!$SIMD` directives, OpenMP, NEC ASLP. | CUDA, OpenCL, HIP, SYCL, Metal. |
| **Memory Hierarchy** | Streaming from main memory + vector cache; programmer-managed prefetch. | HBM/GDDR + L1/L2 caches + shared memory + registers (programmer-managed). |
| **Branch / Divergence** | Handled via Vector Mask Register (VM) — fine-grained lane masking. | Handled via warp predication; divergent warps serialize paths (1× per path). |
| **Latency Hiding** | Through deep vector pipeline + chaining + memory pipelining. | Through massive warp context switching (TLP). |
| **Best Use Case** | HPC streaming, CFD, weather, memory-bound array kernels. | ML training, irregular data parallelism, throughput kernels. |
| **Single-thread Latency** | High (vector + scalar units both). | Low (throughput-oriented). |

---

#### (b) [7 marks] SIMT Execution Model & Latency Hiding

**(1 mark) Definition:** SIMT = Single Instruction, Multiple Threads. Each thread in a warp has its own PC, registers, and address space, but **all threads in a warp execute the same instruction in lockstep** on a 32-wide SIMD unit.

**(1 mark) Diagram reference:** (See SECTION 4.3 — kernel → grid → CTA → warp → 32-wide SIMD lane stack → shared memory + L1 → L2 → HBM).

**(1 mark) Warp lifecycle:** A thread block is divided into warps of 32 threads. The SM maintains an **active warp pool**. A warp scheduler selects a *ready* warp every cycle and issues one instruction to the SIMD unit.

**(1 mark) Latency hiding mechanism:** When a warp issues a memory load that will take 400+ cycles to return from HBM, the SM does NOT stall. It **switches to another ready warp** in the pool. With ~64 warps active per SM, the scheduler always finds a warp that is ready to issue — so memory latency is *completely hidden* provided the warp pool is large enough.

**(1 mark) Mathematical occupancy rule:**

$$
\text{Max Active Warps per SM} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Registers per SM}}{\text{Registers per Thread}} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
\text{Max Active Warps per SM} = \min\left(\text{Register Limit},\; \text{Shared Memory Limit},\; \text{Hardware Limit}\right)
$$

**(1 mark) Branch divergence penalty:**

When threads in a warp take different paths of an `if-else` branch, the hardware executes **all paths serially**, with the divergent lanes masked out. For a branch with $p$ paths taken by equal portions of the warp, the effective SIMD utilization drops to $1/p$.

$$
U_{lane,\,div} = \frac{1}{p} \quad \text{(for p-way divergence in a warp)}
$$

**(1 mark) Mitigation strategies:** (i) Restructure code to minimize intra-warp divergence; (ii) Use `__shfl_sync` for warp-level data exchange without shared memory; (iii) Use predication instead of branches for short conditionals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **SIMD = one instruction, multiple data lanes**, fixed width (AVX-512 = 16 lanes of FP32, 32 lanes of INT8).
- ☐ **Vector Processor = CPU with architectural vector support**: VL, VM, stride, gather/scatter, chaining.
- ☐ **GPU = many-core SIMT** processor; warps of 32 threads, thousands of warps active, hides latency via warp scheduling.
- ☐ **SIMD vs SIMT**: SIMD is hardware execution; SIMT is programming model with scalar threads.
- ☐ **Amdahl's Law caps speedup** by the serial fraction — even infinite SIMD width cannot exceed $1/(1-f)$.
- ☐ **Roofline Model**: $P = \min(\pi_{peak},\, BW \cdot OI)$. Always identify ridge point.
- ☐ **Operational Intensity (OI) = FLOPs/Byte** determines memory-bound vs compute-bound regime.
- ☐ **Strip mining** partitions loops with $N_{loop} > MVL$ into full + remainder chunks.
- ☐ **Vector Mask Register (VM)** enables predicated vector execution without branch divergence.
- ☐ **Branch divergence** in GPUs reduces effective SIMD lane utilization to $1/p$ for $p$-way divergence.
- ☐ **Latency hiding in GPUs** requires enough warps in the pool — low occupancy = poor performance.
- ☐ **Vector chaining** allows back-to-back vector ops to pipeline their data.
- ☐ **Memory bandwidth, not peak FLOPs**, often bottlenecks SIMD/vector/GPU workloads.
- ☐ **For KTU exams**: always show *ideal* AND *real* speedup; comment on engineering insight.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Loops in C vs CUDA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Loops in C vs CUDA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Advanced Computer Architecture**, a **loop** in the **C programming language** is a **sequential control-flow construct** executed by a single thread on the **host (CPU)**, where each iteration of the loop is processed one after another on a single processing core (or time-sliced across cores via OS scheduling). In contrast, a **loop in CUDA (Compute Unified Device Architecture)** is a construct that exists in the **host code** (C/C++) to **launch a kernel**, while the actual computation inside the kernel runs as **massively parallel threads** on the **device (GPU)**, executing under the **SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Thread)** model. A single CUDA kernel invocation launches a **grid** of **thread blocks**, where each thread block contains a fixed number of threads (e.g., **up to 1024 threads per block** on modern NVIDIA architectures such as **Compute Capability 8.6 and above**), and the *implicit loop structure* is replaced by the **unique thread index** of each parallel thread.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Definition:** A CUDA loop is **not** a traditional `for` loop running on the GPU. The `for` loop only **describes the launch configuration**; the parallelism is **physically materialized** by the **thread index** (`threadIdx.x`, `blockIdx.x`, `blockDim.x`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

**Imagine a massive library with 1 million books that need to be sorted.**

- **C Loop (Sequential CPU):** A single librarian walks to each bookshelf, picks up one book, sorts it, then moves to the next book. The librarian is fast, but still works on **one book at a time**. Total time = 1 million × (time per book).

- **CUDA Loop (Parallel GPU):** You hire **thousands of librarians** and station them in different wings of the library simultaneously. Each librarian is told: *"You are librarian #i out of 1 million — sort the book on shelf i."* The "loop" is now a **declaration of work**, and the actual execution is **physically distributed** across thousands of workers who all do the same task on different data in parallel. Total time ≈ time for 1 book (with overhead).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CUDA `for` loop in the **host** only **sets up the workforce size** (number of threads and blocks). The **device** does the heavy lifting in parallel — this is the fundamental paradigm shift from sequential C to data-parallel CUDA.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Ready)

| Term | Definition | Where It Lives |
|---|---|---|
| **Host** | The CPU and its memory (RAM) | Where C code runs sequentially |
| **Device** | The GPU and its memory (VRAM / global memory) | Where CUDA kernels run in parallel |
| **Kernel** | A function marked `__global__` that runs on the device but is called from the host | Device-side parallel code |
| **Grid** | The complete 3D array of thread blocks launched by one kernel call | Device |
| **Block** | A group of threads (max **1024** on most modern GPUs) that can synchronize via `__syncthreads()` | Device |
| **Thread** | The smallest execution unit; one thread = one iteration of the conceptual loop | Device |
| **Warp** | A group of **32 threads** executed in lockstep (SIMT) on one SM | Device |
| **`threadIdx.x`** | Per-thread index inside a block (0 to `blockDim.x - 1`) | Device |
| **`blockIdx.x`** | Per-block index inside a grid (0 to `gridDim.x - 1`) | Device |
| **`blockDim.x`** | Number of threads per block along x-axis | Device |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Two Paradigms

### A. The C Loop Paradigm (Sequential, Scalar)

A standard C `for` loop executes the following way on the CPU:

1. **Initialize** the loop counter `i = 0`.
2. **Test** the condition `i < N`. If false, exit.
3. **Execute** the body (e.g., `C[i] = A[i] + B[i]`).
4. **Update** the counter (`i++`).
5. **Branch back** to step 2.

All these steps happen in **one thread of execution**, on **one core**, one iteration at a time. The CPU may use **pipelining, superscalar issue, or out-of-order execution** to speed up *one* iteration, but the **logical flow is strictly sequential**.

### B. The CUDA Loop Paradigm (Parallel, SIMT)

In CUDA, the structure is conceptually inverted:

1. The host writes a kernel function marked `__global__`.
2. The host calls the kernel with a **launch configuration**: `kernel<<<numBlocks, threadsPerBlock>>>(...)`.
3. The hardware **instantiates `numBlocks × threadsPerBlock` threads** simultaneously.
4. Each thread computes **its own global index** using a closed-form formula (no loop counter, no increment).
5. Each thread uses that index to read/write its **own element** of the data.
6. Threads execute in groups of **32 (a warp)** in lockstep — the SIMT model.

> [!TIP]
> **Why no explicit loop inside the kernel?** Because the hardware **already instantiates** one thread per data element. A loop *inside* the kernel would just be **sequential work per thread**, defeating the purpose of launching thousands of parallel threads.

## 2.2 The Three Core CUDA Indexing Formulas (Board-Exam Gold)

For a 1D launch with `gridDim.x = G` blocks and `blockDim.x = B` threads per block, the mapping from a 3D coordinate `(blockIdx, threadIdx)` to a flat array index is given by the following canonical formulas:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Global thread index (1D):} \quad & idx = blockIdx.x \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x \\[6pt]
\text{Global thread index (2D):} \quad & idx = (blockIdx.y \cdot gridDim.x + blockIdx.x) \cdot (blockDim.x \cdot blockDim.y) + (threadIdx.y \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x) \\[6pt]
\text{Global thread index (3D):} \quad & idx \;=\; \text{(generalized flattening, see derivation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

These are derived and verified in **Section 3**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Value | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Total threads $N_{total}$ | $N_{total} = gridDim.x \times blockDim.x$ | Total parallel workers (dimensionless) |
| Global index (1D) | $i = blockIdx.x \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x$ | Maps $(b, t) \rightarrow i$ |
| Stride loop (grid-stride) | $i = idx + gridDim.x \cdot blockDim.x \cdot k$ | Allows processing $N > N_{total}$ |
| Warp size $W$ | $W = 32$ | Threads per warp (fixed by NVIDIA) |
| Number of warps per block | $W_{block} = \lceil blockDim.x \,/\, 32 \rceil$ | Rounded up |
| Max threads per block | $1024$ | On CC 2.0+ GPUs |
| Max blocks per grid (x) | $2^{31} - 1$ | For 1D launch on modern GPUs |
| Occupancy $\mathcal{O}$ | $\mathcal{O} = \dfrac{\text{Active warps per SM}}{\text{Max warps per SM}}$ | Dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| Memory coalescing condition | Threads in a warp access **contiguous** addresses with stride **1 word** | For maximum bandwidth |
| Sequential C time $T_C$ | $T_C = N \times t_{iter}$ | Linear in $N$ |
| Parallel CUDA time $T_{CUDA}$ | $T_{CUDA} \approx \dfrac{N}{N_{total}} \times t_{iter} + t_{overhead}$ | Sublinear (ideally) |
| Speedup $S$ | $S = T_C \,/\, T_{CUDA}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In markdown tables, **never** use the vertical pipe `\vert` symbol directly. Always use `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` inside math mode, as shown above, to keep the table parser happy.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The C vs CUDA loop comparison is the **conceptual gateway** to every GPU-accelerated application in industry:

- **Scientific simulation:** Molecular dynamics, climate modeling, CFD — code that ran for weeks on CPUs now runs in hours on GPUs.
- **Deep learning:** Every forward/backward pass in a neural network is essentially a parallel loop over matrix elements — this is why PyTorch and TensorFlow have CUDA backends.
- **Image and signal processing:** Pixel-level operations (blur, edge detection, FFT) are embarrassingly parallel.
- **Cryptography & blockchain:** Brute-force hash search is a perfect CUDA loop candidate.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Global Thread Index from (blockIdx, threadIdx) — 1D Case

We have `gridDim.x = G` blocks and `blockDim.x = B` threads per block. We need a **bijection** (one-to-one mapping) from each unique pair $(blockIdx.x, threadIdx.x)$ to a unique global index $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, G \cdot B - 1\}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the counting structure.**
- Block 0 contains threads 0, 1, 2, ..., B−1.
- Block 1 contains threads B, B+1, B+2, ..., 2B−1.
- Block $b$ contains threads $b \cdot B, b \cdot B + 1, \dots, (b+1) \cdot B - 1$.

**Step 2 — Express the start offset of block $b$.**
The first thread in block $b$ has global index equal to the number of threads in all previous blocks:

$$
\text{offset}(b) = b \cdot B = blockIdx.x \cdot blockDim.x
$$

**Step 3 — Add the intra-block offset.**
Within block $b$, the thread with local index $t$ is at position $t$ relative to the block's start:

$$
\text{local offset} = threadIdx.x
$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**
Summing the two offsets gives the unique global index:

$$
\boxed{\,i = blockIdx.x \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with an example.**
Let `blockDim.x = 4`, `blockIdx.x = 3`, `threadIdx.x = 2`:

$$
i = 3 \times 4 + 2 = 14
$$

Check: Block 0 → indices 0,1,2,3. Block 1 → 4,5,6,7. Block 2 → 8,9,10,11. Block 3 → **12,13,14,15**. Thread 2 of block 3 is index 14. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: 2D Global Index from (blockIdx, threadIdx) — 2D Case

For a 2D launch, each block has dimensions `(blockDim.x, blockDim.y)` and the grid has `(gridDim.x, gridDim.y)`.

**Step 1 — Total threads per block.**
A 2D block contains:

$$
B_{2D} = blockDim.x \times blockDim.y
$$

**Step 2 — Block offset in the grid.**
Block `(blockIdx.x, blockIdx.y)` sits in row `blockIdx.y` and column `blockIdx.x` of the grid. Each row of the grid contains `gridDim.x` blocks. So the **linearized block index** is:

$$
b_{flat} = blockIdx.y \cdot gridDim.x + blockIdx.x
$$

**Step 3 — Local thread offset inside the block.**
A 2D block of shape `(blockDim.x, blockDim.y)` is stored in **row-major order** in CUDA's mental model: rows stack vertically. Thread `(threadIdx.x, threadIdx.y)` is at row `threadIdx.y`, and within a row each thread takes `threadIdx.x` positions:

$$
t_{flat} = threadIdx.y \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x
$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$
\boxed{\,i = b_{flat} \cdot B_{2D} + t_{flat} = (blockIdx.y \cdot gridDim.x + blockIdx.x) \cdot (blockDim.x \cdot blockDim.y) + (threadIdx.y \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x)\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify.**
Let `gridDim = (4, 3)`, `blockDim = (2, 2)`. Block `(1, 2)`, thread `(1, 0)`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{flat} &= 2 \times 4 + 1 = 9 \\
B_{2D} &= 2 \times 2 = 4 \\
t_{flat} &= 0 \times 2 + 1 = 1 \\
i &= 9 \times 4 + 1 = 37
\end{aligned}
$$

Cross-check: Rows 0,1,2 of blocks. Block (1,2) is in row 2, column 1. Row 0 has blocks (0,0)..(3,0) → flat 0..3. Row 1 → 4..7. Row 2 → 8..11, so block (1,2) has flat block index 9. Inside that block (4 threads, row-major: 0,1 / 2,3), thread (1,0) is local index 1. Global: 9×4 + 1 = 37. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Grid-Stride Loop (for $N > N_{total}$)

Often $N$ (the data size) is **larger** than $gridDim.x \cdot blockDim.x$. A **grid-stride loop** is the CUDA idiom that lets each thread process **multiple elements**:

**Step 1 — Compute the global stride.**

$$
\text{stride} = gridDim.x \cdot blockDim.x
$$

**Step 2 — Each thread starts at its own index and steps by stride.**

$$
i = (blockIdx.x \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x) + k \cdot \text{stride}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots
$$

**Step 3 — Loop bound.** The thread continues while:

$$
i < N
$$

**Step 4 — Why is this efficient?**
This pattern allows the programmer to **launch fewer threads** (e.g., 1024) but still process **millions of elements**. The hardware overhead of launching is amortized over many iterations per thread, and **memory accesses remain coalesced** because consecutive threads in a warp still access consecutive indices in the first iteration.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Speedup Bound (Amdahl's Law Touchpoint)

Sequential time:

$$
T_C = N \times t_{iter}
$$

Parallel time (ignoring overhead, perfect scaling):

$$
T_{CUDA} = \frac{N}{N_{total}} \times t_{iter}
$$

Theoretical speedup:

$$
S = \frac{T_C}{T_{CUDA}} = \frac{N \times t_{iter}}{(N / N_{total}) \times t_{iter}} = N_{total} = gridDim.x \cdot blockDim.x
$$

In practice, with overhead $t_{oh}$ and serial fraction $f$:

$$
S \approx \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/N_{total} + t_{oh}/T_C}
$$

This shows why $N_{total}$ should be **large** (at least $10^4$–$10^6$ threads) to amortize launch overhead.
