# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain how a `for` loop in a CUDA host program differs in purpose from a `for` loop in a sequential C program.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A `for` loop in a **sequential C program** physically executes the loop body on the CPU, one iteration at a time, with the loop counter being incremented and tested in software (1 mark). In contrast, a `for` loop in a **CUDA host program** is used only to **configure the launch parameters** (number of blocks and threads per block) for a kernel (1 mark). The actual parallelism is implemented **physically on the GPU** by hardware-instantiated threads, each computing its own index using `blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x` (1 mark). Hence, the host `for` loop in CUDA is a **declarative launch scaffold**, not a sequential execution construct.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the global thread index formula in a 1D CUDA kernel launch with `blockDim.x` threads per block and `gridDim.x` blocks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The global thread index is given by:
- **Statement (1 mark):** $i = blockIdx.x \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x$
- **Explanation (1 mark):** $blockIdx.x \cdot blockDim.x$ gives the offset of the current block from the start (i.e., the number of threads in all preceding blocks), and `threadIdx.x` gives the local offset of the current thread within its block.
- **Significance (1 mark):** This formula provides a unique, bijective mapping from each $(blockIdx.x, threadIdx.x)$ pair to a unique element index $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, gridDim.x \cdot blockDim.x - 1\}$, allowing each thread to access its own data element.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** the host-side `for` loop (which sets up the launch) with the **implicit per-thread work** on the device. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this mix-up.
> - **Always show the indexing formula derivation step-by-step** in long answers; a one-line answer gets only 1 mark out of 7.
> - **Mention the warp size (32)** and SIMT execution at least once — it is a high-yield keyword.
> - **Memory coalescing** is a frequent sub-part. If you skip it, expect −2 marks.
> - Students often write "CUDA uses a `for` loop" without distinguishing **host** vs **device** — this is the single most common error.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** With a neat diagram, explain the CUDA thread hierarchy (Grid → Block → Warp → Thread). Compare this with how a sequential C `for` loop organizes its iterations. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write a CUDA kernel to add two vectors $A$ and $B$ of size $N = 1024$ into vector $C$. Show the corresponding C sequential code and the CUDA host-side launch code. Derive the global index formula used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**a) Thread hierarchy & comparison (7 marks):**

The CUDA execution hierarchy is a four-level structure:

- **Grid (1 mark):** The entire set of thread blocks launched by a single kernel call. Sized by `gridDim.x`.
- **Block (1 mark):** A group of up to 1024 threads that can synchronize via `__syncthreads()` and share fast on-chip memory. Sized by `blockDim.x`.
- **Warp (2 marks):** A group of exactly 32 threads scheduled together for SIMT execution. A block of 256 threads contains 8 warps. All threads in a warp execute the **same instruction** on **different data** in lockstep.
- **Thread (1 mark):** The smallest execution unit; one thread = one parallel worker.

**Comparison with C `for` loop (2 marks):**
| Aspect | C `for` loop | CUDA |
|---|---|---|
| Execution unit | One CPU thread, sequential iterations | Many GPU threads, parallel |
| Counter | `i++` updated in software | Index computed from `blockIdx` and `threadIdx` |
| Iteration control | Loop branches in registers | Hardware-scheduled warps |
| Workload scaling | Linear in $N$ | Distributed across $N_{total}$ threads |

(Refer to the **Mermaid diagrams in Section 4** for visual support.)

---

**b) Vector addition code & formula derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — C sequential code (2 marks):**
```c
void vectorAdd(int N, float *A, float *B, float *C) {
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        C[i] = A[i] + B[i];
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — CUDA kernel (device code) (2 marks):**
```c
__global__ void vectorAddKernel(float *A, float *B, float *C, int N) {
    int i = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x;  // global index
    if (i < N) {
        C[i] = A[i] + B[i];
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Host-side launch code (1 mark):**
```c
int threadsPerBlock = 256;
int blocksPerGrid   = (N + threadsPerBlock - 1) / threadsPerBlock;  // = 4 for N=1024
vectorAddKernel<<<blocksPerGrid, threadsPerBlock>>>(A, B, C, N);
```

**Step 4 — Derivation of the index formula (2 marks):**
Let block $b$ have $B$ threads. The first thread in block $b$ has global index $b \cdot B$ (number of threads in all earlier blocks). Thread $t$ within block $b$ has local offset $t$. Adding:

$$
i = b \cdot B + t = blockIdx.x \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x
$$

For $N = 1024$, `threadsPerBlock = 256`, so `blocksPerGrid = 4`. Thread 0 of block 0 handles $i = 0$; thread 0 of block 1 handles $i = 256$; thread 255 of block 3 handles $i = 3 \times 256 + 255 = 1023$. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Internal Choice)

**a)** Explain the concept of the **grid-stride loop** in CUDA. Why is it used when $N > gridDim.x \cdot blockDim.x$? Derive the index expression. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Compare and contrast a sequential C `for` loop and a CUDA kernel-based parallel loop in terms of: (i) execution model, (ii) memory access pattern, (iii) control flow, (iv) scalability, and (v) overhead. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**a) Grid-stride loop (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** A grid-stride loop is a CUDA idiom where each thread processes **multiple data elements** by stepping through the data array using a **stride** equal to the total number of threads in the grid. This decouples the number of launched threads from the problem size.

**Why it is used (2 marks):**
- Launching one thread per data element for very large $N$ (e.g., $10^9$) is wasteful because the GPU has limited resources.
- The grid-stride loop lets you launch a **fixed, modest** number of threads (e.g., 1024) and reuse them to process all $N$ elements.
- It also enables **memory coalescing** because consecutive threads still access consecutive addresses in the first iteration.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Let $S = gridDim.x \cdot blockDim.x$ be the total number of threads. Each thread $t$ (with global index $i_0 = blockIdx.x \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x$) processes elements:

$$
i_k = i_0 + k \cdot S, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, \text{while } i_k < N
$$

**Code:**
```c
__global__ void gridStrideKernel(float *A, float *B, int N) {
    int stride = blockDim.x * gridDim.x;
    for (int i = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x; i < N; i += stride) {
        B[i] = A[i] * 2.0f;
    }
}
```

---

**b) Comparison of C and CUDA loops (7 marks):**

| Aspect | C `for` Loop (CPU) | CUDA Kernel (GPU) | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Execution model** | **SISD** — Single Instruction, Single Data per iteration. One iteration runs at a time on one core. | **SIMT/SIMD** — Single Instruction, Multiple Threads. Thousands of threads run the same instruction on different data in warps of 32. | (1.5) |
| **(ii) Memory access pattern** | Each iteration reads/writes one element from **main memory** (cache-line friendly but sequential). | Threads in a warp ideally access **contiguous addresses** — **memory coalescing** — fetching one cache line for 32 threads in a single transaction. | (1.5) |
| **(iii) Control flow** | `for`, `if`, `break` are software branches executed in CPU pipeline; mispredictions cause stalls. | Warp-level divergence (`if` inside a kernel) causes threads in a warp to **serialize** — costly. No host loop counter; index is computed. | (1.5) |
| **(iv) Scalability** | Limited by CPU core count (typically 4–32). Adding iterations increases time **linearly**. | Scales to **millions of threads** on a GPU; adding data only requires changing grid size. Near-linear speedup up to GPU occupancy limits. | (1.5) |
| **(v) Overhead** | Negligible per-iteration overhead. | Kernel launch overhead ($\sim$5–10 µs), memory transfer between host and device (PCIe/NVLink), thread creation cost. Must be amortized over enough work. | (1.0) |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **C `for` loop = sequential, runs on CPU, one iteration at a time.**
- **CUDA "loop" in host = launch configuration only; the actual parallel work is done by hardware-instantiated threads.**
- **The kernel function** (marked `__global__`) replaces the loop body; it runs **once per thread**, not once per iteration.
- **Global thread index (1D):** $i = blockIdx.x \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x$.
- **Global thread index (2D):** $i = (blockIdx.y \cdot gridDim.x + blockIdx.x) \cdot (blockDim.x \cdot blockDim.y) + (threadIdx.y \cdot blockDim.x + threadIdx.x)$.
- **Warp size = 32** (SIMT execution unit). Always a multiple of 32 for `blockDim.x` to avoid wasted lanes.
- **Max threads per block = 1024** on modern NVIDIA GPUs.
- **Memory coalescing**: consecutive threads accessing consecutive addresses → 1 transaction per warp; strided access → 32 transactions (32× slower).
- **Grid-stride loop**: enables processing $N > gridDim.x \cdot blockDim.x$ with a fixed launch.
- **Host–Device memory transfer** is expensive — keep data on the GPU across multiple kernel launches.
- **Avoid warp divergence** inside kernels (no `if` branches that split a warp).
- **Launch configuration formula**: `blocks = (N + threadsPerBlock − 1) / threadsPerBlock`.
- **Speedup is bounded by Amdahl's law** — the serial fraction (data transfer, launch overhead) limits gains.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always draw the **grid → block → warp → thread** diagram in long answers; it earns easy marks.
- **KTU exam tip:** When writing CUDA code, **always** include the boundary check `if (i < N)` to handle $N$ not being a multiple of `blockDim`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NVIDIA GPU Memory Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** NVIDIA GPU Memory Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **NVIDIA GPU Memory Structure** refers to the multi-tiered, heterogeneous memory subsystem present inside every modern NVIDIA GPU (General Processing Unit) based on the **CUDA (Compute Unified Device Architecture)** programming model. It is a hierarchical collection of on-chip and off-chip memory spaces, each engineered with distinct capacity, latency, bandwidth, scope (visibility to threads), and lifetime characteristics. The hierarchy is designed to expose massive data-level parallelism (DLP) and thread-level parallelism (TLP) by giving the programmer explicit control over data placement and movement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST528 Module 3):**
> *"The GPU memory structure comprises a hierarchy of memory spaces — Registers, Local Memory, Shared Memory, Constant Memory, Texture Memory, Global Memory, L1/L2 Caches, and Texture Cache — each differing in scope, lifetime, access speed, and visibility to threads. Programmers must explicitly orchestrate data movement across this hierarchy to achieve high throughput on highly-parallel kernels."*

The key memory spaces, in order of decreasing speed and increasing capacity, are:

1. **Registers** — Per-thread, fastest storage (~1 cycle).
2. **Shared Memory** — Per-Streaming-Multiprocessor (SM) on-chip SRAM (~20–100 cycles).
3. **L1 / Texture / Constant Cache** — Per-SM on-chip cache, read-only data path.
4. **L2 Cache** — Unified across all SMs on the GPU die (~200 cycles).
5. **Global Memory (DRAM / HBM / GDDR)** — Off-chip, largest, slowest (~400–800 cycles).
6. **Local Memory** — Logical per-thread storage physically residing in Global Memory (used for register spilling, large structs, arrays).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The GPU as a Giant Office Workspace"

Imagine a **massive office building with 10,000 interns** (the GPU threads), all working on the same huge project (the parallel kernel). To keep them productive, the office manager (NVIDIA's memory hierarchy) gives them **different types of desks and cabinets**:

- **The Intern's Notebook (Registers)** → Ultra-fast, but tiny. Each intern has their own private notebook that only they can touch. A single flip of the page is instant.
- **The Team Whiteboard (Shared Memory)** → A large whiteboard shared by all interns sitting at the same desk pod (one SM). It's 100× faster than the central library, but only this team's interns can write on it. They must coordinate to avoid stepping on each other's toes.
- **The Department Notice Board (Constant Memory)** → Read-only boards with company rules. They are aggressively cached and broadcast — every intern can read them at the same speed, but no one can write to them mid-meeting.
- **The Central Library (Global Memory / HBM)** → The huge main library in the basement. It holds terabytes of data, but walking down there takes time. Interns must walk in groups (coalesced access) to be efficient — one intern fetching a single book at a time is slow.
- **The Personal Locker at the Library (Local Memory)** → When an intern's notebook overflows, the extra pages are stored at the library under the intern's name. Technically "private," but physically very slow.
- **The Floor-Manager's Index Card System (L1 / L2 Cache)** → A small, ultra-fast index of recently-used books, kept close to where interns are working. Hits are nearly free; misses require a library trip.

This hierarchical design is the **key reason GPUs outperform CPUs on data-parallel workloads** — by trading single-thread latency for massive bandwidth and parallelism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physical Constants & Typical Metrics (NVIDIA Ampere A100 example):**
> - **HBM2e Global Memory Bandwidth:** ~**1.5 TB/s – 2.0 TB/s**
> - **L2 Cache Size:** ~**40 MB** (unified)
> - **Shared Memory per SM:** up to **164 KB** (configurable with L1)
> - **Registers per SM:** **65,536 × 32-bit**
> - **Max Threads per SM:** **2048** (Ampere)
> - **Memory Clock vs Core Clock:** typically **2:1 ratio** (memory runs half as fast as shader cores)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Memory Space Decomposition

#### 2.1.1 Registers (Per-Thread, On-Chip)
- **Scope:** Private to a single thread.
- **Lifetime:** Exists only for the duration of that thread.
- **Access Time:** ~**1 cycle** (basically free).
- **Capacity:** Limited (~**255 registers per thread** in CUDA, hardware-dependent).
- **Usage:** Compiler allocates automatically for local scalars, loop indices, and intermediate values.
- **Spilling:** When a kernel uses more registers than the hardware allows, the compiler **spills** excess values into **Local Memory** (which resides in slow DRAM). This is called **register pressure** and severely hurts performance.

#### 2.1.2 Shared Memory (Per-Block, On-Chip SRAM)
- **Scope:** Visible to all threads within the same thread block.
- **Lifetime:** Exists from block launch to block termination.
- **Access Time:** ~**20–30 cycles** (similar speed to L1).
- **Capacity:** Up to **48 KB – 164 KB per SM** (configurable).
- **Organization:** Divided into **32 banks**, each **4 bytes wide** (warp-wide access patterns matter).
- **Use Cases:** Inter-thread communication within a block, tile-based matrix multiplication, reduction operations, transpose kernels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bank Conflict Rule:** A **bank conflict** occurs when 2 or more threads in a warp access different addresses in the **same bank** (but different 32-bit words). This serializes the access (k-fold slowdown for k conflicts). Accessing the **same address** results in a *broadcast* (no conflict). Accessing different banks results in *no conflict* (single-cycle access).

#### 2.1.3 Constant Memory (Read-Only, Off-Chip + Cached)
- **Scope:** Readable by all threads (and all blocks); writeable only by the host CPU.
- **Lifetime:** Kernel launch to kernel end (stays in DRAM but cached in the **Constant Cache**).
- **Access Time:** **1 cycle** if cached (broadcast to all threads in warp), otherwise DRAM speed.
- **Capacity:** **64 KB** total per device (Constant Cache portion).
- **Use Cases:** Pre-computed lookup tables, mathematical constants, neural-network weights, transformation matrices.

#### 2.1.4 Texture Memory (Read-Only, Cached, Spatially-Optimized)
- **Scope:** Read-only from kernel; writeable only by host.
- **Lifetime:** Kernel lifetime.
- **Access Time:** Cached in the **Texture Cache** (~few cycles).
- **Capacity:** DRAM-backed.
- **Key Property:** Optimized for **2D spatial locality** — hardware performs **bilinear interpolation**, **clamping**, and **boundary handling** automatically. Ideal for image-processing kernels.

#### 2.1.5 Global Memory (Off-Chip DRAM / HBM / GDDR6)
- **Scope:** Readable/writable by all threads, all blocks, and the host.
- **Lifetime:** Persists across kernel launches.
- **Access Time:** **~400–800 cycles** uncached.
- **Capacity:** **40 GB – 80 GB** on modern data-center GPUs.
- **Use Cases:** Main input/output buffers, large data structures, intermediate results that must survive multiple kernel invocations.
- **Coalescing:** Threads in a warp should access **contiguous 32-byte, 64-byte, or 128-byte aligned segments** to combine individual requests into a single transaction. This is the single most important performance optimization.

#### 2.1.6 Local Memory (Logical Per-Thread, Physically Global)
- **Scope:** Private to a thread.
- **Lifetime:** Thread lifetime.
- **Access Time:** Same as Global Memory (~400+ cycles).
- **Capacity:** Unlimited (backed by DRAM).
- **Use Cases:** Auto-generated for large arrays, register spills, and stack-allocated structures.

#### 2.1.7 L1 Cache (Per-SM, On-Chip)
- **Capacity:** ~**128 KB per SM** (shared with Shared Memory in a configurable ratio, e.g., 64 KB L1 / 64 KB shared on A100).
- **Access Time:** ~**20–30 cycles** on hit.
- **Line Size:** **128 bytes**.
- **Use Cases:** Automatic caching of Global Memory reads.

#### 2.1.8 L2 Cache (Device-Wide, On-Die)
- **Capacity:** **40 MB – 96 MB** (Ampere, Hopper, Ada Lovelace).
- **Access Time:** ~**200 cycles**.
- **Use Cases:** Coalesces and broadcasts data between SMs; reduces redundant DRAM accesses.

### 2.2 The CUDA Memory Hierarchy Diagram (Conceptual)

The hierarchy forms a "pyramid" of speed vs capacity trade-off:

```
Speed (Fast) ▲       Registers
              │     ┌────────────────────┐
              │     │  Shared Memory     │   Per-Block
              │     ├────────────────────┤
              │     │  L1 / Constant /   │   Per-SM
              │     │  Texture Cache     │
              │     ├────────────────────┤
              │     │       L2 Cache     │   Device-Wide
              │     ├────────────────────┤
              │     │   Global / Local   │   Off-Chip DRAM / HBM
              │     │   Memory (GDDR/HBM)│
Capacity (Big)▼     └────────────────────┘
```

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol | Description | Typical Units / Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Memory Bandwidth** | $BW$ | Rate of data transfer between two memory levels | $BW = \dfrac{\text{Data Transferred (bytes)}}{\text{Time (s)}}$ → **GB/s** or **TB/s** |
| 2 | **Effective Bandwidth (Achievable)** | $BW_{eff}$ | Bandwidth actually observed by a kernel (with overheads) | $BW_{eff} = \dfrac{2 \times N \times \text{sizeof(type)}}{T_{kernel} \times 10^{9}}$ GB/s (×2 for read+write) |
| 3 | **Coalesced Transaction Size** | $S_{trans}$ | Bytes fetched in one transaction by a warp of 32 threads | **32 B, 64 B, or 128 B** segments aligned |
| 4 | **Memory Transactions per Warp (Uncoalesced)** | $T_{warp}$ | Number of memory transactions when access is non-contiguous | $T_{warp} = \dfrac{32 \times \text{sizeof(elem)}}{\text{segment\_size}}$ |
| 5 | **Coalescing Efficiency** | $\eta_{coal}$ | Ratio of ideal transactions to actual transactions | $\eta_{coal} = \dfrac{T_{ideal}}{T_{actual}} \times 100\%$ |
| 6 | **Bank Conflict Penalty** | $P_{bank}$ | Serialization factor for shared-memory bank conflicts | $P_{bank} = k$ (k = number of conflicting threads) |
| 7 | **Shared Memory Bank Index** | $b$ | Bank number for address `A` (4-byte words) | $b = (A / 4) \bmod 32$ |
| 8 | **Theoretical Occupancy** | $O_{th}$ | Maximum active warps per SM (limited by hardware) | **64 warps / SM (Ampere)** = **2048 threads** |
| 9 | **Achieved Occupancy** | $O_{ach}$ | Active warps actually scheduled | $O_{ach} = \min(O_{th},\; O_{reg},\; O_{shm},\; O_{blk})$ |
| 10 | **Occupancy Limit by Registers** | $O_{reg}$ | $\min\!\left(64,\;\left\lfloor\dfrac{65536}{\text{regs\_per\_thread} \times 32}\right\rfloor\right)$ | warps/SM |
| 11 | **Occupancy Limit by Shared Memory** | $O_{shm}$ | $\min\!\left(64,\;\left\lfloor\dfrac{\text{shm\_per\_SM}}{\text{shm\_per\_block} \times \text{blocks\_per\_SM}}\right\rfloor\right)$ | warps/SM |
| 12 | **Memory Latency (DRAM)** | $L_{DRAM}$ | Time to fetch from off-chip | **~400–800 cycles** |
| 13 | **Memory Latency Hiding** | $N_{lat}$ | Threads needed to hide one DRAM access | $N_{lat} = \dfrac{L_{DRAM}}{T_{compute\_per\_thread}}$ |
| 14 | **Threads per Warp** | $N_{warp}$ | SIMT width | **32** (fixed by hardware) |
| 15 | **Warps per Block** | — | Programmer-declared block size / 32 | Typically **4 – 32 warps** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The NVIDIA GPU memory hierarchy is the foundational abstraction for **high-performance computing (HPC)**, **deep learning (CUDA, cuDNN, cuBLAS)**, **scientific simulation (climate, molecular dynamics via AMBER/GROMACS)**, **cryptocurrency mining**, **real-time graphics rendering (ray tracing, DLSS)**, and **autonomous vehicle perception**. Mastery of memory coalescing, shared-memory bank conflicts, and occupancy tuning is what separates a 10× kernel from a 100× kernel in production CUDA code.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Memory Bandwidth of a Kernel

**Problem Setup:** A CUDA kernel processes a vector of $N$ single-precision floats. Each element is read once and written once. Total runtime is $T$ seconds. Compute the **effective bandwidth** $BW_{eff}$ in GB/s.

**Given:**
- $N = 10{,}000{,}000$ elements (i.e., 10M)
- Element size = 4 bytes (float)
- Runtime $T = 0.0001$ s = **100 µs**

**Step 1: Total bytes transferred per element.**

Each element is read (4 B) and written (4 B):

$$B_{elem} = 4 + 4 = 8 \text{ bytes per element}$$

**Reasoning:** The kernel performs one read-modify-write operation per element. The total I/O is twice the data size (one for read, one for write).

**Step 2: Total bytes transferred for $N$ elements.**

$$B_{total} = N \times B_{elem} = 10{,}000{,}000 \times 8 = 80{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3: Convert bytes to gigabytes (SI: $10^9$) and divide by runtime.**

$$BW_{eff} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{T \times 10^{9}} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^{6}}{0.0001 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$BW_{eff} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^{6}}{10^{5}} = 800 \text{ GB/s}$$

**Step 4: Compare to peak HBM2e bandwidth (~1500 GB/s on A100).**

$$\eta_{utilization} = \dfrac{BW_{eff}}{BW_{peak}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{800}{1500} \times 100\% \approx 53.3\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> A well-tuned production kernel should achieve **70% – 90%** of peak HBM bandwidth. A 53% figure suggests scope for further optimization (e.g., better coalescing, larger block size, or loop unrolling).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Coalescing Efficiency for a Strided Access Pattern

**Problem Setup:** A warp of 32 threads accesses a global `float` array with **stride 2** (i.e., thread $i$ accesses `A[2*i]`). The cache line segment size is **128 bytes**. Compute the **number of memory transactions** required and the **coalescing efficiency**.

**Step 1: Compute bytes requested by the warp.**

$$B_{req} = 32 \text{ threads} \times 4 \text{ bytes/thread} = 128 \text{ bytes}$$

**Reasoning:** Each thread requests one float (4 B). The warp requests 128 B total — exactly one cache line.

**Step 2: Compute bytes fetched by hardware transactions.**

Because of stride 2, the 32 addresses fall into **two disjoint 128-byte segments**:

$$A[0], A[2], A[4], \ldots, A[62] \quad \text{→ 1st segment}$$
$$A[64], A[66], A[68], \ldots, A[126] \quad \text{→ 2nd segment}$$

So 2 cache lines × 128 B = 256 B must be fetched, but only 128 B is "useful."

**Step 3: Compute number of memory transactions.**

$$T_{actual} = 2 \text{ transactions (two 128-B segments)}$$

**Step 4: Compute ideal transactions.**

$$T_{ideal} = 1 \text{ transaction (one 128-B segment)}$$

**Step 5: Compute coalescing efficiency.**

$$\eta_{coal} = \dfrac{T_{ideal}}{T_{actual}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Result:** A stride-2 access pattern gives **50% coalescing efficiency** — half the bandwidth is wasted. This is why **AoS (Array-of-Structs)** layouts often underperform **SoA (Struct-of-Arrays)** layouts on GPUs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Shared-Memory Bank Conflict Analysis

**Problem Setup:** A warp of 32 threads accesses `__shared__ float sm[32][32]` via the pattern `sm[threadIdx.x][0]`. The shared memory has **32 banks of 4 bytes each**. Compute the **bank-conflict penalty**.

**Step 1: Map each thread's access to a bank.**

For a `float sm[32][32]`, the address of `sm[i][j]` (in 4-byte words) is:

$$A = i \times 32 + j$$

The bank index is:

$$b = A \bmod 32 = (i \times 32 + j) \bmod 32 = j \bmod 32$$

Since $j = 0$ for all threads, every thread maps to **bank 0**.

**Step 2: Classify the access pattern.**

All 32 threads target the **same bank but the same address** (since `sm[0][0] = sm[1][0] = ... = sm[31][0]`? — actually no, these are distinct addresses with the same bank).

Wait — the addresses `sm[0][0], sm[1][0], ..., sm[31][0]` correspond to words $0, 32, 64, \ldots, 992$. These are **all in bank 0** but are **distinct addresses**.

**Step 3: Apply bank-conflict rule.**

Number of distinct addresses hitting bank 0:

$$k = 32$$

Since $k > 1$ and addresses are distinct, this is a **32-way bank conflict** → access is serialized 32 times.

$$P_{bank} = 32$$

**Effective latency:** $32 \times T_{shared} = 32 \times 1$ cycle = **32 cycles** instead of 1.

**Step 4: Fix by adding a stride (padding).**

Declare `sm[32][33]` (one extra column). The address becomes:

$$A = i \times 33 + j \Rightarrow b = (i \times 33 + j) \bmod 32 = (i + j) \bmod 32$$

For $j=0$, $b = i \bmod 32$ — all 32 banks, no conflict! 

$$P_{bank} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Theoretical Occupancy from Register Usage

**Problem Setup:** A kernel uses **48 registers per thread**, and the GPU (Ampere A100) has **65,536 32-bit registers per SM** and supports up to **2,048 threads per SM**. Each block has **256 threads**. Compute the **achievable occupancy**.

**Step 1: Compute maximum threads limited by registers.**

$$N_{reg} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{65{,}536}{48} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 1365.33 \right\rfloor = 1365 \text{ threads/SM}$$

**Step 2: Compute maximum threads limited by hardware.**

$$N_{hw} = 2048 \text{ threads/SM}$$

**Step 3: Compute active blocks per SM (rounded down).**

$$B_{SM} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1365}{256} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 5.33 \right\rfloor = 5 \text{ blocks/SM}$$

**Step 4: Compute achievable active threads.**

$$N_{ach} = 5 \times 256 = 1280 \text{ threads/SM}$$

**Step 5: Compute occupancy percentage.**

$$O_{ach} = \dfrac{1280}{2048} \times 100\% \approx 62.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compiler Flag Tip:** Compile with `-maxrregcount=32` to artificially lower register usage and increase occupancy, at the cost of more local-memory spills. The optimal point is workload-dependent.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Memory Access Patterns and Their Performance Impact

| Access Pattern | Memory Type | Coalesced? | Bank Conflict? | Effective BW | Speedup vs Naive |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Contiguous `A[i]` | Global | ✅ Yes | N/A | **~100%** | **8×** |
| Stride-2 `A[2*i]` | Global | ❌ No | N/A | **~50%** | 1× (baseline) |
| Random scatter `A[r[i]]` | Global | ❌ No | N/A | **~5–15%** | 0.1× (worse) |
| Row-major access | Shared | ✅ Yes | **None** (banks distributed) | **~100%** | 4× |
| Column-major access | Shared | ✅ Yes (warp-wise) | **32-way conflict** | **~3%** | 0.25× |
| Padded column-major (sm[32][33]) | Shared | ✅ Yes | **None** (banks distributed) | **~100%** | 4× |
| Same-address read | Constant | ✅ Broadcast | N/A | **~100%** | 1× (already optimal) |

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: HBM Bandwidth Limit for a Matrix Multiply

**Problem:** Multiply two $N \times N$ single-precision matrices. Compute the minimum theoretical memory-traffic time on an A100 (peak HBM BW = **1.5 TB/s = 1500 GB/s**).

**Step 1: Memory traffic per kernel.**

Read A: $N^2$ floats → $4N^2$ bytes
Read B: $N^2$ floats → $4N^2$ bytes
Write C: $N^2$ floats → $4N^2$ bytes
Total: $12N^2$ bytes

**Step 2: For $N = 4096$:**

$$B_{total} = 12 \times (4096)^2 = 12 \times 16.78 \times 10^6 \approx 201.3 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 3: Compute time at peak bandwidth.**

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{201.3 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}}{1500 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes/s}} \approx 1.34 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 134\ \mu s$$

**Step 4: Compare to cuBLAS SGEMM (which actually runs in ~80 µs).**

cuBLAS exceeds peak HBM because it uses **shared-memory tiling** to keep data on-chip and reuses loaded tiles, achieving **arithmetic intensity > memory-bound regime**.

$$\text{Arithmetic Intensity} = \dfrac{2N^3 \text{ FLOPs}}{12N^2 \text{ bytes}} = \dfrac{N}{6} \text{ FLOPs/byte}$$

For $N = 4096$: $AI = 4096/6 \approx 683$ FLOPs/byte — well into the **compute-bound regime**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: NVIDIA GPU Memory Hierarchy & Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HW[Host CPU and DRAM]
    PCIE[PCIe Gen4 or Gen5 Bus]

    subgraph GPUDEV["NVIDIA GPU Device"]
        L2["L2 Cache 40MB to 96MB On-Die Unified"]
        DRAM["Global Memory HBM2e or GDDR6X Off-Chip 40GB to 80GB"]
        CTRL["DRAM Memory Controller"]
    end

    subgraph SM0["Streaming Multiprocessor SM 0"]
        SCHED["Warp Scheduler and Dispatch Unit 32 Threads per Warp"]
        REG0["Register File 65536 x 32-bit Per Thread"]
        SHM0["Shared Memory 100KB On-Chip SRAM 32 Banks x 4B"]
        L1A["L1 Cache 128B lines Read-Write"]
        LCA["Constant Cache 64KB Read-Only Broadcast"]
        TXCA["Texture Cache Read-Only 2D Spatial"]
    end

    subgraph SM1["Streaming Multiprocessor SM 1"]
        SCHED1["Warp Scheduler"]
        REG1["Register File"]
        SHM1["Shared Memory 32 Banks"]
        L1B["L1 Cache"]
        LCB["Constant Cache"]
        TXCB["Texture Cache"]
    end

    subgraph SM2["Streaming Multiprocessor SM N-1"]
        SCHED2["Warp Scheduler"]
        REG2["Register File"]
        SHM2["Shared Memory"]
        L1C["L1 Cache"]
        LCC["Constant Cache"]
        TXCC["Texture Cache"]
    end

    HW -->|DMA Transfer cudaMemcpy| PCIE
    PCIE --> DRAM
    DRAM --> CTRL
    CTRL <--> L2

    L2 -->|Cache Fill| L1A
    L2 -->|Cache Fill| L1B
    L2 -->|Cache Fill| L1C

    SCHED --> REG0
    SCHED --> SHM0
    SCHED --> L1A
    SCHED --> LCA
    SCHED --> TXCA

    SCHED1 --> REG1
    SCHED1 --> SHM1
    SCHED1 --> L1B
    SCHED1 --> LCB
    SCHED1 --> TXCB

    SCHED2 --> REG2
    SCHED2 --> SHM2
    SCHED2 --> L1C
    SCHED2 --> LCC
    SCHED2 --> TXCC

    DRAM -.->|Register Spill| REG0
    DRAM -.->|Local Memory| REG1

    classDef fast fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slow fill:#90a4ae,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef host fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class REG0,REG1,REG2,SHM0,SHM1,SHM2 fast
    class L1A,L1B,L1C,LCA,LCB,LCC,TXCA,TXCB,TXCC,L2 mid
    class DRAM,CTRL slow
    class HW,PCIE host
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Data-Path During a Kernel Launch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Thread 0] -->|reads reg| W1[Warp 0]
    A2[Thread 1] --> W1
    A3[Thread 2] --> W1
    A4[Thread 31] --> W1

    W1 -->|ld.global ptr| L1X[L1 and L2 Cache Lookup]
    L1X -->|miss| GMX[Global Memory HBM Transaction 128B]
    L1X -->|hit| FAST[Fast Path 1 to 30 cycles]

    GMX -->|fills| L1X
    L1X -->|data| SHMX[Shared Memory Stage Optional]
    SHMX -->|__syncthreads barrier| W1

    B1[Block 0 Warps 0 to 7] --> SMX[SM 0]
    B2[Block 1 Warps 0 to 7] --> SMX
    B3[Block 2 Warps 0 to 7] --> SMX

    SMX -->|atomic or coalesced writeback| L2X[L2 Cache]
    L2X -->|write-allocate| HBMX[Global Memory HBM]

    classDef thread fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef warp fill:#fff59d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef cache fill:#81d4fa,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef dram fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef sm fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#333,color:#000

    class A1,A2,A3,A4 thread
    class W1,B1,B2,B3 warp
    class L1X,L2X,SHMX cache
    class GMX,HBMX dram
    class SMX sm
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Memory-Coalescing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is access pattern contiguous 32-byte aligned}
    Q1 -- Yes --> P1[1 Memory Transaction 128B Fully Coalesced]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is access pattern strided with stride equals 1 element}
    Q2 -- Yes, Stride 1 --> P2[2 Transactions 50 percent Efficiency]
    Q2 -- No, Stride equals 0 --> Q3{Do all threads access the same address}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Broadcast from Constant Cache 1 Cycle]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is the access random or indirect}
    Q4 -- Yes --> P4[Up to 32 Transactions 3 percent Efficiency Severe Penalty]
    Q4 -- No, Structured --> P5[Restructure to SoA Layout or Use Shared Memory Tiling]

    classDef good fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000

    class P1,P3 good
    class P2,P5 mid
    class P4 bad
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** *Differentiate between the different memory spaces available in the NVIDIA CUDA memory hierarchy in terms of scope, lifetime, and access speed.*

**Model Answer:**

The NVIDIA CUDA memory hierarchy consists of six logical memory spaces with the following distinctions:

| Memory | Scope | Lifetime | Access Speed | Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Registers** | Per-thread | Thread | ~1 cycle | On-chip (RF) |
| **Shared Memory** | Per-block | Block | ~20–30 cycles | On-chip SRAM |
| **Constant Memory** | All threads | Kernel | ~1 cycle (cached) | Off-chip + cache |
| **Texture Memory** | All threads | Kernel | ~few cycles (cached) | Off-chip + cache |
| **Global Memory** | All threads | Application | ~400–800 cycles | Off-chip DRAM/HBM |
| **Local Memory** | Per-thread | Thread | ~400–800 cycles | Off-chip (DRAM-backed) |

The hierarchy is arranged to balance **speed, capacity, and programmability** — faster memories are smaller and more restricted in scope. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q2.** *What is meant by a "coalesced" memory access in CUDA? Why is it critical for performance on NVIDIA GPUs?*

**Model Answer:**

A **coalesced memory access** occurs when all 32 threads in a warp access **contiguous, aligned memory addresses** within a single 128-byte (or 64-byte / 32-byte) segment. The hardware then satisfies the entire warp's request in **one memory transaction**.

It is critical for performance because:
1. **It minimizes the number of DRAM transactions** — uncoalesced access can require up to 32× more transactions.
2. **It maximizes effective bandwidth utilization** — modern GPUs like the A100 can deliver ~1.5 TB/s only with coalesced patterns.
3. **It reduces DRAM contention and energy consumption** — fewer transactions = lower power.

Example: A stride-1 `A[threadIdx.x + blockIdx.x * blockDim.x]` pattern is coalesced; a stride-2 `A[2 * threadIdx.x]` is not. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, describe the NVIDIA GPU memory hierarchy. List the various memory spaces, their scope, and access speeds.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *Explain the concept of memory coalescing in CUDA. A warp of 32 threads accesses a `float` array `A[1024]` in the following two ways:*
   - *Pattern 1: `A[threadIdx.x]` (stride 1)*
   - *Pattern 2: `A[2 * threadIdx.x]` (stride 2)*
   *Assuming a 128-byte cache-line segment, compute the number of memory transactions required in each case and the corresponding coalescing efficiency.* **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

The NVIDIA GPU memory hierarchy is a **multi-tiered pyramid** of on-chip and off-chip memories:

1. **Registers** — Per-thread, ~1 cycle access, used for scalar locals.
2. **Shared Memory** — Per-block on-chip SRAM, ~20–30 cycles, used for inter-thread cooperation.
3. **L1 / Texture / Constant Cache** — Per-SM, ~20–30 cycles, read-mostly paths.
4. **L2 Cache** — Device-wide on-die, ~200 cycles, shared by all SMs.
5. **Global Memory** — Off-chip HBM/GDDR, ~400–800 cycles, large capacity.
6. **Local Memory** — Logical per-thread storage, physically in Global Memory (used for spills).

[Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the full block representation.] **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Pattern 1 — Stride 1 (Coalesced):**

Threads $0, 1, 2, \ldots, 31$ access `A[0], A[1], A[2], \ldots, A[31]`.

Each thread requests 4 B. The warp requests $32 \times 4 = 128$ B — exactly one 128-B segment.

$$T_{actual} = 1 \text{ transaction}$$
$$T_{ideal} = 1 \text{ transaction}$$
$$\eta_{coal} = \dfrac{1}{1} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Pattern 2 — Stride 2 (Uncoalesced):**

Threads $0, 1, 2, \ldots, 31$ access `A[0], A[2], A[4], \ldots, A[62]`.

These addresses span $[0, 252]$ in byte offsets, crossing **two** 128-B segments:

$$T_{actual} = 2 \text{ transactions}$$
$$T_{ideal} = 1 \text{ transaction}$$
$$\eta_{coal} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Conclusion:** The stride-2 pattern wastes half the bandwidth. Restructuring to an SoA layout or transposing the access pattern (e.g., accessing `A[threadIdx.x * 2]` over a 64-element block) recovers full coalescing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Explain Shared Memory bank conflicts in CUDA. How are they detected and resolved? Illustrate with the example of a 32×32 shared memory array accessed column-wise. Propose a fix.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *A CUDA kernel processes a vector of $N = 50{,}000{,}000$ `double` elements. Each element is read, squared, and written back. The kernel takes 0.4 seconds to complete. Compute the effective memory bandwidth achieved. Comment on whether this is good utilization for a typical HBM2e GPU.* **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

Shared memory is organized as **32 banks of 4 bytes each**. The bank index for a 4-byte word at address $A$ is:

$$b = A \bmod 32$$

A **bank conflict** occurs when 2+ threads in a warp access **different addresses in the same bank** (serialization by $k$ where $k$ is the conflict degree). Accessing the **same address** results in a *broadcast* (no conflict).

**Column-wise access of `__shared__ float sm[32][32]`:**

For `sm[threadIdx.x][0]`, the word address is $A = \text{threadIdx.x} \times 32 + 0$. The bank index is $b = (32 \cdot t) \bmod 32 = 0$ for **all 32 threads**. They access **32 distinct addresses** all in **bank 0** → **32-way bank conflict** → latency = 32 cycles instead of 1.

**Fix — Padding the second dimension:**

Declare `__shared__ float sm[32][33]`. Now $A = t \times 33 + 0 \Rightarrow b = t \bmod 32$ — all 32 banks used, no conflict, 1 cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Bytes per element (read + write).**

$$B_{elem} = 8 + 8 = 16 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2: Total bytes transferred.**

$$B_{total} = N \times B_{elem} = 50{,}000{,}000 \times 16 = 800{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 800 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 3: Effective bandwidth.**

$$BW_{eff} = \dfrac{800 \text{ MB}}{0.4 \text{ s}} = 2000 \text{ MB/s} = 2 \text{ GB/s}$$

**Step 4: Comment on utilization.**

For an HBM2e GPU (peak ~1500 GB/s, even an RTX-class GDDR6 GPU at ~700 GB/s):

$$\eta_{utilization} = \dfrac{2 \text{ GB/s}}{1500 \text{ GB/s}} \times 100\% \approx 0.13\%$$

This is **catastrophically low** — indicates the kernel is severely under-optimized. Likely causes: uncoalesced access, register spilling, or insufficient parallelism (occupancy near zero). A well-written kernel should achieve **>70%** of peak (~1050 GB/s on A100). **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing Local Memory with Shared Memory** — Local Memory is **per-thread but off-chip**; Shared Memory is **per-block and on-chip**. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to count write bandwidth** — $BW_{eff}$ must include both read and write traffic. Many students compute only the read traffic and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Stating "Registers are shared"** — they are **strictly per-thread**. This is a fundamental error in the CUDA programming model.
> 4. **Ignoring cache line segmentation** — coalescing analysis must specify the segment size (32 B / 64 B / 128 B). Vague answers lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Bank-conflict address calculation** — must show the formula $b = (A/4) \bmod 32$ explicitly. "It causes a conflict" without the calculation gets 0.
> 6. **Spilling Local Memory to Global Memory** — students often call register spills "cache misses." Wrong terminology costs marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ⭐ **Six Memory Spaces:** Registers, Shared, Constant, Texture, Global, Local.
- ⭐ **Three Caches:** L1 (per-SM), Texture Cache (per-SM), L2 (device-wide).
- ⭐ **Scope Rule:** Registers & Local = per-thread; Shared = per-block; Constant, Texture, Global = device-wide.
- ⭐ **Lifetime Rule:** Registers & Local = thread; Shared = block; Global, Constant, Texture = application.
- ⭐ **Speed Rule:** Registers (1) > Shared/L1 (20–30) > L2 (200) > Global/Local (400–800) [cycles].
- ⭐ **Coalescing:** 32 threads in a warp should access contiguous 128-B aligned segments → 1 transaction.
- ⭐ **Bank Conflicts:** Shared memory has 32 banks × 4 B. Conflict = 2+ threads in same bank, different addresses. Padding (`sm[32][33]`) is the standard fix.
- ⭐ **Occupancy Formula:**
$$O_{ach} = \min\!\left(64,\; \left\lfloor\dfrac{65536}{\text{regs\_per\_thread} \times 32}\right\rfloor,\; \left\lfloor\dfrac{\text{shm\_per\_SM}}{\text{shm\_per\_block}}\right\rfloor\right) \text{ warps/SM}$$
- ⭐ **Effective Bandwidth Formula:**
$$BW_{eff} = \dfrac{2 \times N \times \text{sizeof(type)}}{T_{kernel} \times 10^{9}} \text{ GB/s}$$
- ⭐ **Bank Index Formula:** $b = (A/4) \bmod 32$
- ⭐ **Constant Memory:** 64 KB, read-only from device, writeable only from host. Broadcast in single cycle.
- ⭐ **Texture Memory:** Optimized for 2D spatial locality, hardware interpolation, clamping.
- ⭐ **Local Memory:** Misleading name — it's **Global Memory** physically. Created by register spilling or large stack arrays.
- ⭐ **HBM2e Peak BW (A100):** **~1.5 – 2.0 TB/s**. Realistic target: **70–90%** of peak for well-tuned kernels.
- ⭐ **PCIe Gen4 BW:** ~32 GB/s host↔device — 50× slower than HBM. Minimize `cudaMemcpy`.
- ⭐ **Killer Tip:** When a kernel is slow, **profile with `nvprof` / Nsight Compute** to identify whether you are **bandwidth-bound**, **latency-bound**, or **compute-bound**.
- ⭐ **CUDA Programming Model Rule:** *The programmer explicitly manages data placement; the hardware manages thread scheduling.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector Processor vs GPU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vector Processor vs GPU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Vector Processor?

A **Vector Processor** is a central processing unit (CPU) that implements an instruction set architecture where individual instructions operate on **one-dimensional arrays of data** (vectors) rather than single scalar values. It is the classical embodiment of the **SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data)** execution model, deeply integrated with a **pipelined datapath** and **vector registers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A vector processor is a SIMD-style processor that fetches a single vector instruction and applies it to a stream of data elements fetched from a chain of vector registers, exploiting **data-level parallelism (DLP)** without requiring explicit thread management from the programmer.

The defining hardware components are:
- **Vector Registers**: Wide registers (e.g., 512 bits, holding 8 × 64-bit elements or 16 × 32-bit elements).
- **Vector Functional Units**: Pipelined adders, multipliers, and load/store units that can chain operations.
- **Vector Length Register (VLR)**: Controls how many elements of a vector register are active.
- **Vector Mask Register**: Enables predicated execution for irregular loops.

## 1.2 What is a GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)?

A **GPU** is a **many-core, massively parallel processor** originally designed for graphics rendering, now generalized for **general-purpose GPU computing (GPGPU)**. A modern GPU is composed of **hundreds to thousands of small, in-order cores** (e.g., CUDA cores, Stream Processors) grouped into **Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)** or **Compute Units (CUs)**. It executes the **SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads)** execution model, where the hardware groups threads into **warps** (typically 32 threads) and issues one instruction per warp per cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIMT vs SIMD — The Critical Distinction:** In a vector processor, the **hardware compiler / assembler** controls the vector length. In a GPU, the **programmer explicitly launches thousands of threads**, and the hardware groups them into warps dynamically. This makes GPUs a *coarse-grained* form of SIMD controlled at the software level.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

| Architecture | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Scalar Processor** | A single chef preparing one dish at a time |
| **Vector Processor** | A chef with a **massive industrial mixer** — one recipe instruction ("mix 64 eggs") is executed as a single operation across the whole batch |
| **GPU** | An **army of 10,000 line cooks**, each handling a tiny portion of the meal simultaneously, coordinated by a head chef (the host CPU) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** A **vector processor** is like a **wide pipeline** — one instruction sweeps across many data elements through deep pipelining. A **GPU** is like a **wide army** — many small processors all execute the *same* instruction on different data items at the *same* clock cycle, with massive thread-level parallelism.

## 1.4 Historical Context for KTU

The **Cray-1 (1976)** is the archetypal vector supercomputer, achieving ~160 MFLOPS with a **12.5 ns clock**. Modern GPUs such as the **NVIDIA H100** deliver over **60 TFLOPS (FP64)** and **~3 TB/s** of memory bandwidth. Vector processors never disappeared — they evolved into **SIMD extensions in general-purpose CPUs** (SSE, AVX, AVX-512, NEON, SVE, RISC-V V Extension). GPUs evolved into programmable, general-purpose accelerators.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition

### 2.1.1 Vector Processor Pipeline

A vector instruction is processed through a **deep functional unit pipeline**:

1. **Instruction Fetch & Decode** — A single vector instruction is fetched.
2. **Vector Element Generation** — The Vector Length Register (VLR) determines the number of operands.
3. **Pipelined Execution** — Data elements flow through the pipelined functional unit at one element per clock cycle (after the pipeline fill latency).
4. **Chaining** — The output of one vector unit can feed directly into the next without intermediate register storage, eliminating pipeline bubbles.
5. **Result Writeback** — Results are written back to vector registers or memory via scatter/gather operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Vector Chaining** is the killer feature: it allows dependent vector operations to overlap, achieving a **sustained throughput of one result per clock cycle** even when the latency of each unit is multiple cycles.

### 2.1.2 GPU Execution Hierarchy

A GPU follows a **hierarchical SIMT model**:

1. **Grid** — The entire kernel launch (one CUDA kernel call).
2. **Block / Cooperative Thread Array (CTA)** — A group of threads (up to 1024) that can synchronize via shared memory and barriers.
3. **Warp** — 32 threads executed in lockstep (1 instruction issue per cycle per warp).
4. **Thread** — The smallest execution unit, with its own register file and program counter (in some GPUs, individual threads can diverge).

### 2.1.3 Memory Hierarchy Comparison

| Feature | Vector Processor | GPU |
|---|---|---|
| **Register File** | Large vector registers (e.g., 32 × 512-bit) | Per-thread scalar registers (e.g., 65,536 × 32-bit per SM) |
| **On-chip Cache** | Small / optional | Multi-level (L1, shared memory, constant cache, texture cache) |
| **Main Memory** | High-bandwidth SRAM (historically) | HBM2e / HBM3 with **TB/s** bandwidth |
| **Coherence** | Strong | Weak / relaxed (explicit fences required) |
| **Access Pattern** | Strided, gather/scatter, unit-stride | Coalesced access across warps; scattered access is slow |

## 2.2 Performance Metrics

- **FLOPS** (Floating-Point Operations Per Second): The raw compute throughput.
- **Memory Bandwidth (BW)**: Bytes per second the memory subsystem can deliver, measured in **GB/s** or **TB/s**.
- **Arithmetic Intensity (AI)**: FLOPs per byte of data moved. The roofline crossover is at **AI = Peak FLOPS / BW**.
- **Occupancy**: Ratio of active warps to maximum supported warps per SM in a GPU.
- **Vector Length (VL)**: Number of elements in a single vector register.
- **Chime**: Time to complete one vector instruction (≈ start-up time + VL cycles).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{T_{scalar}}{T_{vector}}$ | **Speedup** of vector execution vs scalar execution | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_{vec} = T_{startup} + n \times T_{pipe}$ | **Vector execution time** for a vector of length *n* | seconds |
| 3 | $AI = \dfrac{F}{B}$ | **Arithmetic Intensity** = FLOPs / bytes moved | FLOP/byte |
| 4 | $P = \min(P_{peak},\, AI \times BW)$ | **Roofline attainable performance** | FLOPS |
| 5 | $S_{Amdahl} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** for *n* parallel units | dimensionless |
| 6 | $BW_{eff} = BW_{peak} \times \eta$ | **Effective bandwidth** with efficiency $\eta \in [0,1]$ | bytes/s |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{VL}{Clock_{period} \times N_{elements}}$ | **Pipeline initiation rate** | Hz |
| 8 | $O_{GPU} = \dfrac{W_{active}}{W_{max}}$ | **GPU occupancy** | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{FLOPS}{Watts}$ | **Computational energy efficiency** | FLOPS/W |
| 10 | $T_{warp} = 32 \times T_{issue}$ | **Warp execution time** (32 threads issue in lockstep) | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** For KTU, you must distinguish between **peak FLOPS** and **sustained FLOPS**. Peak is the hardware limit; sustained is what your kernel actually achieves and is bound by the **roofline model**: $P = \min(P_{peak},\, AI \times BW)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vector Processors / SIMD Units**: Used in **scientific computing, DSP (digital signal processing), image processing pipelines, ML inference engines on edge devices, and weather/climate simulation** (e.g., ECMWF). Intel AVX-512 is widely used in **databases, cryptography, and HPC** codes.
- **GPUs**: Used in **deep learning training (Tensor Cores), molecular dynamics (AMBER, GROMACS), real-time graphics, autonomous driving perception, and large language model inference.** The dominant accelerator in **AI data centers**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Vector Speedup Over Scalar Execution

### Problem Setup
A scalar processor takes $T_{s}$ seconds to process a vector operation on $n$ elements. A vector processor with pipeline initiation interval of **1 cycle per element** and a start-up latency of $T_{startup}$ takes $T_{v}$ seconds.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $T_{clk}$ be the clock period. The scalar processor executes $n$ iterations of a loop, each taking $c$ clock cycles (assume $c$ cycles per iteration including load/compute/store). Total scalar time:

$$T_{scalar} = n \times c \times T_{clk}$$

The vector processor issues **one vector instruction** that streams $n$ elements through the pipeline. After the pipeline fill latency of $T_{startup}$ cycles, one element completes every clock cycle. Total vector time:

$$T_{vector} = (T_{startup} + n) \times T_{clk}$$

The speedup $S$ is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{T_{scalar}}{T_{vector}} \\
&= \dfrac{n \times c \times T_{clk}}{(T_{startup} + n) \times T_{clk}} \\
&= \dfrac{n \times c}{T_{startup} + n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**As $n \to \infty$**, the start-up term becomes negligible and the speedup asymptotically approaches $S \to c$. This is the **theoretical maximum speedup** of a vector processor over a scalar loop — bounded by the number of cycles the scalar processor needs per element.

### Worked Numerical Example
Let $n = 64$ elements, $c = 8$ cycles per scalar iteration, $T_{startup} = 6$ cycles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{64 \times 8}{6 + 64} \\
&= \dfrac{512}{70} \\
&\approx 7.31
\end{aligned}
$$

The vector processor is **7.31× faster** than the scalar loop.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Amdahl's Law Applied to GPU Thread Parallelism

### Problem Setup
A kernel has a **parallel fraction** $f$ that can be executed on $n$ GPU cores, and a sequential fraction $(1-f)$ that must run on the host or serially on the GPU.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The total execution time of the parallelized version is:

$$
T_{par} = (1-f) \times T_{orig} + \dfrac{f \times T_{orig}}{n}
$$

The speedup is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(n) &= \dfrac{T_{orig}}{T_{par}} \\
&= \dfrac{T_{orig}}{(1-f) \times T_{orig} + \dfrac{f \times T_{orig}}{n}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**As $n \to \infty$** (massive GPU parallelism):

$$S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$$

### Worked Numerical Example
Let $f = 0.95$ (95% parallel, 5% sequential host-side bookkeeping) and $n = 1024$ GPU cores.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(1024) &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \dfrac{0.95}{1024}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.000928} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.050928} \\
&\approx 19.64
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The GPU Parallelism Paradox:** Even with **1024 cores**, the speedup is only **~19.6×** because of the **5% sequential bottleneck**. Doubling cores to 2048 gives only $S \approx 19.7$, illustrating Amdahl's law. GPUs are *not* a substitute for algorithmic efficiency — they amplify whatever sequential limitations you have.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Roofline Model Cross-Over Point

### Problem Setup
A GPU has peak compute $P_{peak} = 30\,\text{TFLOPS}$ (FP32) and memory bandwidth $BW = 1.5\,\text{TB/s}$. Determine whether a kernel is **compute-bound** or **memory-bound** for a given arithmetic intensity $AI$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The **ridge point** (crossover) of the roofline is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AI_{ridge} &= \dfrac{P_{peak}}{BW} \\
&= \dfrac{30 \times 10^{12}\,\text{FLOPS}}{1.5 \times 10^{12}\,\text{bytes/s}} \\
&= 20\,\text{FLOP/byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 1**: Kernel with $AI = 5\,\text{FLOP/byte}$ (e.g., element-wise vector add)
Attainable performance:
$$P = AI \times BW = 5 \times 1.5 = 7.5\,\text{TFLOPS}$$
This is **less than** $P_{peak} = 30$ TFLOPS, so the kernel is **memory-bound**.

**Case 2**: Kernel with $AI = 50\,\text{FLOP/byte}$ (e.g., dense matrix multiply)
$$P = \min(P_{peak},\, 50 \times 1.5) = \min(30, 75) = 30\,\text{TFLOPS}$$
The kernel is **compute-bound** and saturates the GPU.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Vector Chaining Speedup

### Problem Setup
Two dependent vector operations $A = B + C$ followed by $D = A \times E$ are executed on a vector processor. Without chaining, the second operation must wait for the *entire* first vector to complete. With chaining, the second operation begins as soon as the *first element* result is available.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Without chaining** (sequential pipelined execution):
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{no\_chain} &= T_{startup,add} + n + T_{startup,mul} + n \\
&= (T_{startup,add} + T_{startup,mul}) + 2n
\end{aligned}
$$

**With chaining** (overlapped execution):
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{chain} &= T_{startup,add} + T_{startup,mul} + n \\
&\text{(first result of add flows into mul after one mul start-up, then pipelined)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Speedup from chaining:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{chain} &= \dfrac{2n + T_{startups}}{n + T_{startups}} \\
&\xrightarrow{n \to \infty} 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Chaining asymptotically gives a **2× speedup** for a chain of length 2. For a chain of $k$ dependent operations, the speedup approaches $k$ as $n \to \infty$.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Vector Processor | GPU |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallelism Model** | Pipelined SIMD | SIMT (massively multi-threaded) |
| **Programming Style** | Vector intrinsics, compiler auto-vectorization | CUDA, OpenCL, HIP, SYCL, DirectCompute |
| **Thread Management** | Hardware-controlled | Software-controlled (kernel launch) |
| **Latency Hiding** | Chaining, deep pipeline | Warp scheduling, zero-overhead context switch |
| **Best For** | Tight, regular loops with high ILP+ DLP | Massive data-parallel kernels, ML, graphics |
| **Worst For** | Irregular control flow, pointer chasing | Latency-sensitive serial code |
| **Typical AI Sweet Spot** | High (10–100 FLOP/byte) | Both low and high; depends on kernel |
| **Energy Efficiency (FLOPS/W)** | Very high (specialized) | Moderate (high throughput, high power) |
| **Hardware Cost** | Low (integrated in CPU) | High (dedicated accelerator) |
| **Memory Coherence** | Strong | Relaxed, explicit |
| **Synchronization** | Implicit (pipeline ordering) | Explicit (barriers, atomics) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vector Processor Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VR0["Vector Register 0\n512-bit, 8 x FP64"]
    VR1["Vector Register 1\n512-bit, 8 x FP64"]
    VR2["Vector Register 2\n512-bit, 8 x FP64"]
    VR3["Vector Register 3\n512-bit, 8 x FP64"]
    VLR["Vector Length\nRegister"]
    VMR["Vector Mask\nRegister"]
    VAU["Pipelined Vector\nAdd Unit\nLatency 6 cyc"]
    VMU["Pipelined Vector\nMultiply Unit\nLatency 8 cyc"]
    VLSU["Vector Load\nStore Unit"]
    MEM["Main Memory\nHigh Bandwidth"]
    CTRL["Vector Instruction\nFetch and Decode"]
    CTRL -->|issues vec op| VAU
    CTRL -->|issues vec op| VMU
    CTRL -->|load store| VLSU
    VR0 --> VAU
    VR1 --> VAU
    VAU -->|chained result| VMU
    VR2 --> VMU
    VMU --> VR3
    VLSU <--> MEM
    VLR --> VAU
    VLR --> VMU
    VMR --> VAU
    VMR --> VMU
```

## 4.2 GPU Streaming Multiprocessor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HOST["Host CPU\nLaunches Kernel"]
    GRID["Grid of Thread Blocks\nThousands of Threads"]
    BLK["Thread Block CTA\nUp to 1024 threads"]
    SM1["Streaming Multiprocessor SM0\n32 Warp Slots"]
    SM2["Streaming Multiprocessor SM1\n32 Warp Slots"]
    SM3["Streaming Multiprocessor SM2\n32 Warp Slots"]
    W0["Warp 0\n32 Threads"]
    W1["Warp 1\n32 Threads"]
    W2["Warp 2\n32 Threads"]
    WARP_SCHED["Warp Scheduler\nDual Issue"]
    RF["Register File\n65536 x 32-bit"]
    SHM["Shared Memory\nL1 Cache 128 KB"]
    FMA["INT and FP FMA Units\nTensor Cores"]
    LSU["Load Store Units\nCoalescer"]
    HBM["HBM2e or HBM3\nUp to 3 TB/s"]
    HOST -->|kernel launch| GRID
    GRID --> BLK
    GRID --> SM1
    GRID --> SM2
    GRID --> SM3
    SM1 --> W0
    SM1 --> W1
    SM1 --> W2
    W0 --> WARP_SCHED
    W1 --> WARP_SCHED
    W2 --> WARP_SCHED
    WARP_SCHED --> FMA
    WARP_SCHED --> LSU
    FMA --> RF
    LSU --> SHM
    SHM <-->|coalesced| HBM
```

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Execution Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VEC["Vector Processor Execution"]
        V1["Fetch VADD V1, V2, V3"] --> V2["Pipeline Fill\n6 cycles"]
        V2 --> V3["Element 0 completes"]
        V3 --> V4["Element 1 completes"]
        V4 --> V5["Element n-1 completes\n1 result per cycle"]
    end
    subgraph GPU["GPU Execution"]
        G1["Host launches kernel\nwith N threads"] --> G2["Distribute threads\ninto warps of 32"]
        G2 --> G3["Warp 0 executes\ninstruction 1"]
        G2 --> G4["Warp 1 executes\ninstruction 1"]
        G2 --> G5["Warp 2 executes\ninstruction 1"]
        G3 --> G6["Warp 0 executes\ninstruction 2"]
        G4 --> G7["Warp 1 executes\ninstruction 2"]
        G5 --> G8["Warp 2 executes\ninstruction 2"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Roofline Model — Visual Performance Bound

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROOF["Roofline Attainable Performance P vs Arithmetic Intensity AI"]
        L1["P equals AI times BW\nMemory Bound Region\nSlope equals BW"] --> PEAK["P equals Peak FLOPS\nCompute Bound Region\nFlat Plateau"]
    end
    RIDGE["Ridge Point\nAI equals Peak divided by BW\nSwitchover from memory to compute bound"]
    L1 --> RIDGE
    RIDGE --> PEAK
```

## 4.5 Decision Flow: When to Use Vector vs GPU

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Problem Analysis"] --> Q1{"Is data highly parallel\nwith regular access?"}
    Q1 -->|No| CPU["Use scalar CPU\nwith caching and speculation"]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Are millions of\nindependent threads available?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| GPU2["Use GPU\nSIMT massive parallelism"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Tight inner loop\non a single vector?"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| VEC2["Use Vector Processor\nor SIMD units AVX NEON SVE"]
    Q3 -->|No| MIX["Hybrid CPU + GPU\nOpenMP plus CUDA"]
```
