<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between SIMD and SIMT execution models with a suitable example. (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | SIMD (Vector Processor) | SIMT (GPU) |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Single Instruction, Multiple Data | Single Instruction, Multiple Threads |
| **Control Unit** | One hardware controller issues one instruction at a time | Hardware issues one instruction per *warp* (32 threads) per cycle |
| **Parallelism Granularity** | One instruction operates on a *vector register* of N elements | One instruction operates on N *threads*, each with its own scalar register |
| **Vector Length** | Fixed (e.g., 512 bits = 8 FP64 values) | Dynamic — programmer launches any number of threads |
| **Thread Divergence** | Not supported (hardware masks elements via Vector Mask Register) | Supported within a warp (predicated execution, but causes inefficiency) |
| **Example** | Cray-1, Intel AVX-512, ARM SVE | NVIDIA CUDA, AMD ROCm, OpenCL |

**Example:** Computing $C[i] = A[i] + B[i]$ for $i = 0 \dots 63$:
- **SIMD**: One `VADD` instruction processes 64 elements via 512-bit vector registers.
- **SIMT**: Launch 64 threads, each thread executes the same scalar `add` instruction on its own $A[i]$ and $B[i]$.

---

### Q2. What is meant by "vector chaining" in a vector processor? Why is it important? (3 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Vector chaining is a hardware technique in a vector processor where the **output of one vector functional unit is forwarded directly to the input of another vector functional unit**, allowing the second operation to begin as soon as the first element's result is produced, without waiting for the entire first vector operation to complete.

**Why it is important:**
1. **Eliminates pipeline bubbles** between dependent vector operations.
2. **Achieves a sustained throughput of one result per clock cycle** even for dependent operations.
3. **Asymptotic speedup of k× for a chain of k operations** (vs sequential pipelined execution).
4. **Reduces vector register file pressure** since intermediate results need not be stored.
5. Critical for high-performance kernels such as `D = A * B + C` (FMA chaining) and matrix multiplication.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Comparison

> **Q.A (a)** Compare the architecture of a vector processor and a modern GPU in terms of execution model, memory hierarchy, and programming model. Highlight the key architectural differences with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Vector Processor | GPU |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution Model** | Pipelined SIMD, controlled by hardware | SIMT, controlled by software (kernel launch) |
| **Control Unit** | One scalar control unit drives vector units | Many SMs, each with a warp scheduler |
| **Parallelism Type** | Data-level parallelism within a single instruction stream | Thread-level + data-level parallelism via thousands of threads |
| **Vector Length / Threads** | Fixed by hardware (e.g., 512 bits) | Programmer-defined (1 to millions of threads) |
| **Register File** | Few large vector registers (e.g., 32 × 512-bit) | Many small per-thread scalar registers (e.g., 65K × 32-bit per SM) |
| **Latency Hiding** | Pipelining, chaining | Massive multithreading, zero-overhead warp switching |
| **Memory Hierarchy** | Registers → L1 → Main memory | Registers → Shared memory / L1 → L2 → HBM |
| **Bandwidth** | Moderate (DRAM) | Very high (HBM2e/HBM3, TB/s scale) |
| **Programming Model** | Vector intrinsics, compiler auto-vectorization, OpenMP SIMD | CUDA, OpenCL, HIP, SYCL, DirectCompute, Vulkan Compute |
| **Synchronization** | Implicit via in-order pipeline | Explicit barriers, atomics, memory fences |
| **Power Efficiency** | High (specialized) | Moderate–High (high absolute power, high throughput) |
| **Best Suited For** | Tight loops, signal processing, scientific kernels | Massive parallelism: ML training/inference, graphics, molecular dynamics |

**Architectural Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VP["Vector Processor"]
        VDIR["Vector Instruction\nFetch and Decode"] --> VFU["Pipelined Vector\nFunctional Units"]
        VFU --> VRF["Vector Register File\n32 x 512-bit"]
    end
    subgraph GPUA["GPU"]
        GH["Host CPU\nKernel Launcher"] --> GSM["Streaming Multiprocessors\nSM0 SM1 SM2 ... SMn"]
        GSM --> GWR["Warp Schedulers\nDual Issue"]
        GWR --> GFMA["FMA + Tensor Cores"]
        GFMA --> GRF["Per-thread Register File"]
    end
```

**Conclusion:** Vector processors exploit **deep pipelining and chaining** within a single control flow, while GPUs exploit **massive thread-level parallelism** with hardware-managed scheduling — two complementary approaches to data-level parallelism.

---

> **Q.A (b)** A vector processor with a 512-bit vector register (8 × FP64 elements) and a pipeline initiation interval of 1 cycle per element executes a kernel on 1024 elements. The start-up latency is 12 cycles. A scalar CPU would take 18 cycles per element. Calculate (i) the vector execution time, (ii) the scalar execution time, and (iii) the achieved speedup. **(7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Vector Length (VL) = 1024 elements
- Start-up latency $T_{startup} = 12$ cycles
- Pipeline initiation interval = 1 cycle / element
- Scalar cycles per element $c = 18$ cycles

**(i) Vector Execution Time:**

Since the vector register is 8 elements wide, the number of vector instructions needed:

$$N_{vec} = \dfrac{VL}{8} = \dfrac{1024}{8} = 128 \text{ vector instructions}$$

Each vector instruction takes $T_{startup} + 8$ cycles to complete. With **chaining**, the total vector time is approximately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{vector} &= T_{startup} + VL \\
&= 12 + 1024 \\
&= 1036 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Scalar Execution Time:**

$$T_{scalar} = n \times c = 1024 \times 18 = 18432 \text{ cycles}$$

**(iii) Speedup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{T_{scalar}}{T_{vector}} \\
&= \dfrac{18432}{1036} \\
&\approx 17.79
\end{aligned}
$$

**The vector processor achieves a speedup of approximately 17.79×** over the scalar loop, demonstrating the power of vector processing for data-parallel workloads.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently forget the **start-up latency** term in the vector time formula and incorrectly write $T_{vector} = n$ cycles. Always include $T_{startup} + n$ for full marks. Also remember to convert vector length to **number of vector instructions** when comparing register width to problem size.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — GPU Performance Modeling

> **Q.B (a)** With the help of the **roofline model**, explain how to determine whether a GPU kernel is **compute-bound** or **memory-bound**. A GPU has a peak FP32 throughput of 25 TFLOPS and HBM bandwidth of 1 TB/s. A kernel performs 200 GFLOPs and reads 8 GB of data. Determine the kernel category and its attainable performance. **(7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Roofline Model Theory:**
The roofline model expresses the **attainable performance** of a kernel as the minimum of two bounds:

$$P = \min(P_{peak},\, AI \times BW)$$

where:
- $P_{peak}$ = peak compute throughput (FLOPS)
- $AI = F / B$ = arithmetic intensity (FLOPs per byte)
- $BW$ = memory bandwidth (bytes/sec)

**Graphically**, the model has a sloped line (slope = $BW$) on the left and a flat plateau ($P_{peak}$) on the right, with a **ridge point** at:

$$AI_{ridge} = \dfrac{P_{peak}}{BW}$$

**Calculation:**

Step 1 — Compute Arithmetic Intensity:

$$AI = \dfrac{F}{B} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^{9}\,\text{FLOPs}}{8 \times 10^{9}\,\text{bytes}} = 25\,\text{FLOP/byte}$$

Step 2 — Compute the Ridge Point:

$$AI_{ridge} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{12}}{1 \times 10^{12}} = 25\,\text{FLOP/byte}$$

Step 3 — Compare:
Since $AI = 25 = AI_{ridge}$, the kernel sits **exactly on the ridge point**.

Step 4 — Attainable Performance:
$$P = \min(25 \times 10^{12},\, 25 \times 1 \times 10^{12}) = 25\,\text{TFLOPS}$$

**Conclusion:** The kernel is **balanced (at the ridge point)**, achieving the full **25 TFLOPS** of peak performance. It is simultaneously at the memory and compute limit — the *ideal* operating point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Most real-world ML kernels aim to operate at or near the ridge point. **GEMM (matrix multiplication)** kernels are tuned to do exactly this through tiling and shared-memory reuse, which artificially inflates the effective $AI$ by reducing $B$.

---

> **Q.B (b)** A scientific application has 90% of its execution time in a parallelizable inner loop executed on a GPU with 2048 CUDA cores. The remaining 10% is sequential host code. **(i)** Compute the theoretical speedup using Amdahl's Law. **(ii)** If the GPU is replaced by a vector processor with 32 lanes, what fraction of code must be parallel to achieve the same speedup? **(7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) GPU Speedup with 2048 cores:**

Given $f = 0.90$ (parallel fraction) and $n = 2048$ (GPU cores):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{GPU} &= \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{2048}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.000439} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.100439} \\
&\approx 9.956
\end{aligned}
$$

**The GPU achieves a speedup of ≈ 9.96×** over the unparallelized baseline. Notice that even with 2048 cores, the speedup is bottlenecked by the 10% sequential fraction — additional cores beyond a certain point yield negligible benefit.

**(ii) Required Parallel Fraction for Vector Processor:**

Given $n = 32$ vector lanes, target speedup $S = 9.956$:

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}
$$

Rearranging to solve for $f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{32} &= \dfrac{1}{9.956} = 0.10044 \\
1 - f + 0.03125 f &= 0.10044 \\
1 - 0.96875 f &= 0.10044 \\
0.96875 f &= 0.89956 \\
f &= \dfrac{0.89956}{0.96875} \\
f &\approx 0.9286
\end{aligned}
$$

**Approximately 92.86% of the code must be parallelized** for the vector processor to match the GPU's speedup. This shows that the GPU's massive parallelism compensates for the sequential fraction more effectively than a vector processor with only 32 lanes.

**Comparative Insight:** This is a classic KTU problem testing the understanding that **more parallelism reduces the sensitivity to sequential bottlenecks**, but the bottleneck is never eliminated — only the required *f* shifts.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often write $S = n / f$ or $S = n$ — this is wrong. Always use **Amdahl's formula** $S = 1/((1-f) + f/n)$.
>
> **Pitfall #2:** In part (ii), students may forget to invert the equation and accidentally solve for *n* instead of *f*. Always identify the unknown clearly and isolate it algebraically.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Vector Processor** = **SIMD** (hardware-controlled vector length, deep pipelining, vector chaining).
- ✅ **GPU** = **SIMT** (software-controlled thread count, warp-based issue, massive multithreading).
- ✅ **Key GPU Granularity Levels:** Grid → Block (CTA) → Warp (32 threads) → Thread.
- ✅ **Vector Chaining** = forwarding vector result to next vector unit; eliminates pipeline bubbles.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law**: $S = 1/((1-f) + f/n)$ — sequential fraction always dominates; doubling cores helps less and less.
- ✅ **Roofline Model**: $P = \min(P_{peak},\, AI \times BW)$; ridge point at $AI = P_{peak}/BW$.
- ✅ **Arithmetic Intensity** $AI = \text{FLOPs} / \text{bytes}$ — high $AI$ = compute-bound, low $AI$ = memory-bound.
- ✅ **Vector Execution Time**: $T_{vec} = T_{startup} + n$ (with chaining).
- ✅ **Vector Speedup** over scalar: $S = n \cdot c / (T_{startup} + n) \to c$ asymptotically.
- ✅ **GPUs hide latency** through **warp scheduling** and **zero-overhead context switch**, not through deep pipelining.
- ✅ **Vector processors** are best for **regular, tight inner loops**; GPUs are best for **massive parallel kernels** (ML, graphics, HPC).
- ✅ **Memory Coherence**: Vector = strong; GPU = weak / relaxed, requires explicit fences.
- ✅ **Modern Examples**: Intel AVX-512 (vector), NVIDIA H100 / AMD MI300 (GPU), ARM SVE (vector), Apple M-series GPUs (unified memory GPU).
- ✅ **Energy Efficiency**: $E = \text{FLOPS} / \text{Watts}$ — a key metric for HPC and green computing.
- ✅ **Key Pitfall**: A GPU is **not** always faster than a vector processor — for small data sizes, the GPU launch overhead (kernel launch, memory transfer) may dominate.
- ✅ **Hybrid Trend**: Modern supercomputers (e.g., **Fugaku, Frontier, El Capitan**) combine **vector/SIMD CPUs** (Fujitsu A64FX, AMD EPYC with AVX-512) with **GPU accelerators** — best of both worlds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multimedia SIMD Computers vs GPU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multimedia SIMD Computers vs GPU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Multimedia SIMD Computers

**Multimedia SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) computers** are processor architectures that exploit **data-level parallelism (DLP)** by applying a single instruction to multiple data elements simultaneously. They were historically introduced into general-purpose CPUs through dedicated multimedia extension instruction sets (e.g., Intel **MMX**, **SSE**, **SSE2/3/4**, **AVX**, **AVX-512**, and ARM **NEON**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A Multimedia SIMD computer is a vector/SIMD-enhanced processor that operates on packed integer or floating-point operands (e.g., 64-bit, 128-bit, 256-bit, or 512-bit registers) holding multiple short data elements (8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit), executing the same operation on all elements in lock-step within a single instruction cycle.

### 1.1.1 SIMD Operational Model

In SIMD execution:
- A **single control unit** fetches and decodes one instruction.
- The instruction is **broadcast** to multiple **ALU lanes** (functional units).
- Each lane processes a **different data element** from a packed register.
- The result is stored back into a corresponding packed destination register.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SIMD Lane Counts:** Modern Intel AVX-512 uses **32 × 512-bit** registers (zmm0–zmm31). ARM NEON/SVE provides scalable vector lengths (SVE: 128 to 2048 bits in 128-bit increments).

## 1.2 Graphics Processing Unit (GPU)

A **GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)** is a **many-core, throughput-oriented, massively parallel processor** designed originally for graphics rendering, but now widely used for **General-Purpose computing on Graphics Processing Units (GPGPU)**. Modern GPUs (e.g., NVIDIA A100, H100; AMD MI300) implement the **SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads)** execution model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A GPU is a many-core coprocessor comprising hundreds to thousands of small, in-order execution units (CUDA cores / Stream Processors) grouped into **Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)** / **Compute Units (CUs)**, organized around a high-bandwidth memory subsystem (HBM/GDDR), and coordinated by a host CPU through APIs such as **CUDA, OpenCL, ROCm, or DirectX/Vulkan compute shaders**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **SIMD Analogy — "The Conveyer Belt Chef":**
> Imagine a chef in a kitchen. With a normal CPU, the chef must process vegetables one-by-one (carrot, then onion, then garlic). With **SIMD**, the chef has a special knife with 8 blades — one downward chop slices 8 carrots simultaneously. One instruction, multiple data items, all done in parallel inside the same execution unit.

> [!TIP]
> **GPU Analogy — "The Factory Floor":**
> A GPU is like a **massive factory with thousands of workers (threads)**, each performing a tiny, identical task (e.g., painting one pixel). The foreman (warp scheduler) shouts out the same instruction, and the entire workforce executes it in lockstep on different inputs. It's slower per task than a chef, but the **total throughput** is enormous.

### 1.4 Quick Comparison Snapshot

| Property | Multimedia SIMD | GPU |
|----------|----------------|-----|
| **Core Count** | Few wide ALUs (1–8 lanes) | Thousands of small cores |
| **Execution Model** | SIMD (vector lanes) | SIMT (warps/wavefronts) |
| **Memory** | CPU cache hierarchy | HBM/GDDR + caches |
| **Best For** | Tight inner loops, small vectors | Massive data-parallel kernels |
| **Programming** | Intrinsics, autovectorization | CUDA, OpenCL, HIP |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Multimedia SIMD — Operational Deep-Dive

### 2.1.1 Packed Data Representation

A SIMD register of width $W$ bits, processing elements of size $e$ bits, contains:

$$L = \frac{W}{e}$$

where $L$ is the **number of parallel lanes**. For example, a 256-bit AVX register operating on 32-bit floats holds $L = 256/32 = 8$ lanes.

### 2.1.2 Categories of SIMD Multimedia Instructions

1. **Packed Arithmetic** — `VADDPS`, `VPMULLW` (parallel add/multiply).
2. **Packed Logical/Shift** — `VPXOR`, `VPSRLD` (bitwise operations on lanes).
3. **Packed Compare/Select** — `VPCMPD` (produces mask registers).
4. **Packed Shuffle/Permute** — `VPSHUFB` (cross-lane data movement).
5. **Saturating Arithmetic** — `VPADDSB` (clamps to min/max to prevent overflow in media codecs).
6. **Horizontal Operations** — `VHADDPS` (sums across lanes, used in dot products).

### 2.1.3 The SIMD Speedup Bound

If a fraction $f$ of the program is perfectly vectorizable, the theoretical speedup is given by **Amdahl's Law**:

$$S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{N}}$$

where $N$ is the parallel lane count. As $N \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{1-f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** SIMD lane count beyond a certain point yields diminishing returns if the vectorizable fraction $f < 1$. Multimedia workloads typically achieve $f \approx 0.7$–$0.95$.

## 2.2 GPU — Operational Deep-Dive

### 2.2.1 The SIMT Execution Model

A GPU executes **warps** (NVIDIA: 32 threads) or **wavefronts** (AMD: 64 threads) in lockstep. The hardware uses a **warp scheduler** to issue one instruction per cycle to all threads in a warp.

- Each thread has its **own register file, program counter, and predicate stack** (logical view).
- Physically, all threads in a warp share **one instruction fetch/decode unit** but have **independent ALUs**.
- Divergent branches cause **warp divergence**, where inactive lanes are masked.

### 2.2.2 GPU Memory Hierarchy

A modern GPU memory hierarchy (latency increases, bandwidth decreases downward):

| Level | Typical Size | Latency (cycles) | Bandwidth |
|-------|--------------|------------------|-----------|
| **Registers** | 256 KB/SM | ~1 | ~TB/s |
| **Shared Memory / L1** | 64–192 KB/SM | ~20–30 | ~10 TB/s |
| **L2 Cache** | 4–40 MB (shared) | ~200 | ~5 TB/s |
| **HBM / GDDR** | 16–80 GB | ~400–800 | 1–3 TB/s |

### 2.2.3 GPU Performance Metrics

- **FLOPS (Floating-Point Operations per Second):** $P = N_{\text{cores}} \times F_{\text{clock}} \times \text{IPC} \times 2$ (×2 for FMA).
- **Memory Bandwidth Required for Compute-Bound:** $B_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{P}{A_I}$ where $A_I$ is **arithmetic intensity** (FLOP/byte).
- **Roofline Model:** Performance is bounded by $\min(P_{\text{peak}}, A_I \times B_{\text{peak}})$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $L = W / e$ | SIMD lane count from register width and element size | lanes |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/N}$ | Amdahl's Law speedup | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P = N_{\text{cores}} \times F \times \text{IPC} \times 2$ | Peak GPU FLOPS (with FMA) | FLOPS |
| 4 | $A_I = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{Bytes}}$ | Arithmetic intensity | FLOP/byte |
| 5 | $B_{\min} = P_{\text{peak}} / A_I$ | Min. memory bandwidth to be compute-bound | bytes/s |
| 6 | $P_{\text{roofline}} = \min(P_{\text{peak}},\, A_I \times B_{\text{peak}})$ | Roofline performance bound | FLOPS |
| 7 | $\text{Speedup}_{\text{SIMT}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{GPU}}}$ | GPU speedup over serial code | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Occupancy} = \dfrac{\text{Active warps}}{\text{Max warps per SM}}$ | GPU occupancy ratio | ratio |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Issue cycles}}{\text{Total cycles}}$ | Pipeline / warp-issue efficiency | ratio |
| 10 | $T_{\text{mem}} = \alpha + \beta \cdot N$ | Memory access time (latency + transfer) | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in real-world performance engineering of media codecs (H.264/HEVC), scientific simulations (CFD, molecular dynamics), and AI training (LLM, CNN) to determine whether a workload should run on a vectorized CPU or be offloaded to a GPU.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SIMD Speedup for a Multimedia Kernel

**Problem Setup:** A video processing kernel contains $T_{\text{total}} = 1000$ instructions. Of these, $f = 0.8$ are perfectly vectorizable across $N = 8$ SIMD lanes. The remaining $(1-f) = 0.2$ are scalar (control flow, address calculations).

**Goal:** Derive the speedup and identify the bottleneck.

### Step 1 — Decompose the Execution Time

Let scalar execution take 1 cycle per instruction. The serial time is:

$$T_{\text{serial}} = T_{\text{total}} = 1000 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 2 — Apply SIMD to the Vectorizable Portion

The vectorizable fraction executes in $\frac{f \cdot T_{\text{total}}}{N}$ cycles because $N$ data items are processed per instruction.

$$T_{\text{vec}} = \frac{f \cdot T_{\text{total}}}{N} = \frac{0.8 \times 1000}{8} = \frac{800}{8} = 100 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 3 — Scalar Portion Remains Unchanged

$$T_{\text{scalar}} = (1-f) \cdot T_{\text{total}} = 0.2 \times 1000 = 200 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 4 — Total Parallel Time

$$T_{\text{parallel}} = T_{\text{vec}} + T_{\text{scalar}} = 100 + 200 = 300 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Speedup

$$S = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}} = \frac{1000}{300} = 3.\overline{3}$$

### Step 6 — Cross-Verify with Amdahl's Law

$$S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{N}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + \frac{0.8}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.1} = \frac{1}{0.3} = 3.\overline{3}$$

**Both methods agree** ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: GPU Roofline Analysis

**Problem Setup:** A matrix–matrix multiplication $C = A \times B$ where $A, B, C \in \mathbb{R}^{1024 \times 1024}$ single precision. GPU peak compute $P_{\text{peak}} = 19.5$ TFLOPS, peak bandwidth $B_{\text{peak}} = 1.5$ TB/s.

**Goal:** Determine if the kernel is compute-bound or memory-bound.

### Step 1 — Compute Arithmetic Intensity

GEMM requires $2N^3$ FLOPs and $(3N^2 \times 4)$ bytes of memory traffic (A, B, C single-precision).

$$\text{FLOPs} = 2 \times 1024^3 = 2.147 \times 10^9 \text{ FLOPs}$$

$$\text{Bytes} = 3 \times 1024^2 \times 4 = 1.258 \times 10^7 \text{ bytes}$$

$$A_I = \frac{2.147 \times 10^9}{1.258 \times 10^7} = 170.7 \text{ FLOP/byte}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Memory-Bound Ceiling

$$P_{\text{mem-bound}} = A_I \times B_{\text{peak}} = 170.7 \times 1.5 \text{ TB/s} = 256.0 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

### Step 3 — Compare with Peak Compute

$$P_{\text{roofline}} = \min(P_{\text{peak}},\, P_{\text{mem-bound}}) = \min(19.5,\, 256.0) = 19.5 \text{ TFLOPS}$$

**Conclusion:** GEMM is **compute-bound** ✓ (the kernel can saturate the GPU's FP units).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SIMD vs GPU

| Parameter | Multimedia SIMD (CPU) | GPU (SIMT) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------|
| **Lane / Thread Count** | 4–32 per core | 10,000s across device |
| **Clock Frequency** | 3–5 GHz | 1–2.5 GHz |
| **Per-thread Latency** | Low (in-order) | High (hidden by warps) |
| **Memory Model** | Cache-coherent, low latency | Bulk transfer, high bandwidth |
| **Branching Cost** | Tolerable | Severe (warp divergence) |
| **Energy per Op** | High (deep pipeline) | Low (simple cores) |
| **Programming Model** | Intrinsics / autovectorization | CUDA / OpenCL / HIP / SYCL |
| **Synchronization** | Lightweight (threads) | Lightweight (intra-warp), expensive (inter-block) |
| **Best Workload Size** | Small to medium vectors | Large, regular arrays |
| **Time-to-Market / Dev Cost** | Medium | High (kernel tuning) |
| **Sustained % of Peak** | 10–40% typical | 30–80% (well-tuned) |
| **Power Efficiency (FLOPS/W)** | 1–10 GFLOPS/W | 20–50+ GFLOPS/W (H100: ~50 GFLOPS/W) |

## 3.4 Derivation: When to Use SIMD vs GPU

A useful rule of thumb based on the **break-even problem size** $N^*$:

$$N^* = \frac{C_{\text{overhead,GPU}} - C_{\text{overhead,SIMD}}}{T_{\text{per-element,SIMD}} - T_{\text{per-element,GPU}} / P_{\text{peak ratio}}}$$

For $N > N^*$, the GPU wins on throughput; for $N < N^*$, the SIMD CPU wins on latency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Rule (KTU Examiner-Favorite):** GPU offload is profitable when the kernel has $A_I \ge 10$ FLOP/byte **and** total work $\ge 10^6$ operations. Below this, SIMD/autovectorized CPU code is faster due to launch overhead.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Multimedia SIMD Execution Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Instruction Fetch and Decode] --> B[Control Unit]
    B --> C1[Lane 0 ALU]
    B --> C2[Lane 1 ALU]
    B --> C3[Lane 2 ALU]
    B --> C4[Lane 3 ALU]
    B --> C5[Lane 4 ALU]
    B --> C6[Lane 5 ALU]
    B --> C7[Lane 6 ALU]
    B --> C8[Lane 7 ALU]
    A1[256-bit Source Register 1] --> C1
    A1 --> C2
    A1 --> C3
    A1 --> C4
    A1 --> C5
    A1 --> C6
    A1 --> C7
    A1 --> C8
    A2[256-bit Source Register 2] --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    A2 --> C3
    A2 --> C4
    A2 --> C5
    A2 --> C6
    A2 --> C7
    A2 --> C8
    C1 --> D1[Result Lane 0]
    C2 --> D2[Result Lane 1]
    C3 --> D3[Result Lane 2]
    C4 --> D4[Result Lane 3]
    C5 --> D5[Result Lane 4]
    C6 --> D6[Result Lane 5]
    C7 --> D7[Result Lane 6]
    C8 --> D8[Result Lane 7]
    D1 --> E[256-bit Destination Register]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
    D8 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** A single instruction issued by the Control Unit is broadcast to all 8 ALUs. Each ALU independently reads its corresponding slice from the two source registers and writes back its result slice — all in **one cycle**.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: GPU Streaming Multiprocessor (SIMT)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SM[Streaming Multiprocessor]
        IS[Instruction Fetch and Decode] --> WS1[Warp Scheduler 0]
        IS --> WS2[Warp Scheduler 1]
        IS --> WS3[Warp Scheduler 2]
        IS --> WS4[Warp Scheduler 3]
        WS1 --> DU0[Dispatch Unit]
        WS2 --> DU1[Dispatch Unit]
        WS3 --> DU2[Dispatch Unit]
        WS4 --> DU3[Dispatch Unit]
        DU0 --> EU1[CUDA Core 1]
        DU0 --> EU2[CUDA Core 2]
        DU0 --> EU3[CUDA Core N]
        DU1 --> EU4[Tensor Core / FPU]
        DU1 --> EU5[Load Store Unit]
        DU2 --> EU6[Special Function Unit]
        DU3 --> EU7[INT ALU]
        RF[Register File 256 KB] -.-> EU1
        RF -.-> EU2
        RF -.-> EU3
        RF -.-> EU4
        RF -.-> EU5
        RF -.-> EU6
        RF -.-> EU7
        SMEM[Shared Memory / L1 192 KB] --> EU5
        SMEM --> EU1
    end
    L2[L2 Cache Global 40 MB] --> SM
    HBM[HBM3 Memory 80 GB] --> L2
    HOST[Host CPU PCIe Gen5] --> HBM
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Functional Flow — Data Movement Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU[CPU SIMD Path]
        C1[Memory DRAM] --> C2[L3 Cache]
        C2 --> C3[L2 Cache]
        C3 --> C4[L1 Cache]
        C4 --> C5[SIMD ALU 8 Lanes]
        C5 --> C6[Writeback to L1]
    end
    subgraph GPU[GPU SIMT Path]
        G1[HBM Memory] --> G2[L2 Cache]
        G2 --> G3[L1 and Shared Memory]
        G3 --> G4[Warp 0 Thread 0 to 31]
        G3 --> G5[Warp 1 Thread 0 to 31]
        G3 --> G6[Warp N Thread 0 to 31]
        G4 --> G7[Coalesced Writeback to HBM]
        G5 --> G7
        G6 --> G7
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** The CPU SIMD path is **latency-optimized** with deep cache hierarchies and OoO execution. The GPU SIMT path is **throughput-optimized** with shallow caches, bulk memory access, and latency-hiding via massive thread-level parallelism.

## 4.4 Decision Tree: When to Choose SIMD vs GPU

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Data-Parallel Kernel] --> B{Is the data size large N greater than 1M elements?}
    B -- No --> C[Use SIMD on CPU via intrinsics or autovectorization]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is arithmetic intensity greater than 10 FLOP per byte?}
    D -- No --> E[Memory Bound: SIMD may suffice, or consider GPU with HBM]
    D -- Yes --> F{Are there data-dependent branches in inner loop?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Refactor to remove divergence first, then GPU]
    F -- No --> H[Offload to GPU: Use CUDA or OpenCL or SYCL]
    H --> I[Profile with Roofline Model and tune occupancy]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between SIMD and SIMT execution models with one example each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data):** A single instruction operates on multiple data elements packed into wide registers. All lanes execute the **same** instruction in lockstep. **Example:** Intel AVX `VADDPS ymm0, ymm1, ymm2` adds 8 single-precision floats in one cycle. (1.5 marks)
> - **SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads):** A single instruction is executed by **multiple independent threads**, each with its own program counter and register state, but managed as a warp. Threads can take **different paths** (with mask-based divergence). **Example:** NVIDIA CUDA thread block where a warp of 32 threads executes a single SASS instruction, but each thread holds its own data. (1.5 marks)

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: List any three multimedia SIMD instruction sets and state the register width of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | ISA | Register Width | Bits |
> |-----|----------------|------|
> | Intel MMX | 64-bit MM0–MM7 | (1 mark) |
> | Intel SSE | 128-bit XMM0–XMM15 | (1 mark) |
> | Intel AVX-512 | 512-bit ZMM0–ZMM31 | (1 mark) |
> *(Alternative correct: ARM NEON 128-bit, SVE 128–2048-bit, AVX2 256-bit, AVX-10)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With neat architectural diagrams, explain the organization of a Multimedia SIMD computer. Compare it with a modern GPU in terms of (a) execution model and memory hierarchy, and (b) performance characteristics. Use Amdahl's Law to compute the speedup obtained when 80% of a program is vectorized using 8-lane SIMD.**

#### Part (a) — Architecture & Memory Hierarchy Comparison (7 marks)

> **Model Answer:**
> **Multimedia SIMD Organization:** (3.5 marks)
> - Packed registers (e.g., 256-bit YMM) holding $L = W/e$ data elements.
> - Single control unit broadcasting one instruction to $L$ parallel ALU lanes.
> - Operands loaded from L1 cache or memory; results stored back to wide registers.
> - Execution is **synchronous** across lanes; lane-level masking handled via predicate registers (e.g., AVX-512 `k1`–`k7`).
>
> **GPU Organization (SIMT):** (3.5 marks)
> - Composed of $M$ Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs), each containing many small scalar cores (CUDA cores).
> - Warp of 32 threads issued together; each thread has independent register file entries.
> - Memory hierarchy: registers → shared memory/L1 → unified L2 → HBM (massive bandwidth).
> - Latency hidden by **switching warps** every cycle when one is stalled on memory.

#### Part (b) — Performance & Amdahl's Law (7 marks)

> **Model Answer (Step-by-step):**
> Given: $f = 0.8$, $N = 8$.
>
> $$S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{N}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + \frac{0.8}{8}}$$
>
> $$S = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.1} = \frac{1}{0.3} \approx 3.33$$
>
> **Performance Comparison Table:** (3.5 marks)
>
> | Metric | SIMD CPU | GPU |
> |--------|----------|-----|
> | Peak FLOPS | ~1–2 TFLOPS (AVX-512) | ~20–1000 TFLOPS |
> | Memory BW | ~50–100 GB/s | 1–3 TB/s |
> | Best latency | 1–10 ns | 100–400 ns (hidden) |
> | Power | 100–200 W | 300–700 W |
> | FLOPS/W | ~10 GFLOPS/W | ~50+ GFLOPS/W |
>
> **Conclusion:** SIMD offers best latency on small/medium problems; GPU offers best throughput on large data-parallel problems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Describe the SIMT execution model used in modern GPUs. Using the Roofline model, analyze whether the following kernel is compute-bound or memory-bound: a vector dot product of two arrays of 1 million single-precision elements, executed on a GPU with peak compute = 10 TFLOPS and peak bandwidth = 1 TB/s. State two programming strategies to improve memory-bound kernels.**

#### Part (a) — SIMT Model and Roofline Analysis (7 marks)

> **Model Answer:**
> **SIMT Model:** (3.5 marks)
> - Threads are grouped into **warps** (NVIDIA: 32, AMD: 64) executing the same instruction.
> - Each thread has its own registers and PC; the warp scheduler issues one instruction per cycle.
> - On branch divergence, both paths are serialized with a per-thread **active mask**.
> - Memory accesses are **coalesced** when threads in a warp access contiguous addresses.
>
> **Roofline Analysis:** (3.5 marks)
> - FLOPs for dot product: $2N = 2 \times 10^6$ FLOPs (one multiply + one add per element, then a final reduction).
> - Bytes: $2N \times 4 = 8 \times 10^6$ bytes (one read each from A and B, single precision).
>
> $$A_I = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^6} = 0.25 \text{ FLOP/byte}$$
>
> $$P_{\text{mem-bound}} = A_I \times B_{\text{peak}} = 0.25 \times 1 \text{ TB/s} = 0.25 \text{ TFLOPS}$$
>
> Since $P_{\text{mem-bound}} = 0.25 \text{ TFLOPS} \ll P_{\text{peak}} = 10 \text{ TFLOPS}$, the kernel is **memory-bound** ✓

#### Part (b) — Optimization Strategies (7 marks)

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Increase Arithmetic Intensity via Tiling/Blocking:** (3.5 marks)
>    - Process chunks of arrays in shared memory; reuse loaded values across multiple accumulations.
>    - Example: tile size $B = 1024$ elements → $A_I = 2B / 8 = 256$ FLOP/byte (was 0.25).
> 2. **Memory Access Coalescing & Vector Loads:** (3.5 marks)
>    - Use `float4` (128-bit) loads so that 4 elements are fetched in a single transaction.
>    - Align data to 128-bit boundaries; ensure threads in a warp access consecutive addresses.
>    - Reduces total memory transactions and increases effective bandwidth utilization.
> 3. *(Bonus credit)* **Occupancy tuning & shared memory prefetching** — keep more warps in flight to hide latency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing SIMD with SIMT:** SIMD uses *lanes* inside one instruction; SIMT uses *threads* grouped into warps. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the scalar fraction in Amdahl's Law:** Always show $T_{\text{parallel}} = T_{\text{vec}} + T_{\text{scalar}}$ explicitly.
> 3. **Wrong units in Roofline:** Arithmetic intensity is **FLOP/byte**, not FLOPS/byte. Bandwidth must be in **bytes/s**.
> 4. **Ignoring overheads:** GPU launch latency (~5 µs) means tiny kernels (<10⁴ elements) lose to CPU.
> 5. **Not stating "compute-bound" or "memory-bound" explicitly** — always close the Roofline question with a one-line verdict.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - **SIMD** = Single Instruction, Multiple Data → wide ALU lanes, packed registers (e.g., AVX, NEON, SSE).
> - **SIMT** = Single Instruction, Multiple Threads → warps of 32/64 threads, GPU execution model.
> - **Lane count formula:** $L = W / e$ where $W$ = register width, $e$ = element size.
> - **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/N]$ — speedup is capped by the **serial fraction** $(1-f)$.
> - **Arithmetic Intensity:** $A_I = \text{FLOPs} / \text{Bytes}$ — high $A_I$ ⇒ compute-bound; low $A_I$ ⇒ memory-bound.
> - **Roofline bound:** $P = \min(P_{\text{peak}},\, A_I \times B_{\text{peak}})$.
> - **GPU memory hierarchy (top to bottom):** Registers → Shared/L1 → L2 → HBM/GDDR.
> - **Latency hiding in GPUs** is done by **warp interleaving**, not by deep caches.
> - **CPU SIMD strengths:** low latency, good for small/medium vector sizes, control-flow friendly.
> - **GPU strengths:** massive throughput, best for large data-parallel kernels (matmul, conv, stencil).
> - **Sustained % of peak:** CPU SIMD ≈ 10–40%, well-tuned GPU kernel ≈ 30–80%.
> - **Power efficiency:** GPU delivers 5–10× better FLOPS/W than CPU for parallel workloads.
> - **Programming models:** SIMD → intrinsics/auto-vectorization; GPU → CUDA / OpenCL / HIP / SYCL / compute shaders.
> - **Branch divergence** is expensive on GPUs (warp serialization); avoid divergent inner loops.
> - **Memory coalescing** is critical: contiguous thread → contiguous address yields peak bandwidth.
> - **Empirical GPU offload rule:** profitable when $A_I \ge 10$ FLOP/byte **and** $N \ge 10^6$ elements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiprocessor Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiprocessor Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiprocessor Architecture** is a class of computer architecture in which two or more **Central Processing Units (CPUs)** — referred to as *processors* or *cores* — are integrated within a single computer system to execute multiple instruction streams concurrently, sharing a common main memory and coordinated via a high-speed interconnection network, governed by hardware-supported **coherence** and software-enforced **consistency** mechanisms.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, multiprocessor systems are categorized using **Flynn's Taxonomy** (1966) as:

| Class | Name | Instruction Stream | Data Stream | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SISD** | Single Instruction Single Data | 1 | 1 | Traditional uniprocessor |
| **SIMD** | Single Instruction Multiple Data | 1 | N | GPU shader arrays, Vector units |
| **MISD** | Multiple Instruction Single Data | N | 1 | Fault-tolerant pipelines (rare) |
| **MIMD** | Multiple Instruction Multiple Data | N | N | Multicore CPUs, Clusters |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern **multiprocessors** are predominantly **MIMD** systems — they execute *different* instruction streams over *different* datasets using independent processing units.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large restaurant kitchen** preparing meals for 200 customers:
- A **single chef (uniprocessor)** must cook every dish sequentially — slow, with bottlenecks.
- A **team of 5 chefs sharing one common pantry (shared memory multiprocessor)** can each prepare different dishes simultaneously, but they must constantly coordinate to avoid grabbing the same ingredient at the same time.
- A **team of 5 chefs in separate rooms with their own pantries (distributed memory)**, communicating only by passing notes through a courier (network), must plan even more carefully because pantry updates take time to propagate.

The **multiprocessor architecture** is the engineering blueprint for the "team of chefs" — it dictates *how processors are organized*, *how they share data*, and *how they stay synchronized*.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Boundaries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard metrics used in **KTU evaluations**:
> - **Speedup (S)** = $T_1 / T_n$ (ratio of sequential to parallel execution time)
> - **Efficiency (E)** = $S/n$ (speedup divided by number of processors)
> - **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** — basis of MIMD performance analysis
> - **Bandwidth (B)** — measured in **GB/s** for interconnection links
> - **Cache Coherence Latency** — measured in **clock cycles (cc)**
> - **Speed of Light Limit** in copper: ~ **$5 \text{ ns/m}$** — governs physical scalability of shared buses

---

### 1.4 Architectural Motivation

The shift from uniprocessor to multiprocessor architecture is driven by the **Power Wall**, **Memory Wall**, and **ILP Wall** — three fundamental physical barriers documented by Hennessy and Patterson. Increasing clock frequency became infeasible beyond ~3–4 GHz due to **dynamic power dissipation** $P = \alpha C V^2 f$. The industry response was **Dennard Scaling** breakdown → **multi-core integration** on a single die.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Multiprocessor Memory Organization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the distinction between **UMA**, **NUMA**, **COMA**, and **NORMA**.

#### 2.1.1 Shared Memory Multiprocessors (SMM)

All processors see a **single logical address space**. Two sub-classes:

**A) Uniform Memory Access (UMA)**
- Memory access time is **uniform (identical)** from every processor.
- Typical realization: **Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP)** connected by a **bus** or **crossbar switch**.
- Memory bandwidth degrades as $n$ increases — the **shared bus becomes the bottleneck**.

**B) Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA)**
- Memory is **physically distributed** across processors, but forms a **single logical address space**.
- A processor's **local memory** access is faster than **remote memory** (attached to another node).
- **Cache-Only Memory Architecture (COMA)** is a special NUMA variant where each node's local memory acts as a **large cache**.

#### 2.1.2 Distributed Memory Multiprocessors (DMM)

- Each processor has **private local memory**; inter-processor communication occurs via **explicit message passing** over an **interconnection network**.
- Also called **NORMA (No-Remote-Memory-Access)**.
- Programming model: **MPI (Message Passing Interface)**, OpenSHMEM.

---

### 2.2 Interconnection Networks (KTU High-Yield Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> An **interconnection network** routes data, addresses, and control signals among processors, memories, and I/O devices.

#### 2.2.1 Static (Direct) Networks
Point-to-point links fixed at design time. Topology defines connectivity.

| Topology | Degree | Diameter | Bisection Width | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ring** | 2 | $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ | 2 | Low-cost LAN |
| **Mesh (2D)** | 4 | $2(\sqrt{n}-1)$ | $\sqrt{n}$ | Intel Xeon Phi prototypes |
| **Torus (2D)** | 4 | $\sqrt{n}$ | $2\sqrt{n}$ | Cray XT series |
| **Hypercube (k-cube)** | $k=\log_2 n$ | $k$ | $n/2$ | SGI Origin, classical reference |

For a **k-dimensional hypercube** with $n = 2^k$ nodes:
- Each node has degree **k**.
- Diameter = **k** hops.
- Bisection bandwidth = **$n/2$** links.

#### 2.2.2 Dynamic (Indirect) Networks
Built from **switches** and **links**; topology reconfigurable.

- **Bus**: simplest, $O(n)$ arbitration cost — saturates quickly.
- **Crossbar**: $n \times n$ non-blocking; $O(n^2)$ switch complexity — used in SMP backplanes.
- **Multistage Interconnection Network (MIN)**: e.g., **Omega network** with $\log_2 n$ stages; $O(n \log_2 n)$ switch cost — blocking but cost-efficient.

---

### 2.3 The Cache Coherence Problem (Critical KTU Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When multiple processors cache the **same memory location**, writes by one processor may not be visible to others → **incoherence**.

#### 2.3.1 Snooping (Bus-Based) Protocols
Each cache controller **monitors (snoops)** the shared bus for transactions and takes action based on its current state.

**MESI Protocol (4 states)**:

| State | Meaning | Bus Action on Read | Bus Action on Write |
|---|---|---|---|
| **M**odified | Only this cache holds the line; data is **dirty** | Supply data; transition to **S** | Suppress write-back; transition to **S** |
| **E**xclusive | Only this cache holds the line; data is **clean** | Supply data; transition to **S** | Write to bus; transition to **M** |
| **S**hared | Multiple caches hold the line; data is **clean** | No action | Issue **Bus Upgrade** → invalidate others; transition to **M** |
| **I**nvalid | Line is absent/invalid | Issue **Bus Read**; transition to **S** or **E** | Issue **Bus Read**; transition to **M** |

**State Transition Rules**:
- **PrRd (Processor Read)**: I → S (or E if exclusive); S → S; E → S; M → S (write-back).
- **PrWr (Processor Write)**: I → M (BusRdX); S → M (BusUpgrade, no data); E → M; M → M.
- **BusRd (Bus Read)**: S → S (snoped); E → S; M → S (write-back + supply).
- **BusRdX (Bus Read Exclusive / Invalidate)**: S → I; E → I; M → I (write-back).
- **BusWB (Bus Write-Back)**: M → S (only on PrRd snoop); M → I (on BusRdX).

#### 2.3.2 Directory-Based Protocols (Scalable)
For **large-scale NUMA**, snoop traffic is impractical. A **directory** maintains one entry per memory line recording which caches hold a copy.

Three bits per cache entry: **Shared (S)**, **Uncached (U)**, **Exclusive (D)**, with a **sharing list** of up to $p$ processors.
- **Read miss**: directory adds requestor to list, returns data.
- **Write miss**: directory sends **invalidate** to all sharers, marks state **Exclusive**, returns data.
- **Memory overhead** = $p \cdot m$ bits for $m$ memory lines — manageable because lines are ~64 B.

---

### 2.4 Memory Consistency Models

> [!NOTE]
> A **consistency model** defines *when* a write becomes visible to other processors.

| Model | Guarantee | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequential Consistency (SC)** | All operations appear in a single global order consistent with program order | High |
| **PRAM (Pipelined RAM)** | Writes by a single processor seen in order; reads can overtake writes | Low |
| **Weak Consistency** | Synchronization ops act as memory barriers | Medium |
| **Release Consistency** | Distinguishes `acquire` (read-lock) and `release` (write-unlock) | High |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Speedup** | $S(n) = T_1 / T_n$ | dimensionless; ideal $S = n$ |
| **Efficiency** | $E(n) = S(n)/n$ | fraction (0–1) |
| **Amdahl's Law** | $S(n) = 1 / (f + (1-f)/n)$ | $f$ = serial fraction |
| **Gustafson's Law** | $S(n) = n - f(n-1)$ | scaled problem size |
| **Karp-Flatt Metric** | $f = (1/S - 1/n) / (1 - 1/n)$ | reveals serial fraction empirically |
| **Mean Time to Failure** | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | hours; reliability metric |
| **Crossbar Switches** | $n^2$ | for $n \times n$ crossbar |
| **MIN Stages** | $\log_2 n$ | for Omega/Butterfly MIN |
| **Bandwidth** | $B = W \cdot f$ | $W$ = link width (bits), $f$ = freq (Hz) |
| **MESI Invalidations** | $O(p \cdot w)$ | $p$ = processors, $w$ = writes per line |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE**: Inside markdown tables, never use `|x|`. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead, otherwise the table will break.

---

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Data Centers**: NUMA-aware scheduling improves throughput by 20–40%.
- **HPC (Supercomputers)**: Top500 systems (Frontier, Fugaku) use **Torus/Dragonfly** networks.
- **Smartphones**: ARM's **big.LITTLE** architecture is a heterogeneous multiprocessor.
- **Embedded Real-Time Systems**: Multiprocessors allow partitioned scheduling on AUTOSAR platforms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law

**Problem Statement (KTU Style)**:
A program has a serial fraction $f = 0.10$. Find the maximum speedup achievable using 8 processors.

**Step 1 — Define the Sequential Baseline**
Let $T_1$ be the execution time on a single processor. We normalize $T_1 = 1$ for analytical clarity. The execution time decomposes as:

$$
T_1 = T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}}
$$

where $T_{\text{serial}} = f \cdot T_1 = f$ and $T_{\text{parallel}} = (1-f) \cdot T_1 = (1-f)$.

**Step 2 — Parallel Execution Time**
On $n$ processors, only the parallel portion benefits from parallelization. The serial portion remains unchanged:

$$
T_n = f \cdot T_1 + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_1}{n}
$$

Dividing by $T_1$:

$$
T_n = f + \frac{(1-f)}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Speedup**
By definition:

$$
S(n) = \frac{T_1}{T_n}
$$

Substituting:

$$
S(n) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{(1-f)}{n}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $n$:

$$
S(n) = \frac{n}{f \cdot n + (1-f)} = \frac{n}{f(n-1) + 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Numerical Values**
For $n = 8$, $f = 0.10$:

$$
S(8) = \frac{8}{0.10 \cdot (8-1) + 1} = \frac{8}{0.70 + 1} = \frac{8}{1.70} \approx 4.71
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Efficiency**

$$
E(8) = \frac{S(8)}{8} = \frac{4.71}{8} \approx 0.589 = 58.9\%
$$

**Step 6 — Limiting Behavior**
As $n \to \infty$:

$$
S_{\infty} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n}{f(n-1)+1} = \frac{1}{f}
$$

For $f = 0.10$, the **theoretical ceiling** is $S_{\infty} = 1 / 0.10 = 10$. Adding more than ~16 processors yields diminishing returns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: With only 10% serial code, no matter how many CPUs we throw at the problem, we can never exceed 10× speedup. This is the **fundamental bottleneck of parallel computing**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Hypercube Diameter and Degree

**Problem**: Verify that a 4-dimensional hypercube ($n = 16$ nodes) has diameter 4 and node degree 4.

**Step 1 — Node Count and Dimension**
For a $k$-cube:

$$
n = 2^k
$$

For $k = 4$:

$$
n = 2^4 = 16 \text{ nodes}
$$

**Step 2 — Node Degree**
Each node in a $k$-cube has exactly one link per dimension, hence:

$$
\text{degree} = k = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Diameter**
The longest shortest path between any two nodes equals $k$ (each bit of the binary address must flip once). For $k = 4$:

$$
\text{diameter} = k = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Routing Example**
Send packet from node `0011` (3) to node `1101` (13):
- XOR addresses: `0011 ⊕ 1101 = 1110` (4 differing bits)
- Path length = 4 hops (one per dimension).

**Step 5 — Bisection Width**
A hypercube's bisection cuts the network into two equal halves; the minimum number of links crossed is $n/2$:

$$
B = \frac{n}{2} = \frac{16}{2} = 8 \text{ links}
$$

---

### 3.3 Cache Coherence — State Transition Walkthrough

**Scenario**: Three processors $P_1, P_2, P_3$ each have a cache line for address $X$, initially in state **S** (Shared). $P_1$ writes $X \leftarrow 100$.

| Step | Event | $P_1$ State | $P_2$ State | $P_3$ State | Bus Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial | S | S | S | — |
| 1 | $P_1$ issues PrWr | M | S | S | BusUpgrade (invalidate) |
| 2 | $P_2$ snoops BusUpgrade | M | I | S | — |
| 3 | $P_3$ snoops BusUpgrade | M | I | I | — |
| 4 | $P_2$ issues PrRd | M | S | I | BusRd |
| 5 | $P_1$ snoops BusRd | S | S | I | write-back to memory, supply data |
| 6 | Memory updated | S | S | I | — |

This trace demonstrates the **write-invalidate** policy enforcing coherence.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Multiprocessor Classes

| Parameter | UMA (SMP) | NUMA | COMA | NORMA (Cluster) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Address Space | Single | Single (logical) | Single | Multiple |
| Memory Location | Centralized | Distributed | Cache-Only Distributed | Private per node |
| Access Time | Uniform | Non-uniform | Non-uniform | Non-uniform + message cost |
| Coherence | Snooping | Directory | Hardware directory | Software (MPI) |
| Scalability | Low (~16) | Medium (~256) | Medium | Very High (10⁶) |
| Programming Model | Shared vars | Shared vars + locality | Shared vars | Message passing |
| Example | Intel Xeon E5 | SGI Origin 2000 | KSR-1 | Beowulf Cluster |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Shared Memory Multiprocessor (Bus-Based SMP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPUSet[CPU Subsystem]
        P1[CPU1 Core]
        P2[CPU2 Core]
        P3[CPU3 Core]
        P4[CPU4 Core]
    end

    subgraph CacheLayer[Private L1 L2 Caches]
        C1[L1 L2 Cache for CPU1]
        C2[L1 L2 Cache for CPU2]
        C3[L1 L2 Cache for CPU3]
        C4[L1 L2 Cache for CPU4]
    end

    BUS[Shared System Bus Snooping Protocol]
    MEM[Main Memory DRAM Bank]
    IO[IO Bridge PCIe]

    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> C2
    P3 --> C3
    P4 --> C4
    C1 --> BUS
    C2 --> BUS
    C3 --> BUS
    C4 --> BUS
    BUS --> MEM
    BUS --> IO

    style CPUSet fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style CacheLayer fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style BUS fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style MEM fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **shared bus** is the **single point of serialization** — bus arbitration becomes the bottleneck as $n$ grows.

---

### 4.2 Distributed Memory Multiprocessor (Cluster Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NodeA[Node 0]
        CPU_A[CPU]
        MEM_A[Local Memory]
    end
    subgraph NodeB[Node 1]
        CPU_B[CPU]
        MEM_B[Local Memory]
    end
    subgraph NodeC[Node 2]
        CPU_C[CPU]
        MEM_C[Local Memory]
    end
    subgraph NodeD[Node 3]
        CPU_D[CPU]
        MEM_D[Local Memory]
    end

    SW1[Network Switch Fabric]
    SW2[Network Switch Fabric]
    ROUTER[Interconnection Network e.g. InfiniBand]

    CPU_A --- MEM_A
    CPU_B --- MEM_B
    CPU_C --- MEM_C
    CPU_D --- MEM_D

    NodeA --> SW1
    NodeB --> SW1
    NodeC --> SW2
    NodeD --> SW2
    SW1 --> ROUTER
    SW2 --> ROUTER

    style NodeA fill:#e1f5fe
    style NodeB fill:#e1f5fe
    style NodeC fill:#e1f5fe
    style NodeD fill:#e1f5fe
    style ROUTER fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

---

### 4.3 MESI Coherence State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Invalid

    Invalid --> Exclusive : PrRd BusRd no other sharer
    Invalid --> Shared : PrRd BusRd other sharers exist
    Invalid --> Modified : PrWr BusRdX

    Exclusive --> Shared : BusRd snoop
    Exclusive --> Modified : PrWr

    Shared --> Invalid : BusRdX or BusUpgrade snoop
    Shared --> Modified : PrWr BusUpgrade

    Modified --> Shared : BusRd snoop with write back
    Modified --> Invalid : BusRdX snoop with write back

    Modified --> [*] : Line eviction
    Shared --> [*] : Line eviction
    Exclusive --> [*] : Line eviction
    Invalid --> [*] : Line eviction
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid syntax note**: All node IDs above are alphanumeric (`Invalid`, `Exclusive`, `Shared`, `Modified`) prefixed with letters via capital first character — no reserved keywords are used.

---

### 4.4 Multistage Interconnection Network (Omega Network, 8×8)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[CPU 0] --> S0[Stage 0 Switch 2x2]
    P1[CPU 1] --> S0
    S0 --> S1A[Stage 1 Switch A]
    S0 --> S1B[Stage 1 Switch B]
    S1A --> S2A[Stage 2 Switch A]
    S1A --> S2B[Stage 2 Switch B]
    S1B --> S2C[Stage 2 Switch C]
    S1B --> S2D[Stage 2 Switch D]
    S2A --> M0[Memory 0]
    S2A --> M1[Memory 1]
    S2B --> M2[Memory 2]
    S2B --> M3[Memory 3]
    S2C --> M4[Memory 4]
    S2C --> M5[Memory 5]
    S2D --> M6[Memory 6]
    S2D --> M7[Memory 7]

    style S0 fill:#ffe0b2
    style S1A fill:#ffe0b2
    style S1B fill:#ffe0b2
    style S2A fill:#ffe0b2
    style S2B fill:#ffe0b2
    style S2C fill:#ffe0b2
    style S2D fill:#ffe0b2
```

> [!NOTE]
> An **8×8 Omega network** uses $\log_2 8 = 3$ stages, each with $8/2 = 4$ switches of size $2 \times 2$. Total switch count = $(n/2) \cdot \log_2 n = 12$, vs $n^2 = 64$ for a crossbar — significant cost savings at the price of being **blocking**.

---

### 4.5 Hypercube Topology (4-Cube)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N000[Node 000] --- N001[Node 001]
    N000 --- N010[Node 010]
    N000 --- N100[Node 100]
    N001 --- N011[Node 011]
    N001 --- N101[Node 101]
    N010 --- N011[Node 011]
    N010 --- N110[Node 110]
    N011 --- N111[Node 111]
    N100 --- N101[Node 101]
    N100 --- N110[Node 110]
    N101 --- N111[Node 111]
    N110 --- N111[Node 111]

    style N000 fill:#b3e5fc
    style N111 fill:#ffcdd2
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each node address is a 3-bit binary string. Edges connect nodes differing in exactly **one bit** — the classic **Hamming distance = 1** adjacency rule.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Multiprocessor Architecture" and distinguish between UMA and NUMA systems with one example each.**

**Model Answer**:

A multiprocessor architecture is a computer system with **two or more interconnected processors** that share a common memory and cooperate to execute multiple instruction streams concurrently.

| Feature | UMA | NUMA |
|---|---|---|
| Memory access latency | **Uniform** across processors | **Non-uniform** (local vs remote) |
| Memory organization | Centralized shared memory | Physically distributed, logically shared |
| Scalability | Limited (~16 CPUs) | Higher (~256 CPUs) |
| Example | Intel Xeon SMP server | SGI Origin 2000 |

In **UMA**, any CPU accessing any memory location takes the same time. In **NUMA**, local memory access is faster than remote memory access, requiring locality-aware scheduling.

**(3 marks)** ✓

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the cache coherence problem? Explain any two solutions briefly.**

**Model Answer**:

The **cache coherence problem** arises when multiple processors maintain private caches of the same memory location; a write by one processor may not be propagated to other caches, leading to **inconsistent views** of memory.

**Two Solutions**:
1. **Snooping-based protocols (MESI)**: Each cache monitors the shared bus; on observing a write by another processor, it **invalidates** or **updates** its copy.
2. **Directory-based protocols**: A central directory at the home node tracks which caches hold a copy of each line; on a write, the directory sends **invalidate messages** to all sharers.

Snooping scales poorly with $n$ due to broadcast traffic; directory protocols scale better but incur higher latency and directory memory overhead.

**(3 marks)** ✓

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Flynn's Classification** of computer architecture in detail with neat diagrams and an example for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of a **Shared Memory Multiprocessor**. Discuss **MESI protocol** state transitions with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Flynn's Taxonomy (7 marks)**

Flynn (1966) classified computer architectures based on the multiplicity of **instruction streams** and **data streams**:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SISD[SISD Single Instruction Single Data]
    SIMD[SIMD Single Instruction Multiple Data]
    MISD[MISD Multiple Instruction Single Data]
    MIMD[MIMD Multiple Instruction Multiple Data]

    style SISD fill:#ffcdd2
    style SIMD fill:#c8e6c9
    style MISD fill:#fff9c4
    style MIMD fill:#bbdefb
```

**1. SISD (Single Instruction, Single Data)**
- Conventional sequential computer.
- One processor executes one instruction operating on one data item per cycle.
- *Example*: Intel Core i3 (single-threaded mode), desktop PCs.

**2. SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data)**
- One instruction is broadcast to many processors, each operating on a different data element.
- Highly efficient for vector/matrix operations.
- *Example*: GPU streaming multiprocessors (NVIDIA CUDA cores), Intel SSE/AVX vector units.

**3. MISD (Multiple Instruction, Single Data)**
- Multiple processors execute different instructions on the same data stream.
- Rare; primarily used for **fault-tolerant** pipelined systems.
- *Example*: Space Shuttle flight control computer (redundant channels).

**4. MIMD (Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data)**
- Multiple autonomous processors simultaneously execute different instruction streams on different data.
- Most general and flexible class.
- *Examples*: Multicore CPUs, distributed clusters, supercomputers.

**(7 marks)** ✓

---

#### **Part (b) — Shared Memory Multiprocessor + MESI (7 marks)**

**Shared Memory Multiprocessor Architecture**:
- Multiple CPUs connected to a **single shared memory** via a **system bus** or **interconnection network**.
- All processors see a **uniform address space** (in UMA).
- Communication is implicit via shared variables (load/store semantics).

```
   CPU1 ─┐         ┌── Memory Module 0
   CPU2 ─┤  Bus    ├── Memory Module 1
   CPU3 ─┤         ├── Memory Module 2
   CPU4 ─┘         └── Memory Module 3
```

**MESI Protocol State Transitions**:

The **MESI protocol** uses 4 states per cache line:
- **M**odified: dirty, exclusive to this cache.
- **E**xclusive: clean, exclusive to this cache.
- **S**hared: clean, multiple caches may hold.
- **I**nvalid: not present.

| Current State | Event | Next State | Bus Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | PrRd, no other cache has copy | E | BusRd |
| I | PrRd, other cache has copy | S | BusRd |
| I | PrWr | M | BusRdX |
| E | PrRd by another | S | BusRd (snoop) |
| E | PrWr | M | (silent) |
| S | PrWr | M | BusUpgrade |
| S | BusRdX from another | I | (snoop) |
| M | BusRd snoop | S | Write-back |
| M | BusRdX snoop | I | Write-back |

**Example**: Three CPUs share line X (state S). CPU1 writes X.
- CPU1: S → **M**, sends **BusUpgrade**.
- CPU2, CPU3 snoop BusUpgrade: S → **I**.
- Result: only CPU1 holds a valid (modified) copy; coherence restored.

**(7 marks)** ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **interconnection network topologies** used in multiprocessor systems. Compare **bus, crossbar, ring, mesh, and hypercube** in terms of **diameter, degree, and bisection bandwidth**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Amdahl's Law**? Derive the expression for speedup. A parallel program has a serial fraction of 0.05. Compute the maximum speedup achievable with **(i) 16 processors** and **(ii) 256 processors**. Comment on your results. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Interconnection Networks (7 marks)**

An **interconnection network** connects processors, memory modules, and I/O devices. It is characterized by:
- **Degree**: number of links per node.
- **Diameter**: maximum shortest path between any two nodes.
- **Bisection bandwidth**: number of links crossing the bisection (min cut).
- **Cost**: total number of links/switches.

| Topology | Degree | Diameter | Bisection Bandwidth | Cost (Links) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bus** | 1 (per node, shared) | 1 | 1 | $n+1$ | Small SMP |
| **Ring** | 2 | $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ | 2 | $n$ | LAN, low-cost |
| **2D Mesh** | 4 (interior) | $2(\sqrt{n}-1)$ | $\sqrt{n}$ | $2(n-\sqrt{n})$ | Tiled chips |
| **2D Torus** | 4 | $\lfloor \sqrt{n} \rfloor$ | $2\sqrt{n}$ | $2n$ | Cray XT |
| **Hypercube** | $\log_2 n$ | $\log_2 n$ | $n/2$ | $n \log_2 n / 2$ | SGI Origin |
| **Crossbar** | 1 (input) | 1 | $n$ | $n^2$ | SMP backplane |

**Comparative Analysis**:
- **Bus** is cheapest but a severe bottleneck; only $O(1)$ transactions can occur per arbitration cycle.
- **Crossbar** provides full non-blocking bandwidth ($n$ simultaneous connections) but has $O(n^2)$ cost.
- **Hypercube** offers excellent diameter ($\log n$) but wiring complexity scales as $n \log n$.
- **Mesh/Torus** are **scalable and VLSI-friendly** (planar layout), used in modern Intel/NVIDIA chips.

**(7 marks)** ✓

---

#### **Part (b) — Amdahl's Law (7 marks)**

**Statement**: The speedup of a parallel program is limited by its sequential fraction.

**Derivation** (as in Section 3.1):

$$
S(n) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{(1-f)}{n}} = \frac{n}{f(n-1) + 1}
$$

**Numerical Computation**:

For $f = 0.05$:

**(i) n = 16**:

$$
S(16) = \frac{16}{0.05 \cdot (16-1) + 1} = \frac{16}{0.05 \cdot 15 + 1} = \frac{16}{0.75 + 1} = \frac{16}{1.75} \approx 9.14
$$

**(ii) n = 256**:

$$
S(256) = \frac{256}{0.05 \cdot (256-1) + 1} = \frac{256}{0.05 \cdot 255 + 1} = \frac{256}{12.75 + 1} = \frac{256}{13.75} \approx 18.62
$$

**Limiting Speedup**:

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

**Comments**:
- Increasing processors from 16 → 256 gives only $9.14 \to 18.62$, roughly doubling speedup for **16× more hardware**.
- The asymptotic ceiling is $S_\infty = 20$, demonstrating the **diminishing returns** predicted by Amdahl.
- Even with $f$ as low as 5%, we cannot exceed 20× speedup regardless of processor count — this is the **fundamental limit** of fixed-workload parallelization.

**(7 marks)** ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Confusing Speedup with Efficiency** — Speedup is dimensionless ($T_1/T_n$); Efficiency is a fraction in $[0, 1]$.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in Amdahl** denominator — Students often write $S = n/f$ instead of $S = n/(fn+1-f)$.
> 3. **Treating MESI as "ME-SI"** — M, E, S, I are *states*, not "levels" of sharing.
> 4. **Assuming every invalidation forces write-back** — only Modified lines generate a BusWB; Shared/Exclusive lines are simply invalidated.
> 5. **Mermaid node-ID collisions** — never use keywords like `end` or `subgraph` as standalone IDs.
> 6. **Mixing MIMD and SMP** — SMP is a *type* of MIMD with shared memory; clusters are also MIMD but with distributed memory.
> 7. **Forgetting to normalize** $T_1 = 1$ in Amdahl derivations; the final step requires you to revert to original units if specified.
> 8. **Wrong diameter for hypercube** — diameter = $\log_2 n$, NOT $\log_2 n - 1$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Multiprocessor Architecture**

- [x] **Flynn's Taxonomy**: SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD — modern multiprocessors are MIMD.
- [x] **Memory Classes**: UMA (uniform access, e.g., SMP), NUMA (non-uniform, e.g., SGI Origin), COMA (cache-only), NORMA (clusters).
- [x] **Interconnection Networks**: Bus ($O(1)$), Crossbar ($n^2$ switches), MIN/Omega ($\log_2 n$ stages), Hypercube ($k = \log_2 n$ degree and diameter).
- [x] **Cache Coherence**: Two main strategies — **snooping** (bus-based, MESI) and **directory** (NUMA-scalable).
- [x] **MESI States**: M, E, S, I — Master the transition table for BusRd, BusRdX, PrRd, PrWr.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law**: $S(n) = n / [f(n-1) + 1]$; asymptotic ceiling $S_\infty = 1/f$.
- [x] **Gustafson's Law**: $S(n) = n - f(n-1)$ — applies to **scaled workload** (problem size grows with $n$).
- [x] **Karp-Flatt Metric**: $f = (1/S - 1/n) / (1 - 1/n)$ — empirical serial-fraction estimator.
- [x] **Consistency Models**: SC (strictest) → Release Consistency (most relaxed).
- [x] **Synchronization**: hardware primitives (test-and-set, compare-and-swap, fetch-and-add), software constructs (locks, barriers, semaphores).
- [x] **Dennard Scaling breakdown** led to the multi-core era — motivate why multiprocessors exist.
- [x] **Key numeric to memorize**: For an $n \times n$ crossbar, switch count = $n^2$; for $n$-node hypercube, links = $(n \log_2 n)/2$.
- [x] **Real-world examples**: Intel Xeon (SMP), NVIDIA GPUs (SIMT — close to SIMD), IBM Power (NUMA), Cray (Torus networks).

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Multiprocessor Architecture**. These notes are aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes **CO3** (analyze multiprocessor architectures, coherence protocols, and performance models) and **CO4** (apply Amdahl's and Gustafson's laws to real parallel workloads).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centralized Shared Memory Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Centralized Shared Memory Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Centralized Shared-Memory Architecture (CSMA)**, also known as a **Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP)** or **Uniform Memory Access (UMA)** architecture, is a parallel computing paradigm in which multiple identical processor cores (typically 2 to 64 in modern implementations) share a single, physically centralized main memory subsystem through a common high-speed interconnection fabric. Every processor has equal access time (uniform latency) to every memory location, regardless of which core issues the load or store request.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Symmetric" refers to the fact that all processors are **identical in capability** and have **symmetric (equal) access latency** to shared memory. The interconnect, cache hierarchy, and memory controller design are pivotal to the performance of this architecture.

The defining characteristics mandated by the KTU 2024 scheme for this module are:

- **Single Physical Address Space:** All cores see one global memory map.
- **Cache Coherence Requirement:** Because each core typically has private L1/L2 caches, a hardware coherence protocol (MESI, MOESI, or MSI) is mandatory to maintain a single system-wide view of memory.
- **Shared Interconnect:** A common bus, ring, or crossbar mediates all processor–memory traffic.
- **Sequential Consistency or Relaxed Memory Models:** Defines the legal ordering of memory operations across cores.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Open-Plan Office

Imagine a large open-plan office with **8 senior employees (processors)** seated at desks. In the center of the room sits a **single filing cabinet (shared memory)**.

- **Memory Access:** Any employee can walk to the cabinet and pull out any file (read) or modify it (write). Because they all walk the same distance, the access time is **uniform** — no one is privileged.
- **Personal Sticky Notes (Private Caches):** Each employee keeps a small notepad on their desk. If they recently read a file, they jot down its contents to save a trip. But what happens if two employees modify the same file based on their notepad? They could end up with **outdated, inconsistent notes**.
- **The Office Manager (Coherence Protocol):** To prevent chaos, the office manager enforces a rule: *"Before updating your notepad, you must announce it; before reading a file, check if someone else has a newer version."* This is exactly the role of a **MESI cache coherence protocol**.
- **The Doorway (Interconnect Bus):** Since all employees must walk through the same single doorway to reach the cabinet, it can become a **bottleneck** when the office gets crowded. This is why real SMP systems eventually shift to **multi-stage crossbar switches** instead of a single shared bus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Centralized Shared Memory is like a **single kitchen shared by all cooks in a restaurant** — the kitchen is centrally located, every cook has equal access, but during the dinner rush, traffic jams (memory contention) are inevitable.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are standard in the KTU 2024 evaluation scheme for SMP/CSMA performance analysis:

- **Memory Access Time ($T_{mem}$):** Typical modern DRAM latency is **100 ns – 200 ns** for L1 misses hitting main memory.
- **Interconnect Bandwidth:** A modern shared bus provides **10 GB/s – 32 GB/s**; crossbar switches scale to **100+ GB/s** aggregate.
- **Processor Count ($N$):** KTU 2024 problems typically assume **$N \in \{2, 4, 8, 16, 32\}$** cores.
- **Cache Coherence Protocol Overhead:** Approximately **15–40 additional clock cycles** per cache miss for protocol state transitions.
- **False Sharing Penalty:** When two unrelated variables reside in the same cache line, coherence traffic is generated unnecessarily.

## 1.4 Historical Context (KTU Contextual Knowledge)

The SMP architecture was popularized in the **1980s** and **1990s** by systems such as the **Sequent Symmetry, SGI Power Challenge, and Sun Enterprise 10000**. The fundamental limitation — bus saturation — motivated the transition toward **Distributed Shared Memory (DSM)** architectures (NUMA) covered later in the syllabus.
