# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture Breakdown

A canonical Centralized Shared-Memory Architecture (SMP/UMA) comprises the following logical blocks, listed in the hierarchical order of typical KTU exam diagrams:

### A. Processor Subsystem
- **$N$ identical CPU cores** (with their own private **L1 instruction** and **L1 data caches**).
- Optionally, each core may have a **private L2 cache** before reaching the shared interconnect.
- A **snooping port (snoop tag array)** is attached to each cache controller, monitoring the shared interconnect for transactions.

### B. Interconnection Fabric
The shared-medium fabric can be implemented as:
- **Shared Bus:** Simplest, lowest cost, but bandwidth-limited. Used in early SMPs.
- **Single Bus with Multiple Buses:** Hierarchical (e.g., local memory buses per processor + system bus).
- **Crossbar Switch:** $N \times M$ non-blocking switch fabric, scalable but more expensive.
- **Ring / Mesh (small scale):** Modern Intel/AMD ring-based on-chip interconnects.

### C. Memory Subsystem
- **One physical, unified main memory** partitioned into banks for interleaving.
- **One or more memory controllers** anchor the memory to the interconnect.
- All addresses are accessible by all processors with **uniform latency** (the defining UMA property).

### D. I/O Subsystem
- I/O devices connect via bridges (e.g., Northbridge/Southbridge or PCIe root complexes) and are typically address-mapped into the shared memory space.

## 2.2 The "Why and How" Behind Each Architectural Choice

| Design Decision | Engineering Rationale (Why) | Implementation Mechanism (How) |
|---|---|---|
| **Centralize memory** | Simpler programming model (single address space) | One DRAM bank per channel, all channels equidistant |
| **Use private L1 caches** | Hide memory latency (typically 4–10 cycles for L1 hit) | Split I-cache and D-cache, hardware-managed |
| **Implement snooping** | Scalable coherence for small $N$ | Every cache monitors bus transactions in parallel |
| **Use a directory as $N$ grows** | Snooping broadcasts don't scale beyond ~16 cores | Directory tracks which caches hold which lines |
| **Interleave memory banks** | Exploit bank-level parallelism | Lower address bits select the bank |

## 2.3 Cache Coherence Protocols (The Heart of CSMA)

### 2.3.1 The MESI Protocol (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid)

MESI is the **de-facto industry standard** for SMP coherence and is the most frequently tested topic in the KTU 2024 scheme for this module.

- **Modified (M):** The line is **dirty** (modified) in *this* cache and is the **sole valid copy** in the system. Main memory is stale.
- **Exclusive (E):** The line is **clean** in *this* cache and is the **sole valid copy**. Main memory is up-to-date.
- **Shared (S):** The line is **clean** in *this* cache, and **one or more other caches** also hold a valid copy.
- **Invalid (I):** The line is **not present** in this cache, or its tag is invalid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In M state, the cache must intervene on other cores' read/write requests by providing the data (write-back). In S state, any core can read but writes trigger an upgrade to M, forcing invalidation of other copies (a *bus upgrade* transaction).

### 2.3.2 State Transition Triggers

| Current State | Event | Next State | Bus Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | Read Hit | — | None (local) |
| I | Read Miss | S | **BusRd** (snoop other caches) |
| I | Write Miss | M | **BusRdX** (invalidate others) |
| S | Write Hit | M | **BusUpgr** (invalidate others) |
| M | Read Miss (other) | S | **Flush** (write-back + provide data) |
| M | Write Miss (other) | I | **Flush** (write-back) |

### 2.3.3 Directory-Based Coherence (Snooping Alternative)

When $N > 16$, broadcasting bus traffic becomes unscalable. A **directory-based protocol** centralizes the coherence state:
- A **directory entry** is maintained for each memory block, recording which caches hold a copy.
- Point-to-point messages replace broadcast snoop operations.
- This is the conceptual bridge to **Distributed Shared Memory (NUMA)**, which appears later in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 2.4 Synchronization Primitives

SMP systems must provide hardware and software primitives for inter-processor coordination:

- **Atomic Load-Store:** The fundamental hardware primitive.
- **Test-and-Set:** Reads a memory location and writes a new value atomically.
- **Compare-and-Swap (CAS):** Updates a memory location only if it matches an expected value. Foundation of lock-free data structures.
- **Fetch-and-Add:** Atomically increments a counter and returns the old value.
- **Memory Barriers (Fences):** Force ordering of memory operations across cores (e.g., `MFENCE`, `SFENCE` on x86).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All vertical bar delimiters are rendered as `\vert` to preserve the markdown table structure.

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Relation | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{mem}$ | Main memory access time (L1 miss to DRAM) | $T_{mem} = T_{controller} + T_{DRAM} + T_{interconnect}$ | nanoseconds (ns) |
| $T_{L1}$ | L1 cache hit latency | $T_{L1} \approx 1 - 4$ cycles | cycles |
| $T_{avg}$ | Average memory access time (AMAT) | $T_{avg} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_{L1}) \cdot T_{miss}$ | cycles / ns |
| $H_{L1}$ | L1 hit rate | $0 < H_{L1} < 1$ | dimensionless |
| $S_{speedup}$ | Speedup of $N$ processors | $S_{speedup} = \dfrac{T_{serial}}{T_{parallel}}$ | dimensionless |
| $E_{ff}$ | Parallel efficiency | $E_{ff} = \dfrac{S_{speedup}}{N} = \dfrac{T_{serial}}{N \cdot T_{parallel}}$ | dimensionless |
| $f_{par}$ | Parallel fraction (Amdahl) | $0 \leq f_{par} \leq 1$ | dimensionless |
| $S_{Amdahl}$ | Amdahl's Law speedup bound | $S_{Amdahl} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f_{par}) + \dfrac{f_{par}}{N}}$ | dimensionless |
| $B_{bus}$ | Shared bus bandwidth | $B_{bus} = f_{bus} \cdot W_{bus}$ | bytes / second |
| $B_{required}$ | Total memory traffic demand | $B_{required} = N \cdot (1 - H_{L1}) \cdot R_{mem}$ | bytes / second |
| $T_{snoop}$ | Snoop transaction latency | $T_{snoop} = T_{arb} + T_{prop} + T_{decode}$ | cycles |
| $\rho$ | Bus utilization | $\rho = \dfrac{B_{required}}{B_{bus}}$ | dimensionless |
| $N_{max}$ | Maximum scalable cores (bus-bound) | $N_{max} \approx \dfrac{B_{bus}}{R_{mem} \cdot (1 - H_{L1})}$ | integer count |
| $T_{CAS}$ | DRAM column access strobe latency | Given in DRAM datasheets (e.g., $\vert T_{CL} \vert$ cycles) | clock cycles |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Centralized Shared Memory Architectures underpin **virtually every server, workstation, and modern multi-core laptop/smartphone SoC** on the market:

- **Server CPUs:** Intel Xeon E5/E7 families and AMD EPYC Rome/Milan (in their "uniform" sub-domains) implement SMP within each chiplet.
- **Mobile SoCs:** ARM's big.LITTLE and DynamIQ architectures implement shared-memory clusters of 4–8 cores.
- **Mainframes:** IBM zSeries mainframes are classic SMPs scaled to 200+ cores using SMP-on-a-chip designs.
- **Embedded Control Systems:** Multi-core DSPs and microcontrollers in automotive and aerospace systems use SMP for deterministic real-time processing.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Amdahl's Law Applied to SMP

**Problem Setup:** A program has a parallelizable fraction $f_{par} = 0.9$ (90%) running on an $N = 8$ processor SMP system. Compute the speedup, efficiency, and the marginal speedup gain from doubling to $N = 16$.

### Step 1: Write Amdahl's Law
The execution time of the parallel program is the sum of the **serial portion** $(1 - f_{par}) \cdot T_{total}$ and the **parallel portion** $\dfrac{f_{par} \cdot T_{total}}{N}$.

$$
T_{parallel} = (1 - f_{par}) \cdot T_{serial} + \frac{f_{par} \cdot T_{serial}}{N}
$$

### Step 2: Derive the Speedup Formula
Speedup is the ratio of serial to parallel execution time.

$$
S_{speedup} = \frac{T_{serial}}{T_{parallel}} = \frac{T_{serial}}{(1 - f_{par}) \cdot T_{serial} + \dfrac{f_{par} \cdot T_{serial}}{N}}
$$

### Step 3: Simplify by Cancelling $T_{serial}$

$$
S_{speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - f_{par}) + \dfrac{f_{par}}{N}}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $f_{par} = 0.9$, $N = 8$

$$
S_{speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.9) + \dfrac{0.9}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.1125}
$$

$$
S_{speedup} = \frac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.706
$$

### Step 5: Compute Efficiency

$$
E_{ff} = \frac{S_{speedup}}{N} = \frac{4.706}{8} \approx 0.588 \; (58.8\%)
$$

### Step 6: Recompute for $N = 16$

$$
S_{speedup,16} = \frac{1}{0.1 + \dfrac{0.9}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.05625} = \frac{1}{0.15625} = 6.4
$$

### Step 7: Marginal Gain (Doubling Cost)

$$
\Delta S = S_{16} - S_{8} = 6.4 - 4.706 = 1.694
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the hardware cost (from 8 to 16 cores) yielded only a **1.69× speedup** because of the **serial bottleneck of 10%**. This is the classic Amdahl's Law trade-off and is a frequently tested KTU problem variant.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) with Coherence Miss

**Problem Setup:** A core in an SMP has an L1 hit rate of $H_{L1} = 0.95$, hit time $T_{L1} = 1$ cycle, and a base miss penalty to local L2 of $T_{L2} = 20$ cycles. Of the L1 misses, **10% are coherence misses** (extra 40 cycles to fetch a modified line from another cache) and **90% are cold/capacity misses**. Compute AMAT.

### Step 1: Define AMAT Equation

$$
T_{avg} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_{L1}) \cdot T_{miss,weighted}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Weighted Miss Penalty

Let $f_{coh} = 0.10$ be the fraction of misses that are coherence misses and $f_{cold} = 0.90$ be the fraction that are cold/capacity misses.

$$
T_{miss,weighted} = f_{cold} \cdot T_{L2} + f_{coh} \cdot (T_{L2} + T_{coh})
$$

where $T_{coh} = 40$ cycles is the additional coherence penalty.

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

$$
T_{miss,weighted} = (0.90 \cdot 20) + (0.10 \cdot (20 + 40))
$$

$$
T_{miss,weighted} = 18 + (0.10 \cdot 60) = 18 + 6 = 24 \; \text{cycles}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Miss Rate

$$
(1 - H_{L1}) = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Final AMAT

$$
T_{avg} = 1 + (0.05 \cdot 24) = 1 + 1.2 = 2.2 \; \text{cycles}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The 10% coherence miss fraction **inflated the miss penalty from 20 to 24 cycles** (a 20% increase), despite being only one-tenth of total misses. This shows why **minimizing coherence traffic** (e.g., via proper data placement to avoid false sharing) is a critical optimization in SMP programming.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Bus Saturation Analysis

**Problem Setup:** A 16-core SMP has a shared bus of bandwidth $B_{bus} = 8$ GB/s. Each core generates an average memory request rate $R_{mem} = 0.2$ GB/s of traffic, and the L1 hit rate is $H_{L1} = 0.92$. Determine whether the bus is saturated.

### Step 1: Compute Per-Core Miss Traffic

$$
R_{miss} = (1 - H_{L1}) \cdot R_{mem} = (1 - 0.92) \cdot 0.2 = 0.08 \cdot 0.2 = 0.016 \; \text{GB/s}
$$

### Step 2: Aggregate Traffic from All $N$ Cores

$$
B_{required} = N \cdot R_{miss} = 16 \cdot 0.016 = 0.256 \; \text{GB/s}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Bus Utilization

$$
\rho = \frac{B_{required}}{B_{bus}} = \frac{0.256}{8} = 0.032 \; (3.2\%)
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

$$
\rho = 3.2\% \ll 100\% \implies \text{Bus is NOT saturated.}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Maximum Scalable Core Count

$$
N_{max} = \frac{B_{bus}}{R_{miss}} = \frac{8}{0.016} = 500 \; \text{cores}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Caveat:** This calculation ignores coherence traffic (BusRd, BusUpgr, Flush transactions), I/O traffic, and memory controller bottlenecking. In practice, $N_{max}$ for a single-bus SMP is typically **8–16 cores**, after which a switch fabric is mandated.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Snooping vs Directory

| Parameter | Snooping (Broadcast) | Directory (Centralized) |
|---|---|---|
| **Coherence Traffic** | $O(N)$ per transaction (broadcast) | $O(1)$ per message (point-to-point) |
| **Scalability** | Poor beyond $N \approx 16$ | Excellent up to thousands of cores |
| **Latency** | Low (single transaction) | Higher (directory lookup) |
| **Directory Storage** | Not required | $O(M)$ entries ($M$ = memory blocks) |
| **Implementation Cost** | Cheap (snooping logic in cache) | Expensive (directory memory + logic) |
| **Single Point of Failure** | None (distributed) | Yes (directory is centralized) |
| **Typical Use Case** | Small-scale SMP, on-chip cores | Large-scale NUMA, data centers |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Block Architecture of a Centralized Shared Memory System (SMP)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the high-level functional architecture of a classical Centralized Shared-Memory (SMP/UMA) system. It is **not a physical chip layout**, but a **logical block diagram** showing the data-flow paths between processors, caches, interconnect, and main memory.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Processor Core P1] --> L1A[L1 Cache of P1]
    P2[Processor Core P2] --> L1B[L1 Cache of P2]
    P3[Processor Core P3] --> L1C[L1 Cache of P3]
    P4[Processor Core P4] --> L1D[L1 Cache of P4]

    L1A --> SnoopA[Snoop Tag Array of P1]
    L1B --> SnoopB[Snoop Tag Array of P2]
    L1C --> SnoopC[Snoop Tag Array of P3]
    L1D --> SnoopD[Snoop Tag Array of P4]

    L1A --> BusArb[Central Bus Arbiter]
    L1B --> BusArb
    L1C --> BusArb
    L1D --> BusArb

    SnoopA -.Monitor.-> SharedBus
    SnoopB -.Monitor.-> SharedBus
    SnoopC -.Monitor.-> SharedBus
    SnoopD -.Monitor.-> SharedBus

    BusArb --> SharedBus[Shared Interconnect Bus or Crossbar Switch]

    SharedBus --> MemCtrl[Memory Controller]

    MemCtrl --> DRAMBank0[DRAM Bank 0]
    MemCtrl --> DRAMBank1[DRAM Bank 1]
    MemCtrl --> DRAMBank2[DRAM Bank 2]
    MemCtrl --> DRAMBank3[DRAM Bank 3]

    SharedBus --> IOController[I/O Bridge and Devices]
    SharedBus --> DirCtrl[Optional Directory Controller for Large N]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**

1. The **four processor cores** ($P_1$ through $P_4$) issue loads and stores.
2. Each L1 cache is paired with a **Snoop Tag Array** that continuously monitors the shared bus for coherence transactions.
3. The **Central Bus Arbiter** grants bus access to one core at a time using a fairness protocol (e.g., round-robin, priority-based).
4. The **Shared Interconnect** can be a single bus (for small $N$) or a crossbar switch (for medium $N$).
5. The **Memory Controller** interleaves access across multiple **DRAM banks** to maximize bandwidth.
6. The **Optional Directory Controller** is added when scaling beyond ~16 cores to replace broadcasting with point-to-point coherence messages.

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — MESI Coherence Protocol

This diagram traces the **M state transitions** of a single cache line as observed from one core's perspective. The transitions are triggered by local CPU events (PrRd, PrWr) and snooped bus events (Snoop).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> I : Cold Start

    I --> S : PrRd with BusRd snoop MISS
    I --> M : PrWr with BusRdX snoop MISS

    S --> S : PrRd with BusRd snoop HIT
    S --> M : PrWr with BusUpgr

    M --> S : Snoop BusRd with Flush
    M --> I : Snoop BusRdX with Flush

    E --> E : PrRd
    E --> M : PrWr silent

    S --> I : Snoop BusRdX invalidate
    S --> I : Snoop BusUpgr invalidate
    M --> M : PrWr silent

    note right of M : Modified dirty line, sole valid copy, must write back on eviction
    note right of E : Exclusive clean line, sole valid copy, can silent upgrade to M
    note right of S : Shared clean line, multiple valid copies exist
    note right of I : Line absent or invalid, any access triggers bus transaction
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation Tip:** The transitions `S → M` and `S → I` triggered by **BusUpgr** are particularly important in exam questions. BusUpgr is a **silent invalidation** — the line in the upgrading cache changes state, and all other copies are invalidated, but **no data is transferred on the bus** (unlike BusRdX, which may transfer data in some implementations).

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Cache Read Miss Resolution

The following diagram shows the **decision flow** that the cache controller and bus arbiter follow when a processor issues a read to a line that is **not in its L1 cache**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Processor issues PrRd] --> B{Line in L1?}
    B -->|Hit| C[Return data to CPU - 1 cycle]
    B -->|Miss| D[Issue BusRd on shared interconnect]
    D --> E{Arbiter grants bus?}
    E -->|No| F[Wait for grant]
    F --> E
    E -->|Yes| G[All snoop tags check tag in parallel]
    G --> H{Any cache has Modified copy?}
    H -->|Yes| I[That cache writes back to memory and provides data]
    H -->|No| J{Any cache has Shared copy?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Memory provides data, snooped cache asserts Shared]
    J -->|No| L[Memory provides data from DRAM]
    I --> M[Requesting cache loads line in S or E state]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Return data to requesting CPU]
```

**Key Takeaway from the Flowchart:** A single L1 read miss can require **1 bus arbitration + 1 DRAM access + 1 cache write-back (in the worst case)**. This is why **minimizing L1 miss rate** is the single most important optimization for SMP performance.

## 4.4 Performance Trade-off Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Single Bus SMP] -->|N up to 8| B[Simple, low cost, low latency]
    A -->|N more than 8| C[Bus saturation, severe contention]

    D[Crossbar SMP] -->|N 8 to 32| E[Higher bandwidth, scalable]
    D -->|N more than 32| F[Cost grows as N squared]

    G[Directory SMP] -->|N 32 to 256| H[Scales to large systems]
    G -->|N more than 256| I[Directory becomes bottleneck]

    J[NUMA Distributed] -->|N 256 plus| K[Massive scale, complex programming]
```
