# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Centralized Shared-Memory Architecture. List any two of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Centralized Shared-Memory Architecture is a parallel computer organization in which **multiple identical processors** share a **single, physically centralized main memory** through a common interconnection network. The defining feature is that **all processors have equal (uniform) access time to every memory location**, making it synonymous with **Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP)** or **Uniform Memory Access (UMA)** architecture. [1 mark for definition + 1 mark for SMP/UMA synonym]

**Key Characteristics (any two for 2 marks):**
1. **Single Physical Address Space:** All processors share one global memory map.
2. **Uniform Memory Access Latency:** Every memory location has the same access time from every processor.
3. **Cache Coherence:** Hardware protocols (e.g., MESI) maintain data consistency across private caches.
4. **Shared Interconnect:** A common bus, crossbar, or ring connects all processors to memory.
5. **Sequential or Relaxed Consistency Model:** Defines the legal ordering of memory operations.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is the MESI protocol? Briefly explain the role of the "Modified" state.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**MESI** is a **write-back cache coherence protocol** used in centralized shared-memory multiprocessors. The acronym stands for the four possible states of a cache line: **Modified, Exclusive, Shared, and Invalid**. [1 mark for acronym expansion + 1 mark for protocol purpose]

**Role of the Modified State (2 marks):**

When a cache line is in the **Modified (M) state**, it means:
- The line has been **written (modified) by the owning processor**.
- It is the **sole valid copy** in the entire system — all other caches must have the line in **Invalid (I)** state.
- The copy in **main memory is stale (out-of-date)**.
- The cache has the **responsibility to supply the data** to other processors on a snoop miss, and to **write the line back to memory** upon eviction or replacement.

The Modified state enables **write-back semantics**, reducing main memory traffic and improving performance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a Centralized Shared-Memory Multiprocessor with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of cache coherence in such systems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-way SMP has a base CPI of 1.0 (without memory stalls). Each core has an L1 cache with a hit rate of 90% and a hit time of 1 cycle. The miss penalty to main memory is 100 cycles. Compute the actual CPI, the speedup, and the efficiency of the system when running a program that is 80% parallel.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — SMP Architecture & Coherence [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram Description (2 Marks):**
A Centralized Shared-Memory Multiprocessor (SMP) consists of $N$ identical processor cores, each with a **private L1 instruction cache** and a **private L1 data cache**. These caches are connected via **snoop tag arrays** to a **shared system bus** (or crossbar switch). The bus is arbitrated by a **central arbiter** that grants access to one core at a time. The bus leads to a **memory controller** that interfaces with one or more **DRAM banks** forming the unified main memory. An **I/O bridge** connects peripheral devices to the same shared address space. *(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1.)*

**Architecture Walkthrough (3 Marks):**
- Each processor executes independently, fetching instructions and reading/writing data through its L1 cache.
- On an L1 miss, the cache controller issues a **BusRd** (read) or **BusRdX** (write) transaction on the shared bus.
- All other caches **snoop** the bus in parallel, checking their tag arrays for the requested address.
- If a cache has the line in **Modified (M)** state, it **intervenes** and supplies the data directly (write-back).
- If multiple caches have the line in **Shared (S)** state, the memory controller supplies the data from DRAM.

**Role of Cache Coherence (2 Marks):**
Cache coherence is essential to **maintain a single, system-wide consistent view of memory**. Without it, two processors could simultaneously hold **stale copies** of the same memory line in their private L1 caches, leading to **inconsistent program behavior** (e.g., lost updates, read of stale data). The MESI protocol enforces coherence by **tracking the state of every cache line** and **issuing invalidation or update messages** whenever a write occurs. This guarantees that any subsequent read by any other processor will return the most recently written value.

---

#### Part (b) — Performance Calculation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute the Memory Stall Cycles per Instruction [2 Marks]**

The miss rate is $1 - H_{L1} = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$ and the miss penalty is $T_{miss} = 100$ cycles.

$$
T_{stall} = \text{Miss Rate} \times \text{Miss Penalty} = 0.10 \times 100 = 10 \; \text{cycles per memory instruction}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Actual CPI [2 Marks]**

Assuming the base CPI of 1.0 already accounts for execution, the memory stall is added:

$$
CPI_{actual} = CPI_{base} + T_{stall} = 1.0 + 10 = 11.0
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Speedup using Amdahl's Law [2 Marks]**

With $f_{par} = 0.80$ (parallel fraction) and $N = 4$ (processors):

$$
S_{speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - f_{par}) + \dfrac{f_{par}}{N}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.20} = \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.5
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Efficiency [1 Mark]**

$$
E_{ff} = \frac{S_{speedup}}{N} = \frac{2.5}{4} = 0.625 = 62.5\%
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Actual CPI = **11.0**
- Speedup = **2.5×**
- Efficiency = **62.5%**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the MESI cache coherence protocol. Show the state transition table and explain any two transitions in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider an 8-core SMP system. Each core has an L1 hit rate of 95% and a memory request rate of 1 GB/s to the shared bus. The shared bus has a total bandwidth of 4 GB/s. (i) Compute the total memory traffic demand. (ii) Determine whether the bus is saturated. (iii) If 20% of all L1 misses are coherence misses, compute the additional coherence traffic and the new bus utilization.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — MESI Protocol Explanation [7 Marks]

**MESI State Definitions (2 Marks):**
*(Refer to the Mermaid state diagram in Section 4.2 for visual reference.)*

- **Modified (M):** Line is dirty, sole valid copy, memory is stale.
- **Exclusive (E):** Line is clean, sole valid copy, memory is up-to-date.
- **Shared (S):** Line is clean, multiple valid copies exist, memory is up-to-date.
- **Invalid (I):** Line is absent or the cached copy is not valid.

**State Transition Table (2 Marks):**

| Current State | PrRd (Local Read) | PrWr (Local Write) | Snoop BusRd | Snoop BusRdX / BusUpgr |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | Issue BusRd → S | Issue BusRdX → M | No change | No change |
| S | Hit (no bus action) | Issue BusUpgr → M | Stay in S | → I |
| E | Hit (no bus action) | Silent → M (no bus) | → S | → I |
| M | Hit (no bus action) | Hit (no bus action) | Write-back → S | Write-back → I |

**Detailed Explanation of Two Transitions (3 Marks):**

**Transition 1: S → M on Local Write (PrWr) [1.5 Marks]**
When a processor with a line in **Shared (S)** state issues a write, the cache controller must **invalidate all other copies** of the line in the system. It issues a **BusUpgr** (bus upgrade) transaction, which is a special message that does not transfer data but signals all other caches to mark their copies as **Invalid (I)**. After the bus transaction completes, the local cache silently transitions to **Modified (M)** without needing to read the line from memory (since it already has a valid copy). This is called a **silent upgrade**.

**Transition 2: M → S on Snoop BusRd [1.5 Marks]**
When a cache holds a line in **Modified (M)** state and snoops a **BusRd** from another processor (meaning another core wants to read the line), the current owner has two responsibilities: (1) it must **write the dirty data back to main memory** to maintain coherence, and (2) it must provide the data to the requesting cache. After this transaction, both caches hold the line in **Shared (S)** state, and main memory has the up-to-date value. This is called a **cache-to-cache flush** and is a critical write-back operation in MESI.

---

#### Part (b) — Bus Bandwidth Analysis [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $N = 8$ cores
- $H_{L1} = 0.95$
- $R_{mem} = 1$ GB/s per core
- $B_{bus} = 4$ GB/s
- $f_{coh} = 0.20$ (20% of L1 misses are coherence misses)

**Step (i): Total Memory Traffic Demand [2 Marks]**

The miss rate is $1 - H_{L1} = 0.05$.

$$
R_{miss} = (1 - H_{L1}) \times R_{mem} = 0.05 \times 1 = 0.05 \; \text{GB/s per core}
$$

$$
B_{required} = N \times R_{miss} = 8 \times 0.05 = 0.4 \; \text{GB/s}
$$

**Step (ii): Bus Saturation Check [2 Marks]**

$$
\rho = \frac{B_{required}}{B_{bus}} = \frac{0.4}{4} = 0.10 = 10\%
$$

Since $\rho = 10\% \ll 100\%$, the bus is **NOT saturated**. There is significant headroom for additional workload.

**Step (iii): Coherence Traffic and New Utilization [3 Marks]**

Coherence misses generate **additional bus traffic** in the form of BusRd, BusRdX, BusUpgr, and Flush transactions. Assuming each coherence miss adds one extra bus transaction of equivalent size:

$$
B_{coh} = f_{coh} \times B_{required} = 0.20 \times 0.4 = 0.08 \; \text{GB/s}
$$

$$
B_{total} = B_{required} + B_{coh} = 0.4 + 0.08 = 0.48 \; \text{GB/s}
$$

$$
\rho_{new} = \frac{B_{total}}{B_{bus}} = \frac{0.48}{4} = 0.12 = 12\%
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Total memory traffic demand = **0.4 GB/s**
- (ii) Bus utilization = **10%** (not saturated)
- (iii) Additional coherence traffic = **0.08 GB/s**; new bus utilization = **12%**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing UMA with NUMA:** Students frequently interchange these terms. UMA = **uniform** access time (centralized). NUMA = **non-uniform** access time (distributed). This distinction is heavily weighted in 7-mark questions.
> 2. **MESI state transitions:** Many students forget that the **S → M transition is a BusUpgr** (no data transfer), whereas **I → M is a BusRdX** (data may be transferred). This is a common 1-mark loss point.
> 3. **Amdahl's Law vs Gustafson's Law:** Amdahl assumes a **fixed problem size** (constant work). Gustafson assumes **scaled problem size** (work grows with $N$). KTU 2024 problems often specify which to use; misapplying the wrong one loses full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring coherence traffic in bus analysis:** In the bus saturation problem above, students often forget to add the coherence overhead, leading to an underestimation of bus utilization by up to 20%.
> 5. **AMAT vs CPI:** AMAT is in **cycles per memory access**; CPI is in **cycles per instruction**. These are not interchangeable. Convert using the memory access rate (e.g., 1 memory access per instruction).
> 6. **Forgetting the write-back in M state:** When a Modified line is evicted, the cache **must** write the data to memory. Forgetting this in a 3-mark question costs full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **CSMA = SMP = UMA:** Centralized Shared-Memory, Symmetric Multiprocessor, and Uniform Memory Access are **synonymous terms** in the KTU 2024 syllabus.
- [x] **Single Address Space:** All cores share **one global physical memory**.
- [x] **Uniform Latency:** Every memory location takes the **same time** to access from any core.
- [x] **Private Caches + Coherence Protocol:** Each core has private L1/L2 caches; a coherence protocol (MESI) maintains consistency.
- [x] **M = Modified (dirty, sole valid, write back on eviction).**
- [x] **E = Exclusive (clean, sole valid, can silent upgrade to M).**
- [x] **S = Shared (clean, multiple valid copies).**
- [x] **I = Invalid (absent, triggers bus transaction).**
- [x] **BusUpgr** invalidates others **without data transfer** (S → M).
- [x] **BusRdX** invalidates others **and may transfer data** (I → M).
- [x] **Flush** is the **write-back** of a Modified line (M → S or M → I).
- [x] **Snooping** is for **small $N$ (≤ 16)**; **Directory** is for **large $N$ (≥ 32)**.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f_{par}) + f_{par}/N}$ — **fixed workload**.
- [x] **Gustafson's Law:** $S = (1 - f_{par}) + f_{par} \cdot N$ — **scaled workload**.
- [x] **AMAT:** $T_{avg} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_{L1}) \cdot T_{miss}$.
- [x] **Efficiency:** $E_{ff} = S_{speedup} / N$.
- [x] **Bus Saturation Check:** $\rho = B_{required} / B_{bus}$; saturated when $\rho \to 1$.
- [x] **Synchronization Primitives:** Test-and-Set, Compare-and-Swap, Fetch-and-Add, Memory Fences.
- [x] **False Sharing:** Unrelated variables in the same cache line cause **unnecessary coherence traffic**.
- [x] **Memory Interleaving:** Lower address bits select the bank, enabling parallel DRAM access.
- [x] **Real-World Examples:** Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC (intra-chiplet), ARM big.LITTLE clusters, IBM zSeries mainframes.
- [x] **Bridge to NUMA:** When $N$ grows beyond bus capacity, the architecture evolves into Distributed Shared Memory (NUMA), covered in subsequent KTU modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cache Coherence and Snooping Protocol (Implementation Details Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cache Coherence and Snooping Protocol (Implementation Details Not Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cache Coherence** is a critical concern in multiprocessor and multicore architectures that arises when multiple processing cores (or processors) maintain their own private caches, but share a common main memory. When two or more caches hold a copy of the same memory block (line), any write to that block by one core must be propagated to all other caches that hold a copy, ensuring that all cores observe a **uniform, consistent view** of memory.

A memory system is said to be *coherent* if it satisfies all three conditions formulated by Goodman (1991):

1. **Write Propagation (Update Visibility):** A write to a memory location by processor P_i must eventually become visible to all other processors P_j (j ≠ i).
2. **Transaction Serialization (Read/Write Ordering):** All writes to the same memory location must be observed in the *same order* by all processors. If A writes to location X and then B writes to X, no processor can observe B's write before A's write.
3. **Read Consistency (Coherence):** Once a processor P_i has read a value V from location X, it must not subsequently read an older (stale) value of X.

A **Snooping Protocol** is a distributed cache coherence mechanism where each cache controller continuously *monitors (snoops)* all transactions broadcast on a shared interconnect (typically a bus). When a cache controller detects a transaction that affects a block it holds, it takes appropriate coherence action (invalidate, update, supply data, or change state) based on the protocol's state machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Tag (KTU 2024):** The phrase *"Implementation Details Not Required"* in the topic indicates that students must focus on the *conceptual behavior*, *state transitions*, *protocol classification*, and *trade-offs* of snooping schemes — NOT on circuit-level bus arbitration, signal timing, or hardware FSM design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Shared Whiteboard Problem)

Imagine a small group study room with **four students** preparing for the KTU exam. They all have **personal notebooks** (private caches) and there is **one whiteboard** at the front of the room (shared main memory).

- At the start, all students copy the formula sheet onto their notebooks — they all have the **same value**.
- Now, *Student A* realizes the formula in the textbook is wrong and erases/rewrites it on the whiteboard. However, *Students B, C, D* still have the **old, incorrect formula** in their notebooks.
- If *Student B* glances at his notebook, he will write down a **wrong answer** in the exam.

**The Coherence Problem:** Student A's update must be propagated to everyone.

**The Snooping Solution:** Before any student *looks at their notebook*, they must first "snoop" — *glance at the whiteboard* — to check whether someone has updated the shared information. The shared bus acts like a public announcement channel: every write is *announced* loudly so all other caches can update or invalidate their copies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Summary:** A cache coherence protocol is essentially a set of *rules of engagement* that govern who gets to write, who must erase their old copy, and who must fetch the latest value, ensuring nobody works with stale data.

## 1.3 Why Cache Coherence is Necessary

In a single-core system, the cache hierarchy is private — coherence between L1, L2, and main memory is handled invisibly by hardware. In **multiprocessor systems** (Symmetric Multiprocessors — SMPs, or modern multicore chips), the situation changes:

| Scenario | Single Core | Multicore / Multiprocessor |
|---|---|---|
| Cache Copies | One per level | Many private L1/L2 caches |
| Data Sharing | None | Common shared variables |
| Update Path | Hardware transparent | Requires explicit coherence |
| Stale Data Risk | None | High (if unmanaged) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without coherence, the **Memory Consistency Model** promised by the ISA (e.g., sequential consistency) is broken, leading to nondeterministic and incorrect program behavior — a fatal flaw in shared-memory parallel programming.

## 1.4 Terminology Reference

- **Block / Cache Line:** The smallest unit of data transfer between memory and cache (typically 32, 64, or 128 bytes).
- **Home Location:** The memory module or node that physically owns a block in the global address space.
- **Local Cache:** The L1/L2 cache attached to a particular core.
- **Snooping / Snoop:** The act of monitoring bus traffic for transactions involving addresses currently held in the local cache.
- **Invalidation:** A directive that forces other caches to discard (mark as "not present") their copy of a block.
- **Update / Write-Broadcast:** A directive that forces other caches to overwrite their copy with the new value.
- **Dirty Block:** A modified cache line that differs from the value in main memory (the cache is the *owner* of the latest value).
- **Stale Block:** A cache line whose value no longer matches the value held by another cache or by main memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Coherence Problem in Detail

When two cores `C1` and `C2` both have a copy of block `X` in their L1 caches, three undesirable outcomes are possible:

1. **Read–Write Race:** `C1` writes new value `V'` to its cache (write-back policy). `C2` later reads from its cache and gets the *old* value `V` — a **stale read**.
2. **Write–Write Race:** `C1` writes `V1`, `C2` writes `V2`. A third core `C3` reading the block on either `C1` or `C2` may see either value, violating **transaction serialization**.
3. **Migration Inconsistency:** A block is migrated between cores but the *migrated value* is not propagated, leaving some caches with old content.

## 2.2 Classification of Coherence Protocols

Coherence protocols are broadly divided into two families:

### A. Snooping (Bus-Based) Protocols
- All caches share a **common broadcast medium** (a bus or a ring).
- Every cache *snoops* every transaction.
- **Advantage:** Simpler; leverages broadcast.
- **Limitation:** Scalability constrained by bus bandwidth; practical for small-to-medium core counts (historically up to 16–32 cores).

### B. Directory-Based Protocols
- A centralized or distributed **directory** tracks which caches hold which blocks.
- Point-to-point messages replace the bus.
- **Advantage:** Scales to many cores.
- **Limitation:** Directory is a single point of contention and complex to implement.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** This module emphasizes *Snooping Protocols* only. Directory protocols are typically covered in depth in subsequent courses/modules.

## 2.3 Snooping Protocol Variants

Within snooping protocols, two fundamental write-handling strategies exist:

| Strategy | Mechanism | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Write-Invalidate** | Writer sends a *BusUpgr / BusInvalidate* signal; all other caches discard (invalidate) their copies. | Generates one bus transaction per write *burst* (not per write); works well when multiple writes occur between reads. | Other cores must re-fetch on next read (latency penalty). |
| **Write-Update (Write-Broadcast)** | Writer broadcasts the *new value* on the bus; all other caches update their copies in place. | Other cores always have the latest value — no re-fetch needed. | Every write uses bus bandwidth, even if no other core will read the block again (wasteful). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Trade-off Insight:** Write-Invalidate is preferred in practice because workloads typically exhibit *spatial and temporal locality* — a block is written a few times, then read by many. Invalidation amortizes the bus cost. Modern protocols (MESI, MOESI) are all write-invalidate variants.

## 2.4 The MSI Protocol — The Foundation

The simplest coherent state machine is **MSI**, where each cache line can be in one of three states:

- **M — Modified (Dirty Exclusive):** The cache holds the only valid copy, and it has been modified. The value in main memory is stale. The cache must *write-back* on eviction or when another core requests the block.
- **S — Shared (Clean Shared):** The cache holds a clean, read-only copy. One or more other caches may also hold a valid copy. The cache cannot write without first gaining exclusive ownership.
- **I — Invalid:** The cache line is not present, or has been invalidated. Any read or write must trigger a bus transaction.

### State Transition Triggers (Conceptual)

- **Local Read Hit:** No state change.
- **Local Read Miss:** BusRd issued → cache fills in **Shared** (S) state.
- **Local Write Miss / Write to S line:** BusRdX (Read with Intent to Modify, or BusUpgr) issued → other caches invalidate → cache transitions to **Modified (M)**.
- **Local Write Hit on M:** No bus transaction.
- **Snoop on other core's BusRd:** If in M → must intervene, supply data, transition to **S** (write-back to memory or transfer to requestor).
- **Snoop on other core's BusRdX / BusUpgr:** If in M or S → invalidate, transition to **I**.

## 2.5 The MESI Protocol — Adding the Exclusive State

**MESI** (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) is the most widely used snooping protocol. The **E (Exclusive)** state solves a common inefficiency in MSI:

- In MSI, a core that wants to write must first issue a *BusRd* (to read the block from memory), then later a *BusRdX* (to invalidate other copies) — **two bus transactions**.
- In MESI, a *BusRd* that finds the block in *no other cache* allows the requestor to enter the **E** state directly. A subsequent write to E requires only a *BusUpgr* (no data transfer, no memory read) — **one bus transaction**.

This optimizes the **read-modify-write** sequence typical in synchronization primitives (locks, semaphores).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The *Exclusive* state is a **performance optimization**, not a correctness requirement. It eliminates the need for a memory fetch when transitioning from shared to modified, which is the dominant cost in mutex-style code.

## 2.6 The MOESI Protocol — Adding the Owned State

The **MOESI** protocol (AMD Opteron, etc.) adds an **O (Owned)** state. In MESI, when a cache supplies a shared block in response to a snoop, it transitions to **S** and main memory is updated. In MOESI, the supplier transitions to **O**, retaining the dirty data and acting as the *owner* — main memory is *not* updated immediately, saving a write-back. The Owned state means *"I have the latest dirty copy, and I will supply it to anyone who asks."*

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metrics Sheet

| Metric / Formula | Description | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $N_{\text{cores}}$ | Number of processors/cores sharing the bus | 2 – 64 |
| $B_{\text{line}}$ | Cache line size (block size) | 32, 64, 128 B |
| $T_{\text{transaction}}$ | Time to complete one coherence transaction | 10 – 100 ns |
| $B_{\text{bus}}$ | Bus bandwidth | GB/s |
| $f_{\text{bus}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{coherence\,tx}} \times B_{\text{line}}}{T_{\text{window}}}$ | Coherence bus traffic rate | Transactions/s |
| $C_{\text{protocol}}$ | State storage per line (MSI: 2 bits, MESI: 2 bits, MOESI: 3 bits) | bits/line |
| $S_{\text{coherence}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{false\,sharings}}}{N_{\text{total\,writes}}}$ | False sharing ratio (lowers coherence efficiency) | 0 – 1 |
| $R_{\text{amortized}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{setup}} + N \times T_{\text{write}}}{N}$ | Amortized per-write cost for N consecutive writes | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Rule of Thumb:** In a write-invalidate protocol, $N$ consecutive writes to the same block by one core (while no other core is reading) cost **only 1 bus transaction** — the initial invalidation. After that, the writer is in the *Modified* state and writes are purely local.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chip Multiprocessors (CMPs):** Intel Core i7/i9, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series — all use variants of MESI (Intel uses **MESIF** with a *Forward* state; AMD uses **MOESI**).
- **GPU Computing:** Coherence in GPUs is weak/absent in traditional designs; newer unified-memory architectures (HBM, NVLink) are bringing coherent protocols into the GPU domain.
- **Server-Class Systems:** Large SMP servers (e.g., IBM POWER, Oracle SPARC) historically used snooping buses up to ~32 processors before transitioning to directory-based coherence.
- **Verification & Validation:** Understanding coherence is essential for race-condition analysis using tools like ThreadSanitizer, Valgrind Helgrind, and formal verification (Coq, TLA+).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Coherence Action Triggers

The table below maps every event in a snoopy system to the resulting state change, with full reasoning. This is the kind of matrix KTU examiners expect students to be able to draw and explain.

| Current State | Local Event | Bus Transaction Issued | Snoop Response (from other caches) | New State | Cost (Bus Tx) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | Read Miss | BusRd | None, or Shared-data supply, or Memory supply | S or E | 1 |
| I | Write Miss | BusRdX | Invalidations + data supply | M | 1 |
| S | Read Hit | None | — | S | 0 |
| S | Write | BusUpgr (if already in S) **or** BusRdX (if going from I) | All S → I | M | 1 |
| E | Read Hit | None | — | E | 0 |
| E | Write | None (silent transition) | — | M | 0 |
| M | Read Hit | None | — | M | 0 |
| M | Write Hit | None | — | M | 0 |
| M | Snoop BusRd (other core reads) | — (intervenes, supplies data, may write-back) | — | S | 1 (intervention) |
| M | Snoop BusRdX (other core writes) | — (intervenes, supplies data) | — | I | 1 (intervention) |
| S | Snoop BusRdX / BusUpgr | — | — | I | 0 (just drops) |
| E | Snoop BusRd (other core reads) | — | — | S (downgrade) | 1 (silent snoop) |

## 3.2 Detailed Step-by-Step: Counting Bus Transactions

Let us work through a **K-class exam problem** to determine the total coherence cost of a small parallel code fragment.

### Problem Statement
Two cores, `P1` and `P2`, execute the following pseudocode concurrently. Each variable `X`, `Y`, `Z`, `W` is initially present in main memory only (not in any cache). Assume the block size is one variable. All caches are initially in **Invalid (I)** for every variable. The protocol is **write-invalidate, write-back, write-allocate**.

```
P1:  X = 1;
P1:  Y = 2;
P1:  X = 3;
P2:  Z = 1;
P2:  W = 2;
P2:  Z = 3;
```

All writes are *not interleaved*; `P1` completes its three statements before `P2` starts. Count the **total number of bus transactions** (BusRd, BusRdX, BusUpgr, flush) required to maintain coherence.

### Step 1 — Trace P1's Execution

**Statement 1: `X = 1;`**
- Cache state of P1 for `X`: I.
- Action: Write Miss → must obtain block.
- Issue **BusRdX** (read with intent to modify).
- P1 transitions to **M** for `X`.
- *Bus transactions so far: 1*

**Statement 2: `Y = 2;`**
- Cache state of P1 for `Y`: I.
- Action: Write Miss.
- Issue **BusRdX** for `Y`.
- P1 transitions to **M** for `Y`.
- *Bus transactions so far: 2*

**Statement 3: `X = 3;`**
- Cache state of P1 for `X`: M.
- Action: Write Hit on a *Modified* line.
- No bus transaction needed; value is overwritten locally.
- *Bus transactions so far: 2* (unchanged)

### Step 2 — Trace P2's Execution

**Statement 4: `Z = 1;`**
- Cache state of P2 for `Z`: I.
- Action: Write Miss.
- Issue **BusRdX** for `Z`.
- P2 transitions to **M** for `Z`.
- *Bus transactions so far: 3*

**Statement 5: `W = 2;`**
- Cache state of P2 for `W`: I.
- Action: Write Miss.
- Issue **BusRdX** for `W`.
- P2 transitions to **M** for `W`.
- *Bus transactions so far: 4*

**Statement 6: `Z = 3;`**
- Cache state of P2 for `Z`: M.
- Action: Write Hit on Modified line.
- No bus transaction needed.
- *Bus transactions so far: 4* (unchanged)

### Step 3 — Final Tally and Interpretation

$$
N_{\text{bus}} = 4 \text{ transactions}
$$

**Interpretation using the amortization formula:**

$$
R_{\text{amortized}} = \frac{T_{\text{setup}} + N \times T_{\text{write}}}{N}
$$

For P1's three writes to two unique blocks, the cost is $\frac{2 \times T_{\text{tx}} + 3 \times T_{\text{write}}}{3}$. The second write to `X` is **free** because the line was already in the *Modified* state — no coherence traffic was generated. This is the *temporal locality of writes* that write-invalidate protocols exploit.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: False Sharing Penalty Analysis

### Problem
A 64-byte cache line holds 8 `int64` variables. Two threads, T1 and T2, each write to a *different* variable within the **same** cache line, but they never logically share data. The line ping-pongs between caches on every write.

### Derivation
- Let $W$ be the number of writes per second by each thread.
- Without false sharing, each thread's writes to its own line cost $W$ transactions/sec, with the line in *Modified* state throughout.
- With false sharing, every write by T1 invalidates T2's copy, and vice-versa. T2's next write must first issue a *BusRdX* to re-acquire the line.
- Effective transactions per thread per second:

$$
T_{\text{tx,FS}} = 2 \times W
$$

(The factor of 2 accounts for the *invalidation* traffic for the other thread's writes that this thread must observe, plus the *re-acquisition* cost.)

- **Coherence traffic inflation factor:**

$$
F_{\text{FS}} = \frac{T_{\text{tx,FS}}}{T_{\text{tx,clean}}} = \frac{2 \times W}{W} = 2
$$

So false sharing **doubles** the bus transaction rate, halving effective bus bandwidth for other cores.

### Mitigation Rule
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked about performance, always pad shared-but-independent data structures (e.g., counters in a parallel loop) to a multiple of the cache line size. This eliminates false sharing.

## 3.4 Detailed Step-by-Step: MESI State Trace

### Problem
Three cores P1, P2, P3 all execute the following sequence on the same block `B`, initially in *Invalid* in all caches:

```
P1: read B        // t = 1
P1: write B = 10  // t = 2
P2: read B        // t = 3
P2: write B = 20  // t = 4
P3: read B        // t = 5
P1: read B        // t = 6
```

Trace the state of `B` in each cache after every step, assuming **MESI write-invalidate write-back write-allocate**.

### Step-by-Step Trace

| t | Action | P1 state | P2 state | P3 state | Bus Tx | Memory |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial | I | I | I | — | V₀ |
| 1 | P1: read B | **E** | I | I | BusRd (no other cache has B → goes Exclusive) | V₀ |
| 2 | P1: write B = 10 | **M** | I | I | (Silent: E → M) | V₀ (stale) |
| 3 | P2: read B | **S** (downgrade + flush) | **S** | I | BusRd; P1 supplies data | V₁₀ |
| 4 | P2: write B = 20 | I (invalidated) | **M** | I | BusUpgr/BusRdX | V₁₀ (stale) |
| 5 | P3: read B | I | **O** (or S, depending on policy) | **S** | BusRd; P2 supplies data | V₁₀ (stale) |
| 6 | P1: read B | **S** | **O** | **S** | BusRd; P2 (or P3) supplies data | V₂₀ (updated) |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the state machine naturally *serializes* the writes (transactions 2, 4) — this is the **transaction serialization** property enforced by the bus. The order of BusRdX issuance is the global write order.

## 3.5 Derivation of State-Encoding Bit Cost

For a cache with $L$ lines, the per-line state storage cost in bits is:

$$
S_{\text{state}} = \lceil \log_2(\vert \Sigma \vert) \rceil \text{ bits per line}
$$

where $\vert \Sigma \vert$ is the number of states.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{MSI}}  &= \lceil \log_2(3) \rceil = 2 \text{ bits per line} \\
S_{\text{MESI}} &= \lceil \log_2(4) \rceil = 2 \text{ bits per line} \\
S_{\text{MOESI}}&= \lceil \log_2(5) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits per line} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Total coherence tag storage overhead for a cache of $L$ lines:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = L \times S_{\text{state}} \text{ bits}
$$

For a 64 KB cache with 64-byte lines, $L = 1024$ lines, MESI overhead = $1024 \times 2 = 2048$ bits = **256 bytes** of state storage — a negligible cost for the benefit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Snooping Architecture (Conceptual)

The following diagram illustrates the conceptual flow of a write-invalidate snoop transaction across a shared bus, mapping the interactions among the cores, caches, and main memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CoreP1["Processor Core P1"]
        CPU1["CPU Pipeline"]
        L1C1["L1 Cache Controller\nwith Coherence FSM"]
        CPU1 --> L1C1
    end

    subgraph CoreP2["Processor Core P2"]
        CPU2["CPU Pipeline"]
        L1C2["L1 Cache Controller\nwith Coherence FSM"]
        CPU2 --> L1C2
    end

    subgraph CoreP3["Processor Core P3"]
        CPU3["CPU Pipeline"]
        L1C3["L1 Cache Controller\nwith Coherence FSM"]
        CPU3 --> L1C3
    end

    BUS["Shared Coherence Bus\nBroadcast Medium"]

    MEM["Main Memory\nHome Location"]

    L1C1 -- "Snoop Transactions" --> BUS
    L1C2 -- "Snoop Transactions" --> BUS
    L1C3 -- "Snoop Transactions" --> BUS

    BUS -- "Memory Read/Write" --> MEM

    L1C1 -. "Snoop In" .-> BUS
    L1C2 -. "Snoop In" .-> BUS
    L1C3 -. "Snoop In" .-> BUS

    BUS -- "Invalidate / Data Response" --> L1C1
    BUS -- "Invalidate / Data Response" --> L1C2
    BUS -- "Invalidate / Data Response" --> L1C3
```

## 4.2 MESI State Transition Diagram (Conceptual)

The diagram below captures the conceptual state transitions for a single cache line under the MESI protocol. Each transition is labeled with the triggering event and any associated bus transaction.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InvalidI

    state "Invalid (I)" as InvalidI
    state "Shared (S)" as SharedS
    state "Exclusive (E)" as ExclusiveE
    state "Modified (M)" as ModifiedM

    InvalidI --> SharedS : Local Read Miss / BusRd / Other cache has copy
    InvalidI --> ExclusiveE : Local Read Miss / BusRd / No other cache has copy
    InvalidI --> ModifiedM : Local Write Miss / BusRdX

    SharedS --> SharedS : Local Read Hit
    SharedS --> ModifiedM : Local Write / BusUpgr or BusRdX

    ExclusiveE --> ExclusiveE : Local Read Hit
    ExclusiveE --> ModifiedM : Local Write / Silent Transition

    ModifiedM --> ModifiedM : Local Read or Write Hit / Silent
    ModifiedM --> SharedS : Snoop BusRd / Flush data to memory
    ModifiedM --> InvalidI : Snoop BusRdX / Flush data to requestor

    ExclusiveE --> SharedS : Snoop BusRd / Other core reads
    SharedS --> InvalidI : Snoop BusRdX or BusUpgr / Other core writes
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This block diagram emphasizes the *logical flow* of a coherence transaction (here, an *invalidation* flow triggered by a write) without delving into circuit-level bus arbitration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Core P1 issues STORE to Block B"] --> B{"P1 Cache Lookup\nState of B?"}
    B -- "I (Invalid)" --> C["Issue BusRdX on Shared Bus\nRequest Exclusive Ownership"]
    B -- "S (Shared)" --> D["Issue BusUpgr on Shared Bus\nRequest Ownership Upgrade"]
    B -- "E (Exclusive)" --> E["Silent Transition E to M\nNo Bus Transaction"]
    B -- "M (Modified)" --> F["Silent Write Hit\nNo Bus Transaction"]

    C --> G["All Other Caches Snoop BusRdX\nAny in M or S state invalidate to I\nAny in M also flushes data"]
    D --> G
    G --> H["P1 Cache updates line to M\nHolds Exclusive Dirty Copy"]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I["Subsequent Local Writes to B\nAre Silent, No Bus Traffic"]
```

## 4.4 Protocol Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Cache Coherence Protocol Family"] --> S["Snooping Bus Based"]
    P --> D["Directory Based"]

    S --> W1["Write Invalidate"]
    S --> W2["Write Update Broadcast"]

    W1 --> MSI["MSI Protocol\n3 States: Modified Shared Invalid"]
    W1 --> MESI["MESI Protocol\n4 States: Modified Exclusive Shared Invalid\nAdds E for Read Modify Write Optimization"]
    W1 --> MOESI["MOESI Protocol\n5 States: Modified Owned Exclusive Shared Invalid\nAdds O for Forwarding Dirty Data"]

    W2 --> WB["Write Broadcast Protocols\nDragon, Firefly\nSimpler but higher bus traffic"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Sample Aligned]**

### Question 1
**Define cache coherence. List the three conditions a memory system must satisfy to be considered coherent.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

Cache coherence is the property of a shared-memory multiprocessor system that ensures all processors observe a consistent view of memory, even when each maintains its own private cache.

The three conditions (Goodman, 1991) are:

1. **Write Propagation:** A write to a memory location by one processor must eventually become visible to all other processors.
2. **Transaction Serialization:** All writes to the same memory location must be observed in the same order by every processor.
3. **Read Consistency (Coherence):** A processor that has read a value V from a location must never subsequently read an older (stale) value of the same location.

---

### Question 2
**Differentiate between Write-Invalidate and Write-Update snooping protocols. Which is preferred and why?** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Write-Invalidate | Write-Update |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Writer invalidates other copies; other cores re-fetch on next read | Writer broadcasts new value; all copies updated in place |
| Bus Traffic | One transaction per *write burst*; lower if writes are clustered | One transaction per *write*; higher if writes are scattered |
| Read Latency for Others | Higher (cache miss on next read) | Lower (always up-to-date) |
| Preferred When | Temporal/spatial locality of writes is high (typical workloads) | Producer-consumer with frequent reads by many consumers |

**Write-Invalidate is preferred in practice** because most programs exhibit write locality — a block is written a few times in succession and then shared-read. The invalidation cost is amortized over many subsequent reads, and bus bandwidth is conserved.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Aligned with 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the MESI snooping cache coherence protocol with a clear state diagram. Describe the role of the *Exclusive (E)* state and why it improves performance over the simple MSI protocol.

**Model Answer:**

The **MESI protocol** is a write-invalidate, write-back snooping coherence protocol. Each cache line carries a 2-bit state tag and can exist in one of four mutually exclusive states:

- **Modified (M):** The line is valid, *dirty*, and held in *only this cache*. The value in main memory is stale. The cache is the sole owner.
- **Exclusive (E):** The line is valid, *clean*, and held in *only this cache*. The value matches main memory. No other cache holds a copy.
- **Shared (S):** The line is valid, *clean*, and one or more other caches may also hold a copy. The value matches main memory.
- **Invalid (I):** The line is not present in the cache (or has been invalidated).

**State Transitions (summary):**

1. **Read Miss from I:**
   - If no other cache has the block → transition to **E**. Only one bus transaction: *BusRd*.
   - If another cache has the block in S or M → transition to **S**. *BusRd* issued, data supplied by the holder.
2. **Write Miss from I:** Issue *BusRdX*. All other caches invalidate. Transition to **M**.
3. **Write Hit on S:** Issue *BusUpgr* (or *BusRdX*) to invalidate other copies. Transition to **M**.
4. **Write Hit on E:** *Silent* transition to **M** — no bus transaction, no memory fetch.
5. **Snoop BusRd (other core reads):**
   - If in **M** → flush data to memory (and/or to requestor), transition to **S**.
   - If in **E** → silently downgrade to **S** (no memory write needed; value is clean).
6. **Snoop BusRdX (other core writes):**
   - If in **M** → flush data, transition to **I**.
   - If in **E** or **S** → transition to **I** (no flush needed for clean lines).

**Role of the Exclusive (E) State — The Performance Optimization:**

In the simpler **MSI** protocol, when a core wants to modify a block, it must first perform a *BusRd* to fetch the block from memory (entering the S state) and then later issue a *BusRdX* to invalidate all other copies and gain exclusive ownership — **two bus transactions** and an unnecessary memory fetch.

In MESI, when a core reads a block and the snoop reveals that **no other cache holds a copy**, the core enters the **E** state directly. A subsequent write to an E-state line requires only a **silent transition to M** — *no bus transaction at all* (no BusUpgr, no BusRdX, no memory fetch). This is the critical optimization.

**Quantitative Improvement:**

Consider a critical-section acquisition sequence:

```
acquire_lock(L);
read_data();
modify_data();
release_lock(L);
```

- With **MSI**: Read → BusRd → S. Modify → BusRdX → M. **Total: 2 bus transactions per critical section.**
- With **MESI**: Read → BusRd (no other holder) → E. Modify → Silent → M. **Total: 1 bus transaction per critical section.**

The E state thus **eliminates one bus transaction per read-modify-write sequence**, which is the dominant access pattern for synchronization primitives and per-thread accumulator updates. For a workload with $N$ such operations, the savings are significant.

The E state does **not** affect correctness — it is purely a *latency and bandwidth optimization*. It is enabled by the fact that the snoop response during the *BusRd* tells the requestor whether the block is held elsewhere, providing the necessary "no other copy" information at no extra cost.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Consider a 4-core shared-memory multiprocessor using the MESI protocol. Initially, no cache holds block `X`. Trace the sequence of state changes in all four caches and on the bus for the following stream of memory operations, assuming write-back, write-allocate, and write-invalidate policies:

```
P1:  read  X       // time t1
P1:  write X = 100 // time t2
P2:  read  X       // time t3
P2:  write X = 200 // time t4
P3:  read  X       // time t5
P4:  write X = 300 // time t6
P1:  read  X       // time t7
```

**Model Answer — Step-by-Step Trace:**

| t | Operation | P1 State | P2 State | P3 State | P4 State | Bus Transaction | Memory (Home) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial | I | I | I | I | — | X = 0 |
| 1 | P1: read X | **E** | I | I | I | BusRd (no sharer → E) | 0 |
| 2 | P1: write X = 100 | **M** | I | I | I | Silent (E → M) | 0 (stale) |
| 3 | P2: read X | **S** (flushes 100) | **S** | I | I | BusRd; P1 supplies & writes-back | 100 |
| 4 | P2: write X = 200 | I (invalidated) | **M** | I | I | BusUpgr; P1 invalidates | 100 (stale) |
| 5 | P3: read X | I | **S** (flushes 200) | **S** | I | BusRd; P2 supplies & writes-back | 200 |
| 6 | P4: write X = 300 | I | I (invalidated) | I (invalidated) | **M** | BusRdX; P2 & P3 invalidate | 200 (stale) |
| 7 | P1: read X | **S** (receives 300) | I | I | **M** | BusRd; P4 supplies data | 200 → 300 (if flush) |

**Total bus transactions = 6** (BusRd at t1, silent at t2, BusRd at t3, BusUpgr at t4, BusRd at t5, BusRdX at t6, BusRd at t7 = 6 explicit transactions; the silent E→M at t2 is free).

**Observations for the Answer:**

- **Transaction Serialization in action:** The writes at t2 (P1), t4 (P2), t6 (P4) are serialized by the bus. Every later read sees the most recent write because every writer had to acquire the line exclusively.
- **Write-Invalidate efficiency:** The write at t6 invalidates P1, P2, P3 in a single bus transaction, even though three caches held copies.
- **Memory coherence at t7:** P1 reads `X` and obtains `300` from P4 (the current owner), confirming the system is coherent at every observable point.
- **Cost saved:** The E-state silently handles t2 — no bus transaction for the modification of a line already held exclusively.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the cache coherence problem in shared-memory multiprocessors. Discuss the role of the shared bus in snooping protocols and identify the two main write-handling strategies with their relative merits.

**Model Answer:**

**The Cache Coherence Problem:**

In a shared-memory multiprocessor, each processor (or core) typically has one or more levels of *private* cache to reduce memory access latency and bandwidth pressure on main memory. While caches are beneficial for performance, they introduce a critical correctness challenge: when two or more private caches hold a copy of the same memory block, and one cache modifies its copy (especially under a *write-back* policy, where the main memory is *not* updated on every write), the other caches continue to hold the **stale, old value**.

This phenomenon violates the fundamental abstraction of a single, linear shared-memory space that programmers rely on for correct parallel execution. Without explicit management, a parallel program could observe different values of the same variable from different cores at the same logical time — a violation of the memory consistency model and a source of nondeterministic, often catastrophic, bugs.

**The Role of the Shared Bus in Snooping Protocols:**

A **shared broadcast bus** is the medium through which all cache controllers communicate. Its defining properties are:

1. **Single-Writer at a Time:** The bus arbitration logic ensures that only one cache controller can drive (transmit on) the bus at any moment, providing *implicit serialization* of all bus transactions.
2. **Broadcast:** Every transaction placed on the bus is *visible to every other cache controller*, enabling the *snooping* mechanism.
3. **Total Order:** All bus transactions are observed in the same order by all observers. This total order is what guarantees the *transaction serialization* coherence property.

Each cache controller continuously *monitors* the bus for transactions whose address matches a block it currently caches. When a match occurs, the controller executes the protocol-specific action: *invalidate, supply data, change state, or remain passive*.

**The Two Write-Handling Strategies:**

1. **Write-Invalidate (Most Common):**
   - When a core wants to write to a block, it first acquires *exclusive ownership* by issuing a *BusRdX* (or a *BusUpgr* if the block is already in the clean shared state).
   - All other caches that hold a copy are forced to *invalidate* their entries, marking them as no longer present.
   - The writer now holds the *only valid copy* in the system (typically in the M state).
   - Subsequent writes by the same writer are local until another core requests the block.
   - **Merits:** Low bus traffic when writes are clustered; simple to reason about; matches the typical write-locality of real programs.
   - **Demerits:** Other cores incur a cache miss when they next read the block (latency penalty).

2. **Write-Update (Write-Broadcast):**
   - When a core writes, it broadcasts the *new value* on the bus, and all other caches holding the block update their copies in place.
   - The writer's state may remain Shared if multiple copies are kept coherent.
   - **Merits:** All copies are always up-to-date; readers never see a stale value.
   - **Demerits:** *Every* write consumes bus bandwidth, even if no other core will ever read the block again; performance degrades with write-intensive workloads.

**Why Write-Invalidate Dominates in Practice:** Real workloads exhibit *temporal locality of writes* — a block is typically written a few times in rapid succession (e.g., updating a counter, modifying a struct) before being read by another core. Under write-invalidate, only the *first* write of the burst incurs bus traffic (the invalidation); subsequent writes are local. Under write-update, every write consumes bus bandwidth, wasting it on writes that may never be observed by another core.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a suitable state diagram, describe the **MSI** cache coherence protocol. Compare it with the **MOESI** protocol and explain how the *Owned* state reduces memory write-back traffic.

**Model Answer:**

**The MSI Protocol:**

MSI is the simplest coherent state machine. Each cache line is in one of three states:

- **Modified (M):** The line is dirty; this cache holds the only valid copy, and main memory is stale.
- **Shared (S):** The line is clean; one or more caches may hold a copy that matches main memory.
- **Invalid (I):** The line is not present (or has been invalidated).

**State Transitions (MSI):**

1. **Local Read Miss (I → S):** Issue *BusRd*. If another cache has the block in M, it flushes the data to memory and transitions to S. The requestor enters S.
2. **Local Read Hit (S → S):** No state change.
3. **Local Write Miss (I → M):** Issue *BusRdX*. All other copies are invalidated. Requestor enters M.
4. **Local Write to Shared (S → M):** Issue *BusUpgr* (or *BusRdX*). All other S copies are invalidated. Local cache enters M.
5. **Local Write Hit on M (M → M):** No bus transaction.
6. **Snoop BusRd on M (M → S):** The cache must *intervene* — it supplies the latest data (either directly to the requestor or via memory) and transitions to S. **A write-back to memory occurs here.**
7. **Snoop BusRdX on M (M → I):** The cache supplies the data to the requestor and invalidates its own copy. **A write-back occurs.**
8. **Snoop BusRdX on S (S → I):** Silent invalidation, no data movement.

**Comparison: MSI vs MOESI:**

| Feature | MSI | MOESI |
|---|---|---|
| Number of States | 3 | 5 |
| Modified (M) | Yes | Yes |
| Owned (O) | Absent | **Added** |
| Exclusive (E) | Absent | Yes (also added) |
| Shared (S) | Yes | Yes |
| Invalid (I) | Yes | Yes |
| Memory Write-Back on Snoop BusRd from M | Always to memory | Avoided; owner retains data |
| Primary Use | Educational baseline | AMD Opteron, modern server CPUs |

**The Owned (O) State and Write-Back Reduction:**

In MSI, when a cache holds a line in the M state and another core issues a *BusRd*, the M-state cache must *write the data back to main memory* and transition to S. This is a **mandatory memory write** for every transition from M to S — even if the requesting core will soon write the line and discard the memory copy anyway.

In MOESI, when the M-state cache receives a *BusRd*, it transitions to the new **O (Owned)** state instead of S. The Owned state has the following semantics:

- The cache retains the **only up-to-date copy** of the line.
- Main memory is *not* updated (no write-back yet).
- The cache is now responsible for *forwarding the data* to any future requestor.

When a third core later issues a *BusRd* or *BusRdX* for the same block, the O-state cache **supplies the data directly** (cache-to-cache transfer), again avoiding a memory write-back. The data is only written to memory when the Owned line is **evicted** from the cache, or when a *BusRdX* arrives and the line is given up.

**Quantitative Benefit:**

Consider $K$ cores that all read a block modified by P1 in sequence. With MSI:
- $K$ read misses → $K$ snoop interventions → $K$ write-backs to memory.

With MOESI:
- The first read triggers a snoop intervention, but the data goes **cache-to-cache** (P1 → P2). P1 enters O state. No memory write-back.
- Subsequent reads continue to be served cache-to-cache from the current O-holder.
- Only when the line is *evicted* is the memory updated.

**Memory write-back traffic reduction: from $K$ write-backs down to 1 (or 0, if the line is evicted before any write to memory is needed).**

This optimization is particularly valuable in server workloads with large working sets and frequent read sharing of dirty data.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the *Exclusive* state in MESI explanations.** MESI is *not* MSI. Many students omit E and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing write-back and write-through** when describing protocol actions. The default in MESI/MOESI is **write-back**; this is essential to the Owned-state optimization.
> 3. **Not specifying the bus transaction** for each transition in traces (BusRd, BusRdX, BusUpgr). Examiners look for the explicit transaction name; just writing "P2 reads" is insufficient.
> 4. **Confusing coherence with consistency.** Coherence is *per-location* (a single address); consistency is *across memory locations* (the global ordering of all memory operations). Examiners WILL test this distinction.
> 5. **Failing to state the role of the bus in providing transaction serialization.** The bus is not just a "communication channel" — its *single-writer-at-a-time* property is the foundation of the serialization guarantee.
> 6. **In state-trace problems, forgetting to show the snoop response** of all OTHER caches, not just the acting one. The trace must show *every* cache's state.
> 7. **Miscounting silent transitions.** The E→M silent transition is *free*; examiners often award 1 mark for correctly identifying it.
> 8. **Ignoring false sharing** in performance-related questions. A complete MESI analysis must mention the *false sharing* pathology, especially when discussing multi-variable structures in the same cache line.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this just before walking into the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Coherence** ensures all cores see a consistent value for a *single* memory location. **Consistency** defines the global ordering of *all* memory operations. *Do not confuse them.*
- ✅ A coherent memory system must satisfy **three conditions**: Write Propagation, Transaction Serialization, and Read Consistency (Goodman's conditions).
- ✅ **Snooping protocols** rely on a *shared broadcast bus* whose single-writer-at-a-time property provides implicit total order to all coherence transactions.
- ✅ **Write-Invalidate** is preferred over **Write-Update** because real workloads exhibit temporal locality of writes; invalidation amortizes bus cost over a burst of writes.
- ✅ The **MSI** protocol has 3 states (M, S, I). Transitions involve **BusRd**, **BusRdX**, and **BusUpgr** transactions.
- ✅ The **MESI** protocol adds the **E (Exclusive)** state. E → M is a *silent* transition with **no bus transaction**, optimizing the read-modify-write pattern.
- ✅ The **MOESI** protocol adds the **O (Owned)** state, eliminating the memory write-back on every M → S snoop, retaining dirty data for cache-to-cache forwarding.
- ✅ Per-line state storage: MSI = 2 bits, MESI = 2 bits, MOESI = 3 bits (using $\lceil \log_2(\vert \Sigma \vert) \rceil$).
- ✅ **Transaction counts** for a write-invalidate write-back system: 1 bus transaction to gain exclusive ownership of a clean block; subsequent local writes are free.
- ✅ **False sharing** (independent variables in the same cache line) doubles bus traffic. *Pad data structures* to cache line boundaries to avoid it.
- ✅ The **snoop response** is determined by the current state of the line in the snooping cache. Always document the snoop action for every other cache in state-trace problems.
- ✅ Snooping protocols are **bandwidth-limited** by the shared bus; they scale poorly beyond ~32 cores, motivating the move to **directory-based** protocols in large systems.
- ✅ Common real-world variants: **MESIF** (Intel — adds *Forward* for efficient cache-to-cache transfer), **MOESI** (AMD — adds *Owned*), **MERSI** (some ARM cores).
- ✅ **Key Assumption in KTU problems:** "Implementation details not required" means students do *not* need to draw bus-arbitration state machines, signal-level timing diagrams, or MSI/MESI Verilog code. Focus on the *protocol behavior*, *state diagrams*, *transaction sequences*, and *performance analysis*.
- ✅ **Numerical metrics to memorize for KTU answers:**
  - $S_{\text{state}} = \lceil \log_2(\vert \Sigma \vert) \rceil$ bits per line.
  - Bus transaction amortization: $N$ writes after invalidation cost only the *first* write's transaction.
  - False sharing traffic inflation: factor of 2.
- ✅ **Examiner hot-button:** Always explain *why* a transition happens, not just *that* it happens. The "because E is exclusive" style of reasoning is rewarded.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance of Symmetric Shared-Memory Processors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance of Symmetric Shared-Memory Processors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Symmetric Shared-Memory Multiprocessor (SMP)** is a tightly coupled multiprocessor system in which two or more identical processor cores connect to a single, globally shared physical address space through a common interconnect (bus, crossbar, or network). The term "symmetric" signifies that all processors are peers — every processor has equal access latency, bandwidth, and privilege to the entire shared memory and I/O subsystem. The "performance" dimension of such a system is governed by the interplay of *processor speedup*, *memory bandwidth*, *cache coherence traffic*, *synchronization overhead*, and *memory consistency protocol cost*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECST528, Module 3):**
> *"Symmetric Shared-Memory Multiprocessors evaluate performance using metrics such as speedup, efficiency, scalability, and memory bandwidth utilization, under the constraints imposed by cache coherence, memory consistency, and synchronization primitives."*

### Key Architectural Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Speedup** | $S(N)$ | Ratio of execution time on 1 processor to time on $N$ processors |
| **Efficiency** | $E(N)$ | Speedup divided by number of processors $N$ |
| **Memory Bandwidth** | $B_m$ | Bytes transferred per second between cache hierarchy and DRAM |
| **Cache Miss Rate** | $r_m$ | Fraction of memory accesses that miss in last-level cache |
| **Coherence Traffic** | $T_{coh}$ | Inter-processor messages required to maintain coherence |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Highway & Tollbooth Analogy**
> Imagine a city with **4 highways** (processors) all converging on **one central toll plaza** (shared memory bus). Each highway has its own **fast lane** (L1 cache) where cars (data) zip by instantly. But whenever a car needs to cross the plaza, it must:
> 1. **Check with the other 3 highways** (coherence check) to see if the toll booth already has the ticket (cache line).
> 2. **Stop if any other highway has the ticket** (invalidation or update).
> 3. **Wait in line** behind other cars (contention latency).
>
> Even if you have 4 highways, the **toll booth (memory bandwidth) becomes the bottleneck**. Adding more highways (processors) past a point gives *diminishing returns* — this is the essence of SMP performance limits.

## 1.3 Why Performance is Hard in SMP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Fundamental Bottlenecks of SMP Performance:**
> 1. **Memory Bandwidth Wall** — DRAM cannot feed data fast enough for many cores.
> 2. **Coherence Overhead** — Maintaining a consistent view of shared data requires expensive broadcast/snooping.
> 3. **Synchronization Cost** — Locks, barriers, and atomic operations serialize parallel work, reducing effective speedup.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of SMP Performance

The performance of an SMP is a layered function of four orthogonal subsystems:

### A. Processor-Level Performance (Amdahl's Law)

Speedup is bounded by the **sequential fraction** of the program. Amdahl's Law remains the foundational analytical tool for SMPs.

### B. Memory Hierarchy Performance

Effective memory access time is degraded by **coherence misses** — invalidations that remove a line from a private cache, forcing a re-fetch. There are three categories:

- **True sharing misses** — different processors access *different* words of the same cache line, but the line ping-pongs between caches.
- **False sharing misses** — different processors access *different* but *adjacent* variables that happen to reside on the same cache line; data isn't truly shared, but the line is.
- **Capacity / Cold misses** — independent of the multiprocessor configuration.

### C. Interconnection Performance

The shared bus or interconnect must serialize all memory transactions. As $N$ grows, bus **bandwidth saturation** reduces per-processor throughput.

### D. Synchronization Overhead

Locks, barriers, and atomic instructions cause **idle time** on processors. Lock contention turns parallel speedup into a flat-line.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Amdahl's Speedup** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | $N$ = processors, $f$ = parallel fraction |
| 2 | **Gustafson's Scaled Speedup** | $S_G(N) = N - f \cdot (N - 1)$ | Scaled problem size |
| 3 | **Efficiency** | $E(N) = \dfrac{S(N)}{N}$ | Ideal = **1.0 (100%)** |
| 4 | **Speedup Upper Bound** | $S(N) \le \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ | As $N \to \infty$ |
| 5 | **Effective CPI (Multiprocessor)** | $CPI_{eff} = CPI_{base} + \sum_{i} M_i \cdot P_i$ | $M_i$ = miss rate, $P_i$ = penalty |
| 6 | **Memory Stall Cycles** | $T_{stall} = (M_{L1} + M_{L2} + \ldots) \times P_{mem}$ | Per instruction |
| 7 | **Coherence Miss Rate** | $r_{coh} = r_{true} + r_{false}$ | Per processor |
| 8 | **False Sharing Rate** | $r_{false} = \dfrac{\text{Invalidations due to other cores' data}}{\text{Total misses}}$ | In fraction |
| 9 | **Bus Utilization (Snooping)** | $U_{bus} = N \cdot \lambda \cdot t_{trans}$ | $\lambda$ = request rate |
| 10 | **Lock Contention Cost** | $T_{lock} = T_{acquire} + T_{execute} + T_{release}$ | Critical section time |
| 11 | **Barrier Synchronization Cost** | $T_{barrier} = (N - 1) \cdot t_{msg}$ | Tree barrier |
| 12 | **Bandwidth Requirement** | $B_{req} = N \cdot CPI_{mem} \cdot f_{clk} \cdot W_{line}$ | Bytes/sec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Speedup $S(N) > N$ is **physically impossible** for a fixed workload — this is a direct consequence of Amdahl's Law. Floating-point arithmetic deviations due to non-determinism in reduction operations are the only KTU-accepted exceptions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

SMP performance analysis is the bedrock of:
- **Data center server design** (e.g., NUMA-aware scheduling in cloud VMs)
- **HPC cluster node engineering** (per-node multi-core utilization)
- **Mobile SoC design** (big.LITTLE configurations share memory)
- **Real-time embedded systems** (WCET analysis of shared-memory multi-core)
- **Database query parallelization** (Oracle RAC, PostgreSQL shared-buffers)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Amdahl's Law for SMP

**Given:** A program with parallel fraction $f$ runs on $N$ symmetric processors.

**Step 1:** Total execution time on a single processor is normalized to **1.0**.

$$T_1 = 1.0$$

**Step 2:** The execution time splits into two parts:

$$T_1 = T_{seq} + T_{par} = (1 - f) \cdot T_1 + f \cdot T_1$$

**Step 3:** Sequential portion remains unchanged (runs on 1 processor). Parallel portion divides across $N$ processors:

$$T_{par, N} = \frac{f \cdot T_1}{N}$$

**Step 4:** Total execution time on $N$ processors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_N &= T_{seq} + T_{par, N} \\
&= (1 - f) \cdot T_1 + \frac{f \cdot T_1}{N} \\
&= T_1 \cdot \left[ (1 - f) + \frac{f}{N} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Speedup is the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \frac{T_1}{T_N} \\
&= \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Limiting case as $N \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A 5% sequential fraction caps speedup at $\frac{1}{0.05} = 20\times$, no matter how many processors you add.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Speedup with Overhead

**Given:** System with parallel fraction $f$, overhead $T_o$ (synchronization, coherence, OS), $N$ processors.

**Step 1:** Execution time with overhead added to parallel portion:

$$T_N = (1 - f) + \frac{f}{N} + T_o$$

**Step 2:** Speedup:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N} + T_o}$$

**Step 3:** The optimal $N^*$ is found by differentiation. Taking derivative with respect to $N$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{dS}{dN} = 0 \implies N^* = \sqrt{\frac{f}{T_o}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Students often miss that overhead $T_o$ itself can grow with $N$ (e.g., lock contention). If $T_o(N) = k \cdot N^2$ (quadratic overhead), speedup peaks and then **decreases** — known as **performance inversion**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Memory Stall Time in SMP

**Given:** Per-instruction miss rates $M_i$ for L1 (private) and L2 (shared/inclusive), with penalties $P_i$.

**Step 1:** Base CPI includes ALU, branch, and L1 hit:

$$CPI_{base} = 1.0$$

**Step 2:** Memory stall per instruction:

$$T_{stall} = M_{L1} \cdot P_{L1} + M_{L2} \cdot P_{L2} + M_{L3} \cdot P_{L3}$$

**Step 3:** With $f_{clk}$ frequency and $W_{line}$ = 64 bytes cache line size, memory bandwidth demanded by $N$ cores:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{demand} &= N \cdot \frac{1}{CPI_{total}} \cdot f_{clk} \cdot M_{LLC} \cdot W_{line}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** If $B_{demand} > B_{DRAM}$, system is **bandwidth-bound**; speedup saturates.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A program has parallel fraction $f = 0.90$. Compute the speedup with $N = 8$ processors. What is the efficiency? What happens at $N = 64$?

**Step 1:** Apply Amdahl's Law at $N = 8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(8) &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.90) + \dfrac{0.90}{8}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.1125} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.2125} \\
&\approx 4.71
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Efficiency at $N = 8$:

$$E(8) = \frac{S(8)}{8} = \frac{4.71}{8} \approx 0.589 = 58.9\%$$

**Step 3:** Speedup at $N = 64$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(64) &= \frac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{64}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.01406} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.11406} \\
&\approx 8.77
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Efficiency at $N = 64$:

$$E(64) = \frac{8.77}{64} \approx 0.137 = 13.7\%$$

**Step 5:** Asymptotic limit:

$$S(\infty) = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10\times$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently compute $E(N) = 1 - f$ which is **wrong**. Efficiency is $S(N) / N$, not the parallel fraction.

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — False Sharing Cost

**Given:** A 64-byte cache line holds two 4-byte counters updated by two different processors every loop iteration.

**Step 1:** Cache line ping-pongs every iteration. Transfer time per miss = 50 ns (round-trip).

**Step 2:** Without false sharing: 1 miss per 16 accesses (counter size / line size):

$$T_{no\_fs} = 16 \times T_{hit} + T_{miss}$$

**Step 3:** With false sharing: 1 miss per access:

$$T_{fs} = T_{miss} \text{ per access}$$

**Step 4:** Slowdown:

$$S_{loss} = \frac{T_{fs}}{T_{no\_fs}} \approx 16\times$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remedy:** Pad variables to occupy entire cache line (e.g., `__attribute__((aligned(64)))` in C/C++) or restructure data layout.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — SMP Architecture with Coherence

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP1[CPU 0 Core] --> nodeL1A[L1 Cache 0]
    nodeL1A --> nodeL2A[L2 Cache 0]
    nodeP2[CPU 1 Core] --> nodeL1B[L1 Cache 1]
    nodeL1B --> nodeL2B[L2 Cache 1]
    nodeP3[CPU 2 Core] --> nodeL1C[L1 Cache 2]
    nodeL1C --> nodeL2C[L2 Cache 2]
    nodeP4[CPU 3 Core] --> nodeL1D[L1 Cache 3]
    nodeL1D --> nodeL2D[L2 Cache 3]
    nodeL2A --> nodeBUS[Shared Snooping Bus]
    nodeL2B --> nodeBUS
    nodeL2C --> nodeBUS
    nodeL2D --> nodeBUS
    nodeBUS --> nodeMEM[Main Memory DRAM]
    nodeMEM --> nodeDIR[Memory Controller with Directory]
    nodeBUS --> nodeIO[I/O Subsystem]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — MESI State Transition Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ModifiedState
    ModifiedState --> ExclusiveState: Snoop Read Hit
    ModifiedState --> InvalidState: Cache Eviction
    ModifiedState --> SharedState: Flush to Memory
    ExclusiveState --> ModifiedState: Local Write
    ExclusiveState --> SharedState: Snoop Read
    SharedState --> ModifiedState: Local Write Invalidate
    SharedState --> InvalidState: Snoop Invalidate
    InvalidState --> SharedState: Snoop Read Miss
    InvalidState --> ExclusiveState: Read Miss with No Sharers
    InvalidState --> ModifiedState: Read Miss with Write Intent
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — SMP Performance Bottleneck Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Start Workload on N Processors] --> nodeB1{Bandwidth Limited?}
    nodeB1 --> nodeYes1[Bottleneck: DRAM Bandwidth]
    nodeB1 --> nodeNo1{Coherence Traffic High?}
    nodeNo1 --> nodeYes2[Bottleneck: Bus Contention]
    nodeNo1 --> nodeNo2{Synchronization Heavy?}
    nodeNo2 --> nodeYes3[Bottleneck: Lock Contention]
    nodeNo2 --> nodeNo4{False Sharing Detected?}
    nodeNo4 --> nodeYes4[Bottleneck: Cache Line Ping-Pong]
    nodeNo4 --> nodeNo5[System Near Optimal Speedup]
    nodeYes1 --> nodeFix1[Solution: Add Memory Channels / 3D Stacking]
    nodeYes2 --> nodeFix2[Solution: Use Directory Protocol or Crossbar]
    nodeYes3 --> nodeFix3[Solution: Use Fine-Grained Locking or Lock-Free DS]
    nodeYes4 --> nodeFix4[Solution: Pad Data Structures to Cache Line]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Memory Consistency Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSC[Sequential Consistency] --> nodeTSO[Total Store Order TSO]
    nodeTSO --> nodePSO[Partial Store Order PSO]
    nodeSC --> nodeRC[Release Consistency]
    nodeRC --> nodeWC[Weak Consistency]
    nodeTSO --> nodeX86[x86 Memory Model]
    nodePSO --> nodeSPARC[SPARC Memory Model]
    nodeWC --> nodeARM[ARM PowerPC Memory Model]
    nodeRC --> nodeALPHA[Alpha Memory Model]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Schematic Interpretation:** The above diagrams collectively represent the **functional architecture** of an SMP system's performance characterization — from raw hardware topology (Diagram 4.1) to protocol state machine (4.2), bottleneck diagnosis (4.3), and consistency model selection (4.4). In KTU 2024 ESE questions, expect at least **one flowchart / block diagram question worth 7 marks** from this set.
