<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]
**Define symmetric shared-memory multiprocessor. List any two performance bottlenecks in such a system.**

**Model Answer:**

A *symmetric shared-memory multiprocessor* (SMP) is a multiprocessor system in which two or more identical processor cores share a single physical address space and I/O subsystem, with each processor having uniform memory access (UMA) time. The system is "symmetric" because every processor has equal capability and access latency to all memory locations.

**Two performance bottlenecks:**
1. **Memory Bandwidth Saturation** — As $N$ grows, the aggregate demand of all processors for memory bandwidth exceeds DRAM capacity, throttling overall performance.
2. **Cache Coherence Overhead** — Maintaining consistent shared data across $N$ private caches requires inter-processor messages (invalidation, update, or directory queries), consuming bus/interconnect cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition 2 marks + any 2 bottlenecks ½ mark each = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Question Paper]
**What is false sharing? How does it impact SMP performance?**

**Model Answer:**

*False sharing* occurs when two or more processors access *different* variables that happen to reside on the **same cache line**, causing unnecessary cache invalidations even though the processors are not actually sharing data.

**Impact on SMP performance:**
- Causes **cache line ping-ponging** between cores, where the line is repeatedly invalidated and re-fetched.
- Increases **coherence miss rate** artificially, increasing memory stall time.
- **Reduces effective speedup** — even programs with no true data sharing can suffer from parallel slowdown.
- **Remedy:** Use **cache line padding** (alignment) to ensure independent variables occupy separate cache lines.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]

**(a)** Explain Amdahl's Law in detail. Derive the expression for speedup with $N$ processors. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A program has 25% of its execution time in sequential code. Compute the speedup achievable with (i) 4 processors, (ii) 16 processors. Also find the maximum theoretical speedup. Comment on the efficiency in each case. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Amdahl's Law, formulated by **Gene Amdahl** in 1967, quantifies the theoretical maximum speedup of a parallel program on $N$ processors.

**Derivation:**

Let $T_1$ be the execution time on a single processor. The program is partitioned into:
- Sequential portion: $T_{seq} = (1 - f) \cdot T_1$
- Parallel portion: $T_{par} = f \cdot T_1$

On $N$ processors, the parallel portion is divided equally (ideal load balance):

$$T_{par,N} = \frac{f \cdot T_1}{N}$$

Total execution time on $N$ processors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_N &= T_{seq} + T_{par,N} \\
&= (1 - f) \cdot T_1 + \frac{f \cdot T_1}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Speedup is the ratio:

$$
S(N) = \frac{T_1}{T_N} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

As $N \to \infty$:

$$S(\infty) = \frac{1}{1 - f}$$

**Interpretation:** The sequential fraction $1 - f$ is an **inherent bottleneck** that no amount of parallelism can overcome.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Given: $1 - f = 0.25 \implies f = 0.75$

**(i) Speedup with N = 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \frac{1}{0.25 + \dfrac{0.75}{4}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.1875} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.4375} \\
&\approx 2.29
\end{aligned}
$$

Efficiency: $E(4) = \dfrac{2.29}{4} = 0.572 = 57.2\%$

**(ii) Speedup with N = 16:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(16) &= \frac{1}{0.25 + \dfrac{0.75}{16}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.0469} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.2969} \\
&\approx 3.37
\end{aligned}
$$

Efficiency: $E(16) = \dfrac{3.37}{16} = 0.211 = 21.1\%$

**Maximum theoretical speedup:**

$$S(\infty) = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4\times$$

**Comment:** Doubling processors from 4 to 16 (a 4× increase in $N$) yields only a 1.47× increase in speedup. The diminishing returns clearly demonstrate Amdahl's Law in action. The efficiency drops from 57.2% to 21.1%, indicating severe underutilization of added processors due to the 25% sequential bottleneck.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a symmetric shared-memory multiprocessor. Explain the function of the snooping bus in maintaining cache coherence. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What are memory consistency models? Explain sequential consistency and weak consistency models with examples. Compare their performance implications. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Architecture:**

A symmetric shared-memory multiprocessor consists of:
- **P processors** ($P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{N-1}$), each with its own private L1 and possibly L2 cache.
- A **shared system bus** (snooping bus) interconnecting all processors and memory.
- **Main memory** (DRAM) holding the unified address space.
- **I/O subsystem** accessible via the bus.
- **Memory controller** arbitrating bus access.

```
   P0   P1   P2   P3          (Processors)
   |    |    |    |
  L1   L1   L1   L1            (Private L1 Caches)
   |    |    |    |
  L2   L2   L2   L2            (Optional Private L2)
   \    |    |   /
    \   |    |  /
   =====BUS======              (Snooping Bus)
        |
   Main Memory + I/O
```

**Function of Snooping Bus in Cache Coherence:**

The snooping bus is a **broadcast medium** where every cache controller "snoops" (monitors) all bus transactions. Its role:

1. **Invalidation Protocol (MSI / MESI):** When $P_0$ writes to a cache line, it broadcasts an *invalidate* signal on the bus. All other caches check (snoop) whether they hold that line; if so, they mark it **Invalid**.
2. **Cache-to-Cache Transfer:** When $P_1$ requests a line held by $P_2$ in Modified state, the snooping bus enables $P_2$ to supply the data directly to $P_1$ without going to DRAM — a **cache-to-cache transfer** that reduces memory latency.
3. **Bus Arbitration:** Only one transaction at a time; the memory controller enforces priority/ordering.

**Advantage:** Simple, low-latency for small $N$ (typically 4–8 processors).  
**Disadvantage:** Bus bandwidth becomes the bottleneck as $N$ grows.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Memory Consistency Models:**

A *memory consistency model* defines the legal orderings of memory operations (loads and stores) observed by different processors in a shared-memory system. It is the **contract** between hardware and programmer.

**1. Sequential Consistency (SC):**

> *"The result of any execution is the same as if the operations of all processors were executed in some sequential order, and the operations of each individual processor appear in this sequence in the order specified by its program."* — Lamport, 1979.

**Example:** $P_0$ writes $A = 1$, $P_1$ reads $A$ and then reads flag.

```c
P0:  A = 1;
     flag = 1;

P1:  while (flag == 0);
     print(A);
```

Under SC, if $P_1$ sees `flag = 1`, it **must** see `A = 1`. SC ensures **program order** of all memory operations.

**Pros:** Simple programming model, intuitive.  
**Cons:** Restricts hardware and compiler optimizations; reduces performance.

**2. Weak Consistency (WC):**

Memory accesses are classified as *synchronization* (acquire/release) and *data*. Data accesses can be reordered; only **synchronization operations** act as memory barriers.

**Example:** Same code; under WC, the hardware is allowed to reorder the writes, so $P_1$ may see `flag = 1` before `A = 1`. To prevent this, programmer must use `fence` or `release` operations.

**Pros:** Higher performance — hardware can pipeline and reorder freely.  
**Cons:** Programmer responsibility; debugging is hard.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Sequential Consistency | Weak Consistency |
|---|---|---|
| **Ordering** | Strict total order | Per-variable, with sync ops as barriers |
| **Performance** | Lower | Higher |
| **Programming Ease** | Easy | Harder, requires fences |
| **Hardware Cost** | Higher (buffer flushes) | Lower (allows reordering) |
| **Used in** | Theoretical model | ARM, PowerPC |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Marks Losers):**
> 1. **Confusing cache coherence with memory consistency** — they are *different*. Coherence is about a *single* memory location; consistency is about *ordering* of all memory operations.
> 2. **Forgetting the word "legal"** when defining SC — SC does not prescribe a *specific* order, only that *some* order exists.
> 3. **Mis-drawing the MESI diagram** — students often omit transitions to/from Modified state, losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting bus arbitration** when describing snooping — single bus = serialized, even though "snooping" sounds parallel.
> 5. **Calculating $E = 1 - f$ instead of $E = S/N$** — this is a 1-mark trap in Part A.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — SMP Performance (KTU 2024)**
> - **SMP** = symmetric, UMA, shared address space, private L1 caches, single bus/interconnect.
> - **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + f/N}$; cap at $\dfrac{1}{1 - f}$.
> - **Gustafson's Law:** $S_G(N) = N - f(N-1)$ — for *scaled* workloads.
> - **Efficiency:** $E(N) = S(N) / N$, never $1 - f$.
> - **Three Coherence Miss Types:** True sharing, **false sharing**, capacity/cold.
> - **False sharing fix:** *Pad to cache line size (64 B)*.
> - **MESI States:** Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid.
> - **Coherence ≠ Consistency** — coherence is per-line, consistency is global order.
> - **SC** = strict order; **WC** = sync ops as barriers; **RC** = acquire/release semantics.
> - **Synchronization cost** (locks, barriers) is often the **dominant** overhead at high $N$.
> - **Bus utilization** grows linearly with $N$ in snooping; saturates at $N \approx 8$–$16$.
> - **Memory bandwidth** scales as $O(N)$ in demand but is **fixed** in supply → bottleneck.
> - **Optimal $N^*$** with overhead $T_o$: $N^* = \sqrt{f/T_o}$.
> - **Speedup past optimum** can produce **slowdown** due to overhead exceeding benefit.
> - **Hardware write invalidation** is more bandwidth-efficient than **write update** for most workloads.
> - **Directory-based** coherence scales better than **snooping** for $N > 16$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed Shared Memory and Directory-Based Protocol - Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distributed Shared Memory and Directory-Based Protocol - Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Distributed Shared Memory (DSM) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed Shared Memory (DSM)** is a memory architecture in a multiprocessor or multi-computer system in which physically distributed memory nodes are logically unified into a single, globally addressable shared address space, allowing processors to access remote memory through standard load/store instructions as if it were local memory.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, DSM bridges the gap between two extremes:
- **Shared-Memory Multiprocessors** (e.g., SMPs) — easy programming, but limited scalability.
- **Distributed-Memory Multicomputers** (e.g., clusters) — highly scalable, but hard to program (uses message passing like MPI).

DSM offers the **programming simplicity of shared memory** with the **scalability of distributed memory**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Office Library" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Corporate Office Library**
> 
> Imagine a large company with **5 branch offices** in different cities. Each office keeps some unique books in its own library. To a junior employee, it *appears* that **all books in all libraries** are accessible from a single catalog (the **shared address space**).
> 
> - When you request a book (a **memory read**), the system looks up which branch holds it, orders a copy to be delivered to you, and updates the catalog so other employees know you have it.
> - If you want to *edit* a book (a **memory write**), you must acquire the **only authorized copy** — the system revokes the book from everyone else to maintain consistency.
> - The **directory** acts as the central catalog that tracks *who has which copy, who is reading, and who is editing*.
> 
> This catalog is exactly the **Directory-Based Coherence Protocol** in computer architecture.

## 1.3 Directory-Based Protocol — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Directory-Based Coherence Protocol** is a hardware/software scheme used to maintain **cache coherence** in distributed shared memory systems by keeping a directory at each memory node that records the state (cached/un-cached, shared/exclusive/modified) and the identities of caches holding copies of each memory block.

The directory eliminates the need for **broadcast snooping** (used in bus-based systems), making it **scalable to hundreds or thousands of nodes**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Home Node** | The node that physically owns (stores) a memory block. |
| **Local Node** | The node on which a requesting processor resides. |
| **Remote Node** | Any other node in the system. |
| **Directory Entry** | A record per memory block tracking its state and sharers list. |
| **Sharers List** | Bit vector or linked list of nodes currently caching the block. |
| **Coherence State** | The current access permission (Uncached, Shared, Exclusive/Modified). |

## 1.5 Why DSM and Directory Protocols Matter in KTU Context

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO mapping for Module 3), students must be able to:
> - **CO3:** Analyze memory hierarchy and coherence mechanisms in advanced parallel architectures.
> - **CO4:** Evaluate directory-based and snoopy protocols for large-scale multiprocessor systems.
> 
> DSM + Directory protocols are **direct exam-favorite topics**, frequently appearing in 7-mark and 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Motivation — Why Not Just Use Snooping?

In **bus-based snoopy protocols**, every cache broadcasts transactions on a shared bus. This works for small systems (≤ 16 processors) but fails to scale because:
- Bus becomes a **bottleneck** (single shared medium).
- Broadcast traffic grows as **O(N)** with the number of nodes.
- Physical limitations of bus length, capacitance, and arbitration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Directory-based protocols replace broadcast with point-to-point messaging.** The directory at the home node acts as a "bookkeeper" that knows exactly who needs to be contacted, eliminating the need to broadcast to all nodes.

## 2.2 Directory Organization — The Three Canonical Schemes

### 2.2.1 Full-Map Directory
- Each directory entry contains an **N-bit vector** (one bit per node in the system) plus state bits.
- Allows **any subset** of nodes to simultaneously cache a block.
- **Directory memory cost:** $N$ bits per block.
- **Scalability issue:** For $N=1024$ nodes, that is **128 bytes per block** of directory overhead — impractical for large systems.

### 2.2.2 Limited Directory
- Directory entry has a **fixed small number of pointers** (e.g., k=4 or k=8) to caching nodes.
- When all pointers are in use and a new node requests the block, a **forced replacement** or **broadcast** is performed.
- **Memory cost:** Fixed (e.g., 4 × 6 bits = 24 bits + state).
- **Drawback:** Suffers from "**pointer overflow**" when more than k nodes want to share.

### 2.2.3 Linked/Chained Directory (Dynamic Distributed Directory)
- Directory entry stores **only a head pointer** to a linked list of sharers, kept in a special "directory cache" or in the caches themselves.
- Scales to **arbitrarily many sharers** without per-block growth.
- **Drawback:** Requires chained pointer chasing — higher latency for invalidation.

## 2.3 Directory States (MSI Protocol Example)

A typical KTU-required directory protocol uses three core states (extended from MESI used in snoopy systems):

| State | Meaning | Owner | Memory Copy | Sharers List Action |
|-------|---------|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Uncached (U)** | Block is not in any cache | Home node only | Valid, up-to-date | Empty list |
| **Shared (S)** | Block is in ≥ 1 cache, read-only | Caches in read-only | Valid (could be stale only if dirty was flushed) | List all readers |
| **Modified (M)** | Block is in exactly 1 cache, dirty | Exactly one remote cache | **Stale / Invalid** at home | List contains only the owner |

> [!NOTE]
> In MESI extension, an "**Exclusive (E)**" state is sometimes added — block is in one cache in clean state, memory is up-to-date, sharers list has one node but no invalidation needed on write.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Description |
|---------|------------------|-------------|
| Directory Memory per Block (Full-Map) | $D_{mem} = N$ bits | $N$ = number of nodes |
| Directory Memory per Block (Limited, k pointers) | $D_{mem} = k \cdot \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ bits | $k$ = pointer count |
| Total Directory Memory (Full-Map) | $D_{total} = M_{blocks} \cdot (N + 2)$ bits | $M_{blocks}$ = memory blocks in system |
| Average Memory Access Time (DSM) | $T_{avg} = h \cdot T_{local} + (1-h) \cdot T_{remote}$ | $h$ = hit ratio |
| Remote Access Latency (no contention) | $T_{remote} = T_{msg} + T_{dir\_lookup} + T_{network}$ | Sum of message + directory + network delay |
| Network Traffic per Invalidation | $M_{inv} = 2 \cdot N_{sharers}$ messages | Each sharer gets a "Invalidate" + "Ack" |
| Coherence Miss Rate | $\text{CMR} = \dfrac{\text{Coherence Misses}}{\text{Total Memory References}}$ | Used in performance analysis |
| Speedup (Amdahl, coherence overhead) | $S = \dfrac{N}{1 + (N-1) \cdot f_{coherence}}$ | $f_{coherence}$ = fraction of time lost to coherence stalls |
| Capacity for k-pointer limited dir. | $C_{k} = \binom{N}{k}$ combinations of sharers | Probability-based: $P(\text{overflow}) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k} \binom{N}{i} p^i (1-p)^{N-i}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing these formulas in **KTU answer sheets**, always substitute numerical values step-by-step. Examiners award **partial marks for correct formula selection** even if the final calculation is wrong.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HPC Clusters (e.g., SGI Origin, Sun Enterprise 10000, Intel QPI-based NUMA systems)** use directory-based coherence.
- **Modern multi-core CPUs** use directory-assisted coherence over **CHI/Coherent Fabric** interconnects (ARM, AMD Infinity Fabric, Intel Mesh).
- **NVIDIA NVLink + GPU memory coherence** uses directory-like tracking for unified memory.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Directory Memory Overhead for Full-Map Scheme

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A DSM system has $N = 64$ nodes and $M_{blocks} = 2^{20}$ memory blocks per node. Compute the total directory memory overhead in **Megabytes** assuming a full-map directory with 2 state bits per block.

### Step 1 — Identify Components of Each Directory Entry

For a full-map directory entry, the storage consists of:
- **State bits:** $S = 2$ (to encode U, S, M).
- **Presence vector:** $P = N$ bits (one per node).

$$
\text{Bits per Entry} = S + P = 2 + N
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\text{Bits per Entry} = 2 + 64 = 66 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Bits per Node

$$
\text{Bits per Node} = M_{blocks} \cdot (\text{Bits per Entry})
$$

$$
\text{Bits per Node} = 2^{20} \cdot 66
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Total Directory Memory (All Nodes)

$$
D_{total} = N \cdot \text{Bits per Node}
$$

$$
D_{total} = 64 \cdot 2^{20} \cdot 66
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Megabytes (1 Byte = 8 bits, 1 MB = $2^{20}$ Bytes)

$$
D_{total} = \dfrac{64 \cdot 2^{20} \cdot 66}{8 \cdot 2^{20}} \text{ MB}
$$

$$
D_{total} = \dfrac{64 \cdot 66}{8} = 8 \cdot 66 = 528 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Total directory memory overhead = **528 MB**. This explains why full-map directories become infeasible at $N=1024$ or beyond.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Limited Directory Pointer Overflow Probability

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** In a 16-node DSM, the probability that a given node caches a particular block is $p = 0.25$. Using a limited directory with $k = 3$ pointers, compute the probability of **pointer overflow**.

### Step 1 — Define the Overflow Event

Overflow occurs when **more than k nodes** simultaneously cache the block. Using the binomial model where each of the $N=16$ nodes independently caches with probability $p$:

$$
P(\text{overflow}) = 1 - P(X \leq k)
$$

where $X \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)$.

### Step 2 — Write the Cumulative Binomial Probability

$$
P(X \leq k) = \sum_{i=0}^{k} \binom{N}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{N-i}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $N=16$, $k=3$, $p=0.25$, $1-p=0.75$

$$
P(X \leq 3) = \sum_{i=0}^{3} \binom{16}{i} (0.25)^{i} (0.75)^{16-i}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Each Term

**Term $i=0$:**
$$
\binom{16}{0}(0.25)^{0}(0.75)^{16} = 1 \cdot 1 \cdot (0.75)^{16}
$$

Computing $(0.75)^{16}$:
$$
(0.75)^2 = 0.5625, \quad (0.75)^4 = 0.3164, \quad (0.75)^8 = 0.1001, \quad (0.75)^{16} = 0.01002
$$

So term $i=0 \approx 0.01002$.

**Term $i=1$:**
$$
\binom{16}{1}(0.25)^{1}(0.75)^{15} = 16 \cdot 0.25 \cdot (0.75)^{15}
$$

$$
(0.75)^{15} = \dfrac{0.01002}{0.75} = 0.01336
$$

$$
\text{Term } i=1 = 16 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.01336 = 4 \cdot 0.01336 = 0.05345
$$

**Term $i=2$:**
$$
\binom{16}{2}(0.25)^{2}(0.75)^{14} = 120 \cdot 0.0625 \cdot 0.01782
$$

$$
(0.75)^{14} = \dfrac{0.01336}{0.75} = 0.01782
$$

$$
\text{Term } i=2 = 120 \cdot 0.0625 \cdot 0.01782 = 7.5 \cdot 0.01782 = 0.13365
$$

**Term $i=3$:**
$$
\binom{16}{3}(0.25)^{3}(0.75)^{13} = 560 \cdot 0.015625 \cdot 0.02376
$$

$$
(0.75)^{13} = \dfrac{0.01782}{0.75} = 0.02376
$$

$$
\text{Term } i=3 = 560 \cdot 0.015625 \cdot 0.02376 = 8.75 \cdot 0.02376 = 0.20790
$$

### Step 5 — Sum and Subtract from 1

$$
P(X \leq 3) = 0.01002 + 0.05345 + 0.13365 + 0.20790 = 0.40502
$$

$$
P(\text{overflow}) = 1 - 0.40502 = 0.59498
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $P(\text{overflow}) \approx 0.595$, or **59.5%**. This shows that for $N=16$, $k=3$ is **grossly insufficient** — nearly 60% of accesses will trigger expensive broadcast-based fallback.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Directory Schemes

| Property | Full-Map | Limited (k-pointers) | Linked/Chained |
|----------|----------|----------------------|----------------|
| Directory memory per block | $N + S$ bits | $k \cdot \lceil \log_2 N \rceil + S$ bits | Constant (head ptr) + state |
| Scalability | Poor ($O(N)$) | Moderate | Excellent |
| Invalidation messages per write | Exactly $\vert S \vert$ (sharers count) | Up to $k$, then broadcast | Exactly $\vert S \vert$ but chained |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Real-world example | SGI Origin (distributed dir.) | Dash (Lenoswki et al.) | IEEE Scalable Coherent Interface (SCI) |
| Pointer overflow | Impossible | Possible (handled by bcast) | Impossible |
| Hit latency | $O(1)$ lookup | $O(1)$ lookup | $O(\vert S \vert)$ chase |
| KTU exam weightage | **Very High** | High | Moderate |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Directory Protocol for a Read Miss

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** Node 5 issues a read for block X, currently in **Modified** state at Node 2. Walk through the directory protocol step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Read Miss Generated at Node 5**
- Node 5's cache generates a **read miss** for block X.
- Node 5 sends a `ReadReq` message to the **Home Node** (the node that owns X in main memory).

**Step 2 — Home Node Directory Lookup**
- Home node examines directory entry for X.
- Finds state = **M**, owner = **Node 2**.

**Step 3 — Forward Request to Owner**
- Home node forwards the request to Node 2 with a `FwdRead` message.

**Step 4 — Owner (Node 2) Responds**
- Node 2 transitions X from **M** to **S** locally.
- Sends the data block (cache line) **directly to Node 5** (data reply bypasses home).
- Sends an `InvAck` (data provided) message back to Home.

**Step 5 — Home Node Updates Directory**
- Home sets state of X to **S**.
- Updates sharers list: previously {Node 2}, now **{Node 2, Node 5}**.

**Step 6 — Node 5 Completes**
- Node 5 stores X in cache with state **S**.
- Read completes.

> [!TIP]
> The **"data bypasses home"** optimization is critical — it prevents the home node from becoming a bottleneck. This is the standard **forwarding protocol** used in KTU textbook problems.

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram represents a 4-node DSM system with home memory, caches, and the directory controller. Each node is a complete processing element with a private L1 cache, a portion of physical memory, and a directory entry table.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph node1["NODE 1"]
        cpu1["CPU Core 1"]
        cache1["L1 Cache 1"]

    end

    subgraph node4["NODE 4"]
        cpu4["CPU Core 4"]
        cache4["L1 Cache 4"]
        mem4["Local Memory Bank 4"]
        dir4["Directory Entry Table 4"]
    end

    interconnect["Interconnection Network - Point to Point - Mesh or Ring"]

    cpu1 --> cache1
    cpu2 --> cache2
    cpu3 --> cache3
    cpu4 --> cache4

    cache1 <--> mem1
    cache2 <--> mem2
    cache3 <--> mem3
    cache4 <--> mem4

    mem1 --- dir1
    mem2 --- dir2
    mem3 --- dir3
    mem4 --- dir4

    cache1 <--> interconnect
    cache2 <--> interconnect
    cache3 <--> interconnect
    cache4 <--> interconnect

    dir1 <--> interconnect
    dir2 <--> interconnect
    dir3 <--> interconnect
    dir4 <--> interconnect
```

## 4.2 Directory-Based Protocol State Machine (Read/Write Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The state machine below shows transitions of a **single memory block** at its **home node directory** in response to processor requests. The block starts in Uncached (U) state.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> uncachedU

    uncachedU --> sharedS : ReadReq from Node 5 - add to sharers list
    uncachedU --> modifiedM : WriteReq from Node 7 - grant exclusive ownership

    sharedS --> sharedS : ReadReq from new node - add to sharers list
    sharedS --> modifiedM : WriteReq - invalidate all sharers, transfer ownership

    modifiedM --> sharedS : ReadReq from new node - owner flushes data - sharers list updated
    modifiedM --> modifiedM : WriteReq from current owner - no invalidation needed
    modifiedM --> uncachedU : WriteBack from owner - block returns to home memory
```

## 4.3 Directory Entry Internal Layout (Full-Map)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below illustrates the bit-level layout of a single directory entry for a 16-node system.

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 5
    block:statebits["State - 2 bits"]:2
    block:p0["N0"]:1
    block:p1["N1"]:1
    block:p2["N2"]:1
    block:p3["N3"]:1
    block:p4["N4"]:1
    block:p5["N5"]:1
    block:p6["N6"]:1
    block:p7["N7"]:1
    block:p8["N8"]:1
    block:p9["N9"]:1
    block:p10["N10"]:1
    block:p11["N11"]:1
    block:p12["N12"]:1
    block:p13["N13"]:1
    block:p14["N14"]:1
    block:p15["N15"]:1
```

**Layout Description:**
- **State Bits (2 bits):** Encode $U$, $S$, or $M$.
- **Presence Vector (16 bits):** One bit per node. Bit $= 1$ means that node currently holds the block in its cache.

## 4.4 Message Flow Diagram — Read Miss with Owner in Modified State

> [!NOTE]
> This sequence diagram shows the full message exchange for a Read Miss when the requested block is in Modified state at a remote node. Notice that **data reply bypasses the home node** (forwarding optimization).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant cpu5 as Node 5 - CPU
    participant home as Home Node - Directory
    participant node2 as Node 2 - Owner

    cpu5->>home: ReadReq - Block X
    home->>home: Directory Lookup - State M - Owner Node 2
    home->>node2: FwdRead - Forwarded Request
    node2->>node2: Transition X - M to S
    node2-->>cpu5: Data Block X - Direct Reply
    node2->>home: Data Provided - InvAck
    home->>home: Update Directory - State S - Sharers [2, 5]
    home-->>cpu5: Read Response - Acknowledgment
    cpu5->>cpu5: Load X into Cache - State S
```

## 4.5 Comparison: Snoopy Bus vs Directory Protocol (Block Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart illustrates the control-flow difference between broadcasting (snoopy) and targeted (directory) coherence traffic for a write request.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph snp["SNOOPY BUS PROTOCOL"]
        A1["Node issues Write"] --> B1["Broadcast Invalidate on Bus"]
        B1 --> C1["All Caches snoop"]
        C1 --> D1["All non-owners invalidate or supply data"]
        D1 --> E1["Bus traffic - O of N per write"]
    end

    subgraph dir["DIRECTORY PROTOCOL"]
        A2["Node issues Write"] --> B2["Send WriteReq to Home Directory"]
        B2 --> C2["Directory identifies sharers list"]
        C2 --> D2["Send targeted Invalidate to listed nodes only"]
        D2 --> E2["Network traffic - exactly k per write"]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions test the **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be concise (3–4 lines) and terminology-accurate.

### Question A1: Define Distributed Shared Memory. Mention two advantages over message-passing systems.

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** DSM is a memory architecture where physically distributed memory modules across multiple nodes are abstracted into a single shared address space, accessible via standard load/store instructions.
- **Advantage 1 (1 mark):** Simpler programming model — applications can be written using shared-memory paradigms (threads, OpenMP) without explicit message-passing calls (MPI sends/receives).
- **Advantage 2 (1 mark):** Better portability — same program can run on shared-memory hardware or DSM with minimal code change; plus, DSM scales beyond the bus-based limits of SMP.

---

### Question A2: What is a directory-based coherence protocol? How does it differ from snoopy protocols?

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A directory-based protocol maintains cache coherence by storing, at each home node, a directory entry per memory block that records the block's coherence state and the set of nodes currently caching it. Coherence actions are issued only to nodes listed in the directory.
- **Difference 1 — Scalability (1 mark):** Snoopy protocols use broadcast over a shared bus, limiting them to ~16–32 nodes. Directory protocols use point-to-point messaging and scale to hundreds or thousands of nodes.
- **Difference 2 — Traffic (1 mark):** Snoopy generates $O(N)$ traffic per write (all nodes must snoop); directory generates only $O(\vert S \vert)$ traffic where $\vert S \vert$ is the actual sharer count.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's Part B questions for ESE follow the **Module Internal Choice** pattern. Within Module 3, you will be offered two alternative 14-mark questions; answer **either** of them. Each question typically splits into (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks): Directory Protocol Architectures & Memory Overhead

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Explain the three directory organization schemes — full-map, limited, and linked — with their advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Full-Map Directory (2 marks):**
- Stores an **N-bit presence vector** plus state bits per block, where N = number of nodes.
- **Advantage:** Any subset of nodes can simultaneously cache the block; no overflow possible.
- **Disadvantage:** Memory cost grows as $O(N)$ per block — for $N=1024$, each block needs 128 bytes of directory storage; **does not scale**.

**2. Limited Directory (2.5 marks):**
- Stores a **fixed number k** of node pointers (e.g., $k=4$).
- **Advantage:** Memory cost is bounded — independent of $N$.
- **Disadvantage:** When more than $k$ nodes want to share, a **pointer overflow** occurs, handled by either broadcasting or evicting a sharer — both are expensive.

**3. Linked/Chained Directory (2.5 marks):**
- Directory entry stores only a **head pointer** to a linked list of sharers, with the list itself distributed (in caches or in a special cache).
- **Advantage:** Arbitrarily many sharers; no overflow; memory scales gracefully.
- **Disadvantage:** Invalidation requires **traversing the chain** — $O(\vert S \vert)$ latency; complex to maintain on replacement.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**A 32-node DSM system uses a full-map directory. Each node has 256 MB of main memory divided into 64-byte blocks. Calculate the total directory memory required in MB. Assume 2 state bits per entry.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Bits per Directory Entry (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Bits per entry} = N + S = 32 + 2 = 34 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Memory Blocks per Node (1 mark):**
$$
M_{blocks} = \dfrac{256 \text{ MB}}{64 \text{ B}} = \dfrac{2^{28} \text{ B}}{2^6 \text{ B}} = 2^{22} \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Directory Bits per Node (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Bits per node} = 2^{22} \cdot 34
$$

**Step 4 — Total Directory Bits Across All Nodes (1 mark):**
$$
D_{total} = 32 \cdot 2^{22} \cdot 34
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MB (1 mark):**
$$
D_{total} = \dfrac{32 \cdot 2^{22} \cdot 34}{8 \cdot 2^{20}} \text{ MB} = \dfrac{32 \cdot 4 \cdot 34}{8} = 4 \cdot 34 = 136 \text{ MB}
$$

**Result + Comment (2 marks):**
- Total directory memory = **136 MB**.
- This is **53% overhead** relative to the original 256 MB physical memory — a major scalability concern. In a 1024-node system with the same parameters, directory memory would exceed usable memory, motivating the use of **limited or chained** schemes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to divide by 8 (bits→bytes) and by $2^{20}$ (bytes→MB). Many also confuse whether $N$ bits means $N$ bytes — read the question carefully. **Show unit conversion explicitly** to earn full marks.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks): DSM Coherence Operations & State Transitions

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Describe with state-transition diagrams the directory-based MSI protocol for handling read and write requests. List all possible messages exchanged.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Three States (1.5 marks):**
- **Uncached (U):** Block not in any private cache.
- **Shared (S):** Block is cached in one or more nodes in read-only state.
- **Modified (M):** Block is cached in exactly one node with exclusive ownership; home memory is stale.

**2. Messages Exchanged (1.5 marks):**
- `ReadReq` — from requester to home.
- `WriteReq` — from requester to home.
- `FwdRead` / `FwdWrite` — from home to current owner.
- `Data Reply` — from owner/requester to requester.
- `Invalidate` — from home to all sharers.
- `InvAck` — from invalidated sharer to home.
- `WriteBack` — from owner to home on block replacement.

**3. State Transitions (4 marks):**
- **U → S:** Home receives `ReadReq`, sends data from memory to requester, adds requester to sharers list, sets state S.
- **U → M:** Home receives `WriteReq`, sends data from memory to requester, sets state M with requester as sole owner.
- **S → S (on read):** Home adds new requester to sharers list, sends data from memory.
- **S → M (on write):** Home sends `Invalidate` to all sharers, waits for `InvAck`, then grants ownership to requester with state M.
- **M → S (on read miss at another node):** Home forwards request to current owner; owner flushes data to requester, sends `WriteBack` to home; state becomes S with two sharers.
- **M → M (on write hit):** Owner updates cache; no directory action required.
- **M → U (on owner replacement):** Owner writes back dirty data to home; state returns to U.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Consider a 4-node DSM system. Trace the complete message sequence and directory updates for the following sequence of operations:**
- *Initial state: Block X is in state U, located at home node H=0.*
- *T1: Node 1 reads X.*
- *T2: Node 2 writes X.*
- *T3: Node 3 reads X.*
- *T4: Node 1 writes X.*

**Model Answer:**

**T1: Node 1 reads X (2 marks):**
- Node 1 → Home(0): `ReadReq(X)`.
- Home: directory entry U → S, sharers = {1}, provides data from memory.
- Node 1: X loaded in state S.
- **Final state:** S, sharers = {1}, home(0) memory valid.

**T2: Node 2 writes X (2 marks):**
- Node 2 → Home(0): `WriteReq(X)`.
- Home: sees state S, sharers = {1}.
- Home → Node 1: `Invalidate(X)`.
- Node 1 → Home: `InvAck`. Node 1 invalidates X.
- Home → Node 2: data + ownership grant. Sharers = {2}, state M.
- Node 2: X loaded in state M.
- **Final state:** M, owner = Node 2, home(0) memory **stale**.

**T3: Node 3 reads X (1.5 marks):**
- Node 3 → Home(0): `ReadReq(X)`.
- Home: sees state M, owner = Node 2.
- Home → Node 2: `FwdRead(X)`.
- Node 2 → Node 3: data block (direct reply). Node 2 transitions M → S.
- Node 2 → Home: `DataProvided` (updates sharers).
- Home: state M → S, sharers = {2, 3}.
- Node 3: X loaded in state S.
- **Final state:** S, sharers = {2, 3}, home(0) memory updated.

**T4: Node 1 writes X (1.5 marks):**
- Node 1 → Home(0): `WriteReq(X)`.
- Home: sees state S, sharers = {2, 3}.
- Home → Node 2, Node 3: `Invalidate(X)` (two messages).
- Node 2 → Home: `InvAck`. Node 3 → Home: `InvAck`.
- Both Node 2 and Node 3 invalidate X.
- Home → Node 1: data + ownership. Sharers = {1}, state M.
- Node 1: X loaded in state M.
- **Final state:** M, owner = Node 1, home(0) memory **stale**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to update home memory on state M→S transitions.** Home memory is **stale** during M state; it must be refreshed from the owner during the transition.
> 2. **Confusing `FwdRead` with `Invalidate`.** A read miss to a Modified block is **forwarded to the owner** (not invalidated) — the owner is allowed to share the data.
> 3. **Not showing the sharers list update.** Many students just say "state = S" without specifying the new sharers set, losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Missing the `InvAck` round-trip.** A write in S state requires **all** sharers to acknowledge invalidation **before** the state is changed to M.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- ✅ **DSM Definition:** Physically distributed memory unified into a single logical address space; combines **ease of shared-memory programming** with **scalability of distributed systems**.
- ✅ **Directory Protocol:** Maintains coherence by storing a **directory entry at the home node** for every memory block — replaces snoopy broadcast with **point-to-point** messages.
- ✅ **Three Directory Schemes:**
  - **Full-Map:** $O(N)$ bits per block — accurate, but **not scalable**.
  - **Limited:** Fixed $k$ pointers — **scalable but suffers overflow**.
  - **Linked/Chained:** Constant directory + distributed linked list — **best scalability, complex**.
- ✅ **MSI States:**
  - **U** = Uncached (home memory valid, no sharers).
  - **S** = Shared (≥ 1 reader, all copies clean, home memory valid).
  - **M** = Modified (1 owner, home memory **stale**).
- ✅ **Critical Protocol Rule:** On a write to a block in **S** state, the home node **must** send `Invalidate` to **all** sharers and wait for **all** `InvAck` messages before granting M to the writer.
- ✅ **Optimization:** On a read miss to a block in **M** state, the owner supplies data **directly to the requester** (bypassing the home) — this is called **forwarding** and prevents home-node bottleneck.
- ✅ **Directory Memory Formula (Full-Map):** $D_{total} = M_{blocks} \cdot N \cdot (N+S) / 8$ Bytes. Always convert carefully.
- ✅ **Pointer Overflow Probability (Limited):** Use the binomial cumulative — $P(X > k) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k} \binom{N}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{N-i}$.
- ✅ **Performance Insight:** Snoopy is preferred for ≤ 16 nodes; directory is mandatory for ≥ 32 nodes; modern data centers use **directory-assisted protocols over coherent fabrics** (CHI, CXL, Infinity Fabric).
- ✅ **Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid:**
  - Confusing **home memory stale vs. valid** states.
  - Forgetting to include state bits in directory memory calculations.
  - Missing the `WriteBack` step when an owner replaces a Modified block.
  - Treating `FwdRead` and `Invalidate` as interchangeable — they are not.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always draw a **state diagram** (even if not explicitly asked) — it earns 1–2 easy marks. For numerical problems, write the **formula first**, then **substitute**, then **compute** — partial marks are awarded for each correct step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronization - Basic Hardware Primitives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronization - Basic Hardware Primitives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Synchronization** is the coordination of concurrent processes or threads executing on multiple processors in a shared-memory multiprocessor system to ensure correct access to shared resources, preserve program order, and prevent race conditions. In the context of Advanced Computer Architecture (PECST528), synchronization is achieved by relying on a small set of **Basic Hardware Primitives** — atomic, indivisible, read-modify-write (RMW) instructions that the processor guarantees will execute without interruption, even in the presence of other competing processors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basic Hardware Primitives (BHPs)** are the *lowest-level* synchronization operations directly implemented in hardware. They form the foundation upon which all higher-level synchronization abstractions (locks, semaphores, barriers, monitors) are constructed.

The canonical hardware primitives studied under Module 3 are:

1. **Test-and-Set** (TAS) — atomically reads and writes a memory location.
2. **Compare-and-Swap** (CAS) — atomically conditionally updates a memory location.
3. **Fetch-and-Add** (FAA) — atomically increments and returns the previous value.
4. **Atomic Exchange (Swap)** — atomically swaps two register/memory values.
5. **Load-Linked / Store-Conditional (LL/SC)** — a non-blocking alternative used in RISC ISAs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **public restroom in a busy railway station**. The restroom has a single **physical key** hanging on a hook outside the door.

- A traveler approaches, **picks up the key**, and **locks the door from inside** — this *one combined action* (taking + locking) must be **atomic**, because if two people could simultaneously pick the key, chaos would ensue.
- When the traveler finishes, he **returns the key** to the hook.
- The next person can then take the key — but until the key is back, every other waiting person must **stand in a queue** without grabbing it.

In this analogy:
- The **key** is the *lock variable* in memory.
- The action of **"taking the key while simultaneously locking it"** is the *hardware primitive* (Test-and-Set or CAS).
- The **queue of waiting travelers** is the *software spinlock / blocking lock* built on top of the primitive.
- The **atomicity guarantee** is the *bathroom's mechanical design* — only one key fits on the hook at a time, enforced by the lock manufacturer (hardware).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why can't we just use ordinary loads and stores?** Because a load and a subsequent store are *two separate instructions*. Between them, another processor can sneak in and modify the memory location — this is the classic **race condition**. Hardware primitives fuse the read and write into a *single atomic instruction* that no other processor can interrupt.

## 1.3 Fundamental Properties (with Standard Metrics in **Bold**)

| Property | Definition | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Atomicity** | A primitive executes as a single, indivisible step from the perspective of *all* other processors in the system. | Guaranteed at **bus-cycle** or **cache-coherence-protocol** granularity. |
| **Mutual Exclusion (Mutex)** | At most one process/thread can be inside the critical section protected by the primitive at any given time. | **Safety** property — must **never** be violated. |
| **Progress (Liveness)** | If no process is in the critical section and some processes want to enter, exactly one waiting process must eventually be allowed in. | **Bounded waiting** — typically *O(n)* spins for *n* contending processors. |
| **Fairness** | All contending processes get a bounded chance to acquire the lock. | Often relaxed to **eventual progress** in practice. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Hardware Primitives — Structured Logical Breakdown

To understand *why* BHPs are necessary, examine the failure of naïve software-only synchronization.

**Step 1 — The Race Condition Problem**

Consider two processors P1 and P2 trying to enter a critical section guarded by a flag `lock = 0`.

- P1 reads `lock` → finds **0**.
- P2 reads `lock` → finds **0**.
- Both conclude the lock is free and enter the critical section simultaneously.
- **Result:** Mutual exclusion is violated — a fatal correctness error.

**Step 2 — Why Ordinary Instructions Fail**

A load (`LD`) and store (`ST`) are *two independent instructions*. The bus / interconnect / cache coherency controller allows **interleavings** from other processors between them. There is no hardware-enforced indivisibility.

**Step 3 — The Hardware Solution**

The processor designer adds a special instruction that **physically locks the memory bus** (older designs) or uses a **cache-line exclusive ownership handshake** (modern designs like MESI/MOESI) so that no other processor can read or write the target location until the primitive completes. The instruction is then guaranteed to read the current value, modify it, and write it back **as one unit**.

**Step 4 — Building Higher-Level Abstractions**

Once you have even *one* atomic primitive (e.g., Test-and-Set or CAS), you can construct:
- **Spinlocks** — busy-wait locks.
- **Tournament locks / Queueing locks** (MCS, CLH) — scalable spinlocks.
- **Barriers** — for collective synchronization across all processors.
- **Lock-free data structures** — using CAS in a retry loop.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Eye:** When answering theory questions, *always* emphasize the word **"atomic"** — the defining property of any hardware primitive.

## 2.2 Deep Dive into Each Primitive

### 2.2.1 Test-and-Set (TAS / TST)

**Operational Semantics:**

$$\text{TAS}(x) \;\equiv\; \text{old} \leftarrow x;\; x \leftarrow 1;\; \text{return}\; \text{old}$$

The instruction returns the **previous value** of `x` and simultaneously sets it to **1**. Because the read-modify-write is atomic, the returned value is unambiguous across all observers.

**Use case:** Simplest possible spinlock implementation.

```
Spinlock-Acquire(lock):
   while TestAndSet(lock) == 1:   // spin while locked
       ;                          // busy wait
   // Critical Section
```

### 2.2.2 Compare-and-Swap (CAS)

**Operational Semantics:**

$$\text{CAS}(x, \text{expected}, \text{new}) \;\equiv\; \text{if}\; x == \text{expected}\; \text{then}\; x \leftarrow \text{new};\; \text{return}\; \text{TRUE}\; \text{else return FALSE}$$

The instruction compares `x` to `expected`; if equal, it updates `x` to `new`. Otherwise, it leaves `x` unchanged. The boolean return value tells the caller whether the swap succeeded.

**Use case:** Foundation of *wait-free* and *lock-free* algorithms (e.g., the classic lock-free stack by Treiber, 1986).

### 2.2.3 Fetch-and-Add (FAA)

**Operational Semantics:**

$$\text{FAA}(x, \text{inc}) \;\equiv\; \text{old} \leftarrow x;\; x \leftarrow x + \text{inc};\; \text{return}\; \text{old}$$

Atomically increments `x` by `inc` and returns the previous value. Crucially, **all contending FAA calls return distinct values** — the increments are *serialized* even though the operations may be issued concurrently.

**Use case:** Ticket locks and barrier implementations. Each arriving process gets a unique ticket number; the lock holder's ticket + 1 is the "now serving" number.

### 2.2.4 Atomic Exchange (Swap)

**Operational Semantics:**

$$\text{SWAP}(x, \text{reg}) \;\equiv\; \text{temp} \leftarrow x;\; x \leftarrow \text{reg};\; \text{reg} \leftarrow \text{temp}$$

Swaps the contents of a memory location and a register atomically.

**Use case:** Building locks on architectures that lack a direct TAS instruction (e.g., early ARM, MIPS).

### 2.2.5 Load-Linked / Store-Conditional (LL/SC)

**Operational Semantics:**

$$\text{LL}(x) \;\equiv\; \text{reg} \leftarrow x;\; \text{set}\; \text{reservation}\; \text{on}\; x$$

$$\text{SC}(x, \text{new}) \;\equiv\; \text{if}\; \text{reservation}\; \text{still}\; \text{valid}\; \text{then}\; x \leftarrow \text{new};\; \text{return TRUE}\; \text{else return FALSE}$$

The reservation is **invalidated** if any other processor writes to `x` (or to its cache line) between the LL and the SC.

**Use case:** The RISC philosophy (MIPS, ARM, RISC-V, PowerPC, Alpha). LL/SC avoids the *ABA problem* and is more flexible than TAS/CAS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ABA Problem (KTU Favorite!):** In lock-free algorithms using CAS, a thread may read value A, be descheduled, then find A again on retry — but the location's underlying state may have been changed to B and back to A. The thread's CAS succeeds but the semantic check is wrong. LL/SC sidesteps this because the SC fails on any intervening write, not just on a value mismatch.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Metric | Formula / Definition | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speedup (Amdahl) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | Bounded by serial fraction $1-f$ |
| Synchronization Overhead Fraction | $f_{sync} = \dfrac{T_{sync}}{T_{total}}$ | Often **5 % – 30 %** of total runtime |
| Lock Acquisition Latency | $T_{acq} = T_{primitive} + T_{contention}$ | **10 – 200 cycles** (uncontended) |
| Ticket Lock Fairness Bound | $T_{wait} \leq (n-1) \cdot T_{cs}$ | Bounded by $n$ contenders |
| CAS Retry Probability | $P_{success} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ for $n$ uniform contenders | Geometric mean retry count $\approx n$ |
| Memory Barrier Cost | $T_{mb} \approx 10\text{–}500$ cycles | Architecture-dependent |
| Cache-Line Ping-Pong Rate | $\rho_{ping} = \dfrac{1}{T_{coh}}$ | Coherence transaction time |
| Maximum Useful Thread Count | $N_{max} \approx \dfrac{f_{parallel}}{f_{sync}}$ | Beyond this, sync dominates |

> [!CAUTION]
> In all KTU 2024 answer scripts, **never** use the vertical pipe $\vert$ or $\mid$ inside a markdown table — use `\vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` in LaTeX for absolute value to avoid table-parser breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hardware primitives are *the* foundational mechanism behind:

- **Operating system kernels** — Linux kernel's `cmpxchg`, `xadd`, `lock;` prefixed instructions.
- **Database engines** — lock managers, MVCC version counters.
- **Concurrent data structures** — Java's `AtomicInteger`, `AtomicReference` (built on CAS).
- **GPU computing** — CUDA `atomicAdd`, OpenCL `atomic_cmpxchg`.
- **Lock-free queues** in high-frequency trading systems where nanoseconds matter.
- **JVM garbage collectors** — concurrent collectors use CAS for safe pointer updates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cost of Naïve Non-Atomic Lock Attempt (Proof of Necessity)

We will *prove* that without hardware support, mutual exclusion is impossible on a shared-memory multiprocessor (the classical **two-processor mutual exclusion impossibility** for purely software solutions requires additional assumptions, but for *single-variable* flags it is straightforward).

**Setup:** A single shared variable `lock` (initially 0) and two processes P1 and P2.

**Step 1** — P1 executes `LD lock` → obtains value `0` into register R1.

**Step 2** — Context switch / hardware preemption. P2 is scheduled.

**Step 3** — P2 executes `LD lock` → also obtains value `0` into register R2.

**Step 4** — P1 resumes, checks R1, sees `0`, concludes lock is free, executes `ST lock, 1`.

**Step 5** — P2 resumes, checks R2, sees `0`, also concludes lock is free, executes `ST lock, 1`.

**Step 6** — Both P1 and P2 are now in the critical section.

**Formal Algebraic Expression of the Race Window:**

$$\Delta t_{race} = t_{ST} - t_{LD}$$

The window of vulnerability is the time interval between the load and the subsequent store. Any process executing within $\Delta t_{race}$ can corrupt the synchronization invariant.

**Conclusion:** Because a single load and a single store are *physically distinct* instructions executed across *multiple clock cycles*, $\Delta t_{race} > 0$, and a race condition is unavoidable without hardware-level atomicity.

**Hardware Solution Eliminates the Race:**

The TAS instruction executes the entire read-modify-write within a *single bus transaction*, holding the bus / cache-line exclusive ownership such that:

$$\Delta t_{race}^{TAS} = 0$$

By definition of atomicity, the primitive completes before any other processor's access to the location is permitted to be observed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Performance Impact of Synchronization (Amdahl's Law Extension)

Let $f_{parallel}$ be the parallelizable fraction of the program, $f_{sync}$ the synchronization overhead fraction, and $f_{serial} = 1 - f_{parallel} - f_{sync}$ the strictly sequential fraction.

**Standard Amdahl Form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \frac{1}{f_{serial} + \dfrac{f_{parallel}}{N} + f_{sync}(N)} \\
     &= \frac{1}{(1 - f_{parallel} - f_{sync}) + \dfrac{f_{parallel}}{N} + f_{sync}(N)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Interpretation of Each Term:**

- $(1 - f_{parallel} - f_{sync})$ is the *unparallelizable* fraction that runs on a single processor regardless of $N$.
- $\dfrac{f_{parallel}}{N}$ is the parallel work distributed across $N$ processors, so its wall-clock contribution is divided by $N$.
- $f_{sync}(N)$ is the synchronization overhead, which **grows** with $N$ (more processors → more contention, more coherence traffic, more cache-line invalidations).

**Modeling $f_{sync}(N)$ — Contention Model:**

Assume each atomic primitive takes $T_p$ cycles when uncontended, and there are $C(N) = cN$ cache-coherence transactions per barrier with $c$ a hardware-dependent constant.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{sync}(N) &\approx \frac{C(N) \cdot T_{coh}}{T_{total}} \\
            &= \frac{c N \cdot T_{coh}}{T_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - f_{parallel} - f_{sync}) + \dfrac{f_{parallel}}{N} + \dfrac{c N \cdot T_{coh}}{T_{total}}}
$$

**Differentiating to Find Optimal N:**

For maximum speedup, $\dfrac{dS}{dN} = 0$. Differentiating with respect to $N$ (treating the denominator $D(N)$ and minimizing it):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(N) &= \alpha + \dfrac{\beta}{N} + \gamma N \quad \text{where } \alpha = 1 - f_{parallel},\; \beta = f_{parallel},\; \gamma = \dfrac{cT_{coh}}{T_{total}} \\
\frac{dD}{dN} &= -\dfrac{\beta}{N^2} + \gamma \;=\; 0 \\
\Rightarrow N_{opt} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\beta}{\gamma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_{parallel} \cdot T_{total}}{c \cdot T_{coh}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Beyond $N_{opt}$, synchronization overhead grows faster than parallel work, and adding more processors *hurts* performance — a phenomenon known as **synchronization-induced slowdown**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CAS Retry Probability and Expected Spins

Assume $n$ processes contend for a single CAS-protected location. In each attempt, only one CAS succeeds. If all contenders retry independently in each round, the probability of success for a given process in a given round is $\dfrac{1}{n}$.

**Probability Mass Function for Number of Retries $k$ (Geometric Distribution):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{retry } k \text{ times before success}) &= \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^k \cdot \frac{1}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expected Number of Retries:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[k] &= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^k \cdot \frac{1}{n} \\
     &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{(1 - 1/n)}{(1/n)^2} \quad \text{(using sum of geometric series)} \\
     &= n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Spin Cycles Before Lock Acquisition (including the successful attempt):**

$$
E[T_{acquire}] = (n - 1) \cdot T_{CAS} + T_{CAS} = n \cdot T_{CAS}
$$

where $T_{CAS}$ is the per-attempt cost (including cache-coherence latency for a remote CAS on a shared line).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Primitives

| Primitive | Instruction Count for Lock | ABA-Safe? | Fairness Support | Cache-Line Ping-Pong | Typical ISA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Test-and-Set** | 1 per attempt | Yes (binary) | None (unfair) | Yes — every attempt re-invalidates | x86 (`xchg`, `lock; bts`), IBM z/Architecture |
| **Compare-and-Swap** | 1 per attempt (in retry loop) | **No** — ABA vulnerable | None by itself | Yes — failure still re-invalidates | x86 (`cmpxchg`), ARMv8 (`cas`), SPARC |
| **Fetch-and-Add** | 1 per attempt | Yes | **Yes** — ticket locks | Yes | x86 (`lock; xadd`), IA-64 |
| **Atomic Swap** | 1 per attempt | Yes (binary) | None | Yes | ARM, MIPS |
| **Load-Linked / Store-Conditional** | 2 per attempt | **Yes** — any intervening write fails SC | None by itself | **No** if SC fails — no invalidation | MIPS, ARM, RISC-V, Alpha, PowerPC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Atomic RMW Instruction in a Modern Coherent Multiprocessor

This diagram illustrates how a Test-and-Set or Compare-and-Swap flows through the cache-coherence hardware to achieve atomicity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Processor Core P1"] --> B["Issue Unit Decodes Atomic Opcode"]
    B --> C["Send ReadEx to Cache Controller"]
    C --> D{"Cache Line Present and Exclusive?"}
    D -- No --> E["Issue BusRdX on Shared Bus"]
    E --> F["Other Cores Snoop and Invalidate Their Copies"]
    F --> G["Memory Responds with Cache Line"]
    D -- Yes --> H["Bypass Invalidation"]
    G --> I["Cache Line Held Exclusively by P1"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Read Current Value into Internal Register"]
    J --> K["ALU Performs Modify Operation Set or CAS Logic"]
    K --> L["Write Modified Value Back to Cache Line"]
    L --> M["Mark Line Modified and Release Bus"]
    M --> N["Instruction Retires Atomicity Guaranteed"]
    N --> O["Result Visible to Subsequent Loads from P1"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The atomic opcode triggers an *exclusive* ownership request that is enforced by the **coherence protocol** (MESI / MOESI).
- While P1 holds the line exclusive, **no other processor** can read or write it — this is what guarantees atomicity.
- After the write, the line is in the *Modified* state and other cores must re-fetch on demand.

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Spinlock Acquisition Using TAS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Thread T1 Calls lockAcquire"] --> test1["Execute TAS on Lock Variable"]
    test1 --> decision1{"Returned Value 0 Previously Free?"}
    decision1 -- Yes and Free --> entersec["Enter Critical Section Now Mutually Exclusive"]
    decision1 -- No and Locked --> spinloop["Busy-Wait Loop Spin"]
    spinloop --> test1
    entersec --> work["Execute Critical Section Work"]
    work --> release["Write 0 to Lock Variable ST lock 0"]
    release --> exitproc["Exit lockAcquire Return to Caller"]
```

---

## 4.3 Ticket Lock Architecture Using Fetch-and-Add

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    arrive["Thread Arrives at Barrier"] --> faa1["FAA on now_serving_increment 1"]
    faa1 --> gotTicket["My_Ticket Equals Returned Value"]
    gotTicket --> spinwait["Spin While now_serving Less Than My_Ticket"]
    spinwait --> check{"now_serving equals My_Ticket?"}
    check -- No --> spinwait
    check -- Yes --> proceed["Proceed Past Barrier FIFO Order Guaranteed"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Because FAA returns *distinct* values to each caller (its atomicity guarantees serialization of the increments), every arriving thread receives a *unique* ticket. Threads then wait until `now_serving` matches their ticket, ensuring **FIFO fairness** — unlike a naïve TAS lock which can starve threads.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define a hardware synchronization primitive. Why is atomicity essential for such primitives?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hardware synchronization primitive is a low-level instruction supported directly by the processor that performs a read-modify-write operation on a memory location in a single, indivisible step. Examples include Test-and-Set, Compare-and-Swap, Fetch-and-Add, and Load-Linked/Store-Conditional.

Atomicity is essential because synchronization protects shared resources from simultaneous access by multiple processors. Without atomicity, between the read and the write steps another processor could intervene and corrupt the synchronization state, leading to race conditions and violation of mutual exclusion. Atomicity guarantees that no other processor can observe or perform a conflicting operation on the memory location until the primitive completes, thereby preserving correctness of the synchronization mechanism.

*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark atomicity role, 1 mark example/correctness argument.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Test-and-Set and Compare-and-Swap primitives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Test-and-Set (TAS) | Compare-and-Swap (CAS) |
|---|---|---|
| Signature | `TAS(x) → oldValue; sets x to 1` | `CAS(x, expected, new) → success boolean` |
| Update Rule | Always writes 1 | Writes `new` only if `x == expected` |
| Use Case | Simple spinlocks | Lock-free data structures, optimistic concurrency |
| ABA Safety | Yes (binary state) | **No** — vulnerable to ABA problem |
| Information Returned | Previous value | Boolean + optionally the observed value |

Test-and-Set is simpler and used when the lock is binary (free / locked). Compare-and-Swap is more expressive and forms the basis of lock-free algorithms but requires a retry loop because it may fail without changing state.

*(Award: 1 mark each for any three distinguishing aspects with one example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the operational semantics of Test-and-Set, Compare-and-Swap, Fetch-and-Add, and Load-Linked/Store-Conditional primitives. For each, give a typical use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using a TAS-based spinlock, derive the expected number of spin cycles before lock acquisition when $n$ processors contend, and discuss the cache-line ping-pong effect on performance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q. A (a) — 7 marks:**

**Test-and-Set (TAS):** Atomically reads a memory location `x`, returns its previous value, and unconditionally writes `1` to it. Mathematical definition: $TAS(x): \; temp \leftarrow x;\; x \leftarrow 1;\; return\; temp$. Typical use case is the simplest spinlock implementation for binary mutual exclusion. (1.5 marks)

**Compare-and-Swap (CAS):** Atomically compares `x` with `expected`; if equal, writes `new` to `x` and returns true; otherwise leaves `x` unchanged and returns false. Definition: $CAS(x, e, n): \; \text{if } x == e \text{ then } x \leftarrow n; \text{ return } (x == e)$. Typical use case is lock-free concurrent stacks, queues, and counters. (1.5 marks)

**Fetch-and-Add (FAA):** Atomically increments `x` by `inc` and returns the previous value of `x`. Definition: $FAA(x, inc): \; temp \leftarrow x;\; x \leftarrow x + inc;\; return\; temp$. Typical use case is implementing ticket locks and barriers with FIFO ordering. (1.5 marks)

**Load-Linked / Store-Conditional (LL/SC):** LL reads `x` and establishes a *reservation* on its cache line. SC writes `new` to `x` only if the reservation is still valid (i.e., no other processor wrote to the line between LL and SC); otherwise SC fails without writing. Typical use case is RISC architectures (MIPS, ARM, RISC-V, Alpha) for implementing any of the above primitives without dedicated RMW hardware. (2 marks)

**Closing remark (0.5 mark):** All four primitives share the property of *atomicity* at the hardware level — they execute as a single bus or cache-coherence transaction.

---

**Model Solution to Q. A (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — TAS Spinlock Pseudocode:**

```
Acquire(lock):
   while TAS(lock) == 1:
       ;    // busy-wait
Release(lock):
   lock = 0;
```

**Step 2 — Single Successful Acquisition Cost:**

The first TAS call that observes `lock = 0` returns `0` and the caller enters the critical section. All other $n-1$ callers observe `lock = 1` and re-spin.

**Step 3 — Expected Number of TAS Calls Before Success (per process):**

Assuming uniform random arrival and no priority:

$$
P(\text{success per attempt}) = \frac{1}{n}
$$

The number of attempts follows a geometric distribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{attempts}] &= \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{k-1} \cdot \frac{1}{n} = n
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence expected spins before acquisition is $n - 1$ (excluding the successful attempt). (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Cache-Line Ping-Pong Effect:**

Every TAS on a contested lock causes the *executing* processor to obtain exclusive ownership of the cache line. This forces all *other* processors holding the line (in shared state) to **invalidate** their copies.

When another processor next attempts TAS, it must:
- Issue a BusRdX (or equivalent coherence message).
- Wait for the line to be flushed by the current owner.
- Re-fetch the (now-modified) line exclusive.

This sequence is called **cache-line ping-pong**. Cost per ping-pong cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ping} &= T_{invalid} + T_{flush} + T_{fetch} + T_{coh-protocol-roundtrip} \\
         &\approx 50 \text{ to } 500 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 5 — Total Lock Acquisition Cost Under Contention:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{acquire}^{contended} &\approx n \cdot T_{ping} \\
                        &\approx n \cdot 200 \text{ cycles (typical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n = 32$ processors, $T_{acquire} \approx 6400$ cycles — a substantial fraction of any short critical section. (1.5 marks)

**Step 6 — Mitigation:**

- **Test-and-Test-and-Set (TTS):** Spin on a *plain load* first, only attempt TAS when the line is observed as `0`. This reduces coherence traffic because the plain loads can be satisfied from a shared (read-only) cache state.

```
Acquire_TTS(lock):
   while lock == 1:           // plain read, no invalidation
       ;
   while TAS(lock) == 1:      // only TAS at the race
       ;
```

- **Queueing locks (MCS, CLH):** Each processor spins on a *local* variable, eliminating global cache-line ping-pong entirely. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of cache coherence protocols (MESI) in implementing atomic hardware primitives. Explain the state transitions that occur during a Test-and-Set. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the ABA problem? Show how it manifests in a CAS-based lock-free stack and explain why Load-Linked/Store-Conditional avoids it. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q. B (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Role of Coherence Protocol (2 marks):**

Modern multiprocessors implement atomic primitives by leveraging the cache-coherence protocol (MESI — Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid). The protocol guarantees that at most one cache in the entire system holds a line in *Modified* or *Exclusive* state at any instant. The atomic primitive elevates the line to *Modified* state in a single bus transaction, performs the read-modify-write locally in the cache, and only then releases the line. Because no other cache holds a valid copy during this window, the operation is observed as atomic by all other processors.

**Step 2 — MESI State Transitions During TAS on Initially Shared Line (3 marks):**

Initial state: Line is in **S (Shared)** state in caches of P1, P2, P3.

P1 issues TAS:

- Step A: P1 issues `BusRdX` (Bus Read with Intent to Modify) on the system bus.
- Step B: All other caches (P2, P3) holding the line in **S** state snoop the `BusRdX` and transition their copy to **I (Invalid)**.
- Step C: Memory controller responds with the current cache line contents.
- Step D: P1's cache transitions the line from **I** to **M (Modified)** (or directly to **E (Exclusive)** then **M**).
- Step E: P1 reads the value into an internal register (the "old" value to return).
- Step F: P1's ALU modifies the value (sets it to `1` for TAS).
- Step G: P1 writes the modified value back into its cache line.
- Step H: Line remains in **M** state; P1 reports completion to the instruction pipeline.

Final state: P1 holds line in **M**, all other processors have it in **I**.

**Step 3 — Why This Guarantees Atomicity (1 mark):**

Between Steps E and G, no other processor can read or write the line because every other cache has it marked Invalid. Any concurrent attempt by P2 to issue its own TAS would first need a `BusRdX`, which serializes behind P1's in-flight transaction. Hence the read-modify-write of P1 is observed as a single, indivisible step by the entire system.

**Step 4 — Bus Locking vs Cache Locking (1 mark):**

Older x86 designs physically locked the bus during atomic ops. Modern designs use *cache-line exclusive ownership* (as described) which is more scalable because non-contended atomics do not stall other memory traffic to unrelated addresses.

---

**Model Solution to Q. B (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Definition of the ABA Problem (1.5 marks):**

The ABA problem is a correctness hazard in CAS-based lock-free algorithms. A thread reads value `A` from a shared location, is preempted, and during the preemption another thread changes the value to `B` and then back to `A`. When the original thread resumes and performs its CAS comparing against `A`, the CAS succeeds — but the underlying *semantic state* may have changed (e.g., a pointer's pointee object may have been freed and reallocated). The thread's CAS gives a false sense of safety.

**Step 2 — Manifestation in a Lock-Free Stack (3 marks):**

The classic lock-free stack (Treiber, 1986) uses a linked list with `head` pointer. `pop()` does:

```
pop():
   do:
       oldTop = head;
       newTop = oldTop.next;
   while CAS(head, oldTop, newTop) fails;
   return oldTop;
```

Scenario causing ABA:

- Thread T1 reads `oldTop = A` (where A is a node with `next = B`).
- T1 is preempted.
- Thread T2 executes `pop()` and removes A: `head` now points to B.
- Thread T2 executes `pop()` again and removes B: `head` now points to C.
- Thread T2 executes `push(A)`: re-pushes a node with address A. `head` is again `A`.
- T1 resumes. Its CAS compares `head == oldTop` → both are A. CAS succeeds.
- T1 sets `head = newTop = B`. But B has already been popped (and potentially freed)!
- **Result:** Logical corruption — the stack pointer is now dangling.

**Step 3 — Why LL/SC Avoids ABA (2 marks):**

In LL/SC:

- T1 executes `LL(head)` and obtains value `A`, with a reservation on the cache line of `head`.
- T1 is preempted. T2 modifies `head` (to B, then back to A).
- T1 resumes and executes `SC(head, newTop)`.
- The reservation was **invalidated** the moment T2 performed *any* store to `head` (regardless of whether the value changed). T2 performed two stores — both invalidated the reservation.
- Hence T1's SC fails and returns FALSE. T1 must retry from the beginning with a fresh LL, by which time the inconsistency is detected.

The key insight: **LL/SC detects intervening writes**, not just value changes. Thus ABA cannot fool the primitive.

**Step 4 — Hardware vs Software Solutions to ABA (0.5 mark):**

- **Hardware:** Use LL/SC (preferred).
- **Software:** Tag each pointer with a monotonically increasing version counter (e.g., 128-bit CAS on `{pointer, counter}`); ABA requires both pointer *and* counter to revert, which is practically impossible.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (read carefully before writing your exam):**
>
> 1. **Calling TAS, CAS, FAA "software algorithms" — they are HARDWARE primitives.** Many students lose 1–2 marks by describing them as "software constructs." They are implemented in silicon via cache-coherence protocol transitions.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention "atomic" when defining any primitive.** Every definition MUST include the word atomic or indivisible. Omitting it typically costs 0.5–1 mark per sub-part.
>
> 3. **Confusing Test-and-Set with Test-and-Test-and-Set (TTS).** TAS does a RMW every loop iteration (causing ping-pong). TTS spins on a plain load first to reduce coherence traffic. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Treating LL/SC as equivalent to CAS.** They are NOT the same. LL/SC fails on *any* intervening write; CAS fails only on value mismatch. The ABA problem is the classic consequence of this difference.
>
> 5. **Ignoring cache coherence.** A 14-mark question on hardware primitives that does not mention MESI / cache-line exclusive ownership / bus locking is considered incomplete. Always weave coherence into your answer.
>
> 6. **Arithmetic errors in Amdahl-style derivations.** When deriving $N_{opt}$, carefully distinguish between the *parallelizable fraction* and the *synchronization overhead fraction*. Mixing them up is a 2-mark penalty.
>
> 7. **Not drawing diagrams for "Explain" questions.** A block diagram of the MESI transitions or a flow diagram of spinlock acquisition adds visual clarity and can rescue borderline answers — examiners reward well-illustrated responses.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Synchronization** coordinates concurrent threads; it requires *atomic* operations to prevent race conditions.
- [x] **Basic Hardware Primitives** are the atomic RMW instructions implemented directly in the processor: TAS, CAS, FAA, Swap, LL/SC.
- [x] **Atomicity** is the defining property — no other processor can observe an intermediate state during the primitive's execution.
- [x] **Test-and-Set** is the simplest primitive, returning the old value while unconditionally writing `1`.
- [x] **Compare-and-Swap** conditionally writes `new` only if the current value equals `expected`; foundation of lock-free algorithms.
- [x] **Fetch-and-Add** atomically increments and returns the previous value; basis of ticket locks and barriers with FIFO fairness.
- [x] **Atomic Exchange (Swap)** swaps a register and a memory location in one step.
- [x] **Load-Linked / Store-Conditional (LL/SC)** is the RISC primitive pair; SC fails on any intervening write to the same cache line.
- [x] **Cache coherence protocols (MESI)** are the mechanism by which modern hardware enforces atomicity — exclusive ownership of the cache line for the duration of the RMW.
- [x] **Cache-line ping-pong** is the dominant performance cost of naïve TAS spinlocks; mitigated by Test-and-Test-and-Set (TTS) or queueing locks (MCS, CLH).
- [x] **ABA problem** affects CAS but not LL/SC; software workaround is to embed a version counter alongside the pointer.
- [x] **Ticket locks** (using FAA) provide bounded FIFO fairness — each arrival gets a unique ticket, threads spin until `now_serving` matches.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law** bounds speedup; the synchronization overhead fraction $f_{sync}(N)$ grows with $N$ and eventually dominates.
- [x] **Optimal processor count** $N_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_{parallel} \cdot T_{total}}{c \cdot T_{coh}}}$ — beyond this, adding processors hurts performance.
- [x] **Memory barriers (fences)** are often required alongside hardware primitives to enforce the correct ordering of loads and stores — primitives alone do not guarantee ordering of *other* memory operations.
- [x] **Higher-level constructs** built on hardware primitives: spinlocks, blocking mutexes, semaphores, barriers, lock-free stacks/queues/counters, monitors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
