topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Memory Consistency Models - Sequential and Relaxed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MEMORY CONSISTENCY MODELS: SEQUENTIAL AND RELAXED

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Consistency Model (MCM):** A formal specification (a contract between the programmer and the memory system) that defines the legal orderings of memory operations (loads and stores) issued by one or more processors, and the resulting values that can be observed by any processor in a shared-memory multiprocessor system.

In the context of **Advanced Computer Architecture (PECST528)**, the memory consistency model is the **architectural interface** that hides the implementation details of caches, write buffers, interconnect networks, and out-of-order execution pipelines from the parallel programmer, while still permitting high-performance hardware optimizations.

The two principal families covered in KTU Module 3 are:

| Family | Defining Property | Order Preserved |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequential Consistency (SC)** | Strongest intuitive model | **All** memory operations appear in a single global total order consistent with each thread's program order |
| **Relaxed Consistency Models** | Permits reordering of certain op-pairs | Only specific pairs (e.g., synchronisation ops) are guaranteed ordered |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lamport's Definition (1979) — Sequential Consistency:** *"A multiprocessor is sequentially consistent if the result of any execution is the same as if the operations of all the processors were executed in some sequential order, and the operations of each individual processor occur in this sequence in the order specified by its program."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Notice Board" in a Hostel:**
>
> Imagine 4 students (P1, P2, P3, P4) writing notices on a common notice board (shared memory). A **sequentially consistent board** enforces that *every student walking past sees the notices in the exact same global chronological order*, AND a student who *wrote* notice A *before* notice B is *guaranteed* that any reader sees A before B. The notice board is operated by a single librarian who is excruciatingly slow but fair.
>
> A **relaxed board (relaxed consistency)** allows the librarian to shuffle the notices to save time — students may see notices posted by the same friend in a different order, AS LONG AS the special "Red Pin" notices (synchronisation/locks) are always processed in strict order. This is faster, but you must be careful which notices you read together.
>
> The key insight: **SC trades performance for ease of reasoning; Relaxed models trade ease of reasoning for performance.**

### 1.3 Why Memory Consistency Matters in KTU Context

- **Performance vs. Programmability Trade-off:** Modern CPUs use write buffers, store-forwarding, prefetchers, and out-of-order issue — all of which naturally *reorder* memory operations. The consistency model tells the programmer which reorders are *forbidden*.
- **Correctness of Parallel Programs:** Without a well-defined model, race conditions and bugs become non-deterministic across hardware vendors.
- **Hardware/Software Co-design:** Compiler optimisations (e.g., reordering loads/stores) and hardware buffers must obey the same contract.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric — Effective Memory Throughput (EMT):** $\mathrm{EMT} = \dfrac{N_{\text{ops}}}{T_{\text{latency}} + T_{\text{queueing}}}$, measured in **Million Memory Operations per Second (MOPS)**. Relaxed models aim to **minimise $T_{\text{queueing}}$** by allowing more overlap between independent accesses.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Memory Operations

Every memory instruction is classified as one of two types:

1. **Memory Read (Load):** $L(x)$ — reads value of address $x$ into a register.
2. **Memory Write (Store):** $S(x, v)$ — writes value $v$ to address $x$.

For a parallel program with $N$ threads, the **global execution** consists of $N$ program-order traces, and the consistency model restricts the **interleaving** of these traces.

### 2.2 The Program Order ($<_{\text{po}}$) vs. Memory Order ($<_{\text{mo}}$)

- **Program Order ($<_{\text{po}}$):** The order in which a *single processor* issues its memory operations as per the source program.
- **Memory Order ($<_{\text{mo}}$):** The order in which memory operations are *globally* presented to the shared memory subsystem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Principle:** Every consistency model guarantees that **at least** the program order is preserved for certain operation pairs. The strength of a model is measured by *how many* such pairs are constrained.

### 2.3 The Four Reordering Rules (Critical for KTU)

For any two memory operations $A$ and $B$ issued by the same processor, with $A <_{\text{po}} B$, the reordering $B$ before $A$ is allowed **iff** the model permits it:

| Reorder | Notation | Description | Allowed in SC? | Allowed in Weak? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Write→Read (W→R)** | $S(x); L(y)$ | Store then Load on different addresses | **No** | Yes |
| **Write→Write (W→W)** | $S(x); S(y)$ | Store then Store on different addresses | **No** | Yes |
| **Read→Read (R→R)** | $L(x); L(y)$ | Load then Load on different addresses | **No** | Yes |
| **Read→Write (R→W)** | $L(x); S(y)$ | Load then Store on different addresses | **No** | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** SC forbids **all four** reorders. The most famous and insidious reorder that relaxed models permit is **W→R**, which is the source of the classic "write buffer visibility" bug. The Linux Kernel `smp_wmb()` and `smp_rmb()` macros exist precisely to plug these reorders.

### 2.4 Detailed Model-by-Model Analysis

#### (A) Sequential Consistency (SC)

**Definition (Operational):** There exists a total order $<_{\text{mo}}$ of all memory operations such that:
- For every processor $P_i$: $A <_{\text{po}} B \implies A <_{\text{mo}} B$ (preserves program order).
- Every load returns the value of the *latest* store in $<_{\text{mo}}$ to the same address.

**Hardware Cost:** Requires strict ordering at the L1 cache controller, a fully ordered memory interconnect (e.g., a shared bus or directory with ordered acks), and *no write buffer reordering* across loads.

#### (B) Total Store Order (TSO) — *x86 default*

**Definition (Sparc/Intel):** The weakest model that still preserves the *acquire-release* semantics of x86. Allows:
- **W→R reorder** (stores can overtake later loads).
- All other pairs remain ordered.
- Loads can be **speculative** and re-executed if a later store from another core is detected.

**Hardware Mechanism:** Uses a **Write Combining Buffer (WCB)** per core. Stores go into the WCB and drain to L2 in FIFO order. Loads *bypass* the WCB and read from L1 directly (and may see the WCB entry via *store-to-load forwarding*).

#### (C) Processor Consistency (PC)

A slightly weaker model than TSO:
- Allows **W→R** reorder.
- Allows **W→W** reorder.
- *Requires* the **write-to-memory ordering** be preserved as observed by *all* processors (i.e., writes from the same processor become visible to all others in the same order).

#### (D) Partial Store Order (PSO) — *Sparc default weak*

- Allows **W→R**, **W→W**, and **R→R** reorders.
- Only **R→W** (Read-to-Write) is forbidden.
- Programmer must use `MEMBAR` (memory barrier) instructions to enforce order.

#### (E) Weak Ordering (WO) / Release Consistency (RC)

- **No reordering is forbidden by default** — all four pairs can be reordered.
- **Synchronisation operations** (acquire, release) are *atomic* and ordered.
- **Acquire ($L_a$):** A load followed by an acquire cannot be reordered with any subsequent memory operation.
- **Release ($S_r$):** A store followed by a release cannot be reordered with any prior memory operation.
- **Acquire-Release Pairing:** If $S_r(x)$ synchronises with $L_a(x)$ (i.e., $S_r$ writes a value that $L_a$ later reads), then all writes *before* $S_r$ are visible to all reads *after* $L_a$.

> [!NOTE]
> **ARM/POWER default is Release Consistency (RC).** It is the weakest model among those in KTU Module 3 and provides the highest hardware flexibility.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Sequential order constraint | $<_{\text{mo}} \supseteq <_{\text{po}}$ | Boolean inclusion — strictly enforced in SC |
| Number of forbidden reorders in SC | $\,4\,$ | All four W→R, W→W, R→R, R→W blocked |
| Forbidden reorders in TSO | $\,3\,$ | W→R allowed; W→W, R→R, R→W blocked |
| Forbidden reorders in PSO | $\,1\,$ | Only R→W blocked |
| Forbidden reorders in RC | $\,0\,$ | Default; only acquire/release provide ordering |
| Acquire-Release property | $S_r(x) \xrightarrow{\text{sync}} L_a(x) \Rightarrow$ *all prior writes visible* | Causality guarantee |
| Write Buffer Drain Latency | $T_{\text{drain}} = N_{\text{stores}} \times t_{\text{wb}}$ | seconds, where $t_{\text{wb}}$ is per-store drain time |
| Effective Memory Throughput | $\mathrm{EMT} = \dfrac{N_{\text{ops}}}{T_{\text{latency}} + T_{\text{queueing}}}$ | **MOPS** (Million Operations Per Second) |
| Store-to-Load Forwarding | $L(x)$ reads pending $S(x)$ in WCB | TSO optimisation; same-address only |
| Memory Fence Cost | $T_{\text{fence}} \approx T_{\text{round-trip-interconnect}}$ | Order of **100–500 ns** in NUMA systems |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Operating Systems** | Linux `smp_mb()`, `smp_rmb()`, `smp_wmb()` are explicit fences. |
| **Compilers** | `volatile`, `std::atomic`, `memory_order_relaxed` in C++11/20 map to fences. |
| **GPU Computing** | CUDA's `__threadfence()` and Vulkan memory barriers implement release consistency. |
| **Distributed Systems** | Consistency models of replicated databases (linearisable, causal) are direct analogues. |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reorder Count for Consistency Models

We derive the number of forbidden reorders as a function of the model's permitted reorders.

Let $R_{\text{types}} = \{ W \rightarrow R, \; W \rightarrow W, \; R \rightarrow R, \; R \rightarrow W \}$, with $\vert R_{\text{types}} \vert = 4$.

For any model $M$, let $P(M) \subseteq R_{\text{types}}$ be the set of *permitted* reorders. The set of *forbidden* reorders is $F(M) = R_{\text{types}} \setminus P(M)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of forbidden reorders in } M \;\; &= \;\; \vert F(M) \vert \\
&= \;\; 4 - \vert P(M) \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Sequential Consistency:** By definition, $P(\text{SC}) = \emptyset$, so

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert F(\text{SC}) \vert \;\; &= \;\; 4 - 0 \\
&= \;\; 4
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* All four operation-pair reorders are forbidden. This is the strongest model.

**Step 2 — Total Store Order (TSO):** Permits $W \rightarrow R$ reordering only (the WCB can hoist stores past subsequent loads). So $P(\text{TSO}) = \{W \rightarrow R\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert F(\text{TSO}) \vert \;\; &= \;\; 4 - 1 \\
&= \;\; 3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* $W \rightarrow W$, $R \rightarrow R$, and $R \rightarrow W$ are forbidden. Only the most permissive (yet practical) reorder is allowed.

**Step 3 — Processor Consistency (PC):** Permits $W \rightarrow R$ and $W \rightarrow W$. So $P(\text{PC}) = \{W \rightarrow R, W \rightarrow W\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert F(\text{PC}) \vert \;\; &= \;\; 4 - 2 \\
&= \;\; 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Partial Store Order (PSO):** Permits $W \rightarrow R$, $W \rightarrow W$, and $R \rightarrow R$. So $P(\text{PSO}) = \{W \rightarrow R, W \rightarrow W, R \rightarrow R\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert F(\text{PSO}) \vert \;\; &= \;\; 4 - 3 \\
&= \;\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

*Only* the $R \rightarrow W$ reorder is forbidden.

**Step 5 — Release Consistency (RC):** All four are permitted in the absence of synchronisation. $P(\text{RC}) = R_{\text{types}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert F(\text{RC}) \vert \;\; &= \;\; 4 - 4 \\
&= \;\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* Without explicit acquire/release, the model imposes no ordering. The hardware is fully free to reorder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strong-to-Weak Ordering (Kleene Lattice):** $\text{SC} \sqsubseteq \text{TSO} \sqsubseteq \text{PC} \sqsubseteq \text{PSO} \sqsubseteq \text{WO/RC}$. Each $\sqsubseteq$ step *removes* a constraint. This is a KTU-favourite question: "Arrange the models in order of decreasing strength."

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Per-Model Behaviour on the Dekker Litmus Test

The **Dekker test** is the canonical litmus test for SC. Two threads interlock on two flags:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Thread 1:} \quad & S(\text{flag1}, 1); \\
& r1 = L(\text{flag2}); \\
& \text{if } (r1 == 0) \;\; \text{then critical section}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Thread 2:} \quad & S(\text{flag2}, 1); \\
& r2 = L(\text{flag1}); \\
& \text{if } (r2 == 0) \;\; \text{then critical section}
\end{aligned}
$$

Under SC, the outcome $(r1 = 0 \;\land\; r2 = 0)$ is **impossible** (mutual exclusion). Under relaxed models without fences, the outcome is *allowed*.

**Step-by-step proof of impossibility under SC:**

Let $S_1 = S(\text{flag1},1)$ issued by Thread 1, $S_2 = S(\text{flag2},1)$ by Thread 2. Let $L_1 = L(\text{flag2})$ by Thread 1, $L_2 = L(\text{flag1})$ by Thread 2.

**Step A:** Program order in Thread 1: $S_1 <_{\text{po}} L_1$. Under SC this implies $S_1 <_{\text{mo}} L_1$.

**Step B:** Program order in Thread 2: $S_2 <_{\text{po}} L_2$. Under SC this implies $S_2 <_{\text{mo}} L_2$.

**Step C:** If $r1 = 0$, then $L_1$ *must have been ordered* before $S_2$ in memory, otherwise $L_1$ would have returned the new value $1$. So $S_2 <_{\text{mo}} L_1$ is false, hence $L_1 <_{\text{mo}} S_2$ or they are equal (equal-impossible for distinct values).

**Step D:** Combining A and C: $S_1 <_{\text{mo}} L_1 <_{\text{mo}} S_2 <_{\text{mo}} L_2$.

**Step E:** Therefore $L_2$ is *after* $S_1$ in $<_{\text{mo}}$, so $L_2$ *must* read the value $1$ written by $S_1$. Hence $r2 = 1$, contradicting the assumption $r2 = 0$.

**Conclusion:** The outcome $(r1 = 0 \;\land\; r2 = 0)$ violates SC, proving Dekker's mutual exclusion works under SC.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Which Reorder Breaks Dekker?

| Reorder Allowed | Breaks Dekker? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $W \rightarrow R$ | **Yes** | $S(\text{flag1},1)$ may pass $L(\text{flag2})$ in WCB. |
| $W \rightarrow W$ | **Yes** | $S(\text{flag1},1)$ may pass $S(\text{flag2},1)$ on Thread 2. |
| $R \rightarrow R$ | Marginal | $L(\text{flag1})$ on T2 may see stale value. |
| $R \rightarrow W$ | Yes | $L(\text{flag1})$ on T2 may reorder with $S(\text{flag2},1)$ on T2. |

> [!TIP]
> **Kleene's monotonicity theorem for consistency models:** If a program is *race-free* under model $M$, and $M' \sqsubseteq M$, then the program is also race-free under $M'$ *provided* all races occur on synchronisation variables. This is the formal justification for "SC-by-default" programming with explicit fences.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Consistency Models (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["SC Sequential Consistency"]:::strong
    nodeB["TSO Total Store Order"]:::strong
    nodeC["PC Processor Consistency"]:::med
    nodeD["PSO Partial Store Order"]:::med
    nodeE["WO Weak Ordering"]:::weak
    nodeF["RC Release Consistency"]:::weak
    nodeA -->|"Relax W to R"| nodeB
    nodeB -->|"Relax W to W"| nodeC
    nodeC -->|"Relax R to R"| nodeD
    nodeD -->|"Relax R to W"| nodeE
    nodeE -->|"Add Acq Rel"| nodeF
    classDef strong fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef med fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef weak fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Write Combining Buffer (TSO) Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP1["Core P1 Pipeline"]:::core
    nodeP2["Core P2 Pipeline"]:::core
    nodeWCB1["WCB P1 FIFO"]:::wcb
    nodeWCB2["WCB P2 FIFO"]:::wcb
    nodeL1P1["L1 Cache P1"]:::l1
    nodeL1P2["L1 Cache P2"]:::l1
    nodeBUS["Shared Memory Interconnect"]:::bus
    nodeMEM["Main Memory Directory"]:::mem
    nodeP1 -->|Store| nodeWCB1
    nodeP1 -->|Load bypass| nodeL1P1
    nodeP2 -->|Store| nodeWCB2
    nodeP2 -->|Load bypass| nodeL1P2
    nodeWCB1 -->|FIFO drain| nodeBUS
    nodeWCB2 -->|FIFO drain| nodeBUS
    nodeL1P1 <--> nodeBUS
    nodeL1P2 <--> nodeBUS
    nodeBUS --> nodeMEM
    classDef core fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef wcb fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef l1 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef bus fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef mem fill:#404040,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Acquire-Release Synchronisation in Release Consistency

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeT1A["Thread 1: Write Data"]:::th1
    nodeT1R["Thread 1: Release Store S_r flag"]:::th1
    nodeT2A["Thread 2: Acquire Load L_a flag"]:::th2
    nodeT2R["Thread 2: Read Data"]:::th2
    nodeT1A -->|po| nodeT1R
    nodeT1R -->|sync hb| nodeT2A
    nodeT2A -->|po| nodeT2R
    classDef th1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef th2 fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Fence / Barrier Insertion Pattern

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeOp1["Op A"]:::op
    nodeMB["Memory Barrier MFENCE"]:::fence
    nodeOp2["Op B"]:::op
    nodeOp3["Op C"]:::op
    nodeOp1 -->|po| nodeMB
    nodeMB -->|"guarantees visibility"| nodeOp2
    nodeOp3 -.->|"may reorder with A"| nodeMB
    classDef op fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fence fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SC System

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeCPU1["CPU 1 Issue Unit"]:::cpu
    nodeCPU2["CPU 2 Issue Unit"]:::cpu
    nodeLSU1["LSU 1 In Order"]:::lsu
    nodeLSU2["LSU 2 In Order"]:::lsu
    nodeARB["Central Memory Arbiter SC Enforcer"]:::arb
    nodeMEM["Shared Memory Bank"]:::mem
    nodeCPU1 --> nodeLSU1
    nodeCPU2 --> nodeLSU2
    nodeLSU1 --> nodeARB
    nodeLSU2 --> nodeARB
    nodeARB -->|single total order| nodeMEM
    classDef cpu fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef lsu fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef arb fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef mem fill:#404040,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

#### Question 1. Define Sequential Consistency. State Lamport's two conditions. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
A multiprocessor is **sequentially consistent (SC)** if the result of any execution is the same as if the operations of all the processors were executed in **some sequential order**, and the operations of each individual processor occur in this sequence in the order specified by its program. Lamport's two conditions are:

1. **Program Order Preservation:** For every processor $P_i$, if $A <_{\text{po}} B$ then $A <_{\text{mo}} B$.
2. **Coherent Memory View:** Every load $L(x)$ returns the value of the most recent store $S(x, v)$ in the global memory order.

#### Question 2. Differentiate between **Write-Read** reordering permitted in TSO and forbidden in SC. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
In **SC**, a store $S(x)$ issued by processor $P_i$ cannot be reordered with a later load $L(y)$ issued by the same $P_i$, even when $x \neq y$. In **TSO (Total Store Order)**, the write combining buffer (WCB) permits $S(x)$ to *bypass* $L(y)$ and be drained to memory later. This is called **Write-to-Read (W→R) reordering**. The only restriction in TSO is that $L(y)$ can read the pending $S(x)$ via *store-to-load forwarding* if $x = y$. The W→R reorder is the source of the classic **"store buffer visibility" bug** in lock-free code.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. **Do NOT mix up model strengths.** Many students wrongly state TSO is weaker than PC. Strictly: SC > TSO > PC > PSO > WO/RC.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse the W→R reordering with caching.** Reordering happens in the *write buffer* before reaching the cache, not in the cache itself.
> 3. **When asked for "hardware mechanism," name the buffer/latch.** A bare mention of "ordering" without naming *write combining buffer*, *store queue*, or *invalidate queue* loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Always include program order ($<_{\text{po}}$) vs memory order ($<_{\text{mo}}$) notation** when formalising the model. Examiners award 2 marks for notation alone.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Sequential Consistency (SC)** model in detail. List the four reorder rules it forbids and describe the **Dekker's algorithm** litmus test, proving mathematically why the outcome $(r1 = 0 \land r2 = 0)$ is impossible under SC.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Total Store Order (TSO)** model used in x86 architectures. With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the **Write Combining Buffer (WCB)** and how it enables Write-to-Read reordering while preserving the other three orderings. Show how store-to-load forwarding maintains single-address consistency.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Sequential Consistency:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** Sequential Consistency (Lamport 1979) requires that there exists a **global total order** of all memory operations (denoted $<_{\text{mo}}$) such that:
- (i) For every processor $P_i$ and every pair of operations $A, B$ issued by $P_i$ with $A <_{\text{po}} B$, we have $A <_{\text{mo}} B$.
- (ii) Every load $L(x)$ returns the value written by the *latest* store to $x$ in $<_{\text{mo}}$.

**Step 2 — Four Forbidden Reorders:** SC forbids the reordering of the following four pairs issued by the same processor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{W} \rightarrow \text{R} &: S(x); L(y) \quad \text{store then load} \\
\text{W} \rightarrow \text{W} &: S(x); S(y) \quad \text{store then store} \\
\text{R} \rightarrow \text{R} &: L(x); L(y) \quad \text{load then load} \\
\text{R} \rightarrow \text{W} &: L(x); S(y) \quad \text{load then store}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Dekker's Algorithm Setup:** Two threads, initially both flags = 0, attempt to enter a critical section:

Thread 1: $S(\text{flag1}, 1); \quad r1 = L(\text{flag2});$
Thread 2: $S(\text{flag2}, 1); \quad r2 = L(\text{flag1});$

**Step 4 — Proof that $(r1 = 0 \land r2 = 0)$ is impossible under SC:**

Assume for contradiction that $r1 = 0$ and $r2 = 0$. By program order:
- Thread 1: $S(\text{flag1},1) <_{\text{po}} L(\text{flag2})$
- Thread 2: $S(\text{flag2},1) <_{\text{po}} L(\text{flag1})$

Under SC, this transfers to memory order: $S(\text{flag1},1) <_{\text{mo}} L(\text{flag2})$ and $S(\text{flag2},1) <_{\text{mo}} L(\text{flag1})$.

If $r1 = 0$, then $L(\text{flag2})$ read the *initial* value $0$, which means $S(\text{flag2},1)$ *cannot* be ordered before $L(\text{flag2})$ in $<_{\text{mo}}$. Therefore:

$$
L(\text{flag2}) <_{\text{mo}} S(\text{flag2},1)
$$

Chaining: $S(\text{flag1},1) <_{\text{mo}} L(\text{flag2}) <_{\text{mo}} S(\text{flag2},1) <_{\text{mo}} L(\text{flag1})$. But this means $L(\text{flag1})$ is *after* $S(\text{flag1},1)$ in memory order, so it must read the new value $1$. Thus $r2 = 1$, contradicting $r2 = 0$. QED.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Total Store Order (TSO):**

**Step 1 — Definition:** TSO is a relaxation of SC that permits **only the W→R reorder**. It is the memory model of the x86 architecture family (Intel/AMD).

**Step 2 — Hardware Mechanism (Write Combining Buffer):** Each processor core has a private **Write Combining Buffer (WCB)** — a small FIFO (typically 40–80 entries) sitting between the store pipeline and the L1 cache. Stores are first enqueued in the WCB; the core continues execution immediately. Loads bypass the WCB and go directly to L1.

**Step 3 — Reorder Analysis:** Because the WCB drains stores to L1 *asynchronously* and in a separate transaction from the loads, a store $S(x)$ can be **deferred** while a later load $L(y)$ on a different address is issued and completed. This is the W→R reorder.

**Step 4 — Same-Address Coherence via Store-to-Load Forwarding:** To preserve program order on the *same* address, when a load $L(x)$ is issued, the WCB is *snooped*; if a pending $S(x)$ is found, the WCB entry is forwarded directly to the load without waiting for the L1 miss. This guarantees:

$$
L(x) \;\; \text{returns the value of the most recent pending } S(x) \text{ in the WCB}
$$

**Step 5 — Block Diagram Reference:** The TSO architecture consists of Core $\rightarrow$ WCB $\rightarrow$ L1 $\rightarrow$ Interconnect $\rightarrow$ Memory. Loads bypass the WCB but check it via the *forwarding path*. Stores enter the WCB in program order and drain in FIFO order to L1.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** TSO preserves W→W, R→R, and R→W orderings but allows W→R reorder. The programmer must use a `MFENCE` (full memory barrier) to re-impose W→R ordering when needed, as in spinlock implementations.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Release Consistency (RC)** model. Define the **acquire** and **release** primitives and prove the **synchronises-with** property: if $S_r(x)$ synchronises with $L_a(x)$, then all writes prior to $S_r$ are visible to all reads after $L_a$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **SC, TSO, PSO, and RC** along four dimensions: (i) forbidden reorders, (ii) hardware mechanism, (iii) representative architecture, and (iv) programmer-visible fence instructions. Present your answer as a comparative analytical matrix.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Release Consistency:**

**Step 1 — Motivation:** RC is the **weakest practical model** used in production hardware (ARM, IBM POWER, RISC-V "RVWMO"). It provides no ordering guarantees for ordinary loads and stores — the hardware is free to reorder all four pairs. Ordering is provided *only* by special synchronisation primitives.

**Step 2 — Definitions:**

- **Release Store $S_r(x, v)$:** A store to $x$ marked as a *release*. It is guaranteed to be visible to the memory subsystem **after** all prior ordinary loads and stores issued by the same thread have completed. (Equivalent to a "Store-Fence" + Store.)
- **Acquire Load $L_a(x)$:** A load from $x$ marked as an *acquire*. It is guaranteed to be issued **before** all subsequent ordinary loads and stores issued by the same thread. (Equivalent to Load + "Load-Fence".)

**Step 3 — Synchronises-With Relation ($S_r \xrightarrow{\text{sw}} L_a$):** A release store $S_r(x,v)$ *synchronises-with* an acquire load $L_a(x)$ on the *same address $x$* if $L_a(x)$ reads the value $v$ written by $S_r$ (or any value written by a store in the *coherence order* after $S_r$).

**Step 4 — Proof of Happens-Before Transitivity:**

Let $\{W_1, W_2, \dots, W_k\}$ be the ordinary writes performed by Thread 1 before $S_r(x,v)$, and let $\{R_1, R_2, \dots, R_m\}$ be the ordinary reads performed by Thread 2 after $L_a(x) = v$.

*By Release semantics:* The WCB/Store Buffer of Thread 1 must be drained *before* $S_r$ becomes visible. Therefore:

$$
W_i <_{\text{po}} S_r \;\; \text{and} \;\; W_i <_{\text{hb}} S_r \;\; (i = 1 \dots k)
$$

*By Acquire semantics:* Thread 2's load $L_a(x)$ cannot be reordered with subsequent $R_j$, hence:

$$
L_a <_{\text{po}} R_j \;\; \text{and} \;\; L_a <_{\text{hb}} R_j \;\; (j = 1 \dots m)
$$

*By the Synchronises-With relation:*

$$
S_r \xrightarrow{\text{sw}} L_a
$$

Combining via the *happens-before* ($\text{hb}$) transitive closure:

$$
W_i <_{\text{hb}} S_r \xrightarrow{\text{sw}} L_a <_{\text{hb}} R_j \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; W_i <_{\text{hb}} R_j
$$

Since the system also satisfies **coherence** (single-address total order), $R_j$ is guaranteed to observe the value of $W_i$ (or a later write to the same address). QED.

**Step 5 — Engineering Implication:** In Linux kernel, `smp_store_release(ptr, val)` and `smp_load_acquire(ptr)` implement exactly this pattern. In C++11: `std::atomic<T>::store(val, memory_order_release)` and `std::atomic<T>::load(memory_order_acquire)`.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Comparative Matrix:**

| Dimension | SC | TSO | PSO | RC (Weak) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Forbidden reorders** | All 4 (W→R, W→W, R→R, R→W) | 3 (W→R allowed) | 1 (only R→W) | 0 (none — acquire/release provide ordering) |
| **(ii) Hardware mechanism** | In-order LSU, ordered interconnect, no WCB reordering | Write Combining Buffer per core; loads bypass WCB | Multiple store buffers, allow per-address out-of-order drain | Aggressive out-of-order; tagged load/store queues; explicit fence unit |
| **(iii) Representative architecture** | Classical MIPS R10k (academic examples) | x86 (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen) | SPARC (legacy) | ARMv8, IBM POWER, RISC-V |
| **(iv) Programmer fence** | Not needed | `MFENCE` (full), `LFENCE` (load), `SFENCE` (store) | `MEMBAR #StoreLoad` | `DMB` (ARM), `lwsync`/`sync` (POWER), `fence` (RISC-V) |
| **(v) Relative speed** | 1× (baseline) | ~1.2–1.5× | ~1.5–1.8× | ~2–3× |
| **(vi) Difficulty of programming** | Easy (intuitive) | Moderate (lock-free needs care) | Hard (manual MEMBAR) | Hardest (acquire/release discipline) |

**Step-by-step Reasoning for the Matrix:**

- **SC → TSO:** Removing the W→R restriction is the *smallest* relaxation that admits a write buffer, hence the performance gain is small.
- **TSO → PSO:** Removing W→W allows independent store streams to drain in parallel, doubling throughput on store-heavy workloads.
- **PSO → RC:** Removing R→R and the last constraint lets the OoO engine fully pipeline memory; the only synchronisation cost is on the actual acquire/release instructions, which are rare.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Summary Insight:** Each step down the lattice *removes one or more constraints* on the hardware but *imposes a corresponding burden* on the programmer. RC achieves near-peak hardware performance but requires the most disciplined use of synchronisation primitives.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Sequential Consistency (SC)** preserves *all four* reorder pairs, giving the strongest programmability guarantees but the weakest performance.
- [ ] **Total Store Order (TSO)** is the *x86 default model*; it permits W→R reordering via the **Write Combining Buffer (WCB)** but preserves the other three.
- [ ] **Processor Consistency (PC)** additionally permits W→W reordering but maintains *write-to-memory* consistency as observed by all processors.
- [ ] **Partial Store Order (PSO)** is Sparc's weak default; only R→W is forbidden; requires explicit `MEMBAR` instructions.
- [ ] **Weak Ordering (WO)** and **Release Consistency (RC)** allow *all four* reorders; ordering is achieved solely through **acquire** and **release** synchronisation primitives.
- [ ] **Synchronises-With** relation: $S_r(x) \xrightarrow{\text{sw}} L_a(x)$ if the acquire load reads the value written by the release store.
- [ ] **Happens-Before ($\text{hb}$)** is the transitive closure of program order and synchronises-with; it is the *causality* relation in the memory model.
- [ ] **Dekker's algorithm** is the canonical litmus test for SC; under SC the outcome $(r1 = 0 \land r2 = 0)$ is **impossible**, but it becomes possible under all relaxed models.
- [ ] **Lattice of models:** $\text{SC} \sqsupset \text{TSO} \sqsupset \text{PC} \sqsupset \text{PSO} \sqsupset \text{WO/RC}$. Strength decreases, hardware flexibility increases, programmer burden increases.
- [ ] **Architectures:** x86 = TSO; ARM/POWER/RISC-V = RC; SPARC = PSO (legacy); classical textbook MIPS = SC.
- [ ] **Fence instructions:** `MFENCE` (x86 full), `DMB` (ARM), `lwsync` (POWER), `fence` (RISC-V), `MEMBAR` (SPARC).
- [ ] **Performance metric:** Effective Memory Throughput $\mathrm{EMT} = \dfrac{N_{\text{ops}}}{T_{\text{latency}} + T_{\text{queueing}}}$ in **MOPS** — relaxed models minimise $T_{\text{queueing}}$.
- [ ] **Hardware units to memorise:** Write Combining Buffer (WCB), Store Queue (SQ), Invalidate Queue (IQ), Load Buffer (LB), Fence Unit.
- [ ] **Exam mantra:** "Stronger model = fewer reorders permitted = simpler reasoning, slower hardware."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

topics.title: Warehouse Scale Computers - Goals and Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Warehouse Scale Computers - Goals and Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Warehouse-Scale Computer (WSC)** is a collection of thousands of individual computers (servers, storage, and networking gear) physically co-located, homogeneously designed, operated as a single unified computing resource, and accessed by a high-speed local area network (LAN) that delivers predictable latency to a small number of highly scalable Internet-facing services.

According to the **Hennessy and Patterson** classification used in the KTU 2024 syllabus, a WSC is fundamentally distinct from a traditional data center in three architectural aspects:

- **Homogeneity** of hardware and software platforms
- **Single administrative domain** (single ownership and operation)
- **Service-oriented workload** (typically search, social networking, video streaming, machine learning inference, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept)**
> A WSC is "an entity that deploys, operates, and manages Internet-scale services by housing tens of thousands of servers in a single facility, treating the entire facility as a single, massively parallel machine."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive public library warehouse**:
- A single shelf = 1 server
- An aisle = 1 rack
- The entire warehouse = 1 WSC

When you search Google, you're not asking *one* computer; you are asking **a coordinated fleet of ~50,000–100,000 computers** that split your query, work on fragments in parallel, and reassemble the answer in milliseconds. From your perspective, the entire warehouse behaves as **one giant computer**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU**: The KTU 2024 paper expects students to articulate the *shift in abstraction* — programmers no longer write code for a *machine*, they write code for a *service* running on a *facility*.

### 1.3 Key Physical and Architectural Constants

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Server count per WSC | **10,000 – 100,000** | servers |
| Power consumption per WSC | **10 – 200** | MW |
| Building floor area | **~100,000** | m² |
| Network bisection bandwidth | **> 1** | Tbps |
| PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) | **1.1 – 1.2** | ratio |
| Server power density | **5 – 15** | kW/rack |
| Annual hardware failure rate | **2 – 10 %** | % |
| Tier-IV availability target | **99.995 %** | uptime |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Goals of a WSC (Hierarchical Decomposition)

The architectural goals of a WSC must be viewed as a **stacked hierarchy**, where higher-level goals decompose into lower-level engineering decisions:

**Level 1 — Business Goal**
- Maximize revenue, minimize cost-per-query, ensure 24×7 service availability.

**Level 2 — Service-Level Goal**
- Deliver interactive latency (e.g., search < **300 ms**), high throughput, and elasticity under traffic spikes.

**Level 3 — WSC-Level Goal**
- Achieve a balanced **cost-performance-energy** envelope across the entire facility.

**Level 4 — Hardware/Software-Level Goal**
- Per-server: maximize compute density, minimize energy per instruction, provide redundant paths.

### 2.2 Detailed Goal Decomposition (Engineering 'Why' & 'How')

**Goal 1 — Cost-Efficiency at Scale**
- *Why*: A WSC consumes MW of power; cooling, power distribution, and amortized server cost dominate OpEx.
- *How*: Use low-cost, high-volume commodity hardware, not premium enterprise servers.

**Goal 2 — Predictable, Interactive Latency**
- *Why*: A 100 ms slowdown reduces e-commerce revenue by 7 % (industry data).
- *How*: Tail-latency optimization; P99.9 percentile latency must be < service budget.

**Goal 3 — High Throughput / Bandwidth**
- *Why*: A WSC must serve billions of requests/day.
- *How*: Scale-out architecture; data and work distributed across many servers.

**Goal 4 — Reliability & Availability**
- *Why*: Hardware failures are *normal* events (servers fail daily).
- *How*: Software must tolerate failures gracefully; design for *nines* of availability.

**Goal 5 — Energy Proportionality**
- *Why*: Idle servers still consume 50–60 % of peak power; energy bills are enormous.
- *How*: Use power-aware scheduling, DVFS, server consolidation.

**Goal 6 — Network Scalability**
- *Why*: Bisection bandwidth must scale linearly with server count.
- *How*: Clos / fat-tree topology, optical interconnects, distributed switching.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **NOTE ON KTU 2024 EVALUATION**: For derivations, always use `\vert` (not `\vert` pipes in markdown). Here we use LaTeX display blocks.

$$
\text{Availability} \;=\; \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} \;+\; \text{MTTR}}
$$

$$
\text{Annual Downtime (minutes)} \;=\; 525{,}600 \times \big(1 \;-\; \text{Availability}\big)
$$

$$
\text{PUE} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Facility Power}}{\text{IT Equipment Power}}
$$

$$
\text{Performance/Cost} \;=\; \frac{\text{Throughput (QPS)}}{\text{TCO per Year (\$)}}
$$

$$
\text{Speedup (Amdahl's Law)} \;=\; \frac{1}{(1-p) \;+\; \dfrac{p}{n}}
$$

$$
\text{Tail-Latency Penalty} \;=\; \frac{\text{P99.9 Latency}}{\text{Median Latency}}
$$

$$
\text{Energy-Proportionality Gap} \;=\; \text{Power}_{\text{idle}} \;/\; \text{Power}_{\text{peak}}
$$

$$
\text{Amortized Cost} \;=\; \frac{\text{Server Cost (\$)}}{\text{Lifetime (years)} \times \text{Utilization}}
$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Numerical Facts (Memorize)

| Parameter | Value | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Service budget for interactive search | **< 300 ms** | Hard deadline for WSC architects |
| Target for tail latency | **P99.9 < 1 s** | Drives software-level load balancing |
| Industry PUE leadership | **~ 1.07 – 1.12** | Best in class (Google) |
| Server replacement cycle | **3 – 5 years** | Faster than enterprise norm |
| Cooling efficiency (PUE cooling portion) | **< 1.05** | Free-cooling, hot/cold aisle |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Providers** (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, Alibaba) use WSC principles to bid at lower prices by reducing PUE.
- **AI/ML Training** clusters (e.g., GPT-class models) borrow WSC fabric design for tightly coupled accelerator fabrics.
- **Telecom & 5G Core** networks are increasingly WSC-based for NFV (Network Functions Virtualization).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Availability-to-Downtime Mapping

**Problem**: A WSC operator claims **99.99 % availability** (the "four nines"). Calculate the *allowable* annual downtime for both repair and planned maintenance.

**Step 1 — Identify the constants.**
Total minutes in a year:

$$
T_{\text{year}} \;=\; 60 \times 24 \times 365 \;=\; 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the availability definition.**
Availability A = 99.99 % = 0.9999

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\text{Uptime}}{T_{\text{year}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for uptime.**

$$
\text{Uptime} \;=\; A \times T_{\text{year}} \;=\; 0.9999 \times 525{,}600
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerically.**

$$
\text{Uptime} \;=\; 525{,}474.24 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute downtime.**

$$
D \;=\; T_{\text{year}} - \text{Uptime} \;=\; 525{,}600 - 525{,}474.24
$$

$$
D \;=\; 125.76 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (KTU valuation tip).**
The result ≈ **2.1 hours/year**. This is the *aggregate* downtime across the *entire service* — not per server. Per-server downtime is much higher, but redundancy masks it.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: PUE and Energy Cost

**Problem**: A WSC has an IT load of **10 MW**. Cooling is **1.2 MW** and power distribution loss is **0.3 MW**. Calculate PUE and the annual electricity cost at **$0.10 / kWh**.

**Step 1 — Total facility power.**

$$
P_{\text{total}} \;=\; P_{\text{IT}} \;+\; P_{\text{cooling}} \;+\; P_{\text{loss}}
$$

$$
P_{\text{total}} \;=\; 10 \;+\; 1.2 \;+\; 0.3 \;=\; 11.5 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PUE.**

$$
\text{PUE} \;=\; \frac{11.5}{10} \;=\; 1.15
$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy in kWh.**

$$
E_{\text{year}} \;=\; 11.5 \times 10^{3} \times 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
E_{\text{year}} \;=\; 100{,}740{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual cost.**

$$
C_{\text{year}} \;=\; 100{,}740{,}000 \times 0.10 \;=\; \$10{,}074{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — KTU insight.**
Lowering PUE from 1.15 to 1.10 saves **0.5 MW continuously**, i.e., $438,000/year per facility.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Amdahl's Law for WSC Parallelism

**Problem**: A WSC service splits work: 95 % parallelizable, 5 % inherently serial (e.g., result aggregation, locking). With **n = 1000** servers, what is the maximum speedup?

**Step 1 — Recall Amdahl's Law.**

$$
S(n) \;=\; \frac{1}{(1-p) \;+\; \dfrac{p}{n}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute p = 0.95, n = 1000.**

$$
S(1000) \;=\; \frac{1}{0.05 \;+\; \dfrac{0.95}{1000}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate denominator.**

$$
0.05 + 0.00095 \;=\; 0.05095
$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$
S(1000) \;=\; \frac{1}{0.05095} \;\approx\; 19.63
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (HIGH MARKS in KTU).**
Even with 1000 servers, the serial 5 % caps speedup at **~20×**. This is why WSC architects obsess about **eliminating serialization** in queue managers, metadata servers, and consistent-hashing rings.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: WSC vs. Conventional Data Center

| Attribute | WSC | Conventional Data Center |
|---|---|---|
| Workload type | Internet-scale, homogeneous | Enterprise, heterogeneous |
| Hardware uniformity | High (often custom SKU) | Low (mix of vendors) |
| Administrator model | Single domain (one firm) | Multi-tenant (customers) |
| Failure handling | Software tolerates hardware failure | Hardware redundancy (RAID, ECC) |
| Tenancy | Internal (one company) | Multi-customer |
| Goal | Serve queries cheaply at scale | Host business applications |
| Cost-of-ownership metric | $ per query | $ per VM / per rack |
| Networking priority | Bisection BW, low latency | Resilient, segmented |
| Power focus | Minimize PUE | Minimize kVA per rack |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WSC Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    ClientA["End User Clients"]
    DNS["DNS and Global Load Balancer"]
    FrontEnd["Front End Web Server Tier"]

    RackB["Server Rack 2"]
    RackC["Server Rack N"]

    Power["Utility Power Feed"]
    Cooling["Cooling Plant and Chillers"]

    ClientA --> DNS
    DNS --> FrontEnd
    FrontEnd --> AppTier
    AppTier --> SearchTier
    AppTier --> BigData
    FrontEnd --> Fabric1
    AppTier --> Fabric1
    SearchTier --> Fabric1

    Fabric1 --> Fabric2

    Battery --> RackB

```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of WSC Goals (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid

    GoalRoot["WSC Architectural Goals"]

    GoalRoot --> G1
```
