
### 4.3 WSC Workload Request Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant LB as Load Balancer
    participant FE as Front End
    participant APP as App Tier
    participant CACHE as Memory Cache
    participant DB as Storage Tier

    U->>LB: HTTPS Request
    LB->>FE: Route to least loaded
    FE->>APP: Invoke microservice
    APP->>CACHE: Lookup key
    alt Cache hit
        CACHE-->>APP: Return result
    else Cache miss
        APP->>DB: Sharded query
        DB-->>APP: Results
        APP->>CACHE: Populate
    end
    APP-->>FE: Composed response
    FE-->>U: HTML and JSON
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Warehouse-Scale Computer. How is it different from a traditional data center?*

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
A Warehouse-Scale Computer (WSC) is a collection of thousands of servers homogeneously deployed, homogeneously operated, and exposed as a single, fault-tolerant Internet service. Unlike a traditional data center, which is *heterogeneous* and *multi-tenant* (hosting different customers' applications), a WSC is *single-tenant* and *single-domain*. In a WSC, hardware failures are normal and are masked by software-level replication; in a data center, hardware is highly reliable and rarely fails. WSCs optimize for *cost per query* and *P99 tail latency*, while data centers optimize for *utilization* and *VM consolidation ratio*. The networking, power, and cooling design of a WSC treats the entire building as one machine.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the five most important requirements of a Warehouse-Scale Computer.*

**Model Answer:**
(i) Scalable, fault-tolerant Internet service
(ii) Cost-efficiency at scale (low TCO per query)
(iii) Predictable interactive latency (sub-second P99.9)
(iv) High reliability and availability (≥ 99.99 %)
(v) Energy efficiency and high PUE performance (PUE ≤ 1.2).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Performance & Cost
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** *A WSC consists of 5,000 servers. Each server consumes 200 W idle and 400 W peak. Average utilization is 30 %. Compute the average power drawn by the IT load, and the annual electricity cost at $0.08/kWh. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Define Amdahl's Law. If 4 % of a WSC service is sequential and 96 % is parallel, what is the maximum speedup achievable with 500 servers? Comment on the practical implications. (7 marks)*

**Solution (a):**

Average power per server:

$$
P_{\text{avg}} \;=\; P_{\text{idle}} + (P_{\text{peak}} - P_{\text{idle}}) \times U
$$

$$
P_{\text{avg}} \;=\; 200 + (400 - 200) \times 0.30 \;=\; 200 + 60 \;=\; 260 \text{ W}
$$

Total IT load:

$$
P_{\text{IT}} \;=\; 5000 \times 260 \;=\; 1{,}300{,}000 \text{ W} \;=\; 1.3 \text{ MW}
$$

Annual energy:

$$
E_{\text{year}} \;=\; 1.3 \times 10^{3} \times 8760 \;=\; 11{,}388{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

Annual cost:

$$
C_{\text{year}} \;=\; 11{,}388{,}000 \times 0.08 \;=\; \$911{,}040
$$

**Solution (b):**

Amdahl's Law:

$$
S(n) \;=\; \frac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}
$$

With p = 0.96, n = 500:

$$
S(500) \;=\; \frac{1}{0.04 + \dfrac{0.96}{500}} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.04 + 0.00192} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.04192}
$$

$$
S(500) \;\approx\; 23.85
$$

**Practical implication:** Despite 500 servers, speedup is bounded near 25×. WSC architects must aggressively reduce serial fractions to scale further.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Availability & PUE
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** *A WSC service must provide 99.999 % availability. Compute the allowable annual downtime in minutes. If MTBF of a single server is 30,000 hours, what is the maximum allowable MTTR to achieve this availability at the *server* level? (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A WSC has an IT load of 20 MW, with PUE = 1.2. Cooling accounts for 30 % of the overhead. If PUE is reduced to 1.10 by improving cooling, calculate (i) the new total facility power and (ii) the annual energy savings in kWh and dollars at $0.10/kWh. (7 marks)*

**Solution (a):**

Annual minutes:

$$
T_{\text{year}} \;=\; 525{,}600 \text{ min}
$$

Allowable downtime:

$$
D \;=\; 525{,}600 \times (1 - 0.99999) \;=\; 525{,}600 \times 10^{-5} \;=\; 5.256 \text{ min/year}
$$

Server-level availability:

$$
A_{\text{server}} \;=\; \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

Solve for MTTR:

$$
0.99999 \;=\; \frac{30{,}000}{30{,}000 + \text{MTTR}}
$$

$$
30{,}000 + \text{MTTR} \;=\; \frac{30{,}000}{0.99999} \;=\; 30{,}000.3
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} \;\approx\; 0.3 \text{ hours} \;=\; 18 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**
> Students often confuse **service-level** availability with **server-level** availability. A 99.999 % *service* uptime requires *server-level* MTBF/MTTR ratio of about 30,000 h / 0.3 h, *plus* redundancy. Writing only the formula without substituting numeric MTBF costs 2–3 marks.

**Solution (b):**

Current total power:

$$
P_{\text{total, old}} \;=\; 20 \times 1.2 \;=\; 24 \text{ MW}
$$

Cooling overhead:

$$
P_{\text{cool, old}} \;=\; 24 - 20 \;=\; 4 \text{ MW}
$$

New total power:

$$
P_{\text{total, new}} \;=\; 20 \times 1.10 \;=\; 22 \text{ MW}
$$

Power saved:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; 24 - 22 \;=\; 2 \text{ MW}
$$

Annual energy saved:

$$
\Delta E \;=\; 2 \times 10^{3} \times 8760 \;=\; 17{,}520{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

Dollar savings:

$$
\Delta \$ \;=\; 17{,}520{,}000 \times 0.10 \;=\; \$1{,}752{,}000
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS (Read this last — it is what the examiner tests)**

- A **WSC** is one machine, not a building full of machines.
- Key differentiator: **single domain, single workload class, software-masked failure**.
- The **five pillars** of WSC goals: cost, latency, throughput, reliability, energy.
- **PUE** < 1.2 is the gold standard; PUE = total power / IT power.
- **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR); service-level needs redundancy, not just hardware reliability.
- **Amdahl's Law** dictates that the *serial fraction* caps WSC speedup — usually 4–5 % serial kills scaling beyond ~25×.
- **Tail latency (P99.9)** matters more than median latency in WSC.
- **Clos/fat-tree** topology is the default network architecture for high bisection bandwidth.
- The **Cost-per-query** metric governs software decisions; no WSC service is "free".
- **Energy proportionality** is the gap between idle and peak power; WSCs prefer servers with low idle-power ratios.
- WSCs are designed to *fail continuously*; software must embrace, not fight, hardware faults.
- **Workload homogeneity** allows WSC hardware to be purpose-built (custom SKUs, e.g., Google TPU hosts).
- **Cooling overhead** typically accounts for 10–20 % of total power; hot-aisle/cold-aisle is standard.
- Always quote numerical answers with **units** in KTU valuation.
- For 14-mark questions, every sub-part must have a clearly labeled formula, substitution, and result.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Frameworks for Batch Processing - MapReduce and Hadoop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programming Frameworks for Batch Processing - MapReduce and Hadoop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **MapReduce** is a parallel and distributed programming model proposed by **Google (Dean & Ghemawat, 2004)** designed to process and generate massive volumes of data in parallel across large clusters of commodity hardware. It abstracts the complexity of distributed computing by exposing only two user-defined functions — `map()` and `reduce()` — while the framework handles partitioning, scheduling, fault tolerance, and inter-process communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Hadoop** is an open-source software framework that implements the MapReduce programming model along with the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** and **Yet Another Resource Negotiator (YARN)**. It is the de-facto industrial standard for **batch processing of Big Data** at petabyte scale, providing horizontal scalability, fault tolerance, and data-locality aware scheduling.

The two core components that KTU examiners test are:
1. **HDFS** — distributed, redundant block-storage layer.
2. **MapReduce + YARN** — distributed computation and resource-management layer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine your university has **2 crore answer scripts** to evaluate in 3 days. One professor reading alone would fail. So you:

1. **MAP phase (Distribute):** Break the pile into 1,000 equal bundles. Hand each bundle to a different evaluator in different rooms. Each evaluator grades only their own bundle — this is the **Mapper**.
2. **SHUFFLE & SORT (Group):** Collect all graded scripts. Group them question-wise: all answers to Q1 together, Q2 together, etc. A clerk does this central sorting — this is the **Shuffle/Sort** phase.
3. **REDUCE phase (Aggregate):** Hand each question's pile to a senior professor who computes the final statistics (mean, pass %, topper) for that question. Each senior professor handles one question — this is the **Reducer**.

**Hadoop is the building, the bundles, the clerks, the rooms, the electricity backup, and the rule-book** — MapReduce is just the *method* they use to grade.

### 1.3 Why Batch Processing Frameworks Exist

| Modern Data Challenge | Why Naive Solutions Fail |
|---|---|
| Data size in **Petabytes (PB)** | Single machine cannot load RAM/disk |
| Hardware failures are **normal, not exceptional** | RAID/Single-server RAID is insufficient |
| Need **horizontal scaling** (more nodes) | Vertical scaling hits physical/economic limits |
| Cheap **commodity hardware** | Cannot rely on enterprise-grade reliability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default HDFS Block Size = 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+; 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x). This is **64× larger** than a traditional disk block (typically 4 KB), which **minimizes NameNode metadata** and optimizes sequential large-file reads — a critical KTU exam point.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MapReduce Execution Model — Phases

The MapReduce job executes in a strictly ordered pipeline:

| Phase | Function | Responsibility | Parallelism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **InputFormat / Split** | Framework | Splits input file into logical `InputSplit` (1 per Mapper) | Determined by `min.num.splits` |
| **MAP** | User-defined | Processes `(key1, value1)` → list of `(key2, value2)` | One task per split, runs on data-local node |
| **Combine** *(optional)* | User-defined | Local pre-aggregation per Mapper to reduce shuffle volume | In-Mapper, runs in same JVM |
| **Partition** | Framework | Decides which Reducer receives which key (default: `hash(key) mod R`) | Framework-controlled |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | Framework | Transfers intermediate data across the network and sorts by key | Network-bound |
| **REDUCE** | User-defined | Aggregates list of values per key → final `(key3, value3)` | One task per partition |
| **OutputFormat / Write** | Framework | Writes final output back to HDFS | — |

### 2.2 Hadoop Architecture — HDFS + YARN + MapReduce

#### 2.2.1 HDFS (Storage Layer) — Master/Slave

* **NameNode (Master):** Stores the **fsimage** (file system namespace) and **edit log**. Manages metadata: file → block mapping, block → DataNode mapping. **In-memory only**.
* **Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node):** Periodically merges fsimage + edit log into a new fsimage. **NOT a hot standby** — that role is the **Standby NameNode** in HA mode.
* **DataNode (Slave):** Stores actual **data blocks** (default 128 MB). Sends **heartbeat** every 3 sec and **Block report** every 6 hours to NameNode.
* **Replication Factor (default = 3):** One copy on local node, one on a different rack, one on the same different rack. This gives **rack-aware fault tolerance**.

#### 2.2.2 YARN (Resource Management Layer) — Introduced in Hadoop 2.x

* **ResourceManager (RM):** Global scheduler. Has two sub-components:
  * **Scheduler:** Allocates resources via Capacity/Fair Scheduler.
  * **ApplicationsManager (AsM):** Accepts job submissions and launches the `ApplicationMaster`.
* **NodeManager (NM):** Per-machine agent. Manages containers, monitors CPU/RAM/disk/network, reports back to RM.
* **ApplicationMaster (AM):** **One per job.** Negotiates resources with RM and works with NMs to execute Map/Reduce tasks.
* **Container:** A unit of allocated resources (RAM + CPU cores) on a NodeManager.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **board-exam favourites** for PECST528. Master them.

#### 2.3.1 Amdahl's Law applied to MapReduce Parallelism

$$S(N) \;=\; \frac{1}{(1 - P) \;+\; \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

where:
* $S(N)$ = **Speedup** with $N$ mappers/reducers
* $P$ = **Parallel fraction** of the job (0 to 1)
* $(1 - P)$ = **Serial fraction** (HDFS read, Shuffle setup, Reduce coordination)

#### 2.3.2 Maximum Theoretical Speedup Limit

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - P}$$

This proves why **the serial fraction bottlenecks Hadoop performance** — even with infinite nodes, the overhead of splitting, shuffling, and reduce-coordination cannot be removed.

#### 2.3.3 Total Job Completion Time

$$T_{job} \;=\; T_{split} \;+\; T_{map} \;+\; T_{shuffle} \;+\; T_{sort} \;+\; T_{reduce} \;+\; T_{write}$$

#### 2.3.4 Data Locality Cost Model

$$T_{local} \;\ll\; T_{rack} \;\ll\; T_{off\text{-}rack}$$

A task reading its input from:
* **Local disk** (same node) — $T_{local} \approx 1\times$
* **Same rack, different node** — $T_{rack} \approx 2\times$ (1 switch hop)
* **Different rack** — $T_{off\text{-}rack} \approx 4\times$ (2 switch hops)

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Locality Optimization** is why Hadoop schedules Mappers on the DataNode holding the input block. This is a frequent 7-mark KTU question.

#### 2.3.5 HDFS Storage Overhead

$$S_{total} \;=\; B \times R \times \vert Files \vert$$

where $B$ = block size (128 MB), $R$ = replication factor (default 3), $\vert Files \vert$ = number of files.

#### 2.3.6 Number of Mappers per Job

$$M \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{\text{Input Size (bytes)}}{H_{splitsize}} \right\rceil$$

Typically $H_{splitsize}$ ≈ HDFS block size (128 MB).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Yahoo** processes 100+ PB daily on Hadoop clusters.
* **Facebook** uses Hadoop for warehouse ETL and ML feature engineering.
* **Netflix** runs recommendation batch jobs over viewing logs.
* **Banks** use Hadoop for fraud-detection batch scoring overnight.

### 2.5 Fault Tolerance Mechanisms

| Failure Type | Detection | Recovery Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Task failure** | AM heartbeat timeout (10 min) | Re-schedule on different node; 4 retries default |
| **Node failure** | NM heartbeat timeout | Re-schedule all tasks from that NM |
| **Data block corruption** | Checksum mismatch during read | Use one of the replicated copies |
| **Slow task (straggler)** | Progress-score lag | **Speculative execution** — launch duplicate on faster node |
| **NameNode failure** | HA (ZooKeeper) | Standby NameNode promotes to active |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speculative Execution** is a hallmark KTU question. It does NOT fix the slow task — it launches a **duplicate** on another node and uses whichever finishes first. Cost = wasted compute, Benefit = reduced job tail latency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Amdahl's Speedup Bound for a MapReduce Job

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A Hadoop job spends **20%** of its time in serial phases (HDFS block allocation, NameNode RPC, reduce-coordination setup) and the remaining **80%** in the parallel mappers. Calculate the **speedup with 8 mappers** and the **maximum theoretical speedup**.

**Given Data:**
* Serial fraction $(1 - P) = 0.20$
* Parallel fraction $P = 0.80$
* Number of parallel workers $N = 8$

**Step 1:** Write down Amdahl's Law as the master equation:

$$S(N) \;=\; \frac{1}{(1 - P) \;+\; \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values into the serial portion:

$$(1 - P) \;=\; 0.20$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical value into the parallel portion numerator and denominator:

$$\frac{P}{N} \;=\; \frac{0.80}{8}$$

**Step 4:** Perform the division:

$$\frac{0.80}{8} \;=\; 0.10$$

**Step 5:** Add the serial and parallel portions (denominator of the master equation):

$$(1 - P) \;+\; \frac{P}{N} \;=\; 0.20 \;+\; 0.10 \;=\; 0.30$$

**Step 6:** Compute the final speedup by inversion:

$$S(8) \;=\; \frac{1}{0.30} \;=\; 3.\overline{3}$$

**Step 7:** Express the result as a fraction for clean board-work:

$$S(8) \;=\; \frac{1}{3/10} \;=\; \frac{10}{3} \;\approx\; 3.33\times$$

**Step 8:** Compute the maximum theoretical speedup limit as $N \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) \;=\; \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{N}}$$

**Step 9:** Take the limit — as $N \to \infty$, $\dfrac{0.80}{N} \to 0$:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) \;=\; \frac{1}{0.20 + 0} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.20} \;=\; 5\times$$

**Step 10:** Interpret the result:

> Even with **infinite mappers**, this Hadoop job can never exceed **5× speedup** because the **20% serial overhead** (HDFS RPC, NameNode metadata ops, reduce setup) is an unbreakable bottleneck. This is the **fundamental ceiling** of any MapReduce workload that has irreducible serial components.

$$\boxed{\;S(8) \;\approx\; 3.33\times \quad,\quad S_{max} \;=\; 5\times\;}$$

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Number of Mappers & HDFS Storage

**Problem Statement:** You have a **1.2 TB** input log file ingested into HDFS with **128 MB** block size and **replication factor 3**. Find: (i) number of mappers, (ii) total HDFS storage consumed, (iii) total number of DataNode blocks stored.

**Given Data:**
* Input size $D = 1.2$ TB $= 1.2 \times 1024 \times 1024$ MB $= 1{,}258{,}290.8$ MB
* Block size $B = 128$ MB
* Replication factor $R = 3$

**Step 1:** Compute number of physical HDFS blocks:

$$N_{blocks} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{D}{B} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{1{,}258{,}290.8}{128} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2:** Divide:

$$\frac{1{,}258{,}290.8}{128} \;\approx\; 9{,}830.4$$

**Step 3:** Apply the ceiling function — the last block is partial:

$$N_{blocks} \;=\; 9{,}831 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 4:** Number of mappers (one logical split per block by default):

$$M \;=\; N_{blocks} \;=\; 9{,}831 \text{ mappers}$$

**Step 5:** Total HDFS storage consumed (logical + replicas):

$$S_{total} \;=\; D \times R \;=\; 1.2 \text{ TB} \times 3 \;=\; 3.6 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 6:** Verify with block-level granularity:

$$S_{total} \;=\; N_{blocks} \times B \times R \;=\; 9{,}831 \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3 \;\approx\; 3.6 \text{ TB} \;\checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\;M = 9{,}831 \text{ mappers} \quad,\quad S_{total} \approx 3.6 \text{ TB}\;}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hadoop vs. Classical Distributed Systems

| Feature | Hadoop MapReduce | MPI / HPC Cluster | Traditional RDBMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scale** | Thousands of nodes | Hundreds of nodes | Single/few nodes |
| **Data Model** | Unstructured / Semi-structured | Structured arrays | Structured tables |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Native (re-execution) | Application-managed | Limited |
| **Hardware** | Commodity | High-end HPC | Enterprise servers |
| **Latency** | High (batch, minutes–hours) | Low (real-time HPC) | Low (OLTP) |
| **Cost / TB** | Very low | Very high | Medium |
| **Best For** | Batch analytics, ETL, log mining | Scientific simulation | Transactional queries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Hadoop is **NOT a database replacement**. It is a **batch analytics engine** optimized for **throughput over latency**.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: The Classic WordCount via MapReduce

**Input:** Text file containing sentences.

**Step 1 (MAP):** For each line, emit `(word, 1)` for every word.

$$\text{Mapper} : (line) \;\to\; \big[(w_1, 1), (w_2, 1), \dots\big]$$

**Step 2 (SHUFFLE & SORT):** Framework groups by key and sorts.

$$\big[(``\text{hello}", [1,1,1]), \;(``\text{world}", [1,1]), \dots\big]$$

**Step 3 (REDUCE):** For each key, sum the list.

$$\text{Reducer} : (w, [1,1,\dots,1]) \;\to\; (w, \text{count})$$

**Example Output:**

$$\big[(``\text{hello}", 3), \;(``\text{world}", 2), \;(``\text{hadoop}", 1)\big]$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hadoop Master-Slave Architecture (HDFS + YARN)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client Application]

    subgraph CLUSTER["Hadoop Cluster"]
        subgraph MASTER["Master Node"]
            NN["NameNode\n(Metadata + fsimage)"]
            SNN["Secondary NameNode\n(Checkpoint)"]
            RM["ResourceManager\n(Scheduler + AppsMgr)"]
        end

        subgraph SLAVE1["Slave Node 1"]
            DN1["DataNode 1\n(Blocks + Heartbeat)"]
            NM1["NodeManager 1\n(Containers)"]
            AM1["AppMaster\n(for Job 1)"]
        end

        subgraph SLAVE2["Slave Node 2"]
            DN2["DataNode 2\n(Blocks + Heartbeat)"]
            NM2["NodeManager 2\n(Containers)"]
        end

        subgraph SLAVE3["Slave Node 3"]
            DN3["DataNode 3\n(Blocks + Heartbeat)"]
            NM3["NodeManager 3\n(Containers)"]
        end
    end

    Client -->|submit job| RM
    Client -->|read/write| NN
    RM --> AM1
    AM1 --> NM1
    AM1 --> NM2
    AM1 --> NM3
    NN --> DN1
    NN --> DN2
    NN --> DN3
    SNN -.->|merge edit log| NN
    DN1 -->|heartbeat 3s| NN
    DN2 -->|heartbeat 3s| NN
    DN3 -->|heartbeat 3s| NN
    DN1 -->|heartbeat 3s| RM
    DN2 -->|heartbeat 3s| RM
    DN3 -->|heartbeat 3s| RM
```

### 4.2 MapReduce Data Flow — Map → Shuffle → Sort → Reduce

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[HDFS Block 1] --> M1[Mapper 1]
    A2[HDFS Block 2] --> M2[Mapper 2]
    A3[HDFS Block 3] --> M3[Mapper 3]
    A4[HDFS Block 4] --> M4[Mapper 4]

    M1 -->|k,v pairs| P[Partitioner\nhash key mod R]
    M2 --> P
    M3 --> P
    M4 --> P

    P -->|partition 0| S1[Shuffle and Sort\nGroup by key]
    P -->|partition 1| S2[Shuffle and Sort\nGroup by key]
    P -->|partition 2| S3[Shuffle and Sort\nGroup by key]

    S1 --> R1[Reducer 1]
    S2 --> R2[Reducer 2]
    S3 --> R3[Reducer 3]

    R1 --> O1[HDFS Output 1]
    R2 --> O2[HDFS Output 2]
    R3 --> O3[HDFS Output 3]
```

### 4.3 HDFS Write Pipeline (Replication = 3, Rack-Aware)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client[Client] -->|write request| NN[NameNode]
    NN -->|DataNode list: DN1, DN4, DN7| Client
    Client -->|packet 1| DN1[Rack A: DN1]
    DN1 -->|forward| DN4[Rack B: DN4]
    DN4 -->|forward| DN7[Rack A: DN7]
    DN1 -->|ack| Client
    DN4 -->|ack| Client
    DN7 -->|ack| Client
    Client -->|next packet| DN1
```

### 4.4 YARN Resource Negotiation Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant RM as ResourceManager
    participant AM as ApplicationMaster
    participant NM1 as NodeManager 1
    participant NM2 as NodeManager 2

    C->>RM: submitApplication
    RM->>AM: launch ApplicationMaster
    AM->>RM: register + request containers
    RM-->>AM: allocate Container on NM1
    AM->>NM1: launchContainer (Mapper task)
    AM->>RM: request more containers
    RM-->>AM: allocate Container on NM2
    AM->>NM2: launchContainer (Reducer task)
    NM1-->>AM: task progress
    NM2-->>AM: task progress
    AM-->>RM: unregister on completion
    RM-->>C: job completion notification
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MapReduce on YARN

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1["Job Submission Phase"]
        J1[Client submits JAR] --> J2[ResourceManager\naccepts job]
        J2 --> J3[ApplicationMaster\nbooted in Container 0]
    end

    subgraph T2["Map Phase"]
        J3 --> K1[Map Task 1 on NM-A]
        J3 --> K2[Map Task 2 on NM-B]
        J3 --> K3[Map Task 3 on NM-C]
        K1 --> K4[Local FS\nintermediate]
        K2 --> K4
        K3 --> K4
    end

    subgraph T3["Shuffle and Sort Phase"]
        K4 --> L1[Network Transfer\nto Reducers]
        L1 --> L2[Merge Sort on\nReducer node]
    end

    subgraph T4["Reduce Phase"]
        L2 --> M1[Reduce Task 1 on NM-A]
        L2 --> M2[Reduce Task 2 on NM-B]
    end

    subgraph T5["Output Phase"]
        M1 --> N1[HDFS Output Block]
        M2 --> N2[HDFS Output Block]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Explain the role of the **NameNode** and **DataNode** in HDFS. Why is HDFS designed with a large block size (128 MB)?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **NameNode (Master):** Acts as the central metadata server. It stores the entire file system namespace — file-to-block mapping, block locations, replication factor, and access permissions — in RAM as `fsimage` + `edit log`. It does NOT store actual data.
* **DataNode (Slave):** Each slave machine runs a DataNode daemon. It stores the actual **data blocks** (default 128 MB each) on local disk, serves read/write requests from clients, and periodically sends **heartbeat (every 3 s)** and **block reports (every 6 h)** to the NameNode.
* **Why 128 MB block size:** (i) Reduces NameNode memory footprint (one entry per block, not per file-record). (ii) Reduces **seek-time** vs. **transfer-time** ratio — the file is read mostly sequentially. (iii) Allows MapReduce to assign **one mapper per block**, simplifying data-locality scheduling. (1 mark for any two reasons.)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Differentiate between **Mapper** and **Reducer** in the MapReduce programming model. What is the function of the optional **Combiner**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mapper | Reducer |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | `(k1, v1)` from InputSplit | `(k2, list<v2>)` from Shuffle |
| **Output** | Intermediate `(k2, v2)` pairs | Final aggregated `(k3, v3)` |
| **Number** | 1 per block (≈1000s) | User-set (typically tens) |
| **Runs at** | Data-local node | Anywhere in cluster |

* **Combiner:** A mini-reducer that runs **locally on the Mapper's output** in the same JVM, *before* network shuffle. It performs local pre-aggregation (e.g., summing partial counts) to **reduce the volume of data sent over the network**, improving performance. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Hadoop architecture** covering **HDFS** and **YARN** components. Discuss the responsibilities of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **MapReduce execution flow** with phases. State Amdahl's Law and apply it to a Hadoop job where the serial fraction is **15%** and parallel fraction is **85%** for $N = 10$ mappers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Hadoop Architecture (7 marks):**

* **HDFS Layer:**
  * **NameNode** — master, in-memory metadata store (fsimage + edit log). Does not store actual data. Manages block-to-DataNode mapping.
  * **Secondary NameNode / Standby NameNode** — periodic checkpointing in non-HA mode, hot-failover in HA mode.
  * **DataNodes** — slaves storing 128 MB blocks; send heartbeat (3 s) and block report (6 h) to NameNode.
  * **Default replication factor = 3** with rack-aware policy (1 local, 1 same-rack-different-node, 1 different-rack).

* **YARN Layer (Hadoop 2.x+):**
  * **ResourceManager (RM)** — global arbiter; runs the Scheduler (Capacity/Fair) and the ApplicationsManager.
  * **NodeManager (NM)** — per-node agent; manages **Containers** (CPU + RAM units), monitors resource use.
  * **ApplicationMaster (AM)** — one per job; negotiates resources with RM and assigns Map/Reduce tasks to NMs.
  * **Container** — a logical resource bundle (e.g., 2 GB RAM + 1 vCPU).

* **MapReduce Layer:** Houses the actual `map()` and `reduce()` task execution within containers.

*Marks distribution: 1 for diagram, 2 HDFS, 2 YARN, 1 replication, 1 fault tolerance.*

**(b) MapReduce Flow + Amdahl's Law (7 marks):**

* **MapReduce Phases (4 marks):**
  1. **Input Split** — file divided into logical splits (≈ HDFS block).
  2. **Map** — user-defined `map(k1, v1) → list(k2, v2)` runs in parallel on data-local nodes.
  3. **Combine (optional)** — local pre-aggregation on Mapper output.
  4. **Partition** — assigns keys to reducers via `hash(key) mod R`.
  5. **Shuffle & Sort** — network transfer + group-by-key on reducer side.
  6. **Reduce** — user-defined aggregation `reduce(k2, list<v2>) → list(k3, v3)`.
  7. **Output** — written back to HDFS.

* **Amdahl's Law (3 marks):**

$$S(N) \;=\; \frac{1}{(1 - P) \;+\; \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

**Given:** $(1 - P) = 0.15$, $P = 0.85$, $N = 10$.

$$S(10) \;=\; \frac{1}{0.15 \;+\; \dfrac{0.85}{10}} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.085} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.235} \;\approx\; 4.26\times$$

**Maximum theoretical speedup** as $N \to \infty$:

$$S_{max} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - P} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.15} \;\approx\; 6.67\times$$

> **Interpretation:** Even with infinite parallel resources, this job cannot exceed **6.67× speedup** due to the 15% irreducible serial overhead (HDFS RPC, NameNode metadata, reduce coordination).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **HDFS read and write operations** with diagrams. Discuss **rack-aware replica placement policy**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Hadoop job processes **2 TB** of input data with **128 MB** HDFS block size and **replication factor 3**. Calculate: (i) the number of mappers, (ii) total HDFS storage consumed, (iii) the number of DataNode blocks stored, and (iv) discuss the impact of changing block size to **256 MB**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) HDFS Read/Write + Rack-Aware Placement (7 marks):**

* **Write Operation (3 marks):**
  1. Client calls `create()` on `DistributedFileSystem`, which RPCs to NameNode.
  2. NameNode performs a **file system namespace check** (path validity, permissions) and allocates a new file entry in edit log. Returns a list of DataNode targets for the first block (pipeline of 3).
  3. Client writes packet (64 KB) to first DataNode, which forwards to second, which forwards to third — **pipeline order**.
  4. Each DataNode sends ack back up the pipeline. Once the block is full, the client requests the next block target list from NameNode.

* **Read Operation (2 marks):**
  1. Client calls `open()`, which RPCs to NameNode for block locations.
  2. NameNode returns a sorted list of DataNode IPs (closest first based on network topology).
  3. Client connects to the nearest DataNode and streams the block. Subsequent blocks may be served by different DataNodes.

* **Rack-Aware Replica Placement (2 marks):**
  * **Replica 1** — on the **local node** (or a random node in the cluster if the writer is outside HDFS).
  * **Replica 2** — on a **different rack, different node** (rack-of-racks failure protection).
  * **Replica 3** — on the **same rack as replica 2, but a different node** (inter-rack traffic is minimised).
  * Trade-off: rack failure tolerance + reduced inter-rack bandwidth.

**(b) Hadoop Block Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 2$ TB, $B = 128$ MB, $R = 3$.

**(i) Number of mappers:**

Convert: $2$ TB $= 2 \times 1024 \times 1024$ MB $= 2{,}097{,}152$ MB.

$$M \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{128} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil 16{,}384 \right\rceil \;=\; 16{,}384 \text{ mappers}$$

**(ii) Total HDFS storage:**

$$S_{total} \;=\; D \times R \;=\; 2 \text{ TB} \times 3 \;=\; 6 \text{ TB}$$

**(iii) Number of DataNode blocks (logical + replicas):**

$$N_{dn} \;=\; M \times R \;=\; 16{,}384 \times 3 \;=\; 49{,}152 \text{ block replicas}$$

**(iv) Impact of changing block size to 256 MB (2 marks):**

$$M_{new} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{256} \right\rceil \;=\; 8{,}192 \text{ mappers}$$

* Number of mappers **halves** → **fewer JVMs, less scheduling overhead, less NameNode metadata**.
* **Map task becomes longer-running**, better sequential throughput.
* **Negative:** Poor data-locality for small files; less parallelism for skewed keys.
* **Best practice:** Use larger blocks (256–512 MB) for **large sequential reads** (e.g., log analytics); use default 128 MB for general workloads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deductions:**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing NameNode with DataNode** — NameNode = metadata; DataNode = actual data. Many students swap these. (–1 to 2 marks.)
> 2. ❌ **Writing Secondary NameNode is a "backup"** — it is **NOT a hot standby**. The hot standby is the **Standby NameNode** (HA). (–1 mark)
> 3. ❌ **Forgetting rack-awareness in replication** — default is **rack-aware**, not random. (–1 mark)
> 4. ❌ **Amdahl's Law misapplication** — students often divide $P$ by $N$ incorrectly. Always do $\frac{P}{N}$ first, then add $(1-P)$, then invert. (–1 to 2 marks)
> 5. ❌ **Forgetting the `max S = 1/(1-P)` limit** — examiners specifically test this ceiling.
> 6. ❌ **Stating MapReduce is a database** — it is a **batch processing framework**, not a DB. (–1 mark)
> 7. ❌ **Skipping the Combiner** — mentioning it (when relevant) earns easy marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

* ✅ **MapReduce** = `map()` + `reduce()` user-functions + framework-managed partition, shuffle, sort, fault-tolerance.
* ✅ **Hadoop = HDFS + YARN + MapReduce.** HDFS stores data, YARN manages resources, MapReduce runs jobs.
* ✅ **Default HDFS block = 128 MB**, **replication = 3**, **heartbeat = 3 s**, **block report = 6 h**.
* ✅ **NameNode** holds metadata in RAM (single point of failure in non-HA; HA via ZooKeeper).
* ✅ **DataNodes** are the actual storage workhorses, with rack-aware replica placement.
* ✅ **YARN** = ResourceManager (global) + NodeManager (per node) + ApplicationMaster (per job) + Container (resource unit).
* ✅ **Map phases:** Split → Map → (Combine) → Partition → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce → Output.
* ✅ **Combiner** = local reducer to minimise shuffle volume.
* ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \frac{1}{(1-P) + P/N}$; ceiling = $\frac{1}{1-P}$.
* ✅ **Speedup limit = serial fraction bottleneck** — the core theoretical insight.
* ✅ **Data locality:** $T_{local} \ll T_{rack} \ll T_{off\text{-}rack}$. Hadoop schedules mappers data-local.
* ✅ **Speculative execution** = duplicate slow task on faster node, take winner. Costs compute, saves tail latency.
* ✅ **Hadoop is for BATCH, not OLTP** — high throughput, high latency, not a database replacement.
* ✅ **Job time = split + map + shuffle + sort + reduce + write.**
* ✅ **Number of mappers ≈ $\lceil \frac{\text{Input Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \rceil$**.
* ✅ **HDFS storage = data × replication factor.**
* ✅ **Failure recovery** = NameNode restart (HA), task re-execution (4 retries), block re-replication (from replicas).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Architecture of Warehouse-Scale Computers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computer Architecture of Warehouse-Scale Computers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Warehouse-Scale Computer (WSC)** — A collection of thousands of individual servers, networked together and orchestrated by sophisticated software to function as a single, unified computing platform that delivers Internet-scale services (Web search, e-commerce, social networking, video streaming, cloud storage).

A **Warehouse-Scale Computer (WSC)** represents a paradigm shift from traditional data centers. Unlike conventional data centers (which host diverse, unrelated applications on shared hardware), a WSC is purpose-built to run a **single, tightly integrated application or a small set of related services** at massive scale. The term was popularized by **Luiz André Barroso and Urs Hölzle** in their seminal Google paper *"The Datacenter as a Computer"* (2009).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A WSC is **not** a distributed system in the traditional sense. It is treated as **one machine** — an "M-shaped" architecture where individual servers are the "components" and the entire warehouse is the "computer."

### Key Distinguishing Characteristics of a WSC

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Scale** | 50,000 – 100,000+ servers per warehouse |
| **Workload** | Search, MapReduce, batch analytics, cloud VMs (IaaS) |
| **Homogeneity** | Highly homogeneous hardware (often custom-designed) |
| **Software Stack** | Deeply layered: cluster manager, scheduler, storage, application |
| **Networking** | Clos-style multi-tier, high bisection bandwidth |
| **Reliability Goal** | Tolerate individual server failures (1–5% per year per server) |
| **Power Density** | 7–20 kW per rack; PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) near 1.1 |
| **Cost Model** | Amortized per server, ultra-low commodity hardware economics |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Apartment Block vs. The Shopping Mall"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a WSC like a **single massive shopping mall** rather than an **apartment block**.

- **Traditional Data Center (Apartment Block):** Many independent tenants (companies) rent space, run independent applications, and each manages their own infrastructure. The building owner only provides shell, power, and cooling.
- **Warehouse-Scale Computer (Shopping Mall):** A single operator (Google, Amazon, Facebook) controls **everything** — the building design, lighting, HVAC, security cameras, store layouts, and even the products sold. Every decision is optimized holistically for *one* business goal (e.g., deliver search results faster).

In a WSC, just like a mall:
- Individual servers are "shops" that can fail independently without shutting the mall.
- The **network fabric** is the "walkway infrastructure" connecting all shops.
- The **cluster manager** is the "operations head office" directing all shops.
- **Power, cooling, and building** are the "foundations" — engineered to be ultra-efficient and reliable.

---

## 1.3 Why WSCs Matter — The 5W Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five "W"s of WSC Motivation:**

1. **What** — Internet services require massive parallelism. A single Google query may touch 1000+ servers in 0.25 seconds.
2. **Why** — Moore's Law on a single chip cannot meet the demand. We need to scale **out** (horizontally) not just **up** (vertically).
3. **When** — Triggered by Google's need to crawl, index, and serve the entire Web continuously (early 2000s).
4. **Where** — Warehouses (former factories, low-cost land) in places like The Dalles (Oregon), Council Bluffs (Iowa), Hamina (Finland — cooled by Arctic air).
5. **Who** — Hyperscalers: Google, Amazon (AWS), Microsoft (Azure), Meta, Apple, Alibaba, Tencent, Baidu.

---

## 1.4 Terminology Primer (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Cluster** | A small group of loosely coupled computers working together (10–100s of nodes). |
| **Warehouse-Scale Computer** | A cluster at extreme scale, treated as a single architectural entity (1,000s–100,000s). |
| **Bisection Bandwidth** | The minimum bandwidth crossing any cut that divides the network into two equal halves. |
| **PUE** (Power Usage Effectiveness) | Total facility power / IT equipment power. **PUE = 1.0 is the theoretical ideal.** |
| **PUE Standard Metric** | Industry best-in-class is approximately **1.1**; typical legacy data centers: **1.8–2.0**. |
| **TCO** (Total Cost of Ownership) | Sum of server + power + cooling + networking + real estate + admin costs over lifetime. |
| **Rack** | Standard 19-inch server rack holding 40–80 1U/2U servers. |
| **Tor Switch** | Top-of-Rack Ethernet switch aggregating rack-local traffic. |
| **Fabric** | The complete WSC network (Tor, Aggregation, Spine layers). |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Distinct Levels of Hierarchy in a WSC

A WSC architecture is best understood as a **six-level hierarchical system** (per Barroso & Hölzle). KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes this layered view.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Hierarchical Layers (Top-Down):**

1. **Level 1: Application / Service** — e.g., Google Search, GMail, Maps, YouTube.
2. **Level 2: Software Platform** — BigTable, Spanner, MapReduce, GFS, Borg/Kubernetes.
3. **Level 3: Cluster Manager / Scheduler** — Borg (Google), Mesos, Kubernetes, YARN.
4. **Level 4: Individual Server (Compute Node)** — Custom boards with CPU, DRAM, SSD, NIC.
5. **Level 5: Rack & Top-of-Rack Switch** — 40–80 servers per rack, Tor switch.
6. **Level 6: Network Fabric & Power/cooling** — Clos network, substations, chillers.

---

## 2.2 The Cost of a WSC — Engineering Economics

The total cost of ownership (TCO) of a WSC is dominated by **3-year amortization** of hardware and **10-year amortization** of the building shell.

### 2.2.1 Cost Breakdown (Typical Hyperscaler Distribution)

| Cost Component | % of TCO (Typical) | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|
| **Servers (compute + memory + disk)** | ~45% | Highest single cost; amortized 3 years |
| **Networking equipment** | ~15% | Switches, transceivers, optical fiber |
| **Power Infrastructure (UPS, PDU)** | ~10% | Generators, batteries, distribution |
| **Cooling (chillers, CRAH units)** | ~10% | Liquid + air hybrid in modern WSCs |
| **Building / Real Estate** | ~10% | Amortized 10–15 years |
| **Operations & Maintenance** | ~10% | Sysadmins, monitoring, repair |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pearl:** "Why is networking only 15% of cost but a *primary* design concern?" — Because **bisection bandwidth** determines the **performance ceiling** of all parallel workloads. Under-investment causes cascading bottlenecks.

### 2.2.2 Key Cost Formulas

**Server Cost Amortization:**

$$C_{\text{server-amort}} = \frac{C_{\text{server}} + C_{\text{power,3yr}} + C_{\text{cooling,3yr}}}{3 \times 24 \times 365 \times \text{Util}}$$

Where **Util** is the average utilization (typically 70–90% in mature WSCs; 10–30% in private data centers).

**Effective Server Cost (per useful computation):**

$$C_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C_{\text{TCO,3yr}}}{N_{\text{useful-operations}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **3-year amortization** matches server refresh cycles. By year 4, server failure rates climb, energy efficiency degrades, and operational costs exceed residual value. Hence, hyperscalers run "**churn cycles**" replacing 1/3 of fleet annually.

---

## 2.3 Fault Tolerance and Reliability — The MTTF Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Reliability Principle:** "A WSC is designed to operate **despite** individual server failures. Fault tolerance is a **first-class design goal**, not an afterthought."

### 2.3.1 The MTTF of a WSC (Mean Time To Failure)

For **N independent servers** each with **MTTF = M**, the MTTF of the entire WSC assuming *any single server failure* brings down the warehouse is:

$$MTTF_{\text{WSC}} = \frac{M}{N}$$

However, in a **fault-tolerant WSC** with **redundancy** (e.g., replicated services, software-level failover), the system tolerates individual failures. If the workload can run as long as **at least K** of N servers are alive (where K < N), then:

$$MTTF_{\text{WSC}} \approx \frac{M}{N - K + 1} \quad \text{(approximation for $N \gg K$)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Students often assume the WSC MTTF = M. **Wrong.** The MTTF of a system of N independent components is *worse* (lower) than a single component's MTTF, but **redundancy** recovers much of this.

### 2.3.2 Google's Reliability Target (Industry Benchmark)

> [!NOTE]
> Google's published targets:
> - **Per-server annual failure rate:** **4–8%** (highly variable; can spike to 15%+ with new hardware).
> - **Network element failure rate:** **0.1–1% per year** per switch.
> - **Service availability target:** **99.99% ("four nines")** = 52.6 minutes of downtime/year.
> - **Storage durability target:** **99.999999999% (eleven nines)** for Google Drive/Gmail data.

---

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MTTF_{\text{WSC}} = \frac{M}{N}$ | MTTF of N independent servers | hours / years |
| 2 | $MTTF_{\text{redundant}} \approx \frac{M}{N-K+1}$ | MTTF with K-spare redundancy | hours / years |
| 3 | $PUE = \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{IT}}}$ | Power Usage Effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 4 | $SPUE = \frac{1}{1 - \text{Overhead}}$ | Simplified PUE form | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{BW}_{\text{needed}} = \frac{N \times \text{per-server-BW}}{2}$ | Bisection bandwidth lower bound | Gbps |
| 6 | $\text{TCO}_{3\text{yr}} = C_{\text{server}} + C_{\text{power}} + C_{\text{cool}} + C_{\text{net}} + C_{\text{admin}}$ | Total 3-year cost | USD |
| 7 | $A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$ | Availability (0 to 1) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $A_{99.99} = \frac{525600 - 52.6}{525600} = 0.99990$ | Four-nines availability | dimensionless |
| 9 | $A_{\text{system}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Serial availability composition | dimensionless |
| 10 | $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | Parallel availability composition | dimensionless |
| 11 | $C_{\text{perf/\$}} = \frac{\text{QPS}_{\text{cluster}}}{C_{\text{server}} \times N}$ | Performance per dollar | QPS/USD |
| 12 | $P_{\text{rack}} = N_{\text{servers}} \times P_{\text{server}}$ | Rack power budget | Watts |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX Table Rule:** All vertical bars `|` inside formulas are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table parser breakage. Always double-check before submitting KTU exams.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Hyperscale Cloud Providers (AWS, GCP, Azure):** Use TCO models to decide between purchasing new servers vs. upgrading existing fleets.
2. **Telecom Operators (Airtel, Reliance Jio):** Apply PUE analysis to reduce electricity bills; one Indian operator saved **₹50 Cr/year** by improving PUE from 1.8 to 1.3.
3. **AI/ML Training (HPC):** Modern LLM training requires WSC-grade networking (InfiniBand NDR 400 Gbps) to avoid communication bottlenecks during gradient sync.
4. **Edge Computing:** WSC principles (clustering, fault tolerance) are now applied at micro-scale in CDN edge nodes (Cloudflare Workers, AWS Lambda@Edge).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: MTTF of a Warehouse-Scale Computer with Redundancy

### Problem Statement
A WSC consists of **N = 10,000 servers**, each with a **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) of M = 30 years** (i.e., individual server MTTF = 262,800 hours). The application is designed to remain operational as long as **at least K = 9,900 servers** are alive. Derive the MTTF of the WSC.

### Step 1: Define Per-Server Failure Rate

The failure rate (assuming exponential distribution) is the reciprocal of MTTF:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{M}
$$

Substituting $M = 30$ years:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{30 \text{ years}} = 0.0333 \text{ failures per server per year}
$$

### Step 2: Define the System Failure Condition

The system fails only when **more than N − K = 100 servers** have failed simultaneously. Equivalently, the system is alive while **at most 100 servers** have failed.

For a 10,000-server WSC, we define the random variable **X = number of failed servers in time t**. Then:

$$
X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda N t) \quad \text{(Poisson process model)}
$$

The system is **alive** if $X \le 100$, and **dead** if $X > 100$.

### Step 3: Reliability Function of the WSC

The probability that the WSC is still alive at time t is:

$$
R_{\text{WSC}}(t) = P(X \le 100) = \sum_{k=0}^{100} \frac{e^{-\lambda N t} (\lambda N t)^k}{k!}
$$

### Step 4: MTTF Integral

By definition:

$$
MTTF_{\text{WSC}} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_{\text{WSC}}(t) \, dt
$$

### Step 5: Approximation for N >> K (Large Warehouse)

For $N \gg K$, the system fails when even **one** spare is exhausted. Treating the first arrival of the $(N - K + 1)$-th failure as the system's MTTF:

$$
MTTF_{\text{WSC}} \approx \frac{M}{N - K + 1}
$$

### Step 6: Numerical Evaluation

Plugging in N = 10,000, M = 30 years, K = 9,900:

$$
MTTF_{\text{WSC}} = \frac{30 \text{ years}}{10{,}000 - 9{,}900 + 1} = \frac{30}{101}
$$

$$
\boxed{MTTF_{\text{WSC}} \approx 0.297 \text{ years} \approx 108 \text{ days}}
$$

### Step 7: Interpretation (KTU Exam Insight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without redundancy** (K = 10,000), MTTF would be $\frac{30}{1} = 30$ years. With **1% redundancy** (K = 9,900), MTTF drops to 108 days. This shows that **WSCs are inherently less reliable** than individual servers, but **software-level fault tolerance** (replication, retries, consensus) compensates to deliver high service availability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost-Per-Queries-Per-Second Optimization

### Problem Statement
A WSC operator must choose between **two server types** for a search workload:

| Parameter | Server A | Server B |
|---|---|---|
| Purchase Cost ($) | $4,000 | $6,000 |
| 3-yr Power + Cooling | $1,000 | $800 |
| Queries/Second (QPS) | 800 | 1,400 |
| 3-yr TCO | $5,000 | $6,800 |

Compute the **Cost-per-QPS** for each, and recommend the better design.

### Step 1: Total TCO over 3 Years

Server A:
$$
TCO_A = 4000 + 1000 = 5000 \text{ USD}
$$

Server B:
$$
TCO_B = 6000 + 800 = 6800 \text{ USD}
$$

### Step 2: Cost-per-QPS

Server A:
$$
C_A = \frac{5000}{800} = 6.25 \text{ USD/QPS}
$$

Server B:
$$
C_B = \frac{6800}{1400} = 4.857 \text{ USD/QPS}
$$

### Step 3: Decision

$$
C_B < C_A
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Server B is 22.3\% more cost-efficient.}}
$$

### Step 4: Cluster Sizing for 1 Million QPS

For Server A:
$$
N_A = \lceil \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{800} \rceil = 1250 \text{ servers}
$$

For Server B:
$$
N_B = \lceil \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1400} \rceil = 715 \text{ servers}
$$

Total cost comparison:
$$
\text{Cost}_A = 1250 \times 5000 = 6.25 \text{ M USD}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_B = 715 \times 6800 = 4.86 \text{ M USD}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Savings with Server B: 1.39 M USD (22.3\%)}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Clos Network Bisection Bandwidth

### Problem Statement
A WSC fabric uses a **3-tier Clos topology**:
- **Tier 1 (Spine):** 4 switches
- **Tier 2 (Aggregation):** 8 switches
- **Tier 3 (Tor / Leaf):** 16 Tor switches
- Each Tor has **40 servers** at **10 Gbps** each.

Compute the **bisection bandwidth** of this network.

### Step 1: Number of Servers

$$
N_{\text{servers}} = 16 \times 40 = 640
$$

### Step 2: Maximum Concurrent Traffic (Worst Case)

In the worst case, **all servers in the left half** send to **all servers in the right half**. Half of the servers:

$$
N_{\text{half}} = 320
$$

### Step 3: Required Uplinks from Half → Spines

Each half's 8 Tor switches (left half: 8 Tor; right half: 8 Tor). Each Tor connects to **all 4 spine switches**. We assume each Tor has 4 uplinks of 40 Gbps each (one to each spine).

Total uplink capacity from left half to spines:
$$
\text{BW}_{\text{uplink, half}} = 8 \text{ Tor} \times 4 \text{ uplinks} \times 40 \text{ Gbps} = 1280 \text{ Gbps}
$$

### Step 4: Spine Capacity

Spines have 8 downlinks × 40 Gbps = 320 Gbps per spine. With 4 spines:
$$
\text{BW}_{\text{spines, total}} = 4 \times 320 = 1280 \text{ Gbps}
$$

### Step 5: Bisection Bandwidth

$$
\boxed{BW_{\text{bisect}} = 1280 \text{ Gbps} = 1.28 \text{ Tbps}}
$$

### Step 6: Verification — Non-Blocking Property

For a non-blocking Clos with $m$ spine switches and $k$ ports per Tor, bisection bandwidth is:

$$
BW_{\text{bisect}} = \frac{N \times BW_{\text{per-server}}}{2}
$$

$$
= \frac{640 \times 10}{2} = 3200 \text{ Gbps}
$$

Since 1280 Gbps < 3200 Gbps, this Clos is **not fully non-blocking** — it is **over-subscribed** by a factor of 2.5×. This is **acceptable for many workloads** (search, web serving) where not all servers communicate simultaneously.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Most production WSCs run at **3:1 to 10:1 oversubscription** to save cost. The trade-off is that all-to-all workloads (e.g., ML training) suffer, while partitionable workloads (e.g., MapReduce) thrive.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — WSC vs Traditional Data Center

| Dimension | Traditional Data Center | Warehouse-Scale Computer |
|---|---|---|
| **Workload** | Heterogeneous, multi-tenant | Homogeneous, single operator |
| **Hardware Design** | COTS (commercial off-the-shelf) | Custom, sometimes co-designed with chip vendors |
| **Scale** | 100 – 5,000 servers | 50,000 – 1,000,000+ servers |
| **Application Design** | Independent per tenant | Jointly optimized with hardware |
| **Failure Domain** | Server (tolerated) | Server (transparent, software-recoverable) |
| **Network** | Fat tree, ECMP | Clos, dragonfly, optical circuit-switched |
| **PUE** | 1.5 – 2.0 (typical) | 1.07 – 1.20 (best-in-class) |
| **Cost Sensitivity** | Per-machine | Per-query / per-watt |
| **Software Stack** | Virtualization + multi-tenant | Custom schedulers (Borg, Kubernetes) |
| **Power Distribution** | AC, 120V/208V | DC, 48V or 380V high-voltage DC |
| **Cooling** | Air | Air + liquid + (in some) single-phase immersion |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: PUE Calculation with Numerical Example

### Problem Statement
A WSC consumes **10 MW** of total facility power. The IT equipment (servers, switches, storage) consumes **8.5 MW**. The remaining 1.5 MW goes to **cooling, lighting, and UPS losses**. Calculate PUE and identify the dominant overhead.

### Step 1: PUE Formula

$$
PUE = \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{IT}}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitution

$$
PUE = \frac{10}{8.5} = 1.176
$$

### Step 3: Overhead Percentage

$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{P_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{IT}}}{P_{\text{IT}}} = \frac{1.5}{8.5} = 17.6\%
$$

### Step 4: Best-in-Class Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry benchmarks:**
> - **Legacy enterprise DC:** PUE ≈ 1.8 (44% overhead)
> - **Modern colocation:** PUE ≈ 1.3 (23% overhead)
> - **Hyperscale WSC (Google, Facebook):** PUE ≈ 1.10 (9% overhead)
> - **Theoretical minimum:** PUE = 1.00 (0% overhead)

### Step 5: Annual Energy Savings Calculation

If the same 8.5 MW IT load were moved from a PUE 1.8 facility to a PUE 1.1 WSC, the savings are:

$$
\Delta P = 8.5 \times (1.8 - 1.1) = 5.95 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
E_{\text{saved}} = 5.95 \times 24 \times 365 = 52{,}122 \text{ MWh/year}
$$

At $0.10/kWh:
$$
\text{\$ saved} = 52{,}122 \times 0.10 = 5.21 \text{ M USD/year}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Approximate annual savings: \$5.21 million}}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part A consists of short, direct questions testing *Remember* and *Understand* levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answer in **3–4 sentences** with **one supporting diagram or formula** if relevant.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a Warehouse-Scale Computer (WSC) and list THREE characteristics that distinguish it from a traditional data center.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Warehouse-Scale Computer (WSC)** is a collection of thousands of servers, networked and orchestrated by software to deliver Internet-scale services as if it were a single computing platform.

**Three distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Homogeneous workload:** WSCs typically run a small set of related services owned by one operator, unlike traditional data centers that host diverse, multi-tenant workloads.
2. **Treats the entire warehouse as one machine:** Software and hardware are co-designed; the WSC is an *architectural* entity, not just a collection of servers.
3. **Built-in fault tolerance at software level:** A WSC is designed to tolerate the loss of individual servers, racks, or network paths continuously, achieving high availability (e.g., 99.99%) via software mechanisms (replication, consensus, retries).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is **PUE** and what is its theoretical minimum value? Why is achieving PUE = 1.0 practically impossible?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness)** is defined as:

$$
PUE = \frac{P_{\text{total facility}}}{P_{\text{IT equipment}}}
$$

The **theoretical minimum is 1.0**, meaning all facility power is consumed by IT equipment with zero overhead.

PUE = 1.0 is practically impossible because a working data center *always* needs additional power for **cooling (chillers, pumps, fans)**, **UPS conversion losses**, **lighting**, and **monitoring equipment**. The industry-best achieved PUE is approximately **1.06–1.10** (Google, Meta, Microsoft hyperscale facilities).

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has **internal choice** (Attempt ANY ONE). Total = 14 marks. Sub-parts are typically 7 + 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**(a)** Explain the **six-level hierarchical architecture** of a Warehouse-Scale Computer with a neat block diagram. Compare it with a traditional server. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WSC has **5,000 servers**, each with **MTTF = 25 years**. The application needs at least **4,800 servers** to remain functional (tolerates up to 200 failures). Compute the **MTTF of the WSC** with redundancy. If the same WSC had **no software-level fault tolerance** (i.e., any single server failure causes service outage), what would be the MTTF? Comment on the engineering implications. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Six-Level Hierarchy (7 marks)

| Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **L1: Application / Service** | End-user facing service | Google Search, YouTube |
| **L2: Software Platform** | Distributed storage and data processing | GFS, BigTable, MapReduce |
| **L3: Cluster Manager** | Job scheduling, resource allocation | Borg, Kubernetes, Mesos |
| **L4: Compute Server** | Individual commodity-class server with CPU, DRAM, SSD, NIC | Google custom board |
| **L5: Rack and Tor Switch** | 40–80 servers in a rack with a Top-of-Rack switch | Standard 19" rack |
| **L6: Network Fabric and Building** | Clos network, substations, cooling plant | The Dalles, Oregon campus |

**Comparison with a traditional server:**

- A traditional server has **CPU + Memory + I/O + OS** at one level of abstraction.
- A WSC **elevates the entire warehouse to a single "server"**, where the **cluster manager acts as the "OS"** and individual servers are like **threads** in a multi-core CPU.
- The WSC's "memory" is the **distributed storage layer** (GFS, BigTable); its "compute" is the **collective CPUs** of all servers.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Tip (7 marks):** Award 1 mark per level (6 marks) + 1 mark for the comparison statement.

---

#### Part (b) — MTTF Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$
N = 5000, \quad M = 25 \text{ years}, \quad K = 4800
$$

**Step 2 — Number of allowed failures before system outage:**

$$
N - K = 5000 - 4800 = 200 \text{ servers}
$$

**Step 3 — MTTF with redundancy:**

For large N, the system fails when the $(N - K + 1)$-th failure occurs:

$$
MTTF_{\text{redundant}} \approx \frac{M}{N - K + 1} = \frac{25}{200 + 1}
$$

$$
MTTF_{\text{redundant}} = \frac{25}{201} \approx 0.1244 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\boxed{MTTF_{\text{redundant}} \approx 45.4 \text{ days}}
$$

**Step 4 — MTTF without redundancy (any single failure = outage):**

$$
MTTF_{\text{no-redundancy}} = \frac{M}{N} = \frac{25}{5000} = 0.005 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\boxed{MTTF_{\text{no-redundancy}} \approx 1.825 \text{ days} = 43.8 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Implications:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without software-level fault tolerance, a 5,000-server WSC would suffer a complete outage **every 1.8 days on average**. With 200-server redundancy tolerance, the outage interval extends to **45 days**, a **25× improvement**. This mathematically justifies the enormous investment hyperscalers make in **replication, consensus protocols, and automated failover**.

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the **cost structure of a Warehouse-Scale Computer** in detail. How does the cost amortization cycle influence server refresh decisions? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WSC operator is evaluating **two server designs** for a new deployment:

| Parameter | Design X | Design Y |
|---|---|---|
| Server cost ($) | 3,500 | 4,800 |
| 3-yr power + cooling ($) | 1,200 | 900 |
| Queries/sec (QPS) | 700 | 1,100 |
| MTTF (years) | 8 | 12 |

The workload requires **2 million QPS** sustained, and the WSC currently serves at 70% average utilization.

Compute the **number of servers** and **3-year TCO** for each design. Which design is more cost-efficient per QPS? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Cost Structure (7 marks)

**Major cost components of a WSC:**

| Component | Share | Amortization Period |
|---|---|---|
| Servers (CPU, memory, disk) | ~45% | 3 years |
| Networking (switches, optics) | ~15% | 5 years |
| Power infrastructure (UPS, PDU) | ~10% | 10–15 years |
| Cooling (chillers, CRAH) | ~10% | 10–15 years |
| Building shell | ~10% | 15–20 years |
| Operations & admin | ~10% | annual |

**Refresh cycle reasoning:**

> [!NOTE]
> Server depreciation follows an **"S-curve"** — the first 12 months see high reliability, the second year is steady, and from year 3 onward, **failure rates climb steeply** while **performance-per-watt** falls behind newer designs. Hence, hyperscalers **redeploy 1/3 of the fleet every 12 months**, achieving a 3-year full-refresh cycle. This balances the **steepest depreciation** (servers) with the **slowest** (building).

---

#### Part (b) — Cost-Per-QPS Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — TCO per server (3 years):**

Design X:
$$
TCO_X = 3500 + 1200 = 4700 \text{ USD}
$$

Design Y:
$$
TCO_Y = 4800 + 900 = 5700 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective per-server QPS at 70% utilization:**

Design X:
$$
QPS_X = 700 \times 0.70 = 490 \text{ QPS}
$$

Design Y:
$$
QPS_Y = 1100 \times 0.70 = 770 \text{ QPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of servers needed for 2 million QPS:**

Design X:
$$
N_X = \lceil \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{490} \rceil = 4082 \text{ servers}
$$

Design Y:
$$
N_Y = \lceil \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{770} \rceil = 2598 \text{ servers}
$$

**Step 4 — Total 3-year TCO:**

Design X:
$$
\text{TotalTCO}_X = 4082 \times 4700 = 19{,}185{,}400 \text{ USD} \approx 19.19 \text{ M USD}
$$

Design Y:
$$
\text{TotalTCO}_Y = 2598 \times 5700 = 14{,}808{,}600 \text{ USD} \approx 14.81 \text{ M USD}
$$

**Step 5 — Cost per QPS:**

$$
C_X = \frac{19.19 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6} = 9.595 \text{ USD/QPS}
$$

$$
C_Y = \frac{14.81 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6} = 7.405 \text{ USD/QPS}
$$

**Step 6 — Savings:**

$$
\Delta C = 9.595 - 7.405 = 2.19 \text{ USD/QPS}
$$

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \frac{2.19}{9.595} \times 100 \approx 22.8\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Design Y saves 4.38 M USD (22.8\% cheaper per QPS)}}
$$

**Step 7 — Reliability comment:**

Design Y also has **MTTF = 12 years vs. 8 years** (50% better), so it is the **strictly dominant choice** in this scenario.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting utilization factor** in capacity planning — examiners deduct 2 marks if you assume 100% utilization (unrealistic for WSCs which run at 70–90% with bursts).
> 2. **Confusing server MTTF with system MTTF** — students often write "$MTTF = 25$ years" without applying the $N - K + 1$ denominator.
> 3. **Not including power/cooling in TCO** — many forget the energy column, which is 10–15% of the cost.
> 4. **Mixing up PUE and DCiE** (Data Center Infrastructure Efficiency) — PUE = 1/PUE_efficiency, often reversed.
> 5. **Saying "WSC is a distributed system"** — wrong! WSC is *one* computer at the architectural level; distributed systems appear *inside* the WSC.
> 6. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — always write years/days/MW/USD explicitly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Print This Section!**

- [x] **WSC Definition:** A unified computing platform of 10,000s of servers run as **one computer** by a single operator.
- [x] **Origin:** Google (Barroso & Hölzle, 2009), *"The Datacenter as a Computer"*.
- [x] **Six Layers:** Application → Platform → Cluster Manager → Server → Rack/Tor → Fabric/Power.
- [x] **PUE Formula:** $PUE = P_{\text{total}} / P_{\text{IT}}$ — ideal = 1.0, hyperscale = 1.1.
- [x] **MTTF of N independent servers:** $MTTF = M / N$ (no redundancy).
- [x] **MTTF with K redundancy:** $\approx M / (N - K + 1)$.
- [x] **TCO Composition:** Server 45%, Network 15%, Power 10%, Cooling 10%, Building 10%, Ops 10%.
- [x] **Refresh Cycle:** 3-year full fleet; 1/3 replaced annually.
- [x] **Network:** Clos topology (3-tier: Spine → Aggregation → Tor), oversubscription 3:1 to 10:1 typical.
- [x] **Fault Tolerance Layers:** Hardware → Network → Storage → Scheduler → Middleware → Application.
- [x] **Bisection Bandwidth Lower Bound:** $N \times BW_{\text{per-server}} / 2$.
- [x] **Availability Target:** 99.99% (four nines) ≈ 52.6 min downtime/year.
- [x] **Storage Durability:** 99.999999999% (eleven nines) for Google Drive data.
- [x] **Cost-per-QPS:** $C = TCO / (N_{\text{servers}} \times QPS_{\text{per-server}} \times \text{Util})$.
- [x] **WSC vs Data Center:** WSC = homogeneous, single operator, co-designed; Data Center = heterogeneous, multi-tenant, independent.
- [x] **Power Distribution:** Modern WSCs use **380V DC** or **48V DC** to reduce conversion losses.
- [x] **Cooling:** Hot/cold aisle containment, free cooling (outside air in cold climates), single-phase immersion emerging.
- [x] **Cluster Managers:** Google Borg → open-source Kubernetes; Apache Mesos; YARN (Hadoop).
- [x] **Key Paper to Cite in Exam:** Barroso, L. A., Clidaras, J., & Hölzle, U. (2013). *"The Datacenter as a Computer: An Introduction to the Design of Warehouse-Scale Machines."* Synthesis Lectures on Computer Architecture.

topics.title: Moore's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Moore's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Moore's Law** is an empirical observation formulated by **Gordon E. Moore**, co-founder of Fairchild Semiconductor and Intel, in his seminal 1965 paper *"Cramming more components onto integrated circuits"* published in *Electronics Magazine*. The law observes that the **number of transistors on an integrated circuit (IC) doubles approximately every 18 to 24 months**, while the per-transistor cost decreases. Although originally expressed as a doubling every year (1965), it was revised in **1975** to a doubling every **two years** to better align with empirical IC fabrication data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *Moore's Law states that the number of transistors that can be placed inexpensively on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every two years, leading to exponential growth in computational capability, memory capacity, and sensor technology, accompanied by a corresponding decrease in relative cost.*

### 1.2 The Three Postulates of Moore's Law

For the KTU 2024 scheme, Moore's Law is evaluated under three concurrent predictions:

1. **Transistor Density Postulate:** The number of transistors per unit area on an IC doubles every 24 months.
2. **Performance Postulate:** Computational performance (instructions per second) increases in tandem with transistor density, historically yielding ~**1.5× clock-speed improvement and ~1.5× IPC (Instructions Per Cycle)** combined.
3. **Economic Postulate:** The cost per transistor falls by approximately **50% every two years**, simultaneously reducing unit cost while increasing capability.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Parking Lot Paradigm:**
> Imagine a rectangular parking lot that fits **50 cars** in **1971**. According to Moore's Law, by **2023** (≈ 26 doubling periods), the *same physical lot* should fit **50 × 2²⁶ = 3,355,443,200 cars** — over **3.35 billion cars**! Of course, a real parking lot cannot physically hold this many cars. The magic is that each "car" (transistor) keeps **shrinking** in size (from ~10 µm in 1971 down to ~3 nm in 2023), allowing exponentially more of them to be packed into the same silicon real estate. The lot's physical area does not change — what changes is the **size of each occupant**.

This is the essence of Moore's Law: it is a **geometric shrink** phenomenon, not a physical expansion.

### 1.4 Historical Empirical Anchors (Verifiable Data Points)

| Year | Microprocessor | Transistor Count | Process Node |
|------|----------------|------------------|--------------|
| 1971 | Intel 4004 | **2,300** | 10 µm |
| 1985 | Intel 80386 | **275,000** | 1.5 µm |
| 2000 | Intel Pentium 4 | 42,000,000 | 180 nm |
| 2015 | Intel Skylake (i7-6700K) | **1,750,000,000** | 14 nm |
| 2023 | Apple M2 Ultra | **134,000,000,000** | 5 nm (TSMC N3) |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024 Exams:** Always quote **2 years** as the canonical doubling period unless a question explicitly references the original 1965 formulation (1 year). Examiners penalize loose terminology.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Moore's Law

Moore's Law is **empirical**, not a physical law. Its persistence over six decades is driven by four interlocking industry drivers:

- **Step 1 — Optical Lithography Resolution Advances:** Successive reductions in the wavelength of light used to pattern silicon (g-line 436 nm → ArF immersion 193 nm → EUV 13.5 nm) enable finer feature sizes.
- **Step 2 — Yield Engineering & Defect Density Reduction:** Cleaner fabrication environments and redundancy techniques allow larger dies at acceptable yield.
- **Step 3 — Economic Scaling (Rock's Law):** The cost of fabrication facilities (fabs) doubles roughly every four years, but the per-transistor cost still falls because transistor count grows faster.
- **Step 4 — Architectural Innovation:** When geometric scaling slows, **architectural parallelism** (multi-core, SIMD, GPUs) and **die-level innovations** (3D stacking, chiplets) sustain performance growth.

### 2.2 Moore's Law as an Exponential Process

Moore's Law is a classic **exponential growth** function. Let $t$ = years elapsed, $N_0$ = initial transistor count, and $T$ = doubling period (in years). Then:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$$

If we measure cost per transistor $C(t)$ halving with the same period:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot 2^{-t/T} = \frac{C_0}{2^{t/T}}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$ | Transistor count at time $t$ | $T = 2$ yrs (modern); $T = 1$ yr (1965) |
| 2 | $C(t) = C_0 \cdot 2^{-t/T}$ | Cost per transistor at time $t$ | Halves every $T$ years |
| 3 | $r = 2^{1/T} - 1$ | Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of transistor count | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | $\text{FOM}(t) = N(t)/C(t)$ | Figure of Merit (transistors per dollar) | Quadruples every $T$ years |
| 5 | $L_{\min} \propto t^{-1/\alpha}$ | Minimum feature size scaling | $\alpha \approx 1.4$ historically |
| 6 | $A_{\text{die}} \approx A_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$ | Die area growth (Die-shrink constant) | Die size also grows ~10–14% per gen |
| 7 | $P \propto N \cdot f \cdot V^2$ | Power (Dennard relation — broke ~2005) | $V$ = supply voltage, $f$ = frequency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The CAGR $r$ must **NOT** be reported as a percentage of $T$ directly. For $T = 2$ years, $r = 2^{0.5} - 1 \approx 0.4142$ i.e. **41.42% per year**, not 50% per year. Many students commit this error.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Moore's Law underpins nearly every economic projection in the semiconductor industry:

- **EDA Tool Vendors (Cadence, Synopsys):** Plan verification IP roadmaps assuming 2× transistor counts.
- **Data Center Architects:** Forecast FLOPs and TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) using Moore's projection curves.
- **AI/ML Engineering:** Plan training compute budgets — modern LLM training scales roughly with Moore-derived compute availability.
- **Consumer Electronics Pricing:** Smartphones, SSDs, and GPUs obey Moore-style cost deflation, enabling mass-market penetration.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Plan power-density mitigation strategies using Dennard-broken scaling equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Empirical Observation to Mathematical Form

**Given:** Moore's 1965 observation — transistor count doubles every $T$ years.

**Goal:** Derive a closed-form expression $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$.

**Step 1 — Define the doubling recurrence:**
At each multiple of $T$, the count doubles. So at times $0, T, 2T, 3T, \ldots$, the count is $N_0, 2N_0, 4N_0, 8N_0, \ldots$

**Step 2 — Express the recurrence compactly:**
For an integer $k = t/T$ (number of doubling periods), we have:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{k} = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the boundary condition:**
At $t = 0$:

$$N(0) = N_0 \cdot 2^{0/T} = N_0 \cdot 1 = N_0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Verify the doubling property:**
At $t = T$:

$$N(T) = N_0 \cdot 2^{T/T} = N_0 \cdot 2^1 = 2N_0 \quad \checkmark$$

At $t = 2T$:

$$N(2T) = N_0 \cdot 2^{2T/T} = N_0 \cdot 4 = 4N_0 \quad \checkmark$$

> This closed form is the **fundamental equation of Moore's Law** and is the most-tested identity in KTU's ESE papers.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)

**Given:** $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$. The CAGR $r$ is the constant annual multiplier such that $N_0(1+r)^t = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$.

**Step 1 — Equate the two growth expressions:**

$$(1 + r)^t = 2^{t/T}$$

**Step 2 — Take the $t$-th root of both sides:**

$$1 + r = 2^{1/T}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $r$:**

$$r = 2^{1/T} - 1$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for $T = 2$ years:**

$$r = 2^{1/2} - 1 = \sqrt{2} - 1 \approx 1.41421 - 1 = 0.41421$$

Therefore, the CAGR is approximately **41.42% per year**.

**Step 5 — Evaluate for $T = 1.5$ years (Moore's 1975 revision variant):**

$$r = 2^{1/1.5} - 1 = 2^{2/3} - 1 \approx 1.5874 - 1 = 0.5874$$

Therefore, the CAGR is approximately **58.74% per year**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Moore's Law for Performance — Amdahl-style Decomposition

Performance of a CPU is approximately the product of transistor count $N$, instructions-per-cycle $I$, and clock frequency $f$:

$$P \approx k \cdot N \cdot I \cdot f$$

Given that historically $I \cdot f \approx 1.5$ per generation, and $N$ doubles, the per-generation performance multiplier is:

$$\frac{P_{i+1}}{P_i} \approx 2 \cdot 1.5 = 3.0$$

This yields the well-known **3× per generation ≈ 1.5× per year** performance trend.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Doubling Time from CAGR

If CAGR is given as $r$ (e.g., 0.4142 for $T=2$), then:

$$T = \frac{\ln 2}{\ln(1+r)}$$

**Numerical check for $r = 0.4142$:**

$$T = \frac{\ln 2}{\ln(1.4142)} = \frac{0.6931}{0.3466} \approx 2.000 \text{ years} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Moore's Law Predictions (Intel Reference)

| Year | Reference CPU | Predicted $N$ from $N_0=2300$ (1971) | Actual $N$ | Ratio (Actual/Pred) |
|------|---------------|---------------------------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1971 | 4004 | **2,300** | 2,300 | 1.00 |
| 1985 | 80386 | $2300 \cdot 2^{7} = 294,400$ | 275,000 | 0.93 |
| 2000 | Pentium 4 | $2300 \cdot 2^{14.5} = 37.7$ M | 42 M | 1.11 |
| 2015 | i7-6700K | $2300 \cdot 2^{22} = 9.65$ B | 1.75 B | 0.18 |
| 2023 | M2 Ultra | $2300 \cdot 2^{26} = 154$ B | 134 B | 0.87 |

> The 2015 deviation reflects the **Dennard scaling breakdown** of ~2005, when voltage scaling halted, reducing the per-transistor performance contribution.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A processor fabricated in **2005** had **125 million** transistors. Using Moore's Law with $T = 2$ years, predict the transistor count in **2023**.

**Solution:**

Number of doubling periods:

$$n = \frac{2023 - 2005}{2} = 9$$

Predicted count:

$$N(2023) = 125 \times 10^6 \times 2^{9} = 125 \times 10^6 \times 512$$

$$N(2023) = 64,000 \times 10^6 = 6.4 \times 10^{10} = 64 \text{ billion}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** Always state the **assumed doubling period** at the start of your solution. KTU examiners deduct marks when students mix $T = 1$ and $T = 2$ within the same answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Moore's Law Drivers

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interacting subsystems** that collectively sustain Moore's Law, rather than depicting the law as a single physical artifact.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[MooreObservation1965] --> B[TransistorDensityDoubles]
    A --> C[CostPerTransistorHalves]
    A --> D[PerformanceImproves]
    B --> E[ProcessNodeShrink]
    B --> F[DieAreaGrowth]
    C --> G[RockLawFabCostDoubles4yr]
    D --> H[DennardScalingP2kV2]
    E --> I[OpticalLithographyEUV193nm]
    E --> J[EtchAndDepositionTech]
    F --> K[YieldEngineering]

    H --> M[ScalingBreakdown2005]
    I --> N[PostMooreStrategies]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[ThreeDStacking]
    N --> P[Chiplets]
    N --> Q[SpecializedAccelerators]
    N --> R[QuantumComputing]
    N --> S[NeuromorphicComputing]
```

### 4.2 Moore's Law Lifecycle Flow (From Observation to Limitation)

```mermaid

    P1[Phase1:GeometricScaling1965to2005] --> P2[Phase2:DennardBroken2005to2015]
    P2 --> P3[Phase3:DarkSilicon2015to2025]

    P1 --> S1[SmallerTransistorsLowerVoltageHigherFrequency]

    S2 --> L2[Limit:AmdahlILPWall]

```

### 4.3 Component-Level Mapping: How Moore's Law Translates to System Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[MooreLawInput] --> T1[TransistorCountN]
    M --> T2[CostPerTransistorC]
    M --> T3[FrequencyImprovementf]
    T1 --> R1[MoreCacheMemory]
    T1 --> R2[MoreExecutionUnits]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams in KTU Exams:** When asked to *"explain the implications of Moore's Law"*, you must reference the **three downstream effects** — *cache growth, multi-core scaling, and IPC gains* — as shown in the third diagram. Examiners award full marks only when architecture-level consequences are explicitly named.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
