*State Moore's Law. Mention the year it was formulated and the revised doubling period.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Moore's Law, formulated by **Gordon E. Moore in 1965**, states that the **number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every 12 months**, leading to exponential growth in computational capability. In **1975**, the law was revised to a **doubling period of approximately 24 months (2 years)** to better match the empirical data from semiconductor manufacturing. *(3 marks: 1 mark formulation, 1 mark year, 1 mark revised period.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
*What is meant by the *economic corollary* of Moore's Law?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The economic corollary of Moore's Law states that as the transistor count on an integrated circuit doubles every two years, the **cost per transistor simultaneously falls by approximately 50%**, while the overall productivity and functionality of the chip increase. This means consumers pay less for vastly more powerful devices, enabling mass-market adoption of computing technology. This phenomenon is sometimes called **Rock's Law** in conjunction, which states that the *cost of semiconductor fabs doubles every four years*. *(3 marks: 1 mark statement, 1 mark cost-halving, 1 mark Rock's Law connection.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Module 4 — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical form of Moore's Law $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$ from the empirical doubling observation. Show that the CAGR of transistor count is $r = 2^{1/T} - 1$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The Intel 4004 processor (1971) contained 2,300 transistors. Using Moore's Law with $T = 2$ years, compute:
- (i) The expected transistor count of the Intel Core i7-6700K in **2015**.
- (ii) The actual count was 1.75 billion. Compute the **percentage deviation**.
- (iii) Comment on why the deviation occurred. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer (Q3A):**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks) — Empirical Statement:*
Moore's empirical observation is that the transistor count at time $t + T$ is twice the count at time $t$:

$$N(t + T) = 2 \cdot N(t)$$

*Step 2 (2 marks) — Assume Exponential Form:*
Assume $N(t) = A \cdot b^{t}$ for constants $A$ and $b$. Substituting:

$$A \cdot b^{t+T} = 2 \cdot A \cdot b^{t} \implies b^{T} = 2 \implies b = 2^{1/T}$$

*Step 3 (1 mark) — Apply Initial Condition:*
At $t = 0$, $N(0) = A = N_0$. So $A = N_0$, yielding:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$$

*Step 4 (2 marks) — CAGR Derivation:*
Let $r$ be the CAGR, so $N(t) = N_0(1+r)^t$. Equating:

$$(1+r)^t = 2^{t/T} \implies 1+r = 2^{1/T} \implies r = 2^{1/T} - 1$$

For $T = 2$:

$$r = \sqrt{2} - 1 \approx 0.4142 \text{ or } 41.42\% \text{ per year}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks) — Number of Doubling Periods:*
From 1971 to 2015 = 44 years; with $T = 2$:

$$n = \frac{2015 - 1971}{2} = \frac{44}{2} = 22$$

*Step 2 (2 marks) — Predicted Transistor Count:*

$$N(2015) = 2300 \cdot 2^{22} = 2300 \cdot 4,194,304 = 9,646,899,200 \approx 9.65 \text{ billion}$$

*Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Percentage Deviation:*

$$\% \text{Deviation} = \frac{9.65 \text{B} - 1.75 \text{B}}{9.65 \text{B}} \times 100\% \approx -81.87\%$$

*Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Commentary:*
The actual count is **lower than predicted** because Moore's Law has been **slowing down** since the mid-2000s due to the breakdown of **Dennard Scaling** (voltage could no longer be reduced with feature size), the **power wall**, and the rise of **dark silicon** (transistors that cannot all be powered simultaneously). The industry compensated by transitioning to **multi-core architectures** and **specialized accelerators** (GPUs, TPUs) rather than simply increasing transistor counts at the historical rate.

---

**Q3(B). [Alternative Choice — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **Dennard Scaling** relationship and describe why it broke down around **2005**. Discuss the consequences of this breakdown on Moore's Law. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain **four post-Moore's Law strategies** the semiconductor industry is currently pursuing to sustain performance growth. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer (Q3B):**

**(a) Dennard Scaling and its Breakdown (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks) — Dennard Scaling Rule:*
Dennard Scaling, proposed by **Robert Dennard in 1974**, states that as transistor dimensions scale down by a factor $s$ ($s > 1$):

- Linear dimension $\propto 1/s$
- Voltage $V \propto 1/s$
- Current $I \propto 1/s$
- Capacitance $C \propto 1/s$
- Power per transistor $P \propto V^2 \cdot f \cdot C \propto 1/s^2 \cdot s \cdot 1/s = 1/s^2$
- Power **density** stays constant (because area $\propto 1/s^2$).

*Step 2 (2 marks) — Implication:*
Power per transistor falls as $1/s^2$, so even as transistor count doubles, the total chip power remains constant. This allowed **clock frequency to scale** with transistor count, sustaining single-threaded performance.

*Step 3 (2 marks) — Breakdown (~2005):*
Around **2005**, Dennard scaling broke down because:
- **Threshold voltage** ($V_{th}$) cannot scale below ~0.3 V due to **leakage currents** and **quantum tunneling** at sub-32 nm nodes.
- Subthreshold swing is physically limited to ~60 mV/decade at room temperature (Boltzmann tyranny).
- As a result, $V$ no longer scales with $1/s$, so power density **rises** with each generation.

*Step 4 (1 mark) — Consequence:*
The **"Power Wall"** was reached. The industry responded by halting clock frequency growth at ~3–4 GHz and pivoting to **multi-core architectures**, where more cores replace higher frequencies.

---

**(b) Four Post-Moore Strategies (7 Marks — 1.75 each):**

1. **3D Stacking (e.g., AMD 3D V-Cache, HBM):** Vertically stack multiple silicon dies using Through-Silicon Vias (TSVs) to increase density and bandwidth without lithographic shrink.
2. **Chiplets (e.g., AMD EPYC, Apple M-series):** Disaggregate a monolithic SoC into smaller, separately fabricated dies connected via advanced packaging (CoWoS, InFO), improving yield and enabling heterogeneous integration.
3. **Domain-Specific Accelerators (e.g., GPUs, TPUs, NPUs):** Sacrifice generality for energy-efficient computation of specific workloads (matrix multiply, convolutions, inference), achieving >10× perf/W over CPUs.
4. **Emerging Computing Paradigms (Quantum, Neuromorphic, Photonic):** Explore fundamentally non-CMOS substrates — e.g., superconducting qubits, spiking neural networks, or silicon photonics — to leap beyond CMOS limits entirely.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Moore's Law Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $T = 1$ vs $T = 2$:** Always specify the doubling period explicitly. Mixing both within one answer loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Treating Moore's Law as a *physical* law:** It is **empirical/observational**. Stating otherwise is a 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Ignoring Dennard Scaling:** Any discussion of post-2005 performance trends without mentioning Dennard's breakdown is incomplete.
> 4. **Formula error on CAGR:** $r = 2^{1/T} - 1$, not $r = 1/T$. Forgetting the exponential form loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Using the wrong base year for $N_0$:** Always state your reference year and $N_0$ before computing predictions.
> 6. **Conflating Moore's Law with Koomey's Law or Rock's Law:** Each is a separate empirical law — be precise.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Moore's Law (Module 4):**

- **Originator:** Gordon E. Moore, 1965 (Electronics Magazine). Revised 1975.
- **Original Period:** 1 year (1965) → **Revised to 2 years (modern canonical).**
- **Core Equation:** $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$, with $T = 2$.
- **CAGR Formula:** $r = 2^{1/T} - 1 \approx 41.42\%$ per year for $T = 2$.
- **Cost Equation:** $C(t) = C_0 \cdot 2^{-t/T}$ (halves every $T$ years).
- **Figure of Merit:** $\text{FOM} = N(t)/C(t)$ quadruples every $T$ years.
- **Dennard Scaling:** $P_{\text{density}} = \text{const}$ when dimensions scale; **broke down in 2005** due to leakage current and threshold voltage limits (~0.3 V).
- **Three Pillars of Moore's Law:** (1) Density ↑, (2) Cost ↓, (3) Performance ↑.
- **Three Major Limits:** (1) **Physical** — quantum tunneling, leakage, heat dissipation; (2) **Economic** — fab cost (Rock's Law) doubling every 4 years; (3) **Architectural** — ILP wall, memory wall, power wall.
- **Post-Moore Strategies:** 3D Stacking, Chiplets, Domain-Specific Accelerators, Neuromorphic, Quantum, Photonic computing.
- **Related Empirical Laws:**
  - *Rock's Law:* Fab construction cost doubles every 4 years.
  - *Koomey's Law:* Energy efficiency of computation doubles every 1.57 years.
  - *Wirth's Law:* Software bloat outpaces hardware gains.
  - *Pollack's Rule:* Performance ∝ √(complexity), so doubling transistors yields only ~1.4× perf.
- **Key Data Anchor for Exams:** Intel 4004 (1971, 2,300 transistors, 10 µm) → Apple M2 Ultra (2023, 134 billion, 5 nm).
- **For numerical problems:** Always write $n = (t - t_0)/T$ explicitly, then compute $N_0 \cdot 2^n$.
- **Common unit reminder:** 1 billion = $10^9$; 1 million = $10^6$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dennard Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Dennard Scaling # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Dennard Scaling** (also called the **Dennard–Baccarani–Bhat–Chen–Cody–Low–Ning–Noble–Yu scaling law**) is a formal geometric and electrical scaling theory proposed by **Robert H. Dennard** (IBM) in his landmark 1974 IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits paper *"Design of Ion-Implanted MOSFET's with Very Small Physical Dimensions."* The law prescribes that as MOSFET transistor dimensions are uniformly shrunk by a scaling factor **κ (kappa) > 1**, the operating voltages, currents, doping concentrations, and capacitances must be adjusted simultaneously so that the **electric field inside the device remains constant**. Under these conditions: > **The power density (W/cm²) of an integrated circuit remains constant**, allowing the clock frequency to rise linearly with the scaling factor without increasing the total power budget of the chip. This single principle powered more than **30 years of Moore's-Law-driven microarchitectural progress** (1974–2004), enabling single-core clock frequencies to rise from **5 MHz (Intel 8080)** to **~3.8 GHz (Intel Pentium 4 / Prescott)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECST528, Module 4):** Dennard Scaling is the foundational theory explaining *why* frequency scaling ended and *why* the industry pivoted to multi-core processors, heterogeneous computing, dark silicon, and accelerator-rich architectures. Every architectural choice from 2006 onward is essentially a *reaction* to Dennard Scaling's breakdown. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipe" Intuition Imagine a city water distribution network: - **Transistor channel** = a flexible water pipe of length *L* and diameter *W*. - **Water pressure** = supply voltage *V_dd*. - **Flow rate** = drain current *I_d*. - **Pipe wall thickness** = gate oxide thickness *t_ox*. - **Total city water usage** = total chip power. If you **shrink the pipe** (reduce *L*, *W*, *t_ox* by factor κ) but **keep the water pressure the same**, the pipe walls will burst. So you must **proportionally reduce the water pressure** by the same factor κ. The flow rate drops by κ, but the pipe now responds (switches on/off) κ times faster because the water column is shorter. The beautiful outcome: although *each* pipe uses 1/κ² as much water, you can fit **κ² times more pipes in the same city**, so the **total water demand per square kilometer stays the same** — this is **constant power density**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Dennard Scaling is a **constant-power-density scaling regime**. The chip as a whole can consume the same watts per mm² of silicon regardless of how many transistors are packed in, *provided* voltages and dimensions are reduced in lock-step. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Notes | |--------|----------|---------------|-------| | **κ** | Scaling factor | dimensionless | κ > 1 indicates shrink | | **V_dd** | Supply voltage | Volts (V) | Typical 5 V → 0.7 V by 45 nm | | **t_ox** | Gate oxide thickness | nanometers (nm) | SiO₂ → ~1.2 nm at 65 nm node | | **L** | Channel length | nm | Minimum feature size | | **W** | Channel width | nm | | | **N_a** | Channel doping | cm⁻³ | Increases by κ | | **C_g** | Gate capacitance | Farads (F) | Scales as 1/κ | | **I_d** | Drain current | Amperes (A) | Scales as 1/κ | | **f** | Clock frequency | Hz (MHz/GHz) | Scales as κ | | **P** | Power per transistor | Watts (W) | Scales as **1/κ²** | | **P_density** | Power per unit area | W/cm² | **Constant** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bold-emphasized constants:** The two physical quantities that **do NOT scale** in Dennard's theory are the **electric field** (V/cm) and the **power density** (W/cm²). These are the load-bearing invariants of the entire scaling regime. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Dennard Scaling Dennard's law is built on three coupled assumptions: ### Pillar 1 — Geometric Scaling All horizontal dimensions (channel length *L*, channel width *W*) and all vertical dimensions (gate oxide thickness *t_ox*, junction depths *x_j*) shrink by **1/κ**. ### Pillar 2 — Electrical Scaling (Constant-Field Rule) To prevent the **electric field** in the oxide and channel from becoming destructive, the supply voltage *V_dd* and the threshold voltage *V_th* are both scaled by **1/κ**. ### Pillar 3 — Doping Scaling Channel and substrate doping concentration *N_a* is **increased by κ** to preserve correct transistor threshold behavior under the thinner oxide and shorter channel. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Scaling Chain 1. **Dimensions shrink** by 1/κ → gate area *A_g = W·L* shrinks by 1/κ². 2. **Gate oxide** is thinner by 1/κ → oxide capacitance per unit area $C'_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ **increases** by κ. 3. **Total gate capacitance** $C_g = C'_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$ scales by **(κ)·(1/κ²) = 1/κ**. 4. **Voltage scales** by 1/κ, but current *I_d* is proportional to *V·C'·W·v_sat* → current scales by **1/κ**. 5. **Gate delay** $\tau = C_g \cdot V_dd / I_d$ scales by **(1/κ)·(1/κ)/(1/κ) = 1/κ** → delay falls, frequency **rises by κ**. 6. **Dynamic power** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_dd^2 \cdot f$ scales by **(1/κ)·(1/κ²)·(κ) = 1/κ²**. 7. **Power density** $P/A$ scales by **(1/κ²)/(1/κ²) = 1 (constant)** ✅. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Symbol | Scaling Factor | Relationship | |---|-----------|--------|----------------|--------------| | 1 | Device dimensions (L, W, t_ox) | — | **1/κ** | Linear shrink | | 2 | Supply voltage | V_dd | **1/κ** | Constant-field rule | | 3 | Threshold voltage | V_th | **1/κ** | Tracks V_dd | | 4 | Doping concentration | N_a | **κ** | Increased to suppress short-channel effects | | 5 | Gate oxide capacitance per area | C'_ox | **κ** | $C'_{ox}=\varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ | | 6 | Total gate capacitance | C_g | **1/κ** | Area shrinks faster than C'_ox grows | | 7 | Drain current | I_d | **1/κ** | $I_d \propto \mu C'_{ox} (W/L)(V_{dd}-V_{th})^2$ | | 8 | Gate / intrinsic delay | τ | **1/κ** | $\tau = C_g V_{dd}/I_d$ | | 9 | Clock frequency | f | **κ** | $f = 1/\tau$ | | 10 | Dynamic power / transistor | P_dyn | **1/κ²** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^2 f$ | | 11 | **Power density (chip-level)** | P/A | **1 (constant)** | **Key invariant** | | 12 | Power-delay product (energy) | PDP | **1/κ³** | Energy per switching event | | 13 | Current density | J | **1** | Constant | | 14 | Power-delay product per device | E_sw | **1/κ³** | Energy per switch | | 15 | Switching energy density | E_sw/area | **1/κ** | Improves with shrink | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The "constant field" in Dennard's law means subthreshold slope (S = (kT/q)·ln10 ≈ **60 mV/decade** at 300 K) does NOT scale — this is the *thermodynamic* limit that eventually collapses Dennard Scaling when V_th approaches ~0.3 V. --- ## 2.4 The Critical Power Equation (Dynamic) $$ P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f $$ Where: - $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (typically 0.1 – 0.2) - $C$ = switched capacitance per clock cycle - $V_{dd}$ = supply voltage - $f$ = clock frequency **Why this matters in engineering:** This equation governs everything from smartphone SoC design (Apple M-series at ~0.7–1.0 V) to data-center accelerators. Every modern low-power technique — DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling), clock gating, power gating, near-threshold computing — is an explicit attempt to manipulate one of these four variables because $\alpha$ alone cannot deliver the efficiency we need. --- ## 2.5 The Three Failure Modes of Dennard Scaling > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 2004–2006 Inflection Point — Why Dennard Scaling Broke** 1. **Subthreshold Leakage Explosion** As $V_{th}$ scaled to **~0.3 V**, the subthreshold leakage current became comparable to ON-state current. Static leakage went from <1% of total power to >40%. 2. **Gate Oxide Tunneling** At ~1.2 nm oxide thickness (≈5 SiO₂ monolayers), electrons **quantum-mechanically tunnel** through the oxide. Leakage current rose exponentially, defeating the $V_{dd}^2$ power savings. 3. **Threshold-Voltage Floor** $V_{th}$ cannot drop below ~10× the thermal voltage $kT/q$ ≈ **26 mV**, or the device cannot be turned OFF. Thus voltage scaling stalled around the 90 nm node (≈ 1.0 V). The industry response: **multi-core**, **heterogeneous compute**, **dark silicon**, **specialized accelerators (GPU, TPU, NPU)** — the entire post-2005 compute landscape is a direct consequence of Dennard Scaling's collapse. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: How Power Density Remains Constant We will now derive the central claim of Dennard Scaling from first principles using the canonical MOSFET equations. ### Step 1 — Define the starting (un-scaled) parameters Let the original MOSFET have: - Channel length **L₀**, width **W₀**, oxide thickness **t_ox,0** - Supply voltage **V_dd,0**, threshold voltage **V_th,0** - Gate capacitance per unit area $C'_{ox,0} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox,0}$ - Total gate capacitance **C₀ = C'_{ox,0}·W₀·L₀** - Drain current in saturation: $I_{d,0} = \frac{1}{2}\mu C'_{ox,0} (W_0/L_0)(V_{dd,0}-V_{th,0})^{2}$ ### Step 2 — Apply the scaling transformation (κ > 1, shrinkage) Under Dennard scaling: $$ L = L_0/\kappa, \quad W = W_0/\kappa, \quad t_{ox} = t_{ox,0}/\kappa $$ $$ V_{dd} = V_{dd,0}/\kappa, \quad V_{th} = V_{th,0}/\kappa, \quad N_a = \kappa N_{a,0} $$ ### Step 3 — Derive the new gate capacitance per unit area $$ C'_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox,0}/\kappa} = \kappa \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox,0}} = \kappa \cdot C'_{ox,0} $$ > **Logic:** A thinner oxide stores **more** charge per volt per unit area; hence $C'_{ox}$ scales *up* by κ. ### Step 4 — Derive the new total gate capacitance $$ C = C'_{ox}\cdot W \cdot L = (\kappa C'_{ox,0})\cdot \frac{W_0}{\kappa}\cdot \frac{L_0}{\kappa} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C &= C'_{ox,0}\cdot \frac{W_0 L_0}{\kappa} \\ &= \frac{C_0}{\kappa} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** The area shrinks by **1/κ²**, but the capacitance density rises by **κ**, so the net is **1/κ**. ### Step 5 — Derive the new drain current $$ I_d = \frac{1}{2}\mu\, \kappa C'_{ox,0} \cdot \frac{W_0/\kappa}{L_0/\kappa}\cdot \left(\frac{V_{dd,0}-V_{th,0}}{\kappa}\right)^{2} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} I_d &= \frac{1}{2}\mu\, \kappa C'_{ox,0} \cdot \frac{W_0}{L_0}\cdot \frac{(V_{dd,0}-V_{th,0})^{2}}{\kappa^{2}} \\ &= \frac{1}{\kappa}\cdot \frac{1}{2}\mu C'_{ox,0}\frac{W_0}{L_0}(V_{dd,0}-V_{th,0})^{2} \\ &= \frac{I_{d,0}}{\kappa} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** The κ in the numerator (from $C'_{ox}$) cancels one power of κ from $(V_{dd}-V_{th})^{2}$, leaving an overall **1/κ** in the denominator. ### Step 6 — Derive the gate (intrinsic) delay $$ \tau = \frac{C \cdot V_{dd}}{I_d} = \frac{(C_0/\kappa)(V_{dd,0}/\kappa)}{I_{d,0}/\kappa} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \tau &= \frac{C_0 V_{dd,0}}{I_{d,0}} \cdot \frac{1/\kappa^{2}}{1/\kappa} \\ &= \frac{C_0 V_{dd,0}}{I_{d,0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\kappa} \\ &= \frac{\tau_0}{\kappa} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Delay shrinks by **1/κ**, hence clock frequency $f = 1/\tau$ **grows by κ**. ### Step 7 — Derive the dynamic power per transistor $$ P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f = \alpha\cdot\frac{C_0}{\kappa}\cdot\frac{V_{dd,0}^{2}}{\kappa^{2}}\cdot\kappa f_0 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{dyn} &= \alpha C_0 V_{dd,0}^{2} f_0 \cdot \frac{1}{\kappa^{2}} \\ &= \frac{P_{dyn,0}}{\kappa^{2}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Power per gate falls by **1/κ²**. Of the four factors, $\alpha$ is invariant, $C$ contributes $1/\kappa$, $V_{dd}^{2}$ contributes $1/\kappa^{2}$, and $f$ contributes $\kappa$. Net: $1/\kappa \cdot 1/\kappa^{2} \cdot \kappa = 1/\kappa^{2}$. ### Step 8 — Derive the power density (the central invariant) The silicon area per transistor scales as $(1/\kappa)(1/\kappa) = 1/\kappa^{2}$. Therefore: $$ \frac{P_{dyn}}{A_{device}} = \frac{P_{dyn,0}/\kappa^{2}}{A_0/\kappa^{2}} = \frac{P_{dyn,0}}{A_0} $$ > **Logic:** Both numerator and denominator shrink by the same factor $\kappa^{2}$, so the **ratio is exactly 1 (constant)**. ✅ This is Dennard's central result. --- ## 3.2 Numerical Example (KTU-style Problem) > **Given:** A 90 nm process chip operates at $V_{dd}=1.0\,\text{V}$, $f=3\,\text{GHz}$, with average switched capacitance $C=100\,\text{fF}$ per macro and activity factor $\alpha=0.15$. The chip has $N=10^{8}$ switching macros per clock. We migrate to a 32 nm process using **Dennard constant-field scaling** with $\kappa=90/32 \approx 2.81$. > > **Find:** New $V_{dd}$, $f$, dynamic power per macro, and total chip power. Verify power density invariance. ### Solution **Step 1 — Compute the original dynamic power per macro** $$ P_{dyn,0} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f = 0.15 \times 100\times10^{-15}\times 1.0^{2}\times 3\times10^{9} $$ $$ P_{dyn,0} = 4.5\times10^{-5}\,\text{W} = 45\,\mu\text{W} $$ **Step 2 — Scale each quantity** $$ V_{dd}' = 1.0/2.81 \approx 0.356\,\text{V} $$ $$ f' = 3\,\text{GHz}\times 2.81 \approx 8.43\,\text{GHz} $$ $$ C' = 100\,\text{fF}/2.81 \approx 35.6\,\text{fF} $$ **Step 3 — Compute new dynamic power per macro** $$ P_{dyn}' = 0.15\times 35.6\times10^{-15}\times (0.356)^{2}\times 8.43\times10^{9} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{dyn}' &= 0.15\times 35.6\times10^{-15}\times 0.1267\times 8.43\times10^{9} \\ &= 5.70\times10^{-6}\,\text{W} \\ &\approx 5.70\,\mu\text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Check the 1/κ² law** $$ \frac{P_{dyn}'}{P_{dyn,0}} = \frac{1}{\kappa^{2}} = \frac{1}{2.81^{2}} = \frac{1}{7.90} \approx 0.127 $$ $$ P_{dyn}' = 45\,\mu\text{W} \times 0.127 \approx 5.7\,\mu\text{W} \quad\checkmark $$ **Step 5 — Total chip power** $$ P_{total} = N\cdot P_{dyn}' = 10^{8}\times 5.70\times10^{-6} = 570\,\text{W (per macro-class)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reality Check:** In real silicon this would melt! Dennard Scaling prescribes that *voltage must drop to keep power density constant*, but real chips often **could not drop voltage fast enough**, which is precisely why we hit the **"Power Wall" ~2004**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pre- vs Post-Dennard Era | Attribute | Pre-2004 (Dennard) | Post-2004 (Post-Dennard) | |-----------|--------------------|--------------------------| | Voltage scaling | $V_{dd}$ ↓ with each node | Stuck at ~0.7–1.0 V | | Frequency | ↑ ~40% per generation | Plateaued at 3–5 GHz | | Single-core ILP focus | Dominant | Diminishing returns | | Architecture | Monolithic single core | **Multi-core + accelerators** | | Power management | Static | **DVFS, clock/power gating** | | Process focus | Constant-field scaling | Equivalent scaling, FINFET, GAA | | Dark silicon % | 0% | **20–50%** of die | | Energy efficiency driver | Process shrink | **Architecture + specialization** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Dennard Scaling Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Original MOSFET Process
L0 W0 tox0 Vdd0] --> B[Apply Scaling Factor kappa greater than 1
Geometric shrink 1 over kappa] B --> C[Dimensions L W tox scale 1 over kappa] C --> D[Capacitance per unit area Cox prime scales kappa] D --> E[Total gate capacitance Cg scales 1 over kappa] B --> F[Voltage Vdd Vth scales 1 over kappa
Constant field rule] F --> G[Drain current Id scales 1 over kappa] E --> H[Gate delay tau scales 1 over kappa] H --> I[Clock frequency f scales kappa] F --> J[Dynamic power Pdyn scales 1 over kappa squared] C --> K[Silicon area A scales 1 over kappa squared] J --> L[Power density P over A equals CONSTANT] K --> L L --> M[End of Dennard Regime 2004
Leakage and threshold floor] M --> N[Multi core Dark Silicon
Heterogeneous Architecture] N --> O[DVFS Near Threshold Compute
Accelerators TPU GPU NPU] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Scaling Parameter Interactions ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Geometric_Scaling G1[Length L 1 over kappa] --> G2[Width W 1 over kappa] G2 --> G3[Oxide tox 1 over kappa] G3 --> G4[Doping Na times kappa] end subgraph Electrical_Scaling E1[Vdd 1 over kappa] --> E2[Vth 1 over kappa] E2 --> E3[Current Id 1 over kappa] E3 --> E4[Capacitance Cg 1 over kappa] end subgraph Performance_Output P1[Delay tau 1 over kappa] --> P2[Frequency f times kappa] P2 --> P3[Power per device 1 over kappa squared] P3 --> P4[Power density CONSTANT 1] end G4 --> E1 E4 --> P1 P4 --> Q[Dennard Invariant Achieved] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Timeline — The Collapse of Dennard Scaling ```mermaid flowchart TD T1[1974 Dennard paper formulates
constant field scaling] --> T2[1985 to 2000 Golden Age
frequency rises 5 MHz to 2 GHz] T2 --> T3[2001 to 2004 Voltage scaling slows
Power wall emerges] T3 --> T4[2004 to 2006 Industry pivots
Dual core multi core era begins] T4 --> T5[2010s Dark silicon accelerators
Heterogeneous computing] T5 --> T6[2020s Chiplets 3D stacking
Domain specific architectures] T6 --> T7[Future Near threshold compute
Photonic and quantum beyond CMOS] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — Causes of Dennard Breakdown ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Dennard Scaling Breakdown] --> R1[Subthreshold leakage
Vth cannot go below ~10 kT over q] R --> R2[Gate oxide tunneling
tox less than 1.5 nm] R --> R3[Process variability
Doping fluctuations] R --> R4[Interconnect delay
Wires do not scale like devices] R1 --> S1[Static power dominates] R2 --> S1 R3 --> S2[Yield and reliability issues] R4 --> S3[Frequency ceiling reached] S1 --> X1[Cannot increase clock] S2 --> X1 S3 --> X1 X1 --> Y[Multi core parallel architecture] Y --> Z[Heterogeneous compute and accelerators] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each arrow represents a *causal* or *scaling* relationship, not a logical flow. The "End of Dennard Regime 2004" node is the **inflection point** that defines the entire post-2005 computer architecture research agenda — a topic that the rest of Module 4 (parallelism, ILP, multi-core) explicitly addresses. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]** **State Dennard Scaling Law. List the scaling factors for (i) supply voltage, (ii) gate capacitance, and (iii) power density when device dimensions are scaled by a factor κ.** #### Model Answer (3 marks): Dennard Scaling Law (1974) states that as MOSFET device dimensions are uniformly shrunk by a scaling factor κ > 1, the supply voltage, threshold voltage, and doping concentrations are also adjusted so that the electric field inside the device remains constant and the **power density of the chip remains constant**. | Quantity | Scaling Factor | |----------|----------------| | Supply voltage $V_{dd}$ | **1/κ** | | Gate capacitance $C_g$ | **1/κ** | | **Power density** (W/cm²) | **1 (constant)** | *(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for two correct factors, 1 mark for the third.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary]** **Why did Dennard Scaling break down around 2004–2006? Mention any two reasons.** #### Model Answer (3 marks): Dennard Scaling broke down because the assumptions underlying constant-field scaling could no longer be sustained: 1. **Threshold-voltage floor (~10 × kT/q ≈ 260 mV at 300 K):** $V_{th}$ cannot be reduced further without making the transistor unable to turn OFF, so voltage scaling stalled. 2. **Gate-oxide tunneling leakage:** Below ~1.5 nm oxide thickness, electrons quantum-mechanically tunnel through the SiO₂, producing exponential leakage current and destroying the $V_{dd}^2$ power savings. *(1.5 marks per reason — include the third reason "interconnect delay not scaling with device" optionally for full marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Q. Sample]** > *(a)* Derive the scaling relations for drain current, gate capacitance, and intrinsic delay of a MOSFET under Dennard's constant-field scaling. **(7 marks)** > *(b)* Explain why Dennard Scaling ended and how it influenced modern multi-core processor design. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Under scaling by factor κ, physical dimensions $L, W, t_{ox}$ scale as $1/\kappa$, while $V_{dd}, V_{th}$ scale as $1/\kappa$ and doping $N_a$ scales as κ. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Gate capacitance per unit area: $$ C'_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \kappa \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox,0}} = \kappa \cdot C'_{ox,0} $$ Total gate capacitance: $$ C_g = C'_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L = \kappa C'_{ox,0} \cdot \frac{W_0 L_0}{\kappa^{2}} = \frac{C_{g,0}}{\kappa} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Drain current (long-channel saturation): $$ I_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu C'_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{dd}-V_{th})^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu\,(\kappa C'_{ox,0})\frac{W_0}{L_0}\frac{(V_{dd,0}-V_{th,0})^{2}}{\kappa^{2}} = \frac{I_{d,0}}{\kappa} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Intrinsic delay: $$ \tau = \frac{C_g V_{dd}}{I_d} = \frac{(C_{g,0}/\kappa)(V_{dd,0}/\kappa)}{I_{d,0}/\kappa} = \frac{C_{g,0} V_{dd,0}}{I_{d,0}}\cdot\frac{1}{\kappa} = \frac{\tau_0}{\kappa} $$ ### Part (b) — Why Dennard Scaling Ended (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks):** Dennard Scaling ended due to three physical limits: - Subthreshold leakage (V_th floor ≈ 260 mV) - Gate-oxide tunneling (tox < 1.5 nm) - Interconnect delay not scaling **Step 2 (2 marks):** As $V_{dd}$ stopped falling, $P \propto V_{dd}^{2}$ broke, and per-transistor dynamic power could not be reduced — single-core frequency plateaued at ~3–5 GHz (the **Power Wall**). **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Industry response — **multi-core**: since per-core power could not be cut further, designers placed multiple slower cores on the die, exploiting thread-level parallelism (TLP) instead of instruction-level parallelism (ILP). **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Modern consequences — **dark silicon** (transistors that cannot all be powered simultaneously), **heterogeneous computing** (CPU + GPU + NPU), **DVFS**, **near-threshold / sub-threshold computing**, **accelerators (TPU, DPU, FPGA)**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q. Sample]** > *(a)* A CMOS inverter has switched capacitance $C = 50\,\text{fF}$, activity factor $\alpha = 0.2$, supply voltage $V_{dd} = 1.2\,\text{V}$ and operates at $f = 2\,\text{GHz}$. The process is migrated to a new node with scaling factor $\kappa = 1.5$. Compute the new dynamic power per inverter and verify power-density invariance. **(7 marks)** > *(b)* Discuss the architectural implications of the breakdown of Dennard Scaling on contemporary processor design. Include Amdahl's Law considerations. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Numerical (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Original dynamic power per inverter: $$ P_{dyn,0} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f = 0.2\times 50\times 10^{-15}\times (1.2)^{2}\times 2\times 10^{9} $$ $$ P_{dyn,0} = 2.88\times 10^{-5}\,\text{W} = 28.8\,\mu\text{W} $$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Scaled parameters at new node: $$ V_{dd}' = 1.2/1.5 = 0.8\,\text{V} $$ $$ f' = 2\,\text{GHz}\times 1.5 = 3\,\text{GHz} $$ $$ C' = 50\,\text{fF}/1.5 \approx 33.3\,\text{fF} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** New dynamic power: $$ P_{dyn}' = 0.2\times 33.3\times 10^{-15}\times (0.8)^{2}\times 3\times 10^{9} $$ $$ P_{dyn}' = 1.28\times 10^{-5}\,\text{W} \approx 12.8\,\mu\text{W} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Verification of the $1/\kappa^{2}$ law: $$ \frac{P_{dyn}'}{P_{dyn,0}} = \frac{12.8}{28.8} = 0.444 \approx \frac{1}{1.5^{2}} = \frac{1}{2.25} = 0.444 \quad\checkmark $$ Area also scales as $1/\kappa^{2} = 0.444$, so **power density $P/A$ = constant (1.0)** — Dennard's invariant holds. ### Part (b) — Architectural Implications (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks):** After 2004, single-core frequency scaling ended. Designers shifted from **ILP** (deep pipelines, OoO) to **TLP** via multi-core chips (Intel Core 2 Duo → 64-core Xeon, AMD EPYC, ARM big.LITTLE). **Step 2 (2 marks):** **Amdahl's Law** constrains parallel speedup: $$ S(N) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{N}} $$ where *f* is the serial fraction. Even with *N* → ∞, speedup is bounded by $1/f$. This forces architects to (a) minimize serial code, (b) use specialized accelerators for hard-to-parallelize kernels (e.g., SIMD, vector units, GPUs for data-parallel kernels). **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **Dark silicon problem:** As κ shrinks without voltage scaling, a fraction of the die (20–50% in modern FinFET designs) **cannot be powered simultaneously** within thermal design power (TDP). Solutions: power gating, near-threshold operation, heterogeneous cores. **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** The post-Dennard paradigm is **energy-first design**: architectures are now optimized for performance-per-watt (perf/W) and performance-per-joule, not raw frequency. This drives chiplets, 3D-stacked memory (HBM), and **domain-specific architectures** (DSA) such as Google's TPU, Apple's Neural Engine, NVIDIA Tensor Cores. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Confusing "constant power" with "constant power density":** The *per-transistor* dynamic power scales as **1/κ²** (it DOES decrease), but the *per-area* power density stays constant. Many students wrongly claim "Dennard says power stays the same." > 2. **Forgetting the activity factor α:** Dynamic power is $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$. If α is given in a numerical problem and omitted, you lose 1 mark instantly. > 3. **Mixing up "frequency scales by κ" with "delay scales by κ":** Frequency **rises** by κ, while delay **falls** by κ. Examiners explicitly check this sign convention. > 4. **Year confusion:** Dennard's 1974 paper is the *formulation*, but the **breakdown is 2004–2006**, not 2000 and not 2010. > 5. **Subthreshold slope value:** If asked, the thermal subthreshold slope is **S = (kT/q)·ln10 ≈ 60 mV/decade at 300 K (room temperature)**. Not 60 mV total — it's per decade of current. > 6. **The Power Wall is a consequence, not a cause:** Do not say "Dennard Scaling ended because of the power wall." Correct phrasing: "Dennard Scaling ended due to leakage/threshold limits, which manifested architecturally as the power wall." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - 📌 **Dennard Scaling (1974)** = constant-field geometric scaling of MOSFETs. - 📌 **Three coupled assumptions:** geometric (1/κ), electrical (1/κ), doping (×κ). - 📌 **Power density is the invariant** — not power itself. - 📌 Per-transistor dynamic power scales as **1/κ²**. - 📌 Frequency scales as **κ**; delay scales as **1/κ**. - 📌 Energy per switching event (PDP) scales as **1/κ³**. - 📌 The equation to memorize: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$. - 📌 **Broke down 2004–2006** due to V_th floor, gate tunneling, interconnect delay. - 📌 Subthreshold slope limit = **60 mV/decade at 300 K**. - 📌 Industry response: **multi-core**, **dark silicon**, **heterogeneous / domain-specific architecture**. - 📌 Amdahl's Law becomes the new binding constraint, replacing Dennard. - 📌 Modern metric: **performance-per-watt**, not raw GHz. - 📌 Key constants in bold: **V_dd, V_th, t_ox, κ, P_density, kT/q ≈ 26 mV**. - 📌 Always state units: V, nm, fF, GHz, W/cm². - 📌 For numerical problems: show the **1/κ²** verification explicitly — examiners reward it.

topics.title: Dark Silicon and the Transition Towards Heterogeneous Architectures
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dark Silicon and the Transition Towards Heterogeneous Architectures

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dark Silicon** is formally defined as the *aggregate amount of chip area that, at a given point in time, must remain unpowered (or be powered below the nominal operating voltage) because doing otherwise would exceed the chip's Thermal Design Power (TDP) budget.* The term was coined in the seminal 2010 paper by Esmaeilzadeh, Blem, St. Amant, Sankaralingam, and Burger (ISCA'10 — *"Dark Silicon and the End of Multicore Scaling"*).

Mathematically, if $A_{total}$ is the total chip area and $A_{active}$ is the area that can be simultaneously switched at the nominal frequency, then:

$$\text{Dark Silicon Fraction} = \frac{A_{total} - A_{active}}{A_{total}}$$

This fraction is **growing exponentially** with each process technology node (projected to exceed **50%** at sub-22 nm nodes and approach **80–90%** at 11 nm and below).

## 1.2 The Origin: The Three Walls

Dark Silicon is the convergence of three physical and architectural walls:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Power Wall** — Dennard Scaling broke down around 2005. Voltage ($V_{dd}$) no longer scales linearly with feature size because of sub-threshold leakage current ($I_{off}$). Power density therefore **rises** rather than staying constant.
> - **ILP Wall (Instruction-Level Parallelism Wall)** — The limit on extracting parallelism from a single thread (roughly 4–8 wide issue) was reached around the late 1990s.
> **Utilization Wall / Memory Wall (off-chip bandwidth)** — Multicore processors saturate the limited off-chip pins and DRAM bandwidth, leaving cores idle even when transistors are available.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel Electricity Problem"

Imagine a luxury hotel (your chip) with **1000 rooms** (transistors) and a single power line supplying exactly **500 kWh** (TDP). As the owner, you want every room lit and air-conditioned. But the central breaker will trip if total draw exceeds 500.

> **Room lights + A/C = High Power Draw** → Some rooms must stay dark.

Now, suppose you install **solar panels** (heterogeneous cores/accelerators) that deliver extreme performance-per-watt for *specific* tasks. You no longer need to light every room — only the rooms whose *purpose* requires full power. That is precisely what **Heterogeneous Architectures** do: trade-off generality for energy efficiency, leaving the bulk of the silicon deliberately dark most of the time, but illuminating the right cores for the right job.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Key Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metrics You Must Memorise:**
> - **Dennard Constant-Power Scaling Factor:** Voltage scales as $V \propto s^{-\alpha}$, where $\alpha \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ (not the ideal 1.0).
> - **Doubles-Per-Generation Clock Penalty:** $f \propto s^{0.5}$.
> - **Energy per Switch (Dynamic):** $E_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2$.
> - **Static Power Density (leakage):** $P_{static} = V_{dd} \cdot I_{off} \propto V_{dd} \cdot 10^{-V_t / S}$ where **S = sub-threshold slope ≈ 60 mV/decade** at room temperature.
> - **TDP (Thermal Design Power):** Industry-standard chip envelope = **≈ 100 W (laptop/desktop), 15–45 W (mobile), 1–5 W (tablet).**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics: Why Transistors Went "Dark"

### 2.1.1 Classical Dennard Scaling (Pre-2005)

Dennard's 1974 rule assumed *all four* primary parameters (dimension, voltage, doping, frequency) scale together by a factor $s > 1$ per generation. The **power density remained constant**:

$$\frac{P}{A} \propto \frac{C \cdot V^2 \cdot f}{A} = \text{constant}$$

This held beautifully from the 1 µm node down to **90 nm**. Designers could crank frequency and transistor count, and the chip stayed cool.

### 2.1.2 The Breakdown (Post-2005)

Three forces collided:
1. **Sub-threshold leakage** ($I_{off} = I_0 \cdot e^{-V_{gs}/S}$) blew up because $V_{dd}$ could not go below $\approx 0.7\text{ V}$ (the silicon bandgap constraint of the MOSFET body factor).
2. **Gate-oxide tunneling current** appeared below **2 nm** $t_{ox}$.
3. **Process variation** at small geometries caused $V_t$ mismatches across the die, making uniform voltage scaling dangerous.

The empirical replacement relationship became:

$$V_{dd}(s) \propto s^{-0.5 \text{ to } -0.7} \quad \text{instead of} \quad V_{dd}(s) \propto s^{-1}$$

Plugging $V \propto s^{-0.5}$ into the power equation yields **power density growth ≈ $s^{0.5}$ per generation**, doubling every two process generations.

### 2.1.3 The Dark Silicon Projection (Esmaeilzadeh et al., 2010)

| Process Node | Projected Dark Silicon Fraction |
|--------------|--------------------------------|
| 90 nm | ~ 21 % |
| 65 nm | ~ 30 % |
| 45 nm | ~ 40 % |
| 32 nm | ~ 50 % |
| 22 nm | ~ 58 % |
| 16 nm | ~ 65 % |
| 11 nm | ~ 75 % |
| 8 nm  | ~ 80 % |

> [!NOTE]
> The numbers above are derived under the assumption of constant TDP envelope and continued transistor doubling, validated against ITRS roadmaps.

## 2.2 The Strategic Response: Transition to Heterogeneous Architectures

If you cannot power all transistors at the same voltage/frequency, the *only* logical engineering response is **specialisation** — allocate the dark silicon to domain-specific accelerators whose **performance-per-watt is 10×–1000×** that of a general-purpose core.

### 2.2.1 Spectrum of Heterogeneity (KTU Classification)

| Level | Type | Example | Energy Efficiency Gain |
|-------|------|---------|------------------------|
| L1 | ISA-Homogeneous, Micro-arch. Heterogeneous | Intel Alder Lake (P-cores + E-cores) | 1.5×–3× |
| L2 | ISA-Compatibility, Specialised Blocks | CPU + GPU (Intel Core i-series) | 5×–20× |
| L3 | Domain Accelerators | TPU, NPU, Video Encoder/Decoder | 50×–500× |
| L4 | Full ASIC / Custom Silicon | Apple Silicon (Neural Engine), Google TPUv5 | 500×–5000× |

### 2.2.2 Dark Silicon Mitigation Strategies (Esmaeilzadeh Taxonomy)

1. **Near-Threshold / Sub-Threshold Computing** — Reduce $V_{dd}$ to $\leq 0.5 V$ for some cores; energy drops quadratically ($\sim V^2$) but speed also falls, so only throughput-oriented cores are candidates.
2. **Software-Defined Acceleration** — Map hot loops onto reconfigurable fabric (FPGA / CGRA / coarse-grained array).
3. **Heterogeneous Specialisation** — CPU + GPU + ASIC blocks.
4. **Core Salvaging / Defect Tolerance** — Use redundant dark cores as spares when neighbouring active cores age.
5. **DRAM-centric / Processing-in-Memory (PIM)** — Move compute into memory die to amortise the data-movement energy, the dominant cost in modern workloads.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is **the single most-examined list** on this topic in the KTU 2024 scheme. Memorise the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic switching power | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $P_{static} = V_{dd} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Leakage (sub-threshold + gate) | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $E_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2$ | Energy per transition | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $\text{EDP} = E \cdot t = \frac{C \cdot V_{dd}^2}{f}$ | Energy-Delay Product | J·s |
| 5 | $\text{ED}^2\text{P} = E \cdot t^2$ | Energy-Delay-Squared (perf-opt.) | J·s² |
| 6 | $\text{Perf}_{\text{het}} = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot a_i$ | Heterogeneous speed-up (i = accelerator) | — |
| 7 | $S_{het} = \dfrac{1}{\left(1-\sum_i a_i\right) + \sum_i \dfrac{a_i}{s_i}}$ | Amdahl for heterogeneous cores | Fraction |
| 8 | $A_{dark} = A_{total}\left(1 - \frac{P_{TDP}}{P_{active,total}}\right)$ | Dark area from TDP limit | mm² |
| 9 | $\eta_{util} = \dfrac{\sum_i \text{CorePower}_i}{P_{TDP}}$ | Utilisation factor (≤ 1) | Unitless |
| 10 | $\text{Perf}/W = \dfrac{\text{Throughput}}{P_{total}}$ | Energy efficiency metric | ops/J |

> **Note on table syntax:** Absolute values (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are written with `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Mobile SoCs (Apple M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, MediaTek Dimensity):** Big cores for latency-critical threads, small cores for background, NPU for matrix/tensor ops. Roughly 70 % of the silicon is dark at any instant.
> - **Datacenter Accelerators (Google TPU, AWS Trainium, NVIDIA H100):** Heterogeneous mix of CUDA cores, Tensor cores, video encoders, and DPUs. TDP is fixed at 700 W; if not for specialisation, throughput would be 1/10 of present day.
> - **Edge AI (TinyML):** Fully embraces dark silicon — microcontroller wakes only for inference bursts, sleeping the rest of the time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Dark Silicon Fraction

### 3.1.1 Start from Power Density Equation

For a CMOS circuit operating at clock frequency $f$:

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$$

Let $N$ denote the number of simultaneously switching gates (proportional to active area $A_{active}$), and let $\rho$ denote power density per gate. Then:

$$P_{active} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f = \rho \cdot A_{active}$$

### 3.1.2 Impose TDP Constraint

The chip must satisfy:

$$P_{active} \leq P_{TDP}$$

Therefore:

$$A_{active} \leq \frac{P_{TDP}}{\rho}$$

### 3.1.3 Solve for Dark Silicon

If $A_{total}$ is the total silicon area made available by process scaling, the dark fraction is:

$$\boxed{\text{Dark Fraction} = 1 - \frac{A_{active}}{A_{total}} = 1 - \frac{P_{TDP}}{\rho \cdot A_{total}}}$$

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** $A_{total} = 400 \text{ mm}^2$, $P_{TDP} = 95 \text{ W}$, $\rho = 0.75 \text{ W/mm}^2$ at 22 nm node.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{active} & = \frac{P_{TDP}}{\rho} = \frac{95 \text{ W}}{0.75 \text{ W/mm}^2} \\
           & = 126.67 \text{ mm}^2 \\[4pt]
\text{Dark Fraction} & = 1 - \frac{126.67}{400} \\
                     & = 1 - 0.3167 \\
                     & = 0.6833 \\
                     & \approx 68.3 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Over two-thirds of the 22 nm die must remain dark.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why $V_{dd}$ Cannot Be Scaled Below ~0.7 V

Sub-threshold current is governed by:

$$I_{off} = I_0 \cdot 10^{-V_t / S}$$

where $S$ is the sub-threshold swing. The OFF-state power per gate is therefore:

$$P_{off} = V_{dd} \cdot I_{off} = V_{dd} \cdot I_0 \cdot 10^{-V_t / S}$$

For $V_{dd} \to 0$, $V_t$ must scale in proportion (so that the gate still turns *off* properly). However, $S$ is bounded from below by the *Boltzmann tyranny*:

$$S \geq \underbrace{\frac{k_B T}{q} \ln 10}_{\approx 60 \text{ mV/dec at 300 K}}$$

This floor imposes a lower bound on $V_{dd} \approx 0.6$–$0.7$ V (≈ 10 S). Hence $V_{dd}$ stagnation.

## 3.3 Derivation of Amdahl's Heterogeneous Speed-up

Let $a_i$ be the fraction of execution that can be offloaded to accelerator $i$ with speed-up $s_i$. The heterogeneous parallel fraction is:

$$\sigma = \sum_i a_i$$

The serial fraction is:

$$1 - \sigma = 1 - \sum_i a_i$$

Per the Amdahl model extended for multiple accelerators:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{het} & = \frac{1}{(1 - \sigma) + \sum_i \frac{a_i}{s_i}} \\[4pt]
        & = \frac{1}{1 - \sum_i a_i + \sum_i \frac{a_i}{s_i}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.1 Worked Example

Suppose a workload runs on a homogeneous quad-core CPU at normalised speed 1. We accelerate a 30 % portion on a GPU with speed-up $s_1 = 20$ and a 15 % portion on an NPU with speed-up $s_2 = 50$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma & = 0.30 + 0.15 = 0.45 \\
1 - \sigma & = 0.55 \\[4pt]
\sum_i \frac{a_i}{s_i} & = \frac{0.30}{20} + \frac{0.15}{50} \\
                       & = 0.015 + 0.003 \\
                       & = 0.018 \\[4pt]
S_{het} & = \frac{1}{0.55 + 0.018} = \frac{1}{0.568} \approx 1.76
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even *very* fast accelerators deliver only ~1.76× end-to-end speed-up because the serial fraction (55 %) dominates. The dark silicon on the CPU side is therefore mandatory to absorb the serial fraction at high frequency.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Energy-Delay Trade-off

Let $V_{dd}$ be scaled by a factor $k \in (0, 1]$ while preserving circuit topology (so $\alpha$, $C$, $V_t$, $S$ stay constant).

Dynamic energy and delay:

$$
E \propto V_{dd}^2, \qquad t_d \propto \frac{V_{dd}}{(V_{dd} - V_t)^\beta}
$$

where $\beta \approx 1.3$ (velocity saturation regime). Substituting $V_{dd}' = k V_{dd}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E' & = k^2 E \\
t_d' & = \frac{k V_{dd}}{(k V_{dd} - V_t)^\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

The new **EDP** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EDP}' & = E' \cdot t_d' \\
            & = \frac{k^3 \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot E}{(k V_{dd} - V_t)^\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating and solving $\dfrac{d(\text{EDP}')}{dk} = 0$ yields the **EDP-optimal operating point** $k_{opt} \approx 0.5$, i.e. $V_{dd, opt} \approx 0.5 V_{dd,nom}$. This is the rationale behind *near-threshold computing* (NTC) chips such as Intel's Claremont.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Strategy vs. Performance vs. Dark Silicon

| Strategy | Dark Silicon Mitigation | Performance Gain | Engineering Cost |
|----------|-------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** | Low (0–10 %) | 1.2×–1.4× | Low |
| **Multicore (homogeneous)** | Low (10–20 %) | 2×–4× (Amdahl-limited) | Low |
| **Big.LITTLE (ISA-homogeneous)** | Medium (30–40 %) | 1.5×–3× perf/W | Medium |
| **CPU + GPU (heterogeneous)** | High (40–60 %) | 5×–20× on hot kernels | Medium–High |
| **ASIC / Domain Accelerator** | Very High (60–80 %) | 50×–500× perf/W | High (NRE) |
| **Near-Threshold Computing** | Very High (50–70 %) | 2×–4× perf/W | Very High (variation) |
| **PIM / Processing-in-Memory** | Extreme (70–90 %) | 10×–100× (data-bound) | Very High (3D-stacking) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The "Three Walls" Causality Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Moore Law - Transistor Doubling] --> B[Dennard Scaling Breakdown 2005]
    B --> C[Power Density Rises per node]
    C --> D[TDP Envelope is Fixed]
    D --> E[Dark Silicon Area]
    A --> F[ILP Wall]
    F --> G[Multicore Era]
    G --> D
    A --> H[Memory and Utilization Wall]
    H --> G
    E --> I[Heterogeneous Specialisation]
    I --> J[CPU plus GPU plus ASIC plus NPU]
    I --> K[Near Threshold Computing]
    I --> L[Processing in Memory]
    J --> M[Sustainable Performance per Watt]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: A Modern Heterogeneous SoC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workload Dispatcher] --> B[Big Core A78]
    A --> C[Small Core A55]
    A --> D[GPU Mali]
    A --> E[NPU Tensor Unit]
    A --> F[Video Encoder Decoder]
    A --> G[Display Controller]
    A --> H[Modem DSP]
    B --> I[Shared L3 Cache]
    C --> I
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[DRAM Controller]
    J --> K[System DRAM LPDDR5]
    L[TDP Power Budget 5 to 15 W] -.throttles.-> A
```

**Reading guide:**
- Solid arrows: data flow.
- Dotted arrow: *thermal throttling* signal from the **TDP Power Budget** block, which dynamically gates unused cores (creating *runtime* dark silicon).
- At any instant, **only 1–3 of the 8–12 functional blocks are active**. The rest are dark.

## 4.3 Mapping Between Concept, Cause and Cure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CauseA[Transistor Density Doubles] --> EffectA[Power Density Increases]
    CauseB[Voltage Scaling Stagnates at 0.7 V] --> EffectA
    CauseC[Leakage Current Rises] --> EffectA
    EffectA --> Wall[Power Wall]
    Wall --> Dark[Dark Silicon]
    Dark --> CureA[Heterogeneous Architecture]
    Dark --> CureB[Near Threshold Voltage]
    Dark --> CureC[Specialised Accelerators]
    Dark --> CureD[Processing in Memory]
    CureA --> Goal[Sustainable Performance per Watt]
    CureB --> Goal
    CureC --> Goal
    CureD --> Goal
```

## 4.4 Trade-off Triangle: Performance vs. Power vs. Flexibility

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[High Performance] --> Tradeoff{Architectural Trade off}
    W[Low Power] --> Tradeoff
    Flex[High Flexibility] --> Tradeoff
    Tradeoff --> C1[CPU - Flexible but Power hungry]
    Tradeoff --> C2[GPU - Balanced]
    Tradeoff --> C3[ASIC - Fast and efficient but Inflexible]
    C1 --> DS1[Dark Silicon on idle cores]
    C2 --> DS2[Dark Silicon under bad kernel]
    C3 --> DS3[Dark Silicon when workload changes]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**Define dark silicon. List any two consequences of dark silicon for modern processor design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Dark silicon refers to the fraction of chip transistors that must remain powered off (or below nominal voltage) at any given time due to the Thermal Design Power (TDP) envelope, first formalised by Esmaeilzadeh et al. (2010).
>
> **Consequence 1 (1 mark):** *Forced transition to heterogeneous architectures* — designers cannot power all cores simultaneously, so dark silicon is filled with specialised accelerators (GPU, NPU, ASIC).
>
> **Consequence 2 (1 mark):** *Exponentially growing fraction of unused area* — projected to exceed 80 % at sub-11 nm nodes, dramatically raising per-transistor cost.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`
**State the relationship between dynamic power and supply voltage. Why is reducing $V_{dd}$ the most effective knob for energy savings?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The dynamic power equation is:

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$$

Since $V_{dd}$ appears **quadratically**, halving $V_{dd}$ reduces power by **4×** (1 mark). Simultaneously, the switching energy $E = \alpha C V_{dd}^2$ drops by 4× per transition (1 mark). However, leakage $I_{off}$ grows exponentially as $V_t$ shrinks, and the sub-threshold slope $S \geq 60$ mV/decade imposes a hard floor near $V_{dd} \approx 0.6\text{–}0.7$ V (1 mark).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three architectural walls** (power wall, ILP wall, memory/utilisation wall) that converged around 2005 to trigger the dark-silicon phenomenon. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a **neat block diagram**, show how a modern heterogeneous SoC allocates TDP across CPU cores, GPU, NPU and specialised accelerators. Justify why this design *embraces* rather than *avoids* dark silicon. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Three Walls (7 marks):**

1. **Power Wall (3 marks):** Dennard scaling required voltage to shrink as $V \propto 1/s$ per generation. Post-2005, leakage current and the Boltzmann tyranny ($S \geq 60$ mV/decade) forced $V_{dd}$ to stagnate near 0.7 V. Power density rose as $s^{0.5}$, doubling every two nodes. Designers could no longer crank frequency *and* transistor count within a fixed TDP.

2. **ILP Wall (2 marks):** Wide-issue, deep-pipelined superscalar cores plateaued at 4–8 IPC around 2000 (Hennessy-Patterson; data from SPEC benchmarks). Extracting more ILP required exponentially more energy and transistors (the *Diminishing Returns* of Rau's 1989 study).

3. **Memory/Utilisation Wall (2 marks):** Off-chip pin bandwidth and DRAM latency improved at only ~10 % per year, lagging Moore's Law (~40 %/year). Adding cores faster than bandwidth saturates leaves cores utilisation-poor — the *utilisation wall*.

**Conclusion:** These walls collectively produced the multicore era (2005–2015), but multicore alone cannot scale forever because of the power wall — leading to dark silicon.

**(b) — Heterogeneous SoC Block Diagram (7 marks):**

The student should sketch a figure similar to Section 4.2 of these notes, with the following **mandatory** labels and a 1-page explanation covering:

| Block | TDP Share (typical mobile SoC) | Justification |
|-------|-------------------------------|---------------|
| Big CPU core | ~ 20 % | Single-thread latency-critical |
| Small CPU cores (cluster of 4) | ~ 5 % | Background tasks |
| GPU | ~ 25 % | Throughput-parallel graphics/ML |
| NPU | ~ 20 % | Matrix-multiply, INT8/BF16 inference |
| Video codec | ~ 5 % | Bulk data movement offload |
| DRAM controller & I/O | ~ 10 % | Always-on |
| **Dark** | ~ 15 % buffer + spares | Hot-spare cores, defect tolerance |

**Justification (4 marks):** Heterogeneous design *embraces* dark silicon because the high-performance cores are *sized* to fit the TDP envelope; the GPU and NPU deliver much higher Performance-per-Watt for their respective domains, justifying their silicon area even though they will be dark for non-matching workloads. The utilisation factor $\eta_{util}$ rises from < 30 % (homogeneous) to > 60 % (heterogeneous). The "dark" budget is reserved for **runtime DVFS, fault tolerance, and future workload adaptation**.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**(a)** Derive the **Amdahl's Law extension for heterogeneous systems** and apply it to a workload where 30 % is accelerated on a GPU ($s = 20\times$) and 15 % on an NPU ($s = 50\times$). Compute the end-to-end speed-up. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of near-threshold computing (NTC)** and **processing-in-memory (PIM)** as dark-silicon mitigation strategies, with at least one quantitative justification each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Amdahl Heterogeneous Derivation (7 marks):**

Derivation as in **Section 3.3** of these notes. Final formula (2 marks):

$$S_{het} = \frac{1}{(1 - \sigma) + \sum_i \dfrac{a_i}{s_i}}$$

Substituting $a_1 = 0.30$, $s_1 = 20$, $a_2 = 0.15$, $s_2 = 50$ (3 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma & = 0.45, \quad 1 - \sigma = 0.55 \\
\sum_i \frac{a_i}{s_i} & = \frac{0.30}{20} + \frac{0.15}{50} \\
                       & = 0.015 + 0.003 = 0.018 \\
S_{het} & = \frac{1}{0.568} \approx 1.76
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Only 1.76× end-to-end speed-up despite 20×–50× accelerator speed-ups — the serial 55 % dominates. This is the *Amdahl bottleneck* and motivates offloading *more* of the workload through compiler/runtime scheduling.

**(b) — NTC and PIM (7 marks):**

**Near-Threshold Computing (3 marks):**

NTC operates at $V_{dd} \approx 0.4\text{–}0.5$ V (vs. nominal 1.0 V). Energy per op drops as $V_{dd}^2$, so a 50 % voltage reduction yields **~4× lower dynamic energy**. EDP-optimal point (Section 3.4) lies at $V_{dd,opt} \approx 0.5 V_{dd,nom}$. Caveats: (i) delay increases by 10×, (ii) variability worsens as $V_t$ variation becomes comparable to $V_{dd}$, (iii) leakage becomes significant — hence used only for throughput cores.

**Processing-in-Memory (4 marks):**

Modern workloads are *memory-bound*; data movement can consume 100×–1000× more energy than the compute itself. PIM embeds simple ALUs/tensor units inside the DRAM die (e.g., Samsung HBM-PIM, SK Hynix AiM). Quantitative benefit (per [Mutlu et al., 2022]):

$$\text{Energy}_{\text{data-move}} = C_{wire} \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot n_{hops}$$

Reducing $n_{hops}$ from 5–10 (off-chip) to 1 (in-memory) cuts data-movement energy by **5×–10×**, leaving 60–80 % of the silicon dark most of the time but delivering 10×–100× efficiency on memory-resident workloads (graph analytics, ML inference).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Dark-Silicon Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing dark silicon with "defective silicon."** Dark silicon is *deliberately* unpowered — *not* faulty. Writing "dark silicon = yield loss" loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet.** Examiners often allocate ½ mark just for writing *Watts*, *Joules*, or *mm²*.
> 3. **Skipping the Boltzmann tyranny line** ($S \geq 60$ mV/decade) when explaining why $V_{dd}$ cannot scale further — this is the *single most-tested line* in viva voce.
> 4. **Mixing up EDP vs. ED²P.** Energy-Delay Product vs. Energy-Delay-Squared Product. Use EDP for energy-critical (mobile), ED²P for performance-critical (server).
> 5. **In Amdahl problems, forgetting to verify that $\sigma = \sum_i a_i < 1$.** If students submit $\sigma = 1.2$, marks are docked.
> 6. **Stating "heterogeneous = multicore."** KTU examiners expect the *distinction*: multicore = homogeneous; heterogeneous = specialised blocks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Dark Silicon + Heterogeneous Architectures)**
>
> - **Dark Silicon Definition:** Transistor area that *cannot* be powered within the TDP envelope at the nominal $V_{dd}/f$. Coined by Esmaeilzadeh et al. (ISCA 2010).
> - **Root Cause:** Dennard scaling breakdown (post-2005) → power density rises as $s^{0.5}$ per generation.
> - **Hard Physical Limit:** Sub-threshold slope $S \geq 60$ mV/decade ⇒ $V_{dd}$ floor ≈ 0.6–0.7 V.
> - **Three Walls:** Power Wall, ILP Wall, Memory/Utilisation Wall — all converged ~2005.
> - **TDP Envelope:** Constant (~100 W desktop, ~15 W mobile, ~5 W tablet); hence more and more area must stay dark.
> - **Key Power Equations:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^2 f$; $E_{switch} = \alpha C V_{dd}^2$.
> - **EDP-optimal voltage:** $V_{dd,opt} \approx 0.5 V_{dd,nom}$.
> - **Mitigation Strategies (Esmaeilzadeh taxonomy):** (i) Near-Threshold Computing, (ii) Software-Defined Acceleration, (iii) Heterogeneous Specialisation, (iv) Core Salvaging, (v) Processing-in-Memory.
> - **Heterogeneous Architecture Levels:** ISA-homogeneous (Big.LITTLE) → ISA-compatible (CPU+GPU) → Domain accelerators (NPU/TPU) → Full ASIC.
> - **Amdahl Heterogeneous:** $S_{het} = 1 / \left[(1 - \sum a_i) + \sum (a_i / s_i)\right]$ — serial fraction dominates.
> - **Utilisation Metric:** $\eta_{util} = \sum P_{core} / P_{TDP} \leq 1$. Heterogeneity raises $\eta_{util}$ from ~30 % to > 60 %.
> - **Industry Examples to Quote:** Apple M-series (CPU+GPU+Neural Engine), NVIDIA H100 (CUDA + Tensor + Video), Google TPU (ASIC), Intel Alder Lake (P+E cores).
> - **Quantitative Anchors:** ~80 % dark area projected at 11 nm; 50×–500× perf/W gain from ASIC; 5×–100× from PIM; 1.5×–3× from Big.LITTLE.
> - **Examiner's Mantra:** Heterogeneous architectures *embrace*, not avoid, dark silicon.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asymmetric Multi-Core Architecture - Static and Dynamic (Overall Idea, Example Processors)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Asymmetric Multi-Core Architecture - Static and Dynamic (Overall Idea, Example Processors)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymmetric Multi-Core Architecture (AMCA)** is a chip-multiprocessor (CMP) design paradigm in which the on-chip processing cores are **heterogeneous** — meaning they differ in one or more of the following attributes: instruction-set architecture (ISA) compatibility level, micro-architectural complexity, pipeline depth, issue width, clock frequency, cache hierarchy size, or power budget. This stands in sharp contrast to a Symmetric Multi-Core (SMC) architecture where every core is an identical replica of a single base design.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (Module 4: Parallel Architectures & Advanced Multi-Core Systems), Asymmetric Multi-Core is treated as a response to three converging design pressures:

1. The **Power Wall** — Dennard scaling breakdown makes a single high-frequency core energy-inefficient.
2. The **ILP Wall** — diminishing returns from deeper pipelines and wider issue logic.
3. The **Memory Wall** — disparity between processor speed and DRAM latency.

AMCA solves these by mixing **"big" high-performance cores** with **"LITTLE" energy-efficient cores**, sharing a coherent cache hierarchy and a unified memory address space.

### 1.2 Two Principal Variants

| Variant | Decision Authority | When Assignment Happens | Adaptability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static AMCA** | Hardware/OS based on thread class fingerprint | At thread spawn / process creation | Low — fixed once chosen |
| **Dynamic AMCA** | Runtime scheduler using hardware counters (IPC, cache misses, branch mispredictions) | Continuously, every scheduling quantum | High — migrates threads live |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Specialist Hospital Emergency Department:**
> Imagine a hospital emergency wing with two types of doctors:
> - **Specialist Surgeons (Big Cores):** Senior consultants, slow to respond, take long to consult, but capable of performing the most complex heart surgeries in record time. They consume more salary (power).
> - **General Physicians (LITTLE Cores):** Junior doctors, instantly available, quick to handle routine cases (fever, sprains) very efficiently. They cost less and consume less resources.
>
> - In **Static AMCA**, when you walk in, the *reception desk* classifies your case at the door (at scheduling time) and routes you permanently to either the surgeon or the general physician — no switching mid-treatment.
> - In **Dynamic AMCA**, while you are being treated, a *floor manager* continuously monitors your vitals (runtime metrics like IPC and cache-miss rate). If your condition suddenly becomes critical, you are physically transferred mid-treatment to the surgeon; if you stabilize, you may be shifted back to the general physician to free the expert for a new critical patient.
>
> The hospital's total throughput (jobs completed per joule of electricity) is much higher than if it staffed only surgeons (symmetric big) or only GPs (symmetric small). This is exactly what AMCA achieves inside a single silicon die.

### 1.4 Why AMCA Emerged — The Physical and Economic Rationale

A single-core dynamic power consumption is governed by the well-known CMOS relation:

$$P \propto C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$$

where **C** is switched capacitance, **V** is supply voltage, and **f** is clock frequency. Voltage-frequency scaling (DVFS) gives cubic energy savings at low activity — but only if the workload can be *put* on a slow core. Symmetric designs cannot exploit this because every core is identical. AMCA provides the heterogeneous pool from which a power-aware scheduler can choose.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlighted:** The two most-cited industry metrics for AMCA evaluation are:
> - **Performance-per-Watt (PPW)** — measured in **GOPS/W** (Giga Operations Per Second per Watt), and
> - **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** = Energy × Delay, used to jointly penalize slow execution and high energy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Architectural Anatomy of an AMCA Chip

An AMCA chip is composed of the following building blocks, irrespective of whether it is static or dynamic:

1. **Big Core(s)** — wide-issue (4–8 wide), deep pipeline (14–20 stages), out-of-order, large reorder buffer, large L1/L2 caches, higher TDP (Thermal Design Power, typically **15 W – 125 W** range per core).
2. **LITTLE / Small Core(s)** — narrow-issue (2-wide), shallow in-order pipeline (8–12 stages), small caches, very low TDP (**0.5 W – 5 W** per core).
3. **Shared Last-Level Cache (LLC)** — typically L3, shared by all cores to maintain coherence.
4. **Interconnect Fabric** — a coherent NoC (Network-on-Chip) like ARM CCI (Cache Coherent Interconnect) or Intel Ring/ Mesh.
5. **Global Scheduler / Power-Controller** — the OS thread scheduler (Linux's `schedutil`, `EAS`), or in hardware Intel Thread Director.

### 2.2 Static AMCA — Operational Logic

**Step-by-step operational decomposition:**

- **Step 1 — Design-Time Partitioning:** The compiler (or system integrator) classifies threads into *Compute-Bound* (e.g., scientific kernels, encryption) and *Memory-Bound* (e.g., web browsing, background sync).
- **Step 2 — Affinity Binding:** At thread spawn, the OS pins the thread to a core class using affinity masks (`taskset` in Linux). The decision is **deterministic** and does not change at runtime unless the application explicitly yields.
- **Step 3 — Execution:** Compute-bound threads run on big cores; memory-bound threads run on LITTLE cores. The cores are *spatially and energetically* isolated.
- **Step 4 — No Migration:** Once a thread begins on a core, it stays there for its entire lifetime unless the OS reboots the affinity.

**The "Why":** Static AMCA is simple, predictable, and has near-zero scheduling overhead. It is favored in embedded real-time systems (Automotive AUTOSAR, avionics) where determinism trumps peak performance.

**The "How":** It is implemented purely in software via POSIX thread-affinity APIs, with no hardware support beyond the existing cache-coherence fabric.

### 2.3 Dynamic AMCA — Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Co-Scheduling Baseline:** Multiple threads begin execution, possibly all initially on big cores or in a balanced pool.
- **Step 2 — Runtime Telemetry Collection:** Hardware Performance Monitoring Units (PMUs) sample metrics every few milliseconds: IPC (Instructions Per Cycle), L2 miss rate, branch misprediction rate, stall cycles.
- **Step 3 — Classifier Decision:** A heuristic (Intel Thread Director uses a hardware ML classifier) decides if a thread should be promoted to a big core or demoted to a LITTLE core.
- **Step 4 — Live Migration:** The thread context is transferred across the coherent fabric. State (registers, dirty cache lines) is flushed/forwarded; cost is typically **10 µs – 100 µs**.
- **Step 5 — Iterative Re-Evaluation:** The cycle repeats every scheduling quantum (commonly **1 ms – 10 ms** in mobile SoCs).

**The "Why":** Real workloads are *phasic* — a thread can be memory-bound for 90% of its life and compute-bound for 10%. Static assignment wastes either power or performance in each phase. Dynamic assignment exploits both.

**The "How":** Requires hardware classification (or OS-level `EAS — Energy Aware Scheduling` in Linux 5.x+) and a fast coherent interconnect.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All symbols use the KTU-standard notation. Markdown table delimiter rule observed (no bare `|` inside cells).

| # | Concept | Equation | Variable Glossary | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Symmetric Speedup (Amdahl)** | $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $n$ = cores | Theoretical upper bound for homogeneous cores |
| 2 | **Asymmetric Speedup** | $S_{asym} = \dfrac{T_{big}}{T_{assign}} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha \cdot \dfrac{1}{S_{big}} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \dfrac{1}{S_{little}}}$ | $\alpha$ = fraction on big core, $S_{big}, S_{little}$ = per-class speedups | Used to compare AMCA vs SMC on mixed workloads |
| 3 | **Core-Level Performance Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{IPC_{big}}{IPC_{little}}$ | Ratio of throughputs | Typical industry range: **2 ≤ R ≤ 4** |
| 4 | **Core-Level Power Ratio** | $K = \dfrac{P_{big}}{P_{little}}$ | Ratio of power envelopes | Typical industry range: **5 ≤ K ≤ 20** |
| 5 | **Performance-per-Watt** | $PPW = \dfrac{\text{Throughput (ops/s)}}{P_{total} \text{ (W)}}$ | Scalar efficiency metric | Headline metric for mobile SoC datasheets |
| 6 | **Energy-Delay Product** | $EDP = E \cdot T_{exec}$ | $E$ = energy in joules, $T_{exec}$ = exec time in seconds | Pareto-optimal tradeoff metric |
| 7 | **DVFS Energy Savings** | $E \propto V^{2} \cdot f$ | CMOS dynamic switching energy | Justifies voltage scaling on LITTLE cores |
| 8 | **Migration Cost Penalty** | $T_{mig} = T_{flush} + T_{reload} + T_{coherence}$ | Latency of live thread move | Must be ≪ scheduling quantum to be amortized |
| 9 | **Utilization Bound (AMCA)** | $U_{max} = \min(1, \, \dfrac{n_{big} + n_{little} \cdot (1/R)}{W_{total}})$ | $W_{total}$ = total thread-weight | Shows headroom left by heterogeneous mix |
| 10 | **Critical Thread Speedup (Hill-Marty)** | $S_{hill} = \dfrac{n + \sqrt{n} \cdot (R - 1)}{n}$ for $n$ identical cores; for AMCA modified form uses $R$ | Used to evaluate asymmetric mixes | Quantitative justification for mixing core types |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Use:** The formulas above are routinely used by SoC architects at ARM, Intel, Qualcomm, and Apple to decide the **big:LITTLE core ratio** (e.g., 2:8, 4:4, 6:2) and the **voltage-frequency operating points** of each cluster. The Hill-Marty formula (row 10) is the classical academic justification for heterogeneity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly. No steps are skipped, no placeholder truncation is used.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Asymmetric Speedup vs Symmetric Speedup

We wish to derive the closed-form speedup achievable by an AMCA composed of $n_{big}$ big cores and $n_{little}$ LITTLE cores, compared to a baseline of one big core, when executing a workload of $W$ instructions.

**Step 1 — Define the workload model.**

Let the total workload $W$ consist of two phases for each thread:
- A fraction $\alpha$ that is **compute-bound** (CP instructions) — runs best on a big core.
- A fraction $(1 - \alpha)$ that is **memory-bound** (MP instructions) — runs almost equally well on any core.

**Step 2 — Time on a Symmetric (all-big) baseline.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sym} &= \frac{\alpha \cdot W}{IPC_{big}} + \frac{(1 - \alpha) \cdot W}{IPC_{big}} \\[4pt]
        &= \frac{W}{IPC_{big}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* On an all-big-core baseline, both phases execute at the big-core rate $IPC_{big}$.

**Step 3 — Time on the AMCA system with optimal assignment.**

Compute-bound portion executes on big cores; memory-bound portion executes on LITTLE cores.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{AMCA} &= \frac{\alpha \cdot W}{IPC_{big}} + \frac{(1 - \alpha) \cdot W}{IPC_{little}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The LITTLE core is slower on memory-bound portions by the ratio $R = IPC_{big}/IPC_{little}$.

**Step 4 — Form the speedup ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AMCA} &= \frac{T_{sym}}{T_{AMCA}} \\[4pt]
        &= \frac{\dfrac{W}{IPC_{big}}}{\dfrac{\alpha \cdot W}{IPC_{big}} + \dfrac{(1 - \alpha) \cdot W}{IPC_{little}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $W$ and factor $1/IPC_{big}$ from the denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AMCA} &= \frac{1/IPC_{big}}{\dfrac{1}{IPC_{big}}\left[\,\alpha + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \dfrac{IPC_{big}}{IPC_{little}}\,\right]} \\[6pt]
        &= \frac{1}{\alpha + (1 - \alpha) \cdot R}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R = \dfrac{IPC_{big}}{IPC_{little}}$.

**Step 6 — Sanity-check boundary conditions.**

- If $\alpha = 1$ (all compute-bound): $S_{AMCA} = 1$ — no penalty, all work goes to big cores anyway.
- If $\alpha = 0$ (all memory-bound): $S_{AMCA} = \dfrac{1}{R}$ — the workload is $R$ times slower because it is fully on LITTLE cores. Consistent with definition.
- If $R = 1$ (homogeneous cores): $S_{AMCA} = 1$ — degenerate to symmetric case.

**Final closed-form result:**

$$\boxed{S_{AMCA} = \frac{1}{\alpha + (1 - \alpha) \cdot R}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy-Delay Product Comparison

We derive EDP for AMCA versus SMC for the same workload.

**Step 1 — Define energy and delay per phase.**

| Phase | Delay | Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Compute-bound on big | $T_{cb,b} = \dfrac{\alpha W}{IPC_{big}}$ | $E_{cb,b} = P_{big} \cdot T_{cb,b}$ |
| Memory-bound on little | $T_{mb,l} = \dfrac{(1-\alpha)W}{IPC_{little}}$ | $E_{mb,l} = P_{little} \cdot T_{mb,l}$ |
| Memory-bound on big (SMC) | $T_{mb,b} = \dfrac{(1-\alpha)W}{IPC_{big}}$ | $E_{mb,b} = P_{big} \cdot T_{mb,b}$ |

**Step 2 — Total EDP for AMCA.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EDP_{AMCA} &= (T_{cb,b} + T_{mb,l}) \cdot (E_{cb,b} + E_{mb,l}) \\[4pt]
           &= \left(\frac{\alpha W}{IPC_{big}} + \frac{(1-\alpha)W}{IPC_{little}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\alpha W \cdot P_{big}}{IPC_{big}} + \frac{(1-\alpha)W \cdot P_{little}}{IPC_{little}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* First factor is total execution time; second factor is total energy dissipated. Their product is the EDP.

**Step 3 — Substitute $R = IPC_{big}/IPC_{little}$ and $K = P_{big}/P_{little}$ for normalization.**

Let $W/IPC_{big} = \tau$ (the single-big-core execution time of the whole workload). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{AMCA} &= \alpha \tau + \frac{(1-\alpha)\tau}{R} = \tau \left(\alpha + \frac{1-\alpha}{R}\right) \\[4pt]
E_{AMCA} &= \alpha \tau \cdot P_{big} + \frac{(1-\alpha)\tau \cdot P_{little}}{R} = \tau \left(\alpha \cdot P_{big} + \frac{(1-\alpha) \cdot P_{big}}{K \cdot R}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply to get the closed form.**

$$
\boxed{EDP_{AMCA} = \tau^{2} \cdot P_{big} \cdot \left(\alpha + \frac{1-\alpha}{R}\right) \cdot \left(\alpha + \frac{1-\alpha}{K R}\right)}
$$

**Step 5 — SMC baseline (all work on big cores).**

For SMC, $\alpha = 1$ in both factors, giving:

$$
EDP_{SMC} = \tau^{2} \cdot P_{big} \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = \tau^{2} \cdot P_{big}
$$

**Step 6 — Form the EDP ratio.**

$$
\boxed{\frac{EDP_{AMCA}}{EDP_{SMC}} = \left(\alpha + \frac{1-\alpha}{R}\right) \cdot \left(\alpha + \frac{1-\alpha}{K R}\right)}
$$

*Interpretation:* This ratio must be **less than 1** for AMCA to be Pareto-superior to SMC. Plug in typical industry values $R = 3, K = 10, \alpha = 0.3$:

$$
\frac{EDP_{AMCA}}{EDP_{SMC}} = \left(0.3 + \frac{0.7}{3}\right)\left(0.3 + \frac{0.7}{30}\right) = (0.3 + 0.2333)(0.3 + 0.0233) = (0.5333)(0.3233) \approx 0.1725
$$

So AMCA delivers **~5.8× better EDP** than SMC in this realistic scenario — a dramatic engineering win.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Migration Amortization Bound

For dynamic AMCA, the cost of thread migration $T_{mig}$ must be much smaller than the scheduling quantum $Q$:

$$
\boxed{\text{Migration Overhead Fraction} = \frac{T_{mig}}{Q} \leq 0.01}
$$

This gives the engineering rule of thumb: for a **1 ms** scheduler quantum, $T_{mig}$ must be **≤ 10 µs**. This drives the design of fast coherent interconnects (e.g., ARM CCI-550 with **~150 ns** cache-to-cache transfer).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Functional Block Architecture of an AMCA SoC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef big fill:#FFD7D7,stroke:#B30000,color:#000
```
