```mermaid
    classDef little fill:#D7E8FF,stroke:#003D99,color:#000
    classDef shared fill:#E8FFD7,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFF4CC,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000

    core1["Big Core 0\nOut-of-Order\nWide Issue"]:::big
    core2["Big Core 1\nOut-of-Order\nWide Issue"]:::big
    core3["LITTLE Core 0\nIn-Order\nNarrow Issue"]:::little
    core4["LITTLE Core 1\nIn-Order\nNarrow Issue"]:::little
    core5["LITTLE Core 2\nIn-Order\nNarrow Issue"]:::little
    core6["LITTLE Core 3\nIn-Order\nNarrow Issue"]:::little

    llc["Shared L3 LLC\nLast Level Cache\nCoherent"]:::shared
    noc["Coherent NoC\nInterconnect Fabric"]:::shared
    mem["DRAM Controller\nMain Memory"]:::shared
    pmc["PMU and Power Controller\nRuntime Telemetry"]:::ctrl
    sched["Global Scheduler\nStatic or Dynamic"]:::ctrl

    core1 --> noc
    core2 --> noc
    core3 --> noc
    core4 --> noc
    core5 --> noc
    core6 --> noc

    noc --> llc
    llc --> mem

    pmc -. telemetry .-> core1
    pmc -. telemetry .-> core2
    pmc -. telemetry .-> core3
    pmc -. telemetry .-> core4
    pmc -. telemetry .-> core5
    pmc -. telemetry .-> core6

    sched --> core1
    sched --> core2
    sched --> core3
    sched --> core4
    sched --> core5
    sched --> core6

    pmc --> sched
```

### 4.2 Static vs Dynamic Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef stat fill:#F0E5FF,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef dyn fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000

    A["New Thread Arrives"]:::stat
    B{"Static Classifier\nCompile-time or\nSystem Integrator"}:::stat
    C["Pin to Big Core"]:::stat
    D["Pin to LITTLE Core"]:::stat
    E{"Dynamic Classifier\nRuntime Telemetry\nIntel Thread Director"}:::dyn
    F["Measure IPC, Cache Miss,\nBranch Mispredict"]:::dyn
    G{"Thread Class\nDetected?"}:::dyn
    H["Migrate to Big Core"]:::dyn
    I["Migrate to LITTLE Core"]:::dyn
    J["Stay on Current Core"]:::dyn
    K["Wait Next Quantum\n1 ms to 10 ms"]:::dyn

    A --> B
    B -->|compute-bound| C
    B -->|memory-bound| D

    A --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G -->|compute-bound| H
    G -->|memory-bound| I
    G -->|mixed| J
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> F
```

### 4.3 Mapping of Real-World Processors to AMCA Classes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef arm fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef intel fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef snap fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef apple fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000

    arm1["ARM big.LITTLE\nCortex-A15 + Cortex-A7\nFirst Gen 2011"]:::arm
    arm2["ARM DynamIQ\nCortex-A77 + Cortex-A55\n2019"]:::arm
    arm3["ARM Cortex-X Custom\nCortex-X3 + A715 + A510\n2022 onwards"]:::arm

    int1["Intel Lakefield\nSunny Cove + Tremont\n2020 Hybrid"]:::intel
    int2["Intel Alder Lake\nP-core + E-core\nGolden + Gracemont 2021"]:::intel
    int3["Intel Meteor Lake\nP-core + E-core + LP-E\n2023 Tile-based"]:::intel

    snap1["Snapdragon 8 Gen 1\nCortex-X2 + A710 + A510\n2022"]:::snap
    snap2["Snapdragon 8 Gen 3\nCortex-X4 + A720 + A520\n2023"]:::snap

    app1["Apple A12 Bionic\n2 Vortex + 4 Tempest\n2018"]:::apple
    app2["Apple M2\n4 Avalanche + 4 Blizzard\n2022 Laptop SoC"]:::apple
    app3["Apple A17 Pro\n2 Everest + 4 Sawtooth\n3 nm 2023"]:::apple
```

### 4.4 Performance-Energy Tradeoff Curve (Pareto Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef region fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#333,color:#000

    P1["Symmetric Big\nHigh Perf\nHigh Power"]:::region
    P2["Symmetric LITTLE\nLow Power\nLow Perf"]:::region
    P3["AMCA Dynamic\nHigh Perf per Watt\nPareto Optimal"]:::region
    P4["AMCA Static\nPredictable\nLower Optimum"]:::region

    P1 --- P3
    P2 --- P3
    P1 --- P4
    P2 --- P4
    P3 -. dominates .-> P4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define Asymmetric Multi-Core Architecture (AMCA). Differentiate it from Symmetric Multi-Core Architecture (SMC) with any two distinguishing parameters.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribution shown):**

Asymmetric Multi-Core Architecture (AMCA) is a chip-multiprocessor design in which the on-chip cores are **heterogeneous**, i.e., they differ in micro-architectural features such as issue width, pipeline depth, cache size, or power budget. **(1 mark)**

In contrast, a Symmetric Multi-Core Architecture (SMC) is composed of **identical** replicas of a single core design, replicated across the die. **(1 mark)**

**Two distinguishing parameters:**

| Parameter | SMC | AMCA |
|---|---|---|
| Core homogeneity | All identical | Mix of big and LITTLE |
| Energy efficiency | Lower (all cores burn big-core power) | Higher (workload routed to appropriate core class) **(1 mark)** |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three real-world example processors that implement AMCA and state the core types used in each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **ARM Cortex-A77 + Cortex-A55 (DynamIQ)** — Big performance core + LITTLE efficiency core. **(1 mark)**
2. **Intel Alder Lake (12th Gen)** — P-cores (Performance, Golden Cove) + E-cores (Efficiency, Gracemont). **(1 mark)**
3. **Apple M2 SoC** — Avalanche (big) + Blizzard (LITTLE) cores. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Static AMCA Focus**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model | Expected Time: 25 minutes]**

**(a)** Explain the overall idea of Static Asymmetric Multi-Core Architecture. With a neat block diagram, describe how compute-bound and memory-bound threads are partitioned at design time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List three real-world static-AMCA processors and explain one limitation of static scheduling in modern mixed workloads. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Overall Idea (2 marks):**
Static AMCA partitions the workload at compile time or at system-integration time. Threads are classified into *compute-bound* (CB) and *memory-bound* (MB) classes, then permanently affinity-pinned to either a big core or a LITTLE core respectively. There is **no runtime migration**; the assignment remains fixed for the lifetime of the thread.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   ┌──────────────┐        ┌──────────────┐
   │  Big Core 0  │        │  Big Core 1  │
   │  Wide OoO    │        │  Wide OoO    │
   └──────┬───────┘        └──────┬───────┘
          │                       │
          └──────────┬────────────┘
                     │
              ┌──────┴──────┐
              │ Coherent NoC│
              └──────┬──────┘
                     │
   ┌─────────┬───────┼───────┬─────────┐
   │         │       │       │         │
┌──┴──┐  ┌───┴──┐ ┌──┴──┐ ┌──┴───┐ ┌───┴──┐
│ L0  │  │ L1   │ │ L2  │ │ L3   │ │ L4   │
└─────┘  └──────┘ └─────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘
   MB      MB       MB      MB       MB
```

*(CB threads on big cores at the top; MB threads on LITTLE cores at the bottom; connected via a coherent NoC.)*

**Partitioning Logic (3 marks):**
- The compiler emits thread-class metadata (e.g., `#pragma omp parallel proc_bind(close)` with affinity masks).
- The OS sets CPU affinity via `taskset` or `sched_setaffinity` at thread spawn.
- The system integrator pre-decides which cores are exposed to user space vs. kernel threads.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Three real-world static-AMCA processors (3 marks):**

1. **ARM big.LITTLE (original 2011)** — Cortex-A15 (big) + Cortex-A7 (LITTLE). Global Task Scheduling was the original static-ish mode where the kernel scheduler chose a core per thread.
2. **NVIDIA Tegra X1** — Denver (big, custom ARM) + Cortex-A57 (mid) + Cortex-A53 (LITTLE).
3. **Qualcomm Snapdragon 820** — Kryo "Performance" cores + Kryo "Efficiency" cores, with cluster-based DVFS.

**Limitation (4 marks):**
Static AMCA cannot adapt to **phasic workloads** — a thread whose behavior changes during execution (e.g., a video encoder that is initially compute-bound during motion-estimation and memory-bound during bitstream packing) will be stuck on the wrong core for the lifetime of its activity. This causes either wasted power (if pinned to a big core during memory-bound phases) or stalling (if pinned to a LITTLE core during compute-bound phases). The lack of runtime telemetry feedback makes it unsuitable for **dynamic, user-interactive workloads** like modern Android apps.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Dynamic AMCA Focus**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model | Expected Time: 25 minutes]**

**(a)** Describe the overall architecture of Dynamic Asymmetric Multi-Core Processors. Explain the role of the runtime scheduler and hardware telemetry (PMU). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AMCA SoC has 2 big cores and 4 LITTLE cores. The big core has $IPC_{big} = 4$ and power $P_{big} = 8$ W, while the LITTLE core has $IPC_{little} = 1$ and power $P_{little} = 1$ W. A workload of $W = 10^{9}$ instructions has $\alpha = 0.4$ compute-bound fraction. Compute (i) the speedup over an all-big baseline, and (ii) the energy-delay product ratio relative to the all-big SMC. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Overall Architecture (3 marks):**
Dynamic AMCA consists of:
- A **heterogeneous pool** of big and LITTLE cores.
- A **coherent fabric** (NoC) enabling live thread migration.
- A **hardware telemetry subsystem** — Performance Monitoring Units (PMUs) attached to each core, collecting IPC, cache miss, branch misprediction counters.
- A **global runtime scheduler** (e.g., Intel Thread Director, Linux EAS) that uses telemetry to migrate threads every scheduling quantum (typically 1–10 ms).

**Role of Runtime Scheduler (2 marks):**
The scheduler classifies threads based on their measured behavior. Compute-bound threads (high IPC, low cache miss) are migrated to big cores; memory-bound threads (low IPC, high cache miss) are demoted to LITTLE cores. It also enforces thermal and power budgets (TDP).

**Role of PMU (2 marks):**
PMUs provide per-thread counters at fine granularity (every few µs). These counters feed the heuristic classifier. Without PMU telemetry, dynamic scheduling degenerates to static scheduling.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $W = 10^{9}$ instructions
- $\alpha = 0.4$ (compute-bound fraction)
- $IPC_{big} = 4$, $P_{big} = 8$ W
- $IPC_{little} = 1$, $P_{little} = 1$ W

**Derived parameters:**
- $R = \dfrac{IPC_{big}}{IPC_{little}} = \dfrac{4}{1} = 4$
- $K = \dfrac{P_{big}}{P_{little}} = \dfrac{8}{1} = 8$

**Sub-question (i): Speedup over all-big baseline [3 marks]**

Using the derived formula $S_{AMCA} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha + (1 - \alpha) R}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AMCA} &= \frac{1}{0.4 + (1 - 0.4) \cdot 4} \\[4pt]
        &= \frac{1}{0.4 + 0.6 \cdot 4} \\[4pt]
        &= \frac{1}{0.4 + 2.4} \\[4pt]
        &= \frac{1}{2.8} \approx 0.357
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The AMCA is **slower** than the all-big baseline because 60% of the workload is now running on a core that is 4× slower. This illustrates that AMCA wins on **energy**, not on raw speed.

**Sub-question (ii): EDP ratio [4 marks]**

Using $\dfrac{EDP_{AMCA}}{EDP_{SMC}} = \left(\alpha + \dfrac{1-\alpha}{R}\right)\left(\alpha + \dfrac{1-\alpha}{K R}\right)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{EDP_{AMCA}}{EDP_{SMC}} &= \left(0.4 + \frac{0.6}{4}\right) \cdot \left(0.4 + \frac{0.6}{8 \cdot 4}\right) \\[4pt]
&= (0.4 + 0.15) \cdot (0.4 + 0.01875) \\[4pt]
&= 0.55 \cdot 0.41875 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.2303
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** AMCA delivers **~4.34× better EDP** than the all-big SMC, despite being **2.8× slower in wall-clock time**. This is the classic Pareto tradeoff — slower but dramatically more energy-efficient, ideal for thermally constrained mobile devices.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in AMCA Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Asymmetric" with "Heterogeneous but identical ISA"** — examiners expect you to note that big and LITTLE cores share the **same ISA** (e.g., both AArch64). They differ in micro-architecture, not instruction set. **(Lose 1 mark if missed.)**
> 2. **Forgetting the $R$ ratio in EDP derivation** — students often compute speedup alone and stop. The full marks require both speedup AND EDP. **(Lose 3–4 marks.)**
> 3. **Stating "Intel Core i7" as AMCA** — incorrect. Pre-12th-gen Intel Core i-series are *symmetric* multi-core. AMCA begins with **Lakefield (2020)** and is mainstream in **Alder Lake (2021)** onward. **(Lose 1 mark.)**
> 4. **Treating "big.LITTLE" as a single processor** — it is a **family** of designs spanning multiple generations. Always specify the generation. **(Lose 0.5 mark.)**
> 5. **Saying static AMCA has zero hardware support** — false. Cache coherence fabric and inter-cluster buses are mandatory even in static mode. **(Lose 1 mark.)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Asymmetric Multi-Core Architecture**

- ☐ **Definition:** AMCA = heterogeneous cores on one die, sharing ISA and coherent memory.
- ☐ **Two flavours:** **Static** (compile-time pinning) and **Dynamic** (runtime migration).
- ☐ **Static AMCA pros:** Predictable, low overhead, ideal for real-time.
- ☐ **Static AMCA cons:** Cannot adapt to phasic workloads.
- ☐ **Dynamic AMCA pros:** Adapts to workload phase changes, maximizes PPW.
- ☐ **Dynamic AMCA cons:** Migration cost (µs-scale), needs PMU + fast coherent fabric.
- ☐ **Industry metrics:** **PPW (GOPS/W)** and **EDP**.
- ☐ **Key ratios:** $R = IPC_{big}/IPC_{little} \in [2,4]$; $K = P_{big}/P_{little} \in [5,20]$.
- ☐ **Formula 1:** $S_{AMCA} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha + (1 - \alpha) R}$ — speedup over all-big baseline.
- ☐ **Formula 2:** $\dfrac{EDP_{AMCA}}{EDP_{SMC}} = \left(\alpha + \dfrac{1-\alpha}{R}\right) \left(\alpha + \dfrac{1-\alpha}{K R}\right)$ — energy efficiency.
- ☐ **Example — ARM:** Cortex-A15+A7 (2011) → Cortex-X3+A715+A510 (2022+).
- ☐ **Example — Intel:** Lakefield (2020) → Alder Lake (2021, P+E) → Meteor Lake (2023, Tile-based).
- ☐ **Example — Apple:** A12 Bionic (2018) → M2 (2022) → A17 Pro (2023, 3 nm).
- ☐ **Example — Qualcomm:** Snapdragon 8 Gen 1 / Gen 3 (1+3+4 or 1+5+2 tiered).
- ☐ **Architecture components:** Big cores, LITTLE cores, Shared L3, Coherent NoC, PMU, Scheduler.
- ☐ **Common misconception:** AMCA is **not** about different ISAs — it is about same-ISA, different micro-architecture.
- ☐ **Architectural motivation:** Solves the Power Wall + ILP Wall by trading peak single-thread perf for sustained throughput within a thermal envelope.
- ☐ **Examiner's hot keywords:** Heterogeneity, big.LITTLE, P-core, E-core, Avalanche/Blizzard, DynamIQ, Thread Director, EAS (Energy Aware Scheduling), Hill-Marty, Amdahl asymmetric.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Heterogeneous Multicore Architecture - GPUs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Functional Heterogeneous Multicore Architecture - GPUs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional Heterogeneous Multicore Architecture** refers to a system design paradigm where two or more **architecturally distinct** processor types are integrated on a single chip (or tightly coupled board) to execute different functional roles. Each core type is optimized for a specific class of computation — typically a **latency-optimized general-purpose CPU** combined with **throughput-optimized many-core accelerators** such as **GPUs (Graphics Processing Units)**, DSPs, FPGAs, or AI accelerators.

In the context of **GPUs as functional accelerators**, this architecture is formally defined as:

> A **SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Thread)** based, massively multi-threaded, throughput-oriented co-processor that executes thousands of lightweight threads in parallel under the orchestration of a host CPU, sharing memory and I/O resources through a unified or discrete high-bandwidth interconnect (e.g., **PCIe 4.0/5.0**, **NVLink**).

The governing design philosophy is captured by the throughput metric:
$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Instructions Retired}}{\text{Unit Time}} = \text{IPC} \times \text{Clock Frequency} \times N_{\text{cores}}$$

where GPU architectures deliberately **sacrifice single-thread ILP (Instruction-Level Parallelism)** in exchange for **extreme TLP (Thread-Level Parallelism)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a **high-end restaurant** during the dinner rush:

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Architectural Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Head Chef (CPU)** | Manages orders, plans menu, handles special requests | Latency-optimized, complex control flow, branchy code |
| **Sous Chefs (CPU cores, 4–16)** | Coordinate sub-teams, handle prep work | Sequential & lightly parallel tasks |
| **Line Cooks (GPU SMs, 100+)** | Each cook handles a simple repetitive task (chop, grill, plate) | Massively parallel SIMT workers |
| **Ingredients (Memory)** | Walk-in fridge, pantry, counter space | Memory hierarchy (HBM, L2, shared, registers) |
| **Order Tickets (Threads)** | Hundreds of identical "make 200 salads" tickets | Warps of threads |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Insight:** The CPU is your *master strategist* who makes complex, varied decisions quickly. The GPU is your *army of line cooks* who, given one simple instruction ("chop 10,000 onions"), can crush the workload in parallel. Putting them together in a single kitchen is **heterogeneous computing**.

## 1.3 Why Heterogeneous? The Power/Performance Asymmetry

Modern workloads show a clear divergence between **latency-critical** (UI, OS, branchy code) and **throughput-critical** (graphics, ML, scientific) tasks. A homogeneous CPU cluster is energy-inefficient for the latter. The **Amdahl's Law** constraint is alleviated by accelerating the *parallel portion* with a domain-specific engine:

$$T_{\text{overall}} = T_{\text{serial}} + \frac{T_{\text{parallel}}}{N_{\text{accelerators}} \times \eta}$$

where $\eta$ is the **parallel efficiency** (typically 0.7–0.9 for well-tuned GPU kernels).

## 1.4 GPU vs CPU — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | CPU (e.g., Intel i9) | GPU (e.g., NVIDIA H100) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cores (logical)** | 8–24 | **~16,000+** |
| **Core Design** | Out-of-order, deep pipeline, branch predictor | In-order, shallow pipeline, no branch predictor |
| **Cache (L1 per core)** | **32–64 KB** | **128 KB** (shared memory + L1 combined) |
| **On-chip SRAM** | ~50 MB | **~50 MB** (L1+L2 across SMs) |
| **Memory** | DDR5, ~100 GB/s | HBM3, **~3,350 GB/s** |
| **Thread Count** | ~16 HW threads | **~25,000 resident threads** |
| **Optimization Goal** | Minimize **latency** | Maximize **throughput** |
| **Clock Speed** | 4–6 GHz | 1.5–2.5 GHz |
| **Power** | 125–250 W | 300–700 W |
| **Watt per FLOP** | High | **Very low** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of GPU Architecture

### Pillar 1: Massive Multithreading (TLP)
A single GPU die hosts **dozens of Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)**, and each SM can simultaneously hold **thousands of thread contexts** in hardware. When one warp stalls (memory wait, branch), the SM's **warp scheduler** swaps in another ready warp — this is called **latency hiding via context switching**.

$$\text{Required Threads} \geq \text{Latency} \times \text{Issue Rate}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Students must understand that GPUs have **no data dependency hazard detection logic**. Stalls are *hidden* by switching warps, not by hardware speculation.

### Pillar 2: SIMT Execution Model
**SIMT = Single Instruction, Multiple Thread**. Unlike SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data, e.g., SSE/AVX on CPU), where the programmer explicitly packs data into wide vectors, **SIMT runs scalar code per thread**, but the hardware bundles threads into **warps** (NVIDIA: 32 threads; AMD: wavefronts of 64) that execute in lockstep.

> [!NOTE]
> **SIMD vs SIMT** is a common KTU question:
> - **SIMD:** Explicit vector instructions (`vaddps ymm0, ymm1, ymm2`)
> - **SIMT:** Scalar-looking code, hardware automatically groups threads

### Pillar 3: Memory Bandwidth-Bound Design
GPUs are **bandwidth-bound**, not compute-bound. The performance roofline model states:

$$\text{Achieved Performance} = \min \left( \pi, \beta \times I_{\text{intensity}} \right)$$

where:
- $\pi$ = peak compute throughput (FLOP/s)
- $\beta$ = peak memory bandwidth (bytes/s)
- $I_{\text{intensity}}$ = arithmetic intensity (FLOPs/byte)

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Streaming Multiprocessor (SM)

A modern NVIDIA SM (e.g., Hopper SM) contains:

1. **Front-end:** Instruction cache, dual warp schedulers
2. **Register File:** **65,536 × 32-bit registers** (256 KB) — extremely large
3. **CUDA Cores / INT32 / FP32 units:** 128 per SM
4. **Tensor Cores:** Matrix-multiply accelerators (FP8, FP16, BF16, TF32)
5. **Special Function Units (SFUs):** Transcendental functions (sin, cos, sqrt)
6. **Load/Store Units (LSU):** 16 per SM
7. **Shared Memory + L1 Cache:** 128–256 KB, software-managed scratchpad
8. **Warp Scheduler:** Issues one instruction per cycle per partition

## 2.3 Memory Hierarchy — The GPU's Five-Layer Cake

| Memory Level | Scope | Bandwidth | Latency (cycles) | Size | Managed By |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Registers** | Per thread | ~TB/s | 0 | 64 × 32-bit | Compiler |
| **Shared Memory** | Per block (CTA) | ~TB/s | ~20 | 48–228 KB | Programmer |
| **L1 Cache** | Per SM | ~TB/s | ~20 | 64–128 KB | Hardware |
| **L2 Cache** | Per GPU | ~10 TB/s | ~200 | 40–50 MB | Hardware |
| **Global Memory (HBM3)** | All threads | ~3 TB/s | ~400–800 | 80 GB | Programmer |
| **Constant Memory** | All threads (read-only) | TB/s (cached) | ~20 | 64 KB | Programmer |
| **Texture Memory** | All threads (read-only, spatial locality) | TB/s | ~100 | Up to GB | Hardware |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speedup (Amdahl)** | $S = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}}$ | dimensionless | $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = cores |
| **Roofline Bound** | $\text{Perf} = \min(\pi, \beta \cdot I)$ | FLOPS | Bandwidth-bound if $I < \pi/\beta$ |
| **Occupancy** | $O = \frac{\text{Active Warps}}{\text{Max Warps per SM}}$ | ratio (0–1) | Higher = better latency hiding |
| **Threads per Block Limit** | $N_t \leq 1024$ | threads | CUDA hardware limit |
| **Max Blocks per SM** | $N_b \leq 32$ (Hopper) | blocks | Limited by resources |
| **Warp Size** | 32 threads | threads | NVIDIA fixed |
| **Memory Coalescing Rule** | $T_{\text{trans}} = \frac{N_{\text{bytes}}}{\text{32-byte segments} \times N_{\text{warps}}}$ | transactions | Aligned access → 1 transaction |
| **Peak FLOPs** | $\text{FLOPs} = 2 \times f_{\text{clock}} \times N_{\text{FP32}} \times N_{\text{SM}}$ | FLOP/s | ×2 for FMA |
| **Achieved Bandwidth** | $B_{\text{eff}} = \frac{2 \times S_{\text{bytes}}}{t_{\text{kernel}}}$ | GB/s | ECC ON reduces by ~12% |
| **Compute to Memory Ratio** | $\text{CMR} = \frac{\text{FLOPs issued}}{\text{bytes loaded}}$ | FLOP/byte | Determines roofline regime |
| **Heterogeneous Energy Eff.** | $E_{\text{het}} = \alpha E_{\text{CPU}} + (1-\alpha) E_{\text{GPU}}$ | Joules | $\alpha$ = serial fraction |
| **GPU Pipeline Depth** | $D_{\text{eff}} \approx 6$–$10$ stages | stages | Shallow vs CPU's 20+ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL Markdown Note:** The above table uses `\vert` implicitly by simply using the column separator `|`. For any inline absolute value expressions (e.g., $|x|$), always use LaTeX `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the table renderer.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Scientific Computing:** Climate modeling (NCAR CAM-SE), molecular dynamics (AMBER, GROMACS on GPU)
- **Deep Learning:** Training ResNet-50, GPT-3 inference — Tensor Cores provide **~5× speedup** over CUDA cores
- **Medical Imaging:** CT reconstruction, MRI de-noising
- **Cryptography:** Bitcoin mining, password cracking
- **Automotive:** Tesla FSD chip (custom GPU + NPU) for self-driving perception
- **Edge AI:** Jetson Orin — GPU + Arm CPU + NPU for robotics
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to GPU Acceleration

**Problem Setup:** A program has 95% parallelizable code (good for GPU) and 5% serial code (must run on CPU). The GPU has $N$ processing units with parallel efficiency $\eta = 0.8$.

### Step 1: Define the baseline serial execution time
Let the total serial execution time on a single CPU core be normalized to $T_s = 1$.

### Step 2: Identify serial and parallel fractions
- $P = 0.95$ (parallel fraction)
- $(1 - P) = 0.05$ (serial fraction)

### Step 3: Apply Amdahl's Law with efficiency factor
The accelerated time is:
$$
T_{\text{acc}} = (1 - P) \cdot T_s + \frac{P \cdot T_s}{N \cdot \eta}
$$

**Plain English explanation:** The serial part still takes its full original time (no acceleration possible). The parallel part is divided across $N$ workers, but each worker is only $\eta$ efficient, so we effectively have $N \cdot \eta$ "ideal" workers.

### Step 4: Substitute numerical values
Assume a top-end GPU: $N = 16{,}000$ CUDA cores, $\eta = 0.8$ (real-world average).
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{acc}} &= 0.05 + \frac{0.95}{16{,}000 \times 0.8} \\
&= 0.05 + \frac{0.95}{12{,}800} \\
&= 0.05 + 0.0000742 \\
&\approx 0.050074
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute speedup
$$
S = \frac{T_s}{T_{\text{acc}}} = \frac{1}{0.050074} \approx 19.97\times
$$

### Step 6: Compute the theoretical maximum (perfect scaling)
$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20\times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Even with a *massive* GPU, the speedup is **capped at 20×** because of the 5% serial portion. This is why **Amdahl's Law** is the single most important concept in GPU computing — adding more hardware yields diminishing returns beyond a point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: GPU Peak FLOPS Calculation

**Given:** NVIDIA A100 GPU
- Boost clock $f = 1.41$ GHz
- $N_{\text{SM}} = 108$ Streaming Multiprocessors
- $N_{\text{FP32}} = 64$ FP32 cores per SM
- Fused Multiply-Add (FMA) is 2 FLOPs per instruction

### Step 1: FP32 cores per GPU
$$
N_{\text{FP32,total}} = N_{\text{SM}} \times N_{\text{FP32}} = 108 \times 64 = 6{,}912
$$

### Step 2: FLOPs per cycle (counting FMA as 2 FLOPs)
$$
\text{FLOPs/cycle} = 2 \times N_{\text{FP32,total}} = 2 \times 6{,}912 = 13{,}824
$$

### Step 3: Multiply by clock frequency
$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_{\text{peak}} &= 13{,}824 \times 1.41 \times 10^9 \\
&= 19.49 \times 10^{12} \text{ FLOP/s} \\
&\approx 19.5 \text{ TFLOPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches NVIDIA's quoted **19.5 TFLOPS FP32** for the A100 — confirming the formula.

### Step 4: With Tensor Cores (FP16 + FP32 accumulate)
Tensor Cores reach **312 TFLOPS** — a **16× jump** through matrix-multiply specialization.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Memory Coalescing Transaction Count

**Scenario:** 32 threads in a warp each load one `float` (4 bytes). The threads access addresses `$addr + 0, addr + 4, addr + 8, \ldots, addr + 124$` (i.e., a contiguous, aligned 128-byte block).

### Step 1: Total bytes requested
$$
\text{Bytes} = 32 \times 4 = 128 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 2: Cache line / sector size
Modern GPUs fetch in **32-byte sectors**. Number of sectors needed:
$$
S = \frac{128}{32} = 4 \text{ sectors}
$$

### Step 3: Number of memory transactions
$$
T_{\text{trans}} = 4
$$

### Step 4: Effective bandwidth utilization
$$
\eta_{\text{coalesce}} = \frac{\text{Requested}}{\text{Transferred}} = \frac{128}{4 \times 32} = 100\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-example:** If 32 threads access **strided** data with stride 256 bytes (e.g., columns of a row-major matrix), each thread lands in a *different* cache line:
> $$T_{\text{trans}} = 32 \text{ transactions}$$ — a **8× penalty**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — GPU Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increase → | Decrease → | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core count** | ↑ TLP, ↑ throughput | ↓ single-thread performance | Power, area, complexity |
| **Warp size** | ↑ hardware efficiency, ↓ control overhead | ↑ divergence penalty | Memory coalescing |
| **Register file size** | ↑ occupancy | ↓ SM count per die | Thread context storage |
| **Shared memory size** | ↑ inter-thread communication | ↓ registers, ↓ occupancy | L1 split |
| **Clock frequency** | ↑ single-thread perf | ↑ power, ↓ cores per die | Power wall |
| **HBM bandwidth** | ↑ mem-bound perf | ↑ cost, ↑ power | Roofline ceiling |
| **Branch divergence** | ↓ SIMT efficiency | None directly | Control flow |

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Roofline Model for a Kernel

**Kernel:** Matrix multiplication $C = A \times B$ where $A, B$ are $N \times N$.

### Step 1: Arithmetic intensity
$$
I = \frac{2N^3 \text{ FLOPs}}{3 \times 4N^2 \text{ bytes}} = \frac{N}{6} \text{ FLOP/byte}
$$

### Step 2: Machine parameters (A100)
- $\pi = 19.5 \times 10^{12}$ FLOP/s
- $\beta = 2.0 \times 10^{12}$ bytes/s (1.5 TB/s HBM2e usable)

### Step 3: Crossover (ridge point)
$$
I_{\text{ridge}} = \frac{\pi}{\beta} = \frac{19.5}{2.0} = 9.75 \text{ FLOP/byte}
$$

### Step 4: For $N = 1024$
$$
I = \frac{1024}{6} = 170.7 \text{ FLOP/byte} \gg 9.75
$$

This kernel is **compute-bound** (sits on the horizontal ceiling of the roofline). At $N=1024$, the kernel runs at near-peak $\approx 19$ TFLOPS.

### Step 5: For $N = 32$
$$
I = \frac{32}{6} = 5.33 \text{ FLOP/byte} < 9.75
$$

This small matrix is **memory-bound** — the kernel is limited by HBM bandwidth, achieving only $\beta \times I = 2.0 \times 5.33 = 10.66$ TFLOPS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

> [!NOTE]
> **Branch divergence** causes the warp to execute *both* paths sequentially, with inactive threads masked off. Worst case: a warp that needs to evaluate 32 different branch conditions runs **32× slower** than uniform execution. This is why GPU-friendly algorithms avoid data-dependent branching.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define SIMT Execution Model
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Thread)** is a parallel execution model used in NVIDIA GPUs where a single instruction is issued per cycle, but executed by multiple threads (grouped into *warps* of 32) operating on different data elements.

**(1 mark)** Unlike SIMD (where the programmer explicitly vectorizes), SIMT allows the programmer to write scalar-looking code; the hardware automatically groups threads into warps for lockstep execution.

**(1 mark)** Each thread in a warp has its own **program counter**, **register state**, and **execution path** — but within a single cycle, all active threads execute the same instruction on their own private data.

**(1 mark)** SIMT enables **massive thread-level parallelism** and **latency hiding** by rapid warp switching when memory stalls occur.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate Between SIMD and SIMT Architectures
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **SIMD** | **SIMT** |
|---|---|---|
| **Vector width** | Fixed by ISA (e.g., 256-bit AVX) | Fixed warp size (32 threads) |
| **Programming** | Explicit intrinsics (`vaddps`) | Scalar code, HW groups threads |
| **Branch handling** | Predication or mask registers | Per-thread PC + active mask |
| **Divergence cost** | Predication overhead | Warp serialization |
| **Example** | Intel SSE/AVX, ARM NEON | NVIDIA CUDA, AMD ROCm |

**(1 mark each point × 3 marks):**
1. SIMD requires explicit vector intrinsics; SIMT uses scalar-looking code.
2. SIMD has fixed hardware vector width; SIMT has fixed warp size.
3. SIMT has per-thread program counter enabling easier branching (though at divergence cost).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A: Functional Heterogeneous Multicore Architecture and GPU Design
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the concept of functional heterogeneous multicore architecture. Discuss why GPUs are used as accelerators in such systems, with reference to Amdahl's Law.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition of Functional Heterogeneity (2 marks):**
Functional heterogeneous multicore architecture integrates **architecturally distinct** cores on a single system — typically a **latency-optimized general-purpose CPU** (with deep pipelines, branch prediction, large caches) combined with **throughput-optimized GPU cores** (massive thread count, simple in-order pipeline, wide SIMD/SIMT units). Each core class handles the workload class it is best suited for.

**2. Role Division (2 marks):**
- **CPU** executes serial/control-flow-heavy code: OS, UI, branchy logic, I/O, cache management.
- **GPU** executes data-parallel kernels: matrix math, image processing, ML, scientific simulation.
- **Interconnect** (PCIe 5.0, NVLink) handles data movement and command queues.

**3. Amdahl's Law Justification (3 marks):**
$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N \cdot \eta}}
$$

- The CPU handles $(1-P)$ — the serial portion that cannot be parallelized.
- The GPU handles $P$ with $N$ effective workers.
- Energy efficiency improves since GPU cores have **~10× better FLOPs/Watt** for parallel code.
- Example: For $P = 0.95$ and $N\eta = 12{,}800$, $S \approx 19.97\times$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a neat diagram, describe the internal organization of a modern Streaming Multiprocessor (SM). Explain the role of warp schedulers in achieving latency hiding.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. SM Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The SM consists of:
- Dual **warp schedulers** + **dispatch unit**
- **Register file** (65,536 × 32-bit ≈ 256 KB)
- 4 sub-partitions, each containing:
  - 16 INT32 ALUs
  - 16 FP32 CUDA cores
  - 16 Load/Store units
  - 1 Special Function Unit
  - Tensor Core (Hopper+)
- **L1 cache + Shared memory** (configurable split, total ~192 KB)
- **Texture cache** (read-only)

**2. Warp Scheduler and Latency Hiding (4 marks):**
- Each SM holds up to **64 resident warps** (~2048 threads).
- When a warp issues a **long-latency memory load** (~600 cycles to HBM), the scheduler marks it as stalled.
- The scheduler immediately switches to a different **ready warp** in the next cycle — instruction issue is never idle.
- The original warp resumes when its memory data arrives.
- This **zero-overhead context switching** (no OS involvement) requires only the warp's registers to be preserved (already on-chip) — hence context switch is ~1 cycle vs ~1000+ cycles for OS thread switching on CPU.
- The minimum number of warps required for full latency hiding is given by:
$$
N_{\text{warps}} \geq \frac{L_{\text{memory}}}{I_{\text{issue}}}
$$
where $L_{\text{memory}}$ is the memory latency in cycles and $I_{\text{issue}}$ is the warp issue rate (typically 1 cycle).

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): GPU Memory Hierarchy and SIMT Execution
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Describe the five-tier memory hierarchy of a modern GPU. Explain the programming model that exploits this hierarchy for performance.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Five-Tier Hierarchy (3 marks):**

| Level | Type | Scope | Bandwidth | Programmer Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | **Registers** | Per thread | ~TB/s | Compiler (max 255 regs/thread) |
| L2 | **Shared Memory** | Per CTA/block | ~TB/s | Explicit `__shared__` arrays |
| L3 | **L1 Cache** | Per SM | ~TB/s | Implicit, hardware-managed |
| L4 | **L2 Cache** | All SMs | ~10 TB/s | Read-only `__ldg()` hints |
| L5 | **Global Memory (HBM3)** | All threads + host | ~3 TB/s | `cudaMalloc`, `cudaMemcpy` |

**2. Programming Model — The CUDA Thread Hierarchy (2 marks):**
- **Thread:** Smallest unit, has its own registers and PC.
- **Block (CTA):** Group of up to 1024 threads, all sharing shared memory.
- **Grid:** All blocks for one kernel launch.
- A kernel is invoked as `kernel<<<grid, block>>>(args)`.

**3. Performance Techniques (2 marks):**
- **Memory coalescing:** Adjacent threads access adjacent global memory addresses → 1 transaction.
- **Shared memory tiling:** Load a tile into shared memory, reuse by all threads in block.
- **Constant memory:** For read-only data accessed by all threads (broadcast cache).
- **Texture memory:** For 2D spatial locality (caches by location, not address).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the SIMT execution model in detail. What is branch divergence, why does it occur, and how does it impact GPU performance? Suggest two techniques to mitigate it.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. SIMT Execution Model (3 marks):**
- The hardware groups 32 consecutive threads (in a block) into a **warp**.
- All 32 threads in a warp share a **single instruction issue** — they execute the same PC each cycle.
- Each thread has its **own register state**, allowing different threads to operate on different data with the same instruction.
- The CUDA compiler (`nvcc`) extracts this parallelism automatically from scalar kernel code.

**2. Branch Divergence — Definition and Cause (2 marks):**
- When threads in the same warp take **different branch outcomes** (e.g., `if (threadIdx.x < 16)`), the warp must **serialize** the execution of both paths.
- The hardware uses a **per-warp active mask** (32 bits) — threads not on the current path are masked out (their results discarded).
- The worst case is a **32-way divergence**, where the warp runs 32× slower than uniform execution.

**3. Two Mitigation Techniques (2 marks):**

**Technique 1: Predication (branch-free code):**
- Replace short `if-else` with arithmetic masking:
```c
// Before: divergent
if (x > 0) y = a; else y = b;
// After: branchless
y = (x > 0) ? a : b;  // or: y = (x>0)*a + (x<=0)*b
```

**Technique 2: Restructure data layout (sort by branch condition):**
- Sort data so that threads in the same warp take the **same path** (e.g., Radix sort, BFS frontiers).
- Use **warp-level primitives** (`__ballot_sync`, `__shfl_sync`) to efficiently communicate divergence results.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SIMD with SIMT:** Many students write that SIMT is "just SIMD with threads." This loses 1–2 marks. SIMT has *per-thread program counters* and the *hardware* (not programmer) groups threads into warps.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the role of warp scheduler in latency hiding:** Simply writing "GPUs have many threads" is incomplete. You must explain the **zero-overhead context switch** mechanism and quantify with $N_{\text{warps}} \geq L/I$.
>
> 3. **Mixing up memory types:** Constant memory ≠ texture memory. Shared memory is per-block, not per-thread. Registers are not shared even within a warp.
>
> 4. **Roofline calculation errors:** Forgetting the factor of 2 for FMA (Fused Multiply-Add counts as 2 FLOPs).
>
> 5. **In diagrams:** Do not label the SM block diagram with vague boxes like "ALU stuff" — explicitly mention **warp scheduler, register file, INT32, FP32, SFU, LSU, tensor core, shared memory**.
>
> 6. **In Amdahl's Law:** Students often forget the **efficiency factor $\eta$** and the **serial portion** still taking full time. Write the full formula.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

### ⭐ Definitions You Must Know
- ✅ **SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Thread)** — 32 threads per warp, lockstep execution
- ✅ **Functional Heterogeneity** — architecturally distinct cores (CPU + GPU)
- ✅ **Latency Hiding** — zero-overhead warp switching when stalls occur
- ✅ **Branch Divergence** — serialization of divergent paths within a warp
- ✅ **Memory Coalescing** — 32 threads accessing contiguous aligned addresses = 1 transaction
- ✅ **Occupancy** — ratio of active warps to maximum warps per SM
- ✅ **Roofline Model** — Perf = min(π, β·I)
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law** — S = 1/[(1-P) + P/(N·η)]

### ⭐ Key Numbers to Remember
- ✅ Warp size: **32 threads**
- ✅ Max threads per block: **1024**
- ✅ Max blocks per SM (Hopper): **32**
- ✅ A100: **108 SMs, 6,912 FP32 cores, 19.5 TFLOPS, 1.5 TB/s HBM2e**
- ✅ H100: **132 SMs, ~16,000+ cores, 3.35 TB/s HBM3**
- ✅ Modern L1+Shared memory per SM: **128–228 KB**
- ✅ Register file per SM: **256 KB (65,536 × 32-bit)**

### ⭐ Concepts You Must Be Able to Explain in 2 Minutes
- ✅ Why GPU cores are simpler (in-order) than CPU cores (out-of-order)
- ✅ How warp scheduler hides memory latency
- ✅ Difference between shared memory, L1 cache, and L2 cache
- ✅ The 3 pillars: TLP, SIMT, bandwidth-bound design
- ✅ Why branch divergence is expensive in SIMT
- ✅ How memory coalescing works (aligned vs strided)
- ✅ What Tensor Cores do and why they accelerate deep learning

### ⭐ Common KTU Question Patterns
- ✅ "Differentiate SIMD vs SIMT" — 3 marks
- ✅ "Explain Amdahl's Law with GPU example" — 7 marks
- ✅ "Describe SM internal architecture with diagram" — 7 marks
- ✅ "What is branch divergence? How to reduce it?" — 7 marks
- ✅ "Explain GPU memory hierarchy" — 7 marks
- ✅ "Roofline model for matrix multiplication" — 7 marks
- ✅ "Heterogeneous CPU+GPU execution pipeline" — 7 marks

### ⭐ Pitfalls to Avoid (Revisit Warning Box Above)
- ❌ Don't say "GPU is just a faster CPU"
- ❌ Don't confuse memory types
- ❌ Don't forget the FMA ×2 factor in FLOPS calculation
- ❌ Don't ignore the serial portion in Amdahl's Law
- ❌ Don't draw a vague SM diagram — label every block

> **🎯 Final Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) Diagram with labels → (3) Explanation with formulas → (4) Example/numerical → (5) Comparison/Trade-off**. KTU examiners reward structured, diagram-rich, formula-supported answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 🚀 Module 4: Hardware Accelerators — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware Accelerator (KTU Definition):** A *Hardware Accelerator* is a specialized co-processing unit designed to offload and execute a specific class of computational tasks (e.g., matrix multiplication, convolution, encryption, deep-learning inference) from the general-purpose Central Processing Unit (CPU). It achieves this by exploiting **data-level parallelism (DLP)**, **task-level parallelism (TLP)**, or **model-specific architectural optimizations**, resulting in substantially higher throughput, lower latency, and superior energy efficiency compared to a general-purpose processor running the same workload.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 / Outcome-Based Education)**, accelerators are classified under the broader umbrella of **Heterogeneous Computing Architectures**, where multiple ISA-unique compute units (CPU + GPU + TPU + FPGA) collaborate on a unified memory/IO substrate to satisfy Course Outcomes tied to **CO4: Parallel & Advanced Architecture Design**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PECST528 demands study of *architectural taxonomy of accelerators* — GPU, TPU, FPGA, ASIC, Neuromorphic, and Reconfigurable fabrics — together with their memory hierarchies, programming models, and performance bounding models like **Roofline** and **Amdahl's Law**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **large commercial kitchen** in a hotel:

- The **CPU** is the *Head Chef* — extremely skilled, highly trained, can prepare *any* dish (general-purpose). But if 500 customers order Biriyani simultaneously, the head chef alone becomes the bottleneck.
- The **GPU** is a *brigade of 20 line cooks* — each one makes Biriyani in parallel. Individually less versatile, but together they finish in 1/20th the time (**SIMD / data-parallel workload**).
- The **TPU** is a *dedicated automated Biriyani-vending machine* — it knows *only* the Biriyani recipe, but it produces 100 plates/hour with near-zero human intervention (**domain-specific ASIC**).
- The **FPGA** is a *modular kitchen robot* — its robotic arms can be re-wired every morning to switch between Biriyani, Dosa, or Pasta. Slower than a fixed machine, but flexible (**reconfigurable logic**).

This is exactly why modern data-centers, edge devices, and AI clusters pair a CPU with one or more accelerators — **specialization beats generalization** for a known workload.

## 1.3 Why Accelerators? — The Driving Forces

| Driver | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **End of Moore's Law** | Transistor scaling slowed post-2015; performance must come from *architecture*, not transistor count. |
| **Dark Silicon Problem** | A chip cannot power all transistors simultaneously; accelerators are *dark-silicon-friendly* (low-power, high-density). |
| **Power & Energy Wall** | Mobile and edge devices demand *Joules per inference*, not raw FLOPS. |
| **Domain Explosion** | AI/ML, genomics, crypto, graphics — each domain has unique computational patterns. |
| **Data Movement Cost** | Moving data costs more than computing it — accelerators place compute close to memory (near-data processing). |

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Hardware Accelerators

### A. Classification by Flexibility
1. **General-Purpose GPUs (GPGPU)** — SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Thread) execution; e.g., NVIDIA A100, AMD MI300.
2. **Domain-Specific ASICs** — TPU (Google), NPU (Huawei/Apple), inference engines (Graphcore IPU).
3. **Reconfigurable Logic (FPGA)** — Xilinx Versal, Intel Agilex; bitstream-reprogrammable.
4. **Coarse-Grained Reconfigurable Arrays (CGRA)** — e.g., Samsung's WaveCore.
5. **Neuromorphic Accelerators** — Spiking Neural Networks; Intel Loihi, IBM TrueNorth.

### B. Classification by Parallelism Exploited
- **DLP (Data-Level)** → GPU, SIMD units.
- **TLP (Task-Level)** → Multi-core CPU, heterogeneous SoC.
- **ILP (Instruction-Level)** → VLIW, EPIC, out-of-order cores.
- **MLP (Model-Level / Pipeline)** → Systolic arrays (TPU).

## 2.2 Amdahl's Law — The Fundamental Speedup Bound

Amdahl's Law models the maximum theoretical speedup achievable when only a *portion* of a program is accelerated.

$$S_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}}$$

Where:
- $S_{overall}$ = overall system speedup
- $f$ = fraction of execution time that benefits from acceleration (parallelizable portion), where $0 \leq f \leq 1$
- $s$ = speedup factor of the accelerated portion
- $(1 - f)$ = inherently serial portion (cannot be accelerated)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Pearl:** When $f = 1$ (entire program is parallelizable), $S_{overall} = s$. When $f < 1$, no matter how large $s$ becomes, the speedup is bounded by $\frac{1}{(1-f)}$ — this is the **Amdahl Ceiling**.

## 2.3 Gustafson's Law — Scaled Speedup

$$S_{scaled}(n) = n - f \cdot (n - 1)$$

Where $n$ = number of processors/accelerators.

> Gustafson's Law is more optimistic: it assumes the *problem size grows* proportionally with the number of accelerators, reflecting real-world AI/ML workloads where larger models are trained on more data.

## 2.4 Roofline Performance Model

The **Roofline Model** (Williams, Waterman & Patterson, 2009) is the *de-facto* analytical tool for evaluating accelerator performance.

$$\text{Attainable Performance} = \min \left( \pi, \beta \times I \right)$$

Where:
- $\pi$ = Peak compute performance of the accelerator (FLOP/s or OPS/s)
- $\beta$ = Peak memory bandwidth (Bytes/s)
- $I$ = Arithmetic Intensity (FLOPs per Byte of data moved)
- $\beta \times I$ = the **memory-bound ridge**

### Operational Regimes
| Regime | Condition | Bottleneck |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory-Bound** | $I < \frac{\pi}{\beta}$ | Data movement dominates |
| **Compute-Bound** | $I \geq \frac{\pi}{\beta}$ | Peak FLOPS limits speedup |
| **Ridge Point** | $I = \frac{\pi}{\beta}$ | Crossover point (machine balance) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Used For | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Amdahl's Speedup | $S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/s}$ | Bounding parallel speedup | Dimensionless |
| Gustafson's Scaled Speedup | $S = n - f(n-1)$ | Weak-scaling analysis | Dimensionless |
| Roofline Attainable Perf. | $P = \min(\pi, \beta I)$ | Performance bound | FLOP/s |
| Arithmetic Intensity | $I = \frac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{Bytes Moved}}$ | Workload classification | FLOP/Byte |
| Energy-Delay Product | $EDP = E \times t$ | Accelerator efficiency metric | Joules·second |
| Peak FLOPS | $\pi = N_{cores} \times F \times W$ | Theoretical compute ceiling | FLOP/s |
| Memory Bandwidth | $\beta = \frac{\text{Data Transferred}}{\Delta t}$ | Memory subsystem ceiling | Bytes/s |
| Utilization | $U = \frac{\text{Achieved Throughput}}{\pi}$ | Hardware efficiency | Fraction (0-1) |

> **Engineering Utility:** Roofline + Amdahl together form the **first-pass design space exploration toolkit** used by architects at Intel, NVIDIA, and Google when designing next-generation AI accelerators.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Amdahl's Law

### Statement
Given a program that takes total time $T$ on a single processor, where a fraction $f$ is parallelizable and accelerated by factor $s$, derive the overall speedup.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Partition the execution time:**
The total runtime $T$ is split into two parts — the serial portion and the parallelizable portion.

$$T = T_{serial} + T_{parallel}$$

**Step 2 — Express the fractions:**
Let $f$ be the fraction of the program that is *parallelizable*, so the remaining $(1-f)$ is inherently *serial*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{serial} &= (1 - f) \cdot T \\
T_{parallel} &= f \cdot T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the accelerator speedup:**
The parallel portion is accelerated by factor $s$, so its new execution time becomes $\frac{f \cdot T}{s}$. The serial portion remains unchanged because it cannot be parallelized.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{serial}^{new} &= (1 - f) \cdot T \\
T_{parallel}^{new} &= \frac{f \cdot T}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total new execution time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{new} &= T_{serial}^{new} + T_{parallel}^{new} \\
T_{new} &= (1 - f) \cdot T + \frac{f \cdot T}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the speedup ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{overall} &= \frac{T}{T_{new}} \\
S_{overall} &= \frac{T}{(1 - f) \cdot T + \frac{f \cdot T}{s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $T$ (Common Factor Elimination):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{overall} &= \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{S_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}}}$$

**Step 7 — Boundary Case Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } f = 0 \text{ (no parallelism)} &\Rightarrow S_{overall} = 1 \text{ (no speedup)} \\
\text{If } f = 1 \text{ (fully parallel)} &\Rightarrow S_{overall} = s \\
\text{If } s \to \infty \text{ (ideal accel.)} &\Rightarrow S_{overall} = \frac{1}{1 - f} \text{ (Amdahl's ceiling)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Board Exam Style

> **[Problem 1 — KTU Model Question]**
> A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) training workload spends **85%** of its time in matrix multiplication (parallelizable on a TPU) and **15%** in data preprocessing (serial). If the TPU provides a **40×** speedup on the matrix-multiply portion, compute:
> (a) The overall system speedup.
> (b) The maximum possible speedup even if the TPU became infinitely fast.
> (c) The new Amdahl ceiling if preprocessing is itself accelerated 5×.

### Solution

**(a) Overall speedup with 40× accelerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= 0.85, \quad s = 40 \\
S_{overall} &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.85) + \frac{0.85}{40}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.02125} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.17125} \\
&\approx 5.84
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{S_{overall} \approx 5.84\times}$$

**(b) Amdahl's Ceiling (s → ∞):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}} = \frac{1}{1 - f} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 - 0.85} = \frac{1}{0.15} \\
&\approx 6.67
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{S_{max} \approx 6.67\times}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Even an *infinitely fast* TPU cannot deliver more than 6.67× because the **15% serial preprocessing** acts as a hard ceiling. This is the central reason data scientists spend enormous effort optimizing I/O pipelines, batching, and data augmentation — *you cannot outrun the serial portion.*

**(c) New ceiling with 5× preprocessing acceleration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{new} &= 0.85, \quad s_{serial} = 5 \\
\text{Effective serial fraction} &= \frac{0.15}{5} = 0.03 \\
S_{max}^{new} &= \frac{1}{0.03 + \frac{0.85}{\infty}} = \frac{1}{0.03} \\
&\approx 33.33
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{S_{max}^{new} \approx 33.33\times}$$

> Reducing the serial fraction from 15% → 3% lifts the ceiling by **5×** — a quantitative justification for the design principle: *"first accelerate the serial fraction, then scale the parallel."*

---

## 3.3 Roofline Model — Numerical Application

> **[Problem 2]** An edge TPU has:
> - Peak compute $\pi = 4$ TFLOPS ($4 \times 10^{12}$ FLOP/s)
> - Peak memory bandwidth $\beta = 16$ GB/s
> A convolutional layer requires $3.2 \times 10^{9}$ FLOPs and reads $1.6 \times 10^{8}$ Bytes of feature maps.
> Determine: (a) Arithmetic Intensity, (b) Operating regime, (c) Attainable performance.

### Solution

**(a) Arithmetic Intensity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{Bytes}} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{9}}{1.6 \times 10^{8}} \\
I &= 20 \text{ FLOP/Byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Compute the Ridge Point (Machine Balance):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ridge} &= \frac{\pi}{\beta} = \frac{4 \times 10^{12}}{16 \times 10^{9}} = 250 \text{ FLOP/Byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $I = 20 < I_{ridge} = 250$, the workload is **memory-bound**.

**(c) Attainable Performance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{attainable} &= \min(\pi, \beta \cdot I) \\
&= \min(4 \times 10^{12}, \; 16 \times 10^{9} \times 20) \\
&= \min(4 \times 10^{12}, \; 3.2 \times 10^{11}) \\
&= 3.2 \times 10^{11} \text{ FLOP/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{P_{attainable} = 320 \text{ GFLOPS} \quad \text{(only 8\% of peak)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Takeaway:** The TPU's compute power is *wasted* on this layer — to approach peak, one must either **increase $I$** (operator fusion, kernel batching) or **increase $\beta$** (HBM3, on-chip SRAM caching).
