## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define a hardware accelerator. Differentiate between a GPU and a TPU in terms of their architectural philosophy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hardware accelerator** is a specialized co-processor designed to execute a specific class of workloads with higher performance-per-watt than a general-purpose CPU. It achieves this by exploiting domain-specific parallelism and architectural optimizations.

**GPU vs TPU:**

| Parameter | GPU | TPU |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Thread) | Systolic Array (dataflow) |
| Flexibility | General data-parallel | Domain-specific (matrix ops) |
| Programming | CUDA, OpenCL, ROCm | XLA, TensorFlow Graph |
| Strengths | Graphics, scientific compute | Deep-learning training/inference |
| Cache | Multi-level (L1/L2) | Unified Buffer + Accumulator |
| Instruction | Software-managed warps | Hardware-driven dataflow |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for comparison table)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**State and explain Amdahl's Law. Why does it impose a hard ceiling on accelerator speedup?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Amdahl's Law states that the overall speedup of a system is given by:

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}}$$

where $f$ is the parallelizable fraction and $s$ is the speedup of that fraction. The **hard ceiling** arises because, as $s \to \infty$, the speedup is bounded by $\frac{1}{1 - f}$, meaning the *serial portion* of the program becomes the bottleneck. Even an infinitely fast accelerator cannot overcome the sequential fraction — this is the fundamental reason why accelerator-rich systems invest heavily in minimizing host-CPU bottlenecks (I/O, kernel launches, synchronization). **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A [14 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With neat block diagrams, explain the architectural organization of a TPU. Discuss the role of the systolic array, unified buffer, and activation pipeline.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A workload has 90% of execution time parallelizable ($f = 0.9$) and is accelerated by a GPU with $s = 100\times$. Compute (i) the overall speedup, (ii) the Amdahl ceiling, and (iii) the new ceiling if the serial portion is reduced to 5% by I/O optimization. Comment on the results.

### Model Solution

**(a) TPU Architecture (7 marks):**

The TPU (Tensor Processing Unit) is a domain-specific ASIC designed by Google primarily for neural network inference. Its major blocks are:

1. **Host Interface (PCIe Gen4/Gen5):** Receives instructions from the host CPU.
2. **Unified Buffer (24 MB SRAM):** Stores input activations, weights, and intermediate results close to the compute units to minimize DRAM accesses.
3. **Weight FIFO & Activation FIFO:** Decouple data delivery from the compute unit, enabling pipelined tile-by-tile execution.
4. **Matrix Multiply Unit (MXU) — 128×128 Systolic Array:** A 2D grid of MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) units that performs $128 \times 128$ multiply-accumulate operations per cycle. Data *flows* diagonally through the array (systolic pumping) — weights stream from the top, activations from the left, partial sums accumulate diagonally.
5. **Accumulator Register (4 MB):** Holds 32-bit partial sums, allowing 8-bit / 16-bit inputs to be multiplied with high precision.
6. **Activation Unit:** Performs on-chip ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh — avoiding off-chip transfers.
7. **Pooling/Normalization Block:** Optional but common in modern TPUv4/v5.

**Why systolic?** It maximizes **data reuse** — each weight is loaded once and used for 128 multiplications, drastically reducing memory bandwidth pressure.

**(7 marks distribution: 2 for diagram explanation, 2 for systolic dataflow, 1 for unified buffer, 1 for activation pipeline, 1 for host interface.)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f = 0.9$, $s = 100$.

**(i) Overall Speedup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.9) + \frac{0.9}{100}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.009} = \frac{1}{0.109} \\
&\approx 9.17
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Amdahl's Ceiling (s → ∞):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= \frac{1}{1 - 0.9} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) New ceiling with serial reduced to 5%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{new} &= 0.95 \\
S_{max}^{new} &= \frac{1}{1 - 0.95} = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Commentary (3 marks):**
- With $s = 100$, we are *already* close to the ceiling of 10× (achieving 9.17×) — additional accelerator speedup is wasted on this workload.
- Reducing serial portion from 10% → 5% *doubles* the achievable ceiling (10× → 20×), confirming the **Amdahl's Law design principle**: optimize the serial portion first, then scale the parallel.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students compute Amdahl's ceiling by mistakenly using $f$ directly as the denominator. The correct form is $S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - f}$ — ensure you subtract $f$ from 1 first. Also, do *not* confuse Amdahl's Law with Gustafson's Law; they address different scaling regimes (fixed vs. growing problem size).

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice) [14 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Roofline Performance Model in detail. How is it used to identify whether a workload is memory-bound or compute-bound on an accelerator?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** An accelerator has peak performance of 8 TFLOPS and memory bandwidth of 32 GB/s. A kernel performs $1.6 \times 10^{10}$ FLOPs on $8 \times 10^{8}$ Bytes of data. Determine the arithmetic intensity, the operating regime, and the attainable performance. What single change would you recommend to push this kernel closer to the compute-bound regime?

### Model Solution

**(a) Roofline Model Explanation (7 marks):**

The **Roofline Model** (Williams, Waterman & Patterson, 2009) is a visual + analytical performance-bound model that combines a machine's **peak compute ($\pi$)** with its **peak memory bandwidth ($\beta$)** to predict the maximum attainable performance for any given workload characterized by its **Arithmetic Intensity ($I$)**.

**Core equation:**

$$P = \min(\pi, \beta \cdot I)$$

**How it identifies the regime:**
- Plot $\pi$ (horizontal line) and $\beta \cdot I$ (sloped line) against $I$ on a log-log graph.
- The sloped line is the **memory roof**; the horizontal line is the **compute roof**.
- The intersection $\left( I_{ridge} = \frac{\pi}{\beta} \right)$ is the **ridge point** or **machine balance**.
- If the workload's $I < I_{ridge}$ → **memory-bound** (sloped roof limits).
- If $I \geq I_{ridge}$ → **compute-bound** (horizontal roof limits).

**Utility:** Architects use Roofline *before* implementation to (i) classify kernels, (ii) identify optimization targets (e.g., data reuse, tiling, prefetching), and (iii) compare different hardware platforms for a given workload. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\pi = 8$ TFLOPS = $8 \times 10^{12}$ FLOP/s, $\beta = 32$ GB/s = $32 \times 10^{9}$ Bytes/s.

**(i) Arithmetic Intensity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{Bytes}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{10}}{8 \times 10^{8}} = 20 \text{ FLOP/Byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Operating Regime — Compute Ridge Point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ridge} &= \frac{\pi}{\beta} = \frac{8 \times 10^{12}}{32 \times 10^{9}} = 250 \text{ FLOP/Byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $I = 20 < 250$, the kernel is **memory-bound**.

**(iii) Attainable Performance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \min(8 \times 10^{12}, \; 32 \times 10^{9} \times 20) \\
&= \min(8 \times 10^{12}, \; 6.4 \times 10^{11}) \\
&= 6.4 \times 10^{11} \text{ FLOP/s} = 640 \text{ GFLOPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hardware utilization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{640 \times 10^{9}}{8 \times 10^{12}} = 0.08 = 8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation (2 marks):** To push the kernel toward the compute-bound regime, the engineer should **increase arithmetic intensity** by:
- **Operator fusion:** combine multiple small ops (e.g., Conv + Bias + ReLU) into a single fused kernel so that intermediate feature maps stay in on-chip SRAM.
- **Tiling / Blocking:** process larger data tiles per DRAM access to amortize bandwidth cost.
- **Weight reuse:** exploit the fact that weights in CNNs are shared across many input positions (a systolic array does this inherently).
- Achieving $I \geq 250$ FLOP/Byte would require a 12.5× increase in work-per-byte.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Roofline Pitfalls:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse arithmetic intensity with throughput — $I$ has units of **FLOP/Byte**, not FLOP/s.
> 2. The roofline assumes *infinite cache*; in practice, off-chip memory access is even more expensive than the ridge suggests (consider the **cache-augmented roofline** for advanced problems).
> 3. Units matter: 1 GB = $10^9$ Bytes (decimal) in hardware specs, but sometimes 1 GiB = $2^{30}$ Bytes (binary) — read the problem statement carefully.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🔑 High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams**

- ✅ **Hardware Accelerator** = specialized co-processor for a known workload class; trades generality for performance-per-watt.
- ✅ **Taxonomy:** GPU (GPGPU, SIMT), TPU (ASIC, systolic), FPGA (reconfigurable), CGRA, Neuromorphic.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/s}$ — the serial portion is a *hard ceiling*.
- ✅ **Gustafson's Law:** $S = n - f(n-1)$ — applies when problem size scales with hardware.
- ✅ **Roofline Model:** $P = \min(\pi, \beta I)$; ridge point at $I = \pi / \beta$.
- ✅ **Arithmetic Intensity ($I$)** is in **FLOP/Byte** — never confuse with throughput.
- ✅ **Memory-Bound** vs **Compute-Bound** distinction is central to every accelerator design problem.
- ✅ **Systolic Array** (TPU MXU) maximizes weight reuse — 1 weight read, 128 MACs.
- ✅ **GPU = SIMT** (32-thread warps), **TPU = Systolic dataflow**, **FPGA = LUT + DSP slices**.
- ✅ **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** is the canonical efficiency metric for edge/embedded accelerators.
- ✅ **Dark Silicon + End of Moore's Law** = primary *industrial* motivation for accelerator-centric design.
- ✅ **Common KTU mistake:** mixing up Amdahl's and Gustafson's; remember Amdahl = *fixed problem*, Gustafson = *scaled problem*.
- ✅ **Real-world linkage:** NVIDIA A100 = 312 TFLOPS, Google TPUv5p = 459 TFLOPS, Intel Gaudi3 = 1835 TFLOPS (BF16) — these numbers should be sanity-checked using the roofline.
- ✅ **Architectural trade-off:** ASIC > FPGA > CPU in efficiency, but ASIC < FPGA < CPU in flexibility and NRE cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reconfigurable Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST528 - ADVANCED COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reconfigurable Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Reconfigurable Computing (RC)** is a computer architecture paradigm in which the hardware structure of the processing elements (logic blocks, interconnects, memory, I/O) is dynamically altered at runtime or compile-time to match the computational requirements of a given application. It bridges the gap between the flexibility of general-purpose processors (GPPs) and the raw performance of application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs), by combining programmable logic fabric (typically FPGA-based) with a host processor in a hybrid system.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight**
> A reconfigurable system is characterized by four fundamental properties: (1) **fabric programmability** of logic and routing, (2) **post-fabrication adaptability** to new algorithms, (3) **spatial computation** (parallelism in hardware), and (4) **custom datapath generation** that is tailored per task.

## 1.2 The Compute Continuum

A computer architect views computation as a spectrum. Reconfigurable computing sits precisely between two extremes:

| Architecture Type | Flexibility | Performance (per Watt) | NRE Cost | Time-to-Market |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| General-Purpose Processor (GPP) | **Highest** | Low | None | Immediate |
| Reconfigurable (FPGA/CGRA) | **High** | High | Low | Short |
| ASIC / Hardwired Logic | **None** | **Highest** | Very High | Long |

> [!TIP] **Key Takeaway**
> Reconfigurable computing gives the *illusion of dedicated hardware* while retaining the *flexibility of software* — a concept often called **"soft hardware"**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are a **chef** preparing a multi-course dinner in a kitchen.

- A **General-Purpose Processor** is a chef with a single, universal Swiss Army knife. She can prepare any dish, but switching blades and tools for every cut is slow.
- An **ASIC** is a kitchen permanently built for one specific dish (e.g., a pizza-only oven). It cooks that dish extremely fast, but cannot be used to bake a cake.
- A **Reconfigurable Architecture** is a **modular kitchen** where countertops, burners, ovens, and mixers can be **rearranged and rewired** before or even during cooking. You can reconfigure the station to bake bread in the morning and assemble sushi in the evening — all on the *same physical hardware*.

The "rearrangement" in the hardware world is achieved by writing a **bitstream** that programs the logic cells, switch matrices, and routing channels of an FPGA.

## 1.4 Why Reconfigurable Computing? (Motivation)

1. **Performance Gap** — Moore's Law scaling has slowed for single-thread CPUs, but applications (DSP, ML, cryptography) demand ever-higher throughput.
2. **Energy Efficiency** — A custom datapath eliminates instruction fetch/decode overhead; operations execute directly in hardware gates.
3. **Algorithm Evolution** — Standards change (e.g., 5G, post-quantum crypto). Hardwired ASICs become obsolete; reconfigurable fabrics can be re-targeted.
4. **Time-to-Market** — FPGA development cycles are weeks/months vs. 12–18 months for ASIC fabrication.

## 1.5 Fundamental Trade-off: Flexibility vs. Efficiency

> [!WARNING]
> The fundamental tension: **more flexibility ⇒ more configuration overhead ⇒ lower raw performance**. KTU questions often test whether students understand *when* RC is the right choice (compute-bound, data-parallel, streaming workloads) vs. when a GPP or ASIC is preferable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Reconfigurable System

A canonical reconfigurable platform consists of **three architectural tiers**:

### Tier 1 — Host Processor (GPP)
- Runs the OS, schedules tasks, handles sequential control flow.
- Initiates reconfiguration of the fabric.
- Often an embedded ARM/Intel core on modern SoC-FPGAs (e.g., Xilinx Zynq, Intel Cyclone V SoC).

### Tier 2 — Reconfigurable Fabric (FPGA / CGRA)
- Array of **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)** containing Look-Up Tables (LUTs), flip-flops, and multiplexers.
- **Programmable Interconnect** of horizontal/vertical routing channels and switch matrices.
- **Block RAM (BRAM)** tiles for on-chip data storage.
- **DSP slices** for fast multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations.
- **Hard IP cores** (PCIe, Ethernet MAC, transceivers).

### Tier 3 — Memory & I/O Subsystem
- External DDR/Flash, DMA engines, high-speed serial I/O.

## 2.2 Classification of Reconfigurable Devices

### 2.2.1 Granularity
- **Fine-grained** (e.g., FPGA CLB with 4–6 input LUTs) — handles arbitrary bit-width logic, ideal for control and glue logic.
- **Coarse-grained** (e.g., CGRA, ADRES, MorphoSys) — each processing element is a word-wide ALU; less flexible but far more area- and power-efficient for DSP.

### 2.2.2 Configuration Model
| Model | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Context (SC)** | Whole fabric configured at once | Simple controller | Long reconfiguration time |
| **Multi-Context (MC)** | Multiple bitstreams stored; one active | Fast context switch (ns) | Limited contexts; area overhead |
| **Partial Reconfiguration (PR)** | Only a region is rewritten | True dynamic adaptation | Complex placement & bitstream mgmt |
| **Pipeline Reconfigurable** | Stages of a pipeline configured independently | High throughput streaming | Requires static dataflow model |

### 2.2.3 Reconfiguration Mode
- **Static (Compile-Time) Reconfiguration** — bitstream loaded before task begins; fabric is fixed during execution.
- **Dynamic Run-Time Reconfiguration (RTR)** — fabric is partially or fully rewritten *during* task execution, enabling hardware multitasking.

## 2.3 Reconfiguration Time Analysis (Foundational)

The **Reconfiguration Latency** $T_{\text{reconf}}$ is the time to load a bitstream into the fabric. It is decomposed as:

$$T_{\text{reconf}} = T_{\text{load}} + T_{\text{route}} + T_{\text{verify}}$$

For a bitstream of size $S$ bits loaded over a configuration port of bandwidth $B$ bits/second:

$$T_{\text{load}} = \frac{S}{B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Modern FPGAs use **ICAP (Internal Configuration Access Port)** for internal partial reconfiguration. For Xilinx 7-series, ICAP bandwidth ≈ **400 MB/s**; bitstream size ranges from **< 1 MB** (small PR region) to **> 100 MB** (full device).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield for KTU 2024 Board Exam**
> Memorize the symbols and the **Conditions** column — these appear in derivations.

| # | Symbol | Definition / Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{reconf}}$ | Total reconfiguration time $= \frac{S}{B} + T_{\text{setup}}$ | seconds |
| 2 | $S$ | Bitstream size | bits |
| 3 | $B$ | Configuration port bandwidth | bits/s |
| 4 | $T_{\text{exec}}$ | Execution time on reconfigurable fabric | seconds |
| 5 | $T_{\text{total}}$ | $T_{\text{reconf}} + T_{\text{exec}}$ (effective runtime) | seconds |
| 6 | $S_{\text{reconfig}}$ | Reconfiguration speedup $= \frac{T_{\text{GPP}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $A_{\text{util}}$ | Fabric utilization $= \frac{N_{\text{used}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | $0 \le A_{\text{util}} \le 1$ |
| 8 | $P_{\text{op}}$ | Operations/sec on fabric $= f_{\text{clk}} \cdot N_{\text{LUT}} \cdot \alpha$ | ops/s |
| 9 | $f_{\text{clk}}$ | Fabric clock frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $N_{\text{LUT}}$ | Number of available LUTs | integer |
| 11 | $\alpha$ | LUT efficiency factor ($0 < \alpha \le 1$) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\eta$ | Energy efficiency $= \frac{\text{Workload}}{E_{\text{total}}}$ | ops/Joule |

> [!WARNING] **Equation Typography Note**
> In all formulas above, $\vert$ (absolute value) and $\mid$ are rendered using `\vert` and `\mid` to **prevent breaking markdown table cells**. Do NOT write `|x|` directly inside a table.

## 2.5 Comparison: RC vs. GPP vs. ASIC (Engineering Utility)

| Metric | GPP | RC (FPGA) | ASIC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak GOPS/W | 1–10 | 10–100 | 50–500 |
| Flexibility | High | Medium–High | None |
| Design Effort | Low | Medium | Very High |
| Fault Tolerance | None (SW retry) | Partial (re-route via PR) | None |
| Use Case | OS, GUI, general apps | DSP, crypto, ML inference, prototyping | High-volume mobile/edge silicon |

**Real-world utility:** FPGAs accelerate packet processing (e.g., 100 Gbps firewalls on NetFPGA), real-time image processing pipelines in medical imaging, forward-error-correction in 5G base stations, and CNN inference in low-power edge devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Speedup with Reconfiguration Overhead

Consider a workload partitioned into $k$ sequential kernels, each accelerated on the reconfigurable fabric. The total runtime is the sum of reconfiguration costs and execution costs for every kernel.

**Given:**
- $k$ kernels to be accelerated.
- $S_i$ = bitstream size of kernel $i$ (bits).
- $B$ = configuration port bandwidth (bits/s).
- $T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}}$ = execution time of kernel $i$ on the fabric.
- $T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{GPP}}$ = execution time of kernel $i$ on a general-purpose processor.

**Step 1 — Total reconfiguration time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{reconf,total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{\text{reconf},i} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{S_i}{B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total fabric execution time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{RC,total}} &= T_{\text{reconf,total}} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{S_i}{B} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total GPP execution time.**

$$
T_{\text{GPP,total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{GPP}}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective speedup $S_{\text{reconfig}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{reconfig}} &= \frac{T_{\text{GPP,total}}}{T_{\text{RC,total}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{GPP}}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{S_i}{B} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Critical insight.**

If we take the limit where $S_i \to 0$ (i.e., configuration is instantaneous) **and** $T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}} \ll T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{GPP}}$ (the fabric is vastly faster), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{S_i \to 0,\;\text{fabric}\; \gg \;\text{GPP}} S_{\text{reconfig}} &\to \frac{\sum T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{GPP}}}{\sum T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}}} \\[4pt]
&\approx \frac{T_{\text{GPP,total}}}{T_{\text{RC,total}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This represents the **theoretical upper bound** of speedup. KTU board exam questions frequently test whether the student can identify when reconfiguration overhead negates acceleration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Break-Even Condition for RC Adoption

Define the **break-even point** as the number of times a bitstream must be reused to amortize its reconfiguration cost.

**Step 1 — Cost of one execution of kernel $i$ on the fabric.**

$$
C_{\text{single},i} = \frac{S_i}{B} + T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost of $n$ executions of the *same* kernel (reconfigure once, run $n$ times).**

$$
C_{n,i} = \frac{S_i}{B} + n \cdot T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost of $n$ executions on the GPP (no reconfiguration cost).**

$$
C_{n,i}^{\text{GPP}} = n \cdot T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{GPP}}
$$

**Step 4 — RC is beneficial when $C_{n,i} < C_{n,i}^{\text{GPP}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S_i}{B} + n \cdot T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}} &< n \cdot T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{GPP}} \\[4pt]
\frac{S_i}{B} &< n \cdot \left( T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{GPP}} - T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}} \right) \\[4pt]
n &> \frac{S_i / B}{T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{GPP}} - T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the break-even reuse count $n^{*}$.**

$$
n^{*} = \left\lceil \frac{S_i / B}{T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{GPP}} - T_{\text{exec},i}^{\text{RC}}} \right\rceil
$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Interpretation**
> If the bitstream is loaded $n^*$ or more times in succession (e.g., inside an inner loop), the fabric pays for itself. For **one-shot kernels**, RC may be *slower* than the GPP.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LUT Efficiency and Effective Throughput

Modern FPGAs pack DSP slices, BRAM, and routing around the LUT fabric. Not all LUTs are usable for a given kernel. Define:

- $N_{\text{total}}$ = total LUTs on the device.
- $N_{\text{used}}$ = LUTs consumed by the synthesized kernel.
- $A_{\text{util}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{used}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$.

**Step 1 — Operations per clock cycle.**

If each LUT is configured as a small bit-level operator, the theoretical operations-per-cycle scales with LUT count and a packing efficiency $\alpha$:

$$
P_{\text{op,cycle}} = N_{\text{used}} \cdot \alpha
$$

**Step 2 — Effective throughput.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{op}} &= f_{\text{clk}} \cdot N_{\text{used}} \cdot \alpha \\[4pt]
&= f_{\text{clk}} \cdot A_{\text{util}} \cdot N_{\text{total}} \cdot \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to a GPP core issuing at IPC = 4 (out-of-order, four-wide):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{\text{op,RC}}}{P_{\text{op,GPP}}} &= \frac{f_{\text{clk,RC}} \cdot A_{\text{util}} \cdot N_{\text{total}} \cdot \alpha}{f_{\text{clk,GPP}} \cdot 4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP] **Typical numbers:** A mid-size FPGA has $N_{\text{total}} \approx 500{,}000$ LUTs, $f_{\text{clk,RC}} \approx 200$ MHz, $A_{\text{util}} \approx 0.5$, $\alpha \approx 0.6$, yielding $P_{\text{op,RC}} \approx 30$ Gops/s, often **10–100×** a single GPP core at similar clock.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for KTU Problems

> [!IMPORTANT] Use this matrix when a numerical problem asks "is RC worthwhile?"

| Condition | Interpretation | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|
| $S_i / B \gg T_{\text{exec}}^{\text{GPP}}$ | Bitstream reload dominates | **Do not use RC**; cache bitstream in on-chip BRAM or stay on GPP |
| $A_{\text{util}} < 0.1$ | Poor fabric mapping | Use a coarser-grained CGRA instead of LUT-level FPGA |
| $n \ge n^{*}$ | Reuse count exceeds break-even | RC is profitable |
| Kernel is **streaming** (e.g., FIR, FFT) | Dataflow maps naturally to pipelined fabric | Strong RC candidate |
| Kernel is **control-heavy** with branches | LUT/control overhead dominates | Prefer GPP; offload only hot inner loops |
| Reconfig rate $\ge 100$ Hz | Run-time reconfiguration needed | Need **partial reconfiguration** support |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem (adapted from a KTU 2018 ESE):** A kernel has a 5 MB bitstream. The ICAP configuration bandwidth is 100 MB/s. The kernel takes 40 ms on the GPP and 2 ms on the fabric. Find the break-even reuse count $n^{*}$.

**Step 1 — Compute reconfiguration time.**

$$
T_{\text{reconf}} = \frac{S}{B} = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute per-execution savings.**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{GPP}} - T_{\text{RC}} = 40 - 2 = 38 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply break-even formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n^{*} &= \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{reconf}}}{\Delta T} \right\rceil \\[4pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{50 \text{ ms}}{38 \text{ ms}} \right\rceil \\[4pt]
&= \lceil 1.316 \rceil = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Answer:** The bitstream must be re-used at least **2 times** for the reconfigurable approach to be profitable. The KTU board expects a numeric answer with units and the ceiling function made explicit.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Reconfigurable System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the canonical host–fabric–memory hierarchy used in SoC-FPGA platforms (e.g., Xilinx Zynq-7000, Intel Arria 10 SoC).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Host CPU ARM Cortex A9] -- AXI Bus --> B[Configuration Controller ICAP]
    A -- AXI HP Port --> C[DMA Engine]
    B -- Bitstream Load --> D[Reconfigurable Fabric FPGA]
    C -- Data Transfer --> D
    D <--> E[Block RAM BRAM Tiles]
    D <--> F[DSP Slices MAC Units]
    D <--> G[Programmable Interconnect Switch Matrix]
    A <--> H[External DDR Memory Controller]
    H <-- DDR3/4 --> I[External DRAM]
    D -- PCIe / Ethernet --> J[High Speed IO]
    D -- GPIO UART SPI --> K[Peripherals]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Legend.**
> - **Host CPU** runs the application, schedules kernels, and triggers reconfiguration.
> - **ICAP** is the internal configuration port — it permits the CPU to rewrite fabric regions at runtime.
> - **DMA Engine** streams large data blocks between DRAM and the fabric without CPU intervention (key for throughput).
> - **Reconfigurable Fabric** contains the CLBs, DSP, BRAM, and routing shown in the next sub-diagram.

---

## 4.2 Internal Structure of the Reconfigurable Fabric

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Fabric[Reconfigurable Fabric Region]
        direction LR
        L1[CLB 4 Input LUT] --- L2[CLB 4 Input LUT]
        L2 --- L3[CLB 4 Input LUT]
        L4[CLB 4 Input LUT] --- L5[CLB 4 Input LUT]
        L5 --- L6[CLB 4 Input LUT]
        L1 --- L4
        L2 --- L5
        L3 --- L6
    end
    M1[BRAM Tile 36 Kbit] --- M2[BRAM Tile 36 Kbit]
    D1[DSP Slice 18x18 MAC] --- D2[DSP Slice 18x18 MAC]
    SwitchA[Switch Matrix] --- SwitchB[Switch Matrix]
    SwitchA --- L1
    SwitchA --- L4
    SwitchB --- L3
    SwitchB --- L6
    SwitchA --- M1
    SwitchB --- M2
    SwitchA --- D1
    SwitchB --- D2
```

> [!TIP] **Reading the diagram.**
> The CLB array implements arbitrary combinational and sequential logic. The **Switch Matrices** at the corners connect horizontal and vertical routing channels — the **bitstream** programs these switches to wire LUTs together into the user circuit. BRAM and DSP are interspersed to provide localized memory and arithmetic.

---

## 4.3 Reconfiguration Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Application Starts]) --> S1[Host loads initial bitstream into fabric]
    S1 --> S2{New kernel required?}
    S2 -- No --> S3[Execute kernel on fabric]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Host prepares partial bitstream for PR region]
    S4 --> S5[Write bitstream to ICAP or PCAP port]
    S5 --> S6[ICAP rewrites only the PR region]
    S6 --> S7[Fabric reconfigured fabric continues running]
    S7 --> S3
    S3 --> S8{Kernel done?}
    S8 -- No --> S3
    S8 -- Yes --> S9([Next kernel or terminate])
```

> [!WARNING] **Critical design point for KTU answers:** Notice that the rest of the fabric (outside the PR region) **continues to execute** during partial reconfiguration. This is the core value proposition of run-time reconfiguration — it enables **hardware multitasking** without halting the entire system.

---

## 4.4 Reconfiguration Modes Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reconfiguration Mode] --> B[Static]
    A --> C[Dynamic RTR]
    B --> B1[Bitstream loaded once at boot]
    B1 --> B2[Fabric fixed during execution]
    C --> C1[Full Reconfiguration]
    C --> C2[Partial Reconfiguration PR]
    C2 --> C3[Only selected regions rewritten]
    C3 --> C4[Non PR regions continue running]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between reconfigurable computing and general-purpose computing in terms of flexibility, performance, and power efficiency.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Flexibility:** GPP offers the *highest* flexibility (any algorithm via software), whereas RC offers *moderate* flexibility bounded by the available logic resources on the fabric. (1 mark)
> 2. **Performance:** GPP performance is limited by instruction fetch/decode overhead; RC achieves higher per-watt performance because computations map directly to hardware gates with spatial parallelism. (1 mark)
> 3. **Power Efficiency:** RC eliminates the instruction-fetch and pipeline-control overheads, yielding **10–100×** better energy efficiency (ops/Joule) for data-parallel kernels compared to a GPP. (1 mark)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is partial reconfiguration? Mention one advantage.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Partial Reconfiguration (PR)** is the ability to rewrite a *subset* of the FPGA fabric — typically a designated *PR region* — while the rest of the device continues to operate unaffected. The reconfiguration is performed through the **ICAP (Internal Configuration Access Port)** or **PCAP (Processor Configuration Access Port)**. (2 marks)
> **Advantage:** It enables **hardware multitasking** and drastically reduces reconfiguration latency (only a small region is loaded), allowing time-sharing of scarce fabric resources among multiple kernels in a single application. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [Adapted KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Q.A (a) Explain the architecture of a reconfigurable computing system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the host processor, the reconfigurable fabric, and the configuration controller. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **1. Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a system with three tiers: **Host CPU** (top), **Reconfigurable Fabric** (center, with CLBs/DSP/BRAM), and **External Memory + I/O** (bottom). Show the **ICAP/PCAP** configuration controller and the **DMA** data path. Label bus widths (e.g., AXI 64-bit).
>
> **2. Host Processor (1.5 marks):** The host CPU runs the OS and the main control thread. It is responsible for: (i) task scheduling, (ii) loading bitstreams into the fabric via the configuration controller, (iii) initiating DMA transfers for bulk data, and (iv) handling serial/control portions of the algorithm that are inefficient on the fabric.
>
> **3. Reconfigurable Fabric (1.5 marks):** The fabric is an array of **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)** built from **Look-Up Tables (LUTs)** and flip-flops, interconnected by a **programmable switch matrix** and routing channels. **Block RAM (BRAM)** tiles provide fast on-chip memory, while **DSP slices** accelerate multiply-accumulate operations. The fabric is "soft hardware" — its datapath changes with the bitstream.
>
> **4. Configuration Controller (1 mark):** The ICAP (Internal) or PCAP (Processor-driven) controller loads bitstream frames from external flash or host memory into the configuration memory cells. For **partial reconfiguration**, only specific frame addresses are overwritten, leaving the rest of the device live.
>
> **[Examiner's tip]** — Always include bus labels and arrows for *bitstream flow* vs. *data flow*; these are easy marks and demonstrate depth.

**Q.A (b) A reconfigurable kernel uses a bitstream of size 4 MB, loaded through an ICAP running at 200 MB/s. The kernel takes 50 ms on the host CPU and 5 ms on the fabric. Compute (i) reconfiguration time, (ii) break-even reuse count, and (iii) effective speedup when the bitstream is reused 10 times. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(i) Reconfiguration time** (1.5 marks)
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> T_{\text{reconf}} &= \frac{S}{B} \\[4pt]
> &= \frac{4 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes}}{200 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes/s}} \\[4pt]
> &= 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **(ii) Break-even reuse count** (2.5 marks)
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \Delta T &= T_{\text{GPP}} - T_{\text{RC}} = 50 - 5 = 45 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
> n^{*} &= \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{reconf}}}{\Delta T} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{20}{45} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.444 \rceil = 1
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Conclusion:** Since any $n \ge 1$ already beats the GPP, the break-even is **$n^{*}=1$**, i.e., the kernel is *always* faster on the fabric, even for a single execution.
>
> **(iii) Effective speedup for $n=10$** (3 marks)
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> T_{\text{RC,total}} &= T_{\text{reconf}} + n \cdot T_{\text{RC}} = 20 + 10 \times 5 = 70 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
> T_{\text{GPP,total}} &= n \cdot T_{\text{GPP}} = 10 \times 50 = 500 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
> S_{\text{reconfig}} &= \frac{T_{\text{GPP,total}}}{T_{\text{RC,total}}} = \frac{500}{70} \approx 7.14
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Result:** A speedup of approximately **7.14×** is achieved for 10 reuses.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Adapted KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Q.B (a) Compare fine-grained and coarse-grained reconfigurable architectures. List two examples of each. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> | Aspect | Fine-Grained (FPGA) | Coarse-Grained (CGRA) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Granularity | Bit-level (4–6 input LUT) | Word-level (8/16/32-bit ALU) |
> | Flexibility | Very high (any Boolean fn.) | Lower (limited instruction set per PE) |
> | Area / Power | Higher (more switches) | Lower (less config memory) |
> | Configuration time | Slower (large bitstream) | Faster (compact context) |
> | Ideal workload | Control logic, glue, bit-manipulation | DSP, ML, signal processing |
>
> **Examples (2 marks each = 4 marks):**
> - **Fine-grained:** Xilinx Virtex-7, Intel Cyclone V, Lattice ECP5.
> - **Coarse-grained:** ADRES, MorphoSys, Samsung Warp Processor, Intel's planned FPGAs with hard DSP farms.
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** Fine-grained fabric is chosen when the algorithm is irregular and bit-level; coarse-grained is preferred when the algorithm is regular, data-parallel, and word-oriented — leading to dramatically lower power per operation.

**Q.B (b) Describe the Run-Time Reconfiguration (RTR) model. Explain with an example how partial reconfiguration enables hardware multitasking on a single FPGA. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **1. Definition of RTR (2 marks):**
> Run-Time Reconfiguration (RTR) is the ability to **rewrite portions of the reconfigurable fabric while the application is executing**. The fabric is treated as a *time-shared resource*, similar to how a CPU time-shares its ALU among threads. The configuration is typically managed by an **OS-like runtime** that schedules bitstreams onto PR regions.
>
> **2. Hardware Multitasking via PR (3 marks):**
> Consider an FPGA with two PR regions, **PR-A** and **PR-B**. The system runs two kernels concurrently:
> - While the **CPU** is processing data from a FIR filter, the fabric's **PR-A** region is *reconfigured* in the background to host a **FFT** accelerator.
> - The **PR-B** region is independently rewritten to host a **crypto (AES)** engine, without halting the FIR.
> - A simple **scheduler** decides when each PR region is rewritten based on application priority.
>
> **3. Benefits (1 mark):** Better resource utilization, smaller device size needed, lower BOM cost.
>
> **4. Challenges (1 mark):** Complex placement, bitstream management, and verification — only the *partial bitstream* (not the full bitstream) is loaded, requiring careful floorplanning.
>
> **[Examiner's tip]** — Cite **ICAP bandwidth** and a real platform (e.g., Xilinx Zynq + PYNQ) to gain extra credit.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing static with dynamic reconfiguration** — KTU specifically wants students to mention that RTR occurs *during* execution. Static = before execution only. (–2 marks if confused.)
> 2. **Forgetting to include the ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in the break-even formula. The break-even count is always an **integer** number of reuses.
> 3. **Wrong units in reconfiguration time** — bitstream size is in **bytes** for ICAP specs but **bits** for serial config ports. Always convert explicitly. (–1 mark.)
> 4. **Omitting the configuration controller (ICAP/PCAP)** from the block diagram — this is a high-frequency marker in KTU answer keys.
> 5. **Not distinguishing partial from full reconfiguration** when describing RTR — partial is the *enabling technology* for hardware multitasking.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Save this section for the night before the exam.**

- [x] **Reconfigurable Computing** combines software flexibility with hardware performance.
- [x] Primary platform: **FPGA** (fine-grained) and **CGRA** (coarse-grained).
- [x] **Configuration modes:** Single-context, Multi-context, Partial, Pipeline-reconfigurable.
- [x] **Reconfiguration modes:** Static (compile-time) vs. **Dynamic / RTR** (run-time).
- [x] **ICAP** = internal configuration port; **PCAP** = processor-driven configuration port.
- [x] **Reconfiguration time** $T_{\text{reconf}} = S / B + T_{\text{setup}}$.
- [x] **Break-even reuse count** $n^{*} = \lceil (S/B) \,/\, (T_{\text{GPP}} - T_{\text{RC}}) \rceil$.
- [x] **Effective speedup** $S_{\text{reconfig}} = T_{\text{GPP,total}} / T_{\text{RC,total}}$.
- [x] RC excels for **streaming, data-parallel, compute-bound** kernels (DSP, FFT, crypto, ML inference).
- [x] RC is **not** suitable for control-heavy, branch-intensive code (GPP wins).
- [x] RC gives **10–100×** energy efficiency over GPP and approaches ASIC-level performance at much lower NRE cost.
- [x] **Partial reconfiguration** is the *enabling feature* for **hardware multitasking** on a single FPGA.
- [x] Modern SoC-FPGAs (Zynq, Cyclone V SoC) integrate a hard ARM core with the fabric for true heterogeneous compute.
- [x] Always state **units** in numerical answers and apply the **ceiling function** in break-even calculations.
- [x] Cite real platforms (Zynq, Virtex, ADRES, MorphoSys) in long answers for **bonus marks** under KTU valuation.
